connections.note: Aho-Corasick is how AV / IDS match many signatures in one pass (YARA-style)
Lexing is finite-automaton string matching - the same theory
BPE tokenization is a greedy substring-merging string algorithm

topics.title: Complexity, advanced DS & geometry
body: Where the right structure is non-obvious: **link-cut trees** and Euler-tour trees (dynamic connectivity), **persistent** data structures, **wavelet trees**, and **sqrt-decomposition / Mo's algorithm**. On the theory side: P/NP/PSPACE/#P, NP-completeness reductions, approximation hardness (PCP intuition), and **computational geometry** (convex hull, line sweep, Voronoi/Delaunay).
Capstone the domain by writing a **SAT solver** (DPLL → CDCL) - it ties complexity theory to a tool used in verification, crypto, and program analysis.

subtopics.title: P, NP, and the complexity hierarchy
body: **P** is the class of decision problems solvable in polynomial time. **NP** is the class where a 'yes' answer has a polynomial-time verifiable certificate. P ⊆ NP; whether P = NP is the central open problem in computer science. The key concept: **NP-completeness**. A problem $Q$ is NP-complete if (1) $Q \in NP$ and (2) every problem in NP reduces to $Q$ in polynomial time (NP-hard). Cook's theorem (1971): **SAT** (Boolean satisfiability) is NP-complete. This means if you can solve SAT in polynomial time, you can solve every NP problem in polynomial time (P = NP).

**Polynomial-time reductions** are the tool for proving NP-completeness. To show problem $B$ is NP-hard, construct a poly-time reduction from a known NP-hard problem $A$ to $B$: a function $f$ such that $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$. The classic chain: SAT → 3-SAT → independent set → vertex cover → clique → Hamiltonian cycle → TSP. Each arrow is a poly-time reduction. Knowing this chain lets you immediately classify new problems: if problem $X$ can encode 3-SAT, it is NP-hard; if $X$ reduces to a polynomial problem, it is in P.

**PSPACE** is the class of problems solvable with polynomial space (possibly exponential time). PSPACE ⊆ EXPTIME. PSPACE-complete problems include quantified Boolean formula (QBF), the problem of determining who wins a generic two-player game (like Generalized Geography). **#P** counts the number of satisfying assignments (counting the number of valid Sudoku solutions is #P-hard). **#P-hard** problems are at least as hard as NP (since deciding if the count > 0 is NP). Counting arguments appear in cryptography (counting solutions to discrete log instances) and statistical physics (partition functions).

**The PCP theorem** (1992) states that every NP language has a probabilistically checkable proof (PCP) where a verifier reads only $O(\log n)$ bits of the proof and accepts correct proofs with probability 1 while rejecting incorrect proofs with probability $\ge 1/2$. The PCP theorem implies **inapproximability**: problems like MAX-3-SAT and clique cannot be approximated beyond certain ratios unless P = NP. The Unique Games Conjecture (2002) strengthens this: if true, many problems (vertex cover, max-cut) are hard to approximate beyond specific constants. This is why practitioners use heuristics (simulated annealing, genetic algorithms) for NP-hard optimization: there is no guaranteed polynomial approximation beyond a certain factor.

**Algorithmic strategies for NP-hard problems**: (1) **Exact algorithms for small inputs**: branch-and-bound, dynamic programming (DP over subsets for TSP: $O(2^n n^2)$). (2) **Approximation algorithms**: polynomial-time algorithms with provable approximation ratios. Vertex cover has a 2-approximation (any maximal matching gives a 2-approximation). Set cover has an $H_k \approx \ln k$ approximation. (3) **Fixed-parameter tractability (FPT)**: algorithms polynomial in $n$ and exponential only in a parameter $k$ that is small in practice. Vertex cover has an $O(2^k \cdot n)$ FPT algorithm: branch on an uncovered edge (include one endpoint). (4) **Heuristics**: problem-specific algorithms without approximation guarantees that perform well in practice.

resources.label: Sipser - Introduction to the Theory of Computation
Parameterized Algorithms (free PDF) - Cygan et al.

subtopics.title: Advanced data structures: persistence, link-cut trees, and sqrt decomposition
body: **Persistent data structures** support accessing any historical version while sharing unchanged structure. A **persistent segment tree** creates a new root per update but shares unchanged subtrees with previous versions - $O(\log n)$ new nodes per update, $O(n \log n)$ total for $n$ updates. This enables 'what was the sum of $[l, r]$ at time $t$?' in $O(\log n)$. The canonical application: **offline kth smallest in a range** - build a persistent segment tree over prefix counts sorted by value; query with the root at $r$ and $l-1$.

**Link-cut trees** maintain a forest of trees supporting path queries and modifications in $O(\log n)$ amortized. Operations: `link(u, v)` (add edge making $u$ a child of $v$), `cut(u)` (remove edge to $u$'s parent), `path_query(u, v)` (aggregate over path from $u$ to $v$). Implementation uses **access** (expose the path from a node to its root) with **splay trees** for the internal representation. Applications: dynamic MST, dynamic connectivity, LCT-based network flow. The link-cut tree is the canonical 'hard but useful' data structure in competitive programming.

**Sqrt decomposition** divides an array into blocks of size $\sqrt{n}$, precomputing a block aggregate. Point update: update element and recompute block aggregate: $O(1)$. Range query: full blocks in $O(\sqrt{n})$, partial blocks in $O(\sqrt{n})$. Total: $O(\sqrt{n})$ per operation. It is the standard approach when $O(n \log n)$ preprocessing is too slow or the problem has multiple interleaved operations. **Mo's algorithm** is offline sqrt decomposition on range queries: sort queries by $(\lfloor l/\sqrt{n} \rfloor, r)$ (block of left endpoint, then by right endpoint within block). Adding/removing elements at the range endpoints maintains a current answer. Total: $O((n + q)\sqrt{n})$. Mo's algorithm solves: distinct element count in ranges, mode in ranges, and any query where adding/removing an element from the current set can be done in $O(1)$.

**Wavelet trees** answer a rich set of range queries on integer arrays in $O(\log V)$ per query (where $V$ is the value range): kth smallest in $[l, r]$, count elements in $[l, r]$ with value in $[a, b]$, and rank/select queries. A wavelet tree is a recursively partitioned tree where each node covers a range of values and stores, for each prefix of its array, the count of elements in the lower half of the value range. This allows binary search on the value dimension in $O(\log V)$ using the prefix counts. Wavelet trees are used in bioinformatics (rank/select on DNA sequences), compressed suffix arrays, and competitive programming.

subtopics.title: Computational geometry: convex hull, line sweep, Voronoi
body: **Computational geometry** provides algorithms for geometric objects. The foundation: the **cross product** of 2D vectors $\mathbf{a} = (a_x, a_y)$ and $\mathbf{b} = (b_x, b_y)$ is $a_x b_y - a_y b_x$. Its sign determines orientation: positive = $\mathbf{b}$ is to the left of $\mathbf{a}$ (counterclockwise turn), negative = right (clockwise), zero = collinear. The **CCW test** is the basis of nearly every geometric algorithm. Integer arithmetic avoids floating-point precision issues; scale coordinates to integers when possible.

The **convex hull** of a point set is the smallest convex polygon containing all points. **Graham scan**: $O(n \log n)$ - sort by angle from the lowest point, then greedily add points while maintaining a CCW invariant (pop back from hull if the last three points make a clockwise turn). **Monotone chain**: sort by $(x, y)$, build lower hull left-to-right and upper hull right-to-left using the CCW test. Both are $O(n \log n)$ due to sorting. **Chan's algorithm**: $O(n \log h)$ where $h$ is the hull size - optimal for small hull. Applications: diameter of a point set (farthest pair, found via rotating calipers on convex hull), smallest enclosing circle, gift wrapping, collision detection in physics engines.

**Line sweep** processes events sorted by $x$-coordinate, maintaining an active set of geometric objects. **Segment intersection** (Bentley-Ottmann): sweep a vertical line, maintaining the set of active segments ordered by their $y$ intersection with the sweep line. Swap adjacent segments in the order when they intersect (an event). Total $O((n + k) \log n)$ where $k$ is the number of intersections. **Area of union of rectangles**: sweep by $x$, maintain a segment tree on the $y$-axis to track covered length. **Closest pair of points** (already covered under D&C): the sweep version processes points left-to-right, maintaining a set of points within distance $\delta$ of the current $x$ using a BST.

**Voronoi diagrams** and **Delaunay triangulations** are dual structures. The Voronoi diagram of $n$ points partitions the plane into regions where each region contains points closest to one input point. The Delaunay triangulation connects input points such that no point is inside the circumcircle of any triangle. Fortune's algorithm computes both in $O(n \log n)$ using a beach-line sweep. Applications: nearest-neighbor queries (find which Voronoi region a query point falls in), interpolation (natural neighbor interpolation), mesh generation for finite element analysis, and the topology of protein interaction networks.

**SAT solvers** (DPLL and CDCL) tie complexity theory to practice. **DPLL** (Davis-Putnam-Logemann-Loveland): recursively assign variables, propagate unit clauses (a clause with one unassigned literal must be true), detect conflicts, backtrack. Modern **CDCL (Conflict-Driven Clause Learning)** adds: when a conflict occurs, analyze the **implication graph** to derive a new clause (a nogood) that prevents the same conflict; add it to the clause database; jump back (non-chronologically) to the first point where the new clause is unit. CDCL solvers (MiniSAT, CryptoMiniSat, Z3) routinely solve instances with millions of variables. Applications: hardware verification, bounded model checking, cryptanalysis (encoding cipher constraints as SAT), and automated theorem proving. Writing a CDCL solver is the capstone exercise for this domain.

resources.label: Computational Geometry: Algorithms and Applications - de Berg et al.
Parameterized Algorithms (free PDF) - Cygan et al.

connections.note: Lattice problems (SVP/CVP) and LLL reduction are computational-geometry / complexity results
SAT/SMT solvers drive symbolic execution and automated vuln discovery
Convex hull + line-sweep are the collision-geometry primitives

title: Operating Systems
tagline: From "what is an OS" to writing kernel modules and building your own OS.
overview: The OS is the contract between hardware and every program you'll ever write. Understanding it converts a thousand mysteries into one mental model: processes are the CPU abstraction, virtual memory is the RAM abstraction, files/sockets are the I/O abstraction, and the kernel is the trusted referee enforcing all three.

Learn it by *building*: a shell, an allocator, a FUSE filesystem, a kernel module, and finally a bare-metal OS. The payoff compounds — debugging performance, concurrency, containers, and security all bottom out in OS mechanics.

topics.title: What an OS provides; the syscall boundary
body: The abstractions: CPU→processes, RAM→virtual memory, disk→filesystem. The **fork/exec** model (orphans, zombies, init/systemd as PID 1), **file descriptors** ("everything is a file" — sockets, pipes, `/dev/null`), and **signals** (why `SIGKILL` can't be caught).
Get fluent at the syscall level with `strace`/`ltrace`. Build a shell that handles `fork`, `exec`, pipes, and redirection — it forces every concept here into working code.

connections.note: fds, pipes, and the shell are the concrete form of stdin/stdout you met first
setuid/setgid, /etc/shadow, and process privilege are the attack surface in security
Sockets are file descriptors; the BSD sockets API lives here

topics.title: Processes & scheduling
body: **Context switching** (what the kernel saves/restores), scheduling algorithms ending at Linux's **CFS** (a red-black tree keyed on virtual runtime), **priority inversion** and its fixes, and the container substrate: **cgroups v2** (CPU/mem/IO throttling) and **namespaces** (PID/net/mount/user).
cgroups + namespaces are the entire magic trick behind Docker — there is no "container" object in the kernel, just these two features composed.

connections.note: CFS literally is a red-black tree of runnable tasks
cgroups/namespaces are why Docker works the way it does in production
Container escape & isolation breaks target seccomp/namespaces

topics.title: Virtual memory & allocators
body: **Page tables**, the **TLB**, page faults (minor vs major), x86-64 4-level paging (PML4→PT), **mmap** (how both `malloc` and file I/O use it), **copy-on-write** after fork, and page-replacement (LRU/clock/working-set).
Then allocator internals: `sbrk` vs `mmap`, ptmalloc2/jemalloc/tcmalloc, and **NUMA**. Writing your own `malloc` is the single best way to make all of this concrete.

resources.label: What Every Programmer Should Know About Memory — Drepper (PDF)
Memory allocators 101 — write a simple malloc

connections.note: mmap, sbrk and the free-list ARE the C allocator you implement from scratch
ptmalloc2 bins/tcache are exactly what heap exploitation corrupts
TLB, huge pages and NUMA are the performance side of the same paging machinery

topics.title: Concurrency: futexes, deadlock, lock-free
body: Race conditions and the happens-before model, **mutex internals** (the `futex` fast-path/slow-path split), condition variables (why you always loop on the predicate), deadlock (the four CHMR conditions + Banker's), and reader-writer locks.

Then lock-free programming with atomics: compare-and-swap, the **ABA problem**, and memory ordering. This is the hardest correctness topic in systems and it pays off across kernels, databases, and trading engines.

connections.note: Same atomics/memory-ordering material from the hardware side (acquire/release)
Isolation levels, MVCC and deadlocks are concurrency control in DBs
Lock-free queues are mandatory in low-latency trading hot paths

topics.title: Filesystems & storage
body: Disk layout (MBR/GPT), **ext4** internals (inodes, extent trees), **journaling** (write-ahead logging, ordered vs writeback), the **VFS** layer (`struct inode`/`file`/`dentry`), and copy-on-write filesystems (ZFS/Btrfs — snapshots, checksums, RAID-Z).
Write-ahead logging is the same durability idea databases use; learning it once explains both.

resources.label: Write a FUSE filesystem — libfuse examples

connections.note: WAL journaling and B-tree indexing are shared between filesystems and databases
Extent trees / directory indexes are B-tree variants
Flash wear-leveling and log-structured FS matter on embedded storage

topics.title: Kernel development
body: Kernel vs user space (privilege rings, the `SYSCALL` path), writing a **kernel module** (`module_init`, `printk`, `kmalloc`), character device drivers (`file_operations`, `copy_to_user`), interrupt handling (top/bottom half), and the kernel's most important lock: **RCU**.
Internal allocators (buddy + slab/SLUB) and dynamic instrumentation with **kprobes/eBPF** round this out.

resources.label: The Linux Kernel Module Programming Guide (free)

connections.note: Spinlocks, RCU and memory barriers are kernel concurrency in practice
Driver UAF, ret2usr and commit_creds are kernel-exploitation of this code
Writing a kernel module mirrors writing your own RTOS scheduler

topics.title: Boot, linkers & ELF; build your own OS
body: The full chain: BIOS/UEFI → bootloader (GRUB2) → decompress kernel → `start_kernel()` → init. Write a bootloader that walks **real → protected → long mode**, understand **linker scripts** (`.text`/`.data`/`.bss`, entry point) and the **ELF** format (sections vs segments, PLT/GOT).
Then build a bare-metal x86-64 OS: GDT, IDT, paging, a physical/virtual memory manager, a scheduler, keyboard driver, and VGA text output.

resources.label: Writing an OS in Rust — Phil Opp (blog_os)

connections.note: ELF, linker scripts and PLT/GOT are the linking material, kernel-side
Code generation + assembling end at the same ELF/loader the kernel runs
Vector tables, linker scripts and reset handlers are the embedded analogue of boot

topics.title: Virtualization, containers & eBPF
body: Hardware virtualization (Intel VT-x/AMD-V, VMCS, VM entry/exit), **KVM** (how Linux becomes a hypervisor), **QEMU** + virtio paravirtualization, and container isolation internals (seccomp, AppArmor, SELinux).
**eBPF** deserves special attention: a verified, JIT-compiled VM *inside* the kernel for tracing, networking (XDP), and security — one of the most important systems technologies of the decade.

connections.note: XDP/eBPF at the NIC is the kernel-bypass networking story
eBPF powers modern EDR/detection and sandboxing
VMs vs containers is the substrate of cloud deployment

topics.title: What an OS provides; the syscall boundary
body: The abstractions: CPU→processes, RAM→virtual memory, disk→filesystem. The **fork/exec** model (orphans, zombies, init/systemd as PID 1), **file descriptors** ("everything is a file" — sockets, pipes, `/dev/null`), and **signals** (why `SIGKILL` can't be caught).
Get fluent at the syscall level with `strace`/`ltrace`. Build a shell that handles `fork`, `exec`, pipes, and redirection — it forces every concept here into working code.

subtopics.title: The abstraction stack: processes, virtual memory, and files
body: An operating system is, at its core, a **resource multiplexer and abstraction provider**. The CPU is a single physical resource that can execute one instruction stream at a time on each core; the OS wraps it in the **process abstraction** so that a hundred programs simultaneously believe they own the entire CPU. RAM is a finite flat array of bytes; the OS wraps it in **virtual memory** so each process believes it has its own contiguous address space, isolated from all others. Disks, network cards, keyboards, and GPUs are heterogeneous I/O devices with wildly different hardware interfaces; the OS wraps them all in the **file abstraction** so programs talk to every device through a uniform read/write API.

This three-layer abstraction stack is not incidental — it is the **defining purpose** of an OS. Without it, every program would need to know which physical memory addresses are in use by other programs, write device-specific I/O drivers, and implement its own time-sharing policy for the CPU. The OS does this work once, correctly and securely, and exposes clean interfaces. Everything that appears magical about a modern system — dozens of applications running 'simultaneously', a database and a browser sharing the same RAM without corrupting each other, a single `open()` call working for files, sockets, pipes, and kernel pseudo-files — follows from these three abstractions and the **syscall boundary** that enforces them.

The **kernel** is the trusted software enforcing all three abstractions. It runs at **ring 0** (x86-64 terminology: the CPU's most privileged execution mode) and has unrestricted access to all hardware. User programs run at **ring 3** and cannot directly touch hardware, issue privileged instructions, or access memory outside their virtual address space. The boundary is enforced in hardware: if a user process tries to execute a privileged instruction, the CPU raises a fault and the kernel terminates the process. This is not a software promise — it is a CPU-enforced isolation boundary, which is why OS security fundamentally differs from application-level security.

The mechanism for crossing the boundary in both directions is the **system call**. When your C program calls `malloc`, `printf`, or `read`, it eventually hits a standard library wrapper (`glibc`/`musl`) that executes a special CPU instruction (`SYSCALL` on x86-64, `SVC` on ARM64) with a syscall number in a register and arguments in other registers. The CPU atomically saves the user-mode register state, switches to ring 0, and jumps to the kernel's syscall dispatch table, which calls the appropriate kernel function (e.g., `sys_read`). The kernel performs the operation, writes the result into the return register, and executes `SYSRET`/`ERET` to restore user mode. The entire mechanism is a few hundred nanoseconds but imposes a **context switch overhead** that cumulates: a process making millions of syscalls per second sees significant wall-clock cost from boundary crossings alone.

A concrete way to internalize this: `strace -o trace.txt ./myprogram` intercepts every syscall your program makes and logs its arguments and return value. Run it on any non-trivial program and you will see the exact sequence of kernel requests underlying each high-level action — `openat`, `mmap`, `read`, `write`, `close`, `exit_group`. This is the reality behind C standard library abstractions. Every production engineer debugging a mysterious performance issue eventually ends up reading `strace` output; learn to read it early.

resources.label: OSTEP — Introduction (free textbook)
resources.note: Chapter 1: the three abstractions laid out cleanly

resources.label: The Linux Programming Interface — Ch. 3: System Programming Concepts

subtopics.title: The syscall mechanism in depth: SYSCALL/SYSRET and the vDSO
body: On x86-64 Linux, a syscall follows the **System V AMD64 ABI**: the syscall number goes in `%rax`; arguments in `%rdi`, `%rsi`, `%rdx`, `%r10`, `%r8`, `%r9` (up to six); the return value comes back in `%rax`, with `-errno` indicating error. `SYSCALL` is a fast-path instruction introduced with AMD64 that avoids the overhead of the older `int 0x80` software interrupt path (which required saving/restoring far more state). On `SYSCALL`, the CPU sets `%rcx` to the return `%rip`, `%r11` to `%rflags`, drops privilege to ring 0, and jumps to the address in the `LSTAR` MSR — which the kernel sets on boot to point at `entry_SYSCALL_64`.

The kernel's entry path is worth studying. `entry_SYSCALL_64` in `arch/x86/entry/entry_64.S` saves registers, looks up `sys_call_table[%rax]` (a table of function pointers), calls the handler, saves the return value, and returns via `SYSRET`. Every kernel developer should trace this path at least once: it is where **Spectre/Meltdown defenses** (`SWAPGS`, PTI — Page Table Isolation) live, adding overhead that made some workloads 30% slower when first patched in 2018. The PT isolation defense maintains *two* page table roots — the user-mode one with only the syscall trampoline mapped in kernel space, and the full kernel one — and switches on every boundary crossing. That TLB flush cost is why microarchitectural vulnerabilities can have severe performance consequences.

Many syscalls that only need to *read* kernel state can bypass the full ring switch entirely via the **vDSO** (virtual Dynamic Shared Object): a small kernel-provided shared library mapped into every process's address space at a random address (ASLR). Functions like `clock_gettime(CLOCK_REALTIME)` and `gettimeofday` are implemented entirely in userspace by reading kernel data structures (the `vvar` mapping) without ever entering ring 0. A call to `clock_gettime` with `CLOCK_REALTIME` on a modern Linux system takes ~20ns via vDSO versus ~200ns via a real syscall — an order of magnitude difference. High-frequency applications (trading systems, real-time audio) care deeply about this.

Understanding the calling convention matters when you debug at the assembly level or write syscall wrappers. `strace` uses the `ptrace` syscall to intercept: the tracer process sets `PTRACE_SYSCALL` which causes the tracee to stop at every syscall entry and exit, letting the tracer inspect registers via `PTRACE_GETREGS`. The overhead of `strace` is therefore substantial (two context switches per syscall, plus the IPC to the tracer) — never use it in production; use BPF-based tools (`bpftrace`, `perf`) instead, which attach eBPF probes with near-zero overhead.

A practical exercise to cement the mechanism: write a tiny C program that calls `read(0, buf, 1)`, compile with `gcc -O0 -g`, disassemble with `objdump -d`, and find the `syscall` instruction. Then use `gdb` to set a breakpoint there and step through the transition (in user mode, it will appear to jump back after `syscall` returns). Then rewrite the same program using `syscall(SYS_read, 0, buf, 1)` directly, bypassing libc entirely. This makes the contract concrete: the kernel's ABI, not libc's, is the true API.

subtopics.title: File descriptors, pipes, and 'everything is a file'
body: A **file descriptor** (fd) is a small non-negative integer (typically starting at 3, since 0/stdin, 1/stdout, 2/stderr are pre-opened) that refers to an entry in the kernel's **per-process open-file table**. Each entry points to a **struct file** in kernel memory, which in turn points to a **struct inode** (the actual file's metadata) via a **dentry** (directory entry). When you call `open()`, the kernel allocates a `struct file`, sets its offset to 0, and returns the lowest available fd. When you call `read(fd, buf, n)`, the kernel looks up the `struct file`, calls `file->f_op->read` (a function pointer in the VFS), advances the offset, and copies data to user space.

The power of this model is **polymorphism**: the same `read()`/`write()`/`close()` syscalls work on regular files, directories, pipes, UNIX domain sockets, TCP sockets, character devices (`/dev/tty`, `/dev/urandom`), block devices, pseudo-files in `/proc` and `/sys`, and even abstract file descriptors like `eventfd`, `timerfd`, `signalfd`, and `inotify` fds. Each implements the same `file_operations` vtable in the kernel. This is a real-world application of the **strategy pattern** from OOP, but enforced in C by function pointer tables.

**Pipes** are anonymous one-way byte streams backed by a 64 KB kernel ring buffer (default, tunable via `fcntl(F_SETPIPE_SZ)`). `pipe(int fds[2])` creates two fds: `fds[0]` is the read end, `fds[1]` the write end. When the buffer is full, `write()` blocks; when empty, `read()` blocks. In a shell pipeline `ls | grep foo | wc -l`, the shell calls `pipe()` twice to get two pipes, forks three children, uses `dup2()` to wire up stdin/stdout of each child to the appropriate pipe ends, then closes the originals. The shell's skill is fd plumbing — the actual programs (`ls`, `grep`, `wc`) know nothing about pipes; they just write to fd 1 and read from fd 0.

**`dup2(oldfd, newfd)`** closes `newfd` if open and makes it refer to the same underlying file description as `oldfd`. This is the syscall behind all shell redirection: `./program > output.txt` is `newfd=1`, `oldfd=open("output.txt")`, then close the original fd. The file description (in the kernel open-file table) is shared, meaning the offset is shared — two processes with `dup`-ed fds reading the same file will interleave their reads without overwriting each other's progress. This sharing is intentional and is how `fork()` preserves the parent's file descriptors: after fork, parent and child share the same file descriptions (and therefore the same offset state) for every fd inherited.

Special pseudo-filesystems make the 'everything is a file' philosophy concrete. `/proc/<pid>/maps` shows a process's virtual memory layout. `/proc/<pid>/fd/` contains a symlink for every open fd. `/sys/class/net/eth0/speed` is a file you can `cat` to read the NIC link speed. `/dev/urandom` gives you cryptographically strong random bytes via `read()`. None of these are on disk — they are **filesystems synthesized in memory** by kernel code. Mastery of `/proc` turns debugging from guesswork into direct inspection: `cat /proc/<pid>/status` to see memory usage, `cat /proc/<pid>/wchan` to see what syscall a blocked process is waiting in, `cat /proc/<pid>/syscall` to see the current syscall and arguments live.

resources.label: TLPI — Chapter 5: File I/O and chapter 44: Pipes and FIFOs

subtopics.title: The process lifecycle: fork, exec, wait, signals, and zombies
body: On UNIX, every process (except the first) is born via **`fork()`**: a syscall that creates an exact copy of the calling process. The child gets a copy of the parent's virtual address space (via **copy-on-write** — no physical pages are copied until one side writes), its own PID, copies of all open file descriptions, copies of signal dispositions, and the same program counter. Both parent and child return from `fork()` — the parent gets the child's PID, the child gets 0. This is the language-level explanation; at the kernel level, `fork()` allocates a new `task_struct`, shallow-copies the parent's, flips page table entries to read-only, and increments reference counts on shared resources.

After fork, the child typically calls **`execve(path, argv, envp)`** which replaces the current process image entirely: it loads a new ELF binary from `path`, sets up a fresh stack and heap, initializes registers per the ABI, and jumps to the entry point. The PID is preserved; open file descriptors survive (unless marked `O_CLOEXEC`); the parent-child relationship is preserved. The fork+exec split (versus a monolithic spawn) is a deliberate Unix design: between fork and exec, the child can freely manipulate its fds, set signal handlers, change working directory, set resource limits — all before the new program is loaded. This is how shells implement redirection and piping without the child program needing to know.

**`wait()`/`waitpid()`** is how parents reap children. When a child calls `exit()` or is killed, the kernel transitions it to the **zombie state**: all resources are released (memory, fds) but the `task_struct` persists with the exit status, waiting to be read by the parent. Until the parent calls `wait()`, the zombie occupies a PID in the process table. If the parent never calls `wait()`, zombies accumulate — they consume no memory but do consume PID slots. The fix in daemon code is to either call `waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG)` periodically or to `SIG_IGN` the `SIGCHLD` signal (which tells the kernel to auto-reap). If the *parent* dies before the child, the child becomes an **orphan** and is **reparented to PID 1** (systemd/init), which calls `wait()` in a loop to reap orphans — that is systemd's most fundamental OS role.

**Signals** are asynchronous notifications sent to a process: `kill(pid, signum)` from another process, or generated by the kernel (SIGSEGV on memory fault, SIGBUS on bus error, SIGFPE on arithmetic fault, SIGPIPE on write to a closed pipe). The process can **catch** a signal (install a signal handler with `sigaction`), **ignore** it, or leave the **default action** (usually terminate, core dump for SIGSEGV/SIGBUS/SIGFPE, continue for SIGCONT). **`SIGKILL` (9)** and **`SIGSTOP` (19)** cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored — the kernel handles them directly. SIGKILL causes immediate termination with no cleanup; this is why processes that catch SIGTERM to do graceful shutdown still die to SIGKILL.

Signal delivery is subtle: signals are **not queued** (except real-time signals `SIGRTMIN`-`SIGRTMAX`) — if a signal arrives while it is blocked, a second arrival of the same signal is lost. Signal handlers run on the process's main thread's stack (asynchronously, in the kernel's return-from-syscall path), meaning only **async-signal-safe** functions (listed in `signal-safety(7)`) may be called from handlers. Calling `printf` or `malloc` from a signal handler is undefined behavior because they use locks that may already be held. The idiomatic pattern is to write to a `signalfd` or `pipe` in the handler (both async-signal-safe), then `poll()` on that fd in the main loop — converting async signals to synchronous events.

resources.label: TLPI — Chapter 24: Process Creation (fork) and Chapter 26: Child Processes (wait)
signal-safety(7) — man page listing async-signal-safe functions

subtopics.title: Tracing and inspection: strace, /proc, and perf
body: `strace` is the fastest way to understand what a program is *actually* doing at the kernel boundary. It works via `ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL)`: the kernel delivers a `SIGTRAP` to the tracer each time the tracee enters or exits a syscall, letting the tracer inspect registers. The output format shows `syscall(arg1, arg2, ...) = retval`, with errno decoded as a string on failure. Practical debugging workflow: `strace -e trace=openat ./prog` filters to just `openat` calls — useful to find which config file a program is trying to load. `strace -T` shows per-syscall time. `strace -f` traces child processes too (essential for shell scripts). `strace -p <pid>` attaches to a running process.

The **`/proc` filesystem** is a window into live kernel state, synthesized on demand from kernel data structures. Key entries: `/proc/<pid>/maps` — virtual memory layout (start, end, perms, offset, device, inode, file path for each mapping). `/proc/<pid>/smaps` — detailed per-mapping stats including RSS, PSS, dirty pages. `/proc/<pid>/status` — PID, PPID, UID, GID, VmRSS (physical RAM), VmSize (virtual), thread count, signal masks. `/proc/<pid>/fd/` — one symlink per open fd, pointing to the underlying file or socket. `/proc/<pid>/cgroup` — which cgroup hierarchy the process belongs to. `/proc/meminfo` — system-wide memory breakdown. `/proc/net/tcp` — all TCP connections with local/remote addresses in hex.

`perf` is the Linux performance analysis Swiss Army knife, built on **perf_event_open** syscalls and kernel PMU (Performance Monitoring Unit) hardware support. `perf stat ./prog` reports hardware counters: CPU cycles, instructions, cache misses, branch mispredictions — the raw numbers behind 'this code is slow'. `perf record ./prog && perf report` does statistical CPU profiling via sampling (sends SIGPROF at a configurable rate, records the PC, builds a call graph). The flame graph visualization (`perf script | stackcollapse-perf.pl | flamegraph.pl`) is Brendan Gregg's invention and is now the industry standard for visualizing where CPU time goes.

`ltrace` is the userspace complement to `strace`: it intercepts dynamic library calls (libc functions, not syscalls directly) via `LD_PRELOAD` or `ptrace` + breakpoints on the PLT. `ltrace ./prog` shows every call to `malloc`, `free`, `strlen`, `fopen`, etc. with arguments and return values. This is invaluable for reverse engineering closed-source programs (finding which library functions they call and with what arguments), and for debugging performance regressions in code heavy on allocations. Note that since glibc 2.34 many functions are no longer in libpthread/libc as separate shared libraries, which can reduce what ltrace sees — `strace` remains more reliable.

For production systems, **`/sys` and `/sys/kernel/debug`** (debugfs) expose kernel internals safely: `/sys/kernel/debug/tracing/` is the `ftrace` interface, letting you enable function tracers and event tracepoints without any tools installed. `echo function > /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/current_tracer && cat /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/trace_pipe` streams every kernel function call live. `eBPF`-based tools (`bpftrace`, BCC toolkit) build on this infrastructure: `bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_read { printf("%s\n", comm); }'` prints the process name for every `read` syscall with near-zero overhead. This is the modern production tracing stack and combines the observability of strace with the performance headroom of hardware counters.

resources.label: Brendan Gregg — Linux Performance page (tools & perf)
proc(5) — man page for the /proc filesystem

connections.note: fds, pipes, and the shell are the concrete form of stdin/stdout you met first
setuid/setgid, /etc/shadow, and process privilege are the attack surface in security
Sockets are file descriptors; the BSD sockets API lives here

topics.title: Processes & scheduling
body: **Context switching** (what the kernel saves/restores), scheduling algorithms ending at Linux's **CFS** (a red-black tree keyed on virtual runtime), **priority inversion** and its fixes, and the container substrate: **cgroups v2** (CPU/mem/IO throttling) and **namespaces** (PID/net/mount/user).
cgroups + namespaces are the entire magic trick behind Docker — there is no "container" object in the kernel, just these two features composed.

subtopics.title: Process vs thread: task_struct, PCB, and the scheduler's view
body: In the Linux kernel there is no fundamental distinction between a 'process' and a 'thread' — both are represented by a **`task_struct`** (the Process Control Block, roughly 7 KB of data) and scheduled by the same CFS. The distinction is in *what they share*: two tasks are threads of the same process if they share the same **memory descriptor** (`mm_struct`), the same **file descriptor table**, the same **signal handlers**, and the same **namespace set**. `pthread_create` under the hood calls `clone()` with `CLONE_VM | CLONE_FS | CLONE_FILES | CLONE_SIGHAND | CLONE_THREAD` — a single syscall with flags specifying what the new task shares with its creator.

The `task_struct` is the kernel's model of a schedulable entity. It contains: the process/thread PID and TGID (Thread Group ID — all threads in a process share the TGID, which is the PID visible in userspace); `mm` (pointer to the `mm_struct` describing the virtual address space); `files` (pointer to the open-file table); `fs` (filesystem context, working directory, root directory); `signal` (signal handlers, blocked signals, pending signals); `sched_entity se` (the CFS scheduling entity with virtual runtime); `cred` (UID/GID/capabilities); `nsproxy` (pointers to all namespace structs); and much more. Every kernel mechanism that touches 'a process' actually operates on a `task_struct`.

**Context switching** is the act of pausing one task and resuming another. On x86-64, the kernel saves (to the task's kernel stack): all general-purpose registers, the segment registers, the FPU/SSE/AVX state (lazily — only when the task actually uses floating point), and the `%rsp`. The `%rip` is implicitly saved because the task will resume from `schedule()` — specifically from `__switch_to_asm` in `arch/x86/entry/entry_64.S`, which does a `jmp __switch_to` to finish the hardware context switch. The page-table register `%cr3` is updated to point to the new task's page table root, flushing the TLB (this is the expensive part — hardware context switch costs ~1-10 µs primarily because of TLB refilling).

The **kernel stack** of each task (typically 8 KB on x86-64) is a separate allocation from the user stack and lives in kernel virtual memory. When a task makes a syscall or incurs a fault, execution transitions to this kernel stack. The `task_struct` is at the bottom of the kernel stack page (via `current` macro using `%rsp & ~(THREAD_SIZE-1)`). Understanding that every task has two stacks (user and kernel) is essential for reading kernel stack traces from crash dumps and for understanding kernel exploits (`ret2usr` techniques target the transition between the two).

User-visible concurrency primitives — `pthread` mutexes, condition variables, barriers — are implemented via the `futex` syscall on top of this task machinery. A suspended task (blocked on a mutex) is not spinning; it has called `futex(FUTEX_WAIT)`, which called `schedule()`, which found another runnable task and switched to it. The blocked task's `task_struct` is on a wait queue; waking it means removing it from the queue and calling `wake_up_process()` which re-adds it to the run queue. This demand-driven blocking is why UNIX concurrency primitives scale: idle threads burn zero CPU and have no overhead beyond their kernel stack allocation.

subtopics.title: Scheduling algorithms from FCFS to CFS
body: Scheduling algorithms answer 'which runnable task gets the CPU next?' The design space is defined by several competing goals: **throughput** (jobs completed per second), **latency/response time** (time from arrival to first response — critical for interactive workloads), **fairness** (each task gets a proportional share of CPU), and **predictability** (real-time deadlines met). No algorithm is optimal on all dimensions; the right choice depends on the workload.

**First-Come First-Served (FCFS)** is the simplest: run to completion in arrival order. Throughput is fine for CPU-bound jobs, but **convoy effect** destroys interactive latency: one long batch job blocks all short interactive ones. **Shortest Job First (SJF)** is provably optimal for average turnaround time — run the shortest available job next. The problem is you cannot know job length in advance. **Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF)** is the preemptive version: when a new job arrives with a shorter remaining time than the current job, preempt. Optimal, but requires exact future knowledge and causes frequent preemptions.

**Round-Robin (RR)** introduces the **quantum** (time slice, typically 1-100ms): each task gets a fixed quantum, then is preempted and moved to the back of the queue. Response time is proportional to the number of tasks (worst-case = N × quantum), which is acceptable for interactive workloads. Pure RR is fair but ignores priority. **Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** combines priority with adaptive quantum: tasks start at the highest-priority queue (short quantum), and are demoted to lower-priority queues (longer quanta) if they use their entire quantum without blocking. I/O-bound tasks that block before the quantum expires stay high-priority; CPU-bound batch jobs sink to the bottom. A periodic boost resets all tasks to the top queue to prevent starvation. MLFQ was the dominant Unix scheduler design for decades.

Linux's **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)**, introduced in kernel 2.6.23 (2007), replaces MLFQ with a cleaner model. CFS maintains a **virtual runtime** (`vruntime`) for each task: the total CPU time a task has received, weighted by its **niceness** (priority). A task with `nice=-20` (highest priority) has its vruntime increment at half the rate of a `nice=+19` task. The scheduler always runs the task with the **smallest vruntime** — the task that has received the least weighted CPU time relative to its entitlement. This is implemented with a **red-black tree** keyed on `vruntime`, making the 'pick next' operation O(log N) (with the leftmost node cached for O(1) fast path). A newly woken task's vruntime is set to `min_vruntime` (the current minimum in the tree) minus a small lag, preventing it from immediately monopolizing the CPU after sleeping.

**Load balancing** across CPU cores is CFS's most complex component. Each core has its own run queue (reducing lock contention). Periodically (and on task wakeup), the **load balancer** checks if queues are imbalanced and migrates tasks to equalize load. The scheduler is NUMA-aware: migrating a task to a remote NUMA node costs memory bandwidth, so the load balancer applies a **migration threshold** — only migrate if the imbalance exceeds a weight that accounts for NUMA distance. For real-time tasks (`SCHED_FIFO`, `SCHED_RR`), CFS does not apply — they preempt all `SCHED_NORMAL` tasks unconditionally and are scheduled by a separate real-time scheduler that uses a simple FIFO or round-robin within priority levels. This is how audio/video applications request low-latency scheduling: they set `SCHED_FIFO` with a priority.

subtopics.title: Priority inversion, real-time scheduling, and the Mars Pathfinder incident
body: **Priority inversion** occurs when a high-priority task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a lower-priority task, while a medium-priority task that does not need the resource preempts the low-priority task — meaning the low-priority task never releases the resource, and the high-priority task is effectively blocked by the medium-priority one. This violates the fundamental premise of priority-based scheduling and can cause system-level failures.

The canonical real-world example is the **Mars Pathfinder mission (1997)**. The spacecraft intermittently reset itself on Mars. The cause: a high-priority meteorological data collection task was blocked on a mutex held by a low-priority information bus task. A medium-priority communications task (not needing the mutex) preempted the low-priority task. The high-priority task was stuck indefinitely. The watchdog timer, detecting that the high-priority task missed its deadline, triggered a system reset. Engineers diagnosed it remotely (a heroic debugging feat), uploaded a patch enabling **priority inheritance** on the mutex, and resolved the issue — from 140 million miles away.

The fix is **priority inheritance**: when a high-priority task blocks on a mutex, the task currently holding the mutex temporarily inherits the high-priority task's priority, allowing it to preempt medium-priority tasks and release the lock sooner. The kernel must track the inheritance chain (a high-priority task may be waiting on a mutex held by a task waiting on another mutex, etc.) and propagate priorities transitively. Linux pthreads mutexes support this via `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` mutex attribute. **Priority ceiling** is an alternative: the mutex is assigned a ceiling priority equal to the highest priority of any task that may ever lock it, and any task locking the mutex immediately runs at the ceiling. This prevents all priority inversion but requires knowing all potential lockers at design time.

**Real-time scheduling** (POSIX `SCHED_FIFO` and `SCHED_RR`) gives tasks guaranteed preemption semantics: a `SCHED_FIFO` task at priority P preempts all tasks with priority < P and all `SCHED_NORMAL` tasks immediately on wakeup. Within the same priority, `SCHED_FIFO` tasks run until they voluntarily block or yield; `SCHED_RR` tasks share a round-robin quantum. Setting `SCHED_FIFO` requires `CAP_SYS_NICE`. The `PREEMPT_RT` patchset (now largely merged into mainline as of kernel 5.15+) converts most kernel spinlocks into sleepable mutexes and makes the kernel **fully preemptible** — without it, a real-time task can be blocked for milliseconds in a long kernel path that disables preemption. With `PREEMPT_RT`, worst-case kernel latency drops below 100 µs on modern hardware, making Linux suitable for industrial control and audio.

**CPU affinity** (`sched_setaffinity`) pins a task to specific CPUs, preventing the scheduler from migrating it. Used in high-performance computing and trading systems to eliminate cache-cold migrations and NUMA effects: a task pinned to CPU 3 always finds its data warm in L1/L2. Combined with **CPU isolation** (`isolcpus=3` kernel boot parameter), which removes CPU 3 from the scheduler's pool entirely (no other tasks run there unless explicitly affined), this yields deterministic latency. A real-time audio engine or HFT order management system running on an isolated, affined CPU with `SCHED_FIFO` and huge pages can achieve sub-microsecond latency variance.

resources.label: Mars Pathfinder priority inversion — NASA technical report (Mike Jones)

subtopics.title: cgroups v2: CPU, memory, and I/O throttling
body: **Control Groups (cgroups)** are the Linux kernel mechanism for **organizing processes into a hierarchy and applying resource limits to each group**. cgroups v1 (the original design) was chaotic — each resource controller (cpu, memory, blkio, etc.) had its own independent hierarchy, making it hard to reason about a group's total resource usage. **cgroups v2** (default since kernel 5.2, and in modern distros) unifies all controllers under a single tree: each process belongs to exactly one cgroup, and every cgroup inherits from exactly one parent, forming a tree rooted at `/sys/fs/cgroup`.

The **CPU controller** throttles CPU time via two mechanisms. `cpu.weight` (1-10000, default 100) sets the relative CPU share for CFS scheduling — a cgroup with weight 200 gets twice the CPU of one with weight 100 when both are busy. `cpu.max` sets a hard bandwidth limit: `echo '50000 100000' > cpu.max` allows the cgroup 50ms of CPU per 100ms period (50% of one CPU), regardless of how many idle CPUs exist. This quota-based throttling is implemented via CFS bandwidth control in the kernel. Setting `cpu.max` is how Kubernetes `limits.cpu` works: a pod with `cpu: "0.5"` gets `cpu.max = 50000 100000`. Consistently hitting the limit triggers **CPU throttling** (the pod's processes sleep for the remainder of the period), which can cause latency spikes even when the host has idle CPUs — a common Kubernetes performance misunderstanding.

The **memory controller** tracks and limits physical memory usage. `memory.max` sets a hard limit on RSS; exceeding it invokes the **OOM killer** (Out-Of-Memory killer) within the cgroup, which selects and kills a process to free memory (chosen by `oom_score_adj` and a heuristic scoring how much memory it uses). `memory.high` sets a softer limit: when exceeded, the kernel applies memory pressure (slowing allocations, triggering reclaim) without killing. `memory.swap.max` controls swap usage. Containers that consume unexpected memory (large JVM heap, memory leak) trigger OOM kills — the container exits with status 137 (128 + SIGKILL). Production incident diagnosis: check `dmesg | grep -i oom` or `/sys/fs/cgroup/<path>/memory.events` for `oom_kill` counts.

The **I/O controller** (block I/O throttling) via `io.max` limits bandwidth in bytes/sec and IOPS per device: `echo '8:0 rbps=10485760 wbps=10485760' > io.max` limits a cgroup to 10 MB/s reads and writes on device `8:0`. This uses the kernel's **BFQ (Budget Fair Queueing)** or **CFQ** I/O scheduler. The `io.weight` file sets relative bandwidth shares. The **pressure stall information** files (`cpu.pressure`, `memory.pressure`, `io.pressure`) expose PSI metrics: the fraction of time that processes in the cgroup were stalled waiting for that resource. PSI is the modern way to detect resource contention before it causes OOM kills or latency spikes — monitoring `some` and `full` pressure values allows proactive remediation.

The practical exercise: create a cgroup manually. `mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/mytest`, `echo $$ > /sys/fs/cgroup/mytest/cgroup.procs` (joins the current shell), `echo '200000 1000000' > /sys/fs/cgroup/mytest/cpu.max` (20% CPU), then run a CPU-intensive loop in that shell and observe via `top` that it is throttled at 20%. Then inspect `cpu.stat` to see `throttled_time`. This hands-on confirms that cgroup throttling is real and instantaneous. Docker's `--cpus=0.5` flag does exactly this sequence programmatically.

subtopics.title: Linux namespaces and the anatomy of a container
body: **Namespaces** are the kernel mechanism that provides the **illusion of isolation** without real virtualization. A namespace wraps a global resource (a process ID space, a network stack, a filesystem mount table) and makes it appear that a process has its own private copy of that resource. Processes in different namespaces have different views of the system but share the same kernel. There are eight namespace types in Linux: `pid`, `net`, `mnt`, `uts`, `ipc`, `user`, `cgroup`, and `time` (added in 5.6). `clone()` with namespace flags creates new namespaces; `unshare()` moves the calling process into a new namespace without creating a child; `setns()` joins an existing namespace (used by `docker exec` to enter a running container's namespaces).

The **PID namespace** gives a process tree its own PID numbering starting at 1. The container's init process (PID 1 inside the namespace, but some large number outside) must reap orphaned processes — if it does not implement `waitpid`, orphan processes accumulate as zombies. Docker uses `tini` as PID 1 precisely because bare shells and application servers are not designed to be init processes. Nesting PID namespaces is possible (a process sees its inner PID; parent namespaces see the outer PID), enabling nested containers. The kernel maintains a mapping table for translation.

The **network namespace** gives a process group its own network stack: private loopback, private network interfaces, private routing tables, private iptables rules, and private sockets. Two processes in different net namespaces cannot directly communicate even on the same host. Container networking (Docker bridges, Kubernetes pod networking) works by creating a **veth pair** (virtual ethernet: a kernel-internal pipe with two endpoints) with one end in the host namespace and one in the container namespace. The container's end becomes `eth0`; the host's end attaches to a bridge. The kernel routes packets between them at software speed. Kubernetes CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium, Flannel) automate this wiring at cluster scale.

The **mount namespace** gives a process tree its own filesystem mount table, independent of the host's. A new mount namespace starts as a copy of the parent's (if created with `clone(CLONE_NEWNS)`). The container runtime then performs a sequence of `mount()` calls to bind-mount the container's root filesystem image, mount `/proc` and `/sys` fresh, and use `pivot_root()` or `chroot()` to make the container image the new root. The result: the container's processes see only the image's filesystem. Docker images are built from **overlay filesystems** (OverlayFS): a read-only lower layer (the image) and a read-write upper layer (per-container state). `docker commit` snapshots the upper layer. This is the entire image/layer mechanism — no magic, just `mount -t overlay`.

A **container** therefore is nothing but a Linux process (or process group) that was created with namespace isolation and cgroup resource limits. There is no 'container object' in the kernel. `docker run --rm -it ubuntu bash` resolves to: pull image layers and set up OverlayFS; call `clone()` with all relevant namespace flags; inside the child, mount `/proc`, `/sys`, `devtmpfs`, and bind-mount any volumes; pivot_root into the image filesystem; set cgroup limits; exec `/bin/bash`. The isolation is real (processes in the container cannot see the host's processes or network by default) but the kernel is shared — a kernel exploit works against the host. This fundamental distinction (namespaces isolate the kernel's user-visible APIs, not the kernel itself) is why containers do not replace VMs for strong multi-tenant isolation.

resources.label: namespaces(7) — man page overview of all namespace types
Containers from scratch — Liz Rice (video + Go code walkthrough)

connections.note: CFS literally is a red-black tree of runnable tasks
cgroups/namespaces are why Docker works the way it does in production

topics.title: Virtual memory & allocators
body: **Page tables**, the **TLB**, page faults (minor vs major), x86-64 4-level paging (PML4→PT), **mmap** (how both `malloc` and file I/O use it), **copy-on-write** after fork, and page-replacement (LRU/clock/working-set).
Then allocator internals: `sbrk` vs `mmap`, ptmalloc2/jemalloc/tcmalloc, and **NUMA**. Writing your own `malloc` is the single best way to make all of this concrete.

subtopics.title: Page tables and the TLB: x86-64 four-level paging in depth
body: Virtual memory rests on a single hardware mechanism: the **page table**, a data structure that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses one page at a time (a page is typically 4 KB). The CPU's **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** performs this translation on every memory access, consulting the page table automatically in hardware. The kernel populates the page table entries; the CPU reads them. This division — kernel writes, CPU reads — is the contract underlying virtual memory.

On x86-64, a 64-bit virtual address is divided into five fields: bits [47:39] index the **PML4** (Page Map Level 4, also called CR3 table), bits [38:30] index the **PDPT** (Page Directory Pointer Table), bits [29:21] index the **PD** (Page Directory), bits [20:12] index the **PT** (Page Table), and bits [11:0] are the **page offset** added to the final physical frame address. Each table has 512 entries of 8 bytes each, fitting in one 4 KB page. The CR3 register holds the physical address of the PML4 — changing CR3 (which happens on context switches and explicit writes) switches the entire page table, changing the process's virtual address space. This is why CR3 writes are one of the most expensive operations in the kernel: they flush the TLB (on CPUs that don't support PCID).

Each page table entry (PTE) contains a physical frame number, a **present** bit (is the mapping valid?), **read/write**, **user/supervisor** (ring 3 vs ring 0 access), **NX** (no-execute, prevents code execution from data pages — essential for `W^X` security policy), **accessed** (set by MMU on any access, used by the page reclaimer), **dirty** (set on writes, used by file-mapped pages to know if writeback is needed), and various caching control bits. A PTE with present=0 is 'not mapped': accessing it causes a **page fault** (hardware exception #PF), which the kernel handles via `do_page_fault`/`handle_mm_fault`.

The **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** is a hardware cache of recent virtual-to-physical translations, holding a few hundred to a few thousand entries per core. On a TLB hit, the MMU returns the physical address in ~1 cycle without walking the 4-level table (which would require 4 sequential memory accesses). On a TLB miss, the hardware page-table walker traverses all four levels (or the kernel handles it in software on architectures without hardware walkers), installs the result in the TLB, and proceeds. **TLB misses** are a dominant source of memory access latency in applications with large, pointer-chasing data structures (linked lists, trees, hash tables). Huge pages (2 MB or 1 GB) reduce TLB pressure by covering far more address space per entry.

The practical consequence for code: a tight loop over a 4 KB array fits entirely in the TLB and L1 cache — extremely fast. A random walk through a 100 GB dataset will miss the TLB on almost every access, requiring a 4-level page-table walk (4 additional cache misses if the page table is cold) before the data access itself. This is why pointer-chasing data structures are slow on modern hardware regardless of algorithmic O() — the constant factor includes 4 TLB misses per random access. Column-oriented databases, arena allocators, and B-trees with fat nodes exist partly to improve TLB behavior.

resources.label: Intel 64 and IA-32 Architectures Software Developer Manual — Vol. 3A, Ch. 4 (Paging)

subtopics.title: Page faults, demand paging, and copy-on-write
body: A **page fault** is a hardware exception raised by the MMU when a virtual address access cannot be completed using the current page tables. The CPU saves state, calls the kernel's fault handler (`do_page_fault` on x86), which interprets the fault and either: (1) fills in a page table entry and returns (the instruction retries and succeeds), or (2) determines the access is invalid and delivers SIGSEGV to the process. The distinction between 'minor' and 'major' faults is performance-critical: a **minor fault** requires only allocating a physical frame and zeroing/initializing it (no disk I/O); a **major fault** requires reading a page from disk (swap or file), which takes ~1-10 ms — five orders of magnitude slower than RAM.

**Demand paging** is the principle that pages are not loaded into physical memory until first accessed. When you `exec()` a large binary, the kernel sets up page table entries for all its segments but leaves them all 'not present'. As the program executes, each new code page, data page, and stack page triggers a minor fault, filling in the PTE. Pages that are never accessed (unused library code paths, unreachable branches) never consume physical memory. This is how a system can run programs whose total virtual footprint exceeds physical RAM: only the **working set** (recently accessed pages) needs to be resident.

**Copy-on-write (CoW)** after fork is the mechanism that makes `fork()` fast despite copying a potentially gigabyte-sized address space. After fork, instead of physically copying all parent pages, the kernel marks all PTEs in both parent and child as read-only and points them at the same physical frames. When either process tries to write to a page, the MMU raises a write-protection fault. The kernel handles it by allocating a new physical frame, copying the contents, updating the faulting process's PTE to point to the new frame (writable), and resuming the instruction. Pages that neither process writes are never duplicated. `fork()` + `exec()` is extremely common (shell pipelines), and exec() immediately replaces the address space — the only pages ever CoW-faulted are the ones the child touches between fork and exec, typically none. This is why `fork()` is O(1) despite copying gigabytes conceptually.

The kernel decides which pages to evict from physical memory via **page replacement algorithms** when physical memory is full. Linux uses a **two-list LRU** approximation: an 'active' list of recently-accessed pages and an 'inactive' list. Pages migrate from active to inactive when not accessed for a while; pages on the inactive list are candidates for eviction. The **accessed bit** in PTEs (set by hardware on any access) drives this: the kernel periodically scans PTEs and moves pages with the accessed bit set back to active, clearing the bit. The **dirty bit** determines eviction cost: clean file-backed pages can be discarded (re-read from disk on demand), dirty pages must be **written back** to disk first. Anonymous pages (heap, stack — not backed by a file) must be written to **swap space** to be evicted.

**Memory-mapped files** (`mmap(fd, ...)`) use the same demand-paging and page-fault machinery: the kernel sets up PTEs for the file's pages; on first access, a fault reads the page from the filesystem into a **page cache** frame and installs the PTE. Subsequent accesses to the same file page (from any process mapping it) hit the same physical frame — files are automatically shared. `msync()` writes dirty file pages back to disk. `madvise(MADV_WILLNEED)` hints to the kernel to prefetch pages before they are faulted; `madvise(MADV_SEQUENTIAL)` enables aggressive read-ahead; `madvise(MADV_DONTNEED)` discards pages immediately (zeroing on next access). These hints are the primary tool for avoiding major page faults in memory-mapped database engines like LMDB.

resources.label: OSTEP — Beyond Physical Memory: Mechanisms (Ch. 21) and Policies (Ch. 22)

subtopics.title: mmap: the unified I/O and allocation interface
body: `mmap(addr, length, prot, flags, fd, offset)` is one of the most versatile syscalls in the UNIX toolkit. With `fd=-1` and `MAP_ANONYMOUS | MAP_PRIVATE`, it allocates a new zero-filled private region — this is how `malloc` obtains large chunks of memory from the kernel. With a valid `fd`, it maps file contents into the address space — this is how executable loaders map code segments, how databases read large files, and how shared libraries are loaded. With `MAP_SHARED`, writes to the mapping are visible to all processes mapping the same file (or via a shared anonymous mapping to related processes) — this is POSIX shared memory and the substrate for producer-consumer queues between processes.

The `prot` flags control permissions per-mapping: `PROT_READ`, `PROT_WRITE`, `PROT_EXEC`, and their combinations. The kernel enforces these via PTE permission bits; violating them raises SIGSEGV. The W^X (write XOR execute) security policy is enforced by ensuring no mapping is simultaneously `PROT_WRITE | PROT_EXEC`. JITs (JavaScript engines, Java HotSpot, LLVM MCJIT) must use a dance: `mmap(PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE)` to write machine code, then `mprotect(PROT_READ|PROT_EXEC)` to make it executable, then execute it. On systems with strict W^X (OpenBSD), even this sequence requires special permissions — motivating interpreter-only mode for untrusted code.

**`munmap`** releases a mapping, removing the PTEs. The kernel can immediately reclaim the physical pages if they are clean, or lazily defer reclaim. `mprotect` changes permissions on an existing mapping. `mremap` moves or resizes a mapping (used by `realloc` internally to avoid copying). `mlock/mlockall` prevent pages from being swapped out — essential for real-time applications and encryption code that must not allow key material to be paged to disk. `madvise(MADV_HUGEPAGE)` requests transparent huge pages for a mapping, reducing TLB pressure at the cost of 2 MB granularity internal fragmentation.

The **page cache** is the kernel's global pool of file-backed pages, shared across all processes. When process A `mmap`s a file and process B does the same, they share the same physical pages from the page cache — no data duplication. `read()` on a file also goes through the page cache (the kernel reads into cache, then copies to user buffer with `copy_to_user`). This means `mmap` and `read` on the same file contend for the same cache entries. The page cache grows to consume all available free RAM; the kernel reclaims it under memory pressure (since file-backed pages are clean and easily discarded). `/proc/meminfo` shows `Cached` (file-backed page cache) vs `Buffers` (block device metadata cache). `sync && echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches` flushes caches — useful for benchmarking to get cold-cache timing.

**`sendfile(out_fd, in_fd, offset, count)`** is the zero-copy I/O optimization: transfers bytes from a file fd directly to a socket fd without copying through user space. Normally, `read()` copies file data from page cache to user buffer, then `write()` copies from user buffer to socket buffer — two kernel-user boundary crossings plus two copies. `sendfile` transfers directly from the page cache to the socket buffer in the kernel — one copy total (or zero if the NIC supports DMA scatter-gather). This is how `nginx` serves static files at wire speed. `splice()` generalizes this to arbitrary fd pairs (file→pipe, pipe→socket). Understanding that I/O is fundamentally about managing copies between page cache, kernel buffers, and user buffers clarifies why `sendfile`, `io_uring`, and kernel bypass (DPDK/RDMA) exist: each one reduces copies.

subtopics.title: Heap allocator design: free lists, bins, slab, and tcmalloc
body: The **heap allocator** (`malloc`/`free`) mediates between programs that want variably-sized memory and the OS that manages fixed-size pages. The key challenge is **fragmentation**: after many alloc/free cycles, the heap contains many small free regions interspersed with live allocations, so even if total free bytes exceed the requested allocation size, no single contiguous free region satisfies it. The goal of allocator design is to minimize fragmentation while maintaining O(1) or near-O(1) alloc/free with low per-object overhead and good cache behavior.

The simplest allocator — useful to implement once to understand the constraints — uses a **singly-linked free list** embedded in free blocks. Each free block stores its size and a next pointer; `malloc(n)` walks the list for a block of size ≥ n (first-fit or best-fit policy), splits it if much larger, and returns a pointer past the header. `free(p)` inserts the block at the head or in address order (for coalescing). **Coalescing** adjacent free blocks is essential to prevent fragmentation: after freeing a block, check if the block before or after in memory is also free and merge them. The **boundary tag** trick (Knuth 1973) stores the block size at both the header and footer, allowing O(1) neighbor lookup for coalescing.

**ptmalloc2** (glibc's allocator, based on Doug Lea's dlmalloc with threading extensions) uses **size-segregated free lists called bins**: exact-size bins for small requests (8-1024 bytes in multiples of 8, called 'fastbins' and 'smallbins'), and a sorted bin ('largebin') for larger requests. Fastbins are LIFO and not coalesced immediately (for speed); smallbins are doubly-linked and FIFO; unsorted bin is a staging area. **tcache** (per-thread cache, added in glibc 2.26) gives each thread its own array of per-size singly-linked lists (up to 7 entries per size class by default), making small allocations entirely lock-free. This is why modern heap exploitation techniques target tcache: it is the first lookup path with minimal integrity checks.

**jemalloc** (Facebook, used in Rust's allocator and Firefox) and **tcmalloc** (Google, used in Chrome and Go) address ptmalloc2's thread-contention and fragmentation weaknesses. jemalloc divides memory into **arenas** (typically one per CPU), each managing its own set of size classes in **slabs** (4 MB extents subdivided into identically-sized objects). A thread always allocates from its current arena, avoiding locks. Thread-local caches (**tcaches** in jemalloc terminology) reduce arena contention further. jemalloc's careful size class design (no size class is more than ~12% above the previous, minimizing internal fragmentation) and metadata layout (outside slabs, reducing heap metadata corruption attack surface) make it the preferred allocator in security-conscious settings.

**Writing your own malloc** is the most educational systems programming exercise. Start with: a global free list, `sbrk()` to extend the heap by 4 KB chunks when the list is empty, first-fit search, split on over-large blocks, and immediate coalescing with boundary tags. This 150-line implementation will have correct semantics and terrible performance. The second version adds size bins. The third adds a thread-local cache. By the third version you understand ptmalloc2, jemalloc, and tcmalloc from first principles — and you understand why heap exploits (use-after-free, heap overflow corrupting metadata, tcache poisoning) work the way they do.

resources.label: Memory allocators 101 — write a simple malloc (Dan Luu)

subtopics.title: NUMA, huge pages, and memory performance
body: **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)** is the physical memory architecture of modern multi-socket servers: each processor socket has local DRAM directly attached, with lower latency (typically 80-100ns), while remote DRAM (attached to another socket) is accessed over a QPI/UPI inter-processor interconnect with higher latency (typically 150-300ns, 2-3× slower). A 2-socket server with 256 GB RAM may have 128 GB 'close' and 128 GB 'far' from each CPU's perspective. A program that runs on socket 0 but has its data allocated on socket 1's memory pays the NUMA tax on every access. This is invisible unless you specifically instrument it.

`numactl --hardware` (install `numactl`) shows the NUMA topology: nodes, their CPUs, their memory, and the distance table (a matrix of relative access latencies). `numastat -p <pid>` shows per-NUMA-node memory allocation for a process. `numactl --membind=0 --cpunodebind=0 ./prog` runs a program with memory and CPUs bound to NUMA node 0 — eliminating remote accesses. The Linux kernel's NUMA-aware allocator (`CONFIG_NUMA`) allocates memory on the node *local to the allocating thread* by default, so a process that starts all threads and then allocates memory will get locally-bound allocations — but if threads later migrate CPUs via the scheduler, the allocations become remote. This is why high-performance applications combine NUMA binding with CPU affinity.

**Huge pages** address TLB pressure. A standard 4 KB page requires one TLB entry per 4 KB of address space; a 2 MB huge page covers 512× more with one entry. For a database buffer pool of 128 GB, standard pages require 33 million TLB entries; with 2 MB huge pages, only 65,000. Since the TLB holds ~1,000-4,096 entries, the difference between 33M and 65K entries is the difference between near-constant TLB misses and near-constant TLB hits. Linux supports huge pages via two mechanisms: **HugeTLBfs** (explicit pre-allocation of huge pages in a reserved pool, accessed via `MAP_HUGETLB` or `/dev/hugepages`) and **Transparent Huge Pages (THP)** (automatic promotion of anonymous mappings to huge pages by the kernel's `khugepaged` daemon). THP is convenient but can cause latency spikes when the daemon promotes or splits huge pages; latency-sensitive applications (Redis, Cassandra) often disable THP globally and use HugeTLBfs explicitly.

**Memory bandwidth** is the other key resource beyond latency. Each NUMA node's memory controller has a finite bandwidth (typically 50-100 GB/s per socket for DDR5). Applications that require random access across TBs of data are latency-limited (many sequential accesses, each with high latency). Applications streaming through data sequentially are bandwidth-limited (hardware prefetcher hides latency, but bandwidth caps throughput). Knowing which regime you are in determines whether optimization should focus on reducing working set (to fit in cache), improving access locality (better TLB utilization), or parallelizing across NUMA nodes.

The `perf stat -e LLC-load-misses,LLC-store-misses,node-load-misses,node-store-misses` command shows LLC (last-level cache, 30-60 MB on modern server CPUs) miss rates and NUMA remote access rates for a program. If `node-load-misses` is high, NUMA binding is the fix. If `LLC-load-misses` is high but NUMA misses are low, the working set exceeds the LLC and you need a data layout change (better spatial locality, prefetching, or SIMD to process more per cache line). `perf mem record` and `perf mem report` analyze memory accesses by latency, identifying the specific code lines and data structures causing the most expensive accesses — this is the industrial method for memory performance tuning.
