topics.title: Concurrency: futexes, deadlock, lock-free
body: Race conditions and the happens-before model, **mutex internals** (the `futex` fast-path/slow-path split), condition variables (why you always loop on the predicate), deadlock (the four CHMR conditions + Banker's), and reader-writer locks.

Then lock-free programming with atomics: compare-and-swap, the **ABA problem**, and memory ordering. This is the hardest correctness topic in systems and it pays off across kernels, databases, and trading engines.

subtopics.title: Data races, happens-before, and the memory model
body: A **data race** occurs when two threads concurrently access the same memory location without synchronization and at least one access is a write. In C11/C++11 and POSIX, a data race is **undefined behavior** — not just incorrect output, but anything: the compiler may reorder or eliminate instructions, the CPU may reorder memory accesses, and the result can be arbitrary. This is stronger than 'unpredictable order'; a data race can cause memory corruption in entirely unrelated variables if the compiler used the assumption of no races to optimize code.

The formal framework for reasoning about concurrency is the **happens-before relation** (Lamport 1978). Operation A *happens-before* B if: A and B are in the same thread and A appears before B in program order; or A is a synchronization action (lock release, atomic store) that **synchronizes-with** a corresponding action B (lock acquire, atomic load) in another thread; or A happens-before some C that happens-before B (transitivity). If neither A happens-before B nor B happens-before A, they are **concurrent**. Concurrent accesses where at least one is a write constitute a data race. The memory model guarantees that if the happens-before relation connects all accesses to a shared variable, the program has a well-defined (if potentially surprising) behavior.

The **Java Memory Model (JMM)**, **C++11 memory model**, and **Linux kernel memory model (LKMM)** all formalize happens-before but differ in their primitives. C++11 provides `std::atomic<T>` with explicit memory ordering: `memory_order_seq_cst` (total order, most expensive), `memory_order_acquire`/`release` (the common case — establishes happens-before), `memory_order_relaxed` (no ordering guarantee, just atomicity of the individual read or write). The LKMM additionally has `smp_mb()` (full memory barrier), `smp_rmb()` (read barrier), `smp_wmb()` (write barrier), and `READ_ONCE`/`WRITE_ONCE` which suppress compiler reordering without hardware barriers.

Compilers and CPUs both perform **reordering** within the constraints of the memory model. A compiler may reorder independent stores for register allocation. A CPU with a store buffer (x86's TSO model) may reorder stores past subsequent loads. ARM and POWER CPUs (weakly-ordered) may reorder almost any two accesses. The hardware guarantee for x86 (TSO: Total Store Order) is that stores are seen in program order and loads are seen in program order, but a store by CPU A may not be visible to CPU B until A's store buffer drains. This is why x86 needs `mfence`/`lock; xchg` for full fences but not for acquire/release (TSO provides acquire/release 'for free' with no fence instruction, just a compiler barrier). ARM needs explicit `stlr`/`ldar` instructions for release/acquire. Writing a lock with `relaxed` atomics requires explicit fence analysis that is extremely error-prone.

A concrete exercise: write a 'flag communication' pattern — thread A sets `data = 42; flag = 1`; thread B spins on `flag` then reads `data`. On x86 with a compiler barrier (`std::atomic_signal_fence`) this works. On ARM without `std::atomic<int> flag` with acquire/release semantics, it does not — the compiler or CPU may reorder `data = 42` after `flag = 1`. The fix: make `flag` an `std::atomic<int>` and use `flag.store(1, std::memory_order_release)` / `flag.load(std::memory_order_acquire)`. This establishes a synchronizes-with relationship and therefore a happens-before edge from A's data write to B's data read. The happens-before chain is: `data = 42` → (program order) → `flag.store(release)` → (synchronizes-with) → `flag.load(acquire)` → (program order) → `data read`.

resources.label: Preshing — Memory Ordering at Compile Time

subtopics.title: Mutex internals: the futex fast-path/slow-path split
body: A **mutex** (mutual exclusion lock) implements a critical section: at most one thread may be inside the section at a time. Naively, a mutex could be a kernel object with a syscall for every lock and unlock. But syscalls cost ~100-200ns each, making per-operation locking prohibitively expensive for fine-grained critical sections. The solution is the **futex (Fast Userspace muTEX)**: most lock operations complete entirely in user space (fast path) with a single atomic instruction; the kernel is involved only in the slow path when contention requires blocking.

The futex approach stores the lock state in a **user-space integer** (e.g., 0 = unlocked, 1 = locked without waiters, 2 = locked with waiters). **Locking**: atomically CAS (compare-and-swap) the state from 0 to 1. If this succeeds, the lock is acquired — no syscall, ~10ns. If the CAS fails (another thread holds the lock), atomically set state to 2 (indicating a waiter) and call `futex(addr, FUTEX_WAIT, 2)` to block in the kernel. The kernel checks that `*addr == 2` (atomically — preventing a race where the holder released between our CAS and the syscall), and if so, blocks the thread on a kernel wait queue keyed by the futex address. **Unlocking**: atomically decrement state. If state was 1 (no waiters), simply decrement to 0 — fast path, no syscall. If state was 2 (waiters), decrement to 0 and call `futex(addr, FUTEX_WAKE, 1)` to wake one waiter. The pattern achieves no-syscall operation in the uncontended case.

The **Ulrich Drepper mutex** (glibc's pthread_mutex implementation) adds more states to avoid the spurious `FUTEX_WAKE` in cases where all waiters were already unblocked. The Linux kernel's `futex_wait` path uses a **hash table of wait queues** keyed by the futex's physical address (not virtual address — handling the case where the same physical page is mapped at different virtual addresses in different processes, enabling cross-process futexes). `FUTEX_WAIT` atomically checks the value and enqueues; `FUTEX_WAKE` dequeues and wakes. The `FUTEX_REQUEUE` operation atomically wakes some waiters and moves others from one futex queue to another, used to implement condition variable `broadcast` efficiently.

**Spinlocks** are the alternative to futex-based blocking: repeatedly CAS the lock word without sleeping. On the fast path with low contention, a spinlock is faster than a mutex (no sleep/wake overhead). But a spinning thread consumes a full CPU core — on a single-core system, spinning is self-defeating (the holder cannot run to release). In the kernel, spinlocks are used in code where sleeping is impossible (interrupt handlers, non-preemptible sections): `spin_lock()` / `spin_unlock()`. In user space, spinlocks are only appropriate when: the critical section is very short (fewer than ~100ns), the system has spare CPU cores, and the lock is rarely contended for more than ~1µs. Hybrid **adaptive mutex** implementations (as in jemalloc and Linux pthread_mutexattr with `PTHREAD_MUTEX_ADAPTIVE_NP`) spin for a short period before blocking, covering the common case of brief contention without the overhead of an immediate futex syscall.

**Reader-writer locks** (`pthread_rwlock_t`) allow multiple concurrent readers but exclusive writers. The implementation maintains three state fields: reader count, writer waiting flag, writer holding flag. `pthread_rwlock_rdlock` acquires if no writer holds or waits (cheap CAS on reader count); `pthread_rwlock_wrlock` acquires if reader count is zero and no other writer holds (expensive: must wait for all readers to drain). Reader-writer locks offer a win only when reads significantly outnumber writes and read sections are long enough to amortize the lock overhead. For short critical sections, a plain mutex often wins — the rwlock's larger state and more complex protocol has higher overhead per acquisition. **Seqlocks** (sequence locks) in the kernel offer an alternative: writers increment an odd sequence number, perform the write, increment again (even). Readers read the sequence number before and after — if it changed or was odd, retry. Zero overhead for readers when no writer is active; readers never block writers.

subtopics.title: Condition variables, semaphores, and barrier synchronization
body: A **condition variable (CV)** is a synchronization primitive that allows a thread to atomically release a mutex and sleep until notified. The canonical use: producer-consumer with a shared queue. The consumer holds the mutex, checks `while (queue.empty()) { pthread_cond_wait(&cv, &mutex); }`, and `pthread_cond_wait` atomically releases the mutex and suspends the thread. The producer calls `pthread_mutex_lock`, adds an item, `pthread_cond_signal(&cv)`, and `pthread_mutex_unlock`. The notified consumer reacquires the mutex and retries the predicate check. The **while loop** (not `if`) is mandatory: **spurious wakeups** (POSIX-permitted, and observed on some Linux futex paths) can wake the thread without a signal. Additionally, between the signal and the consumer reacquiring the mutex, another thread may have consumed the item — the consumer must recheck the predicate regardless.

The implementation of `pthread_cond_wait`: atomically enqueue the thread on the CV's wait queue and release the mutex — this atomicity prevents a lost-wakeup race where the producer signals before the consumer has finished enqueuing. `pthread_cond_signal` wakes one waiter; `pthread_cond_broadcast` wakes all. Both use `FUTEX_WAKE` / `FUTEX_REQUEUE` under the hood. A subtle correctness issue: `pthread_cond_broadcast` can cause a **thundering herd** — all N waiters wake up, N-1 of them immediately reblock on the mutex. When only one can proceed (e.g., a work queue with one item), this is O(N) wakeups for O(1) useful work. The fix: use `pthread_cond_signal` when only one consumer can proceed, `broadcast` only when the predicate could be true for all waiters (e.g., the condition is 'application is shutting down').

**Semaphores** (`sem_t`) generalize mutexes: a semaphore has a non-negative integer count. `sem_post` increments (and wakes a waiter if count was 0); `sem_wait` decrements (blocking if count is 0). A semaphore with max count 1 is a binary semaphore (functionally similar to a mutex, but lacking ownership semantics — any thread can post). Semaphores with counts > 1 are useful for rate limiting (a pool of N resources: acquire decrements, release increments) or for producer-consumer signaling without a condition variable. POSIX provides both named semaphores (`sem_open`, inter-process) and unnamed (`sem_init`, in shared memory). Semaphore count manipulation is implemented via futex operations with no kernel syscall in the uncontended case.

**Barriers** synchronize a group of N threads to a rendezvous point: no thread proceeds past `pthread_barrier_wait` until all N threads have called it. The implementation typically uses a counter (protected by a mutex or atomic) decremented by each arriving thread; the last thread to arrive resets the counter and broadcasts on a condition variable. After the broadcast, all threads atomically change to a new generation to avoid the next barrier's threads interfering with this barrier's wakeups. Barriers are used in parallel scientific computing (each iteration of a simulation must complete before the next starts), and in test code to synchronize threads to a known starting point.

The higher-level abstraction above these primitives is the **monitor** pattern (used in Java `synchronized`, Kotlin coroutine `Mutex`, etc.): an object bundles both the mutex and one or more condition variables, and all methods on the object automatically acquire/release the lock. Java's `Object.wait()`/`notify()`/`notifyAll()` implement exactly CV semantics within the JVM's monitor. The pattern is sound but can lead to over-synchronization (a single lock for all state in an object) — modern Java prefers `java.util.concurrent` classes (ConcurrentHashMap, BlockingQueue) which use fine-grained locking or lock-free algorithms internally.

subtopics.title: Deadlock: conditions, prevention, detection, and Banker's algorithm
body: A **deadlock** is a cycle of two or more threads, each holding a resource and waiting for a resource held by the next thread in the cycle — all are blocked indefinitely. The **four necessary and sufficient conditions** (Coffman 1971) for deadlock are: (1) **Mutual exclusion** — resources are held exclusively; (2) **Hold and wait** — a thread holds some resources while waiting for others; (3) **No preemption** — resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread; (4) **Circular wait** — a cycle of threads exists, each waiting for the next. All four must hold simultaneously. Eliminating any one prevents deadlock.

**Deadlock prevention** breaks one condition by design. Eliminating hold-and-wait: require each thread to acquire all resources atomically at the start (or release all held resources before requesting new ones). This is impractical for large systems (reduces concurrency, requires knowing all future requests). Eliminating circular wait: impose a **global ordering on all locks** and require threads to acquire locks in that order. This is the most practical technique: if mutex A always comes before mutex B in the global order, and every thread acquires A before B, no circular wait is possible. Lock ordering is the standard defensive programming practice for multi-mutex code. The challenge is maintaining the order as codebases evolve — `lockdep` (Linux kernel's lock ordering validator) tracks which lock is acquired while holding which other lock at runtime and reports when a new acquisition would form a potential cycle.

**Deadlock detection** allows deadlocks to occur but detects and recovers. The kernel builds a **resource allocation graph** (RAG): nodes for threads and resources, edges from thread to resource (waiting) and resource to thread (held). A cycle in the RAG indicates deadlock. Detection can run periodically or on timeout. Recovery means killing one thread to break the cycle. Databases implement this for transaction deadlocks: when two transactions hold row-level locks and each waits for the other's lock, the DB detects the cycle (typically within 50ms via a timeout or an active cycle-detection algorithm) and aborts the 'youngest' or 'cheapest-to-restart' transaction. This is why DB clients must be prepared to retry on `deadlock detected` errors.

The **Banker's algorithm** (Dijkstra 1965) is **deadlock avoidance**: when a thread requests resources, the algorithm checks whether granting the request would leave the system in a **safe state** — one where there exists at least one sequence of thread completions that allows all threads to finish. It works by simulating: assume the request is granted, check if the remaining resource need of every thread can be satisfied sequentially (the 'safety algorithm'). If safe, grant. If unsafe, make the thread wait. The algorithm requires knowing each thread's **maximum resource demand** in advance — feasible in some batch systems, impractical for general-purpose use. Its main value is conceptual: it precisely defines what 'safe state' means and shows that deadlock-freedom is not the same as absence of blocking.

In practice, most production systems choose **deadlock avoidance by discipline** (global lock ordering) plus **timeout-based detection** (if a lock has been held for >N seconds, log a warning or take a crash dump). The Go runtime detects all-goroutine deadlocks at scheduler quiescence (if all goroutines are blocked and no I/O is pending, `panic: all goroutines are asleep - deadlock!`). Java's `ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads()` provides programmatic detection. The most useful debugging tool is a thread dump (Java `kill -3`, Go `kill -SIGQUIT`, Linux `gdb -p <pid>` + `thread apply all bt`) which shows each thread's current stack and what it is waiting on — a deadlock appears as a cycle in the waiting-on graph.

subtopics.title: Lock-free programming: CAS, ABA problem, and hazard pointers
body: **Lock-free algorithms** use atomic CPU instructions instead of mutexes, guaranteeing that at least one thread makes progress even if others are delayed (though a single thread may be delayed indefinitely — that is **wait-free** guarantee). The primitive is **compare-and-swap (CAS)**: atomically reads a memory location, compares to an expected value, and if equal, writes a new value, returning success/failure. On x86: `LOCK CMPXCHG`. In C++11: `std::atomic<T>::compare_exchange_weak/strong`. The pattern for lock-free update: (1) read current value, (2) compute new value, (3) CAS(location, old, new) — if CAS fails, another thread modified it first; retry from step 1. This is the **optimistic concurrency** pattern: assume no conflict, detect and retry on conflict.

A **lock-free stack** (Treiber stack, 1986) demonstrates the pattern. Push: read `head`, set `new_node->next = head`, CAS(&head, head, new_node). Pop: read `head`, if null return empty, read `next = head->next`, CAS(&head, head, next), return head. The CAS prevents lost updates when two threads push simultaneously: if both read the same `head`, only one's CAS succeeds; the other retries. Correctness depends on the loop correctly retrying — if any operation is not idempotent (doing it twice causes harm), lock-free retry loops are dangerous. Push is safe (setting next is idempotent given a consistent head). Pop requires care: between reading `next = head->next` and the CAS, the node at `head` must not be freed and reused.

The **ABA problem** is the central hazard of CAS-based lock-free algorithms. In the Treiber stack: thread T1 reads `head = A`. Thread T2 pops A, pops B, pushes A back (reuses the node). T1's CAS `(head, A, next_of_A_which_is_B)` succeeds — the value is A again — but the stack's structural invariant is now violated because next_of_A points to B which was popped. The ABA problem occurs whenever a value can be written, deleted, and then written again with the same bits but different semantic state. Solutions: (1) **Tagged pointers** — store a version counter in the low bits of the pointer (Linux `packed_pointer`; common in 32-bit era, harder in 64-bit but the top 16 bits are often unused); (2) **Hazard pointers** (Michael 2002) — each thread declares a 'hazard pointer' to a node before reading through it; the reclaimer checks all hazard pointers before freeing a node; (3) **Epoch-based reclamation** (used in RCU and crossbeam-epoch in Rust) — divide time into epochs; a thread can free nodes only if all threads are in a later epoch than when the node was retired.

**Read-Copy-Update (RCU)** is the kernel's primary lock-free technique for read-heavy data structures. The key insight: readers never take any lock and are never blocked — they just read with `rcu_read_lock()` (a compiler barrier + preemption disable). Writers: make a copy of the data structure, update the copy, atomically swap in the new version, then wait for a **grace period** — a time interval after which all pre-existing readers have finished (detected by each thread passing through a quiescent state: scheduling out, calling `cond_resched()`, or entering `rcu_read_unlock()`). After the grace period, the old version can be freed. RCU is ideal for data read millions of times per second but updated rarely: the kernel uses it for routing tables, network protocol handlers, and the process list. In user space, `liburcu` provides RCU; Rust's `crossbeam` crate provides epoch-based reclamation.

**Memory ordering for lock-free code** is the hardest part. A CAS with `memory_order_seq_cst` is safe but expensive (full fence on x86 = `MFENCE`, on ARM = `DMB ISH`). The pattern for a lock-free queue producer: `data.store(value, relaxed)` then `ready.store(true, release)`. Consumer: `while (!ready.load(acquire)) {}` then `data.load(relaxed)`. The release/acquire pair establishes the happens-before relationship — no fence needed on x86 (TSO provides it implicitly), but explicitly required on ARM. Using `relaxed` ordering throughout and relying on hardware TSO makes code that works on x86 but is broken on ARM — a portability bug that is extremely difficult to detect without hardware that exposes weak ordering. The rule: reason in terms of C++ memory ordering, not hardware. Only optimize to weaker ordering after profiling shows it matters and after careful formal analysis (or using tools like `memory model checker` / CDSChecker).

resources.label: Is Parallel Programming Hard? — McKenney (free PDF, definitive concurrency reference)
Preshing — An Introduction to Lock-Free Programming

Isolation levels, MVCC and deadlocks are concurrency control in DBs

topics.title: Filesystems & storage
body: Disk layout (MBR/GPT), **ext4** internals (inodes, extent trees), **journaling** (write-ahead logging, ordered vs writeback), the **VFS** layer (`struct inode`/`file`/`dentry`), and copy-on-write filesystems (ZFS/Btrfs — snapshots, checksums, RAID-Z).
Write-ahead logging is the same durability idea databases use; learning it once explains both.

subtopics.title: Disk layout, partition tables: MBR vs GPT and LBA addressing
body: Before a filesystem exists, a disk must be **partitioned**: divided into contiguous regions that can hold independent filesystems or be used as swap space. Two partitioning schemes dominate. **MBR (Master Boot Record)**, the legacy scheme (IBM PC, 1983), stores partition metadata in the first 512-byte sector of the disk: 446 bytes of bootloader code, 64 bytes of partition table (4 entries of 16 bytes each — 4 primary partitions maximum), and a 2-byte signature `0x55AA`. Each entry uses a 4-byte LBA (Logical Block Address) start and length, capping disk size at $2^{32}$ × 512 bytes = 2 TB. The limited partition count forces the 'extended partition' trick (one primary partition used as a container for more 'logical' partitions in a linked list), which is fragile.

**GPT (GUID Partition Table)**, specified in the UEFI standard, replaces MBR. The GPT header is at LBA 1 (not sector 0, which still holds a 'protective MBR' for backwards compatibility); a backup GPT header is at the last LBA. Partition entries each use 128 bytes, including two 16-byte GUIDs (partition type and unique ID), 8-byte LBA start/end, 8-byte attributes, and a 72-byte UTF-16 partition name. The standard supports up to 128 partition entries (expandable). LBA addressing uses 64 bits, supporting disks up to 9.4 ZB — effectively unlimited. GPT includes a CRC32 checksum of the header and partition table for integrity checking. Any disk larger than 2 TB requires GPT; UEFI-booting systems (all modern PCs) require GPT; GPT is now universal in production.

**LBA (Logical Block Address)** is the modern disk addressing scheme, replacing the legacy CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector) geometry which was limited to 8 GB. In LBA, blocks are simply numbered 0, 1, 2, ... from the start of the disk. The drive's firmware translates LBA to physical locations, accounting for remapped bad sectors, NAND flash wear leveling, and any internal geometry. The operating system and filesystem see only a flat array of 512-byte (legacy) or 4096-byte (Advanced Format, modern HDDs and NVMe SSDs) logical blocks. **4K native (4Kn)** drives expose 4096-byte sectors natively; **512e** drives emulate 512-byte sectors over 4 KB physical sectors — misaligned partition starts can cause a write to cross two physical sectors, doubling write amplification.

**NVMe (Non-Volatile Memory Express)** is the protocol for flash-based storage, replacing SATA/AHCI which was designed for rotational disks with one command queue of 32 entries. NVMe supports up to 65,535 queues with 65,536 entries each, parallelizing I/O across the SSD's multiple NAND channels. NVMe SSDs connect via PCIe lanes directly to the CPU, eliminating the storage controller bottleneck. Latency: SATA SSD ~70-100µs; NVMe SSD ~20-50µs; Intel Optane (3D XPoint, discontinued) ~2-10µs. These numbers matter for database performance: at 10µs NVMe latency, an `fsync()` costs 10µs; at 100µs SATA latency, it costs 10× more, making commit throughput 10× lower. Production database servers in 2024+ are almost entirely NVMe-based.

**RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks)** provides redundancy and/or performance by striping/mirroring data across multiple drives. RAID 0: striped across N drives (N× throughput, no redundancy — any drive failure loses all data). RAID 1: mirrored on 2 drives (reads from either, writes to both; survives 1 failure). RAID 5: striped with distributed parity (N-1 usable capacity from N drives; survives 1 failure; parity stripe computed as XOR of data stripes). RAID 6: dual parity (survives 2 failures). RAID 10: RAID 1 mirrored pairs striped (performance + fault tolerance). Hardware RAID uses a dedicated controller with NVRAM-backed cache; software RAID (Linux `md`, ZFS, Btrfs) runs in the OS. ZFS's RAID-Z is software RAID with significantly better consistency guarantees than Linux `md`.

subtopics.title: ext4 internals: inodes, extent trees, and block groups
body: **ext4** (fourth extended filesystem) is the default Linux filesystem for most distributions and the best-studied general-purpose on-disk format. Its design builds directly on ext2 (original), adding a journal (ext3) and extents (ext4). Understanding ext4 from first principles reveals the core ideas shared by all Unix filesystems: the inode, block allocation, directory structure, and fragmentation prevention.

The fundamental object is the **inode** (index node). Each file or directory is represented by exactly one inode, stored in the **inode table** — a fixed array preallocated at filesystem creation time (a critical design decision: `mkfs.ext4 -N <count>` sets the maximum number of files). An inode stores: file type and permissions (`st_mode`), owner UID/GID, link count (number of directory entries pointing to this inode), size in bytes, timestamps (atime/mtime/ctime/crtime in ext4), 60 bytes of block pointers (`i_block[]`), and extended attributes. Crucially, an inode does NOT store the filename — filenames are stored in directory entries. This separation enables **hard links**: multiple directory entries pointing to the same inode; the file's data is freed only when the link count reaches zero. `stat file` shows inode number; `ls -i` lists inode numbers.

ext4 stores file block locations using **extent trees**. An extent is a run of consecutive physical blocks, described by `(logical_block_start, physical_block_start, block_count)`. Most files can be described by 4 extents fitting directly in the inode's `i_block[12]` array (the 'inline' extent tree root). Large or fragmented files use an **HTree** (B-tree of extent nodes) stored in allocated blocks, with the root in `i_block`. The key property: sequential file data is stored in extents of up to 128 MB of contiguous blocks, minimizing seek time on HDDs and I/O overhead on SSDs (one DMA descriptor per extent). Contrast with ext2's indirect block pointers: large files require 3 levels of pointer blocks, meaning reading the last byte of a 100 MB file requires 3 additional block reads just for the pointers.

**Block groups** divide the disk into chunks (typically 32 MB, based on the group descriptor's 16-bit block count field with 4 KB blocks) for locality. Each block group contains: a copy of the superblock and group descriptors (every other group), a block bitmap (one bit per block in the group), an inode bitmap (one bit per inode slot), the inode table, and data blocks. When creating a file, ext4 allocates the inode and data blocks in the same block group when possible, keeping file metadata and data co-located on disk. The **block allocator** uses a multi-block allocator that clusters new allocations in extents to prevent fragmentation. `e2fsck` checks and repairs the filesystem by verifying the bitmaps, inode counts, and block pointers against the on-disk data, and `debugfs` lets you explore the raw on-disk structures.

**Directories** in ext4 are implemented as files with special structure. The directory file contains a sequence of variable-length **dentry** records: inode number (4 bytes), record length (2 bytes, padded to 4-byte alignment), name length (1 byte), file type (1 byte), and the filename bytes. A lookup for filename `foo` scans dentries linearly unless the **hash-tree** directory index is enabled (`dir_index` feature, default on ext4), which stores a two-level B-tree indexed by filename hash for O(log N) lookup. Creating a file creates a new inode, writes the inode number and filename into the parent directory, and increments the parent's link count if the new file is a directory (directories have entries `..` pointing back up). Hard-linking a file is just adding a new dentry in any directory pointing to the existing inode — no data copying.

subtopics.title: Journaling and write-ahead logging: modes and crash consistency
body: Without journaling, a filesystem **crash** (power failure, kernel panic) during a multi-step update operation can leave the filesystem inconsistent. The classic example: creating a file requires writing (1) the inode, (2) the block bitmap marking the inode allocated, (3) the directory entry. If the system crashes after (1) but before (3), the inode exists with no directory entry pointing to it — a **leaked inode**, consuming space that cannot be reclaimed without `e2fsck`. If it crashes between (2) and (1), a block is marked allocated but no inode uses it — leaked block space. ext2 required running `fsck` after every unclean shutdown, which took hours on large disks.

**Journaling** (write-ahead logging) solves crash consistency by writing changes to a **journal** (a circular log) before applying them to the main filesystem. The journal is typically 128 MB and stored in the filesystem itself (inode 8 in ext4). The write sequence: (1) write a **journal descriptor block** with the set of blocks to be modified; (2) write the data for those blocks to the journal; (3) write a **journal commit block** — once the commit block is durably on disk (`fdatasync` to the journal), the transaction is **committed**. (4) asynchronously **checkpoint** the blocks to their final locations in the filesystem; (5) once all blocks are checkpointed, mark the journal space as free. After a crash, recovery replays any committed-but-not-checkpointed transactions from the journal — the replay is safe because journal blocks are committed atomically.

ext4 offers three journaling modes. **`data=journal`** (safest): both metadata (inode, bitmaps, directory entries) and file data are written to the journal before being placed in the filesystem. Guarantees that after a crash, both metadata and data are consistent. Cost: every write hits disk twice — once in the journal, once in its final location. Rarely used except for NFS servers. **`data=ordered`** (default): only metadata is journaled; data blocks are written to their final locations *before* the metadata journal is committed. This ensures that after a crash, if a new inode points to data blocks, those blocks contain valid data. Does not protect against data corruption on concurrent writes, but prevents old data from appearing under a newly extended file (the most important correctness property for user data). **`data=writeback`** (fastest): metadata is journaled but data writes can be reordered arbitrarily. A crash can cause 'old data' to appear in file regions that were extended during a transaction. Acceptable for databases that manage their own consistency (PostgreSQL uses its own WAL and disables filesystem journaling with `data=writeback` for the data directory).

The **`fsync()` and `fdatasync()` syscalls** are the user-space durability guarantee: `fsync(fd)` flushes all dirty pages for the file and its metadata to stable storage (waits for disk acknowledgment). `fdatasync(fd)` flushes data and the minimum necessary metadata (size, but not atime). The difference: `fsync` updates mtime, which requires flushing the inode to disk; `fdatasync` skips that if the file size did not change. For a database writing a WAL record: write data, then `fdatasync(wal_fd)` — the write is now durable. On HDDs, `fsync` can cost 5-10ms (one rotational latency for the platter to reach the journal commit location). On NVMe SSDs, ~20-50µs. On cloud block storage (EBS gp3), ~1-2ms. These numbers directly determine maximum write throughput: a database doing `fsync` per transaction is limited to 200-1000 TPS on HDD, 20,000-50,000 on NVMe.

**Barriers** (disk write barriers, `O_DSYNC`, `O_SYNC`) ensure write ordering between operations. A disk with a volatile write-back cache can reorder writes internally; a power failure before the cache flushes loses committed data even if `fsync` returned. A **flush command** (ATA FLUSH CACHE, SCSI SYNCHRONIZE CACHE) forces the drive to flush its cache to the platter/flash. Modern drives that claim 'write cache' behavior must either physically flush to non-volatile media or use a supercapacitor to complete in-progress writes on power loss. AWS EBS, NVMe SSDs with power-loss protection, and all server-grade drives provide this guarantee. Consumer SSDs and spinning disks without supercapacitors are vulnerable to data loss on abrupt power loss even with `fsync`. This is the real meaning of 'durable storage' in distributed systems: not 'I called fsync' but 'the device guarantees non-volatile storage after fsync returns'.

resources.label: OSTEP — Crash Consistency: FSCK and Journaling (Ch. 42)

subtopics.title: The VFS layer: struct inode, dentry, file, and the file_operations vtable
body: The **Virtual Filesystem Switch (VFS)** is the kernel's abstraction layer that presents a uniform API for all filesystem types. When you call `open("/home/user/file.txt")`, the VFS layer handles the path resolution and dispatches to the correct filesystem implementation (ext4, Btrfs, tmpfs, procfs, whatever) via function pointers — without the calling code knowing or caring which filesystem is mounted where. This is a concrete implementation of the **strategy pattern** in C: the VFS defines a set of operations as function pointer tables (`file_operations`, `inode_operations`, `super_operations`), and each filesystem implementation provides its own concrete implementations.

Four key VFS data structures: **`super_block`** represents a mounted filesystem instance — contains filesystem type, block size, root inode, flags (read-only?), and pointers to `super_operations` (sync, evict inode, etc.). **`inode`** represents a file or directory, independent of its name or location in the directory tree — contains permissions, timestamps, size, the `i_ino` number, and pointers to `inode_operations` (lookup, create, link, mkdir, rename, truncate, etc.) and `address_space_operations` (readpage, writepage, for page cache integration). **`dentry`** (directory entry) represents one component of a path and connects a name to an inode — e.g., the name `"file.txt"` in directory `/home/user/`. Dentries are cached in the **dcache** (dentry cache) for fast path resolution. **`file`** represents an open file descriptor — contains the current offset, open flags, a pointer to the inode, and `file_operations` (read, write, seek, mmap, ioctl, poll, etc.).

**Path resolution** (`namei`, the kernel function `path_lookup`) walks the dentry tree: split the path into components, start from `/` (or the process's CWD for relative paths), for each component call `inode_operations->lookup(parent_inode, name)` which returns the child dentry. Each hop checks permissions (`inode_permission`). Symlinks trigger a recursive resolution (depth-limited to avoid loops). Mount points are detected: when a dentry is flagged as a mount point, `follow_mount()` looks up which superblock is mounted there and returns its root dentry instead. The result of path resolution is a `struct path { vfsmount*, dentry* }`. This is why `/proc/mounts` shows what is mounted where and `pivot_root` / `chroot` can change the apparent filesystem root — they manipulate the VFS mount table.

The **`file_operations` vtable** is the interface between VFS and filesystems (and between VFS and user space for device files). For a regular file on ext4, `file->f_op->read_iter` calls `generic_file_read_iter` → `mapping->a_ops->readpage` → `ext4_readpage` → submits a bio (block I/O request) to the block layer. For a character device (`/dev/tty`), `file->f_op` points to the tty driver's operations. For a socket, `file->f_op` points to socket file operations that ultimately call the TCP/IP stack. This is why the 'everything is a file' abstraction works: `select()`/`poll()`/`epoll()` call `file->f_op->poll` on any fd type and get back readability/writability events — tcp sockets, pipes, eventfds, timerfd, inotify all implement the same interface.

The **page cache** integrates with VFS via the `address_space` structure (one per inode). Each file's pages are tracked in a radix tree/xarray keyed by page index (file offset / PAGE_SIZE). When you `mmap()` a file and access a page, the page fault handler calls `address_space->a_ops->readpage()` to fill the page from disk. When you `write()` to a file, the kernel marks the page dirty in the `address_space` and adds it to the writeback list. The **writeback** daemon (`kworker/flush-*`) periodically calls `a_ops->writepage()` to write dirty pages back to disk. `sync()` / `fsync()` force immediate writeback. This centralized page cache means that `read()`, `write()`, `mmap()`, and `sendfile()` all operate on the same pool of cached pages — no duplication, automatic sharing between processes, and OS-managed caching without application complexity.

subtopics.title: Copy-on-write filesystems: ZFS snapshots, checksums, and RAID-Z
body: **Copy-on-write (CoW) filesystems** never overwrite existing data in place. Instead, every write goes to a new location on disk; only after all new blocks are written does the filesystem update the parent pointer to point to the new location. ext4 (with journaling) achieves **crash consistency** but still overwrites blocks in place for checkpointing — a silent hardware write error during that overwrite can corrupt data with no detection. CoW filesystems detect and prevent corruption at a structural level: by never overwriting, they make every write an atomic pointer swap, and by checksumming every block, they detect silent corruption immediately.

**ZFS** (originally from Sun Microsystems, now OpenZFS) is the canonical CoW filesystem. Every data block and metadata block is checksummed (Fletcher-4 or SHA-256 configurable). Checksums are stored *in the parent block* (the pointer to this block), not in the block itself — this allows ZFS to detect corruption even if the block itself appears internally consistent. On read, ZFS recomputes the checksum and compares; mismatch triggers a **read error** and, if redundancy exists, a repair. This is the critical difference from ext4: ext4 can silently return corrupted data to an application; ZFS either returns correct data or returns an error. Silent data corruption (which the Google and Facebook papers estimate at 1-4 per-drive-year on spinning HDDs) is detected and corrected by ZFS.

**Snapshots** are free in ZFS by design: since blocks are never overwritten, a snapshot is just a reference to the current tree root — O(1) creation. As the live filesystem changes, new blocks are allocated for modified data; the snapshot still references the old blocks. Snapshot storage cost is proportional to the amount of data changed since the snapshot, not the total filesystem size. `zfs snapshot pool/dataset@snap` creates a snapshot instantly; `zfs rollback pool/dataset@snap` reverts all changes since the snapshot in seconds. Compare to ext4 snapshots (via LVM thin provisioning): also CoW at the block layer, but without the per-block checksums and with more administrative complexity.

**RAID-Z** is ZFS's software RAID implementation. RAID-Z1 (single parity, like RAID 5) writes each stripe atomically: all data blocks and the parity block are written in one transaction before any metadata update. This eliminates the **RAID 5 write hole** — the classic failure mode where a RAID 5 array's parity is inconsistent after a crash mid-stripe because the data and parity writes were not atomic. ZFS uses variable stripe sizes (each write is its own stripe, width determined by the data size and number of vdevs), so there is no partial-stripe update. RAID-Z2 (double parity) and RAID-Z3 (triple parity) allow 2 or 3 drive failures. `zpool status` shows device health and error counts; `zpool scrub` reads every block and verifies checksums, repairing from redundancy if corruption is found — the only way to proactively find latent corruption before it causes data loss.

**Btrfs** is the Linux-native CoW filesystem with similar features to ZFS: checksums, snapshots, multi-device RAID, and subvolumes. Btrfs uses a B-tree of B-trees architecture (all metadata in B-trees, a global tree of trees mapping objectid to tree roots). Its RAID 5/6 implementation had long-standing write-hole bugs (fixed in kernel 6.6). Btrfs is default on Fedora and used by Facebook's storage infrastructure at petabyte scale, but ZFS (via OpenZFS for Linux) is generally considered more mature and battle-tested for critical data. The choice in production: ZFS for reliability-critical workloads; Btrfs for Linux integration and subvolume-based container storage.

resources.label: Write a FUSE filesystem — libfuse examples

topics.title: Kernel development
body: Kernel vs user space (privilege rings, the `SYSCALL` path), writing a **kernel module** (`module_init`, `printk`, `kmalloc`), character device drivers (`file_operations`, `copy_to_user`), interrupt handling (top/bottom half), and the kernel's most important lock: **RCU**.
Internal allocators (buddy + slab/SLUB) and dynamic instrumentation with **kprobes/eBPF** round this out.

subtopics.title: Kernel vs user space: privilege rings, SYSCALL entry, and Spectre/Meltdown defenses
body: The x86-64 CPU implements four **privilege rings** (0-3), but Linux uses only ring 0 (kernel mode) and ring 3 (user mode). Ring 0 can execute any instruction, access any physical memory, configure all hardware registers (including segment registers and the CR3 page table register), and modify the APIC (interrupt controller). Ring 3 can only execute non-privileged instructions and can only access virtual memory pages marked user-accessible in the page tables. The hardware enforces this: attempting a privileged instruction or accessing a supervisor-only page from ring 3 raises a CPU exception (#GP general protection, or #PF page fault with `U/S=0`), which the kernel handles and typically converts to SIGSEGV.

The transition from ring 3 to ring 0 happens via the `SYSCALL` instruction (the fast path on x86-64) or legacy `INT 0x80` (deprecated). `SYSCALL` loads the kernel entry point from the `LSTAR` MSR and switches to ring 0; the kernel sets `LSTAR` at boot to point to `entry_SYSCALL_64` in `arch/x86/entry/entry_64.S`. This assembly routine saves user registers (via `PUSH_AND_CLEAR_REGS` macro which also zeros sensitive registers on entry to prevent info leaks), switches to the kernel stack (previously saved in the CPU's `GSBASE`-relative `cpu_current_top_of_stack`), and dispatches to the syscall handler via `sys_call_table[%rax]`. Return from kernel mode uses `SYSRET` (or `IRETQ` for certain paths) to restore user registers and switch back to ring 3.

**Meltdown (CVE-2018-3675)** exploited the fact that before Meltdown was patched, the kernel's virtual address space was mapped into every user process's page tables (marked non-accessible at the PTE level, but *present* in the table). Speculative execution across the syscall boundary allowed user-mode code to transiently read kernel memory (before the access permission check was enforced), leak it via a cache side channel, and reconstruct the value. The fix is **Kernel Page Table Isolation (KPTI/PTI)**: user-mode page tables contain only a minimal 'trampoline' for the syscall entry; the full kernel mapping is present only in the kernel-mode page tables. Every syscall or interrupt now requires switching `CR3` (flushing the TLB), costing ~200-500 cycles per transition and causing 5-30% overhead on I/O-heavy workloads.

**Spectre (CVE-2018-3693)** is harder: it exploits the branch predictor. Specifically, a user process can 'train' the branch predictor (using a branch in user-mode code at the same virtual address as a privileged branch in the kernel) so that when the kernel executes the privileged branch speculatively, it follows the user-desired path, transiently accesses kernel memory it should not, and leaks it via a cache side channel. Mitigations include: **Retpoline** (replaces indirect branches with a return-based trampoline that defeats branch-target injection), **IBRS/IBPB** (hardware barrier instructions that flush the branch predictor on privilege change, costly), and **eIBRS** (Enhanced IBRS, Intel's hardware fix that keeps prediction state separate across privilege levels, much cheaper). These mitigations collectively cause 5-20% kernel overhead and sparked a still-ongoing arms race between microarchitectural attack research and hardware/software mitigations.

Understanding these mitigations is not just academic: they determine the performance model of modern Linux. A server running 1 million syscalls/second (common for network-heavy workloads) spends a measurable fraction of CPU time just in the entry/exit machinery. The `perf top` command will show `entry_SYSCALL_64`, `__x86_return_thunk` (retpoline), and `kpti_restore_regs_and_return_to_usermode` at the top for such workloads. io_uring (a kernel-user shared ring buffer for async I/O, introduced in kernel 5.1) was designed partly to reduce syscall count: a single `io_uring_enter` syscall can submit and reap thousands of I/O operations, amortizing the per-syscall overhead.

resources.label: Kernel Page Table Isolation — kernel.org documentation

subtopics.title: Writing a kernel module: module_init, printk, and kmalloc
body: A **loadable kernel module (LKM)** is a compiled object file (`.ko`) that can be dynamically inserted into the running kernel with `insmod` / `modprobe` and removed with `rmmod`. The module runs in kernel space (ring 0) with full hardware access, sharing the kernel's virtual address space. This is both the power and the danger: a module bug can crash the entire system. The module system is how Linux drivers are distributed and updated without recompiling the kernel.

A minimal module requires two functions: `module_init(my_init)` registers an initialization function called at load time; `module_exit(my_exit)` registers a cleanup function called at unload. Both must be declared in the module with `static int __init my_init(void)` and `static void __exit my_exit(void)`. The `__init` annotation marks the function as initialization-only: the kernel frees the memory after the function returns (to save RAM). `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL")` is mandatory for modules that use GPL-only exported symbols (which includes most kernel APIs). `MODULE_AUTHOR` and `MODULE_DESCRIPTION` are informational. The `.ko` file is a regular ELF object with additional `.modinfo` and module-specific sections that `insmod` processes.

`printk(KERN_INFO "message %d\n", value)` is the kernel's `printf`. Log levels range from `KERN_EMERG` (system unusable) through `KERN_DEBUG` (debug). Messages go to the kernel ring buffer, readable via `dmesg`. Unlike userspace, there is no buffered I/O in the kernel — `printk` is directly formatted and stored. `printk` is async-safe (callable from interrupt context) via a per-CPU buffer, but it is slow (~1-10µs) and must not be called in hot paths. The `seq_file` interface is the standard for exposing module state via `/proc` entries: implement `seq_show()` to format output, register with `proc_create_seq()`, and the kernel handles large-buffer management.

`kmalloc(size, GFP_KERNEL)` allocates kernel memory from the **SLUB allocator** (the modern successor to SLAB). The `GFP_KERNEL` flag means allocation may sleep (blocking to reclaim memory); `GFP_ATOMIC` means non-sleeping (for use in interrupt handlers). `kmalloc` returns physically contiguous memory — sizes up to 4 MB work; larger allocations should use `vmalloc` (virtually contiguous, not necessarily physically). Always check the return for NULL (allocation failure). `kfree(ptr)` releases it. For large buffers (e.g., for DMA), `dma_alloc_coherent(dev, size, &dma_addr, GFP_KERNEL)` allocates DMA-capable memory and returns both the kernel virtual address and the device-visible physical address. Misunderstanding this (using `kmalloc` and passing the virtual address to a device register) is a common driver bug that causes system corruption.

The character device skeleton: call `alloc_chrdev_region(&dev_num, 0, 1, "mydev")` to get a major/minor number dynamically. Create a `struct cdev`, initialize with `cdev_init(&my_cdev, &my_fops)` where `my_fops` is a `struct file_operations` with your `open`, `read`, `write`, `release` pointers. `cdev_add` registers it with the kernel. Create a `struct class` and `device_create` to get a `/dev/mydev` entry. In `read`: copy kernel buffer to user with `copy_to_user(user_buf, kernel_buf, count)` — this handles page faults gracefully and returns the number of bytes not copied on failure. NEVER `memcpy` directly to a user pointer; the kernel has no guarantee that the user pointer is valid or mapped. `copy_from_user` is the symmetric operation for writes.

resources.label: The Linux Kernel Module Programming Guide (LKMPG) — free, up to date

subtopics.title: Interrupt handling: top-half, bottom-half, softirqs, and workqueues
body: **Interrupts** are signals from hardware (NIC received a packet, disk I/O completed, timer fired, keyboard pressed) that suspend the current execution context and call an **interrupt handler** (ISR). On x86-64, interrupt delivery: the CPU receives a pin assertion or APIC message, looks up the interrupt vector (0-255) in the **IDT** (Interrupt Descriptor Table set up by the kernel at boot), saves registers on the kernel stack, and calls the handler. The handler runs with interrupts disabled (on the local CPU), so it must complete quickly — every microsecond spent in an ISR is a microsecond of latency for every other interrupt. This constraint splits interrupt processing into two halves.

The **top half** (the ISR itself) does the minimum necessary work with interrupts disabled: acknowledge the interrupt (tell the hardware the interrupt was received, otherwise it will keep firing), read any time-sensitive status registers, wake a waiting process if applicable, and schedule the bottom half. For a NIC: read the packet length from a register, acknowledge the DMA completion, and schedule the NAPI poll. For a timer: increment a counter, schedule a timer softirq. The **bottom half** runs later with interrupts re-enabled, doing the bulk of the work. Two bottom-half mechanisms: **softirqs** and **workqueues**.

**Softirqs** are statically-defined (there are only 10 types, including `NET_TX_SOFTIRQ`, `NET_RX_SOFTIRQ`, `TIMER_SOFTIRQ`, `BLOCK_SOFTIRQ`, `TASKLET_SOFTIRQ`) and run in a special software interrupt context called `ksoftirqd/<cpu>` (a per-CPU kernel thread) or immediately after hardware interrupt return, if below a time budget. Softirq handlers can run on multiple CPUs simultaneously but cannot sleep (they run in interrupt context). **Tasklets** are a higher-level interface over softirqs: you define a function and data, and `tasklet_schedule()` queues it for execution in `TASKLET_SOFTIRQ` context. Tasklets are guaranteed to not run simultaneously on multiple CPUs (for the same tasklet), simplifying locking.

**Workqueues** run deferred work in a **process context** (kernel threads, `kworker/<cpu>` threads), meaning they CAN sleep, allocate memory with `GFP_KERNEL`, and use all kernel APIs. `INIT_WORK(&my_work, my_work_function)` defines a work item; `schedule_work(&my_work)` queues it on the global workqueue (processed by the `kworker` thread pool). `schedule_delayed_work(&my_work, HZ)` schedules after a delay. For high-priority or isolated work, `alloc_workqueue("myqueue", WQ_UNBOUND | WQ_HIGHPRI, 0)` creates a dedicated workqueue. Block I/O completion, filesystem writeback, and SCSI command completion all use workqueues because they need to allocate memory and take sleeping locks.

The **NAPI (New API)** is the modern NIC interrupt handling pattern, illustrating these concepts together. Without NAPI, every received packet triggers a hardware interrupt, and each ISR processes one packet — catastrophic at 10+ Gbps. With NAPI: the first packet triggers an interrupt; the ISR disables further NIC interrupts and schedules a **poll function** (a softirq/NAPI poll). The poll function runs in softirq context, reading up to a **budget** (e.g., 64) packets from the NIC's ring buffer in a loop, processing them into the kernel's socket buffers, and returning the number processed. If the budget was fully used (more packets may be pending), the kernel reschedules the poll. If fewer than budget were processed (ring buffer drained), re-enable NIC interrupts. This **interrupt coalescing** pattern converts O(N) interrupts for N packets into O(N/budget) softirq invocations, dramatically improving high-throughput throughput while maintaining low latency for low-rate traffic.

resources.label: Linux kernel — Softirqs, Tasklets, and Workqueues documentation
NAPI — Linux kernel network driver documentation

subtopics.title: Kernel memory: buddy system, SLUB allocator, and RCU
body: The kernel has two allocators serving different needs. The **buddy system** (or buddy allocator) manages **physical page frames** (4 KB units of physical memory). It maintains N free lists, one for each order 0 through MAX_ORDER (typically 10): order-k list holds blocks of $2^k$ contiguous pages. To allocate $2^k$ pages: if the order-k list is non-empty, return a block; otherwise, split an order-(k+1) block into two **buddies** and return one. To free a block: check if its buddy (at `block_addr ^ (1 << k) * PAGE_SIZE`) is free; if so, merge them into an order-(k+1) block, and recurse up. Allocation and deallocation are O(log max_order) = O(10). The buddy system handles all large kernel allocations (device DMA buffers, huge pages, per-CPU data, etc.) and is the foundation on which all smaller allocators sit.

The **SLUB allocator** (Simplified SLUB, default since kernel 2.6.23) sits on top of the buddy system and provides efficient allocation of identically-sized objects — kernel structs like `task_struct`, `mm_struct`, `inode`, `dentry`, `socket`. For each size class, SLUB maintains a **slab**: one or more pages dedicated to objects of that size, with a per-CPU free list for lock-free fast path. Allocating a `task_struct`: the SLUB cache for `task_struct` (created at kernel init with `kmem_cache_create("task_struct", sizeof(struct task_struct), ...)`) returns a preallocated object from the per-CPU free list in O(1) without any lock. When the per-CPU list is empty, it refills from the node (NUMA-local) partial slab. The SLUB design avoids per-object metadata overhead (unlike SLAB which stored it in the object header), making it both faster and more compact.

**RCU (Read-Copy-Update)** is the kernel's most important synchronization primitive for read-heavy data structures. The invariant: readers need no locks and are never blocked; writers must serialize with each other (they take a write lock) and wait for a **grace period** before freeing old data. The **grace period** is the time interval after which the kernel is certain that no reader can still reference the pre-update version — determined by every CPU passing through a **quiescent state** (scheduling out, going idle, entering RCU read-side critical section with a different generation). `rcu_read_lock()` / `rcu_read_unlock()` mark critical sections (just a preemption-disable + compiler barrier, zero cost). `rcu_dereference(ptr)` safely reads an RCU-protected pointer. `rcu_assign_pointer(ptr, new)` publishes an update. `synchronize_rcu()` waits for a grace period (may sleep). `call_rcu(&old->head, free_func)` schedules `free_func(old)` after the next grace period asynchronously.

RCU is used throughout the kernel where reads vastly outnumber writes: the process list (`tasklist_lock` + RCU for readers, write lock for modifiers), the network routing table (`ip_fib_rules` — routing table lookups at packet-per-second rates with no lock), `struct file_operations` pointers in the VFS, module reference counting, and network filter rules. The design insight is profound: if you can make the common read path zero-cost, you can tolerate expensive write paths without degrading overall throughput. RCU is the reason Linux can route millions of packets per second per CPU core without per-packet locking. For user space, `liburcu` provides the same mechanism. Rust's `arc-swap` and `crossbeam-epoch` are user-space equivalents.

**Per-CPU variables** (`DEFINE_PER_CPU(type, name)`, `get_cpu_var(name)`, `put_cpu_var(name)`) store one instance of data per CPU. Accessed with preemption disabled (so the CPU won't change mid-access), they require no locking whatsoever — each CPU only reads/writes its own instance. Used for performance counters, allocator free lists, and scheduler run queues. The kernel multiplies `offsetof(struct, field)` by `raw_smp_processor_id()` to index into per-CPU storage areas that are laid out contiguously in memory. The hardware `gs` segment register on x86-64 points to the current CPU's per-CPU data area, making access `mov %gs:offset, %rax` — a single memory reference. Understanding per-CPU variables explains why `vmstat`/`/proc/stat` reads can be stale: they sum per-CPU counters after the fact.

resources.label: Paul McKenney — What is RCU, Fundamentally?

subtopics.title: Dynamic instrumentation: kprobes, tracepoints, and eBPF
body: **kprobes** allow inserting breakpoints at any kernel instruction address at runtime, without recompilation. `register_kprobe(&kp)` where `kp.symbol_name = "do_sys_open"` and `kp.pre_handler = my_handler` causes the kernel to: (1) save the instruction at the target address, (2) write a `INT3` (breakpoint) instruction, (3) on hitting the breakpoint, call `my_handler` with the saved registers. The handler runs in interrupt context (no sleeping, no printk). `kretprobe` is the variant for function return interception. kprobes enable production kernel tracing without any kernel modification — used by SystemTap and, historically, DTrace on Linux. The overhead is ~1µs per probe hit.

**Tracepoints** are statically-defined hooks scattered throughout the kernel source at semantically meaningful locations: `trace_sched_switch(prev, next)` in the scheduler, `trace_net_dev_xmit(skb, dev)` in the network driver, `trace_block_rq_issue(q, rq)` in the block layer. Each tracepoint is defined with `DEFINE_TRACE(name, proto, args)` and defaults to a no-op (a branch on a single byte: if the byte is 0, skip; if 1, call the trace function). Enabling a tracepoint patches that byte to 1 (using the same `text_poke` mechanism as kprobes). Tracepoints are preferred over kprobes when available because they are part of the kernel's stable ABI (tracepoint arguments don't change across kernel versions) and have lower overhead (~5-10ns per disabled tracepoint, ~100ns enabled).

**eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter)** is the current standard for kernel tracing, networking, and security. An eBPF program is a sequence of instructions in a restricted ISA (11 64-bit registers, limited stack, bounded loops only), written in a C subset and compiled by LLVM to eBPF bytecode. Before running in the kernel, the bytecode passes through the **eBPF verifier**: a static analyzer that proves the program cannot crash the kernel, access out-of-bounds memory, execute unbounded loops, or call unauthorized helpers. Verified programs are JIT-compiled to native machine code (x86-64, ARM64, etc.) and run at near-native speed. eBPF programs attach to kprobes, tracepoints, network hooks (tc ingress/egress, XDP), syscall entry/exit, LSM hooks, and more.

**eBPF maps** are kernel data structures shared between eBPF programs and user space. Types include: hash maps, array maps, per-CPU variants, ring buffers (`BPF_MAP_TYPE_RINGBUF`, zero-copy output from kernel to user space), LRU hash maps, and program arrays (for tail calls). A typical eBPF tracing program: attach to `tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_execve`, read the filename argument, look it up in a hash map of process names to watch, and if found, emit an event to a ring buffer. The user-space loader (`libbpf`) reads events from the ring buffer and logs them. This is how `bpftrace` works: `bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:syscalls:sys_enter_execve { printf("%s exec %s\n", comm, str(args->filename)); }'` traces every `execve` with process name and path at near-zero overhead.

**XDP (eXpress Data Path)** runs eBPF programs at the earliest possible point in the network stack — in the NIC driver's interrupt handler, before any kernel memory allocation for the packet. An XDP program receives a pointer to the raw packet buffer (from the NIC's ring buffer DMA area) and returns one of: `XDP_PASS` (send to the normal network stack), `XDP_DROP` (drop immediately, zero copy to kernel), `XDP_TX` (send back out the same NIC), `XDP_REDIRECT` (redirect to another NIC or a user-space socket via AF_XDP). DDoS mitigation at 100+ Gbps is feasible with XDP DROP: the kernel can examine and drop millions of packets per second per core using a hash map lookup in an XDP program, before the normal network stack even sees the packets. Cloudflare's public DDoS mitigation infrastructure is XDP-based.
