Writing a kernel module mirrors writing your own RTOS scheduler

topics.title: Boot, linkers & ELF; build your own OS
body: The full chain: BIOS/UEFI → bootloader (GRUB2) → decompress kernel → `start_kernel()` → init. Write a bootloader that walks **real → protected → long mode**, understand **linker scripts** (`.text`/`.data`/`.bss`, entry point) and the **ELF** format (sections vs segments, PLT/GOT).
Then build a bare-metal x86-64 OS: GDT, IDT, paging, a physical/virtual memory manager, a scheduler, keyboard driver, and VGA text output.

subtopics.title: The boot sequence: BIOS/UEFI to kernel entry
body: On power-on, the CPU begins execution at a hardcoded physical address: `0xFFFFFFF0` (real-mode address `0xFFFF:0xFFF0` — a far jump vector in the **BIOS ROM** or **UEFI firmware**). This is the **reset vector**, and it is the very first instruction executed. From here, the firmware (BIOS or UEFI) initializes the CPU, memory controller, and essential chipset registers via the BIOS's POST (Power-On Self-Test): detects and initializes RAM (trains DDR timings), initializes the PCIe fabric, discovers devices. The CPU is in **real mode** (16-bit segment:offset addressing, 1 MB physical address space) at this point — the same mode as the 8086 from 1978.

**Legacy BIOS** then searches for a bootable drive: reads the first 512-byte sector (the **MBR**) from each drive in order, checks for the `0x55AA` boot signature at bytes 510-511, and if found, loads it to physical address `0x7C00` and jumps to it. The MBR contains the **first-stage bootloader** (446 bytes of code) which typically loads a larger **second stage** (GRUB's `core.img`) from a known location. For GPT disks, the stage-1 code is in the MBR and the stage-2 in a dedicated 'BIOS boot partition' (1-2 MB). GRUB2's `core.img` implements a minimal filesystem driver to load `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` and then the Linux kernel and initramfs. The boot process under legacy BIOS is inherently limited by the 446-byte first stage and the need for filesystem-specific code.

**UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface)** replaces BIOS entirely. The UEFI firmware includes a full FAT32 filesystem driver and reads the **ESP (EFI System Partition)** directly. Boot entries in the UEFI NVRAM (configurable via `efibootmgr`) point to EFI executables (`.efi` files) on the ESP: `/EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI` for the default, or distro-specific paths like `/EFI/ubuntu/shimx64.efi` for Secure Boot chains. The bootloader (GRUB2's EFI binary, systemd-boot, or rEFInd) runs as a UEFI application in protected mode (32-bit) or long mode (64-bit) with full UEFI services available: memory map, framebuffer, file I/O, network. UEFI's **Secure Boot** validates each binary against a database of trusted certificates before execution — the chain starts at the firmware's Platform Key.

Once GRUB2 is running, it loads the Linux kernel image (`vmlinuz`) and the **initramfs** (initial RAM filesystem) into memory. `vmlinuz` is a compressed self-extracting archive: the first sector contains a small decompressor that unpacks the kernel into a fixed physical address range and jumps to `startup_64` in `arch/x86/kernel/head_64.S`. `startup_64` sets up 4-level paging (mapping the kernel to the top of virtual address space, `0xffff...`), sets up a minimal GDT, and calls `start_kernel()` in C — the first C function of the OS. `start_kernel()` initializes, in order: lock debugging, CPU features, ACPI tables, memory zones (ZONE_DMA, ZONE_NORMAL, ZONE_HIGHMEM), the buddy allocator, SLUB, scheduler, IRQ subsystem, timers, the VFS, and finally calls `rest_init()` which spawns PID 1 (`/init` from the initramfs, then the real `/sbin/init` or systemd after the root filesystem is mounted).

The **initramfs** is a cpio archive embedded in the kernel boot parameters (or as a separate file). It contains a minimal root filesystem with just enough to mount the real root: kernel modules for the storage controller and filesystem type, `udev` (or `mdev`) for device discovery, and a shell script (`/init`) that mounts the real root and `exec`s into it (`switch_root`). This is necessary because the kernel at boot does not have the modules loaded for the specific storage hardware of your machine — the initramfs bridges the chicken-and-egg problem (you need the filesystem to load the module for the filesystem). `lsinitrd /boot/initramfs-*.img` lists its contents.

subtopics.title: ELF format: sections, segments, symbol tables, and relocations
body: **ELF (Executable and Linkable Format)** is the standard binary format for executables, shared libraries, and object files on Linux, BSD, and embedded systems. An ELF file begins with a 64-byte **ELF header** containing: a 4-byte magic `0x7F ELF`, class (32/64-bit), endianness, OS/ABI, object file type (`ET_EXEC`=executable, `ET_DYN`=shared library, `ET_REL`=relocatable object, `ET_CORE`=core dump), machine architecture (`EM_X86_64=62`), entry point virtual address, and offsets to the **program header table** and **section header table**.

**Sections** are the linker's view: the compiler emits `.text` (executable code), `.rodata` (read-only constants), `.data` (initialized writable data), `.bss` (zero-initialized data — takes no space in the file, only recorded size), `.symtab` (symbol table), `.strtab` (string table for symbol names), `.rela.text` (relocation records for `.text`), `.debug_*` (DWARF debug info), etc. Each section has a name, type, flags (allocatable? writable? executable?), virtual address, file offset, and size. The **section header table** is an array of these descriptors. `readelf -S binary` dumps sections. `objdump -D binary` disassembles all sections.

**Segments** are the loader's view: the OS doesn't care about sections, it cares about which virtual address ranges to map and with what permissions. The **program header table** describes segments: `PT_LOAD` segments are mapped into the process's address space. A typical executable has two `PT_LOAD` segments: one for `.text`+`.rodata` (flags `R|X`), one for `.data`+`.bss` (flags `R|W`). The dynamic linker segment (`PT_INTERP`) names the ELF interpreter (`/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2`). The `PT_GNU_STACK` segment controls whether the stack is executable (should be `RW`, no execute, for security). Sections and segments overlap: each `PT_LOAD` segment covers a range of sections. `readelf -l binary` shows segments and which sections each covers.

**Relocations** are instructions in `.rela.*` sections telling the linker how to patch addresses. When the compiler emits a call to `printf`, it does not know `printf`'s address (it's in libc, resolved at link or load time). Instead it emits a relocation record: `R_X86_64_PLT32: offset=X, symbol=printf, addend=-4` meaning 'at byte X in .text, compute the symbol's address plus addend and store it'. The static linker (for statically-linked binaries) resolves all relocations at link time. For dynamically-linked binaries, a **PLT (Procedure Linkage Table)** and **GOT (Global Offset Table)** provide lazy binding: the first call to `printf` goes to a PLT stub → calls the dynamic linker → resolves the address → patches the GOT entry → subsequent calls go directly through the GOT (one indirect jump, no dynamic linker). `objdump -d -j .plt binary` shows PLT stubs; `objdump -R binary` shows relocation entries the dynamic linker will fill in.

**Symbol visibility** in ELF: each symbol has a binding (local, global, weak) and visibility (default, hidden, protected). `gcc -fvisibility=hidden` marks all symbols hidden by default (not exported from the DSO, invisible to the dynamic linker), with explicit `__attribute__((visibility("default")))` to export specific symbols. Hidden symbols are resolved at link time to direct calls (no PLT overhead). This is how libraries with tight performance requirements (OpenSSL, zlib) avoid PLT overhead for internal calls. `nm --dynamic binary` lists dynamic symbols; `nm binary` lists all symbols including local. Understanding ELF symbols is essential for binary exploitation (finding gadgets, libc base via PLT/GOT, symbol resolution to defeat ASLR).

resources.label: Linkers and Loaders — John Levine (online version)

subtopics.title: Linker scripts: placing sections, entry points, and memory regions
body: A **linker script** (`ld -T script.ld`) gives the linker precise control over how sections from input object files are combined and where they are placed in the output binary. The default linker script (view with `ld --verbose`) is a complex GNU LD script; for OS development and embedded systems, you write a custom script to control the exact physical and virtual layout of your binary.

The basic structure: `ENTRY(start)` sets the entry point (the first virtual address executed after loading). `MEMORY { ... }` defines named memory regions with origins and lengths (used in embedded: `FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 512K; RAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 128K`). `SECTIONS { ... }` maps input sections to output sections: `. = 0x1000;` sets the current location counter; `.text : { *(.text) *(.text.*) }` collects all `.text` sections from all input objects into the output `.text` section at the current location. `. = ALIGN(4096)` rounds up to a page boundary.

OS kernel linker scripts are more complex. The Linux kernel's `arch/x86/kernel/vmlinux.lds.S` (preprocessed to `.lds`) places: `__START_KERNEL_map` (the kernel's virtual base), `.text` with `TEXT_OFFSET` for the 2 MB alignment required by KASLR, `.rodata` aligned to page boundaries, `.data` aligned, then `.bss` at the end. Special symbols like `_end` and `_text` are defined at section boundaries for use by kernel code to iterate its own sections. The `DISCARD` section (`/DISCARD/ : { *(.comment) *(.note.*) }`) drops debug sections from the final binary. `PROVIDE(symbol = expression)` defines a symbol only if not already defined — used for weakly-linked default values.

For a bare-metal OS, a minimal linker script: set entry to `_start` (your assembly bootstrap), map physical address `0x100000` (1 MB, above the BIOS reserved region) for the load address, with the virtual address at `0xFFFFFFFF80100000` (the kernel high-memory region), then collect `.text`, `.rodata`, `.data`, and `.bss`. The difference between load address (where the bootloader places the binary in physical memory) and virtual address (where the binary expects to run) is the key to **position-independent** vs **position-dependent** kernel setup: the kernel switches on paging and then runs at the virtual address, while the bootloader loaded it at the physical one.

After the linker runs, `objcopy -O binary vmlinux vmlinux.bin` strips all ELF metadata producing a raw binary flat-image (used by some bootloaders and embedded systems). `size vmlinux` shows the size of `.text`, `.data`, and `.bss`. `nm vmlinux | sort -k 1` shows all symbols in address order — useful for finding where a crash address falls. `addr2line -e vmlinux 0xffffffff81001000` translates a kernel virtual address to source file and line number (requires DWARF debug info). This tool chain — compile, link, inspect, load — is the complete cycle for any systems binary and must be fluent before attempting OS development.

subtopics.title: x86-64 protected and long mode: GDT, IDT, and page table setup
body: An x86-64 OS starts in 16-bit real mode and must bootstrap itself to 64-bit long mode. The sequence: (1) **Real mode** (16-bit, 1 MB address space). (2) **Protected mode** (32-bit, 4 GB address space, segmentation + paging, separate rings). (3) **Long mode** (64-bit, 64-bit virtual addresses, segmentation mostly unused, 4-level paging required). Skipping steps is not possible on bare metal — hardware starts at real mode. UEFI bootloaders may already be in long mode, simplifying things.

The **GDT (Global Descriptor Table)** is a table of 8-byte **segment descriptors** that defines the properties of each segment (base, limit, ring level, type). In 32-bit protected mode, segmentation is used extensively. In 64-bit long mode, most segment registers are ignored (base forced to 0, limit is effectively unlimited), but the GDT is still required. The minimal 64-bit GDT: a null descriptor (entry 0, required), a kernel code segment (`0x00af9a000000ffff`, DPL=0, 64-bit code, limit=4 GB), and a kernel data segment. `lgdt [gdt_descriptor]` loads the GDT (base address + limit). After loading, a far jump `jmp 0x08:long_mode_start` loads the code segment register and actually enters long mode (the far jump with the code segment selector triggers the CPU to switch to 64-bit mode).

The **IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table)** is a table of up to 256 **gate descriptors** (8 or 16 bytes each) mapping interrupt vector numbers to handler addresses. Each entry specifies: the handler's segment selector and offset (the address), the DPL (what ring can trigger this via `INT n`), the gate type (interrupt gate vs trap gate — interrupt gates automatically disable further interrupts, trap gates don't), and the IST (Interrupt Stack Table index, for switching to a dedicated stack for specific interrupts like double faults). `lidt [idt_descriptor]` loads the IDT. After loading, you must set up handlers for at least: `#PF` (14, page fault), `#GP` (13, general protection fault), `#DF` (8, double fault — fatal), and the timer IRQ (32-47 via PIC/APIC mapping). Without these, any exception crashes the machine with a triple fault (reset).

**Long mode paging setup** requires 4-level page tables (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT, or PML4 → PDPT → 1 GB PD entry with PS=1). At early boot (before the kernel's full page table init), you need identity mapping (virtual == physical for the bootloader region) plus a high-memory mapping for the kernel. Minimal setup: allocate 4 static 4 KB pages for PML4, PDPT, PD, PT. Map 0x0→0x200000 (first 2 MB) as a large 2 MB page (PD entry with PS=1): `pd[0] = 0x000083` (present + writable + huge page bit). Map the same range at `0xFFFFFFFF80000000` for the kernel high address. Load CR3 with PML4 physical address. Set CR4.PAE and CR0.PG=1. Now the CPU is in long mode with paging.

The **APIC (Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller)** replaces the legacy 8259A PIC for multi-core systems. The **local APIC** (one per CPU, memory-mapped at 0xFEE00000) handles per-CPU timer interrupts, inter-processor interrupts (IPIs, used by the scheduler to tell other CPUs to reschedule), and spurious interrupts. The **I/O APIC** (global, memory-mapped at 0xFEC00000) routes hardware interrupts from devices to specific CPUs. Configuration via ACPI tables (MADT) or BIOS/UEFI. For a simple OS: disable the legacy PIC by masking all its interrupts, configure the local APIC timer to fire at a fixed rate, set up one IDT entry to handle the timer, and increment a tick counter. This is the basis for sleep, scheduling, and all time-based functionality.

resources.label: OSDev Wiki — Setting Up Long Mode
Intel SDM Vol. 3A — System Programming Guide (free PDF)

subtopics.title: Building a bare-metal OS: physical memory manager, scheduler, and drivers
body: With paging active and a working IDT/GDT, the next step is a **physical memory manager (PMM)**. The BIOS/UEFI provides a memory map (via `e820` on BIOS, `GetMemoryMap()` on UEFI) listing which physical address ranges are usable RAM, reserved (BIOS, ACPI), MMIO (memory-mapped I/O), or unusable. Parse this map and build a **bitmap allocator**: one bit per 4 KB page frame, 0=free, 1=used. Mark all non-RAM regions and your own kernel pages as used. `pmm_alloc_frame()` finds the first 0 bit and sets it. `pmm_free_frame(addr)` clears it. A more sophisticated PMM uses a buddy allocator for multi-page allocations. Stack-allocated arrays of free frames (a slab per size class) are the production approach.

A **virtual memory manager (VMM)** builds on the PMM. The VMM manages page tables: `vmm_map_page(virt, phys, flags)` allocates page table pages (from the PMM) as needed, sets the appropriate PML4/PDPT/PD/PT entry, and optionally invalidates the TLB entry (`INVLPG virt`). `vmm_unmap_page(virt)` clears the entry and flushes TLB. Each process gets its own PML4 (allocated from PMM); the kernel's page table region is shared across all processes (higher half of the virtual address space). Copying the kernel mappings into a new process's PML4 at fork time (or sharing them via PML4 entries pointing to shared PDPTs) ensures the kernel is always mapped.

A **preemptive scheduler** requires a timer interrupt (APIC or PIT), a run queue, and a context switch routine. At timer interrupt: save the current task's registers (on its kernel stack), pick the next runnable task (round-robin from a circular list), restore its registers, update CR3 if switching processes (page table switch), and `IRETQ` into it. The save/restore must preserve: all general-purpose registers, the instruction pointer and RFLAGS (saved by the CPU on interrupt), the stack pointer. The context switch function is a short assembly routine: push all GPRs onto the current stack, save the stack pointer into the current TCB, load the new TCB's stack pointer, pop registers, ret. The timer ISR does the decision; the context switch does the swap.

A minimal **keyboard driver** reads from the PS/2 controller I/O ports. Port `0x60` returns the scan code; port `0x64` is the status register. The keyboard IRQ (IRQ 1, mapped to IDT vector 33 after PIC remapping) triggers on each key press/release. The ISR reads `inb(0x60)` to get the scan code and translates via a scan-code table to ASCII. A circular ring buffer (producer: ISR; consumer: `getchar()`) decouples the ISR from the polling. The PS/2 controller is legacy and will be absent on modern UEFI systems — UEFI provides an HID (Human Interface Device) protocol over USB. **VGA text mode** (80×25 characters, accessible at physical address `0xB8000`) is the simplest display: write `(character | (color << 8))` as a 16-bit value to `0xB8000 + (row * 80 + col) * 2`. No driver needed, just a pointer write.

Once the OS boots to a prompt, the real work is adding system calls (`SYSCALL` entry point → dispatch table → implementations), a filesystem (start with a ramfs backed by a static byte array, or implement FAT32 on a block device), and a libc port. The recommended path for learning: Phil Opp's 'Writing an OS in Rust' and xv6 (RISC-V version, read both the code and the companion book). xv6 is ~10,000 lines of well-commented C implementing a complete UNIX subset (fork/exec, pipes, filesystem, shell, user programs). Reading and modifying every line of xv6 is the single highest-leverage activity for OS internals understanding. Build the bare-metal x86-64 path yourself to understand the architecture; read xv6 to understand the clean design of a working OS.

resources.label: Writing an OS in Rust — Phil Opp (blog_os)
Writing an OS in Rust — Phil Opp (blog_os)

connections.note: ELF, linker scripts and PLT/GOT are the linking material, kernel-side
Code generation + assembling end at the same ELF/loader the kernel runs
Vector tables, linker scripts and reset handlers are the embedded analogue of boot

topics.title: Virtualization, containers & eBPF
body: Hardware virtualization (Intel VT-x/AMD-V, VMCS, VM entry/exit), **KVM** (how Linux becomes a hypervisor), **QEMU** + virtio paravirtualization, and container isolation internals (seccomp, AppArmor, SELinux).
**eBPF** deserves special attention: a verified, JIT-compiled VM *inside* the kernel for tracing, networking (XDP), and security — one of the most important systems technologies of the decade.

subtopics.title: Hardware virtualization: Intel VT-x, VMCS, and VM entry/exit
body: Before hardware virtualization (pre-2005), running an OS inside another OS required **software emulation** (BOCHS, early QEMU) or **binary translation** (VMware's approach: scan guest code, rewrite privileged instructions to non-privileged equivalents before execution). Both approaches incurred 20-100× overhead. Intel's **VT-x** (Virtualization Technology for IA-32) and AMD's **AMD-V** (SVM, Secure Virtual Machine) extensions added a new CPU execution mode: **VMX non-root mode** (or `guest mode`). Guest code runs directly on the hardware CPU in this mode, at near-native speed, with hardware enforcing isolation — no binary translation needed.

The **VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure)** is a 4 KB memory region (per logical CPU, per VM) that the CPU uses to save and restore guest and host state on VM transitions. It contains three types of fields: **Guest state** (guest register values saved on VM exit and restored on VM entry: CR0, CR3, CR4, RFLAGS, RSP, RIP, segment registers, GDTR, IDTR, MSRs). **Host state** (hypervisor register values restored on VM exit: CR3, RSP, RIP pointing to the VM exit handler, segment selectors). **Control fields** (what causes a VM exit: which I/O ports, which MSR accesses, which CR writes, whether interrupts cause exits, etc.). The VMX instructions (`VMLAUNCH`, `VMRESUME`, `VMREAD`, `VMWRITE`) operate on the VMCS.

The VM lifecycle: `VMXON` enables VMX mode. The hypervisor allocates a VMCS, initializes it with `VMWRITE` (setting guest CS/RIP to the guest's entry point, guest CR3 to the guest's page table, host RIP to the exit handler address). `VMLAUNCH` (first launch) or `VMRESUME` (subsequent) enters the guest — the CPU loads guest state from the VMCS and executes guest code in VMX non-root mode. When the guest executes a privileged instruction (HLT, I/O port access, `CPUID` with certain inputs), accesses a restricted MSR, causes a page fault with EPT (Extended Page Tables), or triggers any other configured exit condition, the CPU performs a **VM exit**: saves guest state into the VMCS, loads host state, and jumps to the VM exit handler. The exit handler checks `VM_EXIT_REASON` from the VMCS, handles the cause (emulating a device, updating shadow state, scheduling the vCPU), and calls `VMRESUME` to continue the guest.

**EPT (Extended Page Tables)** solves the 'how does the guest's page table work?' problem without nested page fault overhead. Without EPT, the hypervisor maintains a **shadow page table** for each guest page table: when the guest modifies its CR3 or page tables, the hypervisor must intercept and update the shadow. This requires intercepting every guest page table write — expensive. EPT adds a **second-level address translation**: guest virtual → guest physical (via guest's own page tables) → host physical (via EPT, maintained by the hypervisor). Hardware walks both tables, requiring 24 memory accesses for a TLB miss (4 levels × (1 guest + 1 EPT) levels) but avoiding all page table interception overhead. The EPT is to VMs what the process page table is to processes: it maps the VM's 'physical' address space to host physical memory.

**VPID (Virtual Processor ID)** allows TLB entries to be tagged by which VM they belong to, preventing TLB flushes on VM entry/exit. Without VPID, every VM exit and entry requires a TLB flush (CR3 write), adding hundreds of cycles of latency per transition. VPID assigns each vCPU a unique ID; the TLB stores `(VPID, virtual address) → physical address` tuples so host and guest TLB entries coexist. Similarly, **ASID (Address Space ID)** on AMD provides the same benefit. These optimizations are the difference between VMs being 'close to native' and 'noticeably slower than native' for I/O-heavy workloads with frequent kernel/hypervisor transitions.

resources.label: Intel 64 and IA-32 Architectures Software Developer Manual — Vol. 3C, Ch. 23-33 (VMX)

subtopics.title: KVM and QEMU: how Linux becomes a hypervisor
body: **KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine)** is a Linux kernel module (`kvm.ko` + `kvm-intel.ko` or `kvm-amd.ko`) that converts the Linux kernel into a **Type-1 hypervisor** (bare-metal) while retaining full Linux functionality. KVM is not a separate OS; it augments the existing Linux scheduler, memory manager, and I/O subsystems. After loading `kvm.ko`, the kernel exposes `/dev/kvm`, a character device. User-space tools (QEMU, libvirt) interact with KVM entirely through ioctls on this device.

The KVM API: `open("/dev/kvm")` to get a KVM fd. `ioctl(kvmfd, KVM_CREATE_VM, 0)` creates a VM and returns a VM fd. `ioctl(vmfd, KVM_CREATE_VCPU, 0)` creates one virtual CPU and returns a vCPU fd. `mmap(vcpufd, KVM_RUN_SIZE)` maps the **`kvm_run` struct** — a shared memory region between kernel and user space that describes the reason for each VM exit. `ioctl(vcpufd, KVM_RUN, 0)` starts (or resumes) the vCPU. The ioctl blocks until a VM exit occurs; then the user-space handler reads `kvm_run->exit_reason` and handles it. For I/O exits (`KVM_EXIT_IO`), the handler reads the port/size/direction and emulates the device. For MMIO exits (`KVM_EXIT_MMIO`), similarly. This loop is QEMU's core: an infinite `ioctl(KVM_RUN)` → handle exit → `ioctl(KVM_RUN)` cycle, one per vCPU thread.

**QEMU** (Quick EMUlator) is the user-space complement to KVM. Without KVM, QEMU fully emulates a CPU in software (Tiny Code Generator, TCG: translates guest instructions to host instructions). With KVM, QEMU handles the non-CPU parts: device emulation (virtual NIC, virtual disk, USB controller, PCI bus, ACPI tables, firmware). QEMU models over 100 device types in software; each is a separate emulation running in user space on exit. Performance-critical devices use **virtio**: a paravirtualized device protocol where the guest knows it is in a VM and uses an optimized ring-buffer protocol instead of emulating legacy hardware. `virtio-net` (network), `virtio-blk` (block), `virtio-scsi`, `virtio-fs` all eliminate device emulation overhead for compatible guest kernels.

**`virtio`** is the I/O side of paravirtualization. The guest virtio driver places descriptors (buffer addresses + lengths) into a **virtqueue** (a ring buffer in shared memory visible to both guest and hypervisor). The guest writes to a 'kick' register (a specific memory write or PCI doorbell) to notify the host. The host QEMU thread wakes, processes the descriptors, performs the actual I/O (read from disk, inject a packet), and places completion descriptors in the used ring. The guest virtio driver processes completions and signals the upper layer. Round-trip: ~5 µs for virtio-net between VMs on the same host (vs. ~100 µs for TCP over a real NIC). `vhost-net` and `vhost-blk` move the host-side processing from QEMU user space into the Linux kernel (a kernel thread processes virtqueue kicks), halving the context switch overhead.

Live **VM migration** is one of KVM/QEMU's most impressive capabilities: moving a running VM from one physical host to another with <1 second downtime. The process: pre-copy dirty memory pages to the destination in a loop (while the VM continues running and dirtying pages); when the dirty page rate drops below a threshold, briefly pause the VM (milliseconds), send remaining dirty pages + CPU state + device state, and resume at the destination. KVM's dirty page tracking (`KVM_CAP_DIRTY_LOG`) marks written pages; QEMU's migration protocol handles the transfer. Live migration is how cloud providers move VMs for host maintenance without customer downtime — when AWS patches hypervisor hosts, tenants' VMs migrate automatically.

subtopics.title: Container security: seccomp, AppArmor, capabilities, and the threat model
body: Containers are not security boundaries — they are **operational isolation** mechanisms (see namespace isolation notes). The kernel is shared; a kernel exploit in a container compromises the host. For defense-in-depth, three additional mechanisms layer security on top of namespace isolation: **capabilities**, **seccomp**, and **LSM (Linux Security Modules)**.

**Capabilities** (POSIX.1e) divide root's privileges into ~40 discrete capabilities. `CAP_NET_ADMIN` allows network configuration. `CAP_SYS_PTRACE` allows `ptrace`. `CAP_SYS_MODULE` allows loading kernel modules. `CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE` bypasses file permissions. Containers by default run with a reduced capability set — Docker drops 14 capabilities including `CAP_SYS_MODULE`, `CAP_SYS_RAWIO`, `CAP_NET_ADMIN` from the default container set. The goal: even if a container process escalates to UID 0 within the namespace, it lacks the capabilities to perform dangerous operations. Check with `capsh --print` inside a container. **User namespace** mapping (e.g., container UID 0 → host UID 100000) means container root is not host root — even without capability restrictions, privilege-escalation bugs in the container cannot grant host root.

**seccomp (Secure Computing mode)** filters syscalls. `seccomp(SECCOMP_SET_MODE_FILTER, ...)` installs a BPF filter that the kernel evaluates on every syscall: `ALLOW`, `ERRNO(err)` (return an error), `KILL_PROCESS` (SIGKILL), or `TRAP` (SIGSYS). Docker's default seccomp profile allows 326 syscalls and blocks 44 dangerous ones: `ptrace` (prevents container escape via process inspection), `kexec_load` (no kernel replacement), `reboot`, `mount` (no mounting new filesystems), `pivot_root`, `create_module`, etc. The profile is a JSON file compiled to a BPF filter. Kubernetes pods can specify `seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault` (Docker default) or `Localhost` (custom). seccomp reduces the kernel attack surface: even if a container process triggers a kernel vulnerability, the exploitable syscall path may be blocked.

**AppArmor** and **SELinux** are LSM (Linux Security Module) frameworks that implement **mandatory access control (MAC)**: security policy set by the system administrator that cannot be overridden by process privileges. AppArmor uses pathname-based policy profiles: a profile for `containerd` restricts which files, capabilities, and network operations it may perform, independent of UID/capability checks. `aa-status` lists loaded profiles; `aa-complain` mode logs violations without blocking (for policy development); `aa-enforce` enforces. Docker's AppArmor profile for containers prevents writes to `/proc/sys/kernel`, access to host networking tools, and more. SELinux uses label-based policy (each file, process, and socket has a security label; policy rules determine which label-to-label transitions are allowed) — more powerful and more complex. Fedora and RHEL use SELinux; Ubuntu uses AppArmor.

The **container escape threat model** has several categories. **Kernel exploits**: unpatched CVEs in kernel code reachable via the allowed syscall set. Defense: keep kernels patched, minimal seccomp profile, capabilities dropped. **Image vulnerabilities**: vulnerabilities in the container's user-space code (app, libraries). Defense: minimal base images (distroless), image scanning, regular rebuilds. **Misconfigurations**: `--privileged` (disables all isolation), `--cap-add SYS_ADMIN`, `--pid=host`, `--net=host`, mounting host directories with write access. Defense: policy-as-code (OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno) rejecting insecure specs. **Supply chain**: malicious images or dependencies. Defense: image signing (Sigstore/Cosign), SBOM (Software Bill of Materials), artifact registries with scan gating. Understanding which of these vectors is relevant to your threat model determines which mitigations to prioritize — there is no single 'container security solution'.

subtopics.title: eBPF in depth: the verifier, JIT compiler, maps, and program types
body: **eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter)** is a general-purpose, safe, sandboxed execution environment inside the Linux kernel. Programs are written in a restricted C subset, compiled to eBPF bytecode by `clang -target bpf`, and loaded into the kernel via `bpf()` syscall. Before any program runs, the kernel's **eBPF verifier** performs static analysis to prove safety: the program must terminate (bounded loops only, max 1M instructions), never access memory out-of-bounds, never dereference null pointers, use only safe helper functions, and have valid register types at each instruction (no integer-as-pointer or pointer arithmetic violations). The verifier performs an exhaustive path simulation, tracking the type and range of each register value at each program point.

Verified programs are **JIT-compiled** to native machine code by `arch/x86/net/bpf_jit_comp.c` (and equivalents for ARM64, RISC-V, etc.). The JIT output is stored in an anonymous executable mapping and called directly. eBPF instruction overhead: for simple programs, a JIT-compiled eBPF program is within ~10-20% of hand-written native code. The JIT can be disabled by setting `net.core.bpf_jit_enable=0`, reverting to slower interpretation. JIT hardening (`net.core.bpf_jit_harden=2`) applies constant blinding (randomizing immediate values to prevent gadget construction) for defense against Spectre-via-JIT attacks.

**eBPF maps** are kernel data structures accessible from both eBPF programs (via helper calls) and user space (via file descriptors and `bpf(BPF_MAP_LOOKUP_ELEM, ...)` ioctls). Key types: `BPF_MAP_TYPE_HASH` (generic hash map, O(1) avg), `BPF_MAP_TYPE_ARRAY` (fixed-size array, O(1)), `BPF_MAP_TYPE_PERCPU_HASH` and `PERCPU_ARRAY` (per-CPU variants, no locking needed for write-heavy counters), `BPF_MAP_TYPE_LRU_HASH` (auto-evicts LRU entries), `BPF_MAP_TYPE_RINGBUF` (lock-free ring buffer, preferred for high-throughput event output from kernel to user space), `BPF_MAP_TYPE_PROG_ARRAY` (array of eBPF programs for **tail calls** — a lightweight `call` between eBPF programs that reuses the same stack frame). Maps persist across program invocations (pinned to `/sys/fs/bpf/` for cross-process sharing).

**eBPF program types** determine where programs attach and what context they receive. `BPF_PROG_TYPE_TRACEPOINT` attaches to static kernel tracepoints with a struct of tracepoint arguments as context. `BPF_PROG_TYPE_KPROBE` attaches to any kernel function entry/return via kprobes. `BPF_PROG_TYPE_PERF_EVENT` attaches to hardware/software perf events (CPU cycles, cache misses). `BPF_PROG_TYPE_XDP` attaches to the network driver receive path — the program receives a `xdp_md` struct with pointers to the raw packet start and end, and returns `XDP_PASS`/`DROP`/`TX`/`REDIRECT`. `BPF_PROG_TYPE_SCHED_CLS`/`ACT` attaches to the traffic control (tc) ingress/egress queues. `BPF_PROG_TYPE_LSM` attaches to LSM hooks (policy enforcement). `BPF_PROG_TYPE_SK_MSG` and `SK_SKB` operate on socket messages — used by service meshes (Cilium, Istio's eBPF data plane) to bypass the full network stack for pod-to-pod traffic on the same host.

The **CO-RE (Compile Once, Run Everywhere)** pattern (using `libbpf` + BTF — BPF Type Format) solves the eBPF portability problem: previously, eBPF programs compiled against one kernel's `vmlinux.h` would break on other kernels if struct layouts changed. CO-RE encodes relocation information in the eBPF object file; `libbpf` resolves struct field offsets against the *running* kernel's BTF (type information exposed via `/sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux`) at load time, patching the correct offsets. This means one compiled eBPF object works across kernel versions 4.18+ without recompilation — critical for distributing observability tools and security agents. `bpftool btf dump file /sys/kernel/btf/vmlinux` shows all kernel types with full field offsets for any running kernel.

resources.label: Cilium — eBPF-based networking and security for Kubernetes

subtopics.title: XDP and AF_XDP: kernel-bypass networking in the kernel
body: **XDP (eXpress Data Path)** hooks eBPF programs at the earliest point in the Linux network receive path: inside the driver's interrupt handler, before any kernel buffer allocation (`sk_buff`) for the packet. This is significant: normal packet processing starts with `alloc_skb()` which allocates a `sk_buff` (socket buffer) structure and copies or maps the DMA data — per-packet allocation overhead that limits throughput. XDP operates directly on the DMA buffer, avoiding this allocation. An XDP DROP (for DDoS mitigation) can be done in ~20ns per packet; a comparable iptables DROP rule takes ~200ns after full `sk_buff` allocation.

XDP supports three driver attachment modes. **Native XDP** (fastest): the eBPF program runs in the NIC driver's `napi_poll` callback, on the DMA buffer, before anything else. Requires driver support (most modern NIC drivers: Intel ixgbe, i40e, Mellanox mlx5, Broadcom bnxt). **Generic XDP** (fallback): runs after `sk_buff` allocation, in the network stack, without driver modifications. Provides the same API but without the performance benefit — useful for testing on any NIC. **Offloaded XDP**: the eBPF program runs *on the NIC's SmartNIC processor* (Netronome Agilio), offloading all processing from the host CPU. Achieves line rate at 100 Gbps on the NIC without any host CPU cycles for matched traffic.

**AF_XDP** (Address Family XDP) is a user-space zero-copy socket type that pairs with XDP. The NIC's RX ring buffer DMA area is mapped into user space; an XDP program redirects selected packets to a user-space `AF_XDP` socket via `XDP_REDIRECT`; user-space reads those packets directly from DMA memory without any copy. Write path is symmetric. The shared memory regions between user space, the XDP program, and the NIC are: **UMEM** (the packet buffer area), **RX ring** and **TX ring** (descriptor rings), **fill ring** and **completion ring** (buffer recycling). Applications using AF_XDP: `libxdp` (reference implementation), Vector Packet Processor (VPP), Suricata IDS, and custom high-frequency trading data feeds.

Comparing network stack bypass approaches: **DPDK (Data Plane Development Kit)** takes the most aggressive approach — completely bypasses the Linux kernel network stack by running a user-space poll-mode driver that directly controls the NIC. DPDK achieves the lowest possible latency (<1µs packet processing) but requires dedicated CPU cores (busy-polling), root access, and its own TCP/IP stack if needed. **RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access)** bypasses the CPU entirely for network transfers: the NIC reads from or writes to remote host memory directly, with the application specifying work requests to a hardware queue. **io_uring** (kernel) and **AF_XDP** occupy the middle ground: kernel-assisted, but with near-zero-copy and reduced syscall overhead. The right tool depends on latency requirements, CPU budget, and NIC capabilities.

Real-world eBPF/XDP deployments at scale: **Cloudflare** uses XDP for DDoS mitigation — their `Gatebot` system automatically synthesizes XDP programs from observed attack patterns and loads them into production within seconds, dropping >1 Tbps of attack traffic without reaching the Linux network stack. **Facebook (Meta)** uses Katran, an XDP-based L4 load balancer handling all TCP/UDP traffic to Facebook's servers. **Cilium** replaces kube-proxy (iptables-based Kubernetes service routing) with eBPF maps — dramatically reducing rule update time from O(rules) to O(1) as cluster size grows, and eliminating DNAT overhead via direct packet rewriting. These deployments demonstrate that eBPF/XDP has crossed from 'experimental' to 'critical production infrastructure' in the networking world.

resources.label: XDP paper — The eXpress Data Path (APNet 2018)

eBPF powers modern EDR/detection and sandboxing
VMs vs containers is the substrate of cloud deployment

title: Compilers & PLT
tagline: From lexing a calculator to designing a typed language with an optimizing backend.
overview: A compiler is a pipeline of total functions: source → tokens → AST → IR → machine code, each stage a clean transformation you can build and test in isolation. Learning it rewires how you see *all* programs — you start noticing grammars, type systems, and dataflow everywhere.

Programming-language theory (PLT) is the math underneath: grammars, type systems, semantics, and proofs of correctness. The combination is unusually transferable — it shows up in databases (query planners), ML (XLA/MLIR), security (symbolic execution), and blockchains (Solidity → EVM).

topics.title: Frontend: lexing, parsing, tree-walking
body: The pipeline mental model, then a working **tree-walking interpreter**: tokenizer → recursive-descent parser → AST → evaluate by walking the tree, with proper **environments and lexical scope**.
Nystrom's *Crafting Interpreters* is the canonical path — Part I builds a complete dynamically-typed language (Lox) and every concept lands as runnable code.

subtopics.title: The compiler pipeline mental model
body: A compiler is not a monolith; it is a **pipeline of total functions**, each with a single clearly-defined input type and output type. The canonical stages are: source text → **lexer** → token stream → **parser** → AST → **semantic analysis** → annotated AST → **IR lowering** → IR → **optimizer** → IR → **codegen** → machine code. Every stage is independently testable: you can feed a lexer a string and inspect its token output, feed a parser a token stream and print the resulting AST, and so on. This composability is not an accident of good engineering — it is a structural property that falls out of keeping each pass's input and output types clean.

Understanding why each stage exists pays dividends immediately. The **lexer** exists because parsing character-by-character is extremely tedious and conflates two distinct concerns: recognizing lexical units (identifiers, numbers, operators) and recognizing their structure (expressions, statements, declarations). Clean separation makes both simpler. The **parser** exists to impose hierarchical structure — a flat token stream has no notion of 'this `*` binds tighter than that `+`'; the parser produces an AST in which precedence is structural. **Semantic analysis** exists because grammatically valid programs can be semantically nonsensical: `x + true` might parse fine but should be rejected. Each stage narrows the space of valid inputs from syntactically correct to semantically meaningful to finally executable.

The distinction between **interpretation** and **compilation** is a point on a spectrum, not a binary. A tree-walking interpreter evaluates the AST directly with no separate codegen step; it is slow but trivially correct. A bytecode compiler lowers the AST to a compact instruction sequence (like CPython's `.pyc`) which a separate VM executes; faster, because dispatch over a flat instruction array beats pointer-chasing through the AST. A native-code compiler lowers further to machine instructions — fast execution but a much harder backend. Many real systems combine stages: V8 interprets bytecode first, then JIT-compiles hot functions to native code. The conceptual pipeline is the same at every level; only the output IR changes.

Your first build target should be a **tree-walking interpreter for a simple expression language** — arithmetic, comparison, if-else, variables, and function calls. This is achievable in a few hundred lines in any language and immediately gives you the full pipeline experience: you write a lexer, watch it tokenize `(1 + 2) * 3`, build a recursive-descent parser, produce an AST, walk it recursively to evaluate. Once the interpreter works you understand every stage viscerally, which makes everything downstream (bytecode VMs, type checkers, optimizers) feel like a natural extension rather than a foreign subject. *Crafting Interpreters* (Nystrom) does this exactly, producing a complete dynamically-typed language called Lox in Part I — every concept introduced is immediately grounded in runnable, debuggable code.

subtopics.title: Lexical analysis: tokenizing source text
body: The lexer (also called a **scanner** or **tokenizer**) reads source text one character at a time and emits a sequence of **tokens** — typed, categorized chunks. A token has a type (IDENT, NUMBER, PLUS, IF, …), a lexeme (the raw substring), and optionally a literal value (the parsed integer or float, or the resolved string). The lexer discards whitespace and comments, handles escape sequences inside strings, and identifies keywords (reserved identifiers like `if`, `while`, `return`) by recognizing them after reading an identifier. This is a compact problem with a clean boundary: in, out is a token stream.

The formal model for a lexer is a **deterministic finite automaton (DFA)** or, equivalently, a set of regular expressions. Each token type corresponds to a regular language — `[0-9]+` for integer literals, `[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*` for identifiers, `"[^"]*"` for strings — and the lexer is effectively running all these regexes in parallel, advancing through the input and consuming the longest match (the **maximal munch** rule). Tools like `lex`/`flex` compile a set of regex rules to a DFA table and generate a lexer in C automatically. Hand-written lexers implement the same DFA logic explicitly as a `switch` or `if-else` chain; they are faster in practice because they avoid the table-lookup indirection and can handle corner cases (e.g., `<<` in C++ generics) that DFA-based tools struggle with.

Practical concerns that textbooks understate: **error tokens** (report an illegal character and continue rather than aborting), **multi-character lookahead** (does `=` end here, or is this `==`?), **string interning** (identical identifiers should produce the same object, so later passes can compare by pointer), and **source positions** (attach line/column to every token for error messages — this is cheap here but invaluable in every later pass). A common implementation is to represent the entire source as an immutable string, have tokens hold a start offset and length into it, and derive line/column lazily from a line table when needed for errors.

One sharp edge: the lexer is **context-free** (it does not know whether an identifier is a variable or a keyword in this position), but real languages sometimes require contextual lexing. In Python, INDENT/DEDENT tokens are synthesized by the lexer based on indentation depth — a stack-based state machine layered on top of the basic character scanner. In C++, `>>` inside template arguments must be tokenized as two `>` tokens, not a `>>` right-shift, so the lexer defers to the parser's context. In Rust, lifetime annotations `'a` share the character `'` with character literals; the lexer peeks ahead to disambiguate. Every real language has these wrinkles; knowing the DFA model tells you when you're extending it and why.

resources.label: Crafting Interpreters — Scanning (chapter 4, full text free)

subtopics.title: Recursive-descent parsing
body: A **recursive-descent parser** is a hand-written top-down parser that directly encodes the grammar as mutually recursive functions: one function per non-terminal, each consuming tokens from the token stream. The grammar `expr → term (('+' | '-') term)*; term → factor (('*' | '/') factor)*; factor → NUMBER | '(' expr ')'` maps to three functions `parseExpr()`, `parseTerm()`, `parseFactor()` where each calls the others. This translation is mechanical once you can read a grammar, and the resulting code is easy to understand, modify, and extend — which is why all production-quality compilers hand-write their parsers rather than generating them.

The key decision at each point in the grammar is **which production to apply**, made by looking at the **current token** (and sometimes a small lookahead). For a `factor`, if the current token is `(`, recurse into `parseExpr()` and consume the closing `)`. If it is a `NUMBER`, consume it and return a literal node. Otherwise, report a syntax error. This lookahead logic is `LL(1)`: Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1-token lookahead. Most grammar constructs in real languages are LL(1); the exceptions (primarily **left recursion** and **ambiguity**) require special handling.

**Left recursion** is the classic pitfall: the grammar `expr → expr '+' term | term` has recursive-descent parse `parseExpr()` calling `parseExpr()` immediately, looping forever. The standard fix is **left-factoring** — rewrite the grammar in iterative form (`expr → term (('+') term)*`) and implement the repetition as a loop rather than a recursive call. The parse tree is equivalent; the code is a `while` loop. Pratt parsing (covered in the Parsing Theory topic) eliminates this rewrite by making operator precedence and associativity explicit as integer **binding powers** rather than as grammar structure.

The output of parsing is an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — a tree of typed nodes, one per grammar construct, that records structure but omits syntactic noise (no parentheses nodes, no comma tokens). A typical AST node hierarchy: `Expr` (supertype) → `BinaryExpr { op, left, right }`, `UnaryExpr { op, operand }`, `LiteralExpr { value }`, `VarExpr { name }`, `CallExpr { callee, args }`. For statements: `Stmt` → `ExprStmt`, `VarDecl`, `IfStmt`, `WhileStmt`, `ReturnStmt`, `Block`. The separation into Expr and Stmt is meaningful — expressions produce values, statements produce effects. Collapsing that distinction (like Rust and Kotlin do, making `if` an expression) is a deliberate language design choice with downstream consequences in the type checker.

Error recovery is what separates a toy parser from a useful one. When a syntax error occurs, a production-quality parser should **not** stop at the first error — it should skip tokens until it finds a **synchronization point** (a `;`, a `}`, a keyword that starts a statement) and then resume parsing, potentially finding many more errors in one pass. The simplest scheme (**panic mode**) tracks a set of safe tokens to skip to; more sophisticated schemes (used in Clang, rust-analyzer) use structured error recovery and can often produce the correct AST even in the presence of local errors. Getting error recovery right is tedious but enormously valuable for IDE tooling, where the user's code is almost always syntactically incomplete.

resources.label: Crafting Interpreters — Parsing Expressions (chapter 6, full text)
Matklad — Error Resilient Parsing in rust-analyzer

subtopics.title: AST design and the expression hierarchy
body: The **Abstract Syntax Tree** is the central data structure of the compiler frontend. Its design deeply influences every later pass: a poor AST design makes visitors verbose, type checking awkward, and code generation error-prone, while a clean AST makes each pass read like a direct statement of its semantics. The key design tension is between **expressiveness** (can the AST represent all programs?) and **precision** (does it make invalid states unrepresentable?). For example, `CallExpr { callee: Expr, args: Vec<Expr> }` is expressive but allows `callee` to be a literal number — you need the semantic analysis pass to reject that. A stricter design `CallExpr { callee: PlaceExpr, args: Vec<Expr> }` rejects it structurally but requires the parser to decide which expressions are valid callees without type information. Both approaches are valid; the choice trades off where complexity lives.

The **Visitor pattern** is the standard way to separate operations from the AST node hierarchy. Without it, adding a new operation (say, a pretty-printer) would require modifying every node class. With Visitor, you define an interface `ExprVisitor<T>` with one `visit` method per node type, and each node implements `accept(visitor)` by calling the right `visit` method on the visitor. This is **double dispatch**: the runtime type of the node dispatches to the right `accept`, which then dispatches to the right `visit` on the visitor. Each compiler pass (resolver, type checker, code generator) is one visitor implementation, and adding a new pass adds a new visitor class with no changes to the node hierarchy. The tradeoff: adding a new *node type* is painful (you must add it to every visitor), while adding a new *operation* is cheap. This is exactly the **Expression Problem** from PLT, and it is why some language workbenches use algebraic data types (pattern matching handles both dimensions cleanly).

**Source location tracking** is a cross-cutting concern that is much easier to get right during AST design than to retrofit later. Every AST node should carry a span (start and end source position). When a type error is reported, the user sees the exact expression that caused it highlighted in their editor — this requires that the expression node carries its position. In practice, positions are often stored as byte offsets into the source string (not line/column, which are derived), and a separate table maps offset ranges to file/line/column for display. Some compilers (like rustc) use a globally unique span ID that encodes macro expansion history, allowing error messages to show the expansion chain — this is complex but very user-friendly.

A note on **concrete syntax trees (CSTs) vs abstract syntax trees (ASTs)**. A CST (also called a parse tree or syntax tree) is a lossless representation: every token, including whitespace, comments, semicolons, and parentheses, is present. An AST discards syntactic noise and keeps only semantic structure. For a compiler backend, an AST is what you want. But for an IDE, a formatter, or a refactoring tool, a CST is essential — you cannot reformat code without knowing where the comments are. Tools like `rust-analyzer` (using the `rowan` crate) and `tree-sitter` use concrete, immutable, error-tolerant syntax trees precisely for this reason. Understanding the tradeoff explains why compiler frontends and IDE language servers tend to build their own distinct tree representations even for the same language.

resources.label: Crafting Interpreters — Representing Code (chapter 5, AST node hierarchy)

subtopics.title: Environments, lexical scope, and closures
body: **Scope** determines where in the program each name is visible. **Lexical scope** (also called **static scope**) means that the scope of a name is determined by its position in the *source text*, not by the runtime call stack. When you see an identifier `x`, the compiler looks it up in the statically enclosing context — the function that textually contains the reference, then the function containing *that*, and so on out to the global scope. This is the scoping rule in virtually all modern languages (Python, JavaScript, Rust, Go, Haskell) because it makes names predictable without running the program.

The data structure for tracking scopes at runtime is an **environment**: a mapping from names to values (or to storage locations). In a tree-walking interpreter, each environment is a linked list of frames: the current frame holds the current scope's bindings, and its `enclosing` pointer links to the outer scope. Variable lookup walks this chain. Assignment updates the frame where the variable was defined. Function call pushes a new frame whose enclosing pointer is the **definition environment** of the function (not the call site) — this is what lexical scope means mechanically.

**Closures** arise naturally from this: a closure is a function bundled with its definition environment. When a nested function captures a variable from an outer scope, and the outer function returns, the captured variable cannot live on the stack (the stack frame is gone). The closure must keep the environment alive. In languages with first-class closures (JavaScript, Python, Rust, Go), the captured variables are promoted to the **heap** — an optimization called **closure conversion** or **lambda lifting**. Lua's implementation uses **upvalues**: while the outer function is still active, the upvalue is an open reference to the stack slot; when the outer function returns, the upvalue is **closed** (the current stack value is copied to the heap and the upvalue now points there). This is an elegant solution that avoids heap allocation in the common case where the closure does not outlive the enclosing scope.

Distinguishing **shadowing** from **mutation** is a subtlety beginners trip over. In a language with block scope, `let x = 1; { let x = 2; }` introduces a *new binding* in the inner scope that shadows (but does not overwrite) the outer `x`. After the block, `x` is still `1`. In contrast, `let mut x = 1; { x = 2; }` *mutates* the single binding. The difference matters for closures: a closure capturing the outer `x` in the shadowing case retains `1`; in the mutation case, it sees `2`. Getting this right in the resolver pass — the pass that resolves names to their definitions before evaluation — is essential for correct semantics, and it is a surprisingly common source of subtle bugs in both language implementations and in user code.

The **resolver pass** in a tree-walking interpreter (chapter 11 of *Crafting Interpreters*) walks the AST *before* evaluation and annotates each variable reference with the exact **distance** (number of enclosing environments to hop) to the defining frame. At evaluation time, variable lookup becomes an $O(1)$ indexed access into the correct frame rather than a string-keyed chain walk. This is a micro-version of the same idea in bytecode compilers: each local variable is assigned a fixed **slot index** at compile time, and the VM accesses locals by slot, making variable access a single array index — fast and with no heap allocation for the lookup.

resources.label: Crafting Interpreters — Resolving and Binding (chapter 11, free)
Crafting Interpreters — Part I (free, full text)
Writing An Interpreter In Go — Thorsten Ball

connections.note: Lexing is finite-automaton string matching — the theory you met in DSA
The AST you build here is the input to bytecode codegen

topics.title: Parsing theory
body: Formal parsing: **LL(k)** (why left recursion breaks recursive descent), **LR/LALR** (shift-reduce, what yacc/bison build), and **Pratt parsing** (top-down operator precedence — the cleanest way to handle expressions). Plus ambiguity (dangling else) and error recovery.
Pratt parsing in particular is a tool you'll reach for again and again whenever you need to parse expressions with precedence.

subtopics.title: Context-free grammars and the Chomsky hierarchy
body: A **context-free grammar (CFG)** consists of a set of **terminals** (tokens), **non-terminals** (grammar variables), a **start symbol**, and **productions** (rewrite rules of the form `A → α` where `A` is a non-terminal and `α` is a string of terminals and non-terminals). A CFG generates a **language** — the set of strings derivable from the start symbol by repeatedly replacing non-terminals with their production right-hand sides. The **Chomsky hierarchy** classifies formal grammars by expressive power: regular (recognized by DFA) ⊂ context-free (recognized by pushdown automaton) ⊂ context-sensitive ⊂ unrestricted. Lexers use regular grammars; parsers use context-free grammars; most real programming languages are context-free (with a few context-sensitive extensions handled ad hoc).

A **derivation** is a sequence of rewrite steps from the start symbol to a terminal string. A **leftmost derivation** always expands the leftmost non-terminal first; a **rightmost derivation** always expands the rightmost. For every ambiguous grammar, a string can have two distinct leftmost derivations, producing two different **parse trees**. The classic example is the **dangling else**: `if E then if E then S else S` — the `else` could attach to either `if`. Grammars in textbooks and formal specifications are often ambiguous for readability; a parser must impose a specific derivation strategy to resolve the ambiguity. The conventional resolution is **else-closest-if** (the else binds to the innermost unmatched if), enforced not by the grammar but by the parser's behavior.

The **parse tree** (or concrete syntax tree) records every symbol in the derivation. The **abstract syntax tree** discards syntactic sugar — parentheses, semicolons, commas, keyword tokens — keeping only the semantically meaningful structure. For the expression `(1 + 2) * 3`, the parse tree has a node for `(`, `1`, `+`, `2`, `)`, `*`, `3` and the grammar non-terminals between them; the AST has a `BinaryExpr(*, BinaryExpr(+, 1, 2), 3)`. Compilers work on ASTs; formatters and IDEs often need the full concrete syntax tree (as discussed in the Frontend topic).

Two properties of a CFG are critical for parser construction. The **FIRST set** of a string `α` is the set of terminals that can begin a string derivable from `α` (plus `ε` if `α` can derive the empty string). The **FOLLOW set** of a non-terminal `A` is the set of terminals that can appear immediately after `A` in any sentential form. These sets are computed by a fixed-point algorithm over the grammar productions and are the foundation of both LL parsing tables and LR lookahead computation. Building them by hand for a small grammar is a good exercise; every parser generator computes them automatically.

resources.label: Stanford CS143 — grammars, FIRST/FOLLOW, parse trees (lecture notes)
Crafting Interpreters — Appendix I: Lox grammar (a clean real-world CFG)

subtopics.title: LL(k) parsing: predictive parsing and its limits
body: An **LL(k) parser** scans the input **L**eft-to-right, constructs a **L**eftmost derivation, and uses **k** tokens of lookahead to decide which production to apply. In practice k=1 suffices for most language constructs; recursive descent is LL(1) in spirit even when it peeks more than one token. The **parsing table** for an LL(1) grammar is a two-dimensional array indexed by [non-terminal][lookahead token] → production; the entry is unique iff the grammar has no LL(1) conflicts. A grammar is LL(1) iff for every pair of productions for the same non-terminal, their FIRST sets are disjoint (and at most one can derive ε).

**Left recursion** breaks LL parsing immediately. If `A → A α | β`, the parser tries to apply `A → A α`, which immediately tries to apply `A → A α` again, looping infinitely before consuming any input. The standard fix is **left-recursion elimination**: rewrite `A → A α | β` as `A → β A'; A' → α A' | ε`. The resulting grammar is right-recursive and iterative, producing the same language but in a form a recursive-descent parser can handle. A mechanical algorithm generalizes this to mutual left recursion across multiple non-terminals. Note that the transformation changes the *structure* of the parse tree (from left-branching to right-branching) which can affect attribute evaluation order — something to be aware of when the grammar directly encodes associativity.

**Left factoring** is a separate transformation needed when two productions for the same non-terminal share a common prefix. If `A → α β | α γ`, the parser seeing `α` cannot decide which production to use. Left factoring pulls out the common prefix: `A → α A'; A' → β | γ`. After factoring, the choice between `β` and `γ` is deferred until the parser has consumed `α` and can look at what comes next. Both left-recursion elimination and left factoring are mechanical but can produce verbose, unintuitive grammars — one reason serious compilers do not use grammar-generator tools for expression parsing and instead use Pratt parsing.

The fundamental limitation of LL(k) parsing is that it must commit to a production by looking only at the *next k tokens* (the prefix of what remains). This makes it impossible to parse certain natural grammar constructs without unlimited lookahead. The textbook example is the **C declaration ambiguity**: `x * y;` could be a multiplication expression-statement or a declaration of `y` as a pointer to `x`; distinguishing them requires knowing whether `x` is a type name, which is not determinable from tokens alone. C compilers handle this by maintaining a type-name symbol table in the parser — a context-sensitive hack layered onto an otherwise context-free framework. Similarly, `foo<T>` in C++ can be a comparison expression `foo < T >` or a template instantiation — the C++ grammar is not context-free and requires the parser to carry type information.

resources.label: Stanford CS143 — LL parsing, FIRST/FOLLOW, parse tables (slides)

subtopics.title: LR parsing: shift-reduce and the automaton
body: **LR(k) parsing** scans **L**eft-to-right, constructs a **R**ightmost derivation in reverse, and uses k lookahead tokens. It is strictly more powerful than LL: every LL(k) grammar is LR(k), but not vice versa, and LR parsers can handle all context-free grammars of practical interest. The central insight is **deferred commitment**: an LR parser does not decide which production to apply until it has seen the entire right-hand side, using a **stack** to remember what it has accumulated so far. This eliminates the left-recursion and left-factoring problems entirely — the parser handles `expr → expr + term` perfectly because by the time it commits to that production it has already parsed the full `expr + term` right-hand side.

The mechanics: an LR parser has two operations. **Shift** pops the next input token and pushes it (along with a parser state) onto the stack. **Reduce** recognizes that the top of the stack matches some production right-hand side `A → α`, pops `|α|` symbols, and pushes `A` (and a new state). The choice between shift and reduce at each step is determined by a finite automaton — the **LR automaton** — whose states represent sets of **LR items** (productions with a dot marking progress: `A → α • β` means we have seen `α` and are expecting `β`). The automaton is constructed from the grammar by computing **closure** (adding items for productions of the next expected non-terminal) and **GOTO** (moving the dot over a symbol). The result is a table-driven parser with $O(n)$ time where n is the input length.

There are three main LR variants with increasing power. **SLR(1)** (Simple LR) resolves reduce conflicts using FOLLOW sets — it can falsely claim a conflict if the grammar is not SLR but is still LR. **LALR(1)** (Lookahead LR, used by yacc/bison) merges states with the same item sets but different lookaheads and is more precise — it handles almost all practical grammars. **LR(1)** (canonical LR) computes lookaheads per item set separately — most powerful but produces a much larger automaton (often 10x the states of LALR). Modern parser generators like Menhir and bison use LALR; Knuth's original paper established LR(1).

**Shift-reduce conflicts** occur when the parser cannot decide whether to shift the next token onto the stack or reduce the current stack top. The **dangling else** produces a classic shift-reduce conflict: after parsing `if E then S`, with `else` as lookahead, should the parser reduce `if E then S` to a complete if-statement (shift-else goes with an outer if), or shift `else` to start the else-branch of this if? The conventional resolution — shift, so `else` binds to the nearest `if` — is baked into parser generator defaults. **Reduce-reduce conflicts** occur when two different productions both match the current stack, and are almost always a grammar design error that must be fixed by restructuring the grammar rather than relying on conflict-resolution heuristics.

resources.label: Engineering a Compiler — Cooper & Torczon, chapter 3 (LR parsing)
Menhir reference manual (industrial LALR(1) parser generator for OCaml)

subtopics.title: Pratt parsing: top-down operator precedence
body: **Pratt parsing** (Vaughan Pratt, 1973) is a top-down parsing technique that handles operator precedence and associativity elegantly without left-recursion elimination or grammar transformation. It assigns every token two integer **binding powers**: a **left binding power (lbp)** measuring how tightly the token binds to its left operand, and a **null denotation / led** function encoding what to do when the token appears as an infix or postfix operator. The central loop: parse a left operand, then keep consuming operators whose lbp exceeds the current **minimum binding power**, with each operator calling its own led to recursively parse the right operand at the right precedence. This naturally handles `1 + 2 * 3` correctly: `*` has higher lbp than `+`, so after parsing `2`, the `*` consumes it before the `+` gets a chance.

Concretely, a Pratt parser has two functions per token category: **nud** (null denotation, used when the token appears at the start of an expression: unary operators, literals, parenthesized groups) and **led** (left denotation, used when it appears in infix/postfix position). For `+`, the nud is undefined (you cannot begin an expression with `+` in most languages), the led parses its right operand at `lbp(+)` — giving left-associativity — or at `lbp(+) - 1` — giving right-associativity (used for `^` exponentiation and `=` assignment). A right-paren has nud that parses a grouped expression and no led; a prefix `-` has nud that parses its operand at a high precedence (unary minus binds tight) and no led. The entire expression parser is about 30 lines of code and handles the full precedence/associativity table for a language like C.

The elegance of Pratt parsing over grammar-rewriting approaches lies in **local reasoning**: precedence is a property of each operator token, not of the grammar structure. Adding a new operator requires only giving it binding powers and a led function — no grammar rule rewrite, no conflict resolution, no recomputing FIRST/FOLLOW sets. This is why Pratt parsing is the choice for expression parsing in production compilers: GCC, rustc, the Go compiler, Clang (for precedence-climbing, a close variant), and V8's parser all use variants of it. The formal equivalence between Pratt parsing, operator-precedence grammars, and precedence-climbing is well-established; the practical advantage is code clarity.

A subtle but important extension: **mixfix** operators and **ternary** expressions. The C ternary `E ? E : E` can be handled in the Pratt framework by giving `?` a led that parses the then-branch at precedence 0 (allowing any expression), then expects `:`, then parses the else-branch at the ternary's own precedence. Similarly, array indexing `a[i]` has `[` as an infix operator whose led parses a subscript expression and expects `]`. Function calls `f(args)` have `(` as an infix operator whose led collects comma-separated arguments and expects `)`. Once you see expressions-within-operators as just recursive calls with a specific minimum binding power, nearly all complex expression syntax falls naturally into the Pratt framework without special cases.

resources.label: Matklad — Simple but Powerful Pratt Parsing (the definitive tutorial)
Crafting Interpreters — Compiling Expressions (Pratt in C, chapter 17)

subtopics.title: Error recovery in parsers
body: A parser used only to accept or reject a program can stop at the first error. A compiler used by a developer must report *as many errors as possible* in one pass — stopping at the first error forces them to iterate through the build cycle for each mistake. **Error recovery** is the mechanism by which the parser continues after a syntax error, finding a consistent state from which parsing can meaningfully resume. The quality of error recovery is one of the biggest practical differences between an academic parser exercise and a production-quality compiler.

**Panic mode** is the simplest recovery strategy: when an error is detected, the parser skips tokens until it finds one in a designated set of **synchronization tokens** (typically statement terminators like `;` or block boundaries like `}` or `end`), then resumes parsing at the next statement. Bison/yacc implement this as the `error` pseudo-token in the grammar: you write `stmt: stmt ';' | error ';'`, which tells the parser to skip to `;` when a statement fails. Panic mode is easy to implement but produces poor error messages and may skip large parts of the input, missing errors beyond the panic boundary.

**Phrase-level recovery** is more targeted: the parser inserts or deletes a single token to make the current production succeed. If the parser expected `=` and sees `;`, it can pretend `=` was present and issue a message 'missing `=`'. If it sees `==` in an assignment context, it can insert a correction. This produces better messages but requires the parser designer to anticipate common mistakes and manually specify the recovery action for each production — tedious but very user-friendly (the Java compiler's missing-semicolon diagnostic has this character).

Modern production parsers (Clang, rustc, rust-analyzer) use **structured error recovery**: the parser speculatively tries multiple alternatives, backtracks on failure, and always produces *some* AST — possibly containing special **error nodes** — that downstream passes can process. rustc's error recovery is famous for producing suggestions like 'help: you might have meant to write `==` instead of `=`'; these require the parser to understand the likely intent and have semantic context. The trend in language tooling is toward **error-resilient parsing** where the parser always succeeds in producing a tree, even for completely invalid input, so that IDE features (autocomplete, hover types, go-to-definition) work on partially-complete code. Tree-sitter is built entirely around this principle.

resources.label: Matklad — Resilient LL Parsing (error recovery in rust-analyzer)
Matklad — Simple but Powerful Pratt Parsing

connections.note: CYK parsing is dynamic programming; grammar complexity uses the same analysis
Browsers parse HTML/CSS with error-recovering parsers built on this theory

topics.title: Semantic analysis & type checking
body: Scoped **symbol tables**, the static/dynamic and nominal/structural distinctions, **type checking** via attribute grammars (synthesized vs inherited attributes), and the **visitor pattern** to separate passes from the AST.
This is where 'is this program meaningful?' gets answered, distinct from 'is it grammatically correct?'.

subtopics.title: Symbol tables and scope management
body: A **symbol table** is a mapping from names to **symbol records** — data structures carrying everything the compiler knows about a named entity: its kind (variable, function, type, parameter), its type, its storage location or slot index, and whether it has been initialized. The parser produces a flat token stream and an AST; the semantic analysis phase populates the symbol table, then uses it to annotate each name reference in the AST with a pointer to the correct symbol record. Every subsequent pass — type checking, code generation, optimization — reads symbol records rather than looking up names by string, making those passes fast and correct.

Scope is implemented by making the symbol table a **stack of scopes**. When the semantic analyzer enters a block (`{`), it pushes a new empty scope frame. When it exits (`}`), it pops and discards that frame (or archivally detaches it, if the language has closures that keep it alive). Name lookup searches from the top of the stack downward — innermost scope first — returning the first match. Name insertion goes into the current top frame. This straightforwardly handles **shadowing**: a new binding in an inner scope hides (but does not delete) the same name in an outer scope. A common pitfall in implementation: forgetting to search all the way to the global scope when looking up a name, or accidentally inserting a new binding into the wrong scope frame.

Languages differ significantly in their scoping rules. **C** has **block scope** for local variables (declared anywhere in a block, visible from the declaration point to the end of the block) and file scope for globals. **JavaScript** (pre-ES6) has **function scope** for `var` declarations — visible throughout the entire function, not just from the declaration point — which is the source of the infamous hoisting behavior (`var x = 1` in the middle of a function acts as if `var x` was at the top). **Python** has LEGB scope lookup (Local → Enclosing function → Global → Built-in). **Rust** has block scope and a distinct **use-before-declare** rule — you cannot use a variable before its `let` binding, and re-binding with `let` in the same scope creates a new shadow (not mutation).

**Forward references** complicate single-pass symbol table construction. In Java and C#, a class body can reference a method defined later in the same class, so a single top-to-bottom pass cannot resolve all names. The standard solution is a **two-pass** approach: pass 1 scans all declarations (without resolving bodies) to populate the symbol table with the names of all classes, methods, and fields; pass 2 resolves references in expression and statement bodies against the now-complete table. C avoids this with **header files**: declarations must appear before uses in the translation unit, and the `#include` mechanism lets you pull in forward declarations. Mutual recursion between functions in C is handled by explicitly writing a forward declaration `void foo();` before the definition.

resources.label: Engineering a Compiler — Cooper & Torczon, chapter 5 (symbol tables)
Crafting Interpreters — Resolving and Binding (the resolver pass)

subtopics.title: Attribute grammars: decorating the AST
body: An **attribute grammar** augments a context-free grammar by associating **attributes** (computed values) with each grammar symbol and **semantic rules** specifying how to compute each attribute in terms of other attributes on the same production. The two kinds of attribute: a **synthesized attribute** of a node `A` is computed from the attributes of `A`'s children — it flows *up* the parse tree. An **inherited attribute** of a node `A` is computed from the attribute of `A`'s parent or siblings — it flows *down* the parse tree. A grammar where all attributes are synthesized is an **S-attributed grammar** and can be evaluated in a single bottom-up pass; a grammar with inherited attributes may require multiple passes or a more complex evaluation strategy.

The classic example of a synthesized attribute is **type propagation**: `BinaryExpr(+, left, right)` has a synthesized type attribute computed from `left.type` and `right.type` by the rule `if left.type == int && right.type == int then result.type = int; else if …`. The classic example of an inherited attribute is **expected type**: in `f(x, y)`, the expected types of the arguments are inherited from the function signature — the symbol table entry for `f` — and flow down into the argument expressions to guide type checking or coercion. In practice, compilers implement attribute evaluation not by generating an evaluator from a formal attribute grammar specification but by writing explicit visitor passes that thread the relevant information through the tree.

The **visitor pattern** (discussed in the Frontend topic) is the practical mechanism for implementing attribute grammar evaluation. A type-checking visitor carries a `TypeEnvironment` (mapping names to types, essentially the type-level symbol table) as state, visits each AST node, computes the node's type, and either annotates the node or returns the type as the visitor method's return value. The synthesized-attribute flow corresponds to bottom-up visitors (the visitor returns a value up from children to parent); the inherited-attribute flow corresponds to the environment passed down as a parameter. Most real type checkers mix both: the environment is threaded down as a parameter (inherited), and the computed type is returned up (synthesized).

A subtle design question: should the type checker **mutate** the AST nodes to attach type annotations, or should it build a separate **type map** (AST node → type) without touching the AST? Mutating the AST is convenient (the code generator can read the type directly from each expression node) but makes the AST stateful and less clean. A separate type map preserves AST immutability and is often the right choice when the same AST is reused across multiple compilation units or when incremental compilation requires precise invalidation.
