Alternatively, the graph is passed to **Triton** for GPU compilation. The key feature of torch.compile is **selective compilation**: Python code that cannot be traced is executed eagerly (with a graph break), while traced portions are compiled. This allows gradual adoption — existing PyTorch models get speedups without rewriting.

**Triton** (OpenAI, 2019) is a language and compiler for GPU kernel programming that sits between CUDA C (too low-level — you manage thread blocks, shared memory, bank conflicts manually) and library calls (too rigid — you can only use pre-written CUDA kernels). Triton operates at the **blocked program tile** level: you write a program that operates on blocks of data (specified as tensors with explicit block sizes), and the Triton compiler lowers it to an efficient CUDA kernel, automatically handling memory coalescing, shared memory tiling, and thread scheduling. The result: a Triton attention kernel written in ~50 lines matches hand-optimized CUDA in performance and is far more readable. Flash Attention v2 is implemented in Triton; most of the CUDA kernels in modern LLM inference runtimes (vLLM, SGLang) are Triton kernels.

The broader pattern these systems illustrate: **ML computation is a new kind of program**, with properties (shape-complete, side-effect-free tensor dataflow graphs) that make aggressive whole-program compilation tractable — things that general-purpose compilers cannot do because general programs have unbounded side effects and unknown shapes. MLIR provides the infrastructure (dialects for each level, lowering passes, polyhedral optimization), XLA/Triton provide the high-level interfaces, and LLVM/CUDA PTX provide the final target. Understanding this stack — from a PyTorch matmul through Torch-MLIR through LLVM to PTX — is the intersection of compilers and ML systems that drives most of the current ML infrastructure engineering work.

connections.note: MLIR/XLA/Triton lower ML graphs to GPU code through LLVM-family IRs
torch.compile and XLA are compiler passes over the ML computation graph
Noir/Circom compile high-level circuits down to constraint systems like a compiler backend

topics.title: Runtime systems: GC & JIT
body: Garbage collection in depth (tri-color mark-sweep, write barriers, generational/copying/concurrent GC: G1, ZGC, Shenandoah), **JIT** compilation (method vs tracing JIT — LuaJIT), **inline caches** (polymorphic ICs in V8), and object layout (hidden classes/shapes).
This is what makes managed languages fast; understanding V8's hidden classes directly explains the web-performance advice you'll meet later.

subtopics.title: The GC contract: tri-color marking and write barriers
body: The **GC contract** between the garbage collector and the rest of the runtime has two parties: the **mutator** (the application code) allocates objects and stores pointers; the **collector** reclaims unreachable objects. A stop-the-world (STW) collector pauses the mutator completely during collection — simple but causes latency spikes proportional to heap size. A **concurrent collector** runs the collector concurrently with the mutator — lower latency but requires synchronization to handle the mutator's changes to the object graph during collection.

The **tri-color abstraction** formalizes the concurrent collection invariant. Objects are colored: **white** (not yet seen — candidate garbage), **grey** (discovered as reachable but not fully traced — children not yet visited), **black** (reachable and fully traced — all children visited). The collector starts by coloring all GC roots grey and repeatedly processes a grey object: visit all its pointers (coloring each white child grey), then color the grey object black. At the end, whites are garbage and are freed. The invariant that must hold throughout: **no black object points directly to a white object** (because the collector will never revisit black objects, so such a white would be missed and incorrectly freed).

The problem with concurrent collection: the mutator can break the tri-color invariant. Specifically, if the mutator (1) stores a pointer from a black object to a white object (creating a black→white edge) and (2) deletes all grey→white paths to that white object, then the collector will incorrectly free the white object. **Write barriers** prevent this: every pointer store in the mutator-compiled code is wrapped with a small snippet that informs the collector (re-grey either the black source or the white target, depending on the barrier variant). The **Dijkstra (insertion) barrier** greys the new pointer's target on store: if you store a pointer to a white object anywhere, immediately grey it. The **Yuasa (deletion) barrier** greys the old pointer's target on store: if you overwrite a pointer, grey whatever the old pointer pointed to. These ensure the invariant is maintained without stopping the mutator.

Write barriers have a direct performance cost: every pointer store in compiled code must execute the barrier. For object-dense code (e.g., a linked list traversal that constantly updates `node = node.next`), barriers add a measurable overhead — typically 5-15% throughput reduction for a fully concurrent collector vs a STW collector. This is the fundamental tradeoff: concurrent GC reduces worst-case pause latency (the tail of the latency distribution) at the cost of throughput (the mean case). For latency-sensitive systems (real-time, game engines, trading) the tradeoff is usually wrong — these systems often use arenas, bump pointers, or explicit memory management instead. Understanding write barriers is the key to understanding why the JVM's G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah each make different performance tradeoffs.

resources.label: The Garbage Collection Handbook — Jones, Hosking, Moss (definitive reference)
GC FAQ — Richard Jones (concise encyclopedia of GC algorithms)

subtopics.title: Generational and copying garbage collection
body: The **generational hypothesis** is an empirical observation consistent across almost all managed languages and workloads: most objects die young. A typical allocation trace shows that 80-95% of objects become unreachable within one GC collection cycle of being allocated. The insight: if most objects are garbage by the time you first collect, the most efficient collector focuses its effort on the newest objects ('the nursery' or 'young generation'), not the old ones. **Generational GC** partitions the heap into a **young generation** (small, collected frequently — a minor GC) and an **old generation** (large, collected infrequently — a major GC). Objects that survive enough minor GCs are **promoted** to the old generation. This dramatically reduces average GC time: minor GCs collect a tiny region (often <10% of heap) and find 90%+ garbage, leaving very little live data to trace.

**Copying collection** (also called **semi-space collection**) is the standard algorithm for the young generation. The heap is split into two equal halves: **from-space** and **to-space**. Allocation is a simple bump pointer in from-space (just increment a pointer — ~3 instructions, no fragmentation). At collection time, trace all live objects from the roots, **copying** each live object from from-space to to-space (updating all pointers to the new location), then swap from and to space. After the swap, from-space is entirely garbage (no sweep step needed) and to-space contains all live objects in compact form. Collection time is proportional to **live data**, not total heap size — the key advantage when most data is dead. The cost: memory waste (the unused half-space) and the extra work of copying and pointer updating.

**Card tables** solve the inter-generational pointer problem: old-generation objects can point to young-generation objects, so young-generation collection must trace those old-to-young pointers too — otherwise live young objects reachable only from old objects would be incorrectly collected. Scanning the entire old generation to find these pointers is too slow. The card table solution: divide the heap into fixed-size **cards** (typically 512 bytes). Whenever the mutator writes a pointer into an old-generation object (tracked by a write barrier), mark the card containing that object as **dirty**. At minor GC time, only scan dirty cards for old-to-young pointers. This reduces the minor GC root set to just the dirty cards (often <1% of old-generation cards) rather than the entire old generation.

The JVM's **HotSpot GC** implements this generational scheme as the baseline. Young generation (Eden + Survivor 0 + Survivor 1): Eden is the primary allocation region; at minor GC, live objects from Eden and one Survivor are copied to the other Survivor; objects that survive a threshold number of minor GCs (configurable with `-XX:MaxTenuringThreshold`) are promoted to the old generation. Old generation: collected by a major (full) GC using one of the configurable collectors (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah, Serial). Understanding the young-generation mechanics explains many JVM performance tuning rules: keeping the short-lived object working set small (it stays in young gen), avoiding premature promotion (objects promoted too early pollute old gen and trigger expensive major GCs), and sizing the young generation appropriately for the allocation rate.

resources.label: JVM Garbage Collection — Oracle documentation (G1, ZGC, tuning)

subtopics.title: Concurrent GC: G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah
body: **G1 GC** (Garbage-First Garbage Collector, default since JDK 9) divides the heap into equal-sized **regions** (1-32 MB each) instead of fixed-size generations. Any region can be Eden, Survivor, old-gen, or humongous (for large objects). G1 tracks the **live data density** of each region and, during mixed GCs, prioritizes collecting the regions with the most garbage (hence 'Garbage-First'). The young-gen is a set of regions; minor GCs collect them with copying. Major collection is a **concurrent marking cycle** (concurrent with the mutator) followed by **mixed GCs** that evacuate old-gen regions. G1 targets a **pause-time goal** (`-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis`, default 200ms) and adapts region selection accordingly. Typical pause times: 10-200ms. G1 is the practical default for most Java applications prioritizing throughput-plus-latency balance.

**ZGC** (JDK 15+, production-ready from JDK 21 with generational ZGC) is designed for pause times under 1 millisecond even on terabyte heaps. The key innovation: **colored pointers**. ZGC encodes GC metadata (marked, remapped, finalizable) directly in the high bits of the 64-bit pointer — 3 bits of the virtual address. When the collector is in a marking phase, every load of a pointer is intercepted by a **load barrier** (code emitted by the compiler at every object field read) that checks these bits: if the pointer is not yet marked, mark it now. This turns marking from a stop-the-world operation into a concurrent one where each pointer is lazily marked on its first read during the collection cycle. ZGC's STW pauses are only for stack scanning (a very short pause proportional to thread count, not heap size). The tradeoff vs G1: 10-20% more throughput overhead from load barriers (every pointer load has extra work); win: latency is bounded to a few milliseconds regardless of heap size.

**Shenandoah** (Red Hat, JDK 12+) uses a similar concurrent approach but with **Brooks forwarding pointers**: every object has an extra word containing a pointer to itself (or to a forwarded copy during evacuation). The load barrier follows this pointer. Concurrent evacuation copies live objects to new locations while the mutator runs, updating the forwarding pointer. The STW phases are minimal: initial marking (roots only) and final marking (flushing remaining work). Shenandoah's advantage over ZGC: no platform requirement for extra pointer bits (works on 32-bit and certain 64-bit platforms that don't support colored pointers). The overhead: every object is 8 bytes larger (the forwarding pointer), and load barriers are comparable in cost to ZGC's. Both Shenandoah and ZGC demonstrate that sub-millisecond GC pause times at scale are achievable — this directly impacts the design of latency-sensitive services built on the JVM.

**Azul's C4 (Continuously Concurrent Compacting Collector)** is the commercial reference point: running in Azul's Zing JVM (and now in prime releases of OpenJDK), C4 achieves near-zero pause times on large heaps by combining concurrent compaction (copying live objects without pausing) with a hardware page-permission trick (GPGC): it maps pages in the to-space as read-protected; when the mutator accesses a page that has been evacuated, the OS SIGSEGV is intercepted by the JVM to remap the page, with no explicit barrier code in the mutator. This is a clever use of virtual memory hardware for GC barrier purposes. C4's design influenced ZGC and is the intellectual ancestor of modern low-latency collectors.

subtopics.title: JIT compilation: method JIT, tiered compilation, deoptimization
body: A **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** compiles bytecode to native machine code at runtime, immediately before (or just after) the code is first executed. The key insight: for most programs, only a small fraction of the code is executed frequently (the **hot path**). Compiling only hot code amortizes the compilation overhead against the execution speedup; cold code remains interpreted. This observation is the basis of **tiered compilation**, used in V8, HotSpot JVM, and .NET CLR: the system starts by interpreting all code (tier 0 — fast startup), then counts invocations and loop iterations, promotes hot functions to a quick-compile tier (tier 1 in V8 = Maglev, in JVM = C1), and finally promotes the hottest functions to a slow-compile, highly-optimizing tier (tier 2 in V8 = TurboFan, in JVM = C2). This pyramid gives fast startup, fast warmup, and eventually near-peak native performance.

**Method JIT** (the standard approach) compiles one method at a time. When a function's invocation count exceeds a threshold, compile it. The compiled function is installed into the dispatch table and subsequent calls go directly to native code. V8 Maglev compiles a function using bytecode-level information: for each bytecode instruction, emit native code that performs the same operation but without the interpreter dispatch overhead. V8 TurboFan goes further: it uses type feedback collected at runtime (from the inline caches, covered in the next subtopic) to emit **speculative optimizations** — compile `a + b` as a raw `ADD` instruction assuming `a` and `b` are integers (fast path), with a check-and-deoptimize guard in case they are not.

**Deoptimization** (also called bailout or uncommon trap) is the mechanism that makes speculative optimization safe. If a speculative assumption is violated at runtime (the guarded types change, an unexpected branch is taken), the JIT-compiled frame must be **deoptimized**: the native stack frame is reconstructed as a bytecode-interpreter frame, and execution continues in the interpreter from the point of the violated assumption. The compiled function is typically either **patched** (the check now always fails, so subsequent calls skip the optimized path) or **discarded** (the JIT recompiles with weaker or different assumptions). Deoptimization requires the JIT compiler to maintain **deopt maps**: for every guarded point in the native code, a record of how to reconstruct the bytecode-level execution state from the native register and stack state. These maps are generated during compilation and are the primary reason JIT compilation is more complex than AOT compilation.

**Tracing JIT** (the alternative, used by LuaJIT) does not compile one method at a time but records and compiles a **trace** — a single linear path through the program including calls. When a loop is hot, LuaJIT records the execution path through it (including calls and branches that always go the same way), compiles that linear trace to native code (no branches to handle — they become guards), and links it into an **inner loop** that executes at near-C speed. Traces cross function boundaries (no call overhead for inlined paths) and are extremely fast when the hot path is consistent. The downside: trace explosion (if many different paths through the loop are hot, many traces are generated) and the complexity of linking traces at their exit points. LuaJIT's implementation is a famous engineering feat — Lua runs faster than Python 3 on most benchmarks, achieved through tracing JIT on an otherwise simple bytecode VM.

resources.label: V8 blog — TurboFan and Maglev compiler architecture
LuaJIT — design notes and tracing JIT implementation
HotSpot JVM internals — tiered compilation and C2 JIT

subtopics.title: Inline caches: monomorphic, polymorphic, megamorphic
body: An **inline cache (IC)** is a small, dynamically-patched stub at a call or property-access site that caches the type information observed at that site and provides a fast path for the common case. They were invented by Deutsch and Schiffman for Smalltalk in 1984 and are the primary technique by which dynamic-language VMs (JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Lua) achieve near-static-language performance on typed code. The key observation: even though JavaScript has no static types, a given field access `obj.x` almost always sees objects of the same shape (the same set of properties in the same order) in practice — so if we cache 'object has shape S → field x is at offset N' and check it quickly, we save the general dynamic lookup on most accesses.

An IC starts as **uninitialized** (no observation yet). On the first execution, the VM performs a full dynamic lookup, caches the result (the object's shape and the field's offset), and patches the IC stub to: check if the current object has the cached shape (a single pointer comparison), and if so, load the field from the known offset (a single memory load). This is **monomorphic IC** — one cached type. If the second call comes with the same shape, the IC fires at near-native speed (pointer check + load). If a different shape arrives, the IC is upgraded to **polymorphic** (typically 2-4 shapes are cached in a small inline table, with a linear search). If many distinct shapes arrive, the IC goes **megamorphic**: at this point, the IC stops caching (too many options) and falls back to the general dynamic lookup every time. Megamorphic sites are permanent performance penalties.

V8's **hidden classes** (also called **shapes** in SpiderMonkey, **maps** in V8 source) are the type representation that ICs key against. When you write `let obj = {}; obj.x = 1; obj.y = 2`, V8 tracks the sequence of property additions as a **transition chain**: empty-shape → shape-with-x → shape-with-x-and-y. Two objects go through the same transition chain → they share the same final shape → ICs for property access on either object can be shared. This is why the V8 performance advice 'always initialize object properties in the same order in the constructor' exists — it ensures all instances share the same shape transition chain and thus have the same final shape, keeping field-access ICs monomorphic.

The IC state machine propagates to the JIT compiler. V8's TurboFan reads the IC state of each bytecode site when it decides to compile a function. A monomorphic property access site in the IC tells TurboFan 'this site always sees shape S → field at offset N'; TurboFan emits a guarded fast load (if shape matches S, load from offset N; else deoptimize). A polymorphic site causes TurboFan to emit a small inline type switch (if shape is S1 → offset N1; if S2 → offset N2; else deoptimize). A megamorphic site is compiled as a call to the runtime lookup (slow). This is the JIT-compiler-as-type-specializer pattern: the IC accumulates type observations, the JIT uses them to emit specialized code, and deoptimization handles the rare unexpected types. The tight IC → JIT interaction is the core of what makes V8 fast.

resources.label: V8 blog — What's up with Monomorphism? (ICs, hidden classes, performance)
Deutsch & Schiffman — Efficient Implementation of the Smalltalk-80 System (IC original paper)

subtopics.title: Object layout: hidden classes, pointer compression, and SMI tagging
body: The **in-memory layout of objects** directly determines the performance of field access, pointer tracing by the GC, and memory consumption. In V8, a JavaScript object is laid out as: a **Map** pointer (the hidden class, 8 bytes), a **properties pointer** (overflow property store, 8 bytes), an **elements pointer** (array-like indexed properties, 8 bytes), and then **in-object property slots** (8 bytes each, for properties that fit within the initial object allocation). The Map pointer is the IC key; the in-object slots are accessed by fixed offsets from the object pointer, as fast as C struct field access. When a property is added that doesn't fit in the in-object slots, it is placed in the properties store (a separately allocated array), accessed via an extra indirection.

**SMI tagging** (Small Integer tagging) is a universal optimization for dynamically-typed VMs: instead of boxing every integer as a heap-allocated object, use the low bit of the pointer as a tag. In V8, a pointer to a heap object always has its low bit set to 0 (objects are 8-byte aligned); a small integer (SMI) is stored directly in the pointer slot with the low bit set to 1 and the value in the upper 31 bits (on 32-bit) or 32 bits (on 64-bit). Type checking is a single bitwise AND; arithmetic on SMIs is direct register arithmetic with a shift. This eliminates heap allocation for integer-intensive code entirely and is one of the biggest performance wins in JavaScript VMs. Similar tagging is used in CPython (using LLTV scheme), HotSpot (using oops), and OCaml (using odd-bit tagging).

**Pointer compression** is V8's technique (since 2020) for reducing heap memory by 40%+ on 64-bit systems. Full 64-bit pointers are 8 bytes; compressed pointers are 4 bytes. V8 allocates all heap objects in a 4 GB heap cage (a contiguous virtual address range). Within this cage, any pointer can be represented as a 32-bit offset from the cage base. When storing a pointer, shift it to 32 bits; when loading, mask + shift + add cage base. This halves the size of all pointer fields (in-object slots, arrays of references) at the cost of two extra instructions per pointer load/store. The result: V8 heap memory reduced by 43% on typical workloads; better cache utilization; faster GC (smaller heap to trace). Pointer compression is also used in HotSpot JVM (`-XX:+UseCompressedOops`, enabled by default for heaps under 32 GB).

**Object layout for ML tensors** is a different regime entirely: tensors are dense rectangular arrays of a scalar type (f32, f16, bfloat16, i8), and their performance is dominated by **alignment** (AVX-512 requires 64-byte alignment for peak throughput), **stride** (contiguous vs non-contiguous memory access patterns), and **quantization** (storing weights in int8 to halve memory bandwidth). A PyTorch tensor object is a thin Python wrapper over a `StorageImpl` (the raw memory buffer) with metadata: `sizes` (shape), `strides` (step per dimension in elements), `dtype`, `device`, `data_ptr`. The stride representation makes zero-copy operations like transpose (swap strides), slice (adjust data_ptr and sizes), and expand (set stride to 0 for broadcast dimensions) $O(1)$ metadata operations — no data copying. Understanding this is essential for reasoning about when PyTorch operations are free (metadata-only) vs expensive (trigger data copies or kernel launches).

resources.label: V8 blog — Fast properties in V8 (hidden classes, in-object slots)
PyTorch internals — tensor storage and strides
The Garbage Collection Handbook — Jones, Hosking, Moss
V8 blog — TurboFan, hidden classes, ICs

connections.note: V8 hidden classes / inline caches are the reason behind JS perf rules
GC and JIT extend the simple bytecode VM you built
vLLM/llama.cpp inference engines are JIT-like runtimes for tensor programs

topics.title: The C memory model & undefined behavior
body: Draw the layout: stack frames, heap, `.data`/`.bss`/`.text`. Master pointers (arithmetic, function pointers, `void*`), manual **memory ownership**, **struct layout** (padding/alignment), and - critically - **undefined behavior** (signed overflow, OOB, strict aliasing) and *why the compiler exploits it*.
Build `malloc`/`free` with a free list, and use Compiler Explorer to watch your C become assembly in real time.

subtopics.title: Process address space: stack, heap, data, and text segments
body: Every running C program exists in a **virtual address space** - a flat, contiguous range of addresses (0 to $2^{48}-1$ on x86-64 Linux) that the OS and hardware MMU map to physical RAM. The virtual address space is divided into segments, each with distinct lifetime and access rules. Understanding this layout is prerequisite to understanding every memory bug, every exploit, and every performance anomaly involving memory.

The **text segment** (`.text`) holds the compiled machine code of the program - your functions as raw bytes. It is mapped read-execute but not writable (writing to it causes a segfault; this protects against accidental code corruption and makes the W^X security policy enforceable). Functions are addresses in `.text`; a function pointer is nothing more than the address of the first byte of a function's prologue.

The **data segment** (`.data`) holds initialized global and static variables - those that have a value in the source code. It is read-write, and its contents are loaded directly from the ELF binary at startup. The **BSS segment** (`.bss`) holds uninitialized global and static variables - those declared without an initializer, or explicitly zero-initialized. BSS is not stored in the binary (it would just be zeros); the OS zero-fills it on demand. This is why a global `int arr[1000000]` does not make your binary 4 MB - it just marks 4 MB of BSS.

The **heap** is a region of memory managed by `malloc`/`free` (or their equivalents). It grows upward (toward higher addresses) as the program allocates. The OS provides it via `brk`/`sbrk` (extending the heap pointer) or `mmap` (for large allocations). The C standard library's allocator sits between you and the OS: it requests large chunks from the OS and subdivides them, maintaining a free list of returned blocks to reuse. This layering is why `malloc` is much faster than a raw `mmap` call for small allocations. The heap has no built-in concept of ownership - the programmer must track who allocated what and ensure exactly one `free` per `malloc`. Double-free corrupts the allocator's internal state; use-after-free accesses memory that may have been reallocated to something else entirely.

The **stack** grows downward (toward lower addresses) and holds automatic-duration variables: local variables, function arguments (beyond the first six on x86-64, which go in registers), return addresses, and saved registers. Each function call pushes a **stack frame** delimited by the frame pointer (`rbp`) and the stack pointer (`rsp`). The frame contains the function's local variables, a saved copy of the caller's `rbp`, and the return address. When the function returns, `rsp` is restored to its pre-call value and control jumps to the return address - logically 'freeing' that frame, though the memory is not zeroed. This is why reading an uninitialized local variable often returns the garbage of a previous function's frame - the bytes are physically present and your variable overlaps them.

Two practical consequences that matter every day: (1) **Stack depth is finite** - typically 8 MB on Linux. Deep recursion that allocates large local arrays will overflow the stack (stack overflow), causing a segfault when the stack pointer crosses into unmapped memory. (2) **Never return a pointer to a local variable** - once the function returns, that stack frame is gone (logically), and the pointer is dangling. The compiler may warn about this; the sanitizers will catch it at runtime; the bug silently manifests as data corruption when the next function call overwrites that region.

resources.label: Beej's Guide to C Programming - ch. 2 (variables, memory)

subtopics.title: Pointers: arithmetic, function pointers, and void*
body: A **pointer** is a variable that holds a memory address. In C, the type of the pointer encodes the type of the object at that address, which determines how pointer arithmetic works and how the pointed-to bytes are interpreted. `int *p` means 'p holds the address of an int'; `p + 1` advances p by `sizeof(int)` bytes (4 bytes on most platforms), not by 1 byte. This is **pointer arithmetic** - it operates in units of the pointed-to type's size, making it correct for iterating over arrays and catastrophic when used on the wrong type.

The rules of pointer arithmetic are strict: you may only perform arithmetic on a pointer if the result remains within the same array object (or one-past-the-end). Arithmetic that crosses object boundaries is **undefined behavior**, even if the resulting address is valid memory. This is not a theoretical nicety - compilers use this assumption to prove that loops terminate, that array accesses cannot alias each other, and to elide bounds checks, leading to real miscompilations when the rule is violated.

**Function pointers** are pointers that hold the address of a function rather than a data object. The syntax is notorious for its readability, but the concept is simple: `int (*fn)(int, char *)` declares `fn` as a pointer to a function taking `int` and `char *` and returning `int`. You can assign any function with a matching signature to `fn` and call it via `fn(arg1, arg2)`. Function pointers are the mechanism behind vtables (C++ polymorphism), callbacks (qsort's comparator), plugin architectures, and jump tables in dispatchers. They are also the target of **function pointer hijacking** attacks - if an attacker can overwrite a function pointer on the heap or in a global table, they redirect execution.

`void *` is the generic pointer type - a pointer to 'some memory of unknown type'. It can hold any data pointer (automatic conversion in C, explicit cast in C++) and can be cast back to any concrete pointer type. It is the foundation of polymorphic C APIs: `malloc` returns `void *`, `memcpy` takes `void *`, `qsort` uses a `void *` comparator context. The key rule: you cannot dereference a `void *` directly (the compiler does not know the type size), and you cannot perform arithmetic on it (same reason). You must cast first. This is exactly the 'type erasure' pattern - drop the type to pass through a generic layer, restore it on the other side.

**Pointer aliasing** is when two pointers of different types point to the same memory. The C standard's **strict aliasing rule** says: a value of type T may only be accessed through a pointer to T, or a pointer to a compatible type (a union containing T, a `char *`, or `void *` after appropriate casting). Accessing an `int` object through a `float *` pointer is undefined behavior - and modern compilers genuinely exploit this: they assume two pointers of different types cannot alias, enabling optimizations like hoisting a load out of a loop. Breaking strict aliasing produces code that compiles and runs but silently produces wrong results under optimization. The safe workarounds are `memcpy` for type-punning and compiler flags like `-fno-strict-aliasing` when you genuinely need aliasing.

resources.label: What Every C Programmer Should Know About UB, Part 1 - LLVM blog
Compiler Explorer - watch pointer arithmetic compile to asm

subtopics.title: Struct layout: padding, alignment, and packing
body: A C `struct` is a contiguous block of memory containing its fields in declaration order, but the compiler inserts **padding bytes** between fields to satisfy **alignment requirements**. Every type has a natural alignment - the requirement that its address be a multiple of its size: `char` aligns to 1 byte, `short` to 2, `int` and `float` to 4, `double` and `long` to 8, pointers to 8 on 64-bit. The CPU can read/write aligned accesses in a single bus transaction; misaligned accesses either cause a fault (on strict architectures like SPARC or older ARM) or are split into two transactions and silently slowed (on x86).

The padding rules follow mechanically: before each field, the compiler inserts enough padding so the field starts at an address that is a multiple of the field's alignment. After the last field, the compiler adds **tail padding** so the struct's total size is a multiple of its largest field's alignment - this ensures that an array of structs has every element correctly aligned. The struct's own alignment is the maximum alignment of any member.

A concrete worked example: `struct S { char a; int b; char c; double d; }`. `a` is at offset 0 (size 1). To align `b` (int, align 4), 3 bytes of padding follow `a`, so `b` starts at offset 4. `b` ends at offset 8. `c` at offset 8 (size 1). To align `d` (double, align 8), 7 bytes of padding follow `c`, so `d` starts at offset 16. Total size: 24 bytes, despite the fields summing to 1+4+1+8 = 14 bytes. **Reordering** to `{ double d; int b; char a; char c; }` gives: `d` at 0, `b` at 8, `a` at 12, `c` at 13, tail padding 2 bytes - total 16 bytes. The reordering saves 8 bytes (33%) purely by putting large-alignment fields first.

The `offsetof(struct, member)` macro returns the byte offset of a member within a struct - this is how Linux's `container_of` macro works, letting you recover a pointer to an enclosing struct from a pointer to one of its members (the pattern behind all of Linux's intrusive linked lists). `sizeof(struct)` returns the full padded size including tail padding. When you serialize a struct to disk or network by casting to `char *` and writing `sizeof(struct)` bytes, you are writing padding bytes too - this is often a bug in protocol implementations, leaking stack or heap content (information disclosure).

`__attribute__((packed))` or `#pragma pack(1)` tells GCC/Clang to suppress all padding, packing fields adjacently regardless of alignment. This is used for protocol headers and hardware register maps where the byte layout is prescribed. The cost: every access to a misaligned field is slower (or faults on strict architectures), and the compiler cannot generate optimal loads/stores. The worst failure mode: a packed struct with a pointer field - taking the address of that pointer field gives a misaligned pointer, which you then pass to code expecting a normal pointer, producing undefined behavior. Use `memcpy` to read/write fields from packed structs.

resources.label: Eli Bendersky - struct layout and alignment in C

subtopics.title: Undefined behavior: what it is and why compilers exploit it
body: **Undefined behavior (UB)** is the C/C++ standard's designation for code whose behavior the standard places no constraints on - the implementation may do anything, including crashing, producing wrong results, or appearing to work correctly under some compilers and optimization levels while silently breaking under others. The key insight that most programmers miss: UB does not mean 'implementation-defined' (where the behavior is unspecified but consistent per implementation). It means the compiler is allowed to assume UB never occurs and optimize on that assumption - which means the resulting code can actively do something *different* from what you wrote.

**Signed integer overflow** is the canonical example. In C, overflow of a signed integer is undefined behavior (unsigned overflow is defined as modular arithmetic). A modern compiler seeing `x + 1 > x` (where x is `int`) is allowed to optimize this to `1` (always true) because if the addition ever overflowed, the behavior would be undefined, so the compiler assumes it does not - meaning `x` must be less than `INT_MAX`, so `x + 1 > x` is always true. This exact optimization has been applied to real Linux kernel code, silently removing overflow checks that existed for security reasons. The fix is to use unsigned integers (which have defined overflow) or compiler builtins like `__builtin_add_overflow`.

**Out-of-bounds array access** is UB because C arrays have no implicit bounds checking. Accessing `arr[-1]` or `arr[n]` (where n is the array size) reads/writes memory at an arbitrary offset from the array's base. What you actually hit depends on the memory layout - often another local variable, a saved register, or the return address. Buffer overflow attacks exploit OOB writes to overwrite the return address on the stack and redirect execution. AddressSanitizer (compile with `-fsanitize=address`) instruments every array access to check bounds at runtime - this is the primary tool for catching OOB bugs during development.

**Strict aliasing violations** and **use-after-free** and **uninitialized reads** round out the major UB categories. Use-after-free is UB because the freed memory may be reallocated - writes to it corrupt a live allocation; reads from it return unpredictable data. Compilers can also eliminate 'dead stores' to freed memory (a security-relevant optimization: if you zero a buffer before freeing it to scrub secrets, the compiler may delete the zero as a dead write). Use `memset_explicit` (C23) or platform equivalents (`SecureZeroMemory`, `explicit_bzero`) to force the scrub.

The practical lesson is not just 'avoid UB' but 'use sanitizers and analyze compiler output'. Run your test suite with `-fsanitize=undefined,address,thread` during development. Study the Compiler Explorer output at `-O2` to see which checks have been compiled away. When you see a surprising optimization, ask: 'did the compiler eliminate this branch because UB makes the condition always-true?' The modern toolchain treats UB as a contract you must uphold - if you do, it generates faster code; if you do not, the code is unpredictably broken.

resources.label: What Every C Programmer Should Know About Undefined Behavior - LLVM blog (3 parts)

subtopics.title: Building malloc: free lists, splitting, and coalescing
body: Implementing your own `malloc`/`free` is the single most educational exercise in C systems programming. It forces you to confront pointer arithmetic, alignment, struct layout, and the gap between a high-level API and raw memory - all at once. The result is also a production skill: custom allocators appear in every high-performance system (game engines, databases, trading systems) where `glibc`'s general-purpose allocator is too slow or too unpredictable for the use case.

The **implicit free list** allocator is the simplest correct implementation. Represent the heap as a sequence of variable-size **blocks**, each with a **header** (and optionally a footer) encoding the block size and whether it is allocated or free. The header is typically a `size_t` with the least-significant bit as the 'allocated' flag (valid because block sizes are always aligned to at least 8 bytes, making the LSB always zero for the size portion). `malloc(n)` traverses the list from the beginning searching for a free block of sufficient size (**first-fit** or **best-fit** policy), splits it if the remainder is large enough to be a useful block (at least header + minimum payload), marks it allocated, and returns a pointer to the payload (just after the header). `free(p)` walks back to the header (the pointer the user has is to the payload, so the header is at `p - sizeof(header)`), marks it free, and coalesces adjacent free blocks.

**Coalescing** is the critical operation for avoiding external fragmentation - the state where free memory exists but is split into too-small pieces to satisfy large requests. When you free a block, check whether the block immediately before it and immediately after it (in memory) are also free; if so, merge them into one larger free block. Coalescing the next block is easy (you can find it via `current_block + current_size`). Coalescing the previous block requires walking from the beginning (slow) unless you maintain a **footer** at the end of each block containing the same size/allocated info as the header - then the previous block's footer is at `current_block - sizeof(footer)`, giving $O(1)$ coalescing in both directions. This is the **boundary-tags** technique from Knuth.

**Explicit free lists** improve allocation speed by maintaining a linked list of only the free blocks (embedded in the free block's payload area, since that space is available). `malloc` traverses only free blocks rather than all blocks. **Segregated free lists** maintain multiple free lists, one per size class (e.g., 8 bytes, 16 bytes, 32 bytes, ..., 1024 bytes, 'large'). Allocation of size n: find the appropriate size class, take the first block from that list. This is $O(1)$ per allocation for common sizes and approaches the design of `tcmalloc` and `jemalloc`. Tracking where each design has fragmentation vs speed trade-offs teaches you to reason about allocator behavior under real workloads.

The production allocator `jemalloc` (used by Firefox, Facebook) and `tcmalloc` (Google, used in Chrome) extend these ideas: thread-local caches for small allocations (avoiding locks on the hot path), size-class bins with separate spans of pages per class, background threads for coalescing and returning memory to the OS. The key insight is that most allocations in practice are small and short-lived - optimizing the fast path for small allocations (thread-local cache, no locking, $O(1)$) and only falling back to a shared heap for large or overflow cases gives a 5-10x speedup over a naive implementation in real workloads.

topics.title: ABI, linking & ELF
body: The **System V x86-64 ABI** (arg registers `rdi,rsi,rdx,rcx,r8,r9`, return in `rax`, the red zone), caller- vs callee-saved registers, the **ELF** format (sections vs segments, symbol table, relocations), static vs dynamic linking, and **PLT/GOT** lazy binding. Plus runtime tricks: `LD_PRELOAD`, `dlopen`/`dlsym`.
Understanding the ABI is what lets you call C from Rust/Python, interpose libraries, and read disassembly fluently.

subtopics.title: The System V x86-64 calling convention
body: A **calling convention** is the agreed protocol by which a caller passes arguments to a function and receives results back - agreed between compilers, the OS, and linkers so that code compiled by different tools can interoperate. The **System V AMD64 ABI** (the standard on Linux, macOS, and BSDs for x86-64) specifies this protocol in exact detail, and every C/C++/Rust/Go/... compiler on those platforms follows it for external functions.

**Integer and pointer arguments** are passed in registers in this order: `rdi`, `rsi`, `rdx`, `rcx`, `r8`, `r9`. If there are more than six integer/pointer arguments, the rest are pushed onto the stack in right-to-left order. **Floating-point arguments** go into `xmm0`-`xmm7`. **Return values**: integers and pointers in `rax` (and `rdx` for 128-bit returns); floats in `xmm0`. This register-first convention is why modern function calls are so fast: for functions with six or fewer arguments, no stack writes happen at all during argument passing.

**Caller-saved registers** (also called 'clobbered' or 'volatile'): `rax`, `rcx`, `rdx`, `rsi`, `rdi`, `r8`-`r11`, `xmm0`-`xmm15`. The callee may freely modify these without restoring them. If the caller needs a value across a function call, it must save those registers (push them onto the stack) before the call and restore (pop) them after. **Callee-saved registers**: `rbx`, `rbp`, `r12`-`r15`. The callee must restore these to their original values before returning. This is why you see function prologues that push `rbx`/`r12`/etc. and epilogues that pop them - the function is fulfilling its ABI obligation.

The **red zone** is a 128-byte region *below* the current stack pointer (`rsp`) that the ABI guarantees will not be clobbered by asynchronous events (signals, interrupts) on the normal execution path. Leaf functions (functions that call no other functions) can use this region for temporary storage without adjusting `rsp` - saving the prologue/epilogue overhead of allocating a stack frame. Compilers use this aggressively for small leaf functions. The catch: OS signal handlers and debugger breakpoints violate the guarantee (signals use the red zone area), so signal handler stacks and interrupt handlers must use `-mno-red-zone`.

The **stack alignment requirement**: the stack pointer must be 16-byte aligned *at the point of the `call` instruction*. A `call` pushes the 8-byte return address, leaving `rsp` at (16n - 8). The callee's prologue typically adjusts `rsp` to realign. This requirement comes from SSE/AVX instructions that need 16-byte-aligned memory operands - violating it causes a segfault on the first SIMD load/store. When writing assembly stubs or JIT code, forgetting alignment is the first bug you will hit.

resources.label: System V AMD64 ABI specification (the primary document)
CS:APP - Procedure Calls and the ABI (CMU 15-213)

subtopics.title: ELF format: sections, segments, symbols, and relocations
body: **ELF (Executable and Linkable Format)** is the binary format for executables, shared libraries, and object files on Linux and most Unix systems. Understanding ELF is understanding how your source code becomes a running process - and it is the foundation for writing linkers, loaders, debuggers, and exploit tools.

An ELF file has two views. The **section view** (used by the linker) divides the file into named sections: `.text` (machine code), `.data` (initialized globals), `.bss` (uninitialized globals, zero-size in the file), `.rodata` (read-only data like string literals and switch jump tables), `.symtab` (symbol table for debugging and linking), `.strtab` (string table for symbol names), `.rel.text` or `.rela.text` (relocation entries). The **segment view** (used by the loader/OS) groups sections into loadable **program headers**: typically one PT_LOAD segment for read-execute (text + rodata) and one for read-write (data + bss). The OS `mmap`s each PT_LOAD segment into the virtual address space with the specified permissions.

The **symbol table** is a list of `(name, value, size, type, binding, section)` entries. `value` is the address (or offset) of the symbol. `type` is STT_FUNC (function), STT_OBJECT (variable), STT_NOTYPE (raw address). `binding` is STB_LOCAL (not visible outside the file), STB_GLOBAL (visible and linkable), or STB_WEAK (visible but overridable). The linker resolves references across object files by matching undefined global symbols in one file with definitions in another. `nm` prints the symbol table; `readelf -s` shows the full entries.

**Relocations** are fixup instructions that the linker applies after deciding final addresses. When the compiler generates an object file, references to external symbols are left as placeholder addresses; a relocation entry records: 'at offset X in section Y, apply fixup of type T using symbol S plus addend A'. The linker, knowing final addresses, applies the formula. Relocation types include R_X86_64_PC32 (PC-relative 32-bit, used for calls to nearby functions), R_X86_64_PLT32 (call via PLT, for dynamic symbols), R_X86_64_64 (absolute 64-bit address). `objdump -r` shows relocation entries in object files. Understanding relocations is essential for writing linker scripts, JIT compilers, and binary patching tools.

`readelf -a` and `objdump -d` are the two essential tools. `readelf` shows the ELF headers, sections, symbols, relocations, and dynamic section in human-readable form. `objdump -d` disassembles the `.text` section, annotating with symbol names. For a deep audit of any binary (security research, reverse engineering, debugging a stripped release binary), these tools plus `nm`, `strings`, and `strace` cover 90% of the investigative surface.

resources.label: Eli Bendersky - load-time relocation of shared libraries

subtopics.title: Static vs dynamic linking: tradeoffs and mechanics
body: **Static linking** combines all object files and library archives (`.a` files) into a single self-contained executable at link time. Every symbol reference is resolved by the linker; the resulting binary contains all code it will ever execute. Advantages: no runtime dependency on library versions (deploys cleanly, no 'missing .so' errors), slightly faster at startup (no dynamic linker work), and full link-time optimization (LTO) across all code including library code. Disadvantages: larger binary, and if a library has a security bug, every binary statically linked against it must be recompiled and redeployed.

**Dynamic linking** produces an executable that lists its needed shared libraries (`.so` files) in a PT_DYNAMIC segment, with undefined symbols to be resolved at load time by the **dynamic linker** (`ld.so`). At startup, `ld.so` is mapped into the process, reads the needed libraries, maps them into the address space, and resolves (patches) the undefined symbols by matching them against the libraries' exported symbol tables. Advantages: shared code in memory (50 processes using `libc.so` share one physical copy), libraries can be updated independently (security patches), and smaller per-binary disk usage. Disadvantages: startup overhead, dependency on matching library versions, and the attack surface of the dynamic linker itself.

**PLT/GOT lazy binding** is the mechanism that makes dynamic linking fast at the call site. For each externally-defined function, the compiler generates a call to a stub in the **Procedure Linkage Table (PLT)** rather than directly to the function. The PLT stub jumps through a pointer in the **Global Offset Table (GOT)**. Initially, the GOT entry points back into the PLT (to a resolver stub); on the first call, the resolver asks `ld.so` to find the function's real address and patches the GOT entry to point directly to it. All subsequent calls go through the GOT directly to the function - a single indirect jump at runtime. This lazy resolution means unused library functions are never resolved, reducing startup time.

Security implications of PLT/GOT: because the GOT is a writable table of function pointers, **GOT overwrite** is a classic exploit technique - if you can write an arbitrary address into a GOT entry (via a buffer overflow or format string), all subsequent calls to that function instead call your injected code. Modern mitigations: **RELRO (Relocation Read-Only)** makes the GOT read-only after startup (full RELRO marks it non-writable before `main` runs), and **PIE (Position Independent Executable)** randomizes the base address (ASLR) so the GOT's address is unpredictable. Check a binary's security features with `checksec --file=binary`.

`LD_PRELOAD` is an environment variable that tells `ld.so` to load a specified shared library *before* all others, allowing its symbols to **shadow** same-named symbols in later libraries (including libc). This is used for: debugging (override `malloc` with a tracking version), profiling, security testing (intercept syscalls), and - if set maliciously - preloading a rootkit. `dlopen`/`dlsym` extend this to runtime: `dlopen(path, RTLD_LAZY)` loads a shared library, `dlsym(handle, symbol)` looks up a symbol by name, returning a function pointer or data pointer. This is how plugin architectures and scripting language FFIs work.

resources.label: Eli Bendersky - position independent code and the PLT/GOT
How the ELF ruined Christmas - symbol preemption deep dive

subtopics.title: Runtime introspection: LD_PRELOAD, dlopen, and dlsym
body: The ability to interpose on library calls at runtime without modifying source is one of the most powerful (and most abused) features of the dynamic linker. It enables live debugging, performance profiling, security testing, and - in adversarial contexts - stealthy code injection. Understanding the mechanism demystifies a wide range of tools: `strace`, `ltrace`, `asan`, `tsan`, and most security hooking frameworks all use variations of this technique.

A minimal `LD_PRELOAD` interposer: create a shared library that defines a function with the same name as a libc function (e.g., `malloc`). At runtime, the dynamic linker resolves `malloc` to your version first. To call the original, use `dlsym(RTLD_NEXT, "malloc")` - RTLD_NEXT searches the library load order starting after the current library, finding the next `malloc` (the real one). This is the standard pattern for wrapping: intercept, record/transform/validate, call through. `mallochooks` in glibc and AddressSanitizer's allocator use exactly this.

`dlopen` and `dlsym` implement explicit dynamic loading at any point during program execution, not just at startup. `dlopen(path, flags)` maps the library, resolves its dependencies, and runs its initializers (the `.init_array` section). `RTLD_NOW` resolves all symbols immediately; `RTLD_LAZY` defers to first call. `RTLD_GLOBAL` makes the library's symbols available to later `dlopen` calls (and can cause symbol collisions across plugins). `dlsym(handle, name)` looks up a symbol name in the given library handle. The returned `void *` is cast to the appropriate function pointer type - there is no type-safety here, and casting wrong is UB.

The **hidden/default visibility** mechanism (`__attribute__((visibility("hidden")))`) controls which symbols are exported from a shared library. Default visibility symbols are exported to the dynamic linker's global namespace and can be preempted by `LD_PRELOAD`. Hidden visibility symbols are internal - not exported, not preemptable, and faster to call (no PLT indirection needed, the compiler can use a direct call). Large libraries (libc, libstdc++) mark internal helpers as hidden to prevent external interposition and enable inlining. When you write a shared library for distribution, mark non-API symbols hidden to reduce the exported symbol table and prevent accidental coupling.

Practical uses in systems programming and security: (1) **Memory profiling**: wrap `malloc`/`free` to track allocations, detect leaks, record stack traces. (2) **Fault injection**: randomly make `malloc` fail 1% of the time to test error paths. (3) **Timing attacks in security research**: wrap `memcmp` to make it constant-time. (4) **Rootkit detection**: check whether well-known libc symbols have been preempted (`dlsym(RTLD_DEFAULT, "malloc") != dlsym(RTLD_NEXT, "malloc")` indicates interposition). (5) **Scripting language FFI**: Python's `ctypes` and Rust's `libloading` use `dlopen`/`dlsym` to call C libraries without a compilation step.

topics.title: x86-64 assembly & SIMD
body: Read and write basic asm (`mov`, `lea`, `call/ret`, `cmp/test`, addressing modes, the flags register), recognize compiler `-O2` patterns, and vectorize with **SIMD** (SSE2/AVX2 intrinsics like `_mm256_add_epi32`).
You don't write much assembly by hand anymore, but reading it is non-negotiable for performance work, reverse engineering, and exploitation.

subtopics.title: x86-64 registers, instructions, and addressing modes
body: x86-64 has 16 general-purpose 64-bit registers: `rax`, `rbx`, `rcx`, `rdx`, `rsi`, `rdi`, `rsp`, `rbp`, and `r8`-`r15`. Each has 32-bit (`eax`, `r8d`), 16-bit (`ax`, `r8w`), and 8-bit (`al`/`ah`, `r8b`) sub-registers aliased to the same physical register. Writing to a 32-bit register (`eax`) zero-extends to the full 64-bit register - a historical design that catches many bugs when porting 32-bit code. Writing to a 16-bit or 8-bit sub-register leaves the upper bits unchanged, which is why you sometimes see `xor eax, eax` instead of `mov eax, 0` to zero a register (the XOR is one byte shorter and avoids a partial-register dependency).

The **RFLAGS register** holds condition codes set by arithmetic operations: `ZF` (zero flag - result was zero), `SF` (sign flag - result was negative), `CF` (carry flag - unsigned overflow or borrow), `OF` (overflow flag - signed overflow), `PF` (parity flag - rarely used). Comparison instructions `cmp a, b` subtract b from a and set flags without storing the result. `test a, b` computes `a AND b` and sets ZF/SF. Conditional jumps (`je`, `jne`, `jl`, `jge`, `jb`, etc.) branch based on flag states. Most programs spend 30-40% of instructions on compare-and-branch.

**Addressing modes** are the ways memory operands can be specified. AT&T syntax: `(%rax)` - indirect (dereference rax); `8(%rax)` - base + displacement (rax+8); `(%rax,%rcx,4)` - base + index*scale + displacement (rax + rcx*4); `array(%rip)` - RIP-relative (for PIC). Intel syntax reverses operand order and uses brackets: `[rax+8]`. The `lea` (load effective address) instruction computes an address *without* dereferencing it - useful for arithmetic: `lea rax, [rax + rax*4]` computes `rax = rax * 5` in one instruction, faster than `imul`.

Core instructions to know fluently: `mov` (copy), `push`/`pop` (stack), `call`/`ret` (function call/return - push/pop return address), `jmp` and conditional jumps, `add`/`sub`/`imul`/`idiv`, `and`/`or`/`xor`/`not`/`shl`/`shr`/`sar` (arithmetic shift right preserves sign), `cmp`/`test`, `movsx`/`movzx` (sign/zero extend), `lea`, `nop`. The instruction encoding is variable-width (1-15 bytes) - `nop` is 1 byte (0x90), `mov rax, [rbx+rcx*8+0x10]` with a 64-bit immediate is 10+ bytes. Disassemblers need to parse this stream from a known boundary (typically a function entry) because parsing backwards is ambiguous.

The compiler's **prologue and epilogue** pattern: a function starts with `push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N` (save frame pointer, set up new frame, allocate N bytes for locals) and ends with `leave; ret` (`leave` is equivalent to `mov rsp, rbp; pop rbp`). With `-fomit-frame-pointer` (default at -O2), the prologue/epilogue is just `sub rsp, N` / `add rsp, N`, and `rbp` is used as a general-purpose register. This makes stack unwinding harder (debuggers and profilers need frame pointer or DWARF unwind tables) but frees a register for the compiler's use.

resources.label: Felix Cloutier - x86 instruction reference (encoding, flags per instruction)

subtopics.title: Reading compiler output: O2 patterns to recognize
body: Compiler Explorer (godbolt.org) is the essential tool: paste C code on the left, see assembly on the right, instantly, for any compiler and optimization level. The skill of reading assembly is fundamentally pattern recognition - the compiler applies a relatively small set of transformations, and once you have seen each pattern once, you can parse disassembly at a useful rate.

**Inlining** is the most impactful optimization: the compiler replaces a function call with the function body, eliminating the call/ret overhead, register save/restore, and enabling further optimizations across the call boundary. At `-O2`, small functions (under ~20 instructions) are almost always inlined if visible to the compiler. You will not see a `call` instruction for them in the output - their body appears directly at the call site. This is why `static inline` and `__attribute__((always_inline))` exist: they force inlining even for larger functions where the compiler might not.

**Constant folding and propagation**: if all inputs to an expression are compile-time constants, the compiler evaluates it at compile time and emits just a `mov rax, <result>` or even nothing. `printf("hello")` with a constant format string becomes a `puts` call. A loop `for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) sum += arr[i]` with all values known at compile time may produce just `mov rax, <precomputed_sum>`.

**Loop transformations** are the highest-impact backend optimizations for numerical code. **Unrolling** reduces branch overhead by replicating the loop body N times, advancing the induction variable by N per iteration. **Vectorization** replaces scalar iterations with SIMD instructions processing N elements at once (N=4 for float with AVX, N=8 for short with AVX2). Look for `vmovdqu`, `vpaddd`, `vpcmpeqd` - these are the vectorized memory, add, and compare instructions. If the loop has a dependency between iterations or a conditional break, auto-vectorization often fails and the compiler falls back to scalar. `#pragma omp simd` or manually written intrinsics force it.

**Tail call optimization**: a function that ends with a `return f(args)` (tail call) does not need its own stack frame - it can reuse it and jump to `f` instead of calling it. This turns tail-recursive functions into loops. C compilers do this under `-O2` when the call truly is a tail call. You will see a `jmp` where you expect a `call ret` pair. Rust and functional-language backends rely on this for recursive style without stack overflow. GCC's `__attribute__((optimize("O2")))` or `-foptimize-sibling-calls` controls this explicitly.

resources.label: Compiler Explorer (godbolt.org) - paste C and see asm live

subtopics.title: SIMD: SSE2, AVX2, and writing intrinsics
body: **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)** executes one instruction on a vector of values simultaneously. SSE2 (2001, guaranteed on all x86-64 CPUs) has 16 128-bit XMM registers. AVX2 (2013, Haswell and later) has 16 256-bit YMM registers. AVX-512 (2017, server Skylake and later) has 32 512-bit ZMM registers. The width of a SIMD register divided by element size gives the **lane count**: a 256-bit register holds 8 floats (32-bit each) or 4 doubles or 32 bytes.

**Intrinsics** are C/C++ functions that map 1:1 to a SIMD instruction, using special vector types (`__m256i` for 256-bit integer, `__m256` for float, `__m256d` for double). The Intel Intrinsics Guide at intel.com/content/www/us/en/docs/intrinsics-guide/ lists every intrinsic with latency, throughput, and the corresponding instruction. A typical vectorized inner loop: load 8 floats with `_mm256_loadu_ps`, add with `_mm256_add_ps`, store with `_mm256_storeu_ps`. The `u` suffix means unaligned - always use unaligned loads/stores unless you have verified 32-byte alignment, as the performance difference on modern CPUs is negligible and misaligned `_mm256_load_ps` crashes.

**Data layout for SIMD**: the most common bottleneck is that the data is in **AoS (Array of Structs)** layout - `struct Particle { float x, y, z, mass; }` laid out as `xyzm xyzm xyzm...` - but SIMD wants to process 8 x-coordinates at once. The transformation to **SoA (Struct of Arrays)** - separate `float xs[], ys[], zs[], masses[]` - enables efficient SIMD: load 8 xs at once, 8 ys at once, and compute in parallel. ECS game engines and physics simulators use SoA as their fundamental data design principle. The cache locality section (ll-cache) discusses the broader tradeoffs, but for SIMD: AoS = scalar-friendly, SoA = vector-friendly.

**Horizontal operations** (summing all elements within one SIMD register) are much more expensive than vertical operations (processing corresponding elements of two registers). To sum 8 floats in a `__m256`, you need a sequence of shuffle and add operations, often called a 'horizontal reduction'. The lesson: design algorithms to avoid horizontal reductions in the inner loop. Sum accumulators vertically (accumulate 8 partial sums in a `__m256`), then reduce horizontally once at the very end.

**Masked operations and gather/scatter** (AVX-512 and AVX2 respectively): `_mm256_i32gather_ps` loads floats from arbitrary indices (non-contiguous memory), enabling SIMD over pointer-chasing structures. Gather is 3-10x slower than contiguous load due to cache misses - use it when the alternative is scalar but data layout prevents contiguous access. AVX-512's mask registers enable conditional SIMD: process all 16 elements but only write back those where a condition mask is set, eliminating branch mispredictions in conditional loops.

resources.label: Intel Intrinsics Guide - latency, throughput, every intrinsic

subtopics.title: Inline assembly and compiler barriers
body: **Inline assembly** (`asm volatile` in GCC/Clang) lets you embed assembly instructions directly within C/C++ code. The syntax is: `asm volatile("instruction" : outputs : inputs : clobbers)`. The outputs section specifies which C variables receive written values; inputs which provide input values; clobbers which registers or memory the assembly modifies (informing the compiler to save/restore them). This is how low-level code accesses instructions the C language cannot express: `rdtsc` (read timestamp counter for cycle-accurate timing), `cpuid` (CPU feature detection), `clflush` (cache line flush), `mfence`/`lfence`/`sfence` (memory fences).

The `volatile` qualifier on `asm` is crucial: it prevents the compiler from moving, removing, or deduplicating the assembly block. Without it, the compiler may conclude the block has no side effects and delete it. Similarly, listing `"memory"` in the clobber list acts as a **compiler barrier** - it tells the compiler to flush all live variables to memory and reload them after the asm, preventing the compiler from reordering memory accesses across the barrier. This is the C-level half of memory ordering; the hardware level is separate (memory fences).

**Compiler barriers vs hardware barriers**: `asm volatile("" : : : "memory")` is a compiler barrier - it prevents the *compiler* from reordering memory accesses across it, but the CPU is still free to reorder them at the hardware level (OOO execution, store buffers). For cross-thread correctness, you need both: a compiler barrier to prevent the compiler from reordering, and a hardware fence (`mfence`, `sfence`, `lfence`) to prevent the CPU. C11/C++11 atomics with appropriate memory orders handle both sides correctly and are preferable to hand-rolling fence instructions.

When inline assembly is necessary (for performance-critical code using CPU-specific features) vs. when it is not: most low-level operations are better expressed as C11 intrinsics or compiler builtins (`__builtin_clz`, `__builtin_popcount`, `__atomic_*`). The compiler knows more about register allocation and scheduling than hand-written asm. Use inline asm only for instructions that have no C equivalent (e.g., `rdtsc`, hardware-specific instructions, or architecturally mandated sequences like the CPUID serialization idiom). For SIMD, always prefer intrinsics over inline asm.

resources.label: GCC inline assembly HOWTO (constraint syntax, clobbers)
Compiler Explorer - see how asm volatile blocks affect optimization
