topics.title: Backend: APIs, auth, real-time
body: HTTP internals → Express/Hono as abstraction, REST design (idempotency, versioning), the middleware pattern, **authentication** (sessions, JWT access/refresh, OAuth 2.0 PKCE, httpOnly cookies), input validation with **zod**, production hardening (rate limiting, CORS, Helmet), and **WebSockets vs SSE vs polling**.
Auth is where most real-world security bugs live; build it carefully and you've covered a huge part of the attack surface.

subtopics.title: HTTP internals: headers, status codes, connection management, and HTTP/2
body: HTTP is a request-response protocol running over TCP (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2) or UDP (HTTP/3/QUIC). Understanding what happens below the `fetch()` call is essential for debugging latency, designing correct APIs, and implementing caching properly. The life of an HTTP request: DNS resolution (cached at multiple layers), TCP handshake (1.5 RTT), TLS handshake (1-2 RTT for TLS 1.2, 1 RTT or 0-RTT for TLS 1.3), send request headers + body, receive response headers + body, optionally keep the connection alive for the next request.

**HTTP/1.1** is text-based and sequential on each connection: one request-response at a time (without pipelining, which was never widely deployed). Browsers work around this by opening 6 concurrent TCP connections per host. The consequence: 6 in-flight requests maximum per origin, which is why concatenating JS files and CSS sprites were optimization techniques - reducing the number of requests. **HTTP/2** is binary-framed and multiplexed: multiple requests share a single TCP connection via streams, each with a stream ID. There is no head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer (a stalled stream does not block others). However, HTTP/2 still has TCP-level head-of-line blocking: a lost packet halts all streams because TCP guarantees delivery order.

**Status codes** carry semantic meaning that clients and intermediaries (proxies, CDNs) act on. The groupings: 1xx (informational, rare in practice - 101 Switching Protocols for WebSocket upgrades), 2xx (success - 200 OK, 201 Created for new resources, 204 No Content for successful deletes), 3xx (redirects - 301 Moved Permanently is cached by browsers indefinitely with no expiry, 302 Found is temporary, 307 Temporary Redirect preserves the HTTP method, 308 Permanent Redirect preserves the method and is cacheable), 4xx (client errors - 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized means 'not authenticated', 403 Forbidden means 'authenticated but not authorized', 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests), 5xx (server errors - 500, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable with `Retry-After`).

**Request headers that matter**: `Content-Type` tells the server what format the body is in (application/json, multipart/form-data). `Accept` tells the server what formats the client can handle. `Authorization: Bearer <token>` carries credentials. `Cache-Control: no-cache` asks the server to validate before using cache; `no-store` prevents caching entirely. **Response headers**: `Cache-Control: max-age=3600, s-maxage=86400` - `max-age` for browser, `s-maxage` for CDNs. `ETag` and `Last-Modified` enable conditional requests (`If-None-Match`, `If-Modified-Since`) that return 304 Not Modified when content has not changed - saves bandwidth without disabling caching. `Vary: Accept-Encoding` tells caches that the response varies by encoding (gzip vs brotli vs uncompressed).

**Idempotency and safety** are the semantics that make HTTP REST APIs predictable. A **safe** method does not modify server state (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS). An **idempotent** method produces the same result when called multiple times (GET, PUT, DELETE - calling DELETE multiple times on the same resource returns 404 after the first, but the server state is the same). POST is neither safe nor idempotent - calling POST multiple times creates multiple resources. This matters for retry logic: it is safe to retry idempotent requests on network failure; retrying a non-idempotent POST may create duplicate records. The `Idempotency-Key` header (popularized by Stripe's API) lets clients attach a unique key to POST requests so the server can detect and deduplicate retries.

resources.label: High Performance Browser Networking - Grigorik (free)
resources.note: HTTP/1, HTTP/2, QUIC in full detail

subtopics.title: Authentication: sessions, JWT, OAuth 2.0, and the security model
body: Authentication (verifying identity) and authorization (verifying permissions) are the most security-critical part of any backend. The vast majority of web application breaches exploit authentication or session management failures. Getting this right is not about following a checklist - it is about understanding the threat model at each layer.

**Session-based authentication**: on login, the server creates a session record in a store (Redis, a database) and returns a **session ID** in a `Set-Cookie` header. Subsequent requests send the session ID via the `Cookie` header; the server looks it up to identify the user. The session is stored server-side - the server controls it and can revoke it instantly. **Security requirements**: session IDs must be cryptographically random (128+ bits), stored in `HttpOnly` cookies (JS cannot read them, preventing XSS theft), `Secure` (HTTPS only), `SameSite=Lax` or `Strict` (prevents CSRF by only sending cookies on same-site or top-level-navigation requests). Rotate session IDs after privilege escalation (login, permission change) to prevent session fixation.

**JWT (JSON Web Tokens)**: a self-contained signed token encoding claims (`{ userId, role, exp }`). The server signs the token with a secret (HMAC-SHA256) or private key (RS256/ES256) and returns it to the client. On subsequent requests, the client sends the JWT (typically in `Authorization: Bearer <token>`). The server verifies the signature and reads the claims - no database lookup needed. The tradeoff: JWTs cannot be individually revoked before expiry. The standard pattern is **short-lived access tokens** (15 min) paired with **longer-lived refresh tokens** (7 days, stored in HttpOnly cookies). On access token expiry, the client uses the refresh token to get a new access token from a `/auth/refresh` endpoint. Compromised access tokens expire quickly; refresh tokens can be revoked via a server-side denylist.

**OAuth 2.0** is a *delegation* protocol (not an authentication protocol - that is OpenID Connect, which is built on OAuth 2.0). It allows a user to grant a third-party application limited access to their resources on another service, without sharing their credentials. The **PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange)** flow for SPAs and mobile apps: (1) the client generates a random `code_verifier`, hashes it as `code_challenge`, (2) redirects to the authorization server with `code_challenge` and `redirect_uri`, (3) user authenticates and consents, (4) authorization server redirects back with a `code`, (5) the client exchanges `code` + `code_verifier` for tokens. The `code_verifier` step prevents authorization code interception attacks because the exchange cannot be completed without the original verifier. Never use the implicit flow (returns tokens in URL fragments, which leak in browser history and server logs).

**Common auth vulnerabilities**: (1) **Storing passwords incorrectly** - never store plaintext or reversibly-encrypted passwords; use bcrypt/Argon2/scrypt with sufficient work factor. MD5 and SHA-1 are too fast for passwords (a GPU can check billions per second). (2) **Missing CSRF protection** - even with HttpOnly cookies, an attacker's page can trigger state-changing requests to your server via forms and `fetch` with credentials. Mitigations: `SameSite=Strict` cookies (breaks OAuth redirects), CSRF tokens (hidden form field synced with session), or the Double Submit Cookie pattern. (3) **JWT algorithm confusion** - never allow the `alg` header in a JWT to be `none` or to be changed to symmetric when asymmetric keys are used; validate the algorithm explicitly on the server. (4) **Insecure direct object references (IDOR)** - authorization checks must verify the *requesting user* has access to the *specific resource*, not just that they are authenticated.

subtopics.title: The middleware pattern and request lifecycle
body: The **middleware pattern** is the fundamental abstraction in Node.js web frameworks (Express, Koa, Hono, Fastify). A middleware function receives the request object, the response object, and a `next` function. It can modify the request, modify the response, terminate the request (send a response), or call `next()` to pass control to the next middleware. The full request-response cycle is a pipeline of middleware functions composed in sequence.

In Express, middleware is registered with `app.use()` or on specific routes. Global middleware runs for every request (logging, CORS, request parsing, rate limiting). Route-specific middleware runs only for matching routes (authentication guards, input validation, role checks). The order of `app.use()` calls matters - middleware runs in registration order. A common bug: placing `app.use(express.json())` after route handlers means those routes never get parsed JSON bodies. Another: an authentication middleware that calls `next()` on authentication failure instead of `res.status(401).json({error: 'Unauthorized'})` - code continues executing past the auth check.

**Input validation** at the boundary is non-negotiable. `zod` is the standard library: define a schema (`z.object({ email: z.string().email(), age: z.number().int().min(0) })`), parse untrusted input (`schema.parse(req.body)`), and the result is strongly typed and validated. `schema.safeParse()` returns a discriminated union instead of throwing, allowing graceful error handling. Validation schemas should be co-located with the route that uses them (not in a separate 'validators' directory) for readability. Validate query parameters, path parameters, headers, and body - all are untrusted. Never trust `Content-Type` headers or client-provided data about data sizes.

**Error handling** in Express requires a 4-argument middleware: `app.use((err, req, res, next) => {...})`. Any thrown error or `next(err)` call reaches this handler. The common mistake: not catching async errors and passing them to `next`. Wrapping async route handlers: `const asyncHandler = fn => (req, res, next) => Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next)` - this ensures async errors are caught and forwarded to the error middleware. Express 5 (now stable) handles this automatically without wrappers. Production error handlers should log the full stack trace server-side but return a generic message to the client - never expose stack traces or internal error messages to the client (information disclosure).

**Rate limiting** is a first-line defense against brute force attacks, credential stuffing, and DoS. `express-rate-limit` is the standard library: configure a window (e.g., 15 minutes) and max requests per window per IP. Apply a stricter limit to authentication endpoints (10 requests / 15 min for `/auth/login`, `/auth/register`). For distributed deployments, use a shared store (Redis) for rate limit counters - in-memory rate limiting only works on a single server instance. **Helmet** sets sensible security headers: `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` (prevents MIME-sniffing), `X-Frame-Options: DENY` (prevents clickjacking), `Strict-Transport-Security` (forces HTTPS), `Content-Security-Policy` (controls what resources can be loaded - most important, also most complex to configure correctly).

subtopics.title: Real-time communication: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling
body: HTTP is inherently request-response: the client asks, the server answers. Real-time features - chat, live notifications, collaborative editing, live dashboards - require the server to *push* updates to clients without the client asking. Three mechanisms exist, with different tradeoffs in complexity, latency, and infrastructure requirements.

**Long polling** is the simplest: the client sends a request, the server holds the connection open until it has data to send (or a timeout expires), then responds. The client immediately sends another request to wait for the next update. This simulates push using standard HTTP - works everywhere, traverses proxies and firewalls trivially. The cost: latency (one round trip per event), high request overhead (HTTP headers sent on every reconnect), and server memory (each held connection occupies a thread or async handle). Acceptable for low-frequency updates; unsuitable for high-frequency real-time.

**Server-Sent Events (SSE)** is a standardized, one-directional push protocol over HTTP. The server sets `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and keeps the response stream open, sending `data: <message>

` formatted chunks. The browser's `EventSource` API reconnects automatically on disconnect with the last received event ID (`Last-Event-ID` header), enabling reliable delivery. SSE is simpler than WebSockets, works over standard HTTP/2 (multiplexed with other requests on the same connection), and does not require special infrastructure. The limitation: server-to-client only. For use cases like live dashboards, feed updates, and notifications where the client does not need to send messages in real-time, SSE is the right choice.

**WebSockets** are full-duplex, persistent connections. The WebSocket handshake starts as an HTTP/1.1 upgrade request (`Upgrade: websocket`, `Connection: Upgrade`, `Sec-WebSocket-Key`). The server responds with 101 Switching Protocols, and the TCP connection is handed over to the WebSocket protocol - no more HTTP overhead per message. Messages are frames: text (UTF-8), binary (`ArrayBuffer`), ping/pong (keep-alive), and close. The WebSocket API in the browser: `ws.send(data)`, `ws.onmessage = (e) => {...}`. On the server, `ws` (Node.js) and `uWebSockets.js` are the standard libraries. **Socket.io** adds rooms, namespaces, automatic reconnection, and fallback to long polling - it is not a thin WebSocket wrapper; it has its own protocol and is not compatible with native WebSocket clients.

**Production WebSocket concerns**: horizontal scaling requires a message broker (Redis pub/sub with socket.io-redis, or a proper message queue) because a WebSocket connection is pinned to one server instance, and a message from any server must reach all connected clients regardless of which server they are on. Load balancers must support sticky sessions (IP hash or cookie-based affinity) to route reconnecting clients to the same server - or use a stateless WebSocket proxy that does not need session affinity. Rate limiting WebSocket messages is harder than HTTP (no built-in middleware per message); implement it application-level with a token bucket per connection. Connection limits: each WebSocket is an open file descriptor; a Node.js process on a modern machine can handle 50,000-100,000 concurrent WebSocket connections, constrained by memory (each connection state uses RAM) and file descriptor limits (ulimit).

topics.title: Databases & caching
body: SQL deeply (JOINs, **window functions**, CTEs, `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`), **PostgreSQL internals** (**MVCC**, vacuum, index types: B-tree/GiST/GIN/BRIN), index strategy (composite/covering/partial), the **N+1 problem**, **Redis** data structures + cache-aside, and transactions/isolation levels (phantom reads, deadlocks).
Kleppmann's *DDIA* is the one book that makes databases and distributed systems click as a coherent whole.

subtopics.title: SQL deep dive: window functions, CTEs, and query planning
body: SQL is a declarative language - you describe *what* you want, not *how* to compute it. The query planner decides the execution strategy. Understanding both the expressive power of SQL and how the planner works lets you write queries that are both correct and fast. The mental model: SQL queries are transformed by the planner into a **plan tree** of physical operators (seq scan, index scan, hash join, sort, aggregate), each with an estimated cost. `EXPLAIN` shows the plan; `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` executes the query and shows actual timings and row counts vs estimates.

**`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` reading**: the key numbers per node are `actual time` (wall clock, inclusive of children, in ms), `rows` (actual rows returned), and `loops` (how many times this node was executed - nested loop joins execute the inner side once per outer row). Width-indent shows the plan tree structure; children feed into parents. The overall query time is the root node's `actual time`. When `rows` in `EXPLAIN` (estimated) diverges wildly from `actual` (real), the planner made a bad decision based on stale statistics - run `ANALYZE tablename` to update statistics. `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)` also shows cache hits vs disk reads, which is often more diagnostic than time.

**Window functions** are the most powerful SQL feature most developers underuse. `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC)` assigns a row number within each department ordered by salary, without collapsing rows like GROUP BY does. The `OVER` clause defines the **window**: `PARTITION BY` divides rows into groups, `ORDER BY` within the window defines the frame ordering. `RANK()` (gaps in numbering for ties), `DENSE_RANK()` (no gaps), `LAG(col, 1)` (previous row's value), `LEAD(col, 1)` (next row's value), `SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY date)` (running total per user). The frame clause `ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` (default for ordered windows) defines which rows are included in aggregate calculations - crucial for running aggregates vs total aggregates.

**CTEs (Common Table Expressions)** defined with `WITH` make complex queries readable by naming intermediate results. In PostgreSQL, `WITH` CTEs are **optimization fences** by default: PostgreSQL materializes the CTE result as a temporary table, preventing the planner from pushing predicates from the outer query into the CTE. `WITH (MATERIALIZED)` forces this; `WITH (NOT MATERIALIZED)` tells PostgreSQL it can inline the CTE and optimize freely (the behavior for simple CTEs in PostgreSQL 12+). **Recursive CTEs** (`WITH RECURSIVE`) enable graph traversal and tree queries: enumerate all ancestors of a node, compute reachability, process hierarchical data. The recursive CTE has a base case (non-recursive term), a union, and the recursive term that references the CTE itself - PostgreSQL executes this iteratively until no new rows are produced.

**JOINs**: `INNER JOIN` returns rows where both sides match. `LEFT JOIN` returns all rows from the left table and matched rows from the right (NULLs where no match). `FULL OUTER JOIN` returns all rows from both sides. The key insight for optimization: JOINs are not inherently slow - a join on indexed foreign keys over millions of rows can be faster than a correlated subquery. The N+1 problem is the failure mode: issuing one query to get N rows, then one query per row to get related data - N+1 total queries instead of one. The fix is a join or a `WHERE id IN (...)` batch query. ORMs often generate N+1 by default; use `include`/`eager_loading` directives to force a join.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke - SQL indexing and performance

subtopics.title: PostgreSQL internals: MVCC, WAL, and the vacuum process
body: PostgreSQL uses **Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC)** to allow readers and writers to operate without blocking each other. The core mechanism: every row has two hidden system columns, `xmin` (the transaction ID that created this row version) and `xmax` (the transaction ID that deleted or updated this row version, or 0 if the row is 'live'). When a transaction reads a row, it checks visibility: is `xmin` committed and older than this transaction's snapshot? Is `xmax` either 0 (not deleted) or uncommitted? This visibility check determines whether the row version is visible to this transaction.

When you `UPDATE` a row in PostgreSQL, the existing row is not modified in place. Instead, the old row version has its `xmax` set to the current transaction ID, and a *new row version* is inserted with `xmin` set to the current transaction and the updated values. Both versions coexist on disk. Similarly, `DELETE` only sets `xmax` - the row data remains on disk until **vacuum** cleans it up. This dead-tuple accumulation is the source of **table bloat**: a heavily-updated table can have a large fraction of its disk space occupied by dead row versions that are no longer visible to any running transaction.

**VACUUM** is PostgreSQL's garbage collection process. Regular `VACUUM` marks dead tuples as reclaimable for future inserts (but does not return space to the OS). `VACUUM FULL` rewrites the entire table to compact it (returns space to OS but holds an exclusive lock - avoid on production without a maintenance window). **Autovacuum** runs automatically in the background, triggered by row change thresholds. The key autovacuum parameters: `autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor` (trigger vacuum when dead tuples exceed this fraction of the table) and `autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor` (trigger analyze for statistics refresh). On high-write tables, autovacuum often cannot keep up - tune these parameters aggressively and monitor `pg_stat_user_tables` for `n_dead_tup` and `last_autovacuum` timestamps.

**WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** is the mechanism for crash recovery and replication. Every data modification in PostgreSQL is first written to the WAL (an append-only log of changes) before modifying the actual data files. On crash, PostgreSQL replays the WAL from the last checkpoint to recover to a consistent state. **Streaming replication** works by continuously shipping WAL records from the primary to replicas, which replay them to stay in sync. **Logical replication** decodes WAL records into row-level changes (the logical level - insert/update/delete with values), enabling selective replication, multi-master scenarios, and cross-version replication.

**Transaction IDs (XIDs)** in PostgreSQL are 32-bit unsigned integers that wrap around at ~2 billion. **Transaction ID wraparound** is one of the most catastrophic PostgreSQL failure modes: if a transaction ID counter wraps around without sufficient vacuuming, old live rows become 'invisible' to the database (because the XID comparison arithmetic makes them appear to be in the 'future'). PostgreSQL will freeze a table (refuse all writes and issue emergency warnings) 40 million transactions before wraparound. Monitor `age(datfrozenxid)` in `pg_database` - if this number approaches 2 billion, you have a vacuum problem that needs immediate attention. In 2019, Sentry had a multi-hour outage due to XID wraparound approaching on a high-write table with inadequate autovacuum.

subtopics.title: Index strategy: B-tree, GiST, GIN, partial, covering, and composite indexes
body: An index is a data structure maintained separately from the table that allows the database to find rows matching a predicate without scanning the full table. All indexes have a maintenance cost (writes are slower because indexes must be updated; disk space is consumed) and a read benefit (queries that use the index are much faster). The art of indexing is choosing which queries to optimize and what index structure supports them.

**B-tree indexes** (the default in PostgreSQL) support equality (`=`), range (`<`, `>`, `BETWEEN`), prefix matching (`LIKE 'foo%'` but not `LIKE '%foo'`), and `NULL` comparisons (`IS NULL`). They are balanced trees; lookups are O(log n) and range scans are O(log n + k) for k results. B-tree indexes store values in sorted order, which is why they can serve ORDER BY efficiently. The index also stores the heap tuple ID (ctid) for each entry so the database can fetch the actual row after finding it in the index.

**Composite indexes** (indexes on multiple columns) follow the **leftmost prefix rule**: an index on `(a, b, c)` can be used for queries on `a`, `a,b`, or `a,b,c`, but not on `b` alone or `b,c` alone. Column order in a composite index matters: put the most selective column first for point queries; put the column used in range predicates last. **Covering indexes** (`CREATE INDEX ON t (a, b) INCLUDE (c)`) store additional columns that are not part of the search key but are needed by the query. If a query selects only `c` after filtering on `(a, b)`, a covering index satisfies the entire query from the index without a heap lookup - a significant speedup for read-heavy workloads.

**Partial indexes** index only the rows that satisfy a `WHERE` clause: `CREATE INDEX ON orders (created_at) WHERE status = 'pending'`. This is dramatically smaller (and faster to update) than a full index if most orders are not pending. It is perfect for 'hot path' queries that filter on a low-cardinality status column combined with other predicates. **GiST (Generalized Search Tree)** is a framework for spatial, range, and full-text indexes - PostgreSQL uses it for geometric types, `tsrange`, and as a fallback for text search. **GIN (Generalized Inverted Index)** is designed for values that contain multiple elements (JSONB fields, arrays, `tsvector` for full-text search). A GIN index on a JSONB column allows efficient queries like `WHERE data @> '{"role": "admin"}'` on millions of rows.

**Index-only scans** happen when all columns needed by a query are in the index (either as key columns or INCLUDE columns), so PostgreSQL does not need to read the heap at all. This requires the **visibility map** to confirm that the page is all-visible (all tuples on the page are visible to all transactions) - which is maintained by vacuum. On tables with recent inserts/updates, many pages may not be all-visible, forcing heap fetches even for index-only queries. This is why 'after autovacuum, the same query got much faster' - vacuum updated the visibility map, enabling index-only scans. Monitor `pg_stat_user_indexes` for `idx_scan`, `idx_tup_read`, and `idx_tup_fetch` to identify unused indexes (candidates for removal) and expensive indexes.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke - Index anatomy

subtopics.title: Redis data structures, caching patterns, and expiration strategies
body: **Redis** is an in-memory data structure store that supports strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, HyperLogLog, streams, and geospatial indexes. Everything is in memory (by default), which gives sub-millisecond latency for most operations. Redis is single-threaded for command processing (I/O multiplexed), which means all commands are serialized - no concurrent mutation bugs, no deadlocks, but also no automatic parallelism. The single thread is fast enough for hundreds of thousands of operations per second on a single instance.

**Cache-aside (lazy loading)** is the most common caching pattern: (1) on a cache miss, fetch from the database, store in cache with an expiry (TTL), return to the caller; (2) on a cache hit, return from cache directly. The tradeoff: the first request after a cache miss or expiry is slow (the 'cold start' problem). **Write-through** updates the cache on every write so it is always warm - eliminates cache misses but adds latency to writes. **Write-behind (write-back)** buffers writes in the cache and asynchronously flushes to the database - lower write latency but risk of data loss if Redis goes down before flushing. The choice depends on read/write ratio and durability requirements.

**Key design** is critical for Redis performance and maintainability. Keys are strings; use a colon-separated namespace convention: `user:12345:profile`, `session:abc123`, `rate_limit:ip:192.168.1.1`. Key length directly affects memory - very long keys waste RAM. **TTL strategy**: every cached key should have a TTL. Forgetting TTLs leads to stale data accumulating indefinitely ('zombie keys'). **Cache stampede** (or 'thundering herd'): when a popular key expires simultaneously for many requests, all requests experience a cache miss at once, flood the database with identical queries, and overwhelm it. Mitigations: probabilistic early expiration (re-cache before TTL expires when a random check fires), mutex lock on the first miss (others wait for the first request to populate the cache), or background refresh (never actually expire, always refresh before TTL in the background).

**Redis data structure applications**: **Sorted sets** (`ZADD`, `ZRANGE`) are perfect for leaderboards (score as the sort key), rate limiting windows (timestamp as score), and priority queues. **Pub/Sub** (`PUBLISH`/`SUBSCRIBE`) is a fire-and-forget message bus - subscribers must be connected to receive messages (no persistence, no delivery guarantees). **Streams** (`XADD`, `XREAD`) are append-only logs with consumer groups, acknowledgments, and message replay - the correct choice when you need durable, at-least-once pub/sub within Redis. **Lua scripts** (`EVAL`) execute atomically on the Redis server - use them for read-modify-write operations that must be atomic (e.g., check-and-set, rate limiter increment-and-check) without multi-key transactions.

**Redis persistence**: `RDB` (Redis Database Backup) takes periodic snapshots and writes them to disk - fast restarts, some data loss between snapshots. `AOF` (Append Only File) logs every write command and replays on restart - near-zero data loss, slower restarts, larger files. `AOF` with `fsync: everysec` (flush to disk once per second) is the common production choice: at most 1 second of data loss. **Redis Sentinel** provides high availability with automatic failover for a standalone Redis setup. **Redis Cluster** shards data across multiple nodes (16384 hash slots), providing both horizontal scaling and automatic failover. Multi-key commands (like transactions and Lua scripts) can only operate on keys in the same hash slot in cluster mode, which constrains key design.

subtopics.title: Transactions, isolation levels, and distributed data consistency
body: A **database transaction** is a sequence of operations that appear to execute atomically (all or nothing - **Atomicity**), in isolation from other concurrent transactions (**Isolation**), and with changes that survive system failures (**Durability**). These are three of the four ACID properties - **Consistency** is the application-level invariant that transactions are expected to preserve (not a database mechanism in itself). Understanding isolation levels is essential because database defaults are often weaker than you assume.

**Isolation levels** (SQL standard): **Read Uncommitted** (can read uncommitted writes from other transactions - 'dirty reads' - almost never used), **Read Committed** (default in PostgreSQL and most databases: sees only committed data, prevents dirty reads but allows 'non-repeatable reads' - the same row read twice in a transaction may have different values if another transaction committed in between), **Repeatable Read** (prevents non-repeatable reads - same row returns the same value within a transaction, but 'phantom reads' are possible: a query returning a set of rows may return different rows if another transaction inserts/deletes qualifying rows), **Serializable** (prevents all anomalies - transactions appear to execute serially, highest isolation, most expensive).

PostgreSQL's implementation differs from the SQL standard: `READ COMMITTED` is the default. `REPEATABLE READ` prevents phantom reads (PostgreSQL's MVCC makes this cheaper than standard SQL would imply). `SERIALIZABLE` in PostgreSQL uses **Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI)** - an optimistic concurrency control technique that detects dangerous read-write dependencies at commit time and aborts transactions that would produce a non-serializable execution. This is safer than pessimistic locking and does not require explicit `SELECT FOR UPDATE`. SSI aborts transactions rather than deadlocking, so applications must handle `SQLSTATE 40001` (serialization failure) by retrying.

**Deadlocks** occur when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs, forming a cycle. Transaction A holds a lock on row 1 and wants row 2; Transaction B holds row 2 and wants row 1 - both wait forever. PostgreSQL detects deadlocks by periodically checking for wait cycles and aborting one transaction (the 'victim') with error code `40P01`. Deadlock prevention strategy: always acquire locks in a consistent global order (e.g., always lock rows by ascending primary key). Use `SELECT FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` (fail immediately if the lock is not available) or `SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` (skip rows that are locked - useful for job queues where any pending job is equivalent).

**Two-phase commit (2PC)** extends atomicity across multiple databases or services. Phase 1 (prepare): all participants write 'prepared' state to their WAL and vote yes/no. Phase 2 (commit or abort): the coordinator sends commit if all voted yes, abort if any voted no. The weakness: if the coordinator crashes between phases, participants are left in 'prepared' state blocking indefinitely (the 'in-doubt transaction' problem). For microservices that span multiple databases, 2PC is rarely used due to the blocking risk and coordination complexity. **Saga patterns** (sequence of local transactions with compensating actions) are the alternative: eventually consistent, no distributed locks, but requires application-level compensation logic for rollbacks.

WAL/journaling and MVCC mirror filesystem durability mechanics
Time-series stores (kdb+/Parquet) are the quant analogue of these DBs

topics.title: Performance engineering
body: **Core Web Vitals** (LCP, INP, CLS - what affects each), the **critical rendering path** (HTML→CSSOM→render tree→layout→paint→composite), resource loading (preload/prefetch/priority hints), V8 optimization (hidden classes, deopt triggers), bundle splitting, image formats (AVIF/WebP), caching (`stale-while-revalidate`, CDN edge), and Service Workers.
Measure with the DevTools Performance panel - taking an LCP from >4s to <1.5s is the canonical reps.

subtopics.title: Core Web Vitals: LCP, INP, and CLS - what drives each metric
body: **Core Web Vitals** are Google's user-centric performance metrics, used as ranking signals in search. They measure real user experience, not synthetic benchmarks. Collecting them at the p75 (75th percentile) of real user sessions via the Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX) determines a page's search ranking. Tools: Chrome DevTools, Lighthouse (synthetic), PageSpeed Insights (both), and the `web-vitals` JS library for field measurement from real users.

**LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)** measures when the largest element visible in the viewport finishes rendering. Typically the hero image, banner text, or main content area. Target: under 2.5s. The LCP element is determined dynamically as the page renders - it can change as larger elements load. What delays LCP: server response time (TTFB), render-blocking resources (synchronous `<script>` and `<link rel='stylesheet'>` in `<head>`), slow resource loading (unoptimized images, uncompressed fonts), and client-side rendering (the JS-rendered content only becomes LCP after JS downloads, parses, and executes). Fixes: optimize TTFB (server-side or CDN), preload the LCP image (`<link rel='preload'>`), use `fetchpriority='high'` on the LCP image, eliminate render-blocking scripts, serve images in next-gen formats (AVIF/WebP).

**INP (Interaction to Next Paint)** replaced FID (First Input Delay) as the responsiveness metric in 2024. It measures the latency of all interactions (click, tap, keyboard input) during the page lifetime and reports the worst one at the p98 percentile. Target: under 200ms. INP measures from user input to the next frame being painted - it includes: the time the browser processes the event (input delay, which includes any queued tasks ahead of the event callback), the event callback's duration itself, and the presentation delay (how long after the callback runs until the browser paints the next frame). Long tasks (> 50ms) on the main thread inflate input delay - any task running when the user taps delays their event being processed. Fixes: break long tasks with `scheduler.yield()` or `setTimeout`, defer non-critical work, virtualize long lists, use `useTransition` in React.

**CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)** measures unexpected visual instability - elements moving around on the page as it loads. Target: under 0.1. Calculated as the sum of individual layout shift scores (impact fraction × distance fraction) that are not preceded by user interaction. The most common causes: images without explicit `width`/`height` attributes (the browser does not reserve space until the image loads), dynamically injected content above existing content (ads, banners, cookie notices), web fonts causing text reflow when the custom font loads (FOUT - Flash of Unstyled Text). Fixes: always set explicit dimensions on images and video (`aspect-ratio: 16/9` in CSS is equivalent), use `font-display: optional` or preload fonts, reserve space for dynamic content with min-height, load ads in reserved containers.

**TTFB (Time to First Byte)** is not a Core Web Vital but is the foundation all others rest on. A slow server (>800ms TTFB) makes every other metric worse - LCP cannot start before the HTML arrives. Improvements: edge caching (serve from a CDN PoP near the user), efficient server-side rendering (avoid unnecessary DB queries during SSR), database query optimization, and HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 (reduces connection overhead). **FCP (First Contentful Paint)** (when the first text or image paints) is a leading indicator of LCP - if FCP is slow, LCP will be too. The 'waterfall' view in DevTools shows the dependency chain between resources that determines the critical path.

subtopics.title: The critical rendering path and resource loading optimization
body: The **critical rendering path** is the sequence of steps the browser must complete before it can paint the first pixel. Understanding it reveals why certain resource patterns block rendering and how to eliminate those blocks. The steps: (1) parse HTML → build DOM, (2) fetch and parse CSS → build CSSOM, (3) combine DOM + CSSOM → render tree (only visible nodes with computed styles), (4) layout (compute geometry for each node), (5) paint (record draw calls), (6) composite (rasterize layers and merge on the GPU). Steps 1-5 happen on the main thread; compositing can happen on the compositor thread.

**Render-blocking resources**: synchronous `<script>` tags in `<head>` pause HTML parsing until the script downloads and executes (because the script might call `document.write()` which can modify the parsing state). CSS stylesheets are also render-blocking: the browser will not start rendering until all referenced CSS is loaded and parsed (because rendering before CSS would show a flash of unstyled content). **Solutions**: place synchronous scripts at the bottom of `<body>` (they execute after the HTML is parsed), or use `async` (download in parallel, execute as soon as downloaded, non-blocking but unpredictable execution order) or `defer` (download in parallel, execute after HTML parsing completes, preserves execution order). CSS cannot be deferred, but it can be split: inline critical CSS (`<style>` in `<head>` for above-the-fold styles) and load the rest with `<link media='print'>` + JS swap trick.

**Resource hints**: `` tells the browser 'this resource will be needed soon, start downloading it at high priority now' - without blocking rendering. Use it for the LCP image, the main font files, and any JS/CSS fetched dynamically that is immediately needed. `` is a low-priority hint: 'this resource may be needed for the next navigation, download it when idle'. Use it for the next page's bundle in single-page apps. `` opens a TCP + TLS connection to an origin early (before the browser knows it will need a resource from there), saving 100-300ms per cross-origin resource. `fetchpriority='high'` on the LCP image `` tag explicitly tells the browser to download it at high priority, overriding the browser's default priority heuristics.

**Image optimization** has the highest ROI of any front-end performance work. AVIF and WebP provide 30-80% smaller file sizes vs JPEG at equivalent visual quality. Use the `` element to provide multiple formats with fallback: ` `. Responsive images with `srcset` and `sizes` attributes let the browser choose the appropriate resolution based on the viewport and device pixel ratio: ``. `loading='lazy'` defers off-screen image loading until the user scrolls near them - use on all images except the LCP image (which should load eagerly). Image CDNs (Cloudinary, Imgix, Next.js Image component) automate all of this.

**Font loading optimization**: web fonts cause FOIT (Flash of Invisible Text - text invisible until the font loads) or FOUT (Flash of Unstyled Text - text shown in fallback font, then swapped). `font-display: swap` shows fallback text immediately, then swaps when the font loads (FOUT). `font-display: optional` only uses the custom font if it loads within a very short window - effectively eliminates layout shift but may show fallback on slow connections. Preloading font files with `<link rel='preload' as='font' crossorigin>` starts downloading them during HTML parsing. Size-adjust and ascent-override CSS properties (part of `@font-face`) let you tune the fallback font metrics to match the custom font, minimizing CLS when the swap occurs.

subtopics.title: JavaScript performance: V8 hidden classes, deopt triggers, and long tasks
body: V8 (Google's JS engine, used in Chrome and Node.js) is optimizing at two levels: the **interpreter** (Ignition, compiles JS to bytecode and interprets it - fast startup, lower peak speed) and the **JIT compiler** (TurboFan for hot code paths, Maglev for mid-tier - compiles frequently-executed bytecode to native machine code based on observed types). The type feedback V8 collects while interpreting is what enables JIT optimization. When observed types change, the JIT must deoptimize (throw away compiled code and fall back to interpreter), which is expensive. Understanding what causes deoptimization is the key to JS performance.

**Hidden classes** are V8's optimization for object property access. When you create `const obj = { x: 1, y: 2 }`, V8 assigns it a hidden class describing its shape (which properties it has, in what order). Objects with the same shape share a hidden class, and property accesses on monomorphic call sites (always the same hidden class) are optimized to direct memory offsets. **Polymorphic** sites (2-4 shapes) use an inline cache; **megamorphic** sites (5+ shapes) fall back to a hash map lookup. To maintain good hidden class behavior: (1) always initialize all properties in the constructor in the same order, (2) do not add or delete properties after construction, (3) avoid `delete obj.prop` (it degrades the hidden class).

**Deoptimization triggers**: (1) **Type changes** - if a function processes a number the first 1000 times and then receives a string, V8 must deoptimize the number-optimized version and generate a more general one. Keep function arguments to a consistent type. (2) **Bounds checks** - array out-of-bounds access deoptimizes array operations from fast packed-SMI mode to 'holes' mode. (3) **`arguments` object** in a function body prevents optimization of that function (use rest parameters `...args` instead). (4) **`try/catch`** blocks prevent inlining in some V8 versions. (5) **`eval()`** prevents almost all optimization of the enclosing function (V8 cannot know what `eval` will do to the scope). The `--trace-opt` and `--trace-deopt` V8 flags (in Node.js) log optimization and deoptimization events.

**Long tasks** are the main thread's primary enemy for INP and animation smoothness. A long task is any task that takes more than 50ms. The browser cannot process input events or paint frames while a task is running. Causes: large array operations, complex DOM manipulations, synchronous `localStorage`, blocking parsers. **Solutions**: `scheduler.yield()` (the modern browser API) yields control back to the browser mid-task: `for (let i = 0; i < largeArray.length; i++) { if (i % 100 === 0) await scheduler.yield(); process(largeArray[i]); }`. For off-main-thread heavy computation, move work to a Web Worker and postMessage results back. **`requestIdleCallback`** runs a callback when the browser is idle between frames - appropriate for analytics, prefetching, non-urgent work. Do not use it for time-sensitive updates.

**Memory and garbage collection**: V8's garbage collector is generational: new objects are in the **Young generation** (nursery + intermediate), collected frequently and cheaply; surviving objects are promoted to the **Old generation**, collected less frequently with a full mark-sweep. **Memory leaks** in JS always involve unintentionally retaining references to objects that should be freed: event listeners not removed, closures in long-lived objects capturing large data, WeakMap misuse, globals accidentally created by forgetting `let`/`const`. Use Chrome DevTools Memory tab: take a heap snapshot, trigger the suspected leak, take another snapshot, and look at the delta - objects that increased in count are the leak. `WeakRef` and `FinalizationRegistry` are the tools for holding references without preventing GC, useful for caches that should release memory under pressure.

subtopics.title: Bundle optimization: code splitting, tree shaking, and build analysis
body: The size of JavaScript downloaded and parsed by the browser has an outsized impact on performance - especially on mobile devices where CPUs are slower and connections are limited. The goal is to deliver the minimum amount of JavaScript needed to make the current page interactive, and defer the rest. Bundle optimization is the set of techniques for achieving this.

**Tree shaking** removes dead code - exports that are imported nowhere in the application. It works only with ES Modules (static import/export - the bundler can determine the import graph statically). For tree shaking to be effective: avoid side effects in module-level code (importing a module should not automatically run code with effects), declare `sideEffects: false` in `package.json` for pure libraries, use named exports over default-exporting objects (a barrel export `export * from './components'` re-exports everything and can defeat tree shaking unless bundlers implement re-export elimination).

**Code splitting** divides the bundle into multiple chunks that are loaded on demand. Dynamic `import()` is the mechanism: `const Chart = lazy(() => import('./Chart'))` - when this line executes, the browser fetches and executes the Chart module's chunk. React's `lazy` + `Suspense` makes this declarative. Route-based splitting (load each page's JS only when that route is visited) is the highest-ROI split - a user visiting the homepage should not download the dashboard's JS. Framework routers (Next.js, React Router, TanStack Router) do this automatically per route. Vite/Rollup `manualChunks` lets you control chunk grouping: put stable third-party libraries in a `vendor` chunk (cached separately, not invalidated when app code changes).

**Bundle analysis**: `rollup-plugin-visualizer` and `webpack-bundle-analyzer` generate an interactive treemap of what is in the bundle. This is the single most useful tool for identifying bloat. Common findings: a utility library being imported as a whole instead of a specific function (lodash - should use `lodash-es` and specific imports), a large component library bringing in all its components (should use `babel-plugin-import` or the library's own tree-shaking support), multiple copies of the same library (peer dependency mismatch - different packages depending on different versions), polyfills for browsers you do not need to support. **Source map explorer** overlays the size breakdown on actual source files, making it easier to see which of your own code is bloated.

**Module preloading and chunk prefetching**: when a dynamic chunk is likely to be needed soon, prefetch it: `<link rel='modulepreload' href='/chunk.js'>` (for immediate use - higher priority than prefetch) or Vite/Webpack's `/* webpackPrefetch: true */` magic comment in dynamic imports (inserts a `<link rel='prefetch'>` automatically). This fills the user's network idle time with the next likely chunks, making dynamic imports feel instant. **HTTP/2 push** was the server-initiated version of this, but it was deprecated in favor of `103 Early Hints` (a preload header the server can send before the full response), which is supported in Chrome and Cloudflare Workers.

resources.label: web.dev - Reduce JavaScript payloads with code splitting

subtopics.title: HTTP caching, CDN architecture, and stale-while-revalidate
body: Caching is a hierarchy: memory (fastest, smallest), disk (browser cache), CDN edge (network hop away), and origin server (the actual application). The goal is to serve each resource from the closest, cheapest layer. Correct cache headers are the instruction set for this hierarchy. Misconfigured caching leads to stale content (cache too long), or no caching (cache too short/disabled), both bad.

**Cache-Control** directives: `max-age=N` tells the browser (and intermediate caches) to consider the response fresh for N seconds. `s-maxage=N` overrides `max-age` for shared caches (CDNs) only. `immutable` tells the browser never to revalidate the resource while it is fresh - used for content-addressed assets (hashed filenames) that are guaranteed to never change. `no-store` prevents any caching. `no-cache` caches but requires revalidation with the origin before using the cached version (used with `ETag`/`Last-Modified`). `private` prevents CDNs from caching (for user-specific content). The strategy for most SPAs: `Cache-Control: no-store` for `index.html` (always fetch the latest entry point), `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` for hashed JS/CSS/image assets (cache forever, the hash changes when content changes).

**`stale-while-revalidate`** is the most useful cache directive most developers do not use. `Cache-Control: max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=600` means: use the cached response if it is under 60 seconds old (fresh), use the stale cached response immediately (but trigger a background revalidation) if it is between 60 and 660 seconds old, and fetch normally if it is over 660 seconds old. The user gets the instant response of a cache hit, and the cache is refreshed in the background - they see the stale response once, then the fresh one on the next request. This is the HTTP equivalent of SWR (stale-while-revalidate), the React data fetching pattern popularized by the `swr` library.

**CDN architecture**: a CDN (Cloudflare, Fastly, Akamai, CloudFront) has hundreds of **Points of Presence (PoPs)** geographically distributed. A user's request is routed via **anycast** (a single IP address announced from multiple locations; BGP routes the request to the nearest PoP) to the nearest PoP. If the PoP has a cached copy, it responds directly; otherwise, it fetches from the origin and caches the response. **Cache invalidation** on CDNs: when you deploy new content, you must purge the CDN cache for the affected URLs, or wait for the TTL to expire. Most CDNs support instant purge by URL, tag, or prefix. The correct strategy: short TTLs (minutes) for HTML (may need to invalidate urgently), immutable caching for hashed static assets (no invalidation needed - the URL changes when content changes).

**Edge functions** (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge Functions, Fastly Compute) run code at CDN PoPs, not at the origin server. Use cases: A/B testing (serve different variants based on a cookie, without a round trip to origin), personalization (inject a user's name into HTML at the edge), redirect logic (handle thousands of URL redirections without origin roundtrips), request coalescing (when many users request the same uncached resource simultaneously, serve the first request and queue the rest until the response is cached). Edge functions typically use the **Web Streams API** for streaming responses and the **Cache API** for programmatic cache management - the same APIs as Service Workers, running on the server side.

connections.note: V8 hidden classes / JIT deopt are the compiler-runtime cause of JS perf
Same 'measure, find the real bottleneck' discipline as CPU perf
INP is dominated by render cost - React optimization lands here

topics.title: System design at scale
body: Load balancing (consistent hashing), horizontal scaling (stateless services, shared cache), CDN internals (anycast, PoPs, origin shield), DB scaling (read replicas, connection pooling, partitioning), the **CAP theorem** (what it actually constrains), **message queues** (Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS, at-least-once vs exactly-once), and event sourcing/CQRS.
This is the vocabulary of building things that don't fall over at 100k req/s.

subtopics.title: Load balancing: algorithms, consistent hashing, and health checking
body: **Load balancing** distributes incoming requests across multiple server instances to prevent any single instance from being overwhelmed. The load balancer sits between clients and servers, terminates connections (or proxies them), and forwards requests based on an algorithm. Load balancers operate at **Layer 4** (TCP/UDP - fast, minimal processing, forward based on IP+port) or **Layer 7** (HTTP - can inspect headers, URLs, cookies; enables routing by path, host-based routing, header injection). Most web applications use L7 load balancing (nginx, HAProxy, AWS ALB, Cloudflare).

**Load balancing algorithms**: **Round Robin** - requests distributed sequentially to each server. Simple, no state. Problem: does not account for server capacity or current load. **Weighted Round Robin** - each server gets a proportion of traffic based on assigned weight (useful when servers have different capacities). **Least Connections** - send each request to the server with the fewest active connections. Better for long-lived connections. **Random** - works surprisingly well at scale (birthday paradox: with many servers and many requests, it converges to even distribution). **IP Hash** - the client IP determines the server (sticky sessions without application state). Problem: uneven distribution if many clients share an IP (e.g., behind a corporate NAT).

**Consistent hashing** solves the problem of server addition/removal in distributed caches. In naive modulo hashing (`server = hash(key) % n`), adding or removing one server remaps almost all keys. Consistent hashing places servers and keys on a hash ring (a modular space from 0 to 2^32). A key is served by the first server clockwise from its hash position. Adding a server only remaps keys between the new server and its predecessor - typically 1/n of keys. Removing a server only remaps that server's keys to the next server on the ring. **Virtual nodes** (each server is represented by multiple points on the ring) distribute the load more evenly and allow finer-grained capacity weighting.

**Health checking** is how a load balancer knows which backends are available. **Active health checks**: the load balancer periodically sends a probe request (HTTP GET to `/health`) and marks the server as up/down based on the response. **Passive health checks**: the load balancer monitors real traffic and marks a server unhealthy when it sees consecutive errors (5xx responses, connection timeouts). The combination is standard: passive checks catch failures fast; active checks detect servers that are up but not serving traffic. **Circuit breakers** (at the application level, e.g., Hystrix, Resilience4j) open when the failure rate exceeds a threshold, returning a cached fallback immediately instead of waiting for the slow/failing upstream - preventing cascading failures where slow upstream calls exhaust a service's thread pool.

**Session affinity (sticky sessions)**: some applications store session state in-process (not in a shared store), requiring all requests from the same user to reach the same server. Load balancers implement this with cookie-based affinity (`AWSALB` cookie on ALB) or IP hash. The problem: if the assigned server fails, the session is lost. The better solution: externalize session state (Redis, a database) and allow any server to serve any session - this is what 'stateless services' means, and it is the prerequisite for true horizontal scaling.

subtopics.title: Horizontal scaling: stateless services, connection pooling, and the shared-nothing architecture
body: **Horizontal scaling** (scaling out) means adding more instances of a service rather than making each instance more powerful (vertical scaling). It requires that service instances are **stateless**: no instance-local state that other instances cannot access. All state must live in a shared external store (a database, Redis, object storage). This is the 'shared-nothing architecture' - each application server is identical and interchangeable. You can add or remove instances without affecting correctness, enable zero-downtime deployments (deploy new instances, drain old ones), and recover from instance failures without data loss.

**Session externalization** is the most common stateless migration. Moving sessions from in-memory to Redis: every request from any server can read any session. The Redis key is the session ID; the value is the session data. TTL is the session expiration. The cost: a Redis read per authenticated request. The mitigation: use an in-process LRU cache of session data (a bounded-size dictionary) that is populated on first Redis hit and invalidated when the session changes - this 'local session cache' eliminates Redis round trips for most requests while keeping the system stateless at the application layer.

**Database connection pooling** is essential for horizontal scaling. Each database connection is expensive to establish (TCP handshake + TLS + authentication protocol) and consumes resources on the database server (memory for process or thread, file descriptors). Without pooling, each application server opens new connections per request - N servers × M concurrent requests = N×M connections, quickly overwhelming the database's connection limit (PostgreSQL default: 100 connections). A connection pool maintains a fixed pool of open connections and lends them to requests: `pgBouncer` (external, process-level pooler for PostgreSQL) or application-level poolers (node-postgres's `Pool`, SQLAlchemy's `pool_size`). **PgBouncer transaction-mode pooling** allows thousands of client connections (from many application servers) to share a small number of actual database connections, because a connection is only held for the duration of a database transaction.

**Caching layers**: beyond session state, shared caching reduces database load. The pattern: check Redis first, fall back to the database, populate Redis on miss. **Cache invalidation** is the hard part: when data changes in the database, stale cached versions must be invalidated or updated. Strategies: TTL-based (accept some staleness, simple), event-based invalidation (the write path explicitly deletes/updates the cache key on every write, keeping cache consistent), or cache-aside with short TTLs for high-consistency requirements. **CDN caching** as an application layer: for API responses that are the same for all users (public data, catalog pages), a CDN cache can serve millions of requests with zero database hits - this is the 'edge caching' strategy.

**Auto-scaling** is the operational mechanism for horizontal scaling. Cloud providers (AWS Auto Scaling Groups, Kubernetes HPA) monitor metrics (CPU utilization, request latency, queue depth) and automatically add or remove instances. The critical parameters: scale-out threshold (trigger when CPU > 70% for 3 minutes), scale-in threshold (trigger when CPU < 30% for 10 minutes), minimum instances (always keep N running for availability), and cooldown period (wait 5 minutes after a scaling action before the next one, to avoid oscillation). **Pre-warming**: auto-scaling reacts to existing load; for predictable traffic spikes (sales, releases, scheduled jobs), pre-warm by scaling out before the spike.

subtopics.title: CAP theorem and distributed consistency models
body: **CAP theorem** (Brewer's theorem): a distributed data store cannot simultaneously provide more than two of: **Consistency** (every read receives the most recent write or an error), **Availability** (every request receives a response, but it might not be the most recent), and **Partition tolerance** (the system continues to operate when network partitions prevent nodes from communicating). Since network partitions are inevitable in any distributed system, the real choice is between consistency and availability *during a partition*.

The theorem is frequently misapplied. In normal operation (no partition), a system can provide both consistency and availability. The partition scenario is the only time you must choose. A **CP system** (prefer consistency): during a partition, refuses to answer rather than risk returning stale data (e.g., ZooKeeper - will not respond if it cannot confirm quorum). A **CA system** is essentially impossible in a distributed setting - any distributed system must tolerate partitions or it is a single-node system. An **AP system** (prefer availability): during a partition, continues to answer with potentially stale data (e.g., DynamoDB in its default mode, Cassandra, CouchDB).

**PACELC** is a more nuanced model than CAP. Even without a partition (the normal case), distributed systems face a tradeoff: **Latency** vs **Consistency**. A system that synchronously replicates writes to all replicas before acknowledging is strongly consistent but has high write latency. A system that acknowledges writes asynchronously and replicates in the background has low latency but eventual consistency. PACELC says: if Partition, choose Availability or Consistency; Else (no partition), choose Latency or Consistency. DynamoDB is PA/EL: available during partition, eventually consistent normally. Megastore/Spanner is PC/EC: consistent during partition, consistent normally at the cost of higher latency (using Paxos/TrueTime).

**Eventual consistency** means that if no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. 'Eventually' can mean milliseconds (in the same datacenter), seconds (across regions), or longer if there are failures. This is often acceptable for social feeds, product views, search indexes. It is not acceptable for financial balances, inventory counts (overselling), or any 'compare-and-swap' operation. **Conflict resolution** is the problem eventual consistency pushes to the application: if two nodes accept conflicting writes (user updates their profile on two different partitions), how do you merge them? Last-Write-Wins (LWW) by timestamp is simple but lossy. **CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types)** are data structures designed so that concurrent modifications always merge automatically without conflicts - counters, sets, and document editors built on CRDTs converge correctly regardless of write order (Figma's multiplayer, Notion's real-time editing use this).

**Distributed transactions** across multiple services are the hardest part. True ACID transactions across service boundaries require distributed coordination (2PC - two-phase commit), which introduces blocking failure modes (if the coordinator fails mid-commit, participants are stuck). Production systems generally avoid 2PC. **Saga pattern**: a long-running transaction is a sequence of local transactions, each publishing events to trigger the next. If a step fails, compensating transactions undo previous steps. The tradeoff: eventual consistency (not immediate), and compensation logic must be written for every step that might need rollback. **Outbox pattern** makes the event publication reliable: write the event to a local 'outbox' table in the same database transaction as the data change, then a separate process reads and publishes the events - guaranteeing at-least-once publication without distributed coordination.

resources.label: Designing Data-Intensive Applications - Kleppmann (Ch. 9: Consistency and Consensus)

subtopics.title: Message queues: Kafka, RabbitMQ, delivery guarantees, and event sourcing
body: **Message queues** decouple producers from consumers: a producer sends a message to the queue and continues without waiting for the consumer to process it. Consumers read from the queue at their own pace. This decoupling enables: **load buffering** (absorb traffic spikes - the queue fills during peaks, drains during troughs), **async processing** (expensive operations like email sending, video encoding, PDF generation happen in the background), **fan-out** (one event triggers multiple independent consumers), and **retry** (failed processing attempts retry the message without losing it).

**RabbitMQ** is a traditional **broker**: messages are routed through exchanges (direct, fanout, topic, headers) to queues, consumers acknowledge individual messages, and acknowledged messages are deleted. RabbitMQ is designed for complex routing, per-message TTLs, dead-letter queues (for failed messages), and priority queues. Messages are not retained long-term; once consumed and acknowledged, they are gone. Best for: task queues, RPC patterns, complex routing, short-lived messages. The delivery semantics: at-most-once (fire and forget, no ack), at-least-once (ack on processing, retry on failure - consumer must be idempotent), exactly-once (requires transactional messaging or deduplication, rarely supported natively).

**Apache Kafka** is a **distributed log**: producers append events to a **topic** (divided into **partitions**). A partition is an ordered, immutable, append-only sequence of records. Each record has an offset. Consumers maintain their own offset and read records sequentially - Kafka does not delete records on read. Records are retained for a configurable period (days, weeks, or forever by policy). This fundamental difference from a queue enables: **replay** (re-read historical events, re-process with new logic), **multiple independent consumer groups** (each group reads all events, maintaining its own offset - events are not 'consumed' once), and **long-term event storage** as the source of truth.

**Kafka's ordering guarantees**: within a partition, messages are strictly ordered. Across partitions (within a topic), there is no ordering guarantee. The **partition key** determines which partition a record goes to (consistent hashing). To guarantee ordering for all events related to a user (e.g., all orders for user ID 123), use the user ID as the partition key - all events for that user go to the same partition and are delivered in order. The tradeoff: this creates potential hot partitions if the key distribution is skewed (all events for one popular entity go to one partition, while others are idle). **Consumer groups**: in a group, each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer; adding consumers up to the number of partitions increases parallelism. More consumers than partitions → idle consumers.

**Event sourcing** is the architectural pattern where the system's state is derived from an **immutable log of events** rather than mutable records. Instead of updating a `users` table row, you append `UserUpdated` events to an event log. The current state is computed by replaying events. Benefits: complete audit log, ability to replay history with new logic, temporal queries ('what was the state at time T'), and event-driven integration with other systems. Kafka is the natural infrastructure for event sourcing at scale. **CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)** is the common companion: write operations (commands) go through the event log; read operations query a **read model** - a materialized view maintained by consuming events. The read model is optimized for the query patterns, potentially denormalized and in a different store (Elasticsearch for search, PostgreSQL for relational queries). The cost: eventual consistency between the write and read models.

subtopics.title: Database scaling: read replicas, sharding, and connection management
body: A single database server eventually becomes the bottleneck as traffic grows. The scaling path is: optimize queries → add indexes → increase hardware (vertical scaling) → add read replicas → introduce caching → shard. Each step is significantly more complex than the previous. Start by measuring where the bottleneck actually is before scaling; premature optimization is the enemy of operational simplicity.

**Read replicas** (streaming replication) replicate the primary's WAL to replica instances that serve read queries. Traffic is split: writes go to the primary, reads go to replicas. In most web applications, reads vastly outnumber writes (social feeds, product pages, dashboards are all read-heavy), so read replicas can multiply effective read throughput proportionally to the number of replicas. The critical limitation: **replication lag** - replicas may be slightly behind the primary (milliseconds in the same datacenter, seconds or more across regions). Reads that must see the latest write (e.g., reading back immediately after writing) must go to the primary. ORMs need explicit 'read from primary' override for these cases.

**Sharding (horizontal partitioning)** splits a table across multiple database instances, where each instance holds a subset of rows. The sharding key determines which shard holds a row (hash of user ID, range of timestamps, geographic region). Sharding enables near-linear throughput scaling and lets each shard's data fit in RAM for in-memory workloads. The costs are severe: cross-shard queries (JOINs across shards require scatter-gather queries to all shards and application-level merging), no cross-shard transactions (distributed transaction coordination required), and schema changes must be applied to all shards. Shard rebalancing (when a shard grows too large and needs to be split) is operationally complex. For most applications, sharding is the last resort, reached only after all other options are exhausted.

**Vertical partitioning** is an alternative to horizontal sharding: move different tables or column groups to different databases or services. A social application might put user data in PostgreSQL, activity feeds in Cassandra (write-heavy, time-ordered), messages in a document database, and search data in Elasticsearch. This is the microservices data model: each service owns its data store optimized for its access patterns. The integration challenge: cross-service joins require API calls or event-driven denormalization, not a database JOIN.

**PgBouncer and connection management at scale**: with horizontal scaling, connection count grows linearly with application instances. 100 application servers × 10 pool connections each = 1000 PostgreSQL connections - often exceeding PostgreSQL's `max_connections` limit (typically 100-300). PgBouncer acts as a multiplexer: thousands of application connections share a handful of actual PostgreSQL connections (often as few as 10-20 per database). In **transaction mode** (the most aggressive), a PostgreSQL connection is only occupied during a database transaction - between transactions, it is available to the pool. This requires prepared statements to be disabled or handled carefully (prepared statements are connection-specific in PostgreSQL, so they do not survive pool reuse). **RDS Proxy** (AWS) provides similar functionality as a managed service.

connections.note: VMs vs containers is the deployment substrate of these systems
Replication/partitioning/CAP are database-scaling decisions
ML inference at scale is a system-design problem (queues, autoscaling)

topics.title: Browser internals, WASM & the edge
body: Rendering pipeline internals (Blink/WebKit, compositor thread, layer promotion), **V8 deep dive** (TurboFan, Maglev, Sparkplug), **HTTP/3 + QUIC** (0-RTT, connection migration), **WebAssembly** (linear memory, WASI, porting C/Rust to the browser), and edge computing (Cloudflare Durable Objects/KV, V8 isolates).
WASM in particular is where web dev meets low-level systems - you ship compiled C/Rust to a browser sandbox.

subtopics.title: Blink rendering pipeline: from DOM to pixels
body: **Blink** (Chrome's rendering engine, a fork of WebKit) turns a document (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) into pixels on screen through a multi-stage pipeline. The stages are not always sequential - Blink's architecture increasingly parallelizes them across threads and frames. Understanding the pipeline is prerequisite to GPU-composited animation, layer promotion strategies, and diagnosing jank (dropped frames).

The stages in order: **Parse** (HTML → DOM, CSS → CSSOM, parallel to HTML parsing where possible), **Style** (cascade computed styles to every DOM node - produces the **ComputedStyle** for each node), **Layout** (compute geometry: position, size, float clearance, for every element - produces the **LayoutObject** tree, one per rendered element), **Pre-paint** (determine which elements need their own **paint layer**, compute transforms and clips), **Paint** (record draw operations for each element into display lists, not actual pixels), **Compositing** (upload display lists as **textures** to the GPU, arrange layers, and let the GPU composite them into the final frame). Compositing runs on the **compositor thread**, separate from the main thread, which is why CSS transforms and opacity transitions are smooth even when the main thread is busy.

**Layer promotion** is the mechanism for GPU compositing. Certain CSS properties cause an element to be promoted to its own **compositing layer** (a separate texture in GPU memory): `transform`, `opacity` with animation, `will-change`, `position: fixed`, elements with a 3D transform or overlapping stacking context, canvas elements. When an animation only changes a promoted layer's transform or opacity, the compositor thread handles it without involving the main thread - achieving 60fps even during JavaScript execution. The cost: each compositing layer consumes GPU memory (the layer's visual region as a texture). Too many layers (over-promotion) can exhaust GPU memory and actually reduce performance. Use `will-change: transform` judiciously on elements that are about to animate, and remove it after the animation completes.

**Forced layout (reflow)** is when reading certain layout properties (e.g., `element.offsetWidth`, `element.getBoundingClientRect()`) causes the browser to synchronously compute layout before returning the value, because the layout may be dirty (a previous JS mutation invalidated it). The worst case is **layout thrashing**: alternating between reads and writes in a loop: `for (const el of elements) { el.style.width = el.parentElement.offsetWidth + 'px'; }`. Each iteration reads `offsetWidth` (forces layout), writes `style.width` (invalidates layout), reads again (forces layout again), and so on. The fix: **batch reads before writes** - collect all the values first, then apply all the mutations. Libraries like FastDOM formalize this pattern.

**Paint invalidation**: when a CSS property changes that cannot be handled purely by the compositor (e.g., `background-color`, `border-radius`, `box-shadow`), the affected element's paint layer must be **repainted** (display list re-recorded and texture re-uploaded to GPU). Repaints are expensive. **Compositor-only properties** that never cause repaint: `transform`, `opacity`, `filter` (some values). The rule for performant CSS animations: animate only `transform` and `opacity`. Animating `width`, `height`, `top`, `left`, `background-color`, or any other property causes layout or paint, not just compositing. Animating `left: 200px` fires layout every frame at 60fps; animating `transform: translateX(200px)` fires compositing only.

subtopics.title: V8 internals: Ignition, TurboFan, Maglev, and Sparkplug
body: V8's compilation pipeline has evolved from a simple two-tier system (interpreter + optimizing compiler) to a sophisticated multi-tier system that trades compilation cost for peak throughput at different stages of execution. Understanding the tiers explains performance behavior that would otherwise be mysterious: why code gets faster after warming up, why certain patterns cause sudden slowdowns, and why startup time and peak throughput require different optimization strategies.

**Ignition** is V8's bytecode interpreter. It compiles JavaScript to a compact bytecode format (not machine code) and interprets it with a register-based virtual machine. Ignition also collects **type feedback**: as it executes, it records what types it observes at each operation site (e.g., 'the + operator at offset 42 has always seen two integers'). This feedback is stored in **feedback vectors** per function. Ignition is fast to start (compiling JS to bytecode is fast, much faster than to machine code) and correct (handles all JS semantics without assumptions), making it the baseline for all code.

**Sparkplug** (added in V8 9.1) is a non-optimizing compiler between Ignition and TurboFan. It compiles bytecode to machine code quickly (without optimization) by doing a simple one-pass translation of each bytecode instruction to machine code, using the same feedback structures Ignition maintains. The machine code runs faster than interpreted bytecode (~20-30% improvement) with near-zero compilation cost. Functions that are moderately hot (called frequently but not hot enough to justify TurboFan's expensive compilation) run in Sparkplug.

**TurboFan** (optimizing JIT compiler) compiles very hot functions to highly optimized machine code using the type feedback collected by Ignition. Key optimizations: **inline caching** (call site specialized for the observed types - property access becomes a direct load at a known offset), **inlining** (replace a function call with the function's body to eliminate call overhead and enable further optimization across the inlined code), **escape analysis** (determine that an object does not escape the function, allocate it on the stack instead of the heap - eliminates GC pressure), **loop optimizations** (vectorization, loop unrolling). TurboFan represents the function as a **Sea of Nodes** graph (nodes are operations, edges are data/control dependencies) which enables global optimizations beyond what a linear IR allows.

**Maglev** (added in V8 11.2, Chrome 117) sits between Sparkplug and TurboFan. It is an optimizing compiler that uses type feedback but is significantly faster to compile than TurboFan. Maglev targets medium-hot functions that currently toggle between Sparkplug-compiled code and TurboFan's expensive compilation. It provides most of TurboFan's benefit (inlining, IC optimization) at a fraction of the compilation time, reducing the 'compilation cliff' where startup is slow because TurboFan is busy compiling everything. The practical impact: faster startup for large applications without sacrificing peak throughput for hot code paths that still escalate to TurboFan.

**Deoptimization**: when TurboFan's assumptions about types prove wrong (a function it compiled assuming integer arguments receives a string), it **deoptimizes** - discards the optimized code and falls back to Ignition bytecode for this call site, then potentially re-optimizes with updated type feedback. Deoptimizations are logged with `--trace-deopt` in Node.js.
