The cost of deoptimization is the one-time fallback, but if a function oscillates between optimized and deoptimized (because it is sometimes called with one type and sometimes another), it may end up in **'optimization disabled'** state, running perpetually in Ignition. Writing 'monomorphic' functions (consistent argument types, consistent object shapes) is the key to preventing this.

subtopics.title: HTTP/3 and QUIC: multiplexing over UDP, 0-RTT, and connection migration
body: **HTTP/3** runs over **QUIC** instead of TCP, addressing fundamental limitations of TCP that HTTP/2 exposed. HTTP/2 solved HTTP-layer head-of-line blocking (multiple streams over one connection) but exposed TCP-layer head-of-line blocking: a lost packet stalls all streams because TCP's delivery guarantees mean the receiver cannot process data beyond the missing packet until it is retransmitted. On high packet-loss networks (mobile, satellite), HTTP/2's throughput degrades significantly. QUIC implements its own transport layer in userspace on top of UDP, with stream multiplexing built in at the transport level - a lost packet stalls only the stream it belongs to, not all streams.

**QUIC's connection establishment** is significantly faster than TCP+TLS. TCP requires a 1.5-RTT handshake; TLS 1.2 requires 2 additional RTTs (2 round trips for TLS handshake). Total: 3.5 RTTs before the first byte of application data. TLS 1.3 over TCP reduces this to 1 RTT for TLS. QUIC combines transport and crypto handshake: **1-RTT** for new connections (both QUIC transport parameters and TLS 1.3 are established in one round trip). **0-RTT resumption**: for a returning client with a cached session ticket, QUIC can include application data in the first packet, before any server round trip. The server processes this '0-RTT data' immediately. The security tradeoff: 0-RTT data is **replay-vulnerable** (an attacker who captures the first packet can replay it). QUIC 0-RTT should only be used for idempotent requests (GET, HEAD - not POST/PUT mutations).

**Connection migration** is QUIC's most operationally significant feature. In TCP, a connection is identified by the 4-tuple: (src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port). Changing any component (switching from WiFi to cellular mid-request) terminates the TCP connection and requires reconnecting. QUIC identifies connections by a **Connection ID**, a client-chosen opaque byte string included in every packet. When the client's network changes (new IP, new port), it continues sending packets with the same Connection ID; the server recognizes the new address and migrates the connection without interruption. HTTP/3 transfers in progress survive network transitions - a large file download or video stream continues seamlessly as a laptop moves between access points.

**QUIC's congestion control** is pluggable (unlike TCP, where the kernel's CC algorithm is fixed system-wide). The default is CUBIC (same as TCP), but Cloudflare and Google have deployed custom CC algorithms tuned for their networks. **BBR** (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip propagation time) models the bottleneck bandwidth and RTT explicitly rather than reacting to packet loss, providing higher throughput on high-bandwidth-delay-product networks (long-fat pipes, satellite). QUIC's userspace implementation also enables faster iteration on CC algorithms without kernel updates.

**HTTP/3 adoption and infrastructure**: as of 2024, HTTP/3 is supported by all major browsers and by Cloudflare, Google, and Fastly (which termination it at the edge and proxy HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1 to origin servers). Server-side implementations: `nginx` (via the `quiche` module), `caddy` (built-in), `h2o`. The primary deployment model: the CDN handles HTTP/3 with users, and the CDN-to-origin connection is HTTP/2 (eliminating the need to upgrade origin servers). The `Alt-Svc` HTTP response header (`Alt-Svc: h3=':443'; ma=86400`) advertises HTTP/3 support to clients that connected via HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 - the client upgrades to HTTP/3 on the next connection. QUIC uses UDP port 443; enterprise firewalls that block UDP may prevent HTTP/3, causing the browser to fall back to HTTP/2.

resources.label: Cloudflare - HTTP/3: the past, the present, and the future
High Performance Browser Networking - QUIC chapter (free)

subtopics.title: WebAssembly: linear memory, WASI, and the compilation toolchain
body: **WebAssembly (WASM)** is a binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine. It is not JavaScript - it is a compilation target for languages like C, C++, Rust, Go, and Zig that compiles to a compact binary format that the browser's WASM engine executes. The goals: near-native performance for compute-intensive tasks in the browser, and a universal, sandboxed runtime for executing untrusted code safely. WASM is now also a server-side runtime format (WASI, Cloudflare Workers, Fastly Compute), positioning it as 'the new Docker' for portable, sandboxed server computation.

**WASM's memory model**: each WASM module has a **linear memory** - a flat, byte-addressable block of memory starting at address 0. It is like an `ArrayBuffer` visible from both the WASM code and JavaScript (as a `WebAssembly.Memory` object). C/Rust/C++ code compiled to WASM uses this linear memory for its heap (managed by `malloc`/`free` in C, Rust's allocator, etc.), stack frames, and global variables. There is no garbage collector in the WASM spec itself (the GC proposal adds references but is opt-in). JavaScript and WASM share memory through the linear memory buffer: pass data by writing into the memory buffer, then passing the pointer (an integer offset) to the WASM function. Strings require encoding/decoding across the JS-WASM boundary - a common source of overhead that the **Component Model** proposal addresses with typed interfaces.

**Compilation toolchain for C/C++**: `emscripten` is the standard. It wraps LLVM (using the `wasm32-unknown-emscripten` target) and provides a JavaScript 'glue' file that initializes the WASM module, handles memory management, and polyfills POSIX APIs (file system via a virtual FS in memory/IndexedDB, networking via WebSockets). For pure computation (no I/O), you can compile a C library directly: `emcc math_lib.c -o math_lib.wasm -s SIDE_MODULE=1`. **Rust to WASM** via `wasm-pack` + `wasm-bindgen` is cleaner: `wasm-bindgen` generates JavaScript bindings for exported Rust functions, handling type conversions for strings, arrays, and JavaScript objects. The resulting package is usable as a normal npm module.

**WASI (WebAssembly System Interface)** is the standard system interface for WASM outside the browser. Where the browser provides DOM and Web APIs, WASI provides POSIX-like system calls: file I/O, networking, clock, randomness, environment variables - implemented as WASM host functions. The key property: **capability-based security** - a WASM module only has access to the filesystem paths and network sockets explicitly passed to it by the host, regardless of what its source code attempts. This makes WASM+WASI an excellent sandboxed execution environment for plugins, serverless functions, and user-defined computation. Runtimes: `wasmtime` (Bytecode Alliance), `wasmer`, `WasmEdge`. The comparison to containers: WASM starts faster (~microseconds vs hundreds of milliseconds), is more portable (no Linux kernel dependency), and is more sandboxed (no host OS calls except those explicitly allowed).

**WASM in the browser - real use cases**: image/video processing (FFmpeg compiled to WASM, used by Figma, Zoom, Squoosh), codec decoding (AV1, HEVC without native support), PDF rendering (PDF.js moved some operations to WASM for speed), scientific computation (NumPy for the web via Pyodide - CPython compiled to WASM), and game engines (Unity, Godot export to WASM). Performance characteristics: WASM can achieve 1.5-2x native speed for compute-bound workloads (optimization headroom due to missing SIMD in early versions, now partially addressed by the **SIMD proposal** - 128-bit SIMD instructions available in all browsers since 2021). The overhead is primarily at the JS-WASM boundary (function calls, memory passing). **Threads in WASM** are built on `SharedArrayBuffer` and `Atomics` - multi-threaded WASM requires cross-origin isolation headers on the page.

resources.label: Bytecode Alliance - WASI spec and runtime
Lin Clark - WebAssembly: Neither fast nor slow (nuanced perf analysis)

subtopics.title: Edge computing: Cloudflare Workers, Durable Objects, and V8 isolates
body: **Edge computing** moves computation from centralized origin servers to the CDN edge - physically close to the user. Rather than a request traveling to a datacenter 200ms away, it is handled at a CDN PoP a few milliseconds away. Edge workers are small, fast-starting JavaScript (or WASM) programs that run at hundreds of locations worldwide. They have access to: the incoming request and full response control, the CDN cache, key-value stores, databases at the edge, and the ability to fetch from origin or other services.

**Cloudflare Workers** use **V8 isolates** as their execution model, not separate processes or VMs. A V8 isolate is a lightweight, isolated JavaScript heap and context. It can be created in microseconds (vs hundreds of milliseconds for a container or process). Each Worker invocation runs in an isolate that is sandboxed from other Workers - no shared memory, no shared global state. After the request completes, the isolate can be reused for the next request to the same Worker (warm start) or discarded. The key advantage over Lambda functions: cold starts are measured in microseconds (isolate creation), not hundreds of milliseconds (container initialization). This makes Cloudflare Workers genuinely serverless - no idle warm-up tricks required.

**Workers' limitations** reflect the isolate model: no `setTimeout` or `setInterval` (replaced by `waitUntil` for background tasks), no Node.js built-ins (no `fs`, `path`, `crypto` - though the Web Crypto API is available), no shared state between requests (isolates are independent). The maximum CPU time per request is 50ms in free tier, up to 30 seconds with the paid tier. Network calls (`fetch`) are async and counted against wall clock time, not CPU time. Workers communicate with each other via service bindings (direct invocation, zero-latency) or via KV/Durable Objects.

**Cloudflare KV** is an eventually-consistent, globally-distributed key-value store. Writes propagate to all edge locations within ~60 seconds. Reads are cached at each edge location - extremely fast (sub-millisecond) but potentially serving slightly stale data. Perfect for: configuration, feature flags, public data that changes infrequently, cached API responses. Not suitable for: counters (concurrent writes overwrite each other), inventory (read-modify-write requires consistency). **Durable Objects** solve the strong consistency problem. Each Durable Object is a single-instance, globally unique, stateful object that runs in exactly one V8 isolate at a time. It has a transactional storage API (`storage.get`, `storage.put` with ACID semantics within the object). All requests to a given Durable Object are serialized through that single instance - providing strong consistency for the object's state, while still running at Cloudflare's edge (the instance is placed near the majority of requesters). Used for: collaborative document editing, chat rooms, rate limiters, distributed locks, game lobbies - any use case requiring strong consistency over a specific unit of data.

**The edge computing model vs traditional serverless**: Lambda (AWS) runs a container per invocation, starts cold containers in 100-1000ms, and has the full Node.js runtime. Workers run isolates per invocation, cold start in <1ms, but have a restricted API surface (browser-like APIs, not Node). The design philosophy of Workers is to embrace the web platform APIs (fetch, Web Crypto, streams, URL, Cache API) and reject Node.js's historical OS-centric API set. This is intentional: Web APIs are portable across browser, server, and edge; Node APIs are not. The trend: **WinterCG** (Web-interoperable Runtimes Community Group) standardizes the shared Web API subset that all runtimes (Node, Deno, Bun, Cloudflare, Fastly, Vercel) should implement identically - making edge-deployable code a first-class concern in the JS ecosystem.

connections.note: WASM's sweet spot is shipping compiled Rust/C to the browser
WASM is an LLVM backend target; the toolchain is compiler tech
WebGPU brings Vulkan-style GPU access to the browser

topics.title: API design: REST, GraphQL, gRPC, and contracts
body: An API is a contract between two systems. The choice of style (REST, GraphQL, gRPC) is not about taste - it is about the access patterns, performance requirements, and who is on the other side of the contract. REST is the web's native language, GraphQL solves the over/under-fetch problem, and gRPC solves high-throughput service-to-service communication. All three are wrong in different situations.
The deeper skill is not picking the right protocol - it is designing APIs that are predictable, evolvable, and impossible to misuse. Bad APIs are bugs that ship to users.

subtopics.title: REST design: resource modeling, HTTP semantics, versioning, and pagination
body: **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style, not a standard. Most 'REST APIs' are actually **HTTP APIs** that follow REST conventions loosely. True REST (as Fielding defined it) requires stateless communication, uniform interface, resource-based addressing, and HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State - responses include links to related actions). In practice, HATEOAS is rarely implemented and the other three constraints are what 'RESTful' means colloquially. The principles that matter: resources are nouns, HTTP verbs carry semantics, status codes communicate outcome, and the URL structure reflects domain concepts.

**Resource modeling**: URLs identify resources, not actions. `POST /payments` creates a payment (noun). `GET /payments/123` retrieves it. `PATCH /payments/123` partially updates it. Never `POST /createPayment` or `GET /getPayment?id=123` - these are RPC-over-HTTP, not REST. Plural nouns for collections (`/users`, `/orders`), singular for the item (`/users/42`). **Nested resources** for owned relationships: `/users/42/orders` (orders belonging to user 42). But avoid deep nesting (`/users/42/orders/7/items/3/reviews`) - it couples the URL structure to the domain model. Two levels deep is the practical limit; for deeper access use flat resources with filter params (`/reviews?orderId=7&itemId=3`). **Sub-resources for actions that do not fit CRUD**: `/payments/123/capture`, `/subscriptions/456/cancel` - these are controller resources, a REST pragmatism for operations that are not simply CRUD on a resource state.

**HTTP semantics** that REST relies on: `GET` is safe and idempotent (no side effects, can be cached and retried freely). `POST` is neither (use for creation and non-idempotent actions). `PUT` replaces a resource entirely (idempotent - calling PUT twice produces the same state). `PATCH` partially updates (semantically non-idempotent unless using a format like JSON Patch that is explicitly idempotent). `DELETE` removes a resource (idempotent - deleting the same resource twice should return 404 on the second call, but the server state is identical). **Status codes**: 201 Created on successful POST (with `Location: /resources/new-id` header), 204 No Content on successful DELETE or PUT with no response body, 400 for validation errors (with a body explaining what is wrong), 409 Conflict for state machine violations (cannot capture an already-captured payment), 422 Unprocessable Entity for semantic errors (the JSON is valid but the data does not make sense).

**API versioning** is the problem of evolving a public API without breaking existing clients. Strategies: **URL versioning** (`/v1/users`, `/v2/users`) - explicit, easy to route, but breaks REST's resource identity principle (the same resource has two URLs). Most practical choice. **Header versioning** (`Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2`) - clean URLs, harder to test in a browser. **Query param versioning** (`/users?version=2`) - easy but pollutes query params. The real solution is **backward-compatible evolution**: add fields (clients ignore unknown fields), never remove or rename fields without a deprecation period, never change a field's type or semantics. **Sunset headers** (`Sunset: Sat, 31 Dec 2025 23:59:59 GMT`) warn clients that a version is being retired. **Breaking change** = clients must update code. Non-breaking = clients work without changes. The discipline is maintaining this distinction rigorously.

**Pagination** is mandatory for any collection endpoint. **Offset pagination** (`?offset=0&limit=20`): simple, allows jumping to any page, but has the **phantom read** problem (items added or removed while paginating cause duplicates or skips) and is inefficient at deep offsets (the database must scan and discard offset rows). **Cursor pagination** (`?cursor=<opaque_token>&limit=20`): the cursor encodes the position in the result set (a timestamp, an ID, or an encrypted composite). No phantom reads (the cursor points to a stable position in the ordered set), efficient at any depth (start from the cursor position, not from offset 0), but cannot jump to arbitrary pages. Return the `nextCursor` in the response and `null` when there are no more pages. **Response envelope**: wrap paginated results in `{ data: [...], meta: { total, nextCursor, limit } }` or follow a standard like JSON:API. Always document the maximum page size and enforce it server-side.

resources.label: Stripe API - the canonical reference for REST API design
resources.note: the gold standard REST API to learn from

subtopics.title: GraphQL: schema design, resolvers, N+1, and DataLoader
body: **GraphQL** is a query language for APIs and a runtime for executing those queries. The core idea: instead of the server defining fixed endpoints that return fixed shapes, the client specifies exactly what data it needs in a typed query, and the server returns exactly that - no more, no less. This eliminates **over-fetching** (REST returning 50 fields when you need 3) and **under-fetching** (needing multiple REST round trips to assemble a view). The schema is a contract written in SDL (Schema Definition Language) that both client and server agree on.

**Schema design**: the schema is the API's surface area. Types are the nouns (`type User { id: ID!, name: String!, email: String! }`), queries are read operations (`type Query { user(id: ID!): User }`), mutations are write operations (`type Mutation { createUser(input: CreateUserInput!): User }`), and subscriptions are real-time streams (`type Subscription { messageAdded(channelId: ID!): Message }`). Design the schema around the **client's data needs**, not the database schema - these often diverge. Use interfaces and unions for polymorphic types. Connections (cursor-based pagination) are the standard pattern for list fields: `users: UserConnection` where `UserConnection { edges: [UserEdge], pageInfo: PageInfo }`. Nullable vs non-nullable (`String` vs `String!`) is a critical contract: non-nullable means the field is always present if the parent resolves (failure to resolve it is a schema violation).

**Resolvers** are the functions that fetch data for each field. Each field in the schema has a resolver. The execution engine calls the root resolver for the query (`Query.user(id: '42')`), then recursively calls child resolvers for each field on the result. The naive implementation has a catastrophic **N+1 problem**: querying a list of users, each with their orders, results in 1 query for the user list + N queries for each user's orders. The resolver for `User.orders` calls `db.orders.findByUserId(user.id)` - called once per user in the list.

**DataLoader** is the standard solution to the N+1 problem. DataLoader collects all the individual loads requested in a single tick of the event loop and **batches** them into a single database query. The `User.orders` resolver calls `ordersLoader.load(user.id)` instead of directly querying the database. DataLoader collects all user IDs requested during the current execution tick, calls the batch function once with all IDs (`WHERE user_id IN (1, 2, 3, ...)`), and resolves each individual load with its result. The key insight: GraphQL execution is synchronous per field but async between fields - all sibling resolver calls in the same execution level happen in the same event loop tick, so DataLoader can batch them. Each DataLoader instance should be **per-request** (not a singleton) to avoid leaking data between users.

**GraphQL in production**: **persisted queries** (client sends a hash of the query instead of the full text, server maps hashes to pre-approved queries) reduce request size and prevent arbitrary queries on public APIs. **Query complexity analysis** limits expensive queries: assign cost to fields (resolving a list of 100 items costs 100, each nested field multiplies), reject queries that exceed a complexity budget. **Depth limiting** prevents deeply nested queries (`user { friends { friends { friends { ... } } } }`). **Schema stitching / federation** (Apollo Federation) composes multiple GraphQL services into a single schema: the `User` type is owned by the user service, the `Order` type by the order service, and the gateway stitches them together. Each subgraph declares which types it owns and how to resolve cross-service references.

subtopics.title: gRPC and Protocol Buffers: streaming, performance, and service contracts
body: **gRPC** (Google Remote Procedure Call) is a high-performance, strongly-typed RPC framework built on HTTP/2 and **Protocol Buffers** (protobuf). Unlike REST (text-based HTTP/1.1 with JSON) or GraphQL (also text-based), gRPC is binary-framed (protobuf encoding), multiplexed (HTTP/2 streams), and typed at compile time from a shared `.proto` schema. The result: 5-10x smaller payloads than JSON, faster serialization/deserialization (protobuf is 6x faster than JSON in benchmarks), and full bidirectional streaming. gRPC is the dominant protocol for internal microservice communication; it is rarely exposed to browsers directly (though gRPC-Web is a subset that works).

**Protocol Buffers** define the schema: `message User { int64 id = 1; string name = 2; repeated string emails = 3; }`. Field numbers (not names) are what actually go on the wire - this is how protobuf achieves compact encoding and why you can add new fields without breaking old consumers (they see an unknown field number and ignore it). **Never reuse a field number for a different type** - an old client will misinterpret the bytes. **Reserved** field numbers prevent accidental reuse after a field is removed. Protobuf encoding: integers use variable-length encoding (varint), small numbers use fewer bytes, strings are length-prefixed, repeated fields are encoded as multiple values. A typical JSON object might be 200 bytes; the same data in protobuf is 20-40 bytes.

**gRPC service definition** in `.proto`: `service UserService { rpc GetUser (GetUserRequest) returns (User); rpc ListUsers (ListUsersRequest) returns (stream User); rpc UpdateUser (stream UpdateUserEvent) returns (UpdateUserResponse); rpc Chat (stream ChatMessage) returns (stream ChatMessage); }`. Four call types: **Unary** (one request, one response - like a function call), **Server streaming** (one request, stream of responses - useful for live feeds, large result sets), **Client streaming** (stream of requests, one response - useful for file upload, batch inserts), **Bidirectional streaming** (both sides stream - chat, collaborative editing, real-time games). The `protoc` compiler generates typed client and server stubs in any supported language (Go, Python, Rust, Java, Node.js, C#) from the `.proto` file - the contract is enforced at compile time.

**gRPC in practice**: **Interceptors** (middleware equivalent) handle cross-cutting concerns: authentication (validate JWT in a unary interceptor), logging (log all RPC calls with timing), tracing (inject trace context into headers), retry (automatically retry on transient failures). **Deadlines/timeouts**: every gRPC call should have a deadline. The deadline propagates through service calls - if the upstream call times out, downstream calls are automatically cancelled. This is the idiomatic way to prevent timeout cascades. **Reflection** allows clients to discover the server's schema at runtime (useful for gRPC tools like grpcurl and Postman's gRPC support, which can query the schema without the `.proto` file). **Load balancing** for gRPC requires client-side load balancing or an L7-aware proxy (Envoy, nginx) - standard L4 load balancers route the whole HTTP/2 connection to one backend, defeating the point of multiple servers.

**When to use each protocol**: **REST** for public APIs consumed by unknown clients (browsers, mobile apps, third-party integrations) - wide tooling support, human-readable, browser-native. **GraphQL** for APIs consumed by a product's own frontend - eliminates over/under-fetch, enables rapid frontend iteration without backend changes, best when the frontend team drives query requirements. **gRPC** for internal service-to-service communication where performance matters - the binary protocol, streaming, and compile-time types reduce bugs at service boundaries. **gRPC-Web** for browser access to gRPC services (requires a proxy like Envoy or grpc-gateway). **WebSocket** for real-time, bidirectional browser-to-server (gRPC bidirectional streaming does not work in browsers without gRPC-Web and a proxy).

subtopics.title: API security: authentication patterns, rate limiting, and API gateways
body: API security is a different threat model from web application security. A web app's primary surface is authenticated users interacting via a browser with CSRF protection, SameSite cookies, and CSP. An API's primary surface is programmatic clients - mobile apps, third-party integrations, internal services - with machine credentials (API keys, service tokens). The attack vectors are: credential theft and abuse, over-permissioned tokens, brute-force, credential stuffing, and abuse of legitimate credentials (an API key leaked in a GitHub repo, scraped by a bot within seconds).

**API key management**: API keys are long-lived credentials. Best practices: never include in URL query parameters (logged by proxy servers and browser history - `?apiKey=xxx` is a data breach waiting to happen); pass in `Authorization: Bearer <key>` or a custom header. Hash API keys before storing (treat them like passwords - a database breach should not expose working keys). Generate keys with sufficient entropy (256 bits minimum). Implement key rotation (customers can issue a new key and revoke the old one). Scope keys by permission (a read-only key cannot create or delete resources). Log all API key usage with the key ID (not the raw key) for audit trails. Immediately detect and alert on anomalous usage patterns (a key normally making 100 calls/hour suddenly making 10,000/hour).

**Rate limiting patterns for APIs**: **Token bucket** (refills tokens at a constant rate; requests consume tokens; excess requests are rejected when the bucket is empty) is the most common for API rate limiting - it allows short bursts while enforcing a sustained rate. **Leaky bucket** (requests queue up and are processed at a constant rate; excess requests are dropped) smooths bursty traffic but does not allow bursts. **Fixed window** (count requests per window; reset at window boundary) is simple but has the **boundary burst problem**: a client can send max requests just before and just after a window boundary, getting 2x the allowed rate in a short period. **Sliding window** (a weighted count across the current and previous window) eliminates the boundary burst problem at slight complexity cost. For distributed rate limiting (multiple API gateway instances), use Redis with Lua scripts for atomic check-and-increment.

**OAuth 2.0 scopes and token introspection for APIs**: when third-party clients access your API with OAuth tokens, scopes limit what they can do (`read:orders` vs `write:orders` vs `admin`). The token should be validated on every request: for JWTs, verify the signature and claims locally (no network call, but tokens cannot be immediately revoked); for opaque tokens, call the authorization server's `/introspect` endpoint (network call on every request, but immediate revocation). The tradeoff is latency vs revocability - short-lived JWTs (15 min access tokens + refresh) are the pragmatic middle ground. **PKCE** for public clients (mobile, SPA) prevents authorization code interception. **mTLS** (mutual TLS) for service-to-service - both client and server present certificates, providing cryptographic identity without API keys.

**API gateways** (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Apigee, Nginx) are the front door for all API traffic, providing: **authentication/authorization** (validate tokens before requests reach your services), **rate limiting** (centralized, across all services), **routing** (route by path prefix, host, or header to the appropriate backend service), **request/response transformation** (add headers, strip internal headers, transform response format), **observability** (log all requests centrally, emit metrics, inject trace IDs), and **caching** (cache GET responses at the gateway to reduce backend load). The gateway pattern means backends do not implement these concerns individually - they trust that traffic reaching them is already authenticated and rate-limited. The tradeoff: the gateway is a single point of failure (mitigated by clustering and health checks) and a potential performance bottleneck (mitigated by keeping transformations lightweight).

subtopics.title: OpenAPI, API contracts, contract testing, and documentation
body: **OpenAPI (formerly Swagger)** is the industry-standard specification for describing REST APIs. An OpenAPI document (YAML or JSON, typically `openapi.yaml`) describes every endpoint, request/response schema, authentication method, and error code in a machine-readable format. The document is the **source of truth** for the API contract. From one OpenAPI document you can generate: interactive documentation (Swagger UI, Redoc), typed client SDKs (openapi-generator in 50+ languages), server stubs (start with working boilerplate), mock servers (for frontend development before the backend is ready), and validation middleware (reject requests that do not match the spec). The spec itself becomes a test artifact - any deviation between the running server and the spec is a bug.

**Writing good OpenAPI**: use `$ref` to define shared schemas once and reference them (`$ref: '#/components/schemas/User'`) - do not duplicate schemas across endpoints. Every property should have a `description`, an `example`, and format constraints (`format: email`, `minLength: 1`, `maxLength: 255`). Every error response should be typed - not just `500: Server Error` but `500: { schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse' } }`. Define authentication schemes in `securitySchemes` and apply globally with `security: [{ bearerAuth: [] }]` - overriding per-endpoint for public routes. Use `tags` to group endpoints logically in generated documentation. **OpenAPI 3.1** (current) aligns schemas with JSON Schema draft 2020-12, enabling `discriminator` for polymorphic types and `if/then/else` for conditional schemas.

**Contract testing** is the practice of verifying that both the API provider (server) and the API consumer (client) agree on the contract, independently. Unlike integration tests (which test the real system end-to-end), contract tests are fast and don't require both sides to be running. **Pact** (the dominant contract testing tool) works bidirectionally: the consumer writes a test that records their expected interactions (request/response pairs) into a **pact file**; the provider runs the pact file against their implementation to verify they can fulfill every interaction. The pact file is shared via a **Pact Broker**. This catches breaking changes before they reach production - if the provider changes a field name that a consumer depends on, the provider's Pact verification fails in CI.

**API documentation as a product**: documentation is the user interface for developers. Stripe, Twilio, and Plaid set the standard: every endpoint has a description, every parameter is explained with its type, constraints, and what it does, every response schema is shown with real examples, error codes are listed with when they occur and how to handle them, and there are copy-pasteable code samples in multiple languages. **Request/response examples** are more important than schema descriptions - developers read examples first. The **API changelog** (a structured history of changes with dates, migration guides, and deprecation notices) is as important as the reference - it is what developers read when upgrading. Tools: **Redoc** and **Stoplight** for rendering OpenAPI into beautiful docs; **ReadMe** as a managed developer portal; **Mintlify** for MDX-based docs with code samples.

**API design review checklist** (the things you catch in code review that save months of pain later): Is every field name consistent with the rest of the API (camelCase vs snake_case - pick one, never mix)? Are timestamps in ISO 8601 with timezone (`2025-01-15T10:30:00Z`)? Are monetary amounts in the smallest denomination (cents, not dollars - floating point is not money)? Are enum values strings or integers - strings are forward-compatible (adding a new value does not break old clients that ignore unknown values), integers are not. Does every collection endpoint support filtering, sorting, and pagination? Are error responses in a consistent format across all endpoints? Is the API idempotent where it needs to be (POST with Idempotency-Key for payments)? Are there any fields that will be a pain to remove later (avoid exposing internal IDs, database table names, or implementation details)?

resources.label: Stripe API docs - the gold standard for API documentation
Stripe API - reference implementation of great REST design

connections.note: API design is the contract layer over the backend patterns (auth, middleware, validation)
API gateways and rate limiting are system-design components at scale
OWASP API Security Top 10 maps directly to web security attack vectors
GraphQL schema and protobuf IDL are type systems with formal contracts

topics.title: DevOps: containers, CI/CD, and observability
body: DevOps is not a role - it is the collapse of the wall between writing software and running it. The tools are Docker (packaging), Kubernetes (scheduling), CI/CD pipelines (shipping), IaC (Terraform/Helm, reproducible infrastructure), and the three pillars of observability (logs, metrics, traces - knowing what your system is doing in production).
The shift: code that works on your machine is a local script. Code that ships reliably to production at any scale requires all of this.

subtopics.title: Docker: image layers, multi-stage builds, and production image optimization
body: **Docker** packages an application and its entire runtime environment (OS libraries, language runtime, dependencies, config) into a portable **image** that runs identically on any host with a Docker daemon. The key abstraction is the **container**: a process isolated from the host via Linux kernel namespaces (PID, network, mount, UTS, IPC) and cgroups (CPU and memory limits). Containers share the host OS kernel - they are not virtual machines. Startup takes milliseconds (no OS boot), and overhead is near-zero compared to the raw process.

**Image layers** are the efficiency mechanism. A Docker image is a stack of read-only layers, each corresponding to a `Dockerfile` instruction (`RUN`, `COPY`, `ADD`). The filesystem is a union mount (OverlayFS on Linux): upper layers override lower layers, and the combined result is what the container sees. Layers are **content-addressed** (stored by hash) and **cached**: if the instruction and its inputs have not changed since the last build, Docker reuses the cached layer. Build order matters enormously for cache efficiency. Put instructions that change rarely (installing system packages, copying `package.json` and running `npm install`) early; put instructions that change frequently (copying application code) late. If you copy all source code before `npm install`, every code change invalidates the dependency install layer.

**Multi-stage builds** are the idiomatic pattern for keeping production images lean. The problem: a Node.js or Go application needs build tools (TypeScript compiler, go build, gcc) to compile, but the compiled output does not need them at runtime. Without multi-stage: the image contains the compiler, dev dependencies, and source code - bloating the image and expanding the attack surface. With multi-stage: `FROM node:20 AS build` installs all dependencies and compiles; `FROM node:20-alpine AS runtime` copies only the compiled output from the build stage. The runtime image has no source code, no dev tools, no compiler. Production Node.js images commonly go from 800MB (naive) to under 100MB (multi-stage alpine).

**Dockerfile best practices**: use specific version tags, not `latest` (builds must be reproducible - `latest` changes without notice). Use minimal base images (`alpine`, `distroless`, `scratch` for compiled Go binaries). Set `USER` to a non-root user - running containers as root means a container escape compromises the host as root. Combine `RUN` commands with `&&` to avoid creating intermediate layers for temporary state: `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` - the cache cleanup happens in the same layer it was dirtied. Use `.dockerignore` (equivalent to `.gitignore`) to prevent copying `node_modules`, `.git`, `.env`, and build artifacts into the image context.

**Container networking**: containers on the same Docker network can reach each other by container name (Docker's built-in DNS). `docker-compose` creates a private network per compose file automatically. **Volumes** persist data across container restarts: bind mounts (`-v /host/path:/container/path`) mount host directories; named volumes (`-v mydata:/container/path`) are managed by Docker and persist independently. The important production rule: **containers are ephemeral** - do not store application state inside the container filesystem. Databases run in containers should use volumes; if the container is replaced (deployment, crash), data must survive in the volume. Any state inside the container's writable layer is lost when the container exits.

subtopics.title: Kubernetes: pods, deployments, services, and the control plane
body: **Kubernetes (K8s)** is a container orchestrator: it schedules containers across a cluster of machines, maintains desired state (restarts crashed containers, scales up when traffic grows), handles rolling deployments, and provides networking and service discovery. It is the operating system for the datacenter. The mental model: you declare *what* you want (N replicas of this container, route port 80 to it), and Kubernetes continuously works to make reality match that declaration. This is **declarative, reconciliation-loop-based** control - the same pattern as React (desired state → render).

**The control plane** runs on master nodes: `kube-apiserver` (the API gateway for all K8s operations, the single source of truth stored in etcd), `etcd` (distributed key-value store that persists cluster state), `kube-scheduler` (assigns pods to nodes based on resource requests and constraints), and `kube-controller-manager` (runs reconciliation controllers: ReplicationController keeps pod count correct, NodeController marks nodes as unreachable, etc.). Worker nodes run `kubelet` (the agent that receives pod specs and manages containers via the container runtime), `kube-proxy` (maintains iptables/IPVS rules for service networking), and the container runtime (containerd, CRI-O).

**Pods** are the smallest deployable unit: one or more containers that share a network namespace (same IP address, same localhost) and can share volumes. Containers within a pod communicate via localhost; pods communicate via the cluster network. The primary pod pattern: one main container + optional **sidecar** containers for cross-cutting concerns (a logging agent sidecar that ships the main container's logs to Elasticsearch, an Envoy sidecar that handles mTLS in a service mesh). **Deployments** manage ReplicaSets (which manage Pods): a Deployment ensures N replicas are running, handles rolling updates (replace old pods with new ones gradually, rolling back if readiness probes fail), and records rollout history.

**Services** are the stable network endpoint in front of pods. Pods have ephemeral IPs that change on restart; a Service provides a stable ClusterIP (virtual IP) that load-balances across all matching pods (selected by label selector). `ClusterIP` (internal only), `NodePort` (exposes a port on every node), `LoadBalancer` (provisions a cloud load balancer - the standard way to expose a service externally). **Ingress** is the L7 HTTP routing layer: an Ingress controller (nginx-ingress, Traefik, AWS ALB Ingress) watches Ingress resources and configures L7 routing (route `/api` to the API service, `/` to the frontend service, TLS termination at the ingress).

**Resource management**: every container should declare `requests` (the minimum resources the scheduler needs to place it on a node) and `limits` (the maximum it can use). `requests.cpu: 100m, requests.memory: 128Mi` tells the scheduler this container needs 0.1 CPU cores and 128MB RAM. `limits.cpu: 500m, limits.memory: 512Mi` prevents it from consuming more. If a container exceeds its memory limit, it is **OOMKilled** (killed by the kernel). If it exceeds its CPU limit, it is **throttled** (slowed down, not killed). **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** watches metrics (CPU utilization, custom metrics via Prometheus adapter) and adjusts replica count automatically. **Liveness probes** (is the container alive? kill and restart if not) and **readiness probes** (is the container ready to serve traffic? remove from service endpoints if not) are the health check mechanisms.

subtopics.title: CI/CD pipelines: GitHub Actions, deployment strategies, and rollbacks
body: **CI (Continuous Integration)** is the practice of merging code changes frequently, automatically building and testing every merge. **CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment)** extends CI to automatically deploy passing builds to production (deployment) or keep them ready to deploy with one click (delivery). The goal: shrink the feedback loop between 'code written' and 'code running in production' from days/weeks to minutes/hours. Small, frequent deployments are lower risk than large, infrequent ones - a change set of 10 lines is trivially debuggable; a change set of 10,000 lines is a nightmare.

**GitHub Actions** is the dominant CI/CD platform for open-source and many enterprise projects. Workflows are YAML files in `.github/workflows/`. A workflow consists of: **triggers** (`on: push, pull_request, schedule`), **jobs** (parallelizable units that run on a runner), and **steps** (sequential commands within a job). Jobs can share data via **artifacts** (uploaded by one job, downloaded by another) and can depend on each other (`needs: [build, test]`). **Actions** are reusable steps from the marketplace: `actions/checkout`, `actions/setup-node`, `docker/build-push-action`. Secrets are stored in GitHub Secrets and injected as environment variables - never hardcoded in workflow files.

A standard pipeline: (1) **CI**: checkout code → install dependencies → lint → type-check → run unit tests → run integration tests → build Docker image → push to container registry. (2) **CD to staging**: triggered on merge to `main` → deploy to staging environment → run smoke tests → notify team. (3) **CD to production**: triggered manually or on tag → deploy to production with a chosen strategy → monitor error rates → auto-rollback or manual approval. **Branch protection rules** enforce the pipeline: PRs cannot be merged unless CI is green, specific reviewers have approved, and the branch is up to date with main.

**Deployment strategies** control how new versions replace old ones. **Rolling update** (K8s default): replace pods one at a time (or in batches), maintaining availability throughout. The issue: old and new versions run simultaneously during the rollout - the API must be backward-compatible. **Blue-green deployment**: maintain two identical environments (blue = current, green = new). Deploy to green, run tests, then switch the load balancer from blue to green. Rollback is instant (switch back). Cost: double the infrastructure during the transition. **Canary deployment**: route a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version, monitor error rates and latency, gradually increase the percentage. If metrics degrade, route all traffic back to the old version. This is the safest strategy for user-facing changes - real traffic validates the new version before it takes 100%. **Feature flags** decouple deployment from release: code is deployed to all users but the feature is only active for users in the flag's target group - enables canary-style gradual rollout at the application layer without infrastructure complexity.

**Rollback** is as important as deployment. For stateless services: re-deploy the previous Docker image tag - takes the same time as a deployment. Kubernetes `kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app` triggers a rollback to the previous ReplicaSet. For database schema changes: **always make schema changes backward-compatible** before deploying code changes that depend on them. Expand-contract migrations: in phase 1, add the new column (old code ignores it, new code uses it); deploy new code; in phase 2, remove the old column (after confirming old code is gone). Never drop a column in the same migration that adds a replacement - it breaks the running old version. **Automated rollback triggers**: monitor error rate and p99 latency for N minutes post-deployment; if thresholds are exceeded, auto-rollback. This is what Spinnaker, Argo Rollouts, and Flagger implement.

subtopics.title: Infrastructure as Code: Terraform and Helm
body: **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** means defining cloud resources (servers, databases, networking, DNS, IAM policies) as declarative configuration files checked into version control. The benefits: reproducibility (the same config produces the same infrastructure, eliminates 'it worked in staging' drift), auditability (infrastructure changes go through code review and git history), and automation (provisioning a new environment is a command, not a runbook). The alternative - clicking through cloud consoles - produces **snowflake servers**: unique, hand-crafted, impossible to reproduce.

**Terraform** (by HashiCorp) is the dominant multi-cloud IaC tool. HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) describes resources declaratively: `resource aws_instance web { ami = 'ami-xxx', instance_type = 't3.micro' }`. `terraform plan` shows a diff of what will change without applying; `terraform apply` makes it real. **State** is Terraform's bookkeeping of what it has provisioned - it maps your config to real cloud resource IDs. State must be stored remotely (S3 + DynamoDB for state locking, Terraform Cloud) in production - local state means your team cannot collaborate and a laptop loss destroys your state. **Modules** are reusable Terraform units: a `vpc` module, an `eks-cluster` module, a `postgres-rds` module - parameterized with variables and called like functions. The Terraform Registry has community modules for every common AWS/GCP/Azure pattern.

**Terraform pitfalls**: **`terraform destroy`** deletes all resources managed by the state file - add lifecycle `prevent_destroy = true` for production databases and stateful resources. **State drift**: if a resource is modified manually (in the cloud console), Terraform's state no longer matches reality; `terraform refresh` reconciles this. **Import**: existing manually-created resources can be brought under Terraform management with `terraform import resource.name cloud_id` (write the config, then import). **Provider version locking**: always pin provider versions (`required_providers { aws = { version = '~> 5.0' } }`) - a provider version bump can break your plan. Run `terraform validate` and `terraform plan` in CI for every PR that touches infrastructure code.

**Helm** is the package manager for Kubernetes. A **Helm chart** is a directory of Kubernetes manifest templates (YAML files with Go templating `{{ .Values.replicaCount }}`), a `values.yaml` (default values), and a `Chart.yaml` (metadata). Installing a chart (`helm install my-release ./my-chart --values prod-values.yaml`) renders the templates with the merged values and applies the resulting manifests to K8s. Charts parameterize the gap between environments: the same chart, different `values.yaml` files for `dev` (1 replica, small resource limits) vs `prod` (5 replicas, larger limits, PodDisruptionBudgets). **Helm repositories** host versioned charts (Artifact Hub, Harbor). **`helm upgrade --install`** is the idempotent form: install if not present, upgrade if already installed - the safe form for CI/CD pipelines.

**GitOps** is the operational model where the Git repository is the single source of truth for both application code and infrastructure state. Changes to infrastructure are made via PRs; a GitOps operator (**Argo CD**, **Flux**) watches the repository and applies changes to the cluster automatically. The invariant: what is in Git is what is in the cluster. Manual `kubectl apply` is forbidden (it creates drift). Argo CD continuously reconciles the live cluster state against the desired state in Git and flags or auto-corrects divergence. This is the production-grade extension of 'infrastructure as code' to the operational model - the audit log is git history, the change process is PRs, and rollback is `git revert`.

subtopics.title: Observability: logs, metrics, and distributed tracing
body: **Observability** is the ability to understand the internal state of a system from its external outputs. The three pillars are **logs** (time-stamped text records of events), **metrics** (numerical measurements aggregated over time), and **traces** (records of a request's path through distributed services). You need all three: metrics tell you *something is wrong*, logs tell you *what happened*, and traces tell you *where in the call graph*. A system that is only instrumented with logs is not observable at scale - searching gigabytes of text logs for a performance regression is slow and imprecise.

**Structured logging**: logs should be machine-parseable JSON, not prose strings. `{ level: 'error', timestamp: '...', service: 'api', traceId: 'abc123', userId: '42', message: 'payment failed', error: 'card declined' }` is searchable, filterable, and joinable on `traceId`. Log aggregation platforms (Datadog, Elastic/Kibana, Grafana Loki) index structured logs for fast queries. **Log levels** (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL) allow filtering noise. The production logging contract: INFO for normal operations (request received, response sent), WARN for recoverable anomalies (retry succeeded, cache miss rate elevated), ERROR for failures that need attention (external API down, DB query failed), with full context (user, request ID, error detail) on ERROR. DEBUG logs are disabled in production (performance cost) and enabled per-service for troubleshooting.

**Metrics with Prometheus**: Prometheus scrapes `/metrics` endpoints from services at a configurable interval and stores time-series data in its TSDB. Metric types: **Counter** (monotonically increasing, e.g. `http_requests_total`) - useful for rates (`rate(http_requests_total[5m])`), **Gauge** (point-in-time value, e.g. `active_connections`, `memory_usage_bytes`), **Histogram** (distribution of values with configurable buckets, e.g. `request_duration_seconds_bucket` - enables `histogram_quantile(0.99, ...)` for p99 latency). **Grafana** dashboards visualize Prometheus data with PromQL queries. **Alertmanager** routes alerts (when `rate(errors[5m]) > 10` for 5 minutes, page the on-call). The **USE method** (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) for resources and the **RED method** (Rate, Errors, Duration) for services are the standard frameworks for deciding what to instrument.

**Distributed tracing**: in a microservices architecture, a single user request may touch 5-10 services. When something is slow or fails, which service is the culprit? Tracing answers this. Each request is assigned a **trace ID** (propagated via HTTP headers: `traceparent` in W3C Trace Context format). Each service creates a **span** (a unit of work with a start time, end time, and metadata) and attaches it to the trace. The result is a tree of spans showing the entire call graph for one request, with timing for each hop. Implementations: **OpenTelemetry** (the vendor-neutral SDK for generating traces, metrics, and logs), with backends like **Jaeger** (open-source), **Tempo** (Grafana), or **Datadog APM**. Key use cases: finding which downstream call is adding 300ms of latency, identifying which service is throwing errors on 5% of requests, and proving that a database query is the root cause of p99 degradation.

**Alerting on symptoms, not causes**: alert on what users experience (error rate > 1%, p99 latency > 1s), not on internal metrics (CPU > 80%). High CPU is not always bad; slow responses always are. **Error budgets and SLOs**: a Service Level Objective (SLO) is a target for a service's reliability (e.g., 99.9% of requests succeed with p99 < 500ms). An error budget is the allowable failure budget per month (0.1% = ~43 minutes of downtime/month). If the error budget is being consumed faster than expected, freeze risky deployments and focus on reliability. This is the SRE model from Google's SRE book - it turns reliability from a vague goal into a measurable, budgeted constraint that engineering and product teams share.

resources.label: Google SRE book - Monitoring distributed systems

connections.note: Containers are kernel namespaces + cgroups - the virtualization layer you study in OS
K8s autoscaling and rolling deploys are applied system-design patterns
MLOps is DevOps applied to model training and serving pipelines
Container networking (CNI, iptables, IPVS) bottoms out in kernel network stack

topics.title: Distributed systems: theory and foundations
body: Distributed systems are multiple independent processes communicating over a network, cooperating to appear as a single coherent system. The network is the adversary: it drops packets, reorders them, delays them arbitrarily, and partitions nodes from each other. Every distributed systems problem - consistency, consensus, replication, time - is ultimately about reasoning correctly despite this adversary.

The theory here is not academic: Paxos and Raft are what etcd (Kubernetes), CockroachDB, and Consul actually run. Vector clocks are how Dynamo, Riak, and Cassandra track causality. FLP impossibility is why every consensus protocol in existence has a liveness caveat. This material explains *why* the distributed databases and message queues in the system design topic behave the way they do.

subtopics.title: The FLP impossibility and why perfect consensus is impossible
body: **FLP impossibility** (Fischer, Lynch, Paterson, 1985) is one of the most important theoretical results in computer science. It proves that in an **asynchronous** distributed system where **even one process can fail** (by crashing, not returning Byzantine behavior), it is **impossible** to guarantee that a consensus algorithm terminates. More precisely: no deterministic algorithm can simultaneously guarantee **safety** (all processes decide the same value) and **liveness** (all processes eventually decide) in the presence of even one crash failure, in an asynchronous network.

The asynchronous model is the key: in it, there is no upper bound on message delivery time. A message might take 1ms or 10 hours - the protocol cannot distinguish 'message in transit' from 'process has crashed'. This means a coordinator waiting for a quorum cannot know if a slow responder has crashed or is just slow. If it waits forever, it may wait for a crashed process (violating liveness). If it times out, it may incorrectly declare a slow-but-alive process as failed and proceed without it, potentially violating safety if that process decides differently.

The practical implication is not 'consensus is impossible' - it is that **all practical consensus protocols make an additional assumption** to escape FLP. The two escapes: (1) **Synchrony assumptions** (partial synchrony) - assume that message delays are bounded *eventually* (not always, but after some global stabilization time). Paxos and Raft use this: they are safe in all conditions but only live under eventual partial synchrony. (2) **Randomization** - Ben-Or's randomized consensus uses coin flips to probabilistically guarantee liveness; no deterministic adversary can keep the algorithm in a non-terminating state forever, but it may take exponentially many rounds. Real systems use approach (1).

**Safety vs liveness** is the fundamental tension in distributed systems. **Safety** (nothing bad happens): all non-faulty nodes agree on the same value; no two nodes decide differently. **Liveness** (something good eventually happens): all non-faulty nodes eventually decide. FLP says you cannot guarantee both. In practice: **always preserve safety, accept bounded liveness violations**. A distributed database that returns inconsistent reads is broken. A distributed database that temporarily refuses writes (not live) during a network partition is correct. This is why consensus systems like Raft refuse to commit entries without a quorum - they halt (sacrifice liveness) rather than split-brain (sacrifice safety).

The **two generals problem** is a related impossibility: two armies can never be certain their attack is coordinated over an unreliable communication channel, because any acknowledgment of receipt itself requires acknowledgment. It proves that **exact-once delivery is impossible over a lossy channel** - protocols can only achieve at-least-once or at-most-once. This is why every message queue and RPC system has exactly-once as an operational concern (deduplication at the application level, idempotent receivers) rather than a protocol-level guarantee. These impossibility results are the load-bearing walls: every protocol in production is an engineering decision made within the constraints they define.

resources.label: Designing Data-Intensive Applications - Ch. 8: The Trouble with Distributed Systems

subtopics.title: Logical clocks, vector clocks, and causality
body: In a distributed system, there is no global clock. Each process has its own local clock, and these clocks drift apart (NTP only keeps them within ~100ms of each other, with occasional jumps). Wall-clock timestamps are therefore **unreliable for ordering events across processes**: you cannot tell if event A at 10:00:00.100 on server 1 happened before event B at 10:00:00.050 on server 2 if the clocks are not perfectly synchronized. The question 'did A happen before B?' requires a different mechanism.

**Lamport logical clocks** (Leslie Lamport, 1978) assign a logical timestamp to every event in a distributed system, capturing the **happens-before** relation ($\rightarrow$). The rules: (1) each process maintains a counter $C$, incremented before each local event; (2) when a message is sent, it carries the sender's current $C$; (3) when a message is received, the receiver sets $C = \max(C_{local}, C_{message}) + 1$. If event $A \rightarrow B$ (A causally precedes B - A is in B's causal history), then $C(A) < C(B)$. The converse does not hold: $C(A) < C(B)$ does not imply $A \rightarrow B$ - concurrent events can have any clock ordering. Lamport clocks give a **total order consistent with causality** (for tie-breaking), but cannot detect concurrent events.

**Vector clocks** (Fidge/Mattern, 1988) solve this. Instead of a scalar counter, each process $P_i$ maintains a vector $V_i[1..n]$ where $n$ is the number of processes. Rules: (1) $P_i$ increments $V_i[i]$ before each local event; (2) messages carry the sender's full vector; (3) on receiving a message from $P_j$, $P_i$ sets $V_i[k] = \max(V_i[k], V_{message}[k])$ for all $k$, then increments $V_i[i]$. Comparison: $V_A < V_B$ iff $V_A[k] \leq V_B[k]$ for all $k$ and $V_A \neq V_B$ (A causally precedes B). If neither $V_A < V_B$ nor $V_B < V_A$, the events are **concurrent** (neither is in the other's causal history). This is the precise test for concurrency that Lamport clocks cannot make.

Vector clocks are used in **conflict detection** for distributed storage. Amazon Dynamo uses them: when a client writes, the new version gets a vector clock advanced at the coordinating node. If two writes conflict (neither's vector clock dominates the other's), both versions are returned to the client with a flag, and the application must reconcile them (the 'shopping cart merge' example: two concurrent adds to a cart both survive, never losing an item). **Dotted version vectors** are an optimization that avoids false conflicts from sibling values. **Version vectors** (the set of `(node, version)` pairs) is the production implementation - semantically identical to vector clocks but more compact.

**TrueTime** (Google Spanner) takes a hardware approach: GPS receivers and atomic clocks in every datacenter, with an API that returns `[earliest, latest]` time intervals (not a point). Spanner's commit protocol waits out the uncertainty interval (`commit_wait`) before declaring a transaction committed, guaranteeing that the transaction's timestamp is in its past. This achieves **external consistency** (real-time ordering) - the strongest consistency property - with actual wall-clock time, eliminating the need for logical clocks. The cost: 7-14ms of commit latency per transaction (waiting out the clock uncertainty). This is why Spanner is used for global financial systems where external consistency is worth the latency, not for low-latency OLTP.

resources.label: Lamport - Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events (1978)
Designing Data-Intensive Applications - Ch. 8: Ordering Guarantees

subtopics.title: Consensus: Paxos, Raft, and the leader election problem
body: **Consensus** is the problem of getting a group of processes to agree on a single value, even when some processes fail. It is the foundation of distributed coordination: replicated state machines, distributed locks, leader election, and atomic broadcast all reduce to consensus. The value of a consensus algorithm is not just correctness - it is the proof of correctness under an adversarial network model. Ad-hoc distributed protocols that 'seem right' routinely have subtle safety violations discovered under unusual network conditions.

**Paxos** (Lamport, 1989, published 1998) is the algorithm that proved distributed consensus could be done safely. The classic **Single-Decree Paxos** reaches consensus on one value across a set of acceptors in two phases. **Phase 1 (Prepare)**: a proposer chooses a unique proposal number $n$ and sends `Prepare(n)` to a quorum of acceptors. Each acceptor responds with a promise not to accept any proposal with number $< n$, and reports the highest-numbered proposal it has already accepted. **Phase 2 (Accept)**: if the proposer receives promises from a quorum, it sends `Accept(n, v)` where $v$ is the value of the highest-numbered accepted proposal in any response (or its own value if none was reported). An acceptor accepts this unless it has already promised to ignore proposals $< n$. If a quorum accepts, consensus is reached. The safety guarantee: any two quorums overlap in at least one acceptor, so any two agreed values must be the same (both passed through the same overlapping acceptor).

**Paxos's practical problems**: the paper describes single-decree consensus; real systems need **multi-decree Paxos** (agree on a log of commands). The extension, Multi-Paxos, is not fully specified in the original paper, and every implementation makes different choices about leader leases, membership changes, and log compaction. Paxos is notoriously hard to implement correctly - Chubby (Google's distributed lock service) took years to get right, and even then required significant engineering to handle leader changes, disk failures, and membership changes. This is what motivated **Raft**.

**Raft** (Ongaro & Ousterhout, 2014) is designed for understandability: same safety properties as Multi-Paxos, but with explicit decomposition into leader election, log replication, and safety. The key design: **strong leader** - all reads and writes go through a single elected leader, which simplifies reasoning (no concurrent proposals). **Leader election**: time is divided into **terms** (monotonically increasing integers). If a follower receives no heartbeat from the leader within its election timeout (150-300ms randomized to avoid split votes), it increments the term, transitions to candidate, and sends `RequestVote` RPCs. A candidate wins if it receives votes from a majority. The election timeout randomization is the liveness mechanism that escapes FLP: it is not a formal synchrony assumption, but it ensures that with high probability only one candidate wins in each term. **Log replication**: the leader appends client commands to its log and sends `AppendEntries` RPCs to followers. An entry is **committed** once a majority of nodes have written it to their log. Committed entries are never overwritten - the **Log Matching Property** guarantees all logs are identical at any committed prefix.

**Raft in production**: etcd (Kubernetes' state store), CockroachDB, TiKV, Consul, RethinkDB, and InfluxDB all implement Raft. **Leader leases** (the leader refreshes a timed lease with each heartbeat; while the lease holds, the leader can serve reads without a round trip to the cluster) reduce read latency from 2 RTT to near-zero but require bounded clock skew. **Joint consensus** handles membership changes (adding or removing nodes) safely by transitioning through a joint configuration where both old and new majorities are required - preventing split-brain during the transition. **Log compaction** (snapshotting): replaying the full log from the beginning is impractical for long-running systems; followers that lag too far behind receive a snapshot of the state machine at a recent committed index and replay only subsequent entries.

resources.label: Raft - In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (paper)
The Raft Visualization (interactive)
resources.note: see leader election and log replication animate in real time

subtopics.title: Replication: single-leader, multi-leader, and leaderless (Dynamo-style)
body: **Replication** is the practice of keeping copies of the same data on multiple nodes. The goals are availability (if one node fails, others serve the data), latency (serve from a node near the user), and scalability (distribute read load). The fundamental challenge is that writes must be applied to all replicas, and network delays mean replicas are never perfectly in sync - replication lag is always present, and how the system behaves in the presence of lag defines its consistency model.

**Single-leader replication** (also called primary-replica or master-slave): one node is designated the leader and accepts all writes. The leader sends a **replication log** (WAL records in PostgreSQL streaming replication, binlog events in MySQL replication) to followers, which apply them in order. Reads can be served from followers (allowing read scaling) with the tradeoff of reading stale data (replication lag). **Synchronous replication**: the leader waits for at least one follower to acknowledge before acknowledging the write to the client - no data loss if the leader crashes (the synchronous follower can be promoted), but write latency increases and the leader blocks if the synchronous follower is slow. **Semi-synchronous** (one synchronous follower, the rest async) is the production default in PostgreSQL. **Read-your-writes consistency** problem: after a write, reading from a lagging follower may return the old value. Fix: route reads to the leader for data that the current user just modified, or track the user's write timestamp and only read from followers with replication lag below that timestamp.

**Multi-leader replication** allows multiple nodes to accept writes. The benefit: writes can be accepted at any datacenter (lower write latency for geographically distributed applications) and the system remains available if one datacenter is unreachable. The cost: **write conflicts**. If two users concurrently edit the same record through different leaders, those leaders will both accept the write, and the replication of each write to the other leader creates a conflict. Conflict resolution strategies: **Last-Write-Wins (LWW)** by wall clock timestamp (simple, data loss - whichever write had the later timestamp wins regardless of which happened first in real time), **application-level resolution** (CouchDB merges conflicting versions and exposes them to the application), or **CRDT-based merging**. Multi-leader replication is complex and error-prone; the practical use case is limited to offline-capable applications (CouchDB sync between a mobile app and a server) and multi-datacenter active-active deployments where the application explicitly handles conflicts.

**Leaderless replication (Dynamo-style)**: no designated leader; any node can accept writes. Amazon Dynamo (2007) popularized this with its **quorum system**: write to $W$ nodes, read from $R$ nodes, total $N$ nodes. If $W + R > N$, read and write quorums overlap and you can guarantee reading the latest written value. The classic configuration: $N=3, W=2, R=2$. To write: send to all 3 nodes, wait for 2 to confirm. To read: send to all 3 nodes, take the value with the highest version (vector clock). The **sloppy quorum** optimization (used by Dynamo and Cassandra in certain configurations): during a network partition, if the normal quorum nodes are unreachable, temporarily accept writes on different nodes with a **hinted handoff** (the temporary node remembers to forward the write to the correct node when it reconnects). This dramatically improves write availability at the cost of potentially serving stale reads.

**Read repair and anti-entropy**: in a leaderless system, replica divergence accumulates over time (nodes miss writes while they are down, network partitions cause partial propagation). **Read repair**: when a read detects that different replicas returned different versions, the coordinator writes the latest version back to the lagging replicas. This is lazy - only repaired when data is read. **Anti-entropy process**: a background process continuously compares replicas and propagates missing updates. Cassandra uses both: read repair plus a periodic Merkle tree comparison between replicas (**Merkle tree sync**) to detect and reconcile differences without transferring all data. The Merkle tree is exactly the structure from the DSA and web3 topics - a hash tree over the data that allows efficient identification of which subtree differs between two replicas, minimizing the data transferred during reconciliation.

resources.label: Amazon Dynamo paper (DeCandia et al., 2007)
resources.note: the paper that defined modern NoSQL

subtopics.title: CRDTs: conflict-free replicated data types
body: **CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types)** are data structures designed so that concurrent modifications from different replicas always **merge automatically and correctly** - no coordination required, no conflicts to resolve, and the result is always the same regardless of the order in which updates are applied. This is the strongest form of eventual consistency: mathematically guaranteed convergence to a single value, provably, for every possible interleaving of operations. CRDTs are the data structure answer to the distributed systems problem of concurrent writes.

The formal requirement: a CRDT's merge operation must be a **join-semilattice** - a partial order where every two elements have a unique **least upper bound** (join). The join is **commutative** ($A \sqcup B = B \sqcup A$), **associative** ($A \sqcup (B \sqcup C) = (A \sqcup B) \sqcup C$), and **idempotent** ($A \sqcup A = A$). These three properties together mean that merging replicas produces the same result regardless of the order or number of merges. Designing a CRDT means finding the right lattice structure for your data type.

**State-based CRDTs (CvRDTs)**: replicas send their full state to each other; merge is the join of two states. Examples: **G-Counter** (grow-only counter): state is a vector of per-replica counts; increment bumps the local counter; merge takes the pairwise max; value is the sum. **PN-Counter** (increment and decrement): two G-Counters (P for increments, N for decrements); value is sum(P) - sum(N). **G-Set** (grow-only set): state is the set of elements; merge is set union; cannot remove elements. **2P-Set** (two-phase set): two G-Sets (added, removed); an element is in the set if it is in added and not in removed; once removed, cannot be re-added. **OR-Set** (observed-remove set): each add operation attaches a unique tag; remove removes all tags observed at remove time; concurrent adds and removes are resolved in favor of add (the add survives). OR-Set is what most real CRDT set implementations use.

**Operation-based CRDTs (CmRDTs)**: instead of merging state, replicas broadcast operations. Operations must be commutative (order-independent when applied): concurrent increments from two replicas produce the same result regardless of which is applied first. Requires reliable, exactly-once, causal delivery of operations - stronger network guarantees than state-based CRDTs (which only require eventual delivery). **RGA (Replicated Growable Array)** is the CRDT underlying real-time collaborative text editing: each character has a unique ID (timestamp + site ID pair), insertions are positions relative to existing IDs, and concurrent insertions at the same position are resolved by a deterministic tiebreaker on the IDs. This is the structure behind Google Docs, Figma's multiplayer, and Notion's real-time editing - they converge without a central coordinator for the merge operation.

**CRDTs in practice**: **Riak** (distributed database) uses CRDTs as first-class data types (Riak DT) for counters, sets, and maps, guaranteeing convergence without application-level conflict resolution. **Redis CRDT** (RedisGears) extends Redis with CRDT-based data types for multi-master replication. **Automerge** and **Yjs** are CRDT libraries for collaborative document editing in JavaScript, used in CodeMirror, ProseMirror, and many collaborative text editors. **Logoot/LSEQ** and **WOOT** are the earlier text CRDT algorithms that RGA improved on. The operational complexity: CRDTs shift reasoning from 'how do we coordinate?' to 'is this data type's semantics a lattice?' - for some use cases (shopping carts, counters, presence indicators) they are the perfect fit; for others (financial transactions with complex invariants) the lattice structure does not naturally exist and Saga/2PC remains necessary.

resources.label: A comprehensive study of CRDTs (Shapiro et al., 2011)
Automerge - CRDT library for collaborative applications
Yjs - CRDT framework for collaborative editing

subtopics.title: Distributed transactions: 2PC, three-phase commit, and the coordinator problem
body: A **distributed transaction** is a transaction that spans multiple nodes (databases, services) and must be atomic: either all nodes commit or all abort. This is the distributed analogue of ACID's atomicity, and it requires coordination - left to their own devices, independent nodes may reach different decisions (one commits, another aborts) leaving the system in an inconsistent state. The coordination protocols that solve this are collectively the most operationally dangerous algorithms in production databases.

**Two-phase commit (2PC)**: the standard protocol. A **coordinator** orchestrates two phases across **participants** (the nodes that hold the data). **Phase 1 (Prepare/Vote)**: the coordinator sends `Prepare` to all participants. Each participant writes 'I will commit this transaction' to its WAL, acquires locks on the affected rows, and responds `Yes` (ready to commit) or `No` (abort). Once a participant votes `Yes`, it is **committed to commit** - it cannot unilaterally abort, even if the coordinator disappears. **Phase 2 (Commit/Abort)**: if all participants voted `Yes`, the coordinator writes `Commit` to its WAL and sends `Commit` to all participants. If any voted `No`, the coordinator sends `Abort`. Participants release locks and apply (or discard) the transaction.

**The 2PC failure modes**: (1) **Coordinator failure after Phase 1**: participants that voted `Yes` are stuck. They hold locks, they cannot commit (the coordinator has not sent `Commit`), they cannot abort (they promised not to), and they cannot recover until the coordinator comes back. This is the **blocking** nature of 2PC - a coordinator crash can hold the system hostage indefinitely. (2) **Participant failure after voting `Yes`**: the coordinator must wait for the participant to recover, replay the `Commit` from WAL, and acknowledge. (3) **Network partition during Phase 2**: the coordinator commits, but the `Commit` message does not reach some participants - they remain in `prepared` state (in-doubt). The recovery: when they reconnect, they query the coordinator's WAL. The coordinator must be durably storing the commit decision so it can re-send on recovery. PostgreSQL calls these **in-doubt transactions** and they show up in `pg_prepared_xacts`; they must be manually resolved if the coordinator is permanently lost.

**Three-phase commit (3PC)** attempts to make consensus non-blocking by adding a phase: `Prepare -> PreCommit -> Commit`. The PreCommit phase means participants can determine the outcome (commit) without the coordinator, because if they see PreCommit, they know the coordinator intended to commit. 3PC is non-blocking **if there are no network partitions** - it breaks under partition (the two sides of the partition may independently decide to commit or abort, creating a split). In an asynchronous network (where FLP applies), no protocol can be both safe and live under crash failures. 3PC trades the blocking problem for the partition problem, which is why it is not used in practice.

**Saga pattern** is the modern alternative to 2PC for microservices. A saga is a sequence of local transactions: $T_1 \to T_2 \to ... \to T_n$. Each $T_i$ has a **compensating transaction** $C_i$ that semantically undoes it (a compensating transaction for 'charge credit card' is 'refund credit card'). If $T_k$ fails, the saga executes $C_{k-1}, C_{k-2}, ..., C_1$ to roll back the completed steps.
