EfficientNet-B7 matched state-of-the-art accuracy with $8.4\times$ fewer parameters than GPT-2-scale CNNs at the time.

**Vision Transformers (ViT)** (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) replaced the convolutional inductive biases entirely: split the image into $16\times 16$ patches, linearly embed each patch to a token, add positional embeddings, and run a standard transformer encoder. ViT requires very large datasets to match CNNs (the lack of translation equivariance must be learned from data) but scales better with compute. **DeiT** (Touvron et al., 2021) showed that ViT can be trained on ImageNet-1k alone via **knowledge distillation** from a CNN teacher. The behavioural difference: CNNs are texture-biased (shape/texture confound experiments show CNNs use texture features strongly), while ViTs are more shape-biased — ViTs generalise better across distribution shift.

resources.label: ViT paper — An Image is Worth 16×16 Words (2020)

subtopics.title: Object Detection: YOLO, Faster R-CNN, and DETR
body: Object detection simultaneously classifies objects and regresses bounding boxes. The **two-stage paradigm** (R-CNN family): (1) propose candidate regions with a region proposal network (RPN); (2) classify and refine each proposal with a separate head. **Faster R-CNN** (Ren et al., 2015) unified these with a shared CNN backbone: the RPN slides over the feature map, predicting **anchor** offsets and objectness scores. Anchors are pre-defined boxes of multiple scales and aspect ratios. The RPN loss is $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}(p_i, p_i^*) + \lambda p_i^* \mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(t_i, t_i^*)$ where $p_i^*$ is the GT label and $t_i$ are the 4 box parameters. The RoI pooling layer crops and resizes each proposal from the feature map to a fixed size for the classification head.

**YOLO (You Only Look Once)** (Redmon et al., 2016) reframed detection as a **single regression problem** on a grid. The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid; each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes (x, y, w, h, confidence) and $C$ class probabilities. The entire detection is one forward pass — hence 'single-stage'. This gives $45$ FPS at launch (Faster R-CNN: $5$ FPS). YOLO loss: $\sum_{i,j}[\mathbf{1}^{obj}((x_i-\hat{x}_i)^2+(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i}-\sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + ...) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}]$. The $\sqrt{w}$ term weights large box errors less (a 10px error on a 100px box is less important than on a 10px box). YOLOv5-YOLOv9 replaced the full-image grid with **anchor-free** heads and CSP (Cross-Stage Partial) backbones, reaching SOTA on COCO at real-time speeds.

**IoU (Intersection over Union)** is the universal detection metric: $\text{IoU}(A, B) = |A \cap B| / |A \cup B|$. A prediction is a true positive if $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$ (PASCAL VOC) or matched at thresholds 0.5:0.05:0.95 (COCO, the harder protocol). **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** averages the area under the Precision-Recall curve across all classes and thresholds. The AP for one class: sort predictions by confidence, compute precision $P(k)$ and recall $R(k)$ at each rank $k$, then $AP = \sum_k (R(k) - R(k-1)) P(k)$. mAP@0.5:0.95 is the standard COCO benchmark metric — harder than mAP@0.5 because it requires tight localisation.

**DETR (Detection Transformer)** (Carion et al., 2020) eliminated anchors and NMS entirely. A CNN extracts features → flattened to a sequence → transformer encoder processes global context → transformer **decoder** produces $N=100$ output tokens (one per potential object), each attending to the encoder via cross-attention. Each output token predicts a (class, box) pair. Training uses **Hungarian matching**: compute the optimal bipartite assignment between the $N$ predictions and the $M$ ground truth objects (padding GT with 'no object' to $N$), minimising the bipartite matching cost. The Hungarian algorithm runs in $O(N^3)$ on this small problem. DETR was the first end-to-end trainable detector with no hand-designed components, but trained slowly (500 epochs for COCO convergence). **Deformable DETR** added sparse deformable attention (each query attends to only $K$ key points per feature map level) for $10\times$ faster convergence.

**Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** is the post-processing step for anchor-based detectors: given overlapping high-confidence predictions for the same object, keep only the highest-confidence box and suppress all others with $\text{IoU} > \theta_{\text{NMS}}$. Greedy NMS is $O(n^2)$ in the number of predictions. **Soft-NMS** decays the scores of overlapping boxes rather than removing them — better for dense scenes. NMS is the main reason anchor-based detectors are not end-to-end trainable (it is not differentiable). DETR's elimination of NMS is therefore architecturally significant.

resources.label: YOLO — You Only Look Once (Redmon et al., 2016)
DETR — End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers (2020)

subtopics.title: Segmentation: U-Net, Mask R-CNN, and SAM
body: **Semantic segmentation** assigns a class label to every pixel. The naive approach (classify each pixel independently) ignores spatial context. CNNs produce coarse feature maps (downsampled by pooling/striding) — you need to upsample back to full resolution. **Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN)** (Long et al., 2015) replaced FC layers with $1\times 1$ convolutions (output per-pixel predictions) and used **bilinear upsampling** or **transposed convolutions** (learnable upsampling) to recover resolution. The key insight: the same weights that classify an image can localise the class by operating as a dense predictor.

**U-Net** (Ronneberger et al., 2015) introduced the **encoder-decoder with skip connections** that is now ubiquitous. The encoder (contracting path) progressively halves spatial dimensions and doubles channels via max-pool + conv. The decoder (expanding path) upsamples with transposed conv, then concatenates with the corresponding encoder feature map (the skip connection). The skip connection provides **fine-grained spatial detail** from early layers to counteract the spatial information lost by pooling. U-Net was originally designed for biomedical image segmentation (where training data is scarce) — the symmetric encoder-decoder with skip connections enabled training on fewer than 100 images via aggressive augmentation. It has since become the backbone of diffusion models' denoisers.

**Instance segmentation** distinguishes individual object instances (two cats are two separate masks, not just 'cat everywhere'). **Mask R-CNN** (He et al., 2017) extends Faster R-CNN with a third branch: after RoI alignment (bilinear interpolation version of RoI pooling, avoiding quantisation artefacts), a small FCN per-RoI predicts a $28 \times 28$ binary mask for each class. The mask branch is decoupled from the class branch: the classification head determines the class, and the mask of that class is selected — this avoids competition between class prediction and mask quality. **RoI Align** replaces integer grid snapping in RoI Pooling with bilinear interpolation at exact sub-pixel coordinates, improving mask AP by $\sim 3\%$.

**Panoptic segmentation** merges semantic (background classes: sky, grass) and instance (foreground: person, car) segmentation into a single prediction. Every pixel gets a $(\text{class}, \text{instance\_id})$ label; background pixels share instance ID 0. The **Panoptic Quality (PQ)** metric is $\text{PQ} = \text{SQ} \times \text{RQ}$ where Segmentation Quality $\text{SQ} = \frac{\sum_{(p,g)\in TP} \text{IoU}(p,g)}{|TP|}$ and Recognition Quality $\text{RQ} = \frac{|TP|}{|TP| + \frac{1}{2}|FP| + \frac{1}{2}|FN|}$. PQ factorises recognition quality from localisation quality.

**SAM (Segment Anything Model)** (Kirillov et al., 2023) redefined segmentation as a **promptable** task: given a point, box, or text prompt, segment the corresponding object. SAM was trained on 1.1 billion masks from SA-1B, a new dataset built by a model-in-the-loop annotation pipeline. Architecture: a ViT image encoder + prompt encoder + lightweight mask decoder (two transformer layers + upsampling convolutions). SAM generalises zero-shot to novel domains (microscopy, satellite, art) without fine-tuning. The key design: ambiguous prompts produce multiple mask candidates (coarse/medium/fine) with confidence scores, enabling the user to pick. **SAM 2** (2024) extends to video: a memory bank propagates mask tokens across frames, enabling real-time interactive video segmentation.

subtopics.title: RNNs and LSTMs: Sequence Modelling and the Vanishing Gradient Problem
body: A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** processes a sequence $x_1, \ldots, x_T$ by maintaining a hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$ updated at each timestep: $h_t = \sigma(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$ and $\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$. The weight matrices $W_{hh}, W_{xh}, W_{hy}$ are **shared across all timesteps** — the same transformation is applied at each step. Training uses **BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time)**: unroll the RNN computation graph over $T$ timesteps and apply standard backprop. The gradient of the loss w.r.t. an early hidden state $h_k$ involves $\prod_{t=k+1}^T \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \prod_{t=k+1}^T \text{diag}(\sigma'(z_t)) W_{hh}$. If the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is $< 1$, this product vanishes exponentially; if $> 1$, it explodes. RNNs cannot reliably propagate information across $T \gg 20$ timesteps.

**LSTMs (Long Short-Term Memory)** (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) add a separate **cell state** $c_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$ that carries information across timesteps via a **constant error carousel** — an additive update path that avoids the multiplicative gradient decay. Four gates control information flow. Let $\tilde{c}_t = \tanh(W_c [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c)$ be the candidate cell update. **Forget gate** $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$: how much of $c_{t-1}$ to keep. **Input gate** $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$: how much of $\tilde{c}_t$ to write. **Cell update**: $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$. **Output gate** $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ and $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)$.

The LSTM cell state update $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ is **additive**, not multiplicative in $c_{t-1}$. The gradient $\partial c_t / \partial c_{t-1} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ — the forget gate values, which can be near 1 for many timesteps if the network learns to keep $f_t \approx 1$. This allows gradients to flow back through many timesteps without exponential decay as long as the forget gate stays open. A forget gate bias of $+1$ at initialisation (Jozefowicz et al., 2015) is a critical practical trick: it initialises LSTM to 'remember everything', then learning selectively closes the forget gate.

**GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** (Cho et al., 2014) simplifies the LSTM to two gates: **reset gate** $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t])$ and **update gate** $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t])$. Candidate: $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W[r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t])$. Hidden update: $h_t = (1-z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$. The update gate $z_t$ interpolates between the old state and the candidate — when $z_t \approx 0$, the old state is copied exactly, providing the same constant-error-carousel effect as the LSTM cell state. GRUs have $\sim 25\%$ fewer parameters than LSTMs and perform comparably on most tasks.

**The end of RNNs**: RNNs and LSTMs dominated NLP from 2014-2018 (seq2seq translation, language models, NER). Their fatal flaw is **sequential computation** — $h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, so the entire sequence must be processed step-by-step, preventing parallelism during training. For a sequence of length $T$, the LSTM forward pass is $O(T)$ sequential operations regardless of the number of GPU cores available. Transformers (2017) replaced this with $O(T^2/n)$ parallel attention operations (on $n$ cores), making training 10-100x faster. LSTMs remain relevant in inference-constrained settings (state space models, Mamba) where the $O(1)$-memory recurrent inference is preferable to $O(T)$-memory KV cache.

resources.label: Hochreiter & Schmidhuber — Long Short-Term Memory (1997, foundational paper)
Colah — Understanding LSTMs (the definitive visual explanation)
Karpathy — The Unreasonable Effectiveness of RNNs

subtopics.title: Attention Mechanisms: From Bahdanau to Scaled Dot-Product
body: **Bahdanau attention** (Bahdanau et al., 2015) was introduced for neural machine translation to fix the encoder bottleneck: in seq2seq, the encoder compresses the entire source sentence into a single fixed vector, losing information for long sequences. Bahdanau attention lets the decoder **attend to all encoder hidden states** at each decoding step. The context vector for step $t$ is: $c_t = \sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{tj} h_j$ where $\alpha_{tj} = \text{softmax}(e_{tj})$ and $e_{tj} = v^T \tanh(W_a h_{t-1}^{\text{dec}} + U_a h_j^{\text{enc}})$. Here $e_{tj}$ is an **alignment score** measuring how well input position $j$ matches the current decoder state — a small MLP evaluates compatibility. This let NMT models handle sentences of 30+ words, previously intractable.

**Scaled dot-product attention** (Vaswani et al., 2017) simplifies the alignment function to a dot product: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ where $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ (queries), $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$ (keys), $V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_v}$ (values). Each query $q_i$ produces a context vector by attending over all key-value pairs: it computes the dot-product similarity $q_i^T k_j / \sqrt{d_k}$ with every key, softmax-normalises to weights $\alpha_{ij}$, and takes the weighted sum of values $\sum_j \alpha_{ij} v_j$. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents the softmax from saturating into one-hot distributions for large $d_k$ (where dot products grow with dimensionality): for $d$-dimensional random unit vectors, $q^T k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, d)$, so variance grows with $d$ without scaling.

**Multi-head attention** runs $h$ independent attention heads in parallel: $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^O$ where $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$ and $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$. Each head projects into a different subspace, allowing attention to simultaneously capture different types of relationships (syntactic dependencies, coreference, semantic similarity). Total parameters: $4 d_{\text{model}}^2$ ($W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O$ — all square matrices of size $d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}$). Compute per head: $O(n^2 d_k)$ for the $QK^T$ matmul; total $O(n^2 d_{\text{model}})$ — quadratic in sequence length.

**Self-attention** is attention where queries, keys, and values all come from the same sequence: $\text{Self-Attn}(X) = \text{Attention}(XW^Q, XW^K, XW^V)$. Every token can attend to every other token, making attention a **global operation** — unlike convolution (local) and recurrence (sequential). The attention matrix $A = \text{softmax}(QK^T/\sqrt{d_k}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ encodes which positions attend to which. **Causal (masked) self-attention** sets $A_{ij} = -\infty$ for $j > i$ before softmax, ensuring autoregressive generation: position $i$ cannot attend to future positions. This is implemented by adding a lower-triangular mask to the pre-softmax logits.

**Efficient attention variants** address the $O(n^2)$ cost. **Sparse attention** (Longformer, BigBird): each token attends to a fixed local window + global tokens — $O(nw)$ cost for window $w$. **Linear attention** (Katharopoulos et al., 2020): replace softmax with a feature map $\phi$ and rewrite attention as $(\phi(Q)(\phi(K)^T V))$ using the associativity of matrix multiplication — computes in $O(nd^2)$ instead of $O(n^2 d)$. **FlashAttention** (Dao et al., 2022): does not reduce FLOPs but reorganises the memory access pattern using tiling to avoid materialising the $n \times n$ attention matrix in HBM — critical for $n > 10^4$ where the matrix does not fit in SRAM.

resources.label: Bahdanau et al. — Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align (2015)
Attention Is All You Need — Vaswani et al. (2017)
The Annotated Transformer — Harvard NLP (full PyTorch implementation)

subtopics.title: The Transformer Architecture: Positional Encoding, FFN, and Layer Structure
body: The **Transformer** (Vaswani et al., 2017) processes a sequence entirely in parallel via self-attention — no recurrence. Each **transformer block** consists of two sublayers: multi-head self-attention and a position-wise **feed-forward network (FFN)**, each wrapped with a residual connection and layer normalisation. The FFN applies the same two-layer MLP independently to each token: $\text{FFN}(x) = W_2 \max(0, W_1 x + b_1) + b_2$ with inner dimension $d_{\text{ff}} = 4 d_{\text{model}}$ (the 4x expansion is a convention from the original paper, since retained). In modern LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral), the FFN is replaced by **SwiGLU**: $\text{FFN}(x) = (W_1 x \odot \sigma(W_3 x)) W_2$ — the gating mechanism improves performance. The FFN is where most factual knowledge is stored, per mechanistic interpretability experiments (the attention sublayer handles routing/position; the FFN handles feature lookup).

**Positional encodings** are needed because self-attention is **permutation invariant** — the attention result for a token is the same regardless of its position in the sequence. The original transformer used **sinusoidal encodings**: $PE(\text{pos}, 2i) = \sin(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d})$ and $PE(\text{pos}, 2i+1) = \cos(\text{pos}/10000^{2i/d})$. These are added to the token embeddings at the input. The sinusoidal form has a useful property: $PE(\text{pos}+k)$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE(\text{pos})$ for any $k$ — the model can learn to attend by relative offset. However, sinusoidal PEs do not generalise well to sequences longer than training length.

**RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)** (Su et al., 2021) encodes position by rotating the query and key vectors in 2D planes: for each dimension pair $(q_{2i}, q_{2i+1})$, rotate by angle $\theta_i \cdot \text{pos}$ where $\theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d}$. The dot product $q^T k$ then depends on the **relative position** $\text{pos}_q - \text{pos}_k$ only (the absolute positions cancel in the dot product). RoPE enables length extrapolation via **YaRN** or **RoPE scaling**: multiply $\theta_i$ by $\lambda > 1$ to extend the context window without fine-tuning. RoPE is now universal in modern LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen).

**ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)** (Press et al., 2022): instead of positional encodings, add a negative linear bias to attention logits proportional to the key-query distance: $\text{Attn}(i,j) = q_i^T k_j / \sqrt{d_k} - m_h |i-j|$ where $m_h$ is a head-specific slope (geometric sequence across heads). The bias penalises attending to distant tokens proportionally — distant keys get exponentially lower attention weights after softmax. ALiBi extrapolates to much longer sequences than training without any positional embedding, and each head has a different recency bias, enabling multi-range information integration.

**Pre-norm vs post-norm**: original Transformer applied LayerNorm **after** the residual addition (post-norm): $x \leftarrow \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$. Modern LLMs use **pre-norm**: $x \leftarrow x + \text{Sublayer}(\text{LayerNorm}(x))$. Pre-norm preserves the residual connection in its pure form, keeping the gradient magnitude stable at initialisation regardless of depth — enabling training very deep transformers without warmup. **RMSNorm** (Zhang & Sennrich, 2019) replaces LayerNorm's mean subtraction with only root-mean-square normalisation: $\text{RMSNorm}(x) = x / \sqrt{\frac{1}{d}\sum_i x_i^2} \cdot \gamma$. It is slightly faster (no mean computation) and empirically matches LayerNorm — used in LLaMA, Gemma.

subtopics.title: BERT vs GPT: Encoder, Decoder, and the Pre-training Paradigm
body: **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** (Devlin et al., 2019) introduced the **masked language modelling (MLM)** pre-training objective: randomly mask 15% of tokens and train the model to predict them, using context from both directions (past and future). This bidirectional context makes BERT excellent for understanding tasks (classification, NER, QA) but prohibits autoregressive generation (you cannot mask and predict future tokens during generation). BERT also uses **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** (predicts if two sentences are consecutive in the corpus) — later shown by RoBERTa to be harmful and removed. BERT's architecture is a standard transformer encoder (full attention, no causal mask) with $L=12$ layers, $H=768$ hidden dim, $A=12$ attention heads (BERT-Base: 110M params).

**GPT (Generative Pre-training Transformer)** (Radford et al., 2018) uses a **causal language modelling** objective: predict the next token given all previous tokens. The causal mask ensures each position only attends to past positions. This makes GPT a natural generative model: sample from $P(x_t | x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1})$ autoregressively. GPT's architecture is a standard transformer decoder (causal self-attention only, no cross-attention). GPT-2 (2019) scaled to 1.5B parameters and demonstrated emergent capabilities at generation quality; GPT-3 (2020, 175B) introduced **few-shot in-context learning** — capabilities emerging without any fine-tuning.

**Fine-tuning paradigm**: BERT introduced the modern NLP workflow: pre-train on massive unlabelled text, then fine-tune on a small labelled dataset for the specific task. For classification: add a linear head on top of the [CLS] token embedding and fine-tune all parameters. For QA (SQuAD): add heads predicting start/end positions for the answer span. For GPT-style models: the same autoregressive objective works directly (add the answer after the question in-context). The key insight: **pre-training on language modelling forces the model to develop general linguistic knowledge** (syntax, semantics, world facts) that transfers to many downstream tasks. Fine-tuning adapts this general knowledge to the specific task with only thousands of labelled examples.

**Encoder-decoder (T5, BART)**: for tasks that naturally have a sequence-to-sequence structure (translation, summarisation, question generation), encoder-decoder models combine both. T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) reformulates all NLP tasks as text-to-text: input is a string like 'summarise: [article]', output is the summary. This unification allows a single model to be fine-tuned on any NLP benchmark by formatting training data as text pairs. BART (Lewis et al., 2020) pre-trains a denoising encoder-decoder: corrupt the input (masking, deletion, sentence permutation) and train the decoder to reconstruct the original. BART excels at generation tasks (summarisation, dialogue) where the bidirectional encoder context is valuable.

**The scaling era**: from GPT-3 (175B, 2020) to GPT-4, PaLM, LLaMA, Gemini — the dominant architectural change has been scale, not design innovation. Key differences: (1) decoder-only became the universal choice for general models (no cross-attention complexity, simpler inference); (2) RoPE replaced sinusoidal PE; (3) SwiGLU replaced ReLU FFN; (4) RMSNorm replaced LayerNorm; (5) grouped query attention (GQA) reduced KV cache memory by sharing keys/values across query heads — instead of one K/V head per Q head, use $G$ groups with $h/G$ Q heads sharing one K/V head. GQA saves $(1 - 1/G) \times 2 d_{\text{model}} T$ bytes of KV cache (halving with $G=2$, 8x with $G=8$) at trivial accuracy cost.

resources.label: GPT-2 — Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners (OpenAI, 2019)
Lena Voita — NLP Course (interactive, embeddings to transformers)

subtopics.title: Scaling Laws: Chinchilla, Compute-Optimal Training, and Emergent Capabilities
body: **Neural scaling laws** (Kaplan et al., 2020) established empirical power-law relationships between model performance and three quantities: number of parameters $N$, dataset size $D$ (tokens), and compute budget $C \approx 6ND$ (FLOPs for one training pass on $D$ tokens with a model of $N$ parameters). On a compute budget $C$, the optimal model satisfies $N^* \propto C^{0.73}$ and $D^* \propto C^{0.27}$ — meaning more of the compute budget should go to larger models than more data. The loss decreases as a power law: $L(N,D) = \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha_N} + \left(\frac{D_c}{D}\right)^{\alpha_D} + L_\infty$ where $\alpha_N \approx 0.076$ and $\alpha_D \approx 0.095$. These exponents imply diminishing returns — doubling parameters improves loss by $2^{0.076} \approx 5\%$.

**Chinchilla scaling laws** (Hoffmann et al., 2022) reran the experiment with more careful methodology and found the Kaplan et al. exponents were wrong. Chinchilla's key finding: $N^*$ and $D^*$ should scale **equally** — $N^* \propto C^{0.5}$ and $D^* \propto C^{0.5}$. The practical implication: given compute $C$, the optimal model has $N^* \approx 20D^*$ parameters and is trained on $D^* \approx N^*/20 \times 20 = D^*$ tokens. For a 70B parameter model, optimal training requires $\sim 1.4T$ tokens. By contrast, GPT-3 (175B params, 300B tokens) was massively under-trained — it had a model far larger than optimal for its data budget. This result shifted the field toward training smaller, better-trained models (LLaMA-7B trained on 1T tokens vs GPT-3).

**Compute-optimal training in practice**: the 'Chinchilla-optimal' frontier is about minimising loss at a fixed compute budget. For **inference** (serving millions of requests), smaller well-trained models dominate: a 7B model trained on 2T tokens can outperform a 70B model trained on 300B tokens on many benchmarks, and at $10\times$ lower inference cost per token. The **inference-compute** tradeoff motivates training well beyond Chinchilla-optimal for deployed models. **LLaMA** (Touvron et al., 2023) explicitly adopted this strategy: train 7B/13B/65B models for 1-1.4T tokens (4-5x Chinchilla-optimal) to produce the best inference-efficient models.

**Emergent capabilities** (Wei et al., 2022) are abilities that appear suddenly at scale thresholds and are absent at smaller scales. Examples: few-shot arithmetic (GPT-3 at 175B), chain-of-thought reasoning (PaLM at 540B), few-shot multi-step reasoning. The 'emergence' appears sharp because the metric used (e.g., exact match on a benchmark) is non-linear in model quality — smooth improvement in next-token prediction corresponds to a phase transition in task performance. **BIG-Bench** catalogued over 200 tasks with emergent transitions. A critical counterargument (Schaeffer et al., 2023): using smooth metrics (rather than exact match) makes emergence disappear — it is an artefact of metric choice, not a phase transition in underlying capabilities.

**Tokenisation**: all LLMs operate on **tokens** (subword units), not characters or words. **BPE (Byte Pair Encoding)** starts with individual bytes, then iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair, building a vocabulary of up to $V$ tokens. The merge table defines the tokeniser. For code, BPE naturally splits identifiers while keeping keywords whole. English text typically tokenises to ~1 token per 0.75 words; Chinese/Japanese can be 1 token per character. **SentencePiece** (Kudo & Richardson, 2018) implements both BPE and unigram LM tokenisation and handles Unicode properly (important for multilingual models). **Tiktoken** (OpenAI) and **HuggingFace tokenizers** are the dominant implementations. Tokenisation affects model capabilities: arithmetic on long numbers is hard because each digit may be a separate token or merged with its neighbour unpredictably.

resources.label: Chinchilla Scaling Laws — Hoffmann et al. (2022)
Kaplan et al. — Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models (2020)

subtopics.title: The KV Cache: Memory Math and Inference Optimisation
body: Autoregressive LLM inference generates one token at a time: to generate token $t$, the model needs attention over all previous $t-1$ tokens. Naively, this requires recomputing keys and values for all past tokens at each step — $O(t^2)$ total work for $t$ tokens. The **KV cache** stores the key and value tensors for all past tokens in memory and reuses them: at step $t$, only compute keys/values for the new token, append to the cache, and attend over the full cache. Inference is now $O(t)$ per step (one new row in the attention matrix) — dramatically cheaper, but requires $O(t)$ memory that grows with sequence length.

**KV cache memory formula**: for a model with $L$ layers, $h$ attention heads of dimension $d_k$, precision $p$ bytes per element, and sequence length $T$: KV cache size $= 2 \cdot L \cdot T \cdot h \cdot d_k \cdot p = 2 \cdot L \cdot T \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot p$. For LLaMA-70B ($L=80, d_{\text{model}}=8192, p=2$ bytes for FP16): $2 \times 80 \times T \times 8192 \times 2 = 2.6$ MB per token. At $T=8192$ tokens: $\sim 20$ GB — more than the model's weights in many efficient configurations. This is why **long-context inference** is expensive: the KV cache, not the model weights, is often the memory bottleneck.

**Grouped Query Attention (GQA)** and **Multi-Query Attention (MQA)** reduce KV cache size by sharing keys/values across heads. In MQA (Shazeer, 2019): all $h$ query heads share a single key head and a single value head — KV cache shrinks by $h\times$. In GQA (Ainslie et al., 2023): $h$ query heads are divided into $G$ groups, each group sharing one K/V head — KV cache shrinks by $G\times$. LLaMA-2-70B uses $G=8$ (GQA), reducing KV cache from $\sim 20$ GB to $\sim 2.5$ GB at the same sequence length, with $< 1\%$ perplexity degradation. The accuracy-memory tradeoff in GQA is favourable because keys/values carry less information than queries — queries need to be diverse to capture different aspects of the same context.

**Continuous batching (iteration-level scheduling)** is the key throughput optimisation in production serving. Traditional static batching: batch all sequences together, pad to max length, process the full batch. Wasteful: short sequences sit idle while long ones generate. Continuous batching (Orca, Yu et al., 2022): at every decode step, check which sequences have finished, remove them from the batch, and insert new waiting sequences. This keeps GPU utilisation at nearly 100% and dramatically increases throughput. **vLLM's PagedAttention** extends this: manage the KV cache like an OS page table — allocate cache pages non-contiguously as they are needed, using block-granularity rather than sequence-granularity allocation. This eliminates memory fragmentation and enables batch sizes $10-20\times$ larger than systems with pre-allocated contiguous KV buffers.

**Speculative decoding** (Leviathan et al., 2023) speeds inference using a small **draft model** $M_q$ (fast, e.g., 7B) and the large target model $M_p$ (accurate, e.g., 70B). The draft model generates $K$ tokens speculatively; the target model verifies them **in parallel** (one forward pass for all $K$ tokens). Accepted tokens are free; rejected tokens require the target model to sample a correction. Expected speedup: if the draft model produces tokens that the target would accept with probability $\alpha$, the mean number of accepted tokens per target model call is $\frac{1 - \alpha^{K+1}}{1-\alpha}$. For $\alpha = 0.8, K=4$: expected $\sim 3.4$ tokens per target model call vs 1 without speculation — $3.4\times$ throughput with identical output distribution (not approximation). The EAGLE series improves the draft model quality by conditioning on the target's hidden states.

subtopics.title: RLHF, Reward Models, and DPO
body: **RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)** (Christiano et al., 2017; Stiennon et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2022) aligns LLMs to human preferences. The pipeline: (1) **SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning)**: fine-tune the pre-trained LLM on high-quality demonstration data for instructions, coding, reasoning. (2) **Reward model training**: collect human preference data — pairs of model outputs $(y_1, y_2)$ for the same prompt $x$, with a human label indicating which is preferred. Train a reward model $r_\phi(x, y)$ to predict the Bradley-Terry preference probability: $P(y_1 \succ y_2 | x) = \sigma(r_\phi(x, y_1) - r_\phi(x, y_2))$. Maximise $\mathbb{E}[\log \sigma(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l))]$ where $y_w$ is the preferred ('winner') and $y_l$ the rejected ('loser') response. (3) **RL optimisation**: optimise the SFT policy $\pi_\theta$ to maximise the reward model's score, subject to a KL penalty from the SFT reference policy.

The **RL objective** in RLHF is: $\max_{\pi_\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot|x)} [r_\phi(x,y)] - \beta \mathbb{KL}[\pi_\theta(\cdot|x) \| \pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot|x)]$ where $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ is the SFT policy and $\beta$ controls the KL penalty (typically $0.01$-$0.1$). The KL penalty prevents **reward hacking**: without it, the policy would exploit weaknesses of the reward model (e.g., generating infinitely long outputs that score high but are useless). Optimised with **PPO**: collect rollouts from $\pi_\theta$, compute advantages using a value function $V_\psi$, update $\theta$ and $\psi$ jointly. PPO's clipped surrogate objective $\min(r_t A_t, \text{clip}(r_t, 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) A_t)$ where $r_t = \pi_\theta(a_t|s_t)/\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t|s_t)$ limits policy updates per step.

**DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation)** (Rafailov et al., 2023) eliminates the separate reward model and RL loop entirely by solving for the optimal policy analytically. From the RL objective, the optimal policy satisfies: $\pi^*(y|x) \propto \pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x) \exp(r^*(x,y)/\beta)$. Rearranging: $r^*(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)} + \beta \log Z(x)$. Substituting into the Bradley-Terry model and cancelling $Z(x)$ (the partition function, which cancels in pairwise comparison): $P(y_w \succ y_l | x) = \sigma\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w|x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l|x)}\right)$. The DPO loss is simply negative log-likelihood of this: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\mathbb{E}[\log \sigma(\beta \log r_\theta(y_w, x) - \beta \log r_\theta(y_l, x))]$$ where $r_\theta(y,x) = \pi_\theta(y|x)/\pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)$. DPO trains the policy directly on preference pairs — no reward model, no RL rollouts, just supervised fine-tuning on a modified loss.

**GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimisation)** (DeepSeek-R1, 2025): eliminates the value function (critic) from PPO by using group-level advantage normalisation. For a batch of $G$ outputs per prompt, advantage is normalised within the group: $A_i = (r_i - \text{mean}(r)) / \text{std}(r)$. This removes the need to train a separate critic network (saving memory and complexity) while still providing relative reward signal. GRPO is the backbone of DeepSeek-R1's reasoning capabilities and is increasingly used for training reasoning models.

**Constitutional AI (CAI)** (Bai et al., 2022) from Anthropic: instead of human preference labels, use **AI feedback** generated by a model following a 'constitution' (a list of principles). Red-team the model to produce harmful outputs, then critique and revise using the constitution principles, and fine-tune on the self-revised data. A reward model trained on AI-generated preference data (RLAIF) replaces human labelling at scale. CAI produced Claude's early harmless behaviour while maintaining helpfulness — the constitution encodes the tension between these as explicit principles rather than hoping human raters capture it implicitly.

resources.label: InstructGPT / RLHF paper — Ouyang et al. (2022)

subtopics.title: LoRA, QLoRA, and Efficient Fine-Tuning
body: **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)** (Hu et al., 2021) fine-tunes LLMs with a fraction of the parameters by injecting trainable low-rank matrices alongside frozen pre-trained weights. For a weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, the adapted weight is $W_0 + \Delta W = W_0 + BA$ where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ with rank $r \ll \min(d,k)$. Only $B$ and $A$ are trained; $W_0$ is frozen. Parameters: $r(d + k)$ vs $dk$ for full fine-tuning — for $d=4096, k=4096, r=16$: $131,072$ vs $16,777,216$ — a $128\times$ reduction. At inference, $W_0 + BA$ is merged into a single matrix, adding **zero inference overhead**.

**The rank-$r$ intuition**: empirically, the update $\Delta W$ during fine-tuning has **intrinsic low rank** — its singular values decay quickly, meaning a low-rank approximation captures most of the update. Hu et al. verified this by comparing LoRA to full fine-tuning on GPT-3 and measuring the rank of $\Delta W$ — the updates are almost always rank $\leq 4$ even when full-rank updates are allowed. The $\alpha/r$ scaling factor (LoRA is initialised as $B=0, A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ and the update is $(\alpha/r) BA$) decouples the learning rate from the rank choice: setting $\alpha = 2r$ keeps the effective LR roughly rank-independent.

LoRA targets which weight matrices? Typically the attention projections ($W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O$), and sometimes the FFN projections. Hu et al. found adapting all four attention matrices at low rank outperforms adapting only one at high rank, suggesting the four matrices jointly capture the fine-tuning signal. **LoRA+** (Hayou et al., 2024) uses different learning rates for $A$ and $B$ (empirically $\eta_B = 16 \eta_A$ works well), improving stability and convergence speed. **DoRA (Weight-Decomposed LoRA)** decomposes weight into magnitude and direction: $W = m \cdot V/\|V\|$ and adapts direction via LoRA and magnitude freely.

**QLoRA (Quantised LoRA)** (Dettmers et al., 2023): combine 4-bit quantisation of the base model with LoRA adapters in full precision. The base model is loaded in **NF4 (NormalFloat4)** — a quantisation format derived from the quantiles of a unit normal distribution, optimal for normally-distributed weights. Quantisation error is absorbed by the LoRA adapters. A **double quantisation** step quantises the quantisation constants themselves, saving $0.37$ bits per parameter on average. A **paged optimiser** uses CPU memory as overflow for GPU optimiser states, enabling training 65B-parameter models on a single 48GB GPU — previously requiring $>8 \times$ A100s. QLoRA democratised LLM fine-tuning, enabling researchers to fine-tune state-of-the-art models on consumer hardware.

**Quantisation methods**: **GPTQ** (Frantar et al., 2022) is a post-training quantisation method based on the OBQ (Optimal Brain Quantisation) framework — a second-order weight quantisation minimising layer-wise reconstruction error $\|W \cdot X - W_q \cdot X\|^2_F$ where $X$ is calibration activations, solved column-by-column using the inverse Hessian. **AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantisation)** (Lin et al., 2023) identifies salient weights by their activation magnitudes and keeps them at higher precision, while scaling the rest — no second-order computation needed, faster and comparable accuracy. **GGUF (llama.cpp)** format enables CPU inference with 4-bit weights: the entire 7B model fits in 4GB RAM, enabling local inference on laptops. The quantisation format encodes both weights and the metadata (zero points, scales) needed for dequantisation during inference.

subtopics.title: DDPM: Forward Process, Reverse Process, and the Variational Lower Bound
body: **DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models)** (Ho et al., 2020) defines a **forward process** that gradually adds Gaussian noise to data over $T$ steps, and a **reverse process** that learns to denoise. Forward process: $q(x_t | x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t} x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ where $\beta_t \in (0,1)$ is a noise schedule increasing from $\beta_1 \approx 10^{-4}$ to $\beta_T \approx 0.02$. Define $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t \alpha_s$. The crucial closed form: $q(x_t | x_0) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0, (1-\bar{\alpha}_t) I)$ — we can directly sample $x_t$ from $x_0$ in one step: $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$. As $T \to \infty$, $\bar{\alpha}_T \to 0$ and $x_T \approx \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ — pure noise.

The **reverse process** is the generative model: starting from $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, iteratively denoise. $p_\theta(x_{t-1} | x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(x_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t))$. The posterior of the forward process is tractable when conditioned on $x_0$: $q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \tilde{\mu}_t(x_t, x_0), \tilde{\beta}_t I)$ where $\tilde{\mu}_t = \frac{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0 + \frac{\sqrt{\alpha_t}(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} x_t$ and $\tilde{\beta}_t = \frac{(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1})\beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$. The reverse process should match this posterior at each step.

**Training objective**: maximise the evidence lower bound (ELBO) $\mathbb{E}[\log p_\theta(x_0)] \geq \mathbb{E}_q[\log p_\theta(x_0|x_1) - \sum_t \text{KL}(q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0) \| p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t))]$. Ho et al. showed that training a neural network to **predict the noise** $\epsilon$ added at step $t$ gives the best results in practice: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}[\|\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \epsilon, t)\|^2]$. This is simpler than predicting $\mu_\theta$ and gives better sample quality. The network $\epsilon_\theta$ is a **U-Net** with time embedding (sinusoidal, same as transformer positional encoding) — the architecture is inherited from image segmentation.

**Noise schedules** matter. Linear schedule ($\beta_t$ increases linearly from $\beta_1$ to $\beta_T$) works for $64\times 64$ images but too-aggressively destroys information at high resolution. **Cosine schedule** (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021): $\bar{\alpha}_t = \cos^2(\frac{t/T + s}{1+s} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2})$ where $s=0.008$ — this keeps $\bar{\alpha}_t$ near 1 for many steps (slow initial noise) then drops rapidly near $t=T$. The cosine schedule improves FID significantly for $256\times 256+$ images. **Learned schedules** (Kingma et al.) parameterise the schedule as a neural network, further improving performance.

**Sampling is slow**: generating one image requires $T=1000$ sequential denoising steps — each step is a full forward pass of the U-Net. At 50ms per step, 1000 steps = 50 seconds per image. All subsequent work on diffusion models is largely about making sampling faster without sacrificing quality. DDIM (10-50 steps), consistency models (1-4 steps), distillation methods — these are the key techniques.

resources.label: DDPM — Ho et al. (2020, original paper)
Improved DDPM — Nichol & Dhariwal (2021, cosine schedule)
Diffusion models visual intro — step by step blog

subtopics.title: Score Matching, SDEs, and the Connection to Energy-Based Models
body: **Score-based generative models** (Song & Ermon, 2019) approach diffusion from a different mathematical angle: instead of the denoising framework, they model the **score function** $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ — the gradient of the log data density. Given the score, one can sample from $p(x)$ using **Langevin dynamics**: $x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{\eta}{2} \nabla_x \log p(x_k) + \sqrt{\eta} z_k$ where $z_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ and $\eta$ is step size. This converges to samples from $p(x)$ as $\eta \to 0$ and $k \to \infty$. The generative model needs only the score, not the full density.

**Denoising score matching** (Vincent, 2011) avoids the intractable $\nabla_x \log p(x)$ by instead matching the score of **noised** distributions $q_\sigma(\tilde{x}|x) = \mathcal{N}(\tilde{x}; x, \sigma^2 I)$. The score of the noised distribution $\nabla_{\tilde{x}} \log q_\sigma(\tilde{x}) = -(\tilde{x} - x)/\sigma^2$ can be computed from data pairs $(x, \tilde{x})$. Training the score network $s_\theta(\tilde{x}, \sigma)$ to match these directions is equivalent to training a denoiser: $s_\theta(\tilde{x}, \sigma) \approx (x - \tilde{x})/\sigma^2$. SMLD (Score Matching with Langevin Dynamics) uses a sequence of noise levels $\sigma_1 < \ldots < \sigma_L$ — this is the score-based analogue of the DDPM forward process.

**Song et al. (2021) unified DDPMs and score-based models** via **stochastic differential equations (SDEs)**. The forward process is a continuous-time SDE: $dx = f(x,t)dt + g(t)dw$ where $f$ is the drift, $g$ is the diffusion coefficient, and $w$ is a Wiener process. The reverse SDE has the same structure: $dx = [f(x,t) - g(t)^2 
abla_x \log p_t(x)]dt + g(t)d ar{w}$ where $
abla_x \log p_t(x)$ is the score of the marginal distribution at time $t$. Train a neural network $s_	heta(x,t) \approx 
abla_x \log p_t(x)$ across all noise levels simultaneously. DDPM corresponds to a specific SDE (**VP-SDE**: $f = - rac{1}{2} eta(t)x$, $g = \sqrt{ eta(t)}$); the score-based framework gives a continuous-time view with greater flexibility.

**Probability flow ODE**: every SDE has a corresponding deterministic ODE with the same marginal distributions: $\frac{dx}{dt} = f(x,t) - \frac{1}{2}g(t)^2 \nabla_x \log p_t(x)$. Sampling using the ODE (via black-box ODE solvers) is deterministic and reversible — the same encoder maps a data point to its latent; the decoder maps back. This is the foundation of **DDIM** (which implicitly uses a specific ODE integration scheme) and **latent diffusion** (where the ODE defines a bijective mapping between data and latent space). The ODE enables **latent interpolation**: encode two images to latents $z_0, z_1$ and decode along the straight line $\lambda z_0 + (1-\lambda)z_1$ — producing smooth semantic interpolations impossible in pixel space.

**Connections to energy-based models (EBMs)**: an EBM defines $p_\theta(x) = \frac{\exp(-E_\theta(x))}{Z_\theta}$ where $E_\theta$ is a learned energy function and $Z_\theta$ is the partition function. The score $\nabla_x \log p_\theta(x) = -\nabla_x E_\theta(x)$ — the negative gradient of the energy. Score networks are therefore equivalent to learning the gradient of an energy surface. The denoising objective is equivalent to minimising the expected energy under noised distributions. This connection provides theoretical tools from statistical physics: detailed balance, Fokker-Planck equations, and the partition function perspective. EBMs trained by contrastive divergence are structurally similar to one-step diffusion.

resources.label: Score-Based Models through SDEs — Song et al. (2021)
Lilian Weng — What are Diffusion Models? (survey blog)

subtopics.title: DDIM, Consistency Models, and Fast Sampling
body: **DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models)** (Song et al., 2020) defines a **non-Markovian** forward process that has the same marginals $q(x_t|x_0)$ as DDPM but different joint distributions. The key: DDIM's reverse update is **deterministic** (no stochastic noise term): $x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \underbrace{\frac{x_t - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\epsilon_\theta(x_t,t)}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}}}_{\text{predicted } x_0} + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \epsilon_\theta(x_t,t)$. Since the update is deterministic, DDIM can **skip steps** — sample at $t = T, T-10, T-20, \ldots$ using a subset of the $T$ timesteps. With 50 steps (vs DDPM's 1000), sample quality is nearly identical. The $10$-$20\times$ speedup comes at no cost to the training procedure — the same $\epsilon_\theta$ trained for DDPM works for DDIM.

**DPM-Solver** and **DPM-Solver++** (Lu et al., 2022) treat the probability flow ODE as a structured ODE and derive high-order numerical solvers specific to diffusion model ODEs. The error of an $k$-th order solver decreases as $O(h^k)$ where $h$ is the step size — higher-order solvers need fewer steps for the same accuracy. DPM-Solver++ achieves $10$-$25$ step sampling at quality matching $1000$-step DDPM, by deriving order-2 and order-3 solvers that exploit the specific structure of the diffusion ODE (the linear part can be solved exactly, only the nonlinear score term is approximated). This is the default sampler in most production diffusion pipelines.

**Consistency Models** (Song et al., 2023) learn a function that maps any noisy sample $x_t$ directly to the clean sample $x_0$, enabling **single-step generation**. The consistency condition: $f_\theta(x_t, t) = f_\theta(x_{t'}, t')$ for any two points $x_t, x_{t'}$ on the same ODE trajectory. Trained either by **consistency distillation** (minimise $\|f_\theta(x_{t+\Delta t}, t+\Delta t) - f_{\theta^-}(x_t, t)\|^2$ where $x_{t+\Delta t}$ is obtained by one ODE step from $x_t$, and $\theta^-$ is an EMA of $\theta$) or by **consistency training** (without a pre-trained diffusion model). At inference: one step for acceptable quality; multiple steps (consistency sampling) for higher quality by adding noise and denoising repeatedly.

**Distillation methods**: **Progressive Distillation** (Salimans & Ho, 2022) trains a student model to match two steps of the teacher in one step, then uses the student as the new teacher. After $\log_2 T$ rounds, a 1-step model is obtained. **Latent Consistency Models (LCM)** apply consistency distillation in the latent space of a pre-trained diffusion model (Stable Diffusion), enabling 2-8 step generation in the latent space. **Stable Diffusion Turbo** (ADD: Adversarial Diffusion Distillation) combines distillation with an adversarial loss from a discriminator — achieving 1-4 step photo-realistic generation by simultaneously distilling the teacher and discriminating against real images.

**Flow Matching** (Lipman et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022) is a cleaner training objective for continuous normalising flows that sidesteps SDE/DDPM complexity. Instead of learning to reverse a diffusion process, learn a **velocity field** $v_\theta(x, t)$ that transports samples from $p_0 = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ to $p_1 = p_{\text{data}}$ via the ODE $dx/dt = v_\theta(x,t)$. Training objective: for each data pair $(x_0, x_1)$, define a conditional flow $p_t(x|x_0, x_1) = (1-t)x_0 + t x_1$ (straight line interpolation). The marginal velocity is $v(x,t) = \mathbb{E}[v(x,t|x_0,x_1)|x_t = x]$ — match this with $v_\theta$. Straight paths are easier to integrate (fewer steps needed) and flow matching has simpler gradients than DDPM's VLB. **Stable Diffusion 3** and **Flux** use flow matching as their training objective.

subtopics.title: Stable Diffusion: Latent Diffusion, CLIP, and Classifier-Free Guidance
body: **Latent Diffusion Models (LDM)** (Rombach et al., 2022) — the basis of Stable Diffusion — move the diffusion process from pixel space to the **latent space of a VAE**. A VAE encoder $\mathcal{E}$ compresses an image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ to $z = \mathcal{E}(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times w \times c}$ with $h = H/8, w = W/8$ (8x spatial compression). The decoder $\mathcal{D}$ reconstructs: $\tilde{x} = \mathcal{D}(z) \approx x$. Diffusion is applied in this compressed latent space: the U-Net denoises $z_t$ rather than $x_t$. The benefits: (1) the latent space is $64\times$ smaller than pixel space in compute ($H/8 \times W/8 \times c$ vs $H \times W \times 3$ with $c < 3 \times 64$), making training and inference far cheaper; (2) the VAE encoder abstracts away perceptual detail, so the diffusion model focuses on semantic content rather than pixel textures.

**Text conditioning via CLIP**: the text prompt is encoded by a frozen **CLIP text encoder** into a sequence of text token embeddings $c_\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$. The U-Net conditions on $c_\tau$ through **cross-attention**: at each resolution level of the U-Net, flatten the spatial feature map to a sequence of spatial tokens, use them as queries and $c_\tau$ as keys/values in cross-attention layers. This lets every spatial location in the image attend to every word in the prompt. CLIP encoders (particularly CLIP-ViT-L/14) were frozen and reused from OpenAI's CLIP training (400M image-text pairs from the internet) — a form of **cross-modal transfer** where the representation learned for contrastive image-text matching is repurposed for conditional generation.

**Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG)** (Ho & Salimans, 2022) is the technique that makes text-to-image generation high quality. Train the denoising network with text conditioning $c$ and also with **unconditional** (empty prompt) conditioning $\varnothing$: randomly drop the text condition with probability $p_{\text{drop}} \approx 0.1$ during training, replacing $c$ with $\varnothing$. At sampling, compute both $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c)$ (conditional) and $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \varnothing)$ (unconditional) and use the **guided** estimate: $\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \varnothing) + w [\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \varnothing)]$ where $w > 1$ is the **guidance scale** (typically 7-15 for Stable Diffusion). The guidance pushes the prediction away from the unconditional mean in the direction of the conditional — amplifying the influence of the text. Higher $w$ gives more text adherence at the cost of diversity and sometimes overexposure.

**The U-Net denoiser architecture** in Stable Diffusion: an encoder-decoder with skip connections (standard U-Net) where each resolution level has ResNet blocks + self-attention layers + cross-attention to text embeddings. The encoder downsamples from $64\times 64$ to $8\times 8$ latents (for SD1.x with 512px input); the decoder upsamples back. Skip connections preserve fine-grained spatial structure. The **time embedding** (sinusoidal, projected to 512-d) is added to every ResNet block's intermediate activations, giving the network access to the current noise level. **Textual inversion** (Gal et al., 2022) personalises SD by optimising a new token embedding to represent a custom concept — 3-5 images of a subject are sufficient to learn the embedding, which can then be used in arbitrary text prompts.

**ControlNet** (Zhang et al., 2023) adds spatial conditioning (edges, depth maps, poses, segmentation masks) to a frozen Stable Diffusion model by training a **trainable copy** of the encoder and middle block of the U-Net, connected to the frozen decoder via **zero-initialised convolutions** (starting at zero, so ControlNet has no initial effect and training discovers what to do). The spatial condition (e.g., a Canny edge map) is processed by the ControlNet branch and its activations are added to the frozen decoder. This preserves the frozen model's generative quality while adding precise spatial control. **IP-Adapter** (Ye et al., 2023) adds image conditioning via a decoupled cross-attention mechanism — a parallel cross-attention head that conditions on CLIP image embeddings instead of text embeddings, enabling image prompt conditioning.

resources.label: Latent Diffusion / Stable Diffusion paper — Rombach et al. (2022)

subtopics.title: MDPs and the Bellman Equations: The Mathematical Foundation of RL
body: **Reinforcement Learning** is formalised as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**: a tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is the state space, $\mathcal{A}$ the action space, $P(s'|s,a)$ the transition probability, $R(s,a,s')$ the reward function, and $\gamma \in [0,1)$ the discount factor. The **Markov property** asserts that $P(s_{t+1}|s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(s_{t+1}|s_t, a_t)$ — the future is independent of the past given the present state. This is the key modelling assumption that enables dynamic programming solutions.

A **policy** $\pi: \mathcal{S} \to \Delta(\mathcal{A})$ maps states to probability distributions over actions. The **state value function** $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t) | s_0 = s]$ is the expected discounted return starting from state $s$ under policy $\pi$. The **action-value function** $Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t) | s_0 = s, a_0 = a]$ is the expected return starting with action $a$ in state $s$, then following $\pi$. Relation: $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a|s) Q^\pi(s,a)$.

**Bellman expectation equations** express $V^\pi$ and $Q^\pi$ as recursive fixed points: $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a|s) \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ and $Q^\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a'|s') Q^\pi(s',a')]$. These are systems of $|\mathcal{S}|$ linear equations in $|\mathcal{S}|$ unknowns — solvable in $O(|\mathcal{S}|^3)$ by linear algebra for small MDPs. **Bellman optimality equations** give the optimal value functions: $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R + \gamma V^*(s')]$ and $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')]$. These are **nonlinear** fixed-point equations (due to the max) solved by dynamic programming.

**Policy evaluation** (computing $V^\pi$) by iterating the Bellman expectation operator $\mathcal{T}^\pi V(s) = \sum_a \pi(a|s) \sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)[R + \gamma V(s')]$ converges geometrically: $\|\mathcal{T}^\pi V - V^\pi\|_\infty \leq \gamma^k \|V_0 - V^\pi\|_\infty$ after $k$ iterations. **Policy improvement theorem**: greedily improve the policy w.r.t. $V^\pi$: $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q^\pi(s,a)$. Then $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^\pi(s)$ for all $s$. **Policy iteration** alternates policy evaluation and policy improvement until convergence — guaranteed to reach the optimal policy $\pi^*$ in at most $|\mathcal{A}|^{|\mathcal{S}|}$ iterations (finite, but exponential in general). **Value iteration** directly applies the Bellman optimality operator: converges to $V^*$ in $O(|\mathcal{S}|^2 |\mathcal{A}| \log(1/\epsilon))$ operations.

The **discount factor** $\gamma$ serves two purposes. Mathematically, it ensures the sum $\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t R_t$ converges (bounded rewards + $\gamma < 1$). Conceptually, $\gamma$ encodes how much the agent values immediate vs future rewards. $\gamma = 0$: myopic (only immediate reward). $\gamma \to 1$: equal weight on all future rewards. Economically, $\gamma = 1/(1+r)$ for interest rate $r$ — a dollar now is worth more than a dollar later because of investment opportunity. The effective **time horizon** is $1/(1-\gamma)$: an agent with $\gamma = 0.99$ has an effective horizon of 100 timesteps, with $\gamma = 0.999$ about 1000 timesteps. Many RL failures stem from misspecified $\gamma$: too low and the agent is short-sighted; too high and the value function is numerically sensitive.

resources.label: Sutton & Barto — Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (free PDF)

subtopics.title: TD Learning, Q-Learning, and DQN
body: **Temporal Difference (TD) learning** bridges Monte Carlo (full episode returns) and DP (one-step bootstrapping). **TD(0)**: after observing $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$, update the value estimate: $V(s_t) \leftarrow V(s_t) + \alpha[r_t + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)]$. The term in brackets is the **TD error** $\delta_t = r_t + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ — the difference between the immediate reward + discounted next-state value estimate (**TD target**) and the current estimate. TD learning is **online** (updates after every step), **model-free** (requires no $P$), and **biased but lower variance** than Monte Carlo (which requires waiting for episode end). The TD target $r + \gamma V(s')$ is a biased estimate of $V^\pi(s)$ because $V$ is approximate — **bootstrapping** off an imperfect estimate.

**$\lambda$-return and TD($\lambda$)**: weight between 1-step TD and full Monte Carlo returns. The $\lambda$-return $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^\infty \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$ where $G_t^{(n)} = r_t + \gamma r_{t+1} + \ldots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n-1} + \gamma^n V(s_{t+n})$ is the n-step return. $\lambda=0$: TD(0); $\lambda=1$: Monte Carlo. **Eligibility traces** implement TD($\lambda$) efficiently without storing all returns: $e_t(s) = \gamma \lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}[s_t = s]$ and $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$. Eligibility traces propagate credit backwards to recently visited states, amplifying the TD error update for states that were recently active.

**Q-learning** (Watkins & Dayan, 1992) learns $Q^*$ directly (off-policy): $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha[r_t + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)]$. The $\max$ operator makes this **off-policy** — the TD target uses the greedy action $\arg\max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$, regardless of the behaviour policy used to collect data. Q-learning converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 under mild conditions (all state-action pairs visited infinitely often, step sizes satisfy Robbins-Monro conditions). **SARSA** (on-policy TD): $Q(s_t,a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t,a_t) + \alpha[r_t + \gamma Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) - Q(s_t, a_t)]$ using the actual next action $a_{t+1}$, not the greedy action — safer in cliff-walking environments where greedy policy is dangerous.

**DQN (Deep Q-Network)** (Mnih et al., 2015) scales Q-learning to high-dimensional state spaces (e.g., raw Atari game pixels $84\times 84\times 4$) using a neural network $Q_\theta(s,a)$. Two key innovations that make neural network Q-learning stable: (1) **Experience replay**: store transitions $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$ in a replay buffer of size $10^6$, sample random mini-batches for training. This breaks temporal correlations (nearby transitions are correlated, violating the i.i.d. assumption of SGD) and enables data reuse. (2) **Target network**: maintain a separate $Q_{\theta^-}$ (periodically copied from $Q_\theta$ every $C$ steps) used for computing TD targets: $y_t = r_t + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s_{t+1}, a')$. Without the target network, the target moves with every parameter update (chasing a moving target) — causing training oscillations.

**DQN improvements**: **Double DQN** (van Hasselt et al., 2016) decouples action selection from value estimation to fix Q-learning's overestimation bias: select the greedy action using $Q_\theta$ but evaluate it using $Q_{\theta^-}$: $y_t = r_t + \gamma Q_{\theta^-}(s_{t+1}, \arg\max_a Q_\theta(s_{t+1}, a))$. **Prioritised Experience Replay** (Schaul et al., 2015) samples transitions with probability proportional to their TD error $|\delta_t|^\omega / \sum |\delta_j|^\omega$ — high-error transitions are more informative and should be replayed more often. Importance sampling weights correct for the sampling bias. **Dueling DQN** (Wang et al., 2016) decomposes $Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a)$ where $V$ is the state value and $A$ is the **advantage** (relative value of action $a$ over the mean). The advantage head is normalised by subtracting the mean: $Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a) - \bar{A}(s)$. This improves learning efficiency when the value of states is important regardless of the specific action chosen.

resources.label: DQN paper — Mnih et al. (2015, Nature)
OpenAI Spinning Up — Q-Learning and DQN implementation
Gymnasium — RL environments for algorithm testing

subtopics.title: Policy Gradients, Actor-Critic, and PPO
body: **Policy gradient** methods directly parameterise the policy $\pi_\theta(a|s)$ and optimise $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[\sum_t \gamma^t r_t]$ by gradient ascent. The **policy gradient theorem** (Sutton et al., 1999): $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\sum_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t|s_t) \cdot G_t\right]$ where $G_t = \sum_{k=t}^\infty \gamma^{k-t} r_k$ is the return from step $t$. Intuitively: increase the log-probability of actions proportional to their returns. **REINFORCE** (Williams, 1992) is the Monte Carlo estimate: run a full episode, compute $G_t$ for each step, and update. High variance (because $G_t$ depends on many stochastic actions) makes REINFORCE slow.

**Variance reduction with baselines**: subtracting a baseline $b(s_t)$ from $G_t$ does not bias the gradient (since $\mathbb{E}[
abla_	heta \log \pi_	heta \cdot b] = 0$) but reduces variance. Optimal baseline: $b^*(s) =  rac{\mathbb{E}[(
abla_	heta \log \pi_	heta)^2 G]}{\mathbb{E}[(
abla_	heta \log \pi_	heta)^2]}$ (the value function $V(s)$ is a near-optimal approximation). The **advantage** $A^\pi(s,a) = Q^\pi(s,a) - V^\pi(s)$ measures how much better action $a$ is than the average — centred, so it has mean zero per state, further reducing variance. Using advantages: $
abla_	heta J \approx \sum_t 
abla_	heta \log \pi_	heta(a_t|s_t) A(s_t, a_t)$.

**Actor-Critic** maintains two networks: **actor** $\pi_\theta(a|s)$ and **critic** $V_\phi(s)$ (or $Q_\phi(s,a)$). The actor improves the policy; the critic estimates values used to compute advantages. TD advantage: $\hat{A}_t = r_t + \gamma V_\phi(s_{t+1}) - V_\phi(s_t)$ — one-step approximation, biased but low variance. **Generalised Advantage Estimation (GAE)** (Schulman et al., 2015): $\hat{A}_t^{\text{GAE}(\gamma, \lambda)} = \sum_{l=0}^\infty (\gamma\lambda)^l \delta_{t+l}$ where $\delta_t = r_t + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ is the TD error. $\lambda$ controls the bias-variance tradeoff: $\lambda=0$ gives one-step TD (high bias, low variance); $\lambda=1$ gives Monte Carlo advantages (low bias, high variance). GAE with $\lambda=0.95, \gamma=0.99$ is the standard choice for PPO.

**PPO (Proximal Policy Optimisation)** (Schulman et al., 2017) solves the instability of standard policy gradient updates: large policy updates can collapse performance, and there is no principled step size. PPO clips the policy update ratio. Let $r_t(\theta) = \pi_\theta(a_t|s_t)/\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(a_t|s_t)$ be the importance weight. PPO objective: $\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_t[\min(r_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t, \text{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}_t)]$ where $\epsilon = 0.2$. The clip prevents $r_t$ from deviating more than $\epsilon$ from 1: if the advantage is positive but the policy has already increased the probability by more than $(1+\epsilon)$, the clipped term dominates and the gradient is zero — no further update in that direction. PPO's full loss adds a value function loss and an entropy bonus: $\mathcal{L} = -\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}} + c_1 \|V_\phi - V_{\text{target}}\|^2 - c_2 H[\pi_\theta]$.

**SAC (Soft Actor-Critic)** (Haarnoja et al., 2018) adds an **entropy term** to the objective: $J(\pi) = \mathbb{E}[\sum_t r_t + \alpha H(\pi(\cdot|s_t))]$ where $\alpha$ is the temperature. Maximising entropy encourages exploration and prevents premature convergence. SAC maintains two Q-networks (taking the minimum to reduce overestimation), an actor, and a temperature $\alpha$ tuned automatically to keep entropy near a target. SAC is the most sample-efficient off-policy algorithm for continuous control (MuJoCo benchmarks). **TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient)** (Fujimoto et al., 2018) is the deterministic policy analogue: twin Q-networks + delayed policy updates + target policy smoothing (adding noise to target actions).

resources.label: Berkeley CS285 — Deep RL lectures (free, Sergey Levine)

subtopics.title: RL for LLMs: InstructGPT, RLHF, and Offline RL
body: RL for LLMs treats the language model as a **policy** $\pi_\theta$ that generates text token-by-token. State: current context (all previous tokens). Action: next token from vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ (typically $|\mathcal{V}| = 32{,}000$-$128{,}000$). Reward: scalar feedback from a reward model $r_\phi(x, y)$ (where $x$ is the prompt and $y$ is the complete response) given at the end of the episode. This is a **delayed, sparse reward** problem with very large action space and long episodes (responses are hundreds of tokens). Standard RL methods must be adapted to this setting.

**InstructGPT** (Ouyang et al., 2022) applied PPO to a 175B GPT-3 model. Key engineering choices: (1) The KL penalty $\beta \text{KL}[\pi_\theta || \pi_{\text{SFT}}]$ is added as a **per-token reward** $-\beta \log[\pi_\theta(y_t|x, y_{<t})/\pi_{\text{SFT}}(y_t|x, y_{<t})]$, summed over the episode. This prevents reward hacking while training with PPO's standard per-step rewards. (2) The reward is given only at the final token, so advantage estimation propagates backwards through the sequence. (3) The value function head is a separate linear head on top of the LLM, trained jointly. InstructGPT demonstrated that RLHF-tuned 1.3B models outperformed base 175B models on instruction following — human preference signal is more valuable than scale alone for alignment.

**Offline RL** learns from a fixed dataset of transitions without further environment interaction — essential when online RL is expensive (robotics, clinical trials, LLM alignment from existing preference data). The core challenge is **distributional shift**: the learned policy may visit state-action pairs not in the dataset, and the Q-network has extrapolation errors there (Q-values may be overestimated, leading to catastrophic actions). **Conservative Q-Learning (CQL)** (Kumar et al., 2020) adds a penalty that pushes down Q-values for actions not in the dataset: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CQL}} = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim D}[\log\sum_a \exp(Q(s,a))] - \mathbb{E}_{(s,a) \sim D}[Q(s,a)] + \text{standard Bellman loss}$. The first term minimises Q-values under the learned policy; the second maximises them under the data distribution — this creates a conservative lower bound. **IQL (Implicit Q-Learning)** (Kostrikov et al., 2021) avoids querying the learned policy altogether during Q-function training by using expectile regression.

**DPO as implicit offline RL**: DPO (covered in the LLM section) can be interpreted as offline RL with a reward implicitly defined by the preference data. The DPO loss corresponds to the closed-form solution of the KL-constrained RL problem — a policy trained with DPO maximises the reward defined by human preferences subject to staying close to the reference policy. The offline interpretation explains DPO's failure modes: if the preference data is biased (e.g., one annotator's idiosyncratic preferences), the reward is biased, and the optimal policy under that reward may not align with broader human values.

**GRPO and reasoning via RL**: recent work (DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-QwQ) trains reasoning models using RL with verifiable rewards — for math problems, the reward is 1 if the final numerical answer is correct and 0 otherwise. The RL loop discovers that **chain-of-thought reasoning** (thinking through intermediate steps before answering) maximises the sparse binary reward, emerging as a learned strategy rather than being explicitly trained. GRPO (group relative policy optimisation) samples multiple responses per prompt, normalises advantages within the group, and updates without a critic. The outcome: models learn to 'think' by producing useful scratchpad reasoning as a strategy to get correct final answers, without explicit supervision of the reasoning process.

subtopics.title: FlashAttention: IO-Aware Exact Attention via Tiling
body: Standard attention $\text{softmax}(QK^T/\sqrt{d})V$ materialises the $n \times n$ attention matrix in **HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)** — the main GPU DRAM. For $n = 4096$ and FP16: the attention matrix is $4096^2 \times 2$ bytes $= 32$ MB per head, per layer, per sample. With $H=32$ heads and batch size $B=8$: 8 GB just for attention matrices. HBM bandwidth is $\sim 2$ TB/s for an A100 — reading/writing 8 GB takes 4 ms per attention computation. FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) achieves the same arithmetic result without ever materialising the full matrix in HBM.

**Tiling**: partition $Q, K, V$ into blocks that fit in **SRAM (L2/shared memory, ~40 MB on A100)** and compute attention block by block. For a block of queries $Q_i$ and key-value block $K_j, V_j$: compute $S_{ij} = Q_i K_j^T / \sqrt{d}$ in SRAM (never written to HBM), apply softmax incrementally, accumulate the weighted sum $O_i += \text{softmax}(S_{ij}) V_j$ with a running correction for the normalisation constant. The challenge: softmax requires the maximum $m = \max_j S_{ij}$ for numerical stability. FlashAttention uses the **online softmax** trick: maintain a running maximum $m_i$ and normalisation $l_i$. When processing block $j+1$: $m_i^{\text{new}} = \max(m_i, m_{i,j+1})$, rescale the accumulated output $O_i \leftarrow O_i \cdot e^{m_i - m_i^{\text{new}}}$, add the new block's contribution.

**IO complexity**: standard attention: $O(n^2)$ HBM reads/writes (materialise and read back the $n\times n$ matrix). FlashAttention: $O(n^2 d / M)$ HBM reads/writes where $M$ is the SRAM size — for $M \gg d$, this is $O(n^2)$ fewer memory operations by a factor of $M/d$. On A100 with $M = 40$ MB and $d = 64$: $\sim 640\times$ fewer HBM accesses asymptotically. In practice: **2-4x wall-clock speedup** and **10-20x memory saving** for long sequences. This is the dominant technique enabling long-context transformers (100k+ tokens).

**FlashAttention-2** (Dao, 2023) improves on v1 by: (1) reducing non-matmul FLOPs (the online softmax bookkeeping was expensive); (2) partitioning work to maximise parallelism across attention heads and sequence positions; (3) better SRAM utilisation on Ampere GPUs. FlashAttention-3 (Shah et al., 2024) targets Hopper (H100) architecture specifics: exploits **warp specialisation** (different warps do compute vs memory operations simultaneously), uses **FP8 precision** for matmuls with software accumulation in FP32, and **asynchronous pipeline** between GEMM and softmax — achieving $75%$ of H100 peak A100-equivalent throughput.

**Flash Attention for decoder inference**: during autoregressive decoding, the query is a single new token and the keys/values are the KV cache of all previous tokens. This is a **memory-bound** rather than **compute-bound** operation (only one row of the attention matrix). The main bottleneck is loading the KV cache from HBM. **Flash Decoding** (Dao, 2023) parallelises across the sequence length dimension (previously impossible since different threads would accumulate the same output row): each thread block computes partial softmax over a subset of keys, then a second pass combines them using the log-sum-exp trick. Enables $8\times$ faster decoding for long contexts.

subtopics.title: Parallelism: Tensor, Pipeline, Data, and Expert Parallelism
body: Training a model with $N$ parameters on a single GPU requires $\sim 16N$ bytes (FP16 params + grads + FP32 master weights + Adam moments) plus activations. A 7B model needs $\sim 112$ GB — a single A100 has 80 GB. **Model parallelism** distributes the model itself across GPUs. The four parallelism dimensions are orthogonal and composable: **DP** (data), **TP** (tensor), **PP** (pipeline), **EP** (expert) form the axes of the **5D parallelism** scheme used in Megatron-LM.

**Tensor Parallelism (TP)** (Shoeybi et al., 2019) shards individual weight matrices across GPUs. For a linear layer $Y = XW$ with $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 4d}$: split $W$ column-wise across $N$ GPUs: each GPU holds $W_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 4d/N}$ and computes $Y_i = X W_i$ (the full $X$ is on every GPU). For attention: split query/key/value projection heads across GPUs; each GPU handles $H/N$ heads. An **all-reduce** (sum across GPUs) is needed after the final projection. Communication volume: $O(B \cdot s \cdot d)$ per all-reduce (batch × sequence × hidden dim), which occurs once per transformer layer per forward pass. TP is **intra-node** (requires high-bandwidth NVLink; PCIe is too slow).

**Pipeline Parallelism (PP)**: assign consecutive transformer layers to different GPUs (stage). The forward pass sends activations between stages; the backward pass sends gradients. Naive pipeline: stage 1 computes forward, sends to stage 2, then is **idle** while stage 2-onwards compute — the **pipeline bubble** wastes $\sim (P-1)/P$ of time for $P$ stages. **GPipe**: split the batch into $m$ micro-batches, pipeline them to reduce bubble fraction to $(P-1)/(m+P-1)$. **PipeDream**: interleave forward and backward passes (1F1B schedule) to minimise both bubble and memory. **DualPipe** (DeepSeek, 2025): overlaps forward and backward of different micro-batches completely, nearly eliminating the pipeline bubble.

**Sequence Parallelism (SP)** distributes the sequence dimension across GPUs within a tensor-parallel group — used for activations that are not split by TP (LayerNorm, dropout). In TP, the attention heads are split but the input sequence is replicated; SP splits the sequence so each GPU holds a contiguous subsequence. An **all-gather** before TP and a **reduce-scatter** after reconstructs/distributes activations. SP reduces activation memory by $N_{\text{tp}}\times$ while maintaining the same TP communication pattern. **Context Parallelism (CP)** extends SP to the attention operation: each GPU holds a subsequence for Q, K, V; an all-gather of K, V is performed before attention (using ring attention patterns to amortise communication).

**Mixture of Experts (MoE)** is a form of **Expert Parallelism (EP)** where each transformer's FFN is replaced by $E$ expert FFNs, and each token is routed to $K$ of them ($K=2$ typically). A small **router network** (one linear + softmax) selects the top-$K$ experts per token. Only $K/E$ of parameters are activated per token — a 64-expert model activates $2/64 \approx 3\%$ of FFN parameters per token, giving $32\times$ theoretical capacity at fixed inference compute. EP places different experts on different GPUs; routing requires **all-to-all** collective (every GPU sends tokens to every other GPU). The main challenge: **load balancing** — without a balancing loss, all tokens route to the same experts. The **auxiliary load-balancing loss** $L_{\text{bal}} = \alpha \sum_e f_e p_e$ (where $f_e$ is the fraction of tokens routed to expert $e$ and $p_e$ is the mean router probability for expert $e$) encourages uniform routing. DeepSeek's fine-grained MoE (DeepSeekMoE) uses many small experts with $K$ large for even better routing granularity.

resources.label: Megatron-LM — Tensor Parallelism paper (Shoeybi et al., 2019)

subtopics.title: Gradient Checkpointing, Mixed Precision, and Memory Optimisation
body: **Activation memory** during training stores all intermediate tensors needed for the backward pass — for a transformer with $L$ layers, $B$ batch size, $s$ sequence length, $d$ hidden dim: activation memory is $O(L \cdot B \cdot s \cdot d)$ per FP32 byte — for LLaMA-7B ($L=32, d=4096$) with $B=1, s=4096$: $\sim 2$ GB just for activations (at FP16). With large $B$ or $s$, activations dominate memory. **Gradient checkpointing** recomputes activations during backward instead of storing them: store only the output of each checkpoint (e.g., every transformer block boundary), recompute the intermediate activations on demand during backward. Memory savings: $O(\sqrt{L})$ instead of $O(L)$ for an optimal checkpoint placement. Cost: $\sim 33\%$ extra FLOPs (roughly one extra forward pass).

**Mixed precision training** uses FP16 (or BF16) for forward and backward passes while maintaining FP32 **master weights** and running the optimiser step in FP32. Reasons: (a) FP32 master weights prevent precision loss from repeated weight updates (each update $\Delta w \sim 10^{-5}$ while $|w| \sim 1$ — ratio is $10^5$, which FP16 cannot represent; FP32 has $\sim 10^7$ precision). (b) Gradient accumulation must happen in FP32 to avoid rounding errors. (c) Loss scaling (GradScaler) multiplies the loss by a large factor (e.g., 65536) before backward to prevent FP16 underflow, then unscales before the optimiser step.
