BF16 has the same range as FP32 (8 exponent bits vs FP16's 5) but less mantissa precision — it rarely requires loss scaling and is now preferred for Ampere+ GPUs.

**Optimiser state sharding**: Adam maintains two extra tensors per parameter ($m$ and $v$, same size as $\theta$) in FP32 — adding $2 \times 4\Psi$ bytes on top of $4\Psi$ for FP32 weights and $2\Psi$ for FP16 weights/gradients, total $16\Psi$ bytes per parameter. For a 7B model: $\sim 112$ GB on one GPU. **Adafactor** (Shazeer & Stern, 2018) stores the second moment as a factored rank-1 approximation: $v_{ij} \approx r_i c_j$ (row and column factors) — reducing from $O(mn)$ to $O(m+n)$ memory per matrix. **Lion optimizer** (Chen et al., 2023) maintains only momentum (not the second moment) and uses the sign of the gradient — $1/3$ the memory of Adam. **Sophia** (Liu et al., 2023) uses a diagonal Hessian approximation for second-order optimisation at Adam's memory cost.

**Activation offloading** moves activations to CPU memory and transfers them back on demand during backward — trading GPU-CPU PCIe bandwidth for GPU memory. Modern PCIe 5.0 bandwidth is $\sim 64$ GB/s vs GPU HBM bandwidth $\sim 2$ TB/s, so this incurs a $\sim 30\times$ slowdown for activation-memory-bound workloads. Useful only when the GPU would otherwise OOM. **Paged optimiser states** (QLoRA) use NVIDIA's unified memory to page optimiser states to CPU on demand — transparent, no code changes, but adds latency spikes when pages fault. **8-bit optimisers** (Dettmers et al., 2022) quantise optimiser states to INT8, halving their memory at small accuracy cost.

**Profiling tools for training efficiency**: `torch.profiler` captures per-operation time and memory statistics, exportable to Chrome trace format. `nvidia-smi dmon` monitors GPU utilisation, memory, and temperature. Key metrics: **MFU (Model FLOP Utilisation)** = actual throughput / peak theoretical throughput. Typical MFU: 30-50% for well-tuned training jobs (vs 100% theoretical). Low MFU indicates bottlenecks: small batch size (memory-bound kernels), data loading (CPU-GPU transfer), or excessive communication overhead. **nsight systems** provides timeline-level visibility including CUDA kernel launches, NCCL collectives, and PCIe transfers.

subtopics.title: Speculative Decoding, Distillation, and Efficient Inference
body: **Knowledge distillation** (Hinton et al., 2015) transfers knowledge from a large **teacher** model $T$ to a small **student** model $S$ by training $S$ on the teacher's **soft targets** (output probability distributions) rather than hard one-hot labels. The distillation loss: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}} = \alpha \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(y, S) + (1-\alpha) T^2 \text{KL}(T/T || S/T)$ where $T$ is the temperature (divides logits before softmax) and $\alpha$ balances hard and soft losses. Soft targets encode the teacher's **dark knowledge** — the relative probabilities between non-correct classes (e.g., a truck image has higher probability for car than for cat) carry structural information not present in one-hot labels. This transfers more effectively to the student than supervised training alone.

**Speculative decoding mechanics** (already covered in LLM section but deeper here): the core bottleneck of LLM inference is **memory bandwidth**, not compute. Generating one token requires loading all $N$ parameters from HBM ($\sim 2N$ bytes for FP16) — at 2 TB/s HBM bandwidth, this takes $N/10^{12}$ seconds. For a 70B model: 70B × 2 bytes / 2 TB/s = 70 ms per token — the **roofline limit**. The target model call is memory-bound regardless of how many tokens it processes in parallel (up to the attention window). Speculative decoding exploits this: processing $K$ tokens in one target model call costs the same in memory bandwidth as processing 1 (since model weights are loaded once), but produces $K$ tokens if all are accepted — $K\times$ throughput improvement.

**Quantisation for inference** — INT4 vs INT8 vs FP8: **INT8 (W8A8)** quantises both weights and activations to 8-bit integers. The key operation $y = Wx$ is computed as $y = (W_q \cdot s_W)(x_q \cdot s_x) = s_W s_x (W_q x_q)$ where the integer matmul $W_q x_q$ uses hardware INT8 tensor cores. Accuracy loss is minimal ($< 0.5\%$ on most benchmarks). **INT4 weight-only quantisation** (GPTQ, AWQ) quantises only weights to 4-bit but dequantises them to FP16 before the matmul — avoids INT4 accumulation errors but is $\sim 1.5\times$ faster than FP16 weight-only (less data to load from HBM). **FP8 (E4M3, E5M2)** is the H100 native format — hardware-accelerated matmuls in FP8 with FP16 accumulation, giving $2\times$ the tensor core throughput of BF16 at similar accuracy for inference.

**Model compression beyond quantisation**: **pruning** removes individual weights (unstructured) or entire neurons/attention heads (structured) based on saliency ($|w|$, gradient magnitude, or Hessian-based scores). Unstructured pruning requires sparse matmul hardware (NVIDIA's 2:4 sparsity pattern — exactly 2 of every 4 weights are zero — is supported by sparse tensor cores with $2\times$ throughput). **Layer dropping** removes entire transformer blocks (typically middle layers) — effective because middle layers contribute least to output quality per sensitivity analysis. **KV cache compression**: instead of caching all past keys/values, methods like **SnapKV**, **StreamingLLM**, and **H2O** identify and retain only the $K$ most important KV pairs per head based on attention scores, enabling $10\times$ context extension with bounded KV cache memory.

topics.title: Graph Neural Networks
body: Graph Neural Networks process graph-structured data (social networks, molecules, knowledge graphs, code ASTs) by iteratively aggregating neighbour information. The message-passing framework (MPNN) unifies GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE. The Weisfeiler-Lehman test formalises GNN expressiveness limits, and spectral graph theory grounds the convolutional perspective.

subtopics.title: Graph Representation and the Message-Passing Framework (MPNN)
body: **Graph neural networks** operate on graphs $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$ nodes and $|E| = m$ edges. Each node $v$ has a feature vector $h_v^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (initial embedding); each edge $(u,v)$ optionally has features $e_{uv}$. The goal: learn node embeddings $h_v^{(L)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d'}$ (for node classification), graph embeddings $\mathbf{h}_G = \text{Readout}(\{h_v^{(L)}\})$ (for graph classification), or edge embeddings (for link prediction). **Why graphs?** Many real-world domains have relational structure: molecules (atoms as nodes, bonds as edges), social networks, citation networks, knowledge graphs, program dependence graphs, and road networks. Standard neural networks cannot directly process graph structures — order doesn't matter, neighbours vary in number, and global structure matters.

**The Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN)** framework (Gilmer et al., 2017) unifies most GNN architectures. Each layer $l$ computes: (1) **Message**: $m_{uv}^{(l)} = \phi^{(l)}(h_u^{(l-1)}, h_v^{(l-1)}, e_{uv})$ — compute a message from each neighbour $u$. (2) **Aggregate**: $M_v^{(l)} = \oplus_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} m_{uv}^{(l)}$ — aggregate all neighbour messages using a permutation-invariant aggregator $\oplus$ (sum, mean, max). (3) **Update**: $h_v^{(l)} = \psi^{(l)}(h_v^{(l-1)}, M_v^{(l)})$ — update the node embedding. After $L$ layers, $h_v^{(L)}$ aggregates information from the $L$-hop neighbourhood of $v$. The choice of $\phi$, $\oplus$, and $\psi$ defines the specific GNN architecture.

**Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)** (Kipf & Welling, 2017) is the simplest MPNN instantiation. Message: $m_{uv} = h_u$ (just the neighbour's embedding, no edge features). Aggregation + update in one step: $h_v^{(l)} = \sigma(W^{(l)} \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v) \cup \{v\}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_v d_u}} h_u^{(l-1)})$ where $d_v = |\mathcal{N}(v)|$ is the node degree and $W^{(l)}$ is the layer weight matrix. The symmetric normalisation $1/\sqrt{d_v d_u}$ prevents high-degree nodes from dominating. In matrix form: $H^{(l)} = \sigma(\hat{A} H^{(l-1)} W^{(l)})$ where $\hat{A} = D^{-1/2} (A+I) D^{-1/2}$ is the normalised adjacency with self-loops. Training: standard backpropagation; loss is defined over labelled nodes (semi-supervised node classification with $1$-$10$ labels per class from 2000+ node graphs).

**Graph Attention Networks (GAT)** (Velickovic et al., 2018) replace the fixed symmetric normalisation of GCN with learnable attention weights. For node $v$ and neighbour $u$: attention coefficient $e_{uv} = \text{LeakyReLU}(\mathbf{a}^T [W h_v || W h_u])$ where $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{2d'}$ is a learnable attention vector and $||$ denotes concatenation. Normalised: $\alpha_{uv} = \text{softmax}_u(e_{uv}) = \frac{\exp(e_{uv})}{\sum_{k \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \exp(e_{kv})}$. Update: $h_v^{(l)} = \sigma(\sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \alpha_{uv} W h_u)$. **Multi-head attention**: use $K$ independent attention mechanisms, concatenate or average outputs. GAT enables the model to learn which neighbours to attend to for each node, enabling dynamic, task-dependent aggregation. GAT v2 (Brody et al., 2022) fixes an issue with the original GAT where attention was not fully expressive (its attention was always a ranking of all neighbourhoods, not able to attend to any node for any query).

**GraphSAGE (Graph SAmple and aggreGatE)** (Hamilton et al., 2017) addresses the inductive setting: generalise to new nodes not seen during training (e.g., new users in a social network). Unlike GCN (which requires all nodes' features during training — transductive), GraphSAGE: (1) **Sample** a fixed-size neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}_k(v)$ (not all neighbours) at each layer; (2) **Aggregate** sampled neighbours: $h_{\mathcal{N}(v)}^{(l)} = \text{AGGREGATE}^{(l)}(\{h_u^{(l-1)}: u \in \mathcal{N}_k(v)\})$; (3) **Concatenate** and update: $h_v^{(l)} = \sigma(W^{(l)} [h_v^{(l-1)} || h_{\mathcal{N}(v)}^{(l)}])$. Sampling a fixed number of neighbours ($5$-$25$ per layer) caps memory and computation per training step regardless of graph size. Aggregators: mean (=GCN), max-pool, LSTM (random order, less principled). GraphSAGE enables training on billion-node graphs with mini-batch SGD.

subtopics.title: Spectral Graph Theory: Laplacian, Fourier, and Spectral GNNs
body: **Graph Laplacian**: the **unnormalised graph Laplacian** $L = D - A$ where $D = \text{diag}(d_1, ..., d_n)$ (degree matrix) and $A$ is the adjacency matrix. Key properties: $L$ is symmetric positive semi-definite (PSD); $x^T L x = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} (x_u - x_v)^2$ — the Laplacian measures the smoothness of a signal $x$ on the graph (how much adjacent nodes differ). Eigendecomposition: $L = U \Lambda U^T$ where $U = [u_1, ..., u_n]$ are the eigenvectors (graph Fourier basis) and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, ..., \lambda_n)$ are eigenvalues ($0 = \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq ... \leq \lambda_n$). The **normalised Laplacian** $\tilde{L} = D^{-1/2} L D^{-1/2}$ has eigenvalues in $[0,2]$.

**Graph Fourier transform**: the graph Fourier transform of a signal $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is $\hat{x} = U^T x$ (project onto the graph Fourier basis). The **inverse GFT** is $x = U \hat{x}$. Low-frequency eigenvectors (small $\lambda$) correspond to smooth signals (slowly varying across the graph); high-frequency eigenvectors correspond to rapidly oscillating signals. **Spectral convolution** of signals $x$ and filter $g$ on a graph: $(x *_G g) = U((U^T x) \odot (U^T g)) = U \hat{g} \hat{x}$ where $\hat{g} = \text{diag}(\hat{g}_1, ..., \hat{g}_n)$ is the filter in frequency domain. This is the graph analogue of the convolution theorem (convolution in space = pointwise multiplication in frequency).

**Spectral GNNs**: the first spectral GNN (Bruna et al., 2014) learned per-frequency filter coefficients $\{\hat{g}_i\}_{i=1}^n$ directly. Limitation: requires $O(n)$ parameters (one per frequency), is not spatially localised, and doesn't transfer across graphs with different Laplacians. **ChebNet** (Defferrard et al., 2016) approximates the filter with a truncated Chebyshev polynomial: $g_\theta(\tilde{\Lambda}) = \sum_{k=0}^K \theta_k T_k(\tilde{\Lambda})$ where $T_k$ is the $k$-th Chebyshev polynomial, $\tilde{\Lambda} = 2\Lambda/\lambda_{\max} - I$ (rescaled eigenvalues to $[-1,1]$). This $K$-order approximation is $K$-hop localised (depends only on $K$-hop neighbours), uses only $K$ parameters, and can be computed without explicit eigendecomposition: $T_0(\tilde{L}) x = x$, $T_1(\tilde{L}) x = \tilde{L} x$, $T_k(\tilde{L}) x = 2\tilde{L} T_{k-1}(\tilde{L}) x - T_{k-2}(\tilde{L}) x$. GCN is ChebNet with $K=1$ and a first-order approximation.

**Beyond spectral: position encodings for graphs**: standard MPNNs are **anonymous** — they cannot distinguish nodes in a regular graph (all look the same from within their $k$-hop neighbourhood). **Positional encodings** assign each node a unique identity. **Laplacian eigenvectors** as PE: $\text{PE}(v) = [u_1(v), u_2(v), ..., u_k(v)]$ (first $k$ Laplacian eigenvectors at node $v$). These encode structural position (nodes with similar positions in the graph have similar PE). **Random walk PE** (Dwivedi et al., 2021): $\text{RWPE}(v)_k = P^k_{vv}$ (probability of being at $v$ after $k$ steps, i.e., the diagonal of the $k$-step random walk matrix $P^k$) — encodes local structural role. **Equivariant PE**: sign and basis ambiguity in eigenvectors (they are defined up to sign) require special equivariant treatment — **SignNet** and **BasisNet** address this.

resources.label: Graph Positional Encodings - Dwivedi et al. (2021)

subtopics.title: GNN Expressiveness: Weisfeiler-Lehman Test, 3-WL, and Limitations
body: **Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) graph isomorphism test** (1968) is a classical algorithm for testing graph isomorphism (are two graphs identical up to node relabelling?). The 1-WL (colour refinement) algorithm: (1) Initialise each node with a colour $c_v^{(0)} = h_v$ (node feature hash). (2) Iteratively update: $c_v^{(l+1)} = \text{HASH}(c_v^{(l)}, \{\{c_u^{(l)}: u \in \mathcal{N}(v)\}\})$ (multiset of neighbour colours). (3) If two graphs produce different colour distributions at any layer, they are non-isomorphic. 1-WL is sound but not complete — some non-isomorphic graphs produce the same colour sequence (e.g., all $3$-regular graphs look the same to 1-WL).

**GNNs and the WL hierarchy** (Xu et al., 2018): any MPNN with injective aggregation (the mapping from neighbourhood multiset to aggregate is injective — no two different multisets produce the same aggregate) is exactly as expressive as 1-WL. **GIN (Graph Isomorphism Network)** achieves this: $h_v^{(l)} = \text{MLP}^{(l)}((1 + \epsilon^{(l)}) h_v^{(l-1)} + \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} h_u^{(l-1)})$. The sum aggregator (not mean or max) is injective for multisets over a countable domain; the MLP (universal approximator) can implement any injective update function. No MPNN can distinguish graphs that 1-WL cannot distinguish — this is the **expressiveness upper bound**.

**Limitations of 1-WL**: (1) **Cannot count triangles or cycles**: two graphs, one a cycle of 6 nodes and one two triangles, are indistinguishable to 1-WL. (2) **Cannot determine graph properties that depend on global structure**: diameter, number of connected components (beyond disconnected graphs), planarity. (3) **Cannot distinguish all regular graphs**: all $k$-regular graphs of the same size produce the same 1-WL colouring. **3-WL test** and **$k$-WL**: higher-order WL tests operate on tuples of nodes. $k$-WL operates on $k$-tuples, with expressiveness strictly increasing with $k$. $3$-WL can distinguish all graphs that $2$-WL cannot (and 2-WL $>$ 1-WL). **Higher-order GNNs** (Morris et al., 2019) implement $k$-WL in neural networks: represent $k$-tuples of nodes as separate 'super-nodes', aggregate based on which tuples share $k-1$ nodes. The cost: $O(n^k)$ super-nodes for $k$-WL, making $k \geq 3$ expensive for large graphs.

**Structural GNNs to overcome 1-WL limits**: (1) **NGNN (Nested GNN)** (Zhang & Li, 2021): for each node $v$, extract the $r$-ego subgraph (induced by $v$'s $r$-hop neighbours), apply a GNN to each subgraph, use subgraph embeddings as additional node features. This provides subgraph structure information beyond 1-WL. (2) **ESAN (Equivariant Subgraph Aggregation Network)** (Bevilacqua et al., 2022): process multiple subgraph views of the graph and aggregate equivariantly. (3) **DE (Distance Encoding)** (Li et al., 2020): encode structural distance between nodes (shortest path distance, landing probabilities) as additional node features — provides global structural context. (4) **Graphormer** (Ying et al., 2021): apply the Transformer to graphs, using shortest-path distance as a spatial bias in attention: $\text{Attn}(i,j) = \text{softmax}((Q K^T + b_{d(i,j)}) / \sqrt{d})$ where $b_{d(i,j)}$ is a learned scalar per distance $d$.

**Molecular GNNs**: the primary application domain where GNN expressiveness matters concretely. **Molecular property prediction**: given a molecular graph (atoms = nodes, bonds = edges), predict toxicity, solubility, protein binding affinity, etc. **Chirality** (a mirror-image of a molecule has different properties) requires **stereochemistry-aware** GNNs — standard MPNNs are chirality-blind (they cannot distinguish a molecule from its mirror image). **3D molecular GNNs** (SchNet, DimeNet, SphereNet) use 3D atom positions: pairwise distances, bond angles, dihedral angles as geometric features. **SE(3)-equivariant networks** (EGNN, PaiNN) are equivariant to rotation and translation of the 3D structure — essential for physical correctness. **AlphaFold2** uses a Evoformer (Transformer with pair features representing all atom pairs) to predict protein 3D structure from sequence — the most impactful GNN-style model to date.

resources.label: How Powerful are GNNs? - Xu et al. (2018)

connections.note: Spectral graph theory (Laplacian, Fourier) and WL expressiveness are deeply mathematical
Code property graphs and program dependence graphs are used in binary analysis and neural program representations
Graph adversarial attacks target GNNs by perturbing graph topology or node features

resources.label: CS224W: Machine Learning with Graphs - Stanford course

topics.title: ML Systems and GPU Programming
body: ML systems bridges machine learning and systems programming: GPU architecture (CUDA memory hierarchy, tensor cores, warp execution), kernel DSLs (Triton, CUTLASS), distributed training internals (NCCL, ring allreduce), and inference engine design (continuous batching, paged KV cache, speculative decoding). Understanding the hardware is prerequisite to writing fast ML code.

subtopics.title: CUDA Architecture: SIMT, Memory Hierarchy, and Tensor Cores
body: **GPU execution model (SIMT)**: a CUDA GPU executes code in **warps** of 32 threads. All threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** simultaneously (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads). Each thread has a unique thread index `(blockIdx, threadIdx)` used to select data — this is how a single kernel function implements data-parallel computation. A **thread block** (up to $1024$ threads = 32 warps) executes on a single **Streaming Multiprocessor (SM)**. All SMs execute blocks simultaneously, enabling massive parallelism (A100 has 108 SMs × $\sim$32 active warps per SM = $\sim 3456$ active warps simultaneously). **Warp divergence**: if threads in a warp take different branches (`if/else`), the GPU serialises execution of both branches — the warp stalls on the inactive branch. Avoid branches that diverge within a warp; instead, use branchless code or ensure all threads in a warp take the same branch.

**GPU memory hierarchy**: (1) **Registers** ($\sim 256$ KB per SM, partitioned among active threads) — fastest, private per thread, holds loop variables and local computation. (2) **Shared memory (SRAM)** ($\sim 160$-$192$ KB per SM on A100) — user-managed, accessible by all threads in a block, $\sim 10$ TB/s bandwidth. Explicitly managed by the programmer: `__shared__` declaration; all threads in a block can read/write; requires `__syncthreads()` for synchronisation. (3) **L1 cache** ($\sim 192$ KB per SM, configurable split with shared memory) — automatic, per-SM. (4) **L2 cache** ($40$ MB total on A100) — on-chip, shared across SMs. (5) **HBM (Global memory)** ($80$ GB on A100) — off-chip, $\sim 2$ TB/s bandwidth, highest latency ($\sim 400$ ns). The key programming insight: operations must be structured to maximise shared memory reuse and minimise HBM accesses — this is why tiling (blocking) is the fundamental GPU optimisation technique.

**Tensor cores** are special-purpose hardware units that compute matrix multiply-accumulate operations in mixed precision: $D = A \times B + C$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times K}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times N}$, $C, D \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$ with $M = N = K = 16$ (the fundamental tile size). Tensor core precision modes: FP16 × FP16 + FP32 → FP32 (Volta), BF16 × BF16 + FP32 → FP32 (Ampere), FP8 × FP8 + FP16 → FP16 (Hopper). Peak throughput: A100 achieves $312$ TFLOPS for FP16 matmul with tensor cores vs $19.5$ TFLOPS for FP32 scalar math — $16\times$ higher throughput. To achieve near-peak performance: the matmul must be implemented to use tensor cores (via WMMA API or via cuBLAS/CUTLASS), the matrices must be in the right memory layout (row-major vs column-major), and the shapes must be multiples of the tensor core tile size ($16$).

**Occupancy and latency hiding**: **occupancy** = (active warps per SM) / (maximum warps per SM). High occupancy enables the GPU to **hide memory latency** — while warp A is waiting for an HBM memory access (400ns latency), the SM switches to warp B (which has data ready). With enough warps, the memory latency is fully hidden and the SM achieves full compute throughput. Occupancy is limited by register usage per thread (more registers = fewer threads per SM), shared memory usage per block (more smem per block = fewer blocks per SM), and the max threads per SM hardware limit. **Roofline model**: any kernel is either **compute-bound** (FLOPs limit throughput, arithmetic intensity $> $ peak FLOPs/bandwidth) or **memory-bandwidth-bound** (HBM bandwidth limits throughput). FlashAttention's key innovation was recognising that naive attention is memory-bandwidth-bound and restructuring to be compute-bound via tiling.

**CUDA programming patterns**: (1) **Grid-stride loops**: if the dataset is larger than the grid, each thread processes multiple elements: `for (int i = blockIdx.x*blockDim.x + threadIdx.x; i < N; i += gridDim.x*blockDim.x)`. (2) **Coalesced memory access**: threads in a warp access consecutive memory addresses — the GPU can serve the entire warp in one or two memory transactions (128-byte cache line). Non-coalesced access (strided, random) requires $32$ separate transactions. (3) **Bank conflicts in shared memory**: shared memory is divided into 32 banks; if multiple threads access the same bank in the same cycle, accesses serialise. Avoid by padding shared memory arrays (`float smem[SIZE + 1]`). (4) **Atomic operations**: `atomicAdd`, `atomicMax` for thread-safe updates to shared/global memory — expensive (serialises conflicting updates) but sometimes unavoidable.

subtopics.title: Triton DSL: Python-Based GPU Kernel Authoring
body: **Triton** (Tillet et al., 2019; OpenAI) is a Python-embedded DSL for writing GPU kernels at a higher level than CUDA C++. The key abstraction: **block-level operations** on tiles of tensors rather than individual element operations. A Triton kernel operates on **1D/2D blocks** of values, and the programmer specifies which block indices to process; Triton handles thread mapping, shared memory allocation, and vectorisation automatically. The programming model: each Triton kernel invocation is a **program instance** with a unique `program_id = tl.program_id(axis=0)`. Multiple programs execute in parallel across GPU SMs — the programmer specifies the grid in the launcher.

**Triton kernel anatomy** for a fused add+relu example: `@triton.jit` decorates the function; `tl.load(ptr + offsets, mask=offsets < N)` loads a block of values from device memory (automatically vectorised and coalesced); `tl.store(ptr + offsets, result, mask=...)` writes back. `BLOCK_SIZE = triton.next_power_of_2(N)` sets the tile size; `@triton.autotune(configs=[...], key=['N'])` automates block size selection via benchmarking. The compiler compiles Triton IR → LLVM IR → PTX (NVIDIA assembly). The key benefit: Triton handles bank conflicts, vectorisation, and warp scheduling automatically — the programmer thinks in tiles, not threads.

**Implementing matmul in Triton**: the fundamental pattern. Split the output matrix $C = A B$ ($M \times N = (M \times K) \times (K \times N)$) into $\lceil M/B_M \rceil \times \lceil N/B_N \rceil$ output tiles. Each program computes one $B_M \times B_N$ tile: `pid_m, pid_n = tl.program_id(0), tl.program_id(1)`. Load tiles of $A$ ($B_M \times B_K$) and $B$ ($B_K \times B_N$) into SRAM; compute $C_{\text{tile}} = A_{\text{tile}} B_{\text{tile}}$ using tensor core operations (`tl.dot`); accumulate across $K$-dimension tiles. Store the final tile to HBM. **Tuning knobs**: `BLOCK_M`, `BLOCK_N`, `BLOCK_K` (tile sizes), `num_stages` (software pipelining depth — number of tiles prefetched), `num_warps`. The optimal configuration depends on the matrix shapes and GPU model; Triton's autotune benchmarks all configurations automatically.

**CUTLASS**: NVIDIA's C++ template library for high-performance linear algebra on GPUs. CUTLASS implements hierarchical tiling: thread block tile (GEMM tile in shared memory) → warp tile (GEMM tile per warp using tensor cores) → thread tile (register-level accumulation). **Epilogue fusion**: CUTLASS enables fusing the GEMM with an arbitrary elementwise operation (bias add, activation, scaling) in the same kernel, eliminating a separate HBM round-trip. **CuTe** (Cute Tensor layouts, within CUTLASS 3.x) abstracts tensor layouts with a composable layout algebra — expressing strides, swizzle patterns, and partitionings algebraically. FlashAttention-3 uses CUTLASS CuTe kernels directly for maximum H100 utilisation.

**Profiling ML kernels**: **NCU (NVIDIA Nsight Compute)** provides detailed kernel-level profiling: achieved occupancy, memory throughput, FLOPs, instruction mix, tensor core utilisation, memory access patterns, and warp stall reasons. Key metrics to inspect: (1) **SM Active Cycles**: should be high ($> 80%$); (2) **Tensor Core Active Cycles**: should be high for matmul kernels ($> 60%$ for well-optimised kernels); (3) **Memory Throughput**: should be near the roofline bound for memory-bound kernels; (4) **Warp Stall - Long Scoreboard**: warp waiting for a memory operation — indicates memory-bound computation or uncoalesced access. **NSys (Nsight Systems)** provides system-level profiling: kernel timelines, CPU-GPU overlaps, NCCL communication events — essential for identifying bottlenecks in distributed training.

subtopics.title: Distributed Training Internals: NCCL, Ring Allreduce, and Communication Overlap
body: **Collective communication operations**: distributed training requires synchronising gradients (data parallelism) and activations (model parallelism) across GPUs. The NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) implements: (1) **AllReduce**: each GPU sends its tensor; all GPUs receive the sum/mean (gradient synchronisation in DDP). (2) **AllGather**: each GPU sends a shard; all GPUs receive the full tensor (ZeRO stage 3 parameter reconstruction). (3) **ReduceScatter**: each GPU sends its full tensor; each GPU receives one shard of the sum (ZeRO stage 3 gradient reduction). (4) **AllToAll**: each GPU sends one shard to each other GPU (MoE expert routing). These are implemented with optimised GPU-to-GPU communication using NVLink (within a node) or InfiniBand RDMA (across nodes).

**Ring allreduce algorithm** (Baidu, 2017): decompose AllReduce into 2 phases for optimal bandwidth utilisation. For $p$ GPUs and a gradient tensor of $N$ values: (1) **Reduce-scatter**: $p-1$ rounds; in each round, GPU $i$ sends its (current) chunk $[i \cdot N/p, (i+1) \cdot N/p)$ to GPU $(i+1) \bmod p$ and receives a chunk from GPU $(i-1) \bmod p$, adds it to its local chunk. After $p-1$ rounds, each GPU holds the reduced (summed) value of one chunk. (2) **AllGather**: $p-1$ rounds; each GPU sends its fully-reduced chunk in a ring. After $p-1$ rounds, all GPUs have all chunks. Total data sent per GPU: $2 \times N(p-1)/p \approx 2N$ — independent of $p$! Ring allreduce has **optimal bandwidth**: it uses $100%$ of the available bidirectional bandwidth regardless of number of GPUs.

**Communication-computation overlap**: in DDP, naively waiting for AllReduce after the full backward pass wastes time. **Gradient bucketing + async AllReduce**: PyTorch DDP groups parameters into buckets; when the last gradient in a bucket is computed, the bucket's AllReduce is launched asynchronously. The backward pass continues computing gradients for earlier layers while the AllReduce for later layers is in flight. With sufficient bucket size ($\sim 25$ MB per bucket) and gradient computation time, the AllReduce is fully hidden behind computation. **FSDP computation-communication overlap**: the all-gather of forward parameters for layer $l+1$ can overlap with the forward computation of layer $l$; the reduce-scatter of layer $l$'s gradients can overlap with the backward computation of layer $l-1$.

**InfiniBand vs NVLink**: NVLink provides $\sim 600$ GB/s bidirectional bandwidth within a node (A100 DGX: 8 GPUs fully connected via NVLink 3.0). InfiniBand (IB) connects nodes: IB HDR provides $200$ Gb/s = $25$ GB/s per port, commonly with $2$-$4$ IB ports per node for $50$-$100$ GB/s. The bandwidth hierarchy: NVLink ($600$ GB/s) >> InfiniBand ($100$ GB/s) >> PCIe ($64$ GB/s) >> Ethernet ($25$ GB/s). For large-scale distributed training (thousands of GPUs), the IB fabric topology matters — **fat-tree** topologies provide full bisection bandwidth; poorly designed topologies create bottlenecks. **RDMA (Remote DMA)**: InfiniBand supports RDMA — the GPU can DMA directly to/from another node's GPU memory without involving either node's CPU. This reduces latency and CPU overhead in cross-node communication.

**Inference engine internals**: production LLM serving combines multiple systems insights. **vLLM continuous batching**: instead of waiting for all sequences in a batch to complete (static batching), process one token at a time and swap sequences in/out of the batch as they complete or are preempted. The scheduler runs every token step, selecting which sequences to run. **Prefill vs decode**: the **prefill** phase (processing the input prompt as one parallel forward pass) is compute-bound (many tokens processed in parallel = high tensor core utilisation); the **decode** phase (generating one token at a time with KV cache) is memory-bandwidth-bound (loading $16N$ bytes of parameters + KV cache). **Chunked prefill**: interleave prefill of new requests with decode of ongoing requests, keeping the GPU compute-bound by ensuring the effective batch size during prefill is large enough to saturate tensor cores.

subtopics.title: Compiler Infrastructure for ML: XLA, torch.compile, and Hardware Backends
body: **XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra)**: Google's ML compiler, the backend for JAX and TensorFlow. XLA takes a **computation graph** (sequence of high-level operations) and produces optimised machine code for the target hardware (GPU, TPU, CPU). Key optimisations: (1) **Operation fusion**: fuse `matmul + bias_add + relu` into a single kernel (eliminating 2 HBM round-trips). (2) **Layout selection**: choose tensor memory layouts (e.g., NHWC vs NCHW for convolutions) to minimise data movement for each hardware. (3) **Memory planning**: allocate tensors at compile time, enabling buffer reuse and minimising peak memory. (4) **HLO (High Level Optimiser)** IR: the intermediate representation XLA optimises. XLA is particularly effective on TPUs (Google's custom ML chips) because the TPU instruction set maps closely to the high-level operations in HLO.

**torch.compile** (PyTorch 2.0) provides a JIT compilation path for PyTorch programs. Architecture: (1) **TorchDynamo** (Dynamo): a Python bytecode interceptor that captures the computation graph by executing the Python code and recording operations as symbolic traces. Handles Python control flow (loops, conditionals) by specialising on concrete values and re-tracing when shapes change. (2) **AOT Autograd**: traces both forward and backward pass ahead-of-time (before execution), enabling joint optimisation of both. (3) **TorchInductor** (default backend): generates Triton kernels (GPU) or C++ (CPU) from the IR. Key fusion patterns: pointwise elementwise chains, matmul + epilogue, attention. Speedup: $1.5$-$3\times$ on BERT, GPT-2, ResNet via operator fusion and reduced Python overhead.

**TPU Architecture**: Google's Tensor Processing Unit is fundamentally different from NVIDIA GPUs. The TPU v4 core: a **systolic array** of $128 \times 128 = 16384$ multiply-accumulate units, processing BF16 matmuls at $275$ TFLOPS per chip. Memory hierarchy: on-chip SRAM ($64$ MB on TPU v4) >> off-chip HBM ($32$ GB). The systolic array feeds data from left to right and accumulates results top to bottom — optimal for GEMM. The key difference from GPU: TPUs lack the general-purpose CUDA programming model; all computation must be expressed as XLA HLO operations, which the TPU compiler maps to systolic array operations. This makes TPUs harder to programme for custom kernels but more efficient at the operations they support. **TPU v4 Pod**: $4096$ TPU chips connected via a custom 3D torus interconnect, providing $4096 \times 1.1$ TB/s = $\sim 4.5$ PB/s total inter-chip bandwidth.

**MLIR (Multi-Level Intermediate Representation)**: an LLVM-inspired compiler infrastructure for ML. MLIR defines a hierarchy of IRs (dialects) with lowering transformations between them: `torch` dialect → `linalg` dialect (tensor operations) → `affine` dialect (loop nests) → `llvm` dialect → machine code. Each lowering step applies optimisations appropriate to that level of abstraction. MLIR is used by: TensorFlow (XLA backend), IREE (inference compiler), ONNX-MLIR, and increasingly PyTorch (through torch-mlir). The key benefit: domain-specific optimisations can be expressed as IR passes at the appropriate level without rebuilding the full compiler infrastructure. MLIR enables reuse of common infrastructure across ML frameworks targeting different hardware backends.

resources.label: Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr - Horace He (compiler overview)

connections.note: FlashAttention, parallelism strategies, and gradient checkpointing are ML systems techniques applied to LLM training
GPU SIMT execution model, memory hierarchy, and tensor cores are specialised microarchitecture concepts
ML compilers (XLA, torch.compile, MLIR) build directly on compiler infrastructure concepts: IR, lowering, optimisation passes

topics.title: Classical XAI: LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM & TCAV
body: **Explainable AI (XAI)** is the discipline of making model decisions human-interpretable, either post-hoc (explaining a trained model) or by design (inherently interpretable models). Classical XAI focuses on the former: given any black-box $f$, produce explanations of the form 'the model predicted $y$ because of features $x_1, x_3$'.

The central tension: *faithfulness* (does the explanation reflect what the model actually computed?) vs *plausibility* (does it match human reasoning patterns?). These are not the same. A paper by Lipton (2018) named this the 'mythos of model interpretability' and remains mandatory reading after touching any XAI method.

subtopics.title: LIME: Local Linear Approximations of Black-Box Models
body: **LIME (Locally Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)** (Ribeiro et al., 2016) answers: for a specific input $x_0$, which features drove the prediction? The key insight is that even a highly non-linear model $f$ is approximately linear in a small neighbourhood around any given point. LIME exploits this by fitting a sparse linear model $g$ to approximate $f$ locally, then reporting $g$'s coefficients as the explanation.

**Algorithm**: (1) Sample perturbations $z'$ around $x_0$ in an interpretable input space (e.g., superpixels for images, word presence for text). (2) Map each $z'$ back to the original feature space as $z$ and query the black-box: $f(z)$. (3) Weight each sample by proximity to $x_0$ using an exponential kernel $\pi_{x_0}(z) = \exp(-D(x_0, z)^2 / \sigma^2)$ where $D$ is a distance metric. (4) Fit a sparse linear model $g$ by solving the weighted LASSO: $$\text{argmin}_{g \in G} \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_{x_0}) + \Omega(g)$$ where $\mathcal{L}$ is weighted squared loss and $\Omega(g)$ penalises the complexity of $g$ (e.g., $\|w_g\|_0 \leq K$ for at most $K$ non-zero weights). The coefficients of $g$ are the LIME explanation.

**Image LIME** partitions images into superpixels (contiguous regions of similar colour via SLIC) and perturbs by toggling superpixels on/off (replacing 'off' superpixels with grey or mean colour). The linear model then assigns a positive or negative weight to each superpixel, directly showing which image regions pushed the prediction up or down. **Text LIME** perturbs by removing words; each word gets a signed weight.

**Limitations are fundamental, not incidental**: (i) *Instability* - the sampling procedure is stochastic, so re-running LIME on the same instance gives different explanations. (ii) *Neighbourhood definition* - the choice of kernel width $\sigma$ and what 'neighbourhood' means is arbitrary and strongly influences results. (iii) *Faithfulness gap* - the linear approximation may be poor if $f$ is highly non-linear locally; you cannot know this without ground truth. (iv) *Feature independence assumption* - LIME treats features as independent in its perturbation, which ignores correlations. These limitations motivated SHAP.

**The trust question**: Ribeiro et al. found that LIME explanations help humans identify whether to trust a specific prediction. In their famous 'Husky vs Wolf' example, LIME revealed that a high-accuracy wolf/husky classifier was actually using snow in the background as its primary feature — a shortcut the model had learned from dataset bias. Without LIME, the 99% accuracy would have been misleading. This is XAI's practical value: not just explaining correct predictions, but exposing model failures.

resources.label: Ribeiro et al. — 'Why Should I Trust You?' LIME paper (2016)
LIME GitHub repository with worked examples

subtopics.title: SHAP: Shapley Values and Unified Feature Attribution
body: **SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)** (Lundberg & Lee, 2017) grounds feature attribution in **cooperative game theory**. A Shapley value $\phi_i(v)$ for player $i$ in a game with value function $v$ is the unique attribution satisfying four axioms: *efficiency* (attributions sum to the total payout), *symmetry* (identical players get equal credit), *dummy* (a player who contributes nothing gets zero), and *linearity* (attributions are additive over independent games). Lundberg & Lee showed that among all additive feature attribution methods, SHAP values are the **unique** method satisfying these axioms.

**Mathematical definition**: For model $f$ and instance $x$, the Shapley value of feature $i$ is: $$\phi_i(f, x) = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{|S|!(|F|-|S|-1)!}{|F|!} \left[ f_{S \cup \{i\}}(x_{S \cup \{i\}}) - f_S(x_S) \right]$$ where $F$ is the full feature set, $S$ ranges over all subsets excluding feature $i$, and $f_S(x_S)$ is the model's expected output when only features in $S$ are known (others are marginalised out). The intuition: $\phi_i$ is the average marginal contribution of feature $i$ across all possible orderings in which features join the 'coalition'.

**Exact Shapley values are exponential** in the number of features ($2^{|F|}$ subsets). SHAP's practical power comes from model-specific approximations. **TreeSHAP** (for tree ensembles) computes exact Shapley values in $O(TLD^2)$ time where $T$ is the number of trees, $L$ is leaves, $D$ is depth — polynomial, not exponential. **KernelSHAP** is model-agnostic and uses a weighted linear regression trick: the Shapley kernel weight for a coalition $S$ is $\kappa(S) = \frac{|F|-1}{\binom{|F|}{|S|}|S|(|F|-|S|)}$, which makes the kernel regression exactly recover Shapley values in expectation. **LinearSHAP** exploits linear model structure for exact computation.

**SHAP enables global explanations** by aggregating local ones. A **SHAP summary plot** shows each feature's Shapley value distribution across the dataset: the x-axis is the SHAP value (impact on output), the y-axis is the feature, and colour encodes the feature value (red=high, blue=low). A **SHAP dependence plot** for feature $i$ shows $\phi_i$ vs $x_i$, revealing non-linear effects. **SHAP interaction values** decompose the contribution of feature pairs $(i, j)$ jointly, capturing interaction effects that univariate attribution misses: $$\phi_{ij}(f,x) = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i,j\}} \frac{|S|!(|F|-|S|-2)!}{2(|F|-1)!} \delta_{ij}(f, S, x)$$

**Critical limitation**: the marginalisation of 'unknown' features ($f_S(x_S)$) requires a choice. The **interventional** approach replaces missing features with samples from the marginal distribution (ignoring correlations), while the **conditional** approach conditions on available features. These give different Shapley values and different explanations. For correlated features (e.g., height and weight), conditional SHAP attributes more to the correlated group while interventional SHAP can assign implausible feature combinations. Neither is uniquely 'correct' — they answer different causal questions.

resources.label: SHAP documentation with visual explanation guides
TreeSHAP paper — polynomial-time exact Shapley for trees

subtopics.title: Grad-CAM: Gradient-Weighted Class Activation Mapping
body: **Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping)** (Selvaraju et al., 2017) produces **visual explanations** for CNN decisions by highlighting which spatial regions of an input image were most important for a predicted class. Unlike LIME and SHAP, Grad-CAM is not model-agnostic — it requires access to gradients and is designed specifically for convolutional networks. Its advantage is speed and the naturalness of the output: a heatmap over the input image.

**Algorithm**: Let $A^k \in \mathbb{R}^{u \times v}$ be the $k$-th feature map of the last convolutional layer, and let $y^c$ be the class score for class $c$ before softmax. Grad-CAM computes the gradient of $y^c$ with respect to each feature map: $\frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$. The **importance weight** for feature map $k$ is the global average pooling of these gradients: $$\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_i \sum_j \frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$$ The Grad-CAM heatmap is then: $$L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\left( \sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k \right)$$ The ReLU discards channels with negative influence (which would indicate evidence *against* the class). The heatmap is upsampled to input resolution via bilinear interpolation.

The ReLU is crucial: without it, you get a map of all activations weighted by gradient magnitude, including those that argue against the class. The ReLU makes Grad-CAM class-discriminative — the same network produces different heatmaps for 'cat' vs 'dog' on the same image. **Guided Grad-CAM** combines Grad-CAM with **guided backpropagation** (which ReLU-gates gradient flow at activation sites as well) for fine-grained pixel-level explanations.

**Why does Grad-CAM work?** The last convolutional layer typically has the highest semantic content (it is closest to the class decision) while retaining spatial information (unlike fully-connected layers which pool everything). The weights $\alpha_k^c$ measure how important each feature map is for class $c$: a feature map that fires strongly for edges in the 'car' class will have high gradient when the car class score changes. The weighted combination thus highlights the spatial regions that the most class-relevant feature maps activate on.

**Limitations and variants**: (i) Grad-CAM is coarse — it operates at the resolution of the last conv layer (e.g., 7×7 for ResNet-50 with 224×224 input). (ii) It can be deceived: Fooling a network while maintaining Grad-CAM explanations (adversarial manipulations that change the prediction without changing the heatmap) has been demonstrated. (iii) **Score-CAM** replaces gradients with forward pass activation scores, avoiding issues with saturated gradients. (iv) **EigenGradCAM** uses the first principal component of the gradients. (v) For transformers, **Attention Rollout** and **Generic Attention-model Explainability** are the analogues.

subtopics.title: TCAV: Testing with Concept Activation Vectors
body: **TCAV (Testing with Concept Activation Vectors)** (Kim et al., 2018) takes a fundamentally different approach to XAI: rather than asking 'which input features matter?', it asks '**which high-level human concepts** does the model use?'. For instance: did the model use the concept of 'stripes' to classify zebras? Did it use 'gender' to predict salary? TCAV enables testing hypotheses about model internals using concepts that humans define, not low-level pixel features.

**Algorithm**: (1) Define a **concept** as a set of example images (e.g., images of striped objects). Define a **random** counter-example set (random images). (2) Train a **linear binary classifier** (an SVM) to separate concept examples from random examples in the activation space of layer $l$: $$h_{C,l}: \mathbb{R}^{d_l} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$$ The normal vector to the decision boundary in activation space is the **Concept Activation Vector (CAV)** $v_{C,l} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_l}$. (3) Compute the **TCAV score**: the fraction of inputs in class $k$ for which the directional derivative of the class logit with respect to the concept direction is positive: $$\text{TCAV}_{Q_{C,k,l}} = \frac{|\{x \in X_k : S_{C,k,l}(x) > 0\}|}{|X_k|}$$ where $S_{C,k,l}(x) = \nabla h_{C,l}(f_l(x)) \cdot v_{C,l}$ is the directional derivative.

A TCAV score of 0.8 for concept 'stripes' in layer $l$ for class 'zebra' means that 80% of zebra images have their class score *increase* when moved in the 'stripes' direction in activation space — the model uses stripes to identify zebras. A TCAV score near 0.5 means the concept is uncorrelated with the class decision. TCAV uses **two-sided hypothesis testing** (comparing against random CAV directions) to determine statistical significance.

**Why linear probes?** The CAV must be a *linear* separator in activation space. This is justified by the **linear representation hypothesis**: that meaningful concepts in neural networks tend to be linearly separable in intermediate activation spaces. If this does not hold, the CAV is not a valid proxy for the concept. This hypothesis is central to mechanistic interpretability too and is the reason linear probes are extensively used across the field.

**Applications and limitations**: TCAV has been used to audit medical AI (does a skin cancer classifier use the concept 'ruler' — a photographic artefact correlated with malignancy in training data?), audit credit scoring models for demographic concepts, and understand what features transformers encode at each layer. Limitations: concept sets must be human-curated (expensive), the linear separability assumption may fail, and TCAV scores can be sensitive to the choice of concept examples. **Net Dissect** is a related method that automatically discovers which neurons in a network correspond to human-defined concept categories from a labelled concept dataset.

resources.label: Lipton — The Mythos of Model Interpretability (2018)
Doshi-Velez & Kim — Towards a Rigorous Science of Interpretable ML (2017)

subtopics.title: Christoph Molnar's IML Framework and Evaluation of Explanations
body: **Interpretable Machine Learning** (Molnar, 2020+) systematically classifies explanation methods along several axes: *model-agnostic* (LIME, SHAP, PDP) vs *model-specific* (Grad-CAM, attention weights); *local* (explain one prediction) vs *global* (explain the full model behaviour); *post-hoc* (after training) vs *intrinsic* (interpretable by design, e.g. linear models, shallow decision trees). Navigating these distinctions is required to choose the right method for a use case.

**Partial Dependence Plots (PDPs)** show the marginal effect of one or two features on the predicted outcome, averaging over all other features: $\hat{f}_{x_S}(x_S) = \mathbb{E}_{x_C}[\hat{f}(x_S, x_C)] = \int \hat{f}(x_S, x_C) d\mathbb{P}(x_C)$, estimated by averaging $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \hat{f}(x_S, x_C^{(i)})$ over training data. **ICE (Individual Conditional Expectation) plots** show one line per data point instead of the average, revealing heterogeneity that PDPs hide. **ALEPlots (Accumulated Local Effects)** fix a known problem: PDPs marginalise over the joint distribution of $(x_S, x_C)$, including combinations that never appear in training data, producing misleading plots for correlated features. ALEs instead accumulate local effects within a small window around $x_S$.

**Counterfactual explanations** answer: 'What would need to change in $x$ for the prediction to flip to $y'$?' Formally: find $x'$ minimising $d(x, x')$ subject to $f(x') = y'$ and $x'$ being realistic. The **Wachter et al. (2017)** objective is: $$\min_{x'} \max_{\lambda} \lambda(f(x') - y')^2 + d(x, x')$$ Counterfactuals are actionable (they suggest concrete changes) and privacy-preserving (they do not require model internals). They are the basis of many regulatory 'right to explanation' implementations. Challenge: there may be many valid counterfactuals (the **Rashomon set**), and the choice among them is non-trivial.

**Evaluating explanations** is the deepest open problem in XAI. Proposed criteria: *faithfulness* (does the explanation match the model's actual computations — measurable via 'faithfulness perturbation tests' that ablate top-k features and measure prediction change); *completeness* (does the explanation account for all model behaviour); *stability* (do nearby inputs get similar explanations); *comprehensibility* (can a human understand and use the explanation). Doshi-Velez & Kim (2017) formalise these as application-grounded (human studies), human-grounded (simplified tasks with humans), and functionally-grounded (proxy metrics without humans) evaluations. The honest conclusion: no single metric captures explanation quality, and the field remains without a ground truth.

resources.label: Christoph Molnar — Interpretable Machine Learning (free online book)
Doshi-Velez & Kim — Rigorous Science of Interpretable ML (2017)
Christoph Molnar — Interpretable Machine Learning (full book)

connections.note: Classical XAI and mechanistic interpretability answer different questions about the same models
Grad-CAM requires access to CNN gradients and activations
Most classical XAI methods were developed for vision models
Adversarial examples expose the gap between XAI explanations and actual model behaviour

topics.title: Generative Adversarial Networks
body: GANs frame image synthesis as a minimax game between a generator and discriminator, enabling photorealistic synthesis, style transfer, and conditional generation. The theoretical foundations touch on optimal transport, divergence geometry, and measure theory.

subtopics.title: GAN Theory: Minimax Objective, Nash Equilibria, and Divergence Geometry
body: **The original GAN objective** (Goodfellow et al., 2014) is the minimax game $\min_G \max_D V(G,D) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. The optimal discriminator for fixed $G$ is $D^*(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x) / (p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_G(x))$, where $p_G$ is the generator's distribution. Substituting $D^*$ back into $V$: $V(G, D^*) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} || p_G)$, where JSD is the Jensen-Shannon divergence. The global minimum $V = -\log 4$ is achieved iff $p_G = p_{\text{data}}$ — the generator perfectly matches the data distribution. This shows that at optimality, the GAN training criterion is equivalent to minimising the JSD between real and generated distributions.

**Mode collapse and training instability** arise from pathological equilibria in the minimax game. Mode collapse: the generator finds a single high-quality output that fools the discriminator, then collapses to producing only that output — it has found a **pure strategy Nash equilibrium** in a game where a **mixed strategy Nash equilibrium** (distribution over all real data) is desired. The discriminator eventually learns to reject this single mode, the generator shifts to another mode, and the process cycles. This is not convergence — the iterative gradient updates (gradient descent on both players alternately) can cycle through modes indefinitely. Formally, simultaneous gradient descent on a bilinear game $\min_x \max_y x^T A y$ converges only for symmetric $A$; the GAN Jacobian is generally asymmetric, leading to rotational dynamics that can diverge.

**Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** (Arjovsky et al., 2017) replaces JSD with the **Earth Mover's Distance (EMD)** or Wasserstein-1 distance: $W(p, q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p,q)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\gamma}[\|x - y\|]$, where $\Pi(p,q)$ is the set of all joint distributions with marginals $p$ and $q$. EMD is superior because it provides meaningful gradients even when $p$ and $q$ have disjoint supports — the JSD is $\log 2$ (maximum) whenever the supports don't overlap, giving zero gradients to the generator. By the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality: $W(p_r, p_g) = \sup_{\|f\|_L \leq 1} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_r}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g}[f(x)]$, where the supremum is over all 1-Lipschitz functions $f$. The **critic** (discriminator without final sigmoid) approximates this supremum. Lipschitz constraint is enforced via **weight clipping** (original WGAN) or **gradient penalty** (WGAN-GP): $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{\hat{x}}[(\|\nabla_{\hat{x}} D(\hat{x})\|_2 - 1)^2]$ evaluated on interpolates $\hat{x} = \epsilon x + (1-\epsilon)\tilde{x}$.

**Spectral normalisation** (Miyato et al., 2018) enforces the Lipschitz constraint by normalising each weight matrix by its **spectral norm** (largest singular value): $\bar{W} = W / \sigma(W)$ where $\sigma(W) = \max_{\|x\|=1} \|Wx\|$. This ensures $\|\bar{W}\|_2 = 1$ and consequently the discriminator is 1-Lipschitz. Spectral norm can be efficiently approximated via power iteration: maintain a left and right singular vector $(u, v)$, update $v \leftarrow W^T u / \|W^T u\|$, $u \leftarrow W v / \|W v\|$, then $\sigma \approx u^T W v$. Each forward pass updates $(u,v)$ one step — convergence is fast because the top singular vector changes slowly during training. Spectral normalisation dramatically stabilises GAN training without requiring hyperparameter tuning for the gradient penalty coefficient.

**Evaluation metrics** for generative models: **Frechet Inception Distance (FID)** measures the distance between the distribution of Inception-v3 features of real vs generated images: $\text{FID} = \|\mu_r - \mu_g\|^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2})$. This is the Frechet (Wasserstein-2) distance between two multivariate Gaussians fit to the feature distributions. Lower FID = more realistic and diverse. **Precision and Recall** (Kynkaanniemi et al., 2019) decompose quality: precision measures what fraction of generated samples fall within the manifold of real data (quality), recall measures what fraction of the real data manifold is covered by generated samples (diversity). A GAN in mode collapse has high precision, zero recall. **Kernel Inception Distance (KID)** is an unbiased alternative to FID using the MMD (Maximum Mean Discrepancy) with an Inception kernel.

resources.label: Original GAN paper - Goodfellow et al. (2014)

subtopics.title: Progressive Growing, StyleGAN, and High-Resolution Synthesis
body: **Progressive GAN (ProGAN)** (Karras et al., 2018) solves the challenge of high-resolution GAN training by starting with tiny images ($4 \times 4$) and progressively adding new layers to both generator and discriminator as training stabilises. At each resolution doubling, the new layers are faded in with a $\alpha \in [0,1]$ blend factor: $\text{output} = (1-\alpha) \cdot \text{upsample}(\text{old}) + \alpha \cdot \text{new\_block}(\text{old})$. This prevents the generator from being overwhelmed by high-frequency detail before learning the coarse structure. ProGAN achieved the first convincing $1024 \times 1024$ face synthesis and introduced **minibatch standard deviation** (measure of batch diversity, appended as a feature map to the discriminator) and **equilised learning rate** (initialise weights from $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, scale at runtime by $\sqrt{2 / n_{\text{inputs}}}$, so all layers train at the same effective learning rate regardless of weight magnitude).

**StyleGAN** (Karras et al., 2019) decouples the latent code from the generator architecture by introducing a **mapping network** $f: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{W}$. The input latent $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ is mapped by an 8-layer MLP to an intermediate latent $w \in \mathcal{W}$. This $w$ is then injected at each synthesis block via **Adaptive Instance Normalisation (AdaIN)**: $\text{AdaIN}(x_i, y) = y_{s,i} \frac{x_i - \mu(x_i)}{\sigma(x_i)} + y_{b,i}$ where $y_{s,i}$ (style scale) and $y_{b,i}$ (style bias) are learned linear projections of $w$. The synthesis network starts from a learned constant $4 \times 4$ spatial input (not $z$ directly). Stochastic details (hair texture, pore placement) are controlled by per-layer noise inputs $B \cdot \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ added before AdaIN. This separation enables **style mixing**: use $w_1$ for coarse layers (pose, identity) and $w_2$ for fine layers (skin texture) — producing coherent interpolations.

**StyleGAN2** (Karras et al., 2020) fixes a characteristic droplet artefact in StyleGAN1 caused by AdaIN normalising away the instance mean, which the network compensated by concentrating signal in one blob per feature map. The fix: **weight demodulation** replaces AdaIN. Given a style $s_i$ and convolutional weight $w_{ijk}$ (input channel $i$, output channel $j$, spatial $k$): scale weights $w'_{ijk} = s_i \cdot w_{ijk}$, then demodulate by normalising output: $w''_{ijk} = w'_{ijk} / \sqrt{\sum_{i,k} w'^2_{ijk} + \epsilon}$. This is equivalent to normalising to unit expected standard deviation, but operates on weights rather than activations — no mean subtraction, eliminating the droplet pathology. StyleGAN2 also introduces **lazy regularisation** (compute path-length regularisation $\mathcal{R}_1$ every 16 mini-batches instead of every batch) and removes progressive growing in favour of **skip connections** and **residual discriminator**.

**Conditional GANs and class conditioning**: **ACGAN** (Odena et al., 2017) adds an auxiliary classifier to the discriminator: $D$ outputs both $P(\text{real})$ and $P(\text{class}|x)$; the classification loss is added to both $D$ and $G$ objectives. **Projection discriminator** (Miyato & Koyama, 2018) is more effective: instead of adding a classification head, project the class embedding $v_y \in \mathbb{R}^d$ onto the discriminator's penultimate feature $h$: $D(x,y) = V(h(x)) + v_y^T h(x)$. This directly measures whether the feature $h(x)$ is consistent with class $y$ via inner product, rather than indirectly via an auxiliary classifier. **BigGAN** (Brock et al., 2018) scales this to ImageNet $512 \times 512$ synthesis using large batch sizes (2048), class-conditional BatchNorm, self-attention at $64 \times 64$ resolution, and truncation trick: sampling from $z sim mathcal{N}(0,I)$ but thresholding $|z| > \psi$ (higher $\psi$ = more diversity, lower quality; lower $\psi$ = higher quality, less diversity).

**StyleGAN-XL and modern synthesis**: **StyleGAN-XL** (Sauer et al., 2022) adapts StyleGAN3 for large-scale class-conditional synthesis using pre-trained feature discriminators (EfficientNet) and projected discriminator. **GigaGAN** (Kang et al., 2023) scales a GAN to 1B parameters matching diffusion model quality at $10\times$ faster inference, using attention in the generator and multi-scale discriminators. The key insight: GANs are not dead — they are faster at inference than diffusion models (single forward pass vs 20-1000 NFEs) and still competitive when carefully scaled. **StyleGAN3** (Karras et al., 2021) focuses on **alias-free synthesis** — standard convolutional networks have spatial aliasing (generated textures are attached to pixel coordinates, not to the generated object's surface). StyleGAN3 uses continuous signal processing: bandlimited upsampling, Fourier features for positional encoding, and careful aliasing suppression to ensure textures 'stick' to the object under transformations.

subtopics.title: Diffusion vs GAN: CycleGAN, Pix2Pix, and Image-to-Image Translation
body: **Pix2Pix** (Isola et al., 2017) is the canonical **paired image-to-image translation** framework: given paired training examples $(x, y)$ (e.g., edge map $x$ + real photo $y$), train a generator $G: X \to Y$ conditioned on $x$. The objective combines adversarial loss with L1 reconstruction: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{L1}}(G)$ where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{L1}} = \mathbb{E}[\|y - G(x)\|_1]$. Pure adversarial loss alone produces sharp but unfaithful outputs; pure L1 produces faithful but blurry outputs. The L1 loss penalises per-pixel deviation, which in ambiguous regions causes the model to predict the mean (blurry). The GAN loss penalises outputs that are not in the distribution of sharp real images, sharpening these regions. The discriminator is a **PatchGAN** — it classifies $70 \times 70$ overlapping patches as real/fake (rather than the whole image), so the discriminator is fully convolutional and can be applied to arbitrary-resolution images.

**CycleGAN** (Zhu et al., 2017) enables **unpaired** image translation: learn the mapping $G: X \to Y$ and $F: Y \to X$ from unpaired collections of images $\{x_i\}$ and $\{y_j\}$. Without paired supervision, the adversarial loss alone is insufficient — $G$ could map all $x$ to the same $y$ that fools $D_Y$. The **cycle consistency loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} = \mathbb{E}[\|F(G(x)) - x\|_1] + \mathbb{E}[\|G(F(y)) - y\|_1]$ forces the round-trip mapping to be approximately the identity, preventing mode collapse. Intuitively: if $G$ translates a horse to a zebra, $F$ should be able to reconstruct the original horse from the zebra. This provides self-supervised signal when paired data doesn't exist. Applications: photo $\leftrightarrow$ painting, summer $\leftrightarrow$ winter, horse $\leftrightarrow$ zebra — all with unpaired data.

**Pix2PixHD** (Wang et al., 2018) extends Pix2Pix to high-resolution ($2048 \times 1024$) synthesis using a coarse-to-fine generator (global generator + local enhancer) and multi-scale PatchGAN discriminators at $3$ resolutions. The **feature matching loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{FM}} = \sum_{i=1}^T \frac{1}{N_i} [\|D^{(k)}_i(x,c) - D^{(k)}_i(G(c),c)\|_1]$ forces the generator to produce activations at each discriminator layer that match the real image's activations — a perceptual consistency loss extracted from the discriminator's internals rather than a fixed pre-trained network. **SPADE** (Park et al., 2019) replaces standard normalisation with spatially adaptive denormalisation: the semantic segmentation map (not just the class label) modulates BatchNorm statistics at each spatial location, enabling precise spatial control of synthesised content.

**GANs vs Diffusion for image synthesis**: diffusion models (DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion, Imagen) have largely replaced GANs for text-conditional image generation because: (1) diffusion models are easier to train (no adversarial instability); (2) they scale better with data and compute; (3) they support text conditioning via CLIP/T5 more naturally. However, GANs maintain advantages in: (a) **inference speed** (single forward pass vs 20-50 diffusion steps); (b) **video synthesis** (temporal consistency is easier to enforce in GANs without per-frame diffusion); (c) **discriminator-as-feature-extractor** (a well-trained discriminator learns powerful perceptual features, useful for transfer learning). Hybrid approaches like **DiffusionGAN** (Xiao et al., 2022) use a GAN with a denoising diffusion process as the generator, getting diffusion's training stability with GAN's inference speed.

connections.note: Both are deep generative models; diffusion has surpassed GANs for text-conditional synthesis but GANs win on speed
GANs power image-to-image translation (pix2pix, CycleGAN) and style transfer in computer vision
GAN discriminators learn perceptual features used in mechanistic analysis of visual representations

resources.label: GAN Zoo - all major GAN variants

topics.title: 3D Computer Vision
body: 3D computer vision reconstructs and synthesises 3D scenes from 2D observations, underpinned by projective geometry, volume rendering, and differentiable rasterisation. NeRF and Gaussian Splatting have redefined novel view synthesis; PointNet and modern architectures handle unstructured 3D data.

subtopics.title: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF): Volume Rendering and Coordinate Networks
body: **NeRF** (Mildenhall et al., 2020) represents a scene as a continuous 5D function $f_\theta(x, y, z, \theta, \phi) \to (\sigma, \mathbf{c})$ where $(x,y,z)$ is 3D position, $(\theta,\phi)$ is viewing direction, $\sigma$ is volume density (opacity), and $\mathbf{c} = (r,g,b)$ is colour. The function is parameterised by an MLP (typically 8 fully-connected layers with ReLU). To render a pixel: cast a ray $\mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{o} + t\mathbf{d}$ from camera origin $\mathbf{o}$ in direction $\mathbf{d}$; sample $N$ points along the ray; query $f_\theta$ at each; accumulate via the **volume rendering integral**: $C(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{t_n}^{t_f} T(t)\sigma(\mathbf{r}(t))\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{r}(t),\mathbf{d})dt$ where $T(t) = \exp(-\int_{t_n}^t \sigma(\mathbf{r}(s))ds)$ is the transmittance (probability of not hitting anything before $t$).

**Discrete approximation**: partition ray $[t_n, t_f]$ into $N$ segments $[t_i, t_{i+1}]$ with $\delta_i = t_{i+1} - t_i$. Sample $t_i' \sim \text{Uniform}[t_i, t_{i+1}]$ (stratified sampling for continuous coverage). Alpha compositing gives: $\hat{C} = \sum_{i=1}^N T_i (1 - e^{-\sigma_i \delta_i}) \mathbf{c}_i$ with $T_i = \exp(-\sum_{j<i} \sigma_j \delta_j)$. Training: minimise photometric reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{\mathbf{r}} \|\hat{C}(\mathbf{r}) - C(\mathbf{r})\|_2^2$ over all rays from all training views. The gradient flows through the volume rendering equation back to the MLP weights. No 3D supervision — only 2D posed images. **Positional encoding** $\gamma(p) = (\sin(2^0 \pi p), \cos(2^0 \pi p), ..., \sin(2^{L-1} \pi p), \cos(2^{L-1} \pi p))$ applied to input coordinates enables the MLP to represent high-frequency details (without it, MLPs are biased toward low-frequency functions — the **spectral bias**).

**Hierarchical sampling** is NeRF's computational efficiency trick. First **coarse network**: sample $N_c = 64$ stratified points per ray, query MLP, get weight $w_i = T_i(1-e^{-\sigma_i \delta_i})$ (the contribution of each point to the rendered colour). Second **fine network**: resample $N_f = 128$ additional points from the distribution defined by $w_i$ (more samples where $w_i$ is large — near surfaces). Query the fine network at all $N_c + N_f$ points. Render with the fine network output. The coarse network is trained with a photometric loss too; the fine network receives the coarse sampling distribution as a free density estimate. This concentrates computation where surfaces are — the decisive advantage over uniform sampling.

**NeRF limitations and instant acceleration**: training one scene takes $1$-$2$ days on a single V100. Rendering one $800 \times 800$ image requires $\sim 1500 \times 800^2 = 10^9$ MLP queries. **Instant-NGP** (Müller et al., 2022) replaces the MLP with a **multi-resolution hash encoding**: at each level $l \in [0, L)$, map $(x,y,z)$ to a grid of resolution $N_l = N_{\text{min}} \cdot b^l$ (exponential spacing); hash the grid coordinates to indices in a fixed-size lookup table of $T$ entries; bilinearly interpolate the looked-up feature vectors. Concatenate features from all levels + spherical harmonics for view direction; pass through a tiny 2-layer MLP. Hash collisions are implicitly resolved by the MLP learning to distinguish. Result: **training in 1-5 seconds** on a single GPU. The hash table parameters are differentiable and learned by gradient descent jointly with the MLP.

**Generalisation and dynamic NeRF**: vanilla NeRF overfits to one scene — it is a per-scene optimisation, not a learned prior. **PixelNeRF** (Yu et al., 2021) conditions the radiance field on image features extracted by a CNN encoder, enabling novel view synthesis from 1-3 input views (few-shot). **HyperNeRF** (Park et al., 2021) handles non-rigid dynamic scenes by learning a 'hyper-space' — a 2D ambient dimension appended to the 3D position, where each moment in time corresponds to a different slice of the 5D representation. **D-NeRF** (Pumarola et al., 2021) uses a deformation field $\Delta x = D_\phi(x, t)$ that maps each 3D point at time $t$ to its canonical position, then evaluates a static NeRF at the canonical position. **NeRF in the Wild** (Martin-Brualla et al., 2021) handles in-the-wild internet photos with varying illumination via per-image appearance embeddings.

resources.label: NeRF original paper - Mildenhall et al. (2020)

subtopics.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting: Rasterisation-Based Scene Representation
body: **3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)** (Kerbl et al., 2023) represents a scene as a set of $N$ 3D Gaussians $\{(\mu_k, \Sigma_k, \alpha_k, \mathbf{c}_k)\}$ where $\mu_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the Gaussian centre, $\Sigma_k \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ is the 3D covariance matrix, $\alpha_k \in [0,1]$ is opacity, and $\mathbf{c}_k$ is colour (represented as spherical harmonic coefficients to model view-dependent appearance). To ensure $\Sigma_k$ is positive semi-definite, parameterise it as $\Sigma_k = R_k S_k S_k^T R_k^T$ where $R_k$ is a rotation (quaternion) and $S_k$ is a diagonal scaling matrix — the **3D ellipsoid** decomposition. This decomposition is differentiable and always produces valid covariances.

**Differentiable rasterisation (splatting)**: project each 3D Gaussian onto the 2D image plane. Given camera transform $W$ and Jacobian of the projective transformation $J$: the 2D covariance is $\Sigma^{2D}_k = J W \Sigma_k W^T J^T$ (standard error propagation). The projected Gaussian is a 2D Gaussian $G_k^{2D}(u) = e^{-\frac{1}{2}(u-\mu_k^{2D})^T (\Sigma_k^{2D})^{-1} (u-\mu_k^{2D})}$. **Alpha compositing** (front-to-back, depth sorted): pixel colour $C = \sum_{i \in N} c_i \alpha_i' \prod_{j<i}(1-\alpha_j')$ where $\alpha_i' = \alpha_i \cdot G_i^{2D}(u)$ is the opacity at the pixel location. This is the same volume rendering equation as NeRF but implemented via rasterisation rather than ray marching — **orders of magnitude faster**.

**Adaptive density control**: during optimisation, Gaussians are adaptively split, cloned, and pruned. **Densification**: regions of the scene with high gradient (under-reconstruction) have new Gaussians added. If a Gaussian is **too small** (understudied region), clone it and perturb. If a Gaussian is **too large** (covers multiple surfaces), split it into two smaller Gaussians. **Pruning**: Gaussians with $\alpha < \epsilon$ or that are too large in 3D/2D are periodically removed. The scene representation grows adaptively from a sparse initial point cloud (from COLMAP or Structure from Motion). Typical scenes use $1$-$6$ million Gaussians for an indoor scene, $10$-$30$ million for outdoor.

**Training and rendering performance**: 3DGS trains in $30$ minutes on a single GPU (vs $1$-$2$ hours for NeRF with Instant-NGP). Rendering is $\sim 100$ FPS at $1080p$ on a single GPU (vs $< 1$ FPS for NeRF). The CUDA rasteriser uses **tile-based rendering**: divide the image into $16 \times 16$ pixel tiles; sort Gaussians by depth; assign each Gaussian to all tiles it overlaps; each tile's thread block alpha-composites Gaussians in depth order. The tile structure enables efficient parallelism. Quality is roughly comparable to NeRF (PSNR $\sim 27$-$32$ dB on NeRF synthetic benchmark), with 3DGS typically slightly better at fine details and much faster.

**Downstream applications**: **4D Gaussian Splatting** (Wu et al., 2024) extends 3DGS to dynamic scenes by adding a deformation network that predicts position/rotation/scale changes per Gaussian per timestamp. **Gaussian Avatar** (Qian et al., 2024) represents human bodies as Gaussians driven by SMPL pose parameters. **GaussianSplatting for SLAM** uses 3DGS as a dense map representation for simultaneous localisation and mapping, with Gaussians updated online. **Dreamgaussian** (Tang et al., 2023) uses 3DGS as the 3D representation for text-to-3D generation — generates a 3D Gaussian representation conditioned on a text prompt via score distillation from a 2D diffusion model. The explicit Gaussian representation (vs NeRF's implicit MLP) enables efficient mesh extraction for downstream use in 3D engines.

resources.label: Gaussian Splatting SLAM - Matsuki et al. (2024)

subtopics.title: PointNet, PointNet++, and Sparse 3D Architectures
body: **Point cloud representation**: a point cloud $P = \{p_i\}_{i=1}^N$ with $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (or $\mathbb{R}^6$ with normals) is the raw output of LiDAR sensors and depth cameras. Unlike voxel grids ($O(d^3)$ memory) or meshes (irregular topology), point clouds are sparse and unordered. Two core challenges: (1) **permutation invariance** — the output must be the same regardless of how points are ordered; (2) **neighbourhood reasoning** — local geometric structure must be aggregated hierarchically. Standard CNNs fail: they require a grid (not available for point clouds) and would need to process all $N!$ orderings for permutation invariance.

**PointNet** (Qi et al., 2017) achieves permutation invariance via a symmetric function: $f(P) = g(h(p_1), h(p_2), ..., h(p_N))$ where $h: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^K$ is a shared MLP (same weights for all points) and $g$ is a symmetric aggregation (max pooling). Global feature: $\mathbf{g} = \max_i(h(p_i)) \in \mathbb{R}^K$. For segmentation, concatenate $\mathbf{g}$ with each point's local feature $h(p_i)$ and apply another MLP per point. **T-Net**: a mini-PointNet that predicts a $3 \times 3$ (or $64 \times 64$) alignment matrix to canonicalise the point cloud before processing — a spatial transformer for 3D data. The key theoretical result: any continuous symmetric function can be approximated by $\max_i h(p_i)$ for appropriate $h$ — PointNet is a universal approximator for permutation-invariant functions over point sets.

**PointNet++** (Qi et al., 2017) adds local geometric reasoning via hierarchical set abstraction. Each level: (1) **Sample** $N'$ centroid points using **Farthest Point Sampling (FPS)** — iteratively select the point farthest from already-selected points, giving uniform coverage; (2) **Group** points within radius $r$ of each centroid; (3) **Encode** each group with a PointNet MLP, producing one feature per centroid. Repeat 2-3 times with increasing $r$ and decreasing $N'$ to build a hierarchical feature pyramid. For segmentation, propagate features back (upsampling) using inverse distance-weighted interpolation: $f(p_q) = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{w_i f(p_i)}{\sum w_i}$ with $w_i = 1/d(p_q, p_i)^2$. **Multi-scale grouping**: apply multiple grouping radii at each level and concatenate features — robust to non-uniform point density (close-up objects have many points; distant objects have few).

**Sparse 3D convolutions**: for volumetric data (voxels), standard dense convolutions waste computation on empty voxels. **Sparse convolution** (Graham et al., 2018) stores only occupied voxels and applies convolution only where input is non-empty. The **rule book** precomputes, for each output location, which input voxels contribute — a sparse matrix of (input index, output index, weight index) triples. The GEMM (generalised matrix multiply) step multiplies input features by the appropriate weight matrices. **MinkowskiEngine** and **spconv** implement sparse convolutions efficiently. **VoxelNet** (Zhou et al., 2018) and **PointPillars** (Lang et al., 2019) convert LiDAR point clouds to voxels/pillars, process with sparse CNNs, and output 3D bounding box predictions — the standard architecture for autonomous driving perception.

**3D object detection and scene understanding**: the standard task for autonomous driving. **CenterPoint** (Yin et al., 2021) detects 3D objects by predicting a heatmap of object centres (not anchors), then regressing offsets, dimensions, and yaw angle. Eliminates the anchor box hyperparameter issue. **BEV (Bird's Eye View) representation**: project LiDAR points to a 2D top-down grid, process with a 2D CNN — computationally efficient and naturally handles occlusion. **LSS (Lift, Splat, Shoot)** (Philion & Fidler, 2020) lifts 2D camera features to 3D via predicted depth distributions, then splats (projects) into the BEV grid — enabling camera-only 3D perception without LiDAR, used in Tesla's and Waymo's camera BEV stacks. **Occupancy networks** predict whether each 3D voxel is occupied (a continuous $f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to [0,1]$ function), enabling arbitrary-resolution mesh extraction via marching cubes.

subtopics.title: DreamFusion, Score Distillation, and Text-to-3D Generation
body: **DreamFusion** (Poole et al., 2022) generates 3D assets from text prompts by distilling a pre-trained 2D diffusion model into a NeRF without any 3D training data. The key insight: if we have a 2D diffusion model $p_\phi(x)$ that assigns high probability to images matching the text prompt, we can optimise a 3D representation (NeRF) such that **all rendered views** have high probability under $p_\phi$. The **Score Distillation Sampling (SDS)** gradient: $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{SDS}} = \mathbb{E}_{t,\epsilon,c}[w(t)(\hat{\epsilon}_\phi(x_t; y, t) - \epsilon) \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta}]$ where $x = g(\theta, c)$ is the rendered image (differentiable NeRF rendering from camera $c$), $x_t = \alpha_t x + \sigma_t \epsilon$ is a noised version, $\hat{\epsilon}_\phi$ is the diffusion model's noise prediction (denoiser), and $\epsilon$ is the added noise. The gradient moves the NeRF's rendered views toward higher-probability regions of the diffusion model's distribution.

**SDS intuition and pathologies**: $\hat{\epsilon}_\phi(x_t; y, t) - \epsilon$ is the difference between what the diffusion model thinks the noise was and what the noise actually was. When this is positive for a direction in image space, it means the diffusion model thinks the rendered image should shift in that direction to better match the text prompt.
