This is a **certified radius** — within the $L_2$ ball of radius $R$, the smoothed classifier is guaranteed to predict $c_A$. Estimation: Monte Carlo estimation of $p_A$ using $n = 100k$ Gaussian samples per test point. Trade-off: larger $\sigma$ = larger certified radius but less accurate base predictions.

**Data poisoning attacks**: instead of perturbing test inputs, the adversary manipulates training data. **Backdoor attacks** (Trojan attacks): inject a small fraction of poisoned examples with a trigger (specific pixel pattern or patch) associated with a target class. The model behaves normally on clean inputs but predicts the target class whenever the trigger is present. **Clean-label backdoor attacks** (Turner et al., 2019): the poisoned examples have correct labels but are adversarially perturbed to be close to the trigger pattern — the model learns to associate the trigger with the poison class without the poisoner needing to modify labels (making the attack undetectable by label inspection). **Supply-chain attacks**: poisoning publicly available datasets (ImageNet, LAION) used for pre-training, affecting all downstream fine-tuned models.

resources.label: Certified Robustness via Randomised Smoothing - Cohen et al. (2019)

subtopics.title: LLM Adversarial Attacks: GCG, AutoDAN, and Multimodal Jailbreaks
body: **Transferring adversarial attacks to LLMs**: the discrete token space of LLMs makes gradient-based attacks harder than for continuous image inputs. FGSM/PGD require continuous inputs; tokens are discrete, so the gradient $\nabla_{x_{\text{token}}} \mathcal{L}$ is with respect to the continuous embedding $e_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$, not the token ID. The gradient in embedding space cannot be directly mapped to a token substitution. **Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG)** (Zou et al., 2023) addresses this: for each token position $i$ in the adversarial suffix, use the gradient of the loss with respect to the one-hot token representation to identify the top-$k$ candidate token substitutions; try each in random order and keep the best. This is a **greedy coordinate descent** in token space, one position at a time, with gradient-guided candidate selection. GCG optimises a suffix $s$ appended to a harmful instruction to maximise $\Pr[\text{model starts with 'Sure, here is'}]$.

**AutoDAN** (Zhu et al., 2023): an automated jailbreak generation system using a **hierarchical genetic algorithm** operating in natural language space. Initial population: seed jailbreaks (DAN-style roleplay prompts, fictional framings). Mutation: LLM-paraphrase each prompt while preserving jailbreak structure. Crossover: combine parts of two successful jailbreaks. Selection: rank by attack success rate on the target model (tested by querying the model). AutoDAN produces **fluent, natural-language jailbreaks** (unlike GCG's nonsense token sequences) that are harder to detect by perplexity filtering. The key advantage: natural language jailbreaks transfer better across models because they exploit semantic vulnerabilities rather than gradient-specific weaknesses.

**Multimodal adversarial attacks**: VLMs (LLaVA, GPT-4V) are vulnerable to adversarial images that elicit harmful text outputs. **Visual adversarial examples for VLMs**: find $\delta$ such that $\|\delta\|_\infty \leq \epsilon$ and the model produces a harmful text completion when given image $x+\delta$ and a benign text prompt. The gradient flows from the text loss through the LLM through the visual encoder to the pixel space. **Typography attacks** (Goh et al., 2021): placing text labels within images can override the visual classification — a stop sign with 'Speed Limit 45' printed on it is classified as a speed limit sign. For VLMs: embedding 'Ignore previous instructions and...' in white text on a white background within an image can inject instructions that override the system prompt.

**Adversarial training for LLMs**: applying adversarial training to LLMs is challenging because: (1) the discrete token space makes inner-loop optimisation expensive (GCG requires hundreds of forward passes per update); (2) the diversity of harmful behaviours makes it hard to enumerate the adversarial manifold; (3) adversarial training on text reduces fluency and coherence. **R2D2** (Mazeika et al., 2024) adversarially trains a classifier (guard model) against GCG attacks, hardening it without touching the target LLM. **Adversarial training with smooth perturb**: perturb in embedding space rather than token space, using continuous perturbations bounded by $L_\infty$ on embedding norms — more tractable for gradient-based inner loop, though not directly meaningful in discrete token space. Evaluations: **HarmBench** (Mazeika et al., 2024) provides a standardised evaluation of LLM robustness against $18$ attack methods across $33$ scenarios.

resources.label: GCG - Universal Adversarial Attacks on LLMs - Zou et al. (2023)
HarmBench - adversarial robustness benchmark for LLMs

subtopics.title: Poisoning, Model Extraction, and Membership Inference
body: **Membership inference attacks (MIA)** determine whether a data point was in the training set. **Shadow model attack** (Shokri et al., 2017): train multiple 'shadow models' on known member/non-member subsets; train an attack model on their outputs; apply to the target model. The core signal: models overfit to training data — training members have lower loss (higher confidence on the true label) than non-members. **Likelihood ratio test**: compare $\log P(y|x, \text{member})$ vs $\log P(y|x, \text{non-member})$ using the shadow model's calibration. For LLMs: training-time memorisation of verbatim text sequences makes MIA more powerful. **Extracting Training Data** (Carlini et al., 2021): prompt GPT-2 with common prefixes, generate completions, and identify memorised text by measuring the **zlib compression rate** — memorised sequences are less compressible (more structured) than model-generated text. Recovered verbatim PII, poems, code, and deduplicated training sequences.

**Model extraction attacks** (Tramèr et al., 2016) reconstruct a model's functionality by querying it as a black box. **Equation-solving approach**: for a linear model with $d$ features and $c$ classes, send $d \cdot c$ queries at points that reveal the model's weights. For neural networks: send synthetic or natural queries, collect (input, output) pairs, and train a local substitute model on them. The substitute model can be used to craft adversarial examples that transfer to the target model. **Adaptive attacks**: if the target model adds noise to outputs (differential privacy) or uses random dropout at inference (MC Dropout), the attacker can average multiple queries per input to recover the true output. Model extraction is a concern for proprietary APIs: an attacker can replicate a model's behaviour at a fraction of the training cost.

**Differential privacy as a defence**: DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) adds calibrated noise to gradients during training, providing $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP guarantees that bound the information leakable via MIA. The **privacy amplification by sampling** lemma: if DP-SGD processes a random mini-batch of size $B$ from dataset $N$, the effective $\epsilon$ is amplified by $B/N$ — a $256/10000 = 2.56%$ batch fraction amplifies an $\epsilon=2$ guarantee to $\epsilon \approx 0.05$. This means tight DP budgets are achievable with small batch ratios. DP-trained models resist MIA by construction: the membership inference advantage is bounded by $e^\epsilon - 1$ (when $\delta$ is negligible). Cost: DP training requires gradient clipping (reducing the signal of large-gradient examples) and Gaussian noise addition, significantly degrading model quality at tight $\epsilon < 1$ budgets.

resources.label: Extracting Training Data from LLMs - Carlini et al. (2021)
Model Stealing Attacks - Tramèr et al. (2016)

connections.note: Jailbreaks and adversarial attacks are the threat model that AI safety defences must guard against
Adversarial training (PGD, TRADES) is an expensive fine-tuning technique requiring inner-loop attack optimisation
Understanding which features cause misclassification requires mechanistic interpretability of decision boundaries

title: Embedded Programming
tagline: From blinking an LED to bare-metal RTOS kernels and safety-critical firmware.
overview: Embedded is systems programming with the safety net removed: no OS (or one you wrote), kilobytes of RAM, hard real-time deadlines, and code that controls physical hardware where a bug can be a fire. It forces a discipline — deterministic timing, static allocation, defensive programming — that makes you a better systems engineer everywhere.

The arc: peripherals and protocols → bare-metal ARM and interrupts → RTOS (use one, then write one) → low-power, safety-critical, and hardware design. It shares the C memory model with low-level systems and the scheduler/driver concepts with OS.

topics.title: MCU anatomy, GPIO & protocols
body: Microcontroller anatomy (CPU core, flash, SRAM, peripherals, clock tree), **GPIO** (pull-up/down, why floating pins are dangerous), the serial bus trio — **UART** (framing, baud), **I2C** (clock stretching, addressing), **SPI** (CPOL/CPHA, full-duplex) — plus PWM and ADC (Nyquist, oversampling).
Embedded C essentials: `volatile` (don't let the optimizer drop hardware reads), bitfields, and bit manipulation with masks instead of `if` for registers.

subtopics.title: MCU anatomy - clock tree, bus fabric, and peripheral hierarchy
body: A microcontroller is a system-on-chip: CPU core, non-volatile flash, SRAM, and a set of peripheral modules all connected by an internal bus fabric and driven by a shared clock tree. Understanding this architecture is the prerequisite for reading datasheets fluently and configuring any peripheral correctly without a HAL.

The **clock tree** is the spine. On STM32F4 as a representative: a high-speed external oscillator (HSE, e.g. 8 MHz crystal) or internal RC (HSI, 16 MHz, less accurate) feeds a **PLL** (Phase-Locked Loop) that multiplies the frequency up to the maximum CPU speed (up to 168 MHz on F4). The PLL output becomes **SYSCLK**. From SYSCLK, **prescalers** divide down to produce **HCLK** (for the AHB bus and CPU), and further to produce **PCLK1** (APB1, ≤42 MHz, for UART2/3, SPI2/3, I2C, timers) and **PCLK2** (APB2, ≤84 MHz, for ADC, SPI1, USART1/6, advanced timers). Get clock config wrong and a peripheral may appear entirely silent or run at the wrong baud.

The **bus fabric** on ARM Cortex-M follows the AMBA standard: an **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** for high-bandwidth masters (CPU, DMA, flash) and one or more **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** bridges for lower-speed peripherals. DMA controllers are AHB masters, meaning they can move data between SRAM and peripherals without involving the CPU at all. The bridge adds a synchronization cost, which is why APB2 timer frequencies follow a 2x rule when PCLK2 is not equal to HCLK.

**Flash** on Cortex-M is memory-mapped — it lives at address `0x08000000` on STM32 — so the CPU fetches instructions from it identically to SRAM. But flash is slow (relative to the CPU). At 168 MHz, the CPU would stall waiting for flash. The solution is the **ART Accelerator** (Adaptive Real-Time) on STM32F4: an 8-line × 128-bit prefetch cache plus a branch buffer. You configure **flash wait states** (`FLASH_ACR.LATENCY`) to match voltage and clock; too few and you get random crashes, too many and you lose throughput. At 3.3V/168 MHz, the correct value is 5 wait states.

**SRAM** is fast (single-cycle on Cortex-M3/M4) but volatile. On F4 series there are three SRAM banks: SRAM1 (112KB), SRAM2 (16KB), and **CCM (Core Coupled Memory)** (64KB). CCM is wired directly to the Cortex-M4 D-code bus, bypassing AHB, giving true zero-latency single-cycle access for the CPU — but DMA cannot reach it, a common trap. Placing your RTOS TCBs and stacks in CCM gives measurable determinism improvements for the scheduler.

The **RCC (Reset and Clock Control)** peripheral controls which peripheral clocks are enabled. By default on reset, nearly all peripherals are clock-gated (off) to save power. Before you write a single bit to GPIO, UART, ADC, or any other peripheral, you must enable its RCC clock bit. The canonical bug for every embedded beginner: configure a GPIO, observe nothing happening, finally realize the RCC enable was missing. Datasheet peripheral chapters always begin with 'enable the clock in RCC_AHB1ENR/RCC_APB1ENR/RCC_APB2ENR' for exactly this reason.

resources.label: STM32F4 Reference Manual (RM0090) - clock/RCC chapter
resources.note: primary reference for all STM32F4 programming

subtopics.title: GPIO - modes, pull resistors, open-drain, and register-level control
body: GPIO (General-Purpose Input/Output) pins are the most fundamental hardware interface. Before any communication protocol, sensor reading, or LED toggle can happen, you must configure the pin's mode, speed, pull, and alternate function — all through memory-mapped registers. Misconfigurations here are silent and frustrating, so understanding the hardware precisely pays off immediately.

Each GPIO pin can be in one of four modes: **Input**, **Output**, **Alternate Function** (AF), or **Analog**. Input mode connects the Schmitt-trigger input buffer to the data register; read `GPIOx_IDR` to see the pin state. Output mode lets you drive `GPIOx_ODR` or use `GPIOx_BSRR` (bit-set/reset register, the preferred approach because it is atomic — no read-modify-write). Alternate Function routes the pin to an on-chip peripheral (UART, SPI, I2C, timer) according to an AF mapping table in the datasheet. Analog mode disables the input buffer entirely, reducing current consumption and noise injection into the ADC.

**Pull resistors** matter most in input mode. An unconnected input pin floats — it picks up electromagnetic noise and can read either 0 or 1 unpredictably, toggling rapidly and causing spurious interrupts. Always configure a pull-up or pull-down to a known default. STM32 internal pulls are typically 30-50 kΩ, which is fine for slow-changing digital signals but too weak for high-capacitance lines; use an external 4.7 kΩ or 10 kΩ in those cases. Buttons to ground → pull-up (pin high when released, low when pressed). Buttons to VCC → pull-down.

**Open-drain** output is an important variant: the pin can only actively pull low (drain current to GND); for a high level, it merely releases the line and relies on an external (or internal) pull-up resistor. Open-drain is essential for I2C (which requires it by spec so multiple devices can share a bus without fighting), for `RESET` lines shared across ICs, and for level-shifting (pulling up to a different voltage rail than the MCU's 3.3V). Push-pull output actively drives both high and low — simpler and faster, used for most GPIO, SPI, and UART TX lines.

**Output speed** (on STM32, Low/Medium/Fast/Very High) controls the slew rate — how fast the output edge rises. A faster slew rate produces sharper edges and higher-frequency harmonics, which radiate EMI. Use the slowest speed that meets your timing requirement. For an LED blinking at 1 Hz, Low is correct; for SPI at 40 MHz, Very High is required. Choosing Very High everywhere is a common beginner mistake that causes EMC failures on FCC/CE testing.

Register-level manipulation uses bit masks and shift constants rather than `if` branches. The idiom is: `GPIOx->MODER = (GPIOx->MODER & ~(0x3 << (pin * 2))) | (mode << (pin * 2));` — mask out the two bits for this pin, OR in the new value. The BSRR register for atomic output: upper 16 bits reset, lower 16 bits set — write a 1 to bit `pin` in the lower half to set, bit `pin+16` in the upper half to clear. This avoids the read-modify-write race that would occur if two ISRs tried to modify `ODR` simultaneously. Internalize these patterns and all peripheral configuration becomes the same exercise in datasheet lookup and bit manipulation.

subtopics.title: UART - framing, baud rate generation, and error detection
body: UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter) is the simplest serial protocol: two wires (TX and RX), no clock signal, and a prearranged speed (baud rate). It is the first protocol to learn and the one you will rely on for debug logging forever after, so understanding it properly pays dividends.

**Framing** is how the receiver knows where a byte starts and ends without a shared clock. The line is idle-high. To transmit a byte, the transmitter pulls the line low for one bit period — this is the **start bit**, the synchronization edge. Then it sends 5-9 data bits (almost always 8) LSB-first. Optionally, a **parity bit** follows (even/odd parity: the parity bit is chosen so the total number of 1s is even or odd). Finally, one or two **stop bits** (high level) mark the end of the frame and give the receiver time to process before the next start. The receiver, on seeing the falling edge of the start bit, starts its own counter and samples the line at the middle of each expected bit period, which maximizes noise tolerance.

**Baud rate generation** on hardware UART peripherals divides PCLK by a fractional divider (USARTDIV) to produce the desired symbol rate. On STM32 the formula is `USARTDIV = f_CK / (8 × (2 - OVER8) × baud)`. The register has integer and fractional parts to allow accurate generation of standard baud rates (9600, 115200, 921600) from non-ideal clock sources. Mismatched baud rates cause framing errors — the stop bit is sampled at the wrong time and the UART reports `FE (Framing Error)`. An error above ~3-5% produces garbage; below that, some bytes are lost. Always verify with a logic analyzer if commissioning a new board.

**Error flags** in the status register: `FE` (framing error — expected stop bit sampled as 0), `PE` (parity error — parity bit mismatch), `NE` (noise error — multiple samples in a bit period disagreed), `ORE` (overrun error — a new byte arrived before the previous one was read from the data register). ORE is the most common in software: if your receive interrupt is too slow, bytes are dropped. Use DMA for high-speed UART receive to avoid this entirely.

RS-232 and TTL are voltage-level conventions, not protocol differences. TTL UART (what MCUs speak) uses 0V for logic-0 and 3.3V/5V for logic-1. RS-232 (the DB9 connector on old PCs) uses +3V to +15V for logic-0 and -3V to -15V for logic-1 — inverted polarity. Connecting an MCU TX directly to an RS-232 RX will silently misread every byte, and the RS-232 voltage may damage the MCU pin. A level-shifter IC (MAX3232) is required. USB-to-UART adapters (CP2102, CH340) present a TTL UART interface to the MCU and enumerate as a virtual COM port on the host — the standard debug channel for embedded development.

subtopics.title: I2C - arbitration, clock stretching, multi-master addressing
body: I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) is a two-wire, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave protocol invented by Philips. It uses only two signals — **SDA** (data) and **SCL** (clock) — both **open-drain** with pull-up resistors to VCC. The open-drain requirement means any device can pull a line low but none actively drives high; the pull-up does that passively. This is what enables multi-master arbitration and clock stretching without bus contention.

Every transaction begins with a **START condition** — SDA falls while SCL is high (a deliberate protocol violation of the 'data stable while clock high' rule, which is what distinguishes START from data). The master sends a 7-bit (or 10-bit) device address followed by a R/W̄ bit. The addressed slave pulls SDA low during the 9th clock pulse to **ACK**. If no slave ACKs (wrong address, device absent, device locked up), SDA stays high — a NACK, which the master should handle by issuing a STOP and reporting an error. Every byte sent (address or data) must be ACKed before the next byte proceeds.

**Clock stretching** allows a slave to pause the master. If the slave needs time to prepare data, it holds SCL low after the master releases it. The master is obligated to wait until SCL goes high before continuing. Many microcontrollers' hardware I2C controllers support clock stretching transparently. Broken implementations sometimes time out instead of waiting, causing mysterious half-received transactions. Always confirm your I2C peripheral handles stretching if using sensors that require it (many EEPROMs, pressure sensors, etc.).

**Multi-master arbitration** happens when two masters start a transaction simultaneously. Each master monitors SDA while driving it. If it drives SDA high but observes SDA low, another master is driving low and wins — the loser backs off immediately, and the wire AND ensures the winning data is unmodified. In practice, multi-master designs are rare in embedded (most systems have one MCU as the only master), but the open-drain + pull-up design makes it physically safe.

**Pull-up resistor sizing** is a practical concern. Too large (10 kΩ at 400 kHz Fast Mode) and the RC time constant slows edge rise times past the allowed maximum, causing setup/hold violations. Too small (1 kΩ) and devices driving SDA/SCL low must sink more current, increasing power consumption and stressing slave output stages. A common formula: $R_{pull} = \frac{t_{rise}}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$. For 100 kHz Standard Mode, 4.7 kΩ with a few hundred pF of bus capacitance is typical. For 400 kHz Fast Mode with long traces or many devices, 2.2 kΩ or 1 kΩ may be necessary. A logic analyzer showing rounded edges and setup violations diagnoses a pull-up that is too large.

Common I2C bugs in firmware: forgetting to enable the RCC clock for the I2C peripheral before configuring it; configuring GPIO in push-pull instead of open-drain (causes two outputs to fight if both drive simultaneously, potentially damaging pins); not sending a STOP condition after an error (leaves the bus in a locked state requiring power cycle); not handling NACK (silently proceeds, reading garbage). The I2C bus can also get stuck (slave holding SDA low mid-transaction if the master was reset mid-byte). The recovery procedure is to toggle SCL nine times manually to flush any slave shift register, then issue START+STOP.

subtopics.title: SPI - clock polarity/phase, master/slave, and full-duplex operation
body: SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) is a synchronous, full-duplex, single-master, multi-slave protocol. Four signals: **SCLK** (clock, driven by master), **MOSI** (Master Out Slave In), **MISO** (Master In Slave Out), and **NSS/CS** (chip select, one per slave, active-low). Because the clock is explicit (unlike UART), SPI can run much faster — common MCU SPI peripherals run at CPU clock / 2, so 84 MHz on an STM32F4. This makes SPI the protocol of choice for high-bandwidth devices: displays, ADCs/DACs, flash memory, SD cards, RF transceivers.

**CPOL (Clock Polarity)** and **CPHA (Clock Phase)** are the two configuration bits that define the SPI mode. CPOL: 0 means clock idle-low, 1 means clock idle-high. CPHA: 0 means data is captured on the leading edge (first transition), 1 means data is captured on the trailing edge (second transition). The four combinations (Mode 0/1/2/3) are standardized. Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0) is the most common: clock idle-low, data captured on the rising edge, data shifted on the falling edge. Always check the device datasheet's SPI timing diagram — using the wrong mode produces a stream of 0xFF or 0x00 because you are sampling at the wrong time.

SPI is **full-duplex**: every clock cycle, the master shifts one bit out on MOSI and simultaneously shifts one bit in on MISO. A transaction always transfers the same number of bytes in both directions. When you 'read' a register, you transmit a command byte on MOSI and receive the response on MISO during the same clocks. When you 'write', you send command+data and ignore MISO. The SPI hardware manages the shift registers and status flags (TXE: transmit buffer empty; RXNE: receive buffer not empty); you poll or interrupt on these, or use DMA for bulk transfers.

**NSS/CS (chip select)** is not part of the SPI protocol itself — it is an external active-low signal the master uses to select which slave participates. The master asserts NSS low before starting the clock, and deasserts it (high) after the last clock. With multiple slaves, each gets its own CS pin. Forgetting to assert CS is the most common SPI commissioning bug. Some slaves also use CS as a framing signal (data is only valid while CS is low); deactivating CS mid-transaction resets their internal state. Some devices have an active-high CS variant (sometimes called SS̄ vs SS).

**DMA-driven SPI** is essential for bulk data (display framebuffers, SD card sectors). Configure DMA to write the TX buffer and read the RX buffer simultaneously; the DMA controller handles the byte-by-byte MOSI/MISO handshake with the SPI peripheral without CPU involvement. With DMA, throughput is nearly wire-speed (SPI clock rate × word size) and the CPU is free for other work. Without DMA, CPU polling a 42 MHz SPI peripheral wastes millions of cycles per transfer. The pattern: set up DMA TX and RX channels, set both counts, enable both channels, then enable SPI — the transfer starts automatically.

SPI **daisy-chaining** is an advanced topology where multiple slaves are chained MOSI→MISO→MISO→MOSI in series, all sharing one CS. When CS is asserted, a shift pulse propagates through all devices. This is how some LED driver ICs (e.g. MAX7219 chains) and JTAG scan chains work. Normal multi-slave SPI uses a star topology (separate CS per device), which is almost always preferred for simplicity.

subtopics.title: PWM and ADC - timer-based generation, Nyquist, and oversampling
body: **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation)** encodes an analog value as the duty cycle of a digital square wave. A 50 Hz PWM signal with 75% duty cycle is high for 15 ms and low for 5 ms of each 20 ms period. The most important application is motor/servo control, but also LED dimming, DAC approximation (with an RC filter to average the signal), and power conversion (buck/boost converters, digital Class-D amplifiers). On Cortex-M MCUs, PWM is generated by a hardware **timer** with an **output compare** unit — no CPU cycles required once configured.

Timer-based PWM works as follows: the timer counts from 0 to **ARR (Auto-Reload Register)** and then resets (the PWM period is ARR + 1 counts). The **CCR (Capture/Compare Register)** holds the compare value. When the counter reaches CCR, the output pin changes state (set or reset depending on the PWM mode); when the counter resets to 0, the pin returns to its default. PWM frequency = TIMCLK / (PSC+1) / (ARR+1). Duty cycle = CCR / (ARR+1). By writing a new CCR value in real-time, you change the duty cycle without restarting the timer — useful for smooth motor control and audio synthesis.

**ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** converts a continuous voltage into a digital number. On STM32F4, the ADC is 12-bit (0-4095 counts for 0–VREF+, typically 3.3V), with a resolution of 3.3V/4096 ≈ 0.8 mV. The conversion requires a **sample time** (charging an internal capacitor to the input voltage) plus a **conversion time** (successive approximation). Minimum sample time in clock cycles depends on the source impedance: a high-impedance source (many kΩ) requires more cycles to charge the sample capacitor; too few and you get systematic error. Formula: $R_{source} le \frac{T_{sample}}{C_{ADC} \cdot \ln(2^{N+1})} - R_{internal}$.

**Nyquist's theorem** states that to faithfully reconstruct a signal with a maximum frequency $f_{max}$, you must sample at least $2 f_{max}$. If you are sampling an audio signal at 44.1 kHz, you can capture frequencies up to 22.05 kHz. Sample too slowly and **aliasing** occurs: high-frequency content folds back into the baseband, appearing as a lower-frequency artifact. The solution is an **anti-aliasing filter** (low-pass filter with cutoff ≤ fs/2) placed before the ADC input. On a microcontroller, the timer interrupt or DMA trigger determines the sampling rate; keep it at least 2x your highest signal frequency.

**Oversampling and decimation** is a powerful technique for getting more than the hardware ADC's 12-bit resolution. If you sample at 4x the required rate, then average (decimate) 4 samples into 1, you gain 1 extra bit of resolution (6 dB SNR improvement). More precisely, oversampling by a factor of $4^n$ and decimating gives $n$ extra bits. Oversampling by 64 gives 3 extra bits, raising 12-bit hardware to 15-bit effective resolution — for free in software. The STM32 ADC has a hardware oversampling unit (DFSDM or ADC_OFR) that does this automatically. The trade-off is reduced effective sample rate, which is fine for slowly changing signals like temperature or battery voltage.

subtopics.title: Embedded C essentials - volatile, bitfields, and register macros
body: Embedded C differs from systems C in one key way: the code talks directly to hardware, and the hardware can change memory values without the CPU's knowledge. The C abstract machine assumes that if you wrote to a variable, its value is what you wrote, and if nothing in the code changes it, it stays that way. Hardware breaks both assumptions. The `volatile` keyword and disciplined register access patterns are how you restore correctness.

`volatile` tells the compiler: do not cache this variable in a register, do not reorder reads/writes to it, and do not eliminate seemingly redundant accesses. For a hardware register, every read and write has a physical side effect (reading a status register may clear its flags; writing to a control register fires a hardware action). Without `volatile`, the optimizer may cache the value in a CPU register, skip a re-read that would detect a hardware flag change, or reorder two register writes (changing their relative order changes behavior). The rule: every memory-mapped peripheral register must be accessed through a `volatile`-qualified pointer or structure. CMSIS headers declare all device registers as `volatile`, which is why they work correctly.

**Bitfields** in a struct let you name individual bits or groups of bits in a register. But C bitfields have implementation-defined layout — the standard does not specify whether bit 0 maps to the MSB or LSB, or how bits are allocated across words. GCC for ARM typically does what you expect (bit 0 = LSB, packed LSB-first), but portable embedded code uses **bitmask + shift** macros instead: `#define USART_SR_RXNE (1u << 5)`, then test with `(USART1->SR & USART_SR_RXNE)`. CMSIS headers define both a bitfield struct and mask constants; use the mask/shift approach for portable critical code.

**Register macro idioms** are the vocabulary of bare-metal C. Setting a bit: `REG |= (1u << N)`. Clearing a bit: `REG &= ~(1u << N)`. Toggling: `REG ^= (1u << N)`. Testing: `if (REG & (1u << N))`. Setting a multi-bit field: `REG = (REG & ~(MASK << POS)) | ((VALUE & MASK) << POS)`. For atomic set/clear, use hardware-provided BSRR (GPIO), CCMR (timers), or `__LDREX`/`__STREX` for lock-free memory. Never read-modify-write a status register that has write-1-to-clear (W1C) bits — you may inadvertently clear a flag you were not handling.

**Memory barriers** (`__DSB()`, `__ISB()`, `__DMB()`) matter for sequences where ordering guarantees are required at the hardware level, not just the software level. On Cortex-M3/M4, the write buffer can defer peripheral register writes, so `__DSB()` (Data Synchronization Barrier) ensures all pending writes complete before proceeding. `__ISB()` (Instruction Synchronization Barrier) flushes the pipeline, necessary after enabling caches or modifying the VTOR. This matters most when switching CPU modes, enabling the MPU, or switching from flash-executing code to RAM-executing code — cases where the CPU's cached state must be flushed before the new configuration takes effect.

**`__attribute__((section(".sram2")))` and similar linker attributes** let you place specific data structures in specific memory regions, such as DMA buffers in non-cacheable SRAM on MCUs with data cache, or hot functions in CCM RAM for zero-wait-state execution. This is the intersection of C language and linker scripting that distinguishes serious embedded work from beginner firmware. Mastering section attributes, `__packed__`, `__aligned__(N)`, and `__attribute__((used))` (prevent LTO from eliminating ISR-table entries) gives you precise control over the binary layout the linker produces.

CMSIS documentation — Cortex-M device access layer

connections.note: volatile, bitfields and manual memory are low-level C made physical
Memory-mapped registers are how you drive all of this without a HAL

topics.title: Bare-metal ARM (STM32)
body: ARM **Cortex-M** architecture (M0/M3/M4/M7, Thumb-2), register-level programming (memory-mapped I/O, reading datasheets), the **reset sequence** (vector table → reset handler → `SystemInit` → main), clock config (HSE/HSI, PLL — get it wrong and nothing works), **DMA** (offload the CPU), timers, and **linker scripts** (`.data` copied to RAM, `.bss` zeroed).
Configuring an STM32 with no HAL — just registers — is the rite of passage that demystifies all higher-level embedded code.

subtopics.title: Cortex-M architecture - pipeline, Thumb-2, operating modes, and special registers
body: The ARM Cortex-M family (M0, M0+, M3, M4, M7, M33, M55) shares a common architecture but differs in features: M0/M0+ are the simplest (no hardware multiply/divide on some, no bit-banding, no Thumb-2 extensions); M3/M4 add Thumb-2, hardware multiply/divide, bit-banding, optional FPU (M4F); M7 adds dual-issue pipeline, TCM, and optional double-precision FPU. All run the **Thumb-2 instruction set** — a seamlessly mixed 16/32-bit instruction encoding that achieves roughly 70% of ARM 32-bit code density while keeping 32-bit power. There is no mode switch needed (unlike classic ARM Thumb mode), which is why the pipeline never stalls on instruction fetches the way early ARM7TDMI did.

The **programmer's model** is: 13 general-purpose registers (R0–R12), stack pointer (R13, banked as MSP and PSP), link register (R14), and program counter (R15). The **PSR (Program Status Register)** combines APSR (condition flags N/Z/C/V/Q), IPSR (current exception number), and EPSR (execution state, always Thumb on Cortex-M). The **CONTROL** register's bits select the stack pointer (MSP vs PSP) and privilege level. Write to CONTROL only when in privileged Thread mode; in Handler mode the processor always uses MSP and is always privileged.

Cortex-M has two **operating modes**: **Thread mode** (normal execution, can be privileged or unprivileged) and **Handler mode** (interrupt/exception handler, always privileged, always MSP). An RTOS uses both: kernel runs in privileged Thread mode using MSP; user tasks run in unprivileged Thread mode using PSP (one PSP per task, saving/restoring it on context switch). This separation means a buggy task cannot corrupt the kernel stack. Privilege separation is enforced by the optional **MPU** (covered in the RTOS-own topic).

**Special registers** accessed with MRS/MSR instructions: `PRIMASK` (1 bit — disables all interrupts except NMI and HardFault, used for critical sections), `BASEPRI` (N bits — masks interrupts with priority ≤ N, used for selective masking while still allowing high-priority interrupts), `FAULTMASK` (1 bit — disables even configurable faults, only used during fault recovery), `CONTROL` (stack pointer selection + privilege). These are not memory-mapped; you must use the ARM intrinsics (`__get_PRIMASK()`, `__set_BASEPRI()`, etc.) or inline assembly (`MSR PRIMASK, r0`).

The **3-stage pipeline** (M3/M4: fetch → decode → execute; M7 has a 6-stage dual-issue pipeline) means most instructions execute in one cycle. However, **branch misprediction** flushes the pipeline (2-3 cycle penalty for short branches on M3/M4). Cortex-M has a small branch predictor and the tail-chaining optimization (see the interrupts topic) that eliminates pipeline flush overhead between consecutive exceptions. For tight loops, the compiler may use `CBZ/CBNZ` (Compare and Branch if Zero/Non-Zero) to avoid flushing for common conditions.

resources.label: The Definitive Guide to ARM Cortex-M3 and M4 — Joseph Yiu

subtopics.title: Memory-mapped I/O and reading datasheets
body: On Cortex-M, **peripheral registers are memory-mapped** — every control and status register of every peripheral (GPIO, UART, SPI, ADC, DMA, timers) exists at a specific fixed address in the CPU's address space. There is no `IN`/`OUT` instruction as in x86; you simply read and write ordinary memory addresses, and the hardware bus fabric routes those accesses to the correct peripheral. This uniformity means the same C pointer-dereference that reads an integer from SRAM can configure a peripheral register.

The **memory map** of a Cortex-M MCU is standardized at the architecture level: 0x00000000-0x1FFFFFFF is Code space (flash typically at 0x08000000 on STM32), 0x20000000-0x3FFFFFFF is SRAM, 0x40000000-0x5FFFFFFF is peripheral space (split into APB1/APB2/AHB1/AHB2 sub-regions), 0xE0000000-0xE00FFFFF is the **Private Peripheral Bus (PPB)** containing the NVIC, SysTick, SCB, and DWT (the debug/trace hardware). Everything above 0xE0000000 is ARM-defined and identical across all Cortex-M MCUs; everything below 0xE0000000 is vendor-defined.

Reading a **Reference Manual (RM)** is the core skill. The workflow: find the peripheral's base address in the memory map table, go to the peripheral's chapter, find the register you need (e.g. `USART_CR1`, `GPIO_MODER`), read the bit field table to understand each field's offset, reset value, and Read/Write/Read-Write semantics. The 'reset value' column tells you what the register contains after reset — essential for knowing which bits need explicit configuration vs which you can leave at their default. Read-Only (R) bits reflect hardware state; Write-Only (W) bits trigger actions; Read/Write (RW) bits are the config you set; Write-1-to-Clear (W1C or RC_W1) bits are status flags that clear on a 1-write.

**Accessing registers in C** without a HAL uses the CMSIS device header (e.g. `stm32f4xx.h`). CMSIS defines a struct for each peripheral with all registers as `volatile` members. You get the peripheral pointer via a macro: `#define GPIOA ((GPIO_TypeDef *) GPIOA_BASE)` where `GPIOA_BASE` is the address constant. Then `GPIOA->MODER` is a `volatile uint32_t` at the correct address — the compiler generates a 32-bit memory write directly. No function-call overhead, no HAL abstraction layer, just the struct access the optimizer can see through completely.

A practical exercise that builds intuition: pick a peripheral (e.g. USART1 on STM32F4), open RM0090, find `USART_BRR`, compute the correct value by hand for 115200 baud with PCLK2=84 MHz, set the register directly, and verify with a logic analyzer. Then compare against what the HAL `HAL_UART_Init` computes. If they match, you understand CMSIS-level register access. If they differ, you understand why HALs add complexity (clock source selection, word-length encoding, etc.) and what they are actually doing.

resources.label: STM32F4 Reference Manual RM0090 (memory map section 2.3)

subtopics.title: The reset sequence - vector table, startup code, and SystemInit
body: When a Cortex-M device powers on or is reset, it does not jump to `main()`. The CPU executes a precise hardware-defined bootstrap sequence. Understanding this sequence explains every aspect of startup behavior, why the stack pointer matters before the first line of C runs, and how to customize or extend the startup for production firmware (bootloaders, secure boot, dual-bank applications).

On power-on, the CPU loads the **initial stack pointer** from address `0x00000000` (which maps to wherever the VTOR points, usually flash at `0x08000000`). The word at `0x08000000` is the first entry in the **vector table**: the initial value of MSP (Main Stack Pointer). The second word at `0x08000004` is the **reset handler address** (with the Thumb bit, bit 0, set to 1). The CPU loads MSP and jumps to the reset handler. This all happens before any C code executes — if MSP points outside valid SRAM, or the reset handler address is wrong, the system hard-faults immediately.

The **vector table** continues beyond the reset handler: NMI handler, HardFault handler, MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault, then the SVC/PendSV/SysTick entries, and finally the device-specific IRQ handlers (UART IRQ, SPI IRQ, etc.). The table is an array of function pointers, stored in flash, declared in the startup assembly or C file. The `__Vectors` symbol is the table itself; the linker places it at the start of flash. On runtime, the **VTOR (Vector Table Offset Register)** at `0xE000ED08` tells the CPU where the table lives. After copying firmware to RAM and enabling the cache, you may update VTOR to point to a RAM copy for faster dispatch.

The **startup file** (startup_stm32f4xx.s or .c) performs: (1) set MSP (already done by hardware, but some startup files reinforce it), (2) copy initialized variables from flash (LMA) to SRAM (VMA) — the `.data` section, (3) zero the `.bss` section (uninitialized globals), (4) call `SystemInit()` to configure PLL and flash wait states, (5) call `__libc_init_array()` for C++ static constructors, (6) call `main()`. Step 2 and 3 are critical: without them, global variables with non-zero initialization have garbage values, and uninitialized globals are not zero as the C standard requires.

`SystemInit()` is a CMSIS-provided function (in `system_stm32f4xx.c`) that configures the PLL and flash latency to reach the target system clock frequency. On STM32F4, it sets the flash wait states to 5 (correct for 168 MHz/3.3V) and enables the ART Accelerator, then starts the HSE, waits for HSE_RDY, configures the PLL, and switches SYSCLK to PLL. If the crystal fails to start (damaged, wrong load capacitors), the code hangs in the HSE_RDY wait loop — a real failure mode that happens when the PCB crystal circuit is wrong. Startup code is not abstract; it maps 1:1 to hardware bring-up.

subtopics.title: DMA - channels, modes, and memory-to-peripheral transfers
body: DMA (Direct Memory Access) is a hardware engine that moves data between memory and peripherals (or memory-to-memory) without CPU involvement. The CPU configures the DMA, arms it, and moves on. The DMA controller operates in parallel, firing an interrupt only on completion or error. On an MCU running at 168 MHz, a peripheral DMA transfer at 1 Mbps costs ~168 CPU cycles of setup plus essentially zero cycles per byte transferred — vs. 168 cycles per byte if the CPU polls. For any significant data rate, DMA is mandatory.

On STM32F4, DMA1 and DMA2 each have 8 streams, each stream configurable to one of 8 channels (a channel selects which peripheral triggers the stream). Each stream has source address, destination address, count, FIFO, burst configuration, and priority. You configure it: set `DMA_SxPAR` (peripheral address, e.g. `&USART1->DR`), `DMA_SxM0AR` (memory address), `DMA_SxNDTR` (count), `DMA_SxCR` (direction M→P or P→M, data size, increment mode, circular mode, interrupts). Enable the DMA request in the peripheral (e.g. `USART_CR3.DMAT` for UART TX DMA), then enable the stream — the first peripheral 'data register empty' signal starts the transfers.

**Circular mode** is essential for continuous streaming (audio, ADC sampling, UART RX ring buffer). The DMA reloads from M0AR when the count reaches zero, creating an infinite loop. The half-transfer and transfer-complete interrupts fire at the halfway point and end of the buffer respectively, allowing the CPU to process completed half-buffers while the DMA fills the other — classic **double buffering**. Without circular mode, you must reconfigure the DMA on every completion (adding latency) or miss samples during reconfiguration.

**Cache coherency** on MCUs with D-cache (STM32H7, Cortex-M7) is a critical trap. The CPU cache may hold stale data after a DMA write to SRAM, or the DMA may see stale data from SRAM if the CPU wrote to cache but the write-back has not happened. The fix: either place DMA buffers in a non-cached SRAM region (marked as `__attribute__((section(".noncache_data")))`), or explicitly flush/invalidate the cache around DMA operations using SCB_CleanDCache/SCB_InvalidateDCache. On Cortex-M4 (no D-cache), this is not an issue, which is why M4 DMA code that 'just works' breaks mysteriously on M7.

**DMA error handling** (half-transfer, transfer-complete, transfer-error interrupts) is often omitted in tutorial code but is essential in production. The transfer-error interrupt fires on bus errors (illegal address, DMA attempting to access an area the MPU forbids). Without handling it, the DMA silently stops and the application sees no data without knowing why. Log the error, stop the stream (`DMA_SxCR.EN = 0`), and reinitialize. Similarly, `USART_SR.ORE` (overrun) fires if the DMA did not drain the receive buffer fast enough — the UART flags the new byte as lost and must be cleared explicitly to resume.

subtopics.title: Linker scripts and ELF sections - LMA/VMA, startup copy loops
body: A **linker script** (`.ld` file) is the configuration file that tells `ld` (or `lld`) how to arrange the compiled object files into a final binary. It defines: the memory regions (flash and SRAM), the output sections (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.rodata`, etc.), which input sections go into which output section, where each output section is loaded (LMA) and where it executes (VMA), and how to produce the symbol table. Getting this wrong produces silent corruption: zeroed data, random crashes, or peripherals that initialize from garbage.

**LMA (Load Memory Address)** is where the data resides in the binary (in flash). **VMA (Virtual Memory Address)** is where the code expects to find it at runtime (in SRAM for `.data`). For the `.text` section (code), LMA = VMA = some address in flash — the code runs from flash directly. For the `.data` section (initialized globals), LMA is in flash (right after `.text`), VMA is in SRAM — the CPU reads code and global variables from different places. The startup code's copy loop is: from `.data_start` to `.data_end` in SRAM, copy from `_sdata_flash` (the LMA). If this loop is wrong, every global variable with a non-zero initializer has a garbage value.

Standard sections: `.text` (machine code), `.rodata` (read-only data: string literals, const globals, vtables), `.data` (initialized writable globals), `.bss` (zero-initialized globals — no actual data in flash, just size; startup zeroes SRAM), `.stack` (task stacks), `.heap` (if dynamic allocation is used). Special embedded additions: `.ccmram` (CCM RAM placement on F4), `.noncache` (non-cacheable SRAM on H7), `.fastcode` (hot functions placed in SRAM for zero-wait-state execution). Each section in the script assigns input sections to output sections: `*(.text .text.*)` collects all `.text` subsections from all objects.

**Weak symbols** allow the startup file to define default ISR handlers (all defaulting to an infinite loop or `DefaultHandler`) that application code can override. The linker uses a strong definition over a weak one of the same name. So if you define `void USART1_IRQHandler(void) { ... }` in your application, it replaces the weak no-op in the startup file. If you forget to define it and an interrupt fires, the weak handler (infinite loop) runs — which is actually better than a random jump to garbage, because you can catch it in a debugger.

Practical exercise: open the STM32F4 linker script, find where `.data` is loaded and linked, trace the startup assembly code that copies it, then add a custom `.ccmram` section, declare a variable with `__attribute__((section(".ccmram")))`, and verify in the map file that it landed at a CCM address. The `.map` file (produced with `-Wl,-Map=output.map`) is the definitive truth about where every symbol ended up — essential for diagnosing 'how did my flash usage grow by 10KB?' questions.

resources.label: GNU ld manual — linker scripts chapter

subtopics.title: Clock configuration - HSE, PLL, wait states, and the AHB/APB tree
body: Clock configuration is the first real task after 'can I toggle a pin'. Everything else — UART baud accuracy, SPI speed, ADC sample rate, timer frequency — depends on clocks being correct. Misconfiguration is silent: the chip runs, but peripherals behave at wrong frequencies, baud rates are off, or at extreme overclocking the MCU intermittently corrupts data.

The STM32F4 clock tree starts at one of three sources: **HSI** (High-Speed Internal, 16 MHz RC, ±1% accuracy, always available, no external components), **HSE** (High-Speed External, crystal or oscillator input, typically 8–25 MHz, more accurate, requires startup time), or **LSE** (Low-Speed External, 32.768 kHz, for RTC). The HSI or HSE feeds the **PLL**. The PLL has three parameters: `/M` (pre-divider, 2–63, brings input to 1–2 MHz), `×N` (VCO multiplier, 50–432, brings VCO to 100–432 MHz), `/P` (output divider, 2/4/6/8, brings to final SYSCLK ≤168 MHz). Additionally `/Q` feeds USB OTG/SDIO/RNG (must be 48 MHz exactly for USB), and `/R` (F446+) feeds SAI/I2S.

For 168 MHz from 8 MHz HSE: M=8, N=336, P=2 gives VCO=336 MHz, SYSCLK=168 MHz, Q=7 gives 48 MHz for USB. Flash wait states for 168 MHz at 3.3V = 5 (`FLASH_ACR.LATENCY = 5`). AHB prescaler = 1 → HCLK = 168 MHz. APB1 prescaler = 4 → PCLK1 = 42 MHz (UART2/3/4/5/I2C TIM2-7 at 42×2=84 MHz). APB2 prescaler = 2 → PCLK2 = 84 MHz (USART1/6, SPI1, ADC). Timers on APBx run at 2× PCLK when the APB prescaler is not 1 — a common source of off-by-2x errors in timer frequency calculations.

**Flash wait states** are required because flash read cycles are slower than the CPU at high frequencies. With too few wait states, the CPU reads a half-computed flash data value — producing random instruction corruption that appears as erratic crashes. Always set wait states *before* switching to the high-speed PLL, not after. The safe sequence: (1) set wait states for the target frequency, (2) enable ART accelerator and prefetch, (3) start HSE, wait for HSE_RDY, (4) configure PLL, (5) enable PLL, wait for PLL_RDY, (6) switch SYSCLK to PLL, (7) wait for SWS to confirm switch. Never switch directly from HSI to HSE without this sequence.

For production firmware that must survive component variation, the **MCO (Master Clock Output)** pins let you output SYSCLK or HSE directly to an external pin, which you can measure with an oscilloscope to confirm the actual frequency. Alternatively, configure a timer in input-capture mode with a known reference to cross-check the clock. Field failures caused by marginal crystals failing to start (wrong load capacitors, cold-solder on oscillator pads) are surprisingly common and caught only by testing clock startup under voltage and temperature stress.

STM32CubeMX — clock tree visualizer (free tool)
