topics.title: Low-power design
body: ARM low-power modes (Sleep/Stop/Standby — wake sources, startup time), power domains (keeping just RTC + backup RAM alive), clock gating, power profiling (Nordic PPK2, measuring μA), and energy harvesting (sizing solar/supercap for a duty cycle).
Getting a sensor node to single-digit-μA average is its own deep optimization discipline.

subtopics.title: ARM low-power modes - WFI/WFE, Sleep, Stop, and Standby
body: Cortex-M provides a hierarchy of power states, each trading off **power consumption vs resume latency**. Understanding this tradeoff precisely is the first step to meeting a power budget: you choose the deepest state whose resume latency fits within your application's response time requirement.

`WFI` (Wait For Interrupt) is the one-instruction entry into **Sleep mode**. The CPU halts; all SRAM and peripheral state is preserved; the AHB bus clock stops. All interrupts (any pending or incoming IRQ) wake the CPU. Resume latency: 1-2 clock cycles. Active current on an STM32L4 at 4 MHz: ~1 mA. Sleep current: ~200 µA. This mode is the correct choice when the CPU is idle but peripherals (ADC, UART, DMA) are still running and may produce data at any moment.

**Stop mode** (STM32 family): the main oscillator (HSE, HSI, PLL) is switched off. Only the LSI (32 kHz RC) or LSE (32.768 kHz crystal) can run for the RTC and the Low-Power Timer (LPTIM). SRAM and peripheral register state is retained. NVIC wake sources must be EXTI lines (GPIO edge, RTC alarm, USART wake-on-byte with LSI). Resume: the CPU boots from the Stop mode wakeup vector, re-enables the PLL, waits for PLL lock (~100-200 µs), and resumes. Current in Stop2 mode on STM32L4: ~0.4 µA with RTC running. This is the mode for sleeping between measurements in a sensor node — sleep for 10 seconds, wake, sample, transmit, sleep again.

**Standby mode**: most of SRAM is powered off (only 4KB of Backup SRAM and RTC registers survive). The RTC continues running. Wake sources are WKUP pins (specific GPIOs), RTC alarm, or RTC tamper. On wakeup, the system resets from the beginning (vector table, startup code, main) — equivalent to a cold start. Current: ~0.5 µA. Use Standby when the system will be asleep for minutes to hours and data continuity is not required (or backed up to the small Backup SRAM / external flash before sleep).

**Shutdown mode** (available on some STM32L4+/L5 devices): the entire voltage regulator is powered off except for the wakeup comparator. Only RTC can run. No SRAM. Current: ~30 nA. Wake: only WKUP pins. This is the deepest available state and is used for shipping-mode in consumer electronics (device powered off in the box, wakes when a button is pressed). The tradeoff: WKUP pin pull-down leakage and PCB leakage current can dominate at this level — the MCU is no longer the limiting factor.

resources.label: ST — STM32L4 low-power modes application note AN4767

subtopics.title: Clock gating, dynamic voltage/frequency scaling, and peripheral power control
body: Even in active mode, most of the power consumed by a microcontroller is in the **clock distribution tree and clocked peripherals**. Clock gating — disabling the clock to peripherals that are not in use — is the first and cheapest optimization: it requires only a register write and saves power proportional to the switching activity of the gated logic.

On STM32, the **RCC (Reset and Clock Control)** peripheral has enable bits for every peripheral clock in `RCC_AHBxENR` and `RCC_APBxENR` registers. After initialization but before entering an idle loop, disable every peripheral you are not using: `RCC->AHB2ENR &= ~RCC_AHB2ENR_GPIOBEN;` disables GPIOB's clock. Savings: each unused peripheral at 168 MHz contributes 50-500 µA of clock-switching current. Disabling all unused peripherals on an STM32F4 application can reduce active current by 30-50%.

**Dynamic Voltage/Frequency Scaling (DVFS)**: at lower clock frequencies, the supply voltage can be reduced (lower switching losses: $P \propto C V^2 f$). STM32L4 and similar ultra-low-power MCUs support voltage range selection: at 1.0V, maximum frequency drops to 16 MHz but power consumption drops dramatically. For a task that is memory-bandwidth-limited (e.g. copying data) rather than compute-limited, running at 16 MHz/1.0V vs 80 MHz/1.2V may take 5x longer but consume less total energy (energy = power × time; if power drops 10x and time increases 5x, net energy is halved). Always calculate **energy per operation**, not just power.

**GPIO power management**: every GPIO configured as input with no pull-up/pull-down and floating will oscillate, drawing dynamic current as the Schmitt trigger bounces. In Stop/Standby mode, configure all unused GPIO pins as analog (analog mode disables the Schmitt trigger input buffer, eliminating the oscillation current). This is documented in ST application notes as the single largest contributor to unexpectedly high Stop mode current in student and prototype designs.

**Peripheral wakeup from Stop mode with UART**: with STM32L4's LPUART (Low-Power UART), the UART receiver can operate from the LSE (32.768 kHz) in Stop mode, wake the MCU on receipt of a byte (address match or any byte), and the CPU picks up the full message after PLL restart. This enables truly autonomous wake-on-command operation — the MCU sleeps until a command arrives from a host, processes it, and returns to sleep — achieving sub-µA average current for command-driven devices.

subtopics.title: Power profiling - Nordic PPK2, current measurement, and the µA regime
body: You cannot optimize what you cannot measure. Power profiling in embedded requires hardware tools capable of measuring current at the **µA level with millisecond time resolution** — a combination not achievable with a standard multimeter (insufficient sensitivity) or an oscilloscope (measures voltage, not current).

The **Nordic Power Profiler Kit II (PPK2)** is the de-facto standard tool for embedded current measurement. It measures supply current to your device with 1-µA resolution, up to 1 A, with 100 kHz sampling rate and a direct USB interface to the Nordic Power Profiler desktop application. You see a time-domain plot of current vs time — you can zoom in to see exactly how long each sleep mode lasts, what each wake burst consumes, and the leakage current at rest. At $100, it is the best ROI tool in embedded power engineering.

Alternative measurement: a **low-value shunt resistor** (10Ω or 100Ω for µA ranges) in series with VCC, monitored by an oscilloscope with a differential probe or a precision ADC. The voltage across the shunt equals I × R: 10 µA through 100Ω gives 1 mV — at the noise floor of most oscilloscopes. Use a dedicated current probe (LeCroy CP031, Tektronix TCP0030) for higher precision, or use the INA219/INA3221 (precision current-sense amplifier with I2C, 12-bit ADC, 400 µA to 26A range, ±1% accuracy) integrated directly on the board.

**Battery lifetime calculation**: average current $I_{avg} = I_{active} \times D + I_{sleep} \times (1-D)$ where $D$ is duty cycle (fraction of time active). For a 1000 mAh coin cell and $I_{avg}$ = 10 µA: lifetime = 1000 mAh / 0.010 mA = 100,000 hours ≈ 11 years — battery life exceeds battery shelf life. But at 100 µA average: 10,000 hours ≈ 1.1 years. The difference between 10 µA and 100 µA is the difference between a 10-year device and an annual-battery-replacement product. This is why single-digit-µA average current is worth significant engineering effort.

**Common power bugs**: (1) Leaving a GPIO in input mode without pull-up/pull-down in Stop mode (adds 10-100 µA from oscillating input). (2) Forgetting to disable the 32 kHz LSE oscillator output (drives an unused GPIO, drawing 5 µA). (3) Leaving I2C/SPI peripheral in an active-clock state while sleeping. (4) An attached sensor drawing current in Stop mode via its pull-up resistors — sensor power must be switched off (use a P-MOSFET load switch) before entering Stop mode. (5) USB D+ pull-up resistor staying asserted, drawing 1.5 mA from the host. Profile first, fix individually, confirm improvement — do not guess.

resources.label: Nordic PPK2 — product page and Power Profiler app
Memfault Interrupt — low power measurement techniques

subtopics.title: Duty-cycle optimization and tickless idle
body: The fundamental pattern for battery-operated embedded systems is **burst-then-sleep**: stay powered down for most of the time, wake up briefly to sample, compute, and transmit, then return to sleep. The fraction of time spent active is the **duty cycle** D. Minimizing D (while meeting all application requirements) is the primary low-power optimization objective.

**Optimizing wake burst duration**: every microsecond of active time costs energy. Profile the wake burst with PPK2 or a GPIO toggle + scope: (1) PLL startup time (~100-200 µs on STM32L4), (2) sensor measurement time (ADC conversion, I2C read), (3) computation (e.g. filter, threshold check), (4) transmission (BLE advertisement, LoRa packet), (5) pre-sleep housekeeping. Each phase should be minimized: pre-configure peripherals that support it (ADC in low-power auto-conversion mode), cache sensor config in SRAM to avoid re-sending it, use the fastest transmission mode for the data size.

**Tickless idle** in FreeRTOS (covered in the RTOS topic) is the software half of low-power operation. Without it, SysTick fires 1000 times/second even during Stop mode, waking the CPU to run the scheduler and returning to Stop mode — adding up to hundreds of µA of unnecessary active time. With tickless idle, the CPU enters Stop mode and only wakes when a real event occurs (GPIO edge, RTC alarm, UART byte). Combined with aggressive peripheral clock gating and GPIO analog mode, a typical FreeRTOS application can achieve 1-5 µA average current in periodic-sensing applications.

The **wakeup sequence time** is the latency from the wakeup event to the first instruction of the application running at full speed. On STM32L4: LPTIM wakeup from Stop2 → HSI16 auto-enable (1 µs) → software re-enables PLL (200 µs wait for lock) → switch SYSCLK to PLL (1 µs) → resume RTOS tasks. The 200 µs PLL lock time is the dominant delay. For applications that need sub-millisecond response time, either run from HSI16 directly (no PLL) at lower frequency, or use a lower-power MCU with faster PLL lock (nRF52840 PLL locks in 10-50 µs from radio-optimized PLL).

**Duty cycle calculation for sensor nodes**: target a 5-year battery life from a 2000 mAh AA cell. Average current budget: 2000 mAh / (5 × 8760 h) ≈ 46 µA average. If the MCU draws 3 mA active and 1 µA in Stop2, and transmission draws 20 mA for 5 ms every 10 seconds: average current = (3 mA × 0.1 s + 20 mA × 0.005 s) / 10 s + 1 µA = (300 µA + 100 µA) / 10 + 1 µA = 41 µA. This meets the 46 µA budget with slight margin. Increasing the sample period to 15 seconds gives more margin; adding a second sensor that wakes the MCU for another 50 ms changes the math significantly. Systematic energy budgeting before hardware selection avoids discovering your MCU choice is 5x over budget at prototype stage.

subtopics.title: Energy harvesting - solar, thermoelectric, supercapacitor sizing, and MPPT
body: Energy harvesting replaces or supplements batteries by converting ambient energy (light, heat, RF, vibration) into electrical power. It enables indefinite device lifetimes — a solar-powered sensor node lasts as long as the hardware, not the battery. The engineering discipline is tight: harvested power is small and variable, and the load must be shaped to stay within the harvested envelope.

**Solar harvesting**: a small solar panel (e.g. 5 cm × 5 cm amorphous silicon, ~1 mW peak in office light, 10-50 mW in direct sunlight) produces open-circuit voltage of 3-6V. The operating point that maximizes power output is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, which depends on light intensity and load. **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** algorithms (perturb-and-observe, incremental conductance) periodically vary the load to track the MPP as conditions change. ICs like the AEM10941 or BQ25505 implement MPPT with a boost converter, cold-start from voltages as low as 80 mV, and charge management for a supercapacitor or rechargeable battery.

**Thermoelectric harvesting**: a TEG (Thermoelectric Generator) produces voltage proportional to the temperature differential across it: V = N × S × ΔT where N is the number of junctions, S is the Seebeck coefficient (~200 µV/K for Bi₂Te₃), and ΔT is the temperature difference in Kelvin. A 20°C differential on a standard 40-junction module produces ~160 mV open-circuit, with an optimum load current in the µA-mA range. Because the output voltage is very low, a high-efficiency boost converter with ultra-low startup voltage (like the LTC3108, operational from 20 mV) is required. TEG harvesting is viable in industrial environments (machinery, pipelines) with persistent thermal gradients.

**Supercapacitor sizing**: a supercapacitor (EDLC, electrochemical double-layer capacitor) is the ideal intermediate storage for energy harvesting: millions of charge/discharge cycles (vs ~1000 for Li-Po), wide temperature range, high peak current capability. Sizing: $C = \frac{2 E}{V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2}$ where E is energy needed to sustain load during the worst-case no-harvest period (e.g., 3 days of darkness). For E = 100 mJ (10 µA × 3.3V × 3 days ≈ adequate margin), V_max = 3.3V, V_min = 1.8V (minimum for MCU operation): $C = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{3.3^2 - 1.8^2} = \frac{0.2}{10.89 - 3.24} \approx 26 mF$. A 100 mF supercapacitor provides comfortable margin.

**System design for harvesters**: the harvester charges the supercapacitor during daylight; the MCU draws from the supercapacitor. A comparator or the PMIC monitors supercapacitor voltage: if voltage drops below V_min (MCU loses regulation), shut down gracefully — write state to non-volatile memory and enter Standby. On voltage recovery, restart with the saved state. This **brownout graceful degradation** is what separates a prototype from a deployable node. Commercial PMIC solutions (AEM10941, SPV1050, BQ25505) handle the MPPT, boost conversion, and comparator signaling as an integrated system, leaving the MCU to focus on the application.

topics.title: Safety-critical embedded
body: Functional-safety standards (**IEC 61508** SILs, **ISO 26262** ASILs for automotive), **MISRA-C** (rules *and* rationale), defensive programming (assert everywhere, watchdogs, CRC on config), and formal verification for embedded (CBMC, Frama-C, TLA+).
When firmware controls brakes or insulin pumps, 'works on my bench' is replaced by documented, analyzable correctness.

subtopics.title: Functional safety standards - IEC 61508, ISO 26262 ASILs, and DO-178C
body: **IEC 61508** ('Functional Safety of E/E/ES Safety-Related Systems') is the foundational generic standard. It defines **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1-4)**, where SIL 4 is the most stringent. Each SIL specifies a **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**: SIL 1: 10⁻¹-10⁻², SIL 2: 10⁻²-10⁻³, SIL 3: 10⁻³-10⁻⁴, SIL 4: 10⁻⁴-10⁻⁵ per demand. Achieving SIL 4 means the safety function fails catastrophically less than 1 time in 100,000 demands. The standard covers the full **safety lifecycle**: concept, development, operation, modification, and decommissioning — safety is not a testing phase, it is a design property.

**ISO 26262** ('Road Vehicles — Functional Safety') is the automotive adaptation of IEC 61508. It replaces SILs with **ASILs (Automotive Safety Integrity Levels): ASIL A, B, C, D** (D is most critical). ASIL assignment is determined by **risk analysis using three parameters**: **Severity** (S0-S3, ranging from no injury to life-threatening), **Exposure** (E0-E4, how often the hazardous situation occurs), and **Controllability** (C0-C3, ability of driver/others to avoid the hazard). ASIL = combination of S, E, C per a look-up matrix. A brake-by-wire system controlling emergency braking is likely ASIL D (S3, E4, C0); an interior lighting malfunction would be QM (Quality Managed — no safety requirement).

**ASIL D requirements** for software include: formal verification or exhaustive structural coverage (MC/DC — Modified Condition/Decision Coverage), no dynamic memory allocation, no uninitialized variables, no undefined behavior, all code paths analyzed for WCET, every 'shall' requirement traced to test evidence, independence between development and V&V (different engineers/teams). These requirements explain why automotive-grade software development costs 10-100x more per line of code than commercial embedded software.

**DO-178C** ('Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification') is the avionics equivalent. It defines Design Assurance Levels (DAL A-E) where DAL A is the most critical (software whose failure contributes to a catastrophic failure condition). DAL A requires: decision coverage at 100% (MC/DC), formal methods for complex logic, structural coverage verification, and independence in all development activities. The F-35's flight control software is DAL A; a cabin pressure indicator might be DAL C. DO-178C compliance is what allows software-only systems to control fly-by-wire aircraft.

**Practical significance for embedded engineers**: most commercial embedded work does not require full IEC 61508 or ISO 26262 certification, but the *techniques* those standards mandate — static analysis, defensive programming, WCET analysis, code review checklists, independent verification — are valuable at every level. Even for a non-safety-critical project, applying MISRA-C and running a static analyzer (Polyspace, PC-lint) catches real bugs that testing misses. The standards codify what good engineering looks like under the highest stakes.

resources.label: MISRA-C 2012 — guidelines for the use of C

subtopics.title: MISRA-C - rules, rationale, and static analysis tools
body: **MISRA-C** (MISRA: Motor Industry Software Reliability Association) is a set of coding guidelines for safety-critical C code, originally published for automotive but now used across medical, aerospace, and industrial embedded. MISRA-C:2012 has 143 guidelines divided into **mandatory** (must be obeyed), **required** (should be obeyed with documented justification for any deviation), and **advisory** (best practice, deviations are expected in some contexts).

The rules target the worst C language behaviors: undefined behavior, implementation-defined behavior, and language features whose misuse is statistically correlated with defects. Key rules: **Rule 13.4** (assignments in expressions), **Rule 14.4** (conditions must have bool type — prevents `if (ptr)` where you mean `if (ptr != NULL)`), **Rule 15.5** (function must have a single exit point), **Rule 17.1** (va_args banned), **Rule 18.1** (pointer arithmetic within bounds only), **Rule 21.3** (dynamic allocation banned). Rules 14.4 and 15.5 are the most debated; the single-exit-point rule is a legacy requirement that modern safety engineers often grant deviations for because early returns can be clearer.

**Understanding the rationale** matters more than memorizing rules. Example: **Rule 13.2** (value of an expression shall not be used elsewhere after a side-effect). This catches `a = a++` (undefined behavior in C99), `f(a++, a++)` (argument evaluation order unspecified), and `x = foo() + bar()` where both functions modify a shared global. The rule is motivated by real defects, not aesthetics. When you understand *why* a rule exists, you can judge whether a deviation is truly safe for a specific case.

**Static analysis tools** automate MISRA compliance checking and find additional bugs. PC-lint/FlexeLint (Gimpel): the classic commercial tool, highly configurable, run on source files as part of build. Polyspace Code Prover (MathWorks): formal abstract interpretation that proves absence of certain runtime errors (buffer overflow, division by zero, NULL pointer dereference) — produces 'No issue' (proven safe), 'Potential issue' (may fail under some inputs), or 'Definite issue' (always fails) classifications. LDRA Testbed: coverage measurement and MISRA checking combined. Helix QAC: MISRA-focused commercial tool widely used in automotive. Free options: Cppcheck (basic MISRA support), Clang Static Analyzer, Infer (Facebook, flow-sensitive interprocedural). None replaces careful code review, but static analysis finds whole classes of bugs (uninitialized memory, use-after-free, integer overflow) that reviewers miss.

**MISRA compliance in practice**: few projects adopt all MISRA-C rules — the set is too restrictive for general programming and some rules conflict with each other or with practical requirements. The standard approach is a **deviation process**: document every guideline that is not followed, provide the safety rationale for the deviation, obtain approval from the safety manager, and record in the project's **Software Quality Plan**. For example, 'Rule 21.3 (dynamic allocation banned) deviations: during task initialization only, heap is used to allocate TCBs; after initialization, the heap is locked; rationale: static allocation of all 17 tasks requires prohibitive SRAM reservation; risk: heap allocation failure during init is handled by a safe-state reset.' This is what 'documented' means in a safety context.

resources.label: Polyspace Code Prover — formal verification for embedded C

subtopics.title: Defensive programming - watchdogs, assertions, CRC config, and safe-state transitions
body: **Defensive programming** in embedded is the practice of assuming the system will be subjected to unexpected conditions — electrical noise, cosmic ray bit flips, software bugs not found in testing, hardware aging — and designing the firmware to detect and recover from these conditions rather than silently propagating corrupted state to the actuators.

**Watchdog timers** are the first line of defense. The **IWDG (Independent Watchdog)** on STM32 is clocked from the internal LSI oscillator, independent of all application clocks and the main software. Configure it for a timeout of 2-5x the maximum expected interrupt-to-interrupt or task-to-task period. A watchdog task or the main loop must 'kick' (refresh) the watchdog within the timeout. If the firmware crashes, deadlocks, or enters an infinite loop — anything that prevents the kick — the watchdog fires a reset. The IWDG is the embedded equivalent of a circuit breaker: the absolute safety net when all else fails. In a safety-critical design, the watchdog **must** be enabled; it is impermissible to disable it in production.

**Assertions** are runtime checks that verify assumed invariants. In production firmware, `assert(ptr != NULL)` should not call `abort()` (which may not exist or may leave hardware in a bad state). Instead, the assert handler should: disable all safety-critical outputs (de-energize actuators, disable motor drive), log the fault information (file, line, register state) to a persistent fault log in Backup SRAM or external flash, and trigger a controlled system reset. This is a **safe state transition**: when the firmware detects an internal inconsistency, it moves the hardware to a known-safe configuration before resetting. An insulin pump that fails without de-energizing the drive pump is catastrophic; one that asserts, stops the pump, and resets is recoverable.

**CRC on config and persistent data**: any configuration or calibration data stored in flash or EEPROM must be protected by a CRC (or checksum/MAC for stronger guarantees). On every boot, compute the CRC over the configuration region and compare against the stored value. If it fails, the config is corrupt — use the factory-default safe config and alert the operator. Without this check, a cosmic ray bit flip in a calibration constant (gain factor, PID coefficient, setpoint limit) could cause the system to operate outside safe parameters indefinitely without any indication. The STM32 hardware CRC engine (CRC32, CRC16-CCITT selectable) computes checksums in hardware at negligible time cost.

**Redundant execution and diversity**: for SIL 2+ systems, critical calculations are performed by two independent channels (often on different MCU cores or entirely separate MCUs) and the results are compared. A discrepancy triggers a safe state. For software diversity (to catch systematic faults: bugs that exist in both copies of the same code), the two channels may use different algorithms or different compilers. This is called **1oo2 (1 out of 2)** voting with **diverse redundancy**. Medical infusion pumps and automotive brake systems routinely use this architecture — two microcontrollers running diverse software, communicating via CAN, both required to agree before energizing the output.

resources.label: Better Embedded System Software — Philip Koopman (blog + book)
Memfault — fault handling and watchdog guide

subtopics.title: Formal verification - CBMC, Frama-C, and TLA+ for embedded protocols
body: **Formal verification** proves (mathematically, not empirically) that a program satisfies a specification, for all possible inputs and execution paths. Testing can show the presence of bugs; formal methods can prove their absence within the scope of the model. For safety-critical embedded, where even a single input that causes failure may kill people, this distinction is significant.

**CBMC (C Bounded Model Checker)** is a formal verification tool for C programs. It unrolls loops to a bounded depth, encodes the resulting acyclic program as a Boolean satisfiability (SAT) or SMT problem, and uses a SAT/SMT solver (MiniSat, Z3) to prove or disprove safety properties (absence of buffer overflow, division by zero, pointer dereference out-of-bounds, integer overflow, assertion violations). CBMC is sound within its bounds: if it says 'no counterexample found within N loop iterations', you have a proof within that bound. Usage: `cbmc file.c --unwind 10 --bounds-check --pointer-check --overflow-check --assertion-check`. AWS uses CBMC to verify memory safety in their FreeRTOS port and C standard library implementations.

**Frama-C** is a framework for static analysis and formal verification of C code. Its **WP (Weakest Precondition) plugin** uses **Hoare logic** to prove functional correctness: you annotate functions with **ACSL (ANSI/ISO C Specification Language)** contracts — preconditions, postconditions, and loop invariants — and Frama-C generates verification conditions discharged by SMT solvers (Alt-Ergo, Z3, CVC4). Example annotation: `/*@ requires \valid(buf + (0..size-1)); @ requires size > 0; @ ensures \result == sum; @*/`. The key advantage over CBMC: Frama-C proves full functional correctness (output is correct) not just safety (output does not crash). The key cost: writing complete and correct ACSL specifications is a significant effort, roughly equal to writing the code itself.

**TLA+ (Temporal Logic of Actions)** is a specification language for concurrent and distributed systems. It is used to model protocols — the algorithm that runs on the embedded MCU, not the C code itself. For example, specifying and model-checking a custom communication protocol's state machine: 'prove that the protocol never reaches a state where both master and slave believe they hold the bus simultaneously'. TLA+ is the tool AWS used to find a 35-instruction bug in DynamoDB's replication protocol, and it is directly applicable to embedded: modeling an RTOS scheduler, a safety interlock state machine, or an I2C multi-master arbitration protocol. The Toolbox model checker explores all reachable states exhaustively up to a configurable depth.

**Integration with CI**: formal verification tools fit into automated pipelines. CBMC can be run on every commit as a regression check: verify that specific safety-critical functions (range checkers, CRC validators, state machine transitions) remain free of the checked error classes. If a developer changes a function in a way that introduces a potential buffer overflow, CBMC catches it automatically — before code review, before testing, before the binary ships. This shifts bug discovery left to the earliest, cheapest point in the development lifecycle, which is precisely what functional safety standards incentivize.

resources.label: CBMC — C Bounded Model Checker documentation
Frama-C — WP plugin tutorial and ACSL specification

subtopics.title: Hardware redundancy - lockstep cores, ECC memory, and hardware self-test
body: Software correctness is necessary but not sufficient for safety-critical systems. **Hardware faults** — transient (single-event upsets from cosmic rays), intermittent (marginal transistors under voltage/temperature stress), and permanent (hard failures from aging or manufacturing defects) — can corrupt program execution even when the software is perfectly written. Safety standards require architectural measures to detect and handle hardware faults.

**Lockstep dual-core MCUs** (ARM Cortex-R5F in lockstep mode, used in Texas Instruments TMS570, Infineon AURIX TC3xx, Renesas RH850) execute the same instruction stream on two independent processor cores from the same instruction memory, then compare results cycle-by-cycle. Any discrepancy (bit flip in a register, cache corruption, arithmetic unit fault) is detected within one cycle and triggers a fault handling response. This is the implementation of **1oo2 voting in hardware**: both cores must agree on every output, or the system is shut down. Lockstep cores achieve ASIL D hardware integrity at the MCU level without requiring software redundancy.

**ECC (Error Correcting Code) memory** detects and corrects bit flips in SRAM and flash. Hamming code ECC on SRAM can correct single-bit errors (SEC — Single Error Correction) and detect two-bit errors (DED — Double Error Detection). On automotive MCUs like the TMS570 or AURIX, all SRAM and flash have ECC. On standard Cortex-M MCUs (STM32), SRAM does not have ECC. The implication: a standard STM32F4 in a high-radiation environment (space, aircraft, some automotive underhood locations) will experience bit flips that corrupt data silently. For such applications, either use an MCU with ECC SRAM, or implement software-level duplication of critical variables (store each critical value in two separate locations and compare before use).

**Hardware self-test (POST: Power-On Self-Test)**: safety standards require testing the hardware during startup before putting the system into operation. Typical tests: CPU register test (write/read/verify patterns to all registers), SRAM test (march algorithms — march C, march X, checkerboard), flash CRC verification (all of firmware CRC matches stored value), clock frequency verification (measure SYSCLK against RTC reference), and peripheral functional tests (ADC self-calibration, watchdog test-then-restart). On TMS570 (Cortex-R4F), the LBIST (Logic Built-In Self-Test) and MBIST (Memory Built-In Self-Test) are automated in hardware and run before the CPU starts executing application code.

**Fault injection testing** is the verification methodology that completes the hardware safety case: deliberately inject faults (bit flips via hardware fault injection, function parameter errors, timing violations) and verify that the detection and recovery mechanisms work correctly. ASIL D requires fault injection testing of all claimed safety mechanisms. Software fault injection (SWIFI) instruments the C code to flip bits, corrupt return values, or simulate failed assertions, then verifies the system reaches the safe state. Hardware fault injection (laser fault injection, electromagnetic fault injection at the package level, supply voltage glitching) tests the hardware protections in a more realistic failure model. This is the final validation step before a safety-critical embedded product ships.

resources.label: Texas Instruments — TMS570 lockstep safety MCU overview
Infineon — AURIX TC3xx lockstep and safety architecture

connections.note: Formal verification (CBMC/Frama-C) applies the proof methods from PLT
Secure boot and signed firmware bring crypto into safety-critical systems

topics.title: Hardware design & debugging
body: **KiCad** (schematic capture, PCB layout, DRC, Gerbers), decoupling capacitors (bypass on every VCC pin), high-speed design (50Ω impedance, differential pairs, ground planes), EMC basics, and **JTAG/SWD** debugging (J-Link, breakpoints, RTT logging).
Designing and assembling a custom board closes the loop from firmware to physical product.

subtopics.title: KiCad workflow - schematic capture, net assignment, footprints, and Gerber output
body: **KiCad** is the leading open-source EDA (Electronic Design Automation) suite for schematic capture and PCB layout. It has reached professional quality and is used for production hardware at companies like CERN and Arduino. The workflow: create a project → draw the schematic in Eeschema → assign footprints to symbols → export netlist → import into Pcbnew → route the PCB → run DRC → export Gerbers and drill files → send to a PCB fab.

**Schematic best practices**: use hierarchical sheets for complex designs (top-level sheet references sub-sheets for power supply, MCU, communication interfaces, etc.). Use power symbols (VCC, GND, +3V3) rather than drawing power rail wires — cleaner and less error-prone. Add **ERC (Electrical Rules Check)** annotations and resolve all errors before starting layout. Label every net that connects between sheets or that you need to probe during debugging — named nets appear in the netlist and make PCB routing intent clear. Add component values and tolerances in the schematic; do not defer to the BOM.

**Footprint assignment** is the bridge between schematic and PCB. Each schematic symbol (a resistor, MCU, connector) must be associated with a physical footprint (the copper pads and courtyard that match the real component's physical package). Common mistakes: using a 0402 footprint for a 0603 component (assembly failure — wrong placement pick-and-place offset), wrong pad pitch on a QFN or TSSOP (causes misalignment during reflow), incorrect courtyard (causes DRC clearance violations). Use the KiCad footprint browser to inspect the footprint dimensions and match the datasheet's land pattern recommendation.

**PCB layout workflow in Pcbnew**: import netlist, place components (MCU first, then crystals close to it, then bypass caps close to power pins, then connectors at board edges, then supporting components). Set design rules (min trace width, clearance, via size) matching your fab's capabilities. Route critical nets first (clock, differential pairs, power). Run **DRC (Design Rules Check)** to verify no clearance violations, no unconnected nets, no overlapping courtyard. Export **Gerbers** (one file per layer: F.Cu, B.Cu, F.SilkS, B.SilkS, F.Mask, B.Mask) and Excellon drill files. Submit to your fab along with stack-up specification.

**Fab-ready checklist**: verify the board outline is on the Edge.Cuts layer (not a drawing layer). Confirm all vias have the correct drill size and annular ring for the fab's minimum capability. Check that there are no slivers of copper (thin copper remnants from boolean operations) that the fab may not be able to etch reliably. Use the 3D viewer (`Alt+3`) to visually confirm component placement makes sense. Order an unpopulated prototype (bare board) from a low-cost fab (JLCPCB, PCBWay) to verify dimensions before committing to assembly.

resources.label: Phil's Lab — KiCad PCB design tutorial (YouTube)

subtopics.title: Power supply design - LDO vs buck, decoupling capacitors, and bulk capacitance
body: Every digital system starts with a power supply. The power supply is often the most neglected part of a student board design and the most common source of mysterious bugs: supply voltage ripple coupling into analog measurements, insufficient transient response causing voltage sag during radio transmission, or excessive heat from an inefficient regulator.

**LDO (Low Drop-Out linear regulator)** converts a higher input voltage to a regulated lower output voltage by dissipating the excess as heat: $P_{heat} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_{load}$. Simple, low-noise, two-component (regulator + bypass caps). Appropriate when: voltage difference is small (<1V), current is low (<200 mA), PCB space is constrained, or output noise requirements are strict (ADC reference, RF front-end). Inappropriate for: 5V input to 1.8V output at 500 mA (power = 1.6W, requires a heatsink) or battery-powered designs where efficiency matters (LDO efficiency = V_out / V_in, so a 5V-to-3.3V LDO at 66% efficiency wastes 34% of battery energy as heat).

**Buck (step-down switching) converter** achieves 85-95% efficiency by rapidly switching the input through an inductor, using the inductor's stored energy to maintain output voltage. Switching frequency (100 kHz–3 MHz) must be chosen to keep the inductor and output capacitor values practical (higher frequency = smaller components) while keeping switching losses acceptable. Buck converters produce **switching noise** on the output at the switching frequency and harmonics — problematic for ADC references and sensitive RF circuits. Mitigation: add a small LC low-pass filter between the buck and the sensitive analog supply, or use a separate LDO for the analog section (powered from the buck's output).

**Decoupling capacitors** are the most important passive components on any digital board. Every VCC pin of every IC needs a **local bypass capacitor** (100 nF ceramic, 0402 or 0603, X5R or X7R dielectric, placed as close to the VCC pin as possible — trace length < 2mm). This capacitor provides the instantaneous current during fast signal transitions (CMOS gates switching draw spike currents in nanoseconds that the power supply rail cannot respond to fast enough). Without it, the VCC pin dips momentarily, causing glitches in any logic dependent on that supply. Additionally, add a **bulk capacitor** (10-47 µF electrolytic or tantalum) near the power entry point to handle slower transient loads (motor startup, radio transmission bursts). Placing bypass caps on the wrong side of a via (even a short via adds inductance, reducing the capacitor's effectiveness at high frequency) is a common mistake that shows up as supply ripple at GHz frequencies.

The **capacitor placement rule of three**: (1) 100 nF ceramic at every IC VCC pin (high-frequency bypass). (2) 1-10 µF ceramic or tantalum per power domain (mid-frequency bulk). (3) 47-100 µF electrolytic at the supply input (low-frequency bulk, handles large step loads). The physics: an ideal capacitor is a short circuit at all frequencies, but real capacitors have **ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance)** and **ESL (Equivalent Series Inductance)** that make them ineffective above their self-resonant frequency. A 100 µF electrolytic has high ESR and is useless above ~1 MHz; a 100 nF ceramic has low ESR and is effective to 100 MHz. The combination of values covers the full frequency range of interest.

resources.label: Analog Devices — power supply design guide (MT-101)
Texas Instruments — LDO vs buck converter selection guide

subtopics.title: High-speed PCB design - impedance control, differential pairs, length matching
body: When signal edges are fast enough that the electrical wavelength is comparable to the trace length, a PCB trace behaves as a **transmission line** rather than a simple wire. The transition is roughly: edge rate rise time $t_r$ < 2× trace propagation delay. At a propagation speed of ~150 ps/inch in FR4, a 10 ns rise time trace shorter than ~33 inches is lumped; a 1 ns rise time trace longer than ~3.3 inches is distributed (transmission line). USB 3.0 (1 ns edges, 10 cm traces) is firmly in the high-speed regime.

**Characteristic impedance** of a PCB trace is determined by its geometry and the dielectric constant of the surrounding PCB material. For a microstrip (trace over a ground plane): $Z_0 \approx \frac{87}{\sqrt{\epsilon_r + 1.41}} \ln\left(\frac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ where $h$ is the dielectric thickness, $w$ is the trace width, $t$ is the trace thickness, and $\epsilon_r$ is the dielectric constant (FR4: ~4.2-4.5 at 1 GHz). USB requires 90Ω differential (45Ω single-ended); Ethernet requires 100Ω differential; PCIe requires 85Ω differential. KiCad's interactive router calculates the required trace width for a target impedance if you set the dielectric parameters. Impedance mismatch causes signal reflections: energy bounces back from the mismatch point, appearing as a voltage double-dip or overshoot on the signal, causing setup/hold violations.

**Differential pairs** (D+/D- in USB, LVDS, PCIe, Ethernet) carry complementary signals: D+ and D-. The receiver amplifies the *difference*, making the signal immune to common-mode noise (any noise that appears identically on both traces cancels out). For differential pairs to work correctly, the two traces must be routed in parallel (edge-to-edge spacing of 2-3× trace width for tight coupling), have exactly the same electrical length (length-matched to within a few hundred microns — even a 100 mil mismatch at GHz frequencies causes phase skew and eye diagram degradation), and maintain the target differential impedance (100Ω differential = each trace 50Ω single-ended over a solid ground reference plane).

**Via placement in high-speed design**: vias have parasitic inductance (~0.3-1 nH per via) that disrupts impedance and creates reflections. Minimize vias on high-speed signals; if layer changes are required, add **via-in-pad** (via in the center of a surface-mount pad, filled and capped by the fab) to keep trace length constant. Add **return current vias** (ground vias) adjacent to every signal via — the return current follows the signal current and must have a low-impedance path to follow the signal as it changes layers. A signal via without a nearby ground via forces return current to detour through the ground plane, creating a significant impedance discontinuity at GHz frequencies.

**Ground planes** are the most important single layout feature for high-speed and EMC. A continuous, uninterrupted ground plane provides: (1) a low-impedance return path for all signal currents, (2) a reference for impedance control, (3) shielding that reduces radiated emissions, (4) a thermal heat spreader. Never cut slots or routes through the ground plane under high-speed signals — any discontinuity in the plane forces return current to detour, creating a loop antenna and degrading signal integrity. Separate analog and digital ground planes, connect them at a single point (the star ground point, typically at the power supply) to prevent digital switching noise from coupling into analog ground.

resources.label: Phil's Lab — high-speed PCB design (YouTube series)

subtopics.title: EMC fundamentals - ground planes, filtering, ferrite beads, and regulatory testing
body: **EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility)** is the discipline of ensuring that a device neither emits electromagnetic interference that disturbs other devices (**emissions**) nor is disturbed by external interference (**immunity**). Regulatory certification (FCC in the US, CE in Europe, ICTCRR in India) is legally required for electronic products sold to consumers and requires passing EMC tests. A board that passes functional tests but fails EMC testing can be extremely costly to fix if major layout changes are needed after the first prototype.

**Radiated emissions** are RF energy radiated from the PCB as an antenna. The primary source is **differential-mode currents** (signal currents in loops formed by the signal trace and its return path) and **common-mode currents** (currents on cables and enclosure that all flow in the same direction). Minimize the area of high-current switching loops: place bypass capacitors as close to power pins as possible (small bypass loop area), use a solid ground plane (minimizes the area of the return current path beneath signal traces), and avoid letting fast edges drive cable connections directly (add series resistors 22-33Ω on signal lines to slow edges before they reach cables).

**Ferrite beads** are components placed in series on power or signal lines to attenuate high-frequency noise. A ferrite bead has very low DC resistance (acceptable for power) but high impedance at frequencies above its characteristic frequency (100 MHz-1 GHz, depending on the bead). Placed in series between the switching power supply output and the analog power domain, the ferrite bead blocks high-frequency switching ripple while passing DC cleanly. Ferrite beads are also used on USB power lines, Ethernet transformer midpoints, and common-mode chokes on differential pairs (a common-mode choke has high impedance to common-mode currents but low impedance to differential currents — exactly what differential pairs need).

**Shielding and filtering on connectors**: every cable leaving the enclosure is a potential antenna. USB, Ethernet, UART, and analog signal cables should exit through a connector that has: (1) a metal shell or shield tied directly to chassis ground at the PCB connector footprint, (2) TVS (Transient Voltage Suppression) diodes for ESD (IEC 61000-4-2 Class 4 requires surviving ±8 kV contact discharge), (3) common-mode choke on differential pairs, (4) series resistors on single-ended lines for edge-rate control. These filtering components should be placed at the connector, not inside the PCB — keeping noise outside the enclosure is simpler than keeping it from spreading inside.

**Pre-compliance testing**: before investing in a certified EMC test (which costs $5,000-$20,000 and requires a lab), run a pre-compliance test with a near-field probe and a spectrum analyzer (or low-cost SDR + EMC scanning software like EMCView). Near-field probes held above the PCB identify which components and traces are radiating — the MCU's SDIO clock, the switch-mode power supply, or the USB D+ line. Fix the highest emitters first (add ferrite beads, reduce switching speed, improve ground plane coverage). Pre-compliance testing is not a substitute for formal testing but dramatically reduces the number of test lab iterations needed.

resources.label: Würth Elektronik — EMC design guide (free PDF)

subtopics.title: JTAG/SWD debugging - DAP protocol, J-Link, breakpoints, and RTT logging
body: **JTAG (Joint Test Action Group, IEEE 1149.1)** is the standard for boundary-scan testing and debug access on integrated circuits. It uses 4-5 signals: **TDI** (test data in), **TDO** (test data out), **TCK** (test clock), **TMS** (test mode select), and optionally **TRST** (test reset). Instructions and data shift through a daisy-chainable scan chain. For Cortex-M debugging, JTAG gives access to the ARM **Debug Access Port (DAP)**, which provides the **AHB-AP (AHB Access Port)** — a bus master that can read/write any memory-mapped address on the AHB bus, including all peripheral registers, SRAM, and flash. This is how a debugger sets breakpoints (writes to flash/FPGA BRAM) and reads memory without the CPU knowing.

**SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** is ARM's two-wire alternative to JTAG: only **SWDIO** (bidirectional data) and **SWDCLK** (clock). The same ARM DAP is accessible; the protocol is different but the capability is equivalent. SWD is the standard for Cortex-M debugging — all STM32, nRF52, and other Cortex-M debuggers use SWD. It uses only 2 pins instead of JTAG's 5, which matters on pin-constrained designs. SWD does not support the JTAG boundary scan functionality, so if you need in-circuit board testing (ICT), you need JTAG on the board even if you debug over SWD.

**Hardware debug probes**: J-Link (SEGGER) is the standard; the J-Link EDU version (~$60) is full-featured for non-commercial use. ST-Link (ST's own probe, included on Nucleo/Discovery boards) is free with ST MCUs. OpenOCD (open-source debug server) supports dozens of probes and targets. The debug probe connects to the host via USB and presents a GDB server interface; your IDE (VS Code + Cortex-Debug extension, CLion, IAR) connects to the GDB server and provides the debug UI.

**Breakpoints**: hardware breakpoints use the **FPB (Flash Patch and Breakpoint)** unit (Cortex-M3/M4 has 6 hardware breakpoints). When the CPU fetches an instruction at a hardware breakpoint address, it halts before executing, giving the debugger control. Software breakpoints insert a `BKPT` instruction directly into code in RAM (or flash with a patched copy) — unlimited but cannot be set in read-only memory or used in ISR contexts reliably. **Watchpoints** (using the **DWT** — Data Watchpoint and Trace unit) break on data accesses to a specific address — invaluable for finding which code corrupted a specific variable.

**SWO and RTT for logging**: `printf` via UART requires a dedicated UART pin and is too slow for tight loops. **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** is a single-pin output that uses the ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell) to stream `printf`-style trace data at 1-12 Mbps — much faster than UART and viewable in real time in J-Link RTT Viewer or OpenOCD + itm2console. **RTT (Real-Time Transfer)** (SEGGER proprietary but widely supported) is even better: it uses a shared ring buffer in SRAM; the probe reads via the DAP bus without interrupting the CPU. Throughput up to 1 MB/s, zero CPU overhead (the probe reads while the CPU runs), and usable in ISRs and HardFault handlers where ITM is unreliable. RTT is the preferred debug logging method for production embedded firmware development.

connections.note: PCB design is where the GPIO/protocol theory becomes copper

topics.title: Threshold, MPC & FHE
body: **Shamir's Secret Sharing** (polynomial interpolation, (t,n)-threshold), **threshold signatures** (FROST, GG18/20), **multi-party computation** (Yao's garbled circuits, SPDZ + Beaver triples, ORAM), and **homomorphic encryption** (Paillier additive; FHE: BFV/BGV/CKKS/TFHE) - computing on encrypted data, at a $10^6\text{-}10^9\times$ cost.
These power private ML inference, MPC wallets, and on-chain FHE — the privacy frontier.

subtopics.title: Shamir's Secret Sharing — polynomial interpolation and threshold schemes
body: **Shamir's Secret Sharing (SSS)**, introduced by Adi Shamir in 1979, is an elegant scheme for distributing a secret $s$ among $n$ parties such that any $t$ parties can reconstruct it but no $t-1$ parties learn anything about $s$. The construction is based on the fundamental theorem of algebra: a polynomial of degree $t-1$ is uniquely determined by $t$ points, but under-determined by any fewer. This gives a simple recipe for a $(t, n)$-threshold scheme.

**Share generation**: choose a random polynomial $f(x) = s + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \ldots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1}$ over $\mathrm{GF}(q)$ (a prime field, $q > n$) with the secret as the constant term ($f(0) = s$). Distribute share $i$ as $(i, f(i))$ - the evaluation of the polynomial at distinct non-zero points $x = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. **Reconstruction**: given $t$ shares $\{(x_j, y_j)\}$, apply **Lagrange interpolation**: $f(x) = \sum_{j} y_j \cdot \prod_{k \ne j} (x - x_k) / (x_j - x_k)$. Evaluating at $x = 0$ gives the secret: $s = f(0) = \sum_j y_j \cdot L_j(0)$ where $L_j(0) = \prod_{k \ne j} (0 - x_k) / (x_j - x_k)$ are the Lagrange basis coefficients, computable from the point indices alone. Reconstruction is $O(t^2)$ field operations.

**Information-theoretic security**: no coalition of $t-1$ or fewer shareholders learns *anything* about $s$ - not even a single bit of information. This is a perfect secrecy property, stronger than computational security: even an adversary with infinite computing power cannot determine $s$ from $t-1$ shares, because for any candidate secret $s'$, there is a valid polynomial with $t-1$ points that has $f(0) = s'$. The $t-1$ points are compatible with all possible secrets equally.

**Verifiable Secret Sharing (VSS)** extends SSS to the case where the dealer might be malicious. In **Feldman VSS**, the dealer publishes commitments $g^{a_0}, g^{a_1}, \ldots, g^{a_{t-1}}$ (in a group where DLP is hard). Each shareholder can verify their share $(i, f(i))$ satisfies $g^{f(i)} = \prod_j (g^{a_j})^{i^j}$ - a consistency check that catches cheating dealers without revealing the secret. **Pedersen VSS** extends this with computationally-hiding commitments, allowing the dealer to verifiably distribute shares without the verification exposing information to passive adversaries. VSS is a foundational building block for threshold signatures and distributed key generation (DKG) protocols.

Applications: SSS is used in hardware security modules (HSMs) for key backup — an HSM's master key is split among 5 executives requiring any 3 to recover. Cryptocurrency wallet recovery schemes (Ledger Recover) distribute wallet seed phrases as Shamir shares to custodians. **Distributed Key Generation (DKG)** builds on SSS to generate a joint public key for threshold signatures without any single party knowing the full private key — each party contributes a random polynomial, and the joint secret is the sum of all constant terms. Ethereum validator key distribution and MPC wallet protocols are production examples.

resources.label: Shamir 1979 — How to Share a Secret (original 3-page paper)

subtopics.title: Threshold signatures — FROST and GG18/20
body: A **threshold signature scheme** allows any $t$ of $n$ parties to jointly produce a signature without any single party knowing the full private key. The signature verifies under the joint public key — indistinguishable from a standard single-party signature. This is strictly stronger than a secret-sharing-based scheme where parties assemble the key in memory to sign — there, any one party's compromise at signing time leaks the key. In a proper threshold scheme, even the fully reconstructed signing operation is never performed by a single party.

**ECDSA threshold signatures** are technically challenging because ECDSA's non-linearity (the $k^{-1}$ in the signature computation) requires MPC for the inversion step. **GG18** (Gennaro, Goldfeder 2018) and **GG20** are the most widely deployed: they use Paillier homomorphic encryption (see FHE subtopic) to enable parties to jointly compute the ECDSA signature without any party learning the nonce or the other parties' key shares. The protocol requires multiple rounds of communication and is computationally expensive (Paillier operations are slow) but correct and secure under standard assumptions. GG18/20 are deployed in **Fireblocks**, **Web3Auth**, and other institutional MPC wallet products managing billions in digital assets.

**FROST (Flexible Round-Optimized Schnorr Threshold Signatures)**, by Komlo and Goldberg (2020), is a simpler and more efficient threshold signature scheme for Schnorr signatures (and therefore directly applicable to Taproot/BIP-340 Bitcoin signatures and any Ed25519-compatible context). FROST is a 2-round protocol (preprocessing + signing) with concurrently secure signing — multiple signing sessions can occur simultaneously without compromising security, which was a significant open problem for Schnorr threshold schemes. FROST uses Shamir secret sharing for the key shares and a specific nonce generation protocol (multiplicative shares of the nonce's DL, combined via Lagrange interpolation) to maintain the Schnorr security properties.

**Distributed Key Generation (DKG)** is the setup protocol for threshold signatures. Each party runs a VSS of a random polynomial, broadcasts commitments, and the joint public key is computed from the sum of the constant terms' group elements. No trusted dealer is needed — the DKG is executed once at wallet creation. The most efficient DKG for FROST is **Pedersen DKG** (multiple simultaneous Feldman VSS instances). The critical security property: even if $t-1$ parties collude, they cannot determine the full private key (information-theoretic from SSS) nor generate a signature without the cooperation of the $t$-th party.

Production deployment considerations: MPC threshold wallets have significantly different operational complexity than standard hardware wallets. Key ceremonies (DKG) require all $n$ parties to be online simultaneously. Signing requires $t$ parties to complete the protocol, potentially requiring network connectivity and availability from multiple parties (which must be operationally reliable for a wallet that needs to sign transactions on demand). **Threshold signature schemes do not rotate key shares** automatically — if a party's device is compromised, the shares must be refreshed (proactive secret sharing), which requires another ceremony. Despite these complexities, institutional clients prefer MPC wallets because they eliminate single points of failure: no single device or person holds the full private key.

resources.label: GG20 — One Round Threshold ECDSA (Gennaro, Goldfeder)

subtopics.title: Yao's garbled circuits and general MPC
body: **Multi-Party Computation (MPC)** is the general problem: $n$ parties, each holding a private input $x_i$, wish to jointly compute a function $f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ such that each party learns the output but no party learns anything about the others' inputs beyond what the output reveals. The security definition is formalized by the **ideal/real paradigm**: the real-world protocol is secure if whatever an adversary learns in the real protocol could have been simulated by the adversary in the ideal model (where a trusted third party computes f directly). If no additional information is leaked in the real world versus the ideal, the protocol is private.

**Yao's garbled circuits** (Andrew Yao, 1986) was the first general MPC construction, for the 2-party case. The idea: represent $f$ as a Boolean circuit; party P1 (the 'garbler') randomly encodes ('garbles') each gate's truth table by encrypting each output wire label with the two input wire labels. P1 sends the garbled circuit to P2 (the 'evaluator'). P1 also sends the input wire labels for P1's input. P2 uses **1-out-of-2 Oblivious Transfer (OT)** to obtain the wire labels corresponding to P2's input (without P1 learning which input was chosen, and P2 learning only the label, not the other one). P2 evaluates the garbled circuit gate-by-gate (decrypting the correct output label at each gate) and obtains the output.

Security: P2 learns only the output labels (not intermediate values) because each gate encrypts output values under both input wire labels simultaneously — without knowing both labels for a gate, P2 cannot evaluate the wrong row. P1 learns nothing because P2 evaluates locally without communicating intermediate results. The garbling is done with AES: each gate has a 4-row truth table; P1 encrypts the output label for each row under the two input labels using a hash or AES with both labels as keys. **Free XOR** (Kolesnikov, Schneider 2008) optimizes XOR gates to zero cost by choosing wire labels with a global XOR offset — a major practical improvement. Modern garbling schemes (e.g. Half Gates by Zahur et al.) reduce the garbled table size from 4 to 2 ciphertexts per gate.

**Oblivious Transfer (OT)** is the fundamental primitive for MPC: in a 1-of-2 OT, the sender has two values $(m_0, m_1)$ and the receiver has a bit $b$; the receiver obtains $m_b$ without the sender learning $b$, and without the receiver learning $m_{1-b}$. OT was shown by Kilian (1988) to imply all of MPC (OT-complete). Efficient OT is achieved via the **OT extension** of Ishai, Kilian, Nissim, and Petrank (2003): starting from a small number of 'base OTs' (using public-key OT which is expensive), generate arbitrarily many OTs cheaply using only symmetric operations (pseudorandom generators). This reduces OT to near-free at scale — modern OT extension processes millions of OTs per second. OT is the workhorse of all scalable MPC.

**Multi-party semi-honest vs. malicious security**: Yao's GC and most simple MPC constructions are secure against **semi-honest** adversaries (who follow the protocol honestly but try to learn from transcripts). A **malicious** adversary can deviate from the protocol. Extending GC to malicious security requires additional cryptographic machinery: **cut-and-choose** (generate multiple garbled circuits, reveal half for verification, use the others for evaluation — catching cheaters at the cost of replication factor), or **authenticated garbling** (MAC-based guarantees that gate evaluations are consistent). The distinction matters enormously in practice — most deployed MPC systems target malicious security for high-value applications.

resources.label: A Pragmatic Introduction to Secure Multi-Party Computation (free book)

subtopics.title: SPDZ and Beaver triples for arithmetic MPC
body: Garbled circuits are efficient for Boolean functions but expensive for arithmetic operations (addition and multiplication over large fields are decomposed into many gates). **Arithmetic MPC** works directly with secret-shared values over finite fields, enabling efficient addition and multiplication at field size. The dominant paradigm is the **SPDZ protocol** (Damgård, Pastro, Smart, Zakarias, 2011-2012) and its successors (SPDZᵢ, MASCOT, SCALE-MAMBA, MP-SPDZ), which provide malicious-secure arithmetic MPC in the preprocessing model.

**Additive secret sharing** over a field: a value $v$ is secret-shared as $n$ random values $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in F$ with $v_1 + \ldots + v_n = v$. Each party holds one share. Addition of two shared values $[a] + [b]$ is performed locally: each party adds their shares $a_i + b_i$ (the sum of the shares equals $a + b$). Multiplication is the hard part: $[a] \times [b]$ requires parties to compute $[ab]$ from $[a]$ and $[b]$, but naively this would require communication proportional to $n^2$.

**Beaver triples** (Donald Beaver, 1992) make multiplication efficient using a preprocessing step. Before the computation, during a preprocessing/offline phase, parties generate a random **multiplication triple** $([a], [b], [c])$ where $c = ab$ and all three values are random (the parties themselves do not know $a$, $b$, or $c$ individually). During the computation (online phase), to multiply $[x] \times [y]$: compute $[\varepsilon] = [x] - [a]$ and $[\delta] = [y] - [b]$ (both done locally via addition), reveal $\varepsilon$ and $\delta$ to all parties (these are random-looking due to the masking by $a$ and $b$), then compute $[xy] = [c] + \delta[a] + \varepsilon[b] + \varepsilon\delta$. This uses only 2 rounds of communication per multiplication, regardless of the number of parties.

The preprocessing phase (generating Beaver triples) dominates the cost. SPDZ generates triples using **oblivious transfer** (for 2-party) or **homomorphic encryption** (for n-party). The **MASCOT protocol** uses OT extension to generate triples at millions per second on modern hardware. **SCALE-MAMBA** and **MP-SPDZ** are production frameworks that implement the full SPDZ pipeline. Typical performance: on a LAN, SPDZ can evaluate millions of multiplications per second (using the pre-generated triples). On a WAN with cross-continental latency (~100ms RTT), the round-trip communication limits multiplication rate to thousands per second — network latency is the dominant bottleneck.

**SPDZ for ML inference**: privacy-preserving machine learning using SPDZ works by encoding the model weights as secret shares (one party holds the model, another the data), performing a forward pass of the neural network using SPDZ's arithmetic operations (matrix multiplications as batched Beaver-triple multiplications, ReLU activations using garbled circuits for the comparison), and outputting the classification without either party seeing the other's input. Commercial services offering 'private inference' (e.g. Duality Technologies, Inpher) use SPDZ-based frameworks. The practical cost is ~1000-10000× overhead versus plaintext inference, making it suitable only for high-value, low-frequency computations (e.g. medical diagnosis, financial scoring).

resources.label: MP-SPDZ — flexible MPC framework (open source, with 30+ protocols)

subtopics.title: Homomorphic encryption — Paillier, BFV, BGV, CKKS, and TFHE
body: **Homomorphic encryption** allows computation on encrypted data without decrypting it. Given ciphertexts $\mathrm{Enc}(a)$ and $\mathrm{Enc}(b)$, a homomorphic scheme can compute $\mathrm{Enc}(a \mathbin{op} b)$ for some operation `op`, without knowing $a$, $b$, or the result. The holy grail is **Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** — computing *arbitrary* functions on ciphertexts — which was long believed impossible and was shown constructable by Craig Gentry in 2009.

**Paillier encryption** (Pascal Paillier, 1999) is **additively homomorphic**: given $\mathrm{Enc}(a)$ and $\mathrm{Enc}(b)$, anyone can compute $\mathrm{Enc}(a + b) = \mathrm{Enc}(a) \times \mathrm{Enc}(b)$ (ciphertext multiplication corresponds to plaintext addition). The scheme is based on composite residuosity in $\mathbb{Z}_{n^2}^*$: public key $n = pq$, encryption $\mathrm{Enc}(m) = g^m r^n \bmod n^2$ for random $r$, decryption via Carmichael's theorem on $\mathbb{Z}_{n^2}^*$. The multiplication-corresponds-to-addition property follows from the group homomorphism $g^m r^n \cdot g^{m'} r'^n = g^{m+m'} (rr')^n$. Paillier is used in voting protocols (tallying encrypted votes without decryption), threshold ECDSA (GG18/20), and federated learning (encrypted gradient aggregation). It does not support multiplication of plaintexts (no XOR/AND), so it is not FHE.

**BGV** (Brakerski-Gentry-Vaikuntanathan, 2011) and **BFV** (Brakerski, Fan-Vercauteren, 2012) are the first practically efficient **FHE schemes** for integer arithmetic. Both are based on RLWE and support both addition and multiplication on ciphertexts, where plaintexts are integers modulo a parameter $t$. The fundamental challenge of FHE: every ciphertext carries **noise** (inherent from the LWE structure), and multiplication multiplies the noise levels. After $\sim O(\log \lambda)$ multiplications, the noise exceeds the threshold for correct decryption — the ciphertext becomes undecryptable. **Bootstrapping** (Gentry's technique) reduces noise by homomorphically decrypting the ciphertext: apply the decryption circuit to the ciphertext, with the secret key encrypted, to produce a 'fresh' ciphertext encoding the same plaintext with reduced noise. Bootstrapping is the technical miracle that enables unbounded computation — but it adds significant overhead (milliseconds to seconds per bootstrapping operation).

**CKKS** (Cheon-Kim-Kim-Song, 2017) is the dominant FHE scheme for machine learning applications. Unlike BGV/BFV (which encrypt exact integers), CKKS encrypts and operates on **approximate floating-point numbers** — accepting small errors in computation results in exchange for massively improved efficiency. CKKS's plaintext space is complex numbers, and packing SIMD-style allows operating on $\sim n/2$ complex values simultaneously in one ciphertext (for polynomial ring degree $n$). Matrix-vector multiplications (the core operation of neural networks) can be performed on packed CKKS ciphertexts using a 'diagonal matrix rotation' technique. CKKS can evaluate a full ResNet-20 inference in ~2 minutes on a CPU - about $10^6\times$ slower than plaintext but feasible for high-stakes batch inference.

**TFHE** (Chillotti-Gama-Georgieva-Izabachène, 2016) is optimized for **Boolean circuit evaluation** and supports fast bootstrapping (~1-10ms per gate, vs. seconds for BGV). TFHE encrypts one bit at a time and operates bitwise — each NAND/AND/OR gate requires one bootstrapping operation. This makes TFHE efficient for arbitrary boolean circuits (routing, control flow, comparisons, byte-level operations) but slow for integer arithmetic (128 serial gates to add two bytes). The applications: **computing arbitrary programs on encrypted data** where the program has complex branching logic (e.g. SQL queries on encrypted databases, encrypted OS execution). Zama.ai's **fhEVM** uses TFHE to execute Solidity smart contracts on encrypted state — a specific use case for on-chain privacy where smart contract logic runs on encrypted inputs without the blockchain knowing the transaction details.

resources.label: FHE.org — resources, tutorials, and community for homomorphic encryption
OpenFHE — open-source FHE library (BGV, BFV, CKKS, TFHE)

subtopics.title: ORAM — Oblivious RAM and access pattern hiding
body: **Oblivious RAM (ORAM)**, introduced by Goldreich and Ostrovsky (1996), addresses a subtle but critical privacy problem: even if the data in memory (or on a server) is encrypted, the *pattern of memory accesses* can leak information. An observer who sees which memory addresses are accessed, and in what order, can learn the data's structure, what computations are being performed, or what inputs triggered the computation — without ever decrypting the data. ORAM provides a protocol for accessing encrypted memory such that the sequence of addresses accessed by the client appears uniformly random to any observer, regardless of the actual access pattern.

Concrete threat: a client stores an encrypted database on an untrusted server. The server can see which records are retrieved (even if not their contents). If record 42 is always retrieved when the user checks their health score, and the server knows that record 42 corresponds to 'HIV status', the access pattern reveals sensitive information without breaking encryption. ORAM ensures that even the server hosting the encrypted database cannot distinguish whether the client accessed record 42, record 7, or any other record. The price is **$O(\log^2 N)$ bandwidth overhead** for accessing $N$ items: the client performs a 'shuffle' of the database periodically, mixing accessed and dummy elements to hide which were real.

**Path ORAM** (Stefanov et al., 2013) is the practical standard. Data is stored in a binary tree of $N/Z$ buckets ($Z$ a small constant). Each block is randomly mapped to a 'path' from root to a leaf, and each bucket contains encrypted blocks from multiple paths (up to $Z$). A read or write operation: (1) map the block to its current leaf path; (2) fetch all buckets on the root-to-leaf path; (3) re-encrypt and rescatter blocks back to random positions further up the tree. The amortized bandwidth is $O(\log N)$ server accesses per client access. Path ORAM is used in trusted execution environments (Intel SGX, AMD SEV) to prevent memory access side-channels from leaking computation internals to untrusted hypervisors.

**Ring ORAM** and **Circuit ORAM** further improve constants, bringing Path ORAM's practical overhead from ~10× to ~3-5× versus naive access. **Oblivious data structures** (oblivious hash tables, oblivious priority queues) apply ORAM ideas to specific data structures for tighter bounds. The combination of ORAM + encryption provides the full model of **oblivious computation**: the server sees only a random-looking sequence of encrypted accesses, regardless of the data or computation. This is the model required for **secure enclaves** (SGX) performing private computations on sensitive data stored by an untrusted cloud provider.

ORAM's connection to MPC: ORAM is a key building block for **RAM-model MPC** (secure computation over programs with random memory access, like sorting, graph algorithms, database queries). Without ORAM, secure programs must be expressed as circuits (fixing access patterns at compile time), which is exponentially expensive for data-dependent access. With ORAM, a secure program can make arbitrary memory accesses and still hide the access pattern from all parties. **Oblivious Machine Learning** (secure inference where the model and the query are both private) combines ORAM with arithmetic MPC or FHE — an active research frontier with direct applications in privacy-preserving AI.

resources.label: Path ORAM — Stefanov et al. (practical ORAM for cloud storage)
A Pragmatic Introduction to Secure Multi-Party Computation — ch. 6 (ORAM)
A Pragmatic Introduction to Secure MPC (free)

connections.note: Threshold sigs and on-chain FHE (fhEVM) extend privacy to blockchains
FHE enables private ML inference on encrypted inputs

topics.title: Symmetric crypto, hashing & MACs
body: **Kerckhoffs's principle**, AES as a substitution-permutation network, **block-cipher modes** (ECB's penguin failure, CBC + padding oracles, CTR, **GCM/AEAD**), hash functions (SHA-256 Merkle-Damgård, SHA-3 Keccak sponge, BLAKE3) and their three resistance properties, **MACs** (HMAC - why not $H(\mathrm{key} \mathbin{\Vert} \mathrm{msg})$ due to length extension), and password hashing (bcrypt/scrypt/**Argon2id**).
Cryptopals Sets 1-2 — implement these *and* break them — is the fastest route to real understanding.

subtopics.title: Block ciphers and the AES substitution-permutation network
body: A **block cipher** takes a fixed-length block of plaintext (128 bits for AES) and a key, and produces a ciphertext block of the same length. The cipher must be a **bijection** for each key — every plaintext maps to a unique ciphertext, and vice versa — so that decryption exists. The key is the only secret; per **Kerckhoffs's principle**, the algorithm itself must be assumed public. This is not a concession but a design discipline: a cipher that relies on secrecy of the algorithm is weaker (once the algorithm leaks, everything encrypted with it is broken) and harder to audit. Any cipher you cannot publish in a journal is not a cipher you should trust.

AES (Advanced Encryption Standard, standardized by NIST in 2001 after the Rijndael submission won an open competition) is a **substitution-permutation network (SPN)**. The core intuition is that security comes from iterating two complementary operations many times: **substitution** (non-linear mixing that destroys algebraic structure) and **permutation** (linear diffusion that spreads influence across all bits). Neither alone is secure — substitution without diffusion leaks positional statistics; diffusion without non-linearity is linear algebra and trivially invertible. Alternating the two, many rounds, gives a design where every output bit depends on every input bit in a complex, non-linear way.

Concretely, AES operates on a **4×4 byte state array** (16 bytes = 128-bit block). Each of 10/12/14 rounds (for 128/192/256-bit keys) applies four transformations: **SubBytes** replaces each byte through a fixed 8-bit S-box (the $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$ multiplicative inverse, chosen for maximally bad differential/linear properties from an attacker's view); **ShiftRows** rotates each row of the state by 0, 1, 2, 3 bytes cyclically (ensuring bytes from different columns interact in subsequent rounds); **MixColumns** multiplies each column by a fixed matrix over $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$ (providing diffusion — each output byte depends on all four input bytes of the column); and **AddRoundKey** XORs the state with a 128-bit subkey derived from the key schedule. The final round omits MixColumns.

The **key schedule** expands a 128/192/256-bit master key into 11/13/15 round keys, also using SubBytes and XOR operations, so the round keys have similar mixing properties. AES has no known practical attack faster than brute force — the best known attack on AES-128 reduces the work from $2^{128}$ to roughly $2^{126}$ (a marginal improvement with no practical consequence). What breaks AES in practice is always the **mode of operation** or the **implementation** (timing leaks, nonce reuse), not the round function itself. The S-box, the diffusion layer, and the round count were designed with differential and linear cryptanalysis in mind — and that rigor has held for 25 years.

One nuance worth internalizing: a block cipher alone encrypts exactly one block and is **deterministic** — encrypting the same plaintext with the same key always gives the same ciphertext. This is useless for most real purposes, where messages are longer than 16 bytes and you need the encryption to look different each time. That is what modes of operation fix. The block cipher is a keyed pseudo-random permutation; the mode builds a useful encryption scheme on top.

resources.label: NIST FIPS 197 — AES standard (the exact specification)

subtopics.title: Block cipher modes — ECB failure, CBC, CTR, and AEAD
body: A **mode of operation** wraps a block cipher to encrypt arbitrary-length messages. The choice of mode is where most symmetric-crypto disasters happen in practice. The mode determines whether the same plaintext block encrypts to the same ciphertext, whether a single bit-flip in ciphertext corrupts just one block or cascades, and — most importantly — whether the encryption is merely **confidential** or also **authenticated**.

**ECB (Electronic Codebook)** simply encrypts each block independently: $C_i = E_k(P_i)$. This is catastrophically broken for any structured data. Because the same plaintext block always maps to the same ciphertext block under the same key, patterns are preserved. The canonical demonstration is the 'ECB penguin': encrypting a bitmap of the Linux penguin Tux with AES-ECB produces a ciphertext that is still recognizably a penguin in outline form, because the background regions (uniform color → repeated blocks) all encrypt identically. For any realistic message — HTTP headers, protocol framing, JPEG thumbnails, structured file formats — ECB leaks structural information. Never use ECB for anything.

**CBC (Cipher Block Chaining)** fixes ECB's determinism by XORing each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block before encryption: $C_i = E_k(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$, starting with a random **initialization vector (IV)** for $C_0$. The same plaintext block at different positions or with different IVs now encrypts differently. But CBC introduces a subtle and brutally exploited weakness: **padding oracle attacks**. Because a block cipher requires exactly 16-byte blocks, the last plaintext block must be padded (typically with PKCS#7: N bytes each with value N). If the decryption endpoint tells you whether decryption produced valid padding — even by a different error message, a timing difference, or a different HTTP status code — an attacker can decrypt arbitrary ciphertext by iteratively querying the oracle, 16 bytes at a time, without knowing the key. This is the **padding oracle attack**, and it has been found in real systems: Lucky13 (TLS), the original POODLE attack, and numerous web application frameworks. CBC also requires an unpredictable (random) IV and is **not authenticated** by default — an attacker can flip bits in a ciphertext block to make predictable changes in the next plaintext block (CBC is malleable).

**CTR (Counter Mode)** turns the block cipher into a **stream cipher**: generate a keystream by encrypting a sequence of counters `(nonce ‖ counter)` and XOR the keystream with plaintext. $C_i = P_i \oplus E_k(\mathrm{nonce} \mathbin{\Vert} i)$. CTR requires no padding (it just truncates the last keystream block), is parallelizable (each block of keystream is independent), and admits random access (to decrypt block i, just compute one block of keystream). The catastrophic weakness: if the *same* (key, nonce) pair is ever used twice, the XOR of the two ciphertexts equals the XOR of the two plaintexts — and if one plaintext is known, the other is immediately recovered. **Nonce reuse is fatal for CTR**, and this same principle applies to every stream cipher and GCM mode. Many nonce-reuse disasters have occurred in the real world: RC4's RC4-biases (WEP), AES-CTR nonce reuse in TLS before explicit IVs, and various VPN protocol mistakes.
