High-beta stocks do not earn higher returns than low-beta stocks - the security market line is much flatter than CAPM predicts, and over some periods even negatively sloped. This is the **beta anomaly** (also called the low-volatility anomaly): low-beta stocks earn higher risk-adjusted returns, the opposite of what CAPM says.

**Why CAPM fails**: its assumptions are violated in practice. (1) Investors are not mean-variance optimizers with homogeneous beliefs. (2) Leverage constraints (many investors cannot borrow freely at the risk-free rate) push them toward high-beta assets, bidding up their prices and reducing their returns. (3) Taxes, transaction costs, and short-sale constraints prevent the no-arbitrage equilibrium from holding. (4) CAPM is a single-period model; real investment is multi-period with changing opportunity sets. The failure of CAPM in empirical tests directly motivated the factor model revolution: if beta alone cannot price assets, what other characteristics explain returns?

Despite its empirical failures, CAPM remains indispensable for three reasons. (1) **Beta is a useful risk measure** even if the linear CAPM relation does not hold exactly - a stock with $\beta = 2$ will approximately double the market's return swings, which matters for risk management. (2) **CAPM is the null hypothesis** against which all factor models are tested. (3) **Beta decomposition** (market-adjusted returns, beta-hedged portfolios, long-short portfolios designed to be market-neutral) is the building block of every equity long-short hedge fund strategy. Understanding CAPM thoroughly is required to understand why and how Fama-French improves on it.

resources.label: Fama and French (1992) — The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns (paper)
Kenneth French — data library, factor portfolios

subtopics.title: Fama-French 3 and 5-factor models
body: Fama and French (1993) extended CAPM with two additional factors motivated by persistent cross-sectional anomalies: the **size effect** (small-cap stocks earn higher returns than predicted by CAPM) and the **value effect** (stocks with high book-to-market ratio earn higher returns). The 3-factor model: $r_i - r_f = \alpha + \beta_m (r_m - r_f) + \beta_{SMB} \cdot SMB + \beta_{HML} \cdot HML + \epsilon$, where **SMB (Small Minus Big)** is the return spread between small-cap and large-cap portfolios, and **HML (High Minus Low)** is the return spread between high book-to-market (value) and low book-to-market (growth) portfolios.

**SMB construction**: each June, rank all US stocks by market cap. The 'small' portfolio is the bottom 50% by market cap; the 'big' portfolio is the top 50%. Then intersect with book-to-market terciles (low, middle, high) to create 6 portfolios (2 size × 3 value). SMB = average return of the 3 small portfolios minus average return of the 3 big portfolios. This construction ensures SMB is orthogonal to the value effect. **HML construction**: the 'high' portfolio is the top 30% of book-to-market stocks; the 'low' portfolio is the bottom 30%. HML = average return of the 2 high B/M portfolios minus the 2 low B/M portfolios.

The Fama-French 3-factor model dramatically reduced the anomalies that CAPM left unexplained. A pure value portfolio looks like it has positive CAPM alpha, but its 3-factor alpha is near zero - the value 'anomaly' is mostly explained by HML loading. However, the 3-factor model still leaves unexplained anomalies: profitability (profitable firms earn higher returns) and investment (conservative capital investment predicts higher returns). Fama and French (2015) extended to the **5-factor model** with **RMW (Robust Minus Weak)** profitability factor and **CMA (Conservative Minus Aggressive)** investment factor.

**Carhart (1997)** added a **momentum factor (WML: Winners Minus Losers)** to create the 4-factor model, responding to Jegadeesh and Titman's finding that 12-1 month momentum predicts returns. The 4-factor model is the industry standard for **performance attribution**: decompose a portfolio's return into market beta, size tilt, value tilt, momentum tilt, and residual alpha. A manager claiming alpha after removing factor tilts is demonstrating genuine skill; a manager claiming alpha that disappears after factor adjustment is just charging for beta exposure.

**Why do these factors exist?** Two competing explanations: (1) **Risk-based** - HML is compensation for distress risk (value firms have high book-to-market because the market discounts them heavily due to high risk of financial distress; investors demand a premium). SMB is compensation for illiquidity risk of small caps. (2) **Behavioral** - investors systematically over-extrapolate recent growth trends (overpaying for glamour growth stocks, undervaluing boring value stocks), and arbitrageurs cannot fully correct this because of limits to arbitrage. Both explanations have supporting evidence. The practical implication: factor premia may compress or disappear if too much capital chases them, or if the risk they compensate is diversified away.

resources.label: Fama and French (2015) — A five-factor asset pricing model (paper)

subtopics.title: Factor construction: sorted portfolios, IC, and decay
body: A **factor** in practice is a cross-sectional signal that predicts the next-period return distribution. Constructing a factor properly involves: (1) computing the signal across the investable universe at a point-in-time, (2) ranking or scoring all assets, (3) forming long and short portfolios, (4) evaluating the signal's predictive power statistically, and (5) measuring how quickly the predictive power decays. Each step has pitfalls.

**Quantile portfolio construction**: rank all stocks by the factor signal and divide into N quantiles (typically 5 or 10 deciles). Form equal-weighted or cap-weighted long-short portfolios: long the top decile, short the bottom decile. The factor return is the spread between these portfolios. A factor is economically meaningful if this spread is consistently positive after transaction costs across multiple market environments and geographies. The **monotonicity of quintile returns** is a useful sanity check: factor returns should be monotonically increasing from the worst quintile to the best, not just the top vs bottom.

The **Information Coefficient (IC)** is the Pearson correlation between the factor signal at time $t$ and the actual realized return over $[t, t+h]$: $IC_t = \text{Corr}(\text{signal}_t, r_{t \to t+h})$. IC ranges from -1 to 1; 0 means no predictive power. The **IC mean** (average over time) measures the average signal quality; the **IC standard deviation** measures consistency; the **Information Ratio** $IR = IC_{mean} / IC_{std}$ measures risk-adjusted quality. In practice, a mean IC of 0.03-0.05 is considered good for daily signals; a mean IC of 0.10+ is excellent. These seem small, but compound over many positions and many periods into significant alpha.

**IC decay** measures how quickly a signal's predictive power diminishes with the forecast horizon. Compute IC at horizons $h = 1, 2, 5, 10, 20$ days and plot. A signal with fast IC decay (near zero by day 5) must be traded frequently to capture its value; one with slow decay (IC still significant at day 20) can be traded less often with lower turnover. IC decay curves vary dramatically by signal type: short-term reversal signals decay in 1-2 days; earnings surprise signals decay over weeks; fundamental value signals decay over months. Matching your trading frequency to your signal's IC decay curve is the core of implementation efficiency.

**Factor orthogonalization and neutralization**: raw factors are often correlated. A momentum signal is correlated with growth (both favor recent winners, which tend to be growth stocks) and anticorrelated with value. **Orthogonalizing** a factor (regressing it on existing known factors and using the residual) measures the incremental predictive power after removing known effects. **Sector and market-cap neutralization** (computing factor z-scores within each GICS sector and size bucket) prevents the factor from simply loading on sector bets. A properly neutralized factor portfolio has near-zero beta, near-zero sector tilts, and near-zero size tilts - only the targeted characteristic varies across the portfolio.

resources.label: Active Portfolio Management — Grinold & Kahn (ch 6, information coefficient)

subtopics.title: Barra-style risk models and PCA
body: A **risk model** decomposes asset return covariance into structured (factor) and unstructured (idiosyncratic) components: $\Sigma = B F B' + D$, where $B$ is the $N \times K$ matrix of factor exposures (betas), $F$ is the $K \times K$ factor covariance matrix, and $D$ is a diagonal matrix of idiosyncratic variances. This decomposition matters because a full $N \times N$ covariance matrix for $N = 3000$ stocks requires $3000 \times 3001 / 2 = 4.5M$ parameters - far more than the 252 daily observations in a year can reliably estimate. The factor model compresses this to $K \times N$ exposures and $K \times K$ factor covariance, where $K$ is typically 20-100.

**Barra (now MSCI Barra)** is the commercial standard for equity risk models. Barra USE4 (United States model version 4) uses ~60 factors: 11 industry factors (GICS sectors) and ~50 style factors (market sensitivity, medium-term momentum, short-term reversal, size, value, earnings yield, volatility, leverage, liquidity). Each stock's factor exposures are updated monthly from fundamental data. Barra's proprietary covariance estimation uses Newey-West adjustments, EWM (exponentially weighted moments) for recency, and eigenvector deflation to handle small-sample instability.

**PCA-based risk models** are the quant's homegrown alternative. Run PCA on the historical return covariance matrix of the stock universe. The first principal component (PC1) typically captures the market (the factor all stocks load on); PC2 and PC3 often capture sector or style tilts. Using the top $K$ principal components as factors: $r_i = \sum_{k=1}^K \beta_{ik} f_k + \epsilon_i$. PCA factors are orthogonal by construction and explain the maximum variance with the minimum number of factors. The tradeoff: PCA factors are statistical artifacts with no direct economic interpretation (unlike Barra's labeled style factors), which makes them harder to debug and communicate.

The **fundamental law of active management** (Grinold, 1989) relates expected IR to factor model inputs: $IR \approx IC \sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the number of independent investment decisions. The intuition is that alpha compounds with breadth: a manager with IC = 0.05 making 100 independent bets per year achieves IR ≈ 0.5; making 1000 independent bets achieves IR ≈ 1.58. 'Independent' is the key word - adding correlated positions does not increase $N$. The factor risk model quantifies dependency: two stocks with high factor loading overlap have low independent information content. This is why diversification in factor investing requires spreading exposure across many low-factor-correlation positions.

**Risk model applications in portfolio management**: (1) **Active risk budgeting** - decompose portfolio tracking error into contributions from factor bets vs idiosyncratic bets; a portfolio heavily concentrated in factor bets is less diversified than it appears from position count. (2) **Factor hedging** - remove unwanted factor exposures (e.g., hedge out the market beta of a long-short equity book) by adding offsetting exposure in futures or ETFs. (3) **Portfolio optimization** - use the factor model as the covariance matrix in Markowitz optimization instead of the full sample covariance, producing more stable and better-conditioned optimization problems. (4) **Attribution** - decompose realized P&L into factor contributions to understand whether returns came from skill (idiosyncratic alpha) or luck (factor bets that happened to pay off).

resources.label: MSCI Barra — factor model research papers
Active Portfolio Management — Grinold & Kahn (ch 3, fundamental law)

connections.note: Factor exposures feed risk models and portfolio optimization
PCA/eigendecomposition underpins statistical risk factors

topics.title: Options & derivatives pricing
body: Payoffs and moneyness, **Black-Scholes** (derive via replicating portfolio / risk-neutral pricing), the **Greeks** (delta/gamma/vega/theta/rho), implied vol and the **vol smile/skew**, the **binomial tree** (backward induction - DP!), **Monte Carlo** pricing (GBM, variance reduction), and exotics.
Options are where probability, PDEs, and DP meet; building a pricing library with Greeks is the milestone.

subtopics.title: Options fundamentals: payoffs, moneyness, and put-call parity
body: A **call option** gives the holder the right (not obligation) to buy an asset at price $K$ (the **strike**) before or on date $T$ (expiry). A **put option** gives the right to sell at $K$. The buyer pays the **premium** upfront; the seller (writer) receives it and bears the obligation to fulfill if exercised. The premium is the market's price for the right. American options can be exercised at any time before $T$; European options only at $T$. Most exchange-traded equity index options (NIFTY options, SPX) are European; most single-stock options on US exchanges are American.

**Payoff at expiry**: for a call, $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ where $S_T$ is the asset price at expiry. If $S_T = 110$ and $K = 100$, the call pays 10; if $S_T = 90$, it pays 0 (not -10 - the holder simply does not exercise). For a put, $\max(K - S_T, 0)$. A put with $K = 100$ pays 10 if $S_T = 90$, zero if $S_T = 110$. These truncated payoffs are what makes options non-linear instruments: their P&L is asymmetric (limited downside for the buyer, unlimited upside for calls), which is qualitatively different from any linear combination of the underlying.

**Moneyness** describes the relationship between the current price $S$ and the strike $K$. **In-the-money (ITM)**: for a call, $S > K$ (would have positive intrinsic value if exercised now). **At-the-money (ATM)**: $S \approx K$. **Out-of-the-money (OTM)**: for a call, $S < K$ (zero intrinsic value now). ATM options have the highest **time value** (the portion of the premium attributable to the possibility of moving into the money before expiry). ITM options have high intrinsic value and low time value (already profitable). OTM options are pure time value - they are cheap in premium but highly leveraged: a 5% move in the underlying can triple or zero an OTM call.

**Put-call parity** is the fundamental no-arbitrage relationship between European call and put prices: $C - P = S - K e^{-rT}$, where $C$ and $P$ are call and put premiums, $r$ is the risk-free rate, and $T$ is time to expiry. Derivation: the portfolio (long call + short put + short stock + lend $Ke^{-rT}$) has zero payoff in every state at expiry, so it must have zero value today. This means call and put with the same strike and expiry always imply each other - if you know the call price, you can compute the put price exactly. Put-call parity is used to detect mispricings, to synthesize positions (a synthetic long is long call + short put at the same strike), and to infer the risk-free rate or forward price implied by option markets.

**Risk in options**: options have non-linear payoff, so their risk is path-dependent. The **breakeven** for a call buyer is $K + C$ (need the stock to rise above strike plus premium paid). The maximum loss for a call buyer is the premium paid; for a call seller, it is theoretically unlimited. **Option Greeks** quantify how the option price changes with each input - this is where the practical risk management begins, covered in the next subtopic. Understanding put-call parity and the Greeks together gives you enough to analyze any vanilla options position before ever computing a Black-Scholes price.

resources.label: Hull — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (ch 1, 10)

subtopics.title: Black-Scholes derivation and the risk-neutral pricing framework
body: The **Black-Scholes model** (Black & Scholes, 1973; Merton, 1973) derives the fair price of a European option by constructing a **replicating portfolio** that perfectly hedges the option. The derivation begins with the assumption that the underlying asset $S_t$ follows Geometric Brownian Motion: $dS_t = \mu S_t dt + \sigma S_t dW_t$, where $\mu$ is the drift, $\sigma$ is the volatility, and $W_t$ is a Wiener process. By **Itô's lemma**, the option price $V(S_t, t)$ satisfies: $dV = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \mu S \frac{\partial V}{\partial S} + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2}\right) dt + \sigma S \frac{\partial V}{\partial S} dW_t$.

The replicating portfolio construction: hold $\Delta = \partial V / \partial S$ units of stock and borrow the rest. This **delta hedge** perfectly cancels the stochastic $dW_t$ term: $d(V - \Delta S) = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2}\right) dt$. The hedged portfolio is instantaneously riskless - it grows at the risk-free rate $r$: $d(V - \Delta S) = r(V - \Delta S) dt$. Combining these and rearranging yields the **Black-Scholes PDE**: $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + rS \frac{\partial V}{\partial S} + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2} = rV$.

Solving the PDE with terminal boundary condition $V(S_T, T) = \max(S_T - K, 0)$ (call payoff at expiry) gives the famous **Black-Scholes formula**: $C = S N(d_1) - K e^{-rT} N(d_2)$, where $d_1 = \frac{\ln(S/K) + (r + \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}}$, $d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T}$, and $N(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF. The interpretation: $N(d_2)$ is the risk-neutral probability that the call finishes in the money; $S N(d_1)$ is the expected value of receiving the stock conditional on exercise; $K e^{-rT} N(d_2)$ is the expected discounted cost of paying the strike. The put formula follows from put-call parity: $P = K e^{-rT} N(-d_2) - S N(-d_1)$.

The deeper framework is **risk-neutral pricing**. The key insight: in a complete market, there exists a **risk-neutral measure** $\mathbb{Q}$ under which all assets earn the risk-free rate. Under $\mathbb{Q}$, the GBM drift changes from $\mu$ to $r$ (the risk-free rate), and option prices are: $V_t = e^{-r(T-t)} \mathbb{E}^{\mathbb{Q}}[\text{payoff at T} \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$. This is the **Feynman-Kac theorem**: the solution to the Black-Scholes PDE is the discounted expectation under $\mathbb{Q}$. Risk-neutral pricing completely decouples option pricing from the actual drift $\mu$ (which is notoriously hard to estimate) - the drift drops out and only the volatility $\sigma$ and risk-free rate $r$ matter. This is why options traders focus obsessively on volatility.

**Black-Scholes assumptions and their violations**: (1) Constant volatility - real volatility clusters and varies (GARCH); this generates the volatility smile. (2) Continuous trading - in reality, markets have jumps and close overnight; this matters most for short-expiry options. (3) Lognormal returns - real returns have fat tails; this underprices OTM options. (4) No dividends - easily relaxed by using forward prices. (5) No transaction costs - relevant for high-frequency delta-hedgers. Despite these violations, Black-Scholes is used universally because it provides a common language (implied volatility) and because its price is a good approximation for liquid near-ATM options. The model's failures are addressed by local vol, stochastic vol (Heston), and jump-diffusion models in practice.

resources.label: Black and Scholes (1973) — The pricing of options and corporate liabilities
Hull — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (ch 15, Black-Scholes)

subtopics.title: The Greeks: delta, gamma, vega, theta, and rho
body: The **Greeks** are partial derivatives of the option price with respect to each input. They are the risk management toolkit for options desks: every options portfolio is analyzed as a bundle of Greek exposures, and traders hedge each Greek separately. Understanding the Greeks is essential before running any options strategy.

**Delta** ($\Delta = \partial V / \partial S$) is the rate of change of option price per unit change in the underlying. For a call, $\Delta = N(d_1) \in [0, 1]$; for a put, $\Delta = N(d_1) - 1 \in [-1, 0]$. A call with $\Delta = 0.5$ gains 0.50 rupees for every 1 rupee rise in the stock. Delta also approximates the risk-neutral probability of expiring ITM (put: $N(-d_2)$). **Delta hedging**: hold the underlying to make the portfolio delta-neutral. A short call with $\Delta = 0.5$ requires holding 0.5 shares of stock (or equivalent futures) to be delta-neutral. Because delta changes as the underlying moves, delta hedging requires continuous rebalancing - hence the phrase 'delta hedging is a process, not a trade'.

**Gamma** ($\Gamma = \partial^2 V / \partial S^2 = \partial \Delta / \partial S$) measures how fast delta changes - it is the convexity of the option price. Gamma is always positive for both calls and puts (the option price is convex in $S$). High gamma means the delta hedge must be rebalanced frequently as the stock moves. Gamma is highest for ATM options near expiry. A **long gamma** position profits from large moves (in either direction) because the option gains more from moves in the favorable direction than it loses from the delta-hedge rebalancing cost. The cost of being long gamma is **theta** (daily time decay). Short gamma (selling options) earns theta but loses from large moves - the daily P&L of market-making an options book is a race between gamma P&L and theta collection.

**Vega** ($\nu = \partial V / \partial \sigma$) measures sensitivity to implied volatility. Both calls and puts have positive vega: when the market's implied volatility rises (e.g., before a major economic release), options become more expensive. ATM options have maximum vega. A long straddle (long call + long put at same strike and expiry) is a pure vega play: it profits if realized volatility exceeds implied volatility regardless of direction. Short vega strategies (selling options) collect premium when volatility is high and lose when it expands further. **Vomma** ($\partial \nu / \partial \sigma$, sometimes called volga) is the rate of change of vega with respect to volatility - relevant for options with significant convexity in vol.

**Theta** ($\Theta = \partial V / \partial t < 0$) is the daily time decay of option price. Options lose value as expiry approaches, all else equal. ATM options decay fastest in the last 30 days (theta is proportional to $1/\sqrt{T}$ approximately). Theta and gamma are linked via the Black-Scholes PDE: $\Theta + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 S^2 \Gamma = rV$ (for a delta-hedged portfolio). Long options pay theta but collect gamma; short options collect theta but pay gamma. **Rho** ($\rho = \partial V / \partial r$) is sensitivity to interest rates - relevant for long-dated options but usually small for near-term options. **Charm** ($\partial \Delta / \partial t$) and **color** ($\partial \Gamma / \partial t$) are higher-order Greeks used by sophisticated options desks to manage intraday delta-hedge rebalancing needs.

resources.label: Hull — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (ch 19, Greeks)

subtopics.title: Implied volatility, the vol smile, and skew
body: **Implied volatility (IV)** is the value of $\sigma$ that, when plugged into the Black-Scholes formula, gives the market's observed option price. It is a measure of the market's consensus forecast of future realized volatility. Since Black-Scholes has no closed-form inverse for $\sigma$, IV is computed numerically (Newton-Raphson on the BS price function is fast). IV is expressed annualized (e.g., 'NIFTY IV is 14%' means the market implies an annualized standard deviation of 14% in NIFTY). The relationship between option price and IV is monotone (higher vol → higher option price), so IV is a cleaner coordinate for comparing options across strikes and expiries than raw price.

If Black-Scholes were exactly right, all options on the same underlying with the same expiry would imply the same $\sigma$ - the 'true' volatility of the lognormal process. But in practice, plotting IV against strike produces a **volatility smile** or **skew**: IV is not flat across strikes. For equity indices (NIFTY, SPX), IV is highest for OTM puts (protective puts are expensive because of crash risk demand) and decreases toward ATM and ITM options, creating a **downward-sloping skew** or **volatility smirk**. For commodities, the smile is often symmetric (both OTM calls and puts are expensive relative to ATM). For FX, a 'smile' shape is common.

The **vol skew** has a precise economic interpretation: it reveals the market's implied distribution of the underlying. A symmetric smile (higher IV for both OTM calls and puts vs ATM) implies fat tails relative to lognormal - actual crash risk and spike risk are higher than BS assumes. A downward skew (higher IV for OTM puts) implies **negative skew in the risk-neutral return distribution** - the market assigns higher probability to large down moves than a lognormal would. The skew is quantified as the difference in IV between the 25-delta put and 25-delta call (the '25-delta risk reversal').

**Term structure of volatility**: IV also varies by expiry. Plotting IV for ATM options across expiries (1W, 1M, 3M, 6M, 1Y) gives the **vol term structure**. In normal markets, shorter-dated options have lower IV (mean reversion: tomorrow's vol is less uncertain than next year's). Before a known event (Fed meeting, earnings release), near-dated IV spikes while longer-dated IV moves less - creating an inverted term structure. After the event passes, near-dated IV collapses (the 'volatility crush'). Understanding this event-driven dynamics is critical for options strategies around earnings.

**Local vol models** (Dupire, 1994) calibrate to the full observed vol surface - the set of all $(K, T)$ market IVs - and produce a deterministic volatility function $\sigma(S, t)$ such that the resulting model exactly reprices every quoted option. Local vol is widely used as a mark-to-market model and for hedging exotic options. **Stochastic vol models** (Heston, SABR) model volatility as a random process (a second SDE), producing richer smile dynamics including a vol-of-vol parameter (vomma). The trade-off: local vol calibrates perfectly but has poor forward-vol dynamics (it underestimates forward skew); stochastic vol is less perfectly calibrated but has better dynamics for pricing path-dependent exotics. In practice, banks use a hybrid: local-stochastic vol (LSV) models.

resources.label: Gatheral — The Volatility Surface: A Practitioner's Guide

subtopics.title: Binomial trees, Monte Carlo, and exotic pricing
body: The **binomial tree** discretizes the underlying's price path into up/down moves at each step. At each node, the stock can move up by factor $u = e^{\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}}$ or down by $d = 1/u$ over a small time step $\Delta t$. The **risk-neutral probability** of an up move is $p = (e^{r\Delta t} - d) / (u - d)$. With $N$ steps, the tree has $N+1$ terminal nodes; the option value is computed by **backward induction**: at expiry, set option value to the payoff; at each earlier node, take the discounted expected value under $p$. For European calls, the binomial tree converges to Black-Scholes as $N \to \infty$.

The binomial tree's real power is in **American options**. At each node, compare the immediate exercise value (for a put: $\max(K - S, 0)$) with the discounted continuation value. Take the maximum: $V_{\text{node}} = \max(K - S, e^{-r\Delta t}[p V_{\text{up}} + (1-p) V_{\text{down}}])$. This early-exercise comparison at every node is impossible in closed form (there is no Black-Scholes formula for American puts) but trivial in the tree. American put prices are always higher than European put prices; the early-exercise premium reflects the value of getting the strike cash sooner when deep ITM. American calls on non-dividend stocks are never optimally exercised early (classical result from put-call parity arguments) - so American calls = European calls in this case.

**Monte Carlo pricing** is the most flexible approach: simulate $M$ paths of the underlying under the risk-neutral measure, compute the payoff on each path, and take the discounted average. For a European call: generate $M$ samples of $S_T = S_0 \exp\left((r - \sigma^2/2)T + \sigma \sqrt{T} Z\right)$ where $Z \sim N(0,1)$, compute $\text{payoff}_i = \max(S_{T,i} - K, 0)$, then $V \approx e^{-rT} \cdot \frac{1}{M} \sum_i \text{payoff}_i$. The standard error is $e^{-rT} \cdot \sigma_{\text{payoff}} / \sqrt{M}$ - reducing by half requires quadrupling $M$. Monte Carlo is naturally extended to path-dependent payoffs (Asian options: payoff depends on the average price), multi-asset options (basket options), and any model for which simulation is easy.

**Variance reduction** techniques are essential for Monte Carlo efficiency. **Antithetic variates**: for each sample $Z$, also evaluate $-Z$; the average of the two estimates is lower variance because they are negatively correlated. **Control variates**: simultaneously simulate a related option with a known price (e.g., a European call) and use the discrepancy to reduce variance: $V_{\text{adjusted}} = V_{\text{MC}} + (V_{\text{true, CV}} - V_{\text{MC, CV}}) \cdot \beta$, where $\beta$ is estimated from the correlation between payoffs. **Quasi-Monte Carlo** replaces uniform random samples with **low-discrepancy sequences** (Sobol, Halton), which fill the space more evenly and reduce error from $O(1/\sqrt{M})$ to nearly $O(1/M)$ for smooth integrands. These techniques can reduce the required number of simulations by 10-100x.

**Exotic options** are payoffs beyond simple European/American calls and puts. **Barrier options**: activated (knock-in) or deactivated (knock-out) if the underlying hits a level $H$ before expiry - e.g., a down-and-out call goes to zero if $S_t < H$ at any time. Barriers create discontinuous payoffs near $H$, requiring careful numerical treatment. **Asian options**: payoff depends on the average price over the period - common in commodity markets to prevent manipulation near expiry. **Lookback options**: payoff based on the maximum or minimum price over the period - floating strike lookback call is $S_T - \min_t S_t$. **Digitals (binary options)**: pay a fixed amount if the underlying is above/below the strike at expiry - used as building blocks for structured products and their discontinuous payoffs require special care near expiry where Black-Scholes Greeks blow up.

resources.label: Hull — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (ch 13, 21)
Hull — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives

GBM/SDE simulation shares Itô calculus with diffusion models

topics.title: Portfolio construction & risk
body: **Markowitz** mean-variance (efficient frontier) and its failure (estimation error, ill-conditioned covariance), **Black-Litterman**, **risk parity**, the **Kelly criterion** (and fractional Kelly), **CVaR** optimization, and risk management (VaR vs Expected Shortfall, stress testing, volatility targeting).
Optimization here is convex programming (cvxpy); the lesson is that estimation error usually dominates clever objectives.

subtopics.title: Markowitz mean-variance optimization and the efficient frontier
body: Markowitz (1952) formalized the insight that the variance of a portfolio is less than the weighted average of individual variances when assets are not perfectly correlated. For a portfolio with weight vector $\mathbf{w}$, assets with expected return vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\Sigma$: portfolio expected return $= \mathbf{w}' \boldsymbol{\mu}$, portfolio variance $= \mathbf{w}' \Sigma \mathbf{w}$. The optimization problem: minimize variance subject to a target return $\mu^*$: $\min_\mathbf{w} \mathbf{w}' \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ s.t. $\mathbf{w}' \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mu^*$, $\mathbf{w}' \mathbf{1} = 1$. The solution traces the **efficient frontier**: the set of minimum-variance portfolios for each return level.

The efficient frontier is a hyperbola in mean-standard-deviation space. Every point on the upper (positively sloped) arm dominates every point below it: for the same volatility, you get a higher expected return. The **minimum-variance portfolio** is the leftmost point. The **tangency portfolio** (the point where a line from the risk-free rate is tangent to the frontier) maximizes the Sharpe ratio. Under CAPM's assumptions, all investors hold some combination of the risk-free asset and the tangency portfolio - the latter is then the market portfolio. Computing the efficient frontier in Python: `cvxpy.Problem(Minimize(quad_form(w, Sigma)), constraints)` parameterized by target return.

**The practical failure of Markowitz** is well documented. The weights produced by mean-variance optimization are extremely sensitive to small estimation errors in $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\Sigma$, particularly $\boldsymbol{\mu}$. A 1% error in an expected return estimate can completely change the optimal weights. The result: the 'optimal' portfolio from unconstrained Markowitz is usually a highly concentrated bet on a few assets with extreme long and short weights - a disaster in practice. This is called the **error amplification** problem: the optimizer treats estimation error as genuine signal and tilts heavily into it.

**Shrinkage and regularization** are the standard fixes. **Ledoit-Wolf covariance shrinkage**: blend the sample covariance $\hat{\Sigma}$ toward a structured target (e.g., the identity scaled by average variance, or a single-factor model): $\tilde{\Sigma} = (1-\delta)\hat{\Sigma} + \delta T$ where $T$ is the target and $\delta$ is the shrinkage intensity estimated analytically. For expected returns, **shrink toward zero**: replace $\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}$ with $\delta \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}$ where $\delta < 1$. This controls the optimizer's tendency to bet heavily on imprecisely estimated high-return assets. **Constraints** (long-only, maximum position size, sector limits) are the most common and effective regularization in practice - they prevent extreme solutions.

**Portfolio optimization as convex programming** is now straightforward with cvxpy. The general form: minimize a risk measure (variance, CVaR, tracking error) subject to return target, budget constraint $\sum w_i = 1$, long-only $w_i \geq 0$, turnover limits $\sum |w_i - w_i^{\text{prev}}| \leq \Delta_{\max}$, and factor exposure constraints $|\mathbf{b}' \mathbf{w}| \leq \beta_{\max}$ for each factor. Cvxpy constructs the problem symbolically and calls a solver (OSQP for quadratic programs, ECOS/SCS for more general conic programs). The key skill is formulating the portfolio problem as a valid convex program - this requires the objective and constraints to be convex (for minimization) or concave (for maximization) in $\mathbf{w}$.

subtopics.title: Black-Litterman: incorporating views into optimization
body: The Black-Litterman model (Black & Litterman, 1992) solves Markowitz's estimation error problem by starting from an equilibrium prior and updating it with investor views. The starting point: assume the market is in equilibrium (all investors hold the market cap-weighted portfolio). **Reverse optimization** computes the implied expected returns from market cap weights: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{eq}} = \delta \Sigma \mathbf{w}_{\text{mkt}}$, where $\delta$ is risk aversion and $\mathbf{w}_{\text{mkt}}$ is the market cap weight vector. These equilibrium returns are the **prior** - they encode the wisdom of market prices.

An investor then specifies $K$ **views**: 'Asset A will outperform by 2% per year over the next quarter (with confidence $\sigma_k^2$)'. Each view is formalized as: $P \boldsymbol{\mu} = Q + \epsilon$ where $P$ is a $K \times N$ pick matrix (1 for long legs, -1 for short legs), $Q$ is the vector of expected view returns, and $\epsilon \sim N(0, \Omega)$ where $\Omega = \text{diag}(\sigma_k^2)$ encodes view uncertainty. The view 'A outperforms B by 2%' has a row in $P$ with +1 at A's column and -1 at B's column.

**Bayesian update**: combine the prior (equilibrium returns) with the views (likelihood) using Bayes' theorem. The posterior expected return vector: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{BL}} = [(\tau \Sigma)^{-1} + P' \Omega^{-1} P]^{-1} [(\tau \Sigma)^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{eq}} + P' \Omega^{-1} Q]$, where $\tau$ is a scalar (typically 0.025-0.05) representing uncertainty in the prior. The intuition: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{BL}}$ blends the equilibrium return and the views, weighted inversely by their uncertainties. A high-confidence view shifts the posterior strongly; an uncertain view shifts it little.

The practical result: Black-Litterman portfolios are much more stable and diversified than raw Markowitz. When an investor has no views ($K = 0$), the model returns market cap weights - a sensible default. When views are specified, the weights tilt toward the view portfolios in proportion to confidence. The model is popular among institutional asset managers because it provides a principled way to translate qualitative investment convictions (analyst forecasts, macro calls) into quantitative portfolio weights without the wild gyrations of unconstrained optimization.

**View specification in practice** is the most art-intensive step. The confidence parameter $\sigma_k^2$ must be estimated - the standard approach (He & Litterman) sets $\Omega = \tau P \Sigma P'$, making the view uncertainty proportional to the uncertainty in the equilibrium returns for that view portfolio. This is the 'proportional to equilibrium uncertainty' assumption and it simplifies the model to a single parameter $\tau$. In practice, views should be expressed relative to the equilibrium prior (e.g., 'I believe tech will outperform by 1% relative to its equilibrium return, not 5% in absolute terms') to avoid the model being dominated by overly aggressive views.

resources.label: He and Litterman (1999) — Intuition behind Black-Litterman (Goldman Sachs)

subtopics.title: Risk parity and the Kelly criterion
body: **Risk parity** (Qian, 2005) allocates capital so that each asset contributes equally to total portfolio risk, rather than allocating by capital or mean-variance efficiency. The **risk contribution** of asset $i$ is: $RC_i = w_i \frac{\partial \sigma_p}{\partial w_i} = w_i \frac{(\Sigma \mathbf{w})_i}{\sigma_p}$, where $\sigma_p = \sqrt{\mathbf{w}' \Sigma \mathbf{w}}$. Risk parity sets $RC_i = RC_j$ for all $i, j$, i.e., $\mathbf{w} \odot (\Sigma \mathbf{w}) = k \mathbf{1}$ for some scalar $k$. For uncorrelated assets with equal volatilities, risk parity reduces to equal capital weights. For assets with different volatilities, risk parity over-weights low-volatility assets and under-weights high-volatility assets.

The economic motivation for risk parity: in the 60/40 portfolio (60% equities, 40% bonds), roughly 90% of the risk comes from equities - it is effectively an equity portfolio with a bond drag. Risk parity would allocate much more to bonds (using leverage if necessary to achieve the return target) and less to equities, resulting in a more genuinely diversified risk exposure. Bridgewater's All Weather Fund is the famous implementation. The Sharpe ratio of the equal-risk-contribution portfolio is the highest achievable when the Sharpe ratios of all assets are equal and there is no alpha from market timing - a theoretical result that justifies risk parity as a default without views.

**Kelly criterion** (Kelly, 1956) answers the question: what fraction of wealth should you bet on each opportunity to maximize long-run wealth growth? For a binary bet (win $b$ with probability $p$, lose 1 with probability $1-p$): Kelly fraction $f^* = p - (1-p)/b = p/1 - (1-p)/b = (pb - (1-p)) / b$. For a Gaussian return distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$: $f^* = \mu / \sigma^2$. In portfolio terms, the Kelly portfolio maximizes the expected log of wealth, which maximizes long-run growth rate (by the ergodic theorem). The Kelly portfolio is the same as the maximum Sharpe portfolio when you can leverage.

**Fractional Kelly** is the practical modification. Full Kelly results in **enormous volatility** and drawdowns (it is theoretically optimal but operationally unbearable). A half-Kelly strategy (use $f^*/2$) has slightly lower long-run growth but roughly half the variance and much smaller drawdowns. The tradeoff is that **over-betting (Kelly × 2)** is actually worse than under-betting in expectation: over-betting eventually leads to ruin because the geometric mean of wealth falls below 1 per bet. A key result: the optimal bet size depends on the Sharpe ratio of the opportunity, not just its expected return. High Sharpe opportunities merit larger position sizes; low Sharpe merit smaller, regardless of absolute return.

**Connecting Kelly and mean-variance**: the Kelly portfolio maximizes $\mathbb{E}[\ln W_{T+1}] \approx \mathbf{w}' \boldsymbol{\mu} - \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}' \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ (by Taylor expansion of log). This is exactly the Markowitz objective with risk aversion $= 1$. So the Kelly portfolio is a specific point on the efficient frontier - the one with unit risk aversion. Fractional Kelly with fraction $\alpha$ corresponds to risk aversion $1/\alpha$ in mean-variance terms. This connects the two frameworks: risk parity (robust to estimation error), Kelly (growth-optimal), and Markowitz (flexible) are all addressing the same problem from different angles.

resources.label: Kelly (1956) — A new interpretation of information rate (paper)
Roncalli — Introduction to Risk Parity and Budgeting (free)

subtopics.title: CVaR optimization and coherent risk measures
body: The mean-variance framework penalizes upside and downside deviation equally. But investors care about **tail risk**: how bad do losses get in the worst scenarios, not just the average? **CVaR (Conditional Value at Risk)**, also called Expected Shortfall (ES), measures the expected loss in the worst $\alpha$% of outcomes: $\text{CVaR}_{\alpha} = -\mathbb{E}[r \mid r < \text{VaR}_{\alpha}]$. CVaR is a **coherent risk measure** (satisfies subadditivity, monotonicity, homogeneity, and translation invariance), making it theoretically superior to VaR for portfolio optimization.

**CVaR optimization** is a convex program. Rockafellar and Uryasev (2000) showed that CVaR can be minimized as a linear program: $\text{CVaR}_{\alpha}(\mathbf{w}) = \min_{\zeta} \left\{ \zeta + \frac{1}{(1-\alpha)M} \sum_{i=1}^M \max(-\mathbf{w}' \mathbf{r}^{(i)} - \zeta, 0) \right\}$, where $\mathbf{r}^{(i)}$ are $M$ historical return scenarios. This is an LP with $M$ auxiliary variables (one per scenario). For 2,000 historical scenarios and 100 assets, the LP has ~2,100 variables and solves in seconds. In cvxpy: `cvar_loss = cp.Variable(); losses = [...]; objective = cvar_loss + cp.sum(cp.pos(-w @ returns - cvar_loss)) / (M * (1-alpha))`.

**Scenario-based optimization** generalizes CVaR: instead of using only historical scenarios, include stress scenarios (a 2008-style market crash, a 1998 LTCM liquidity crisis, a 2020 COVID shock) as additional constraints. Require the portfolio return to exceed a floor in each stress scenario: $\mathbf{w}' \mathbf{r}_{\text{stress}} \geq L$. This is a **robust optimization** approach: protect against the worst-case scenario you can identify, even if it has never occurred in the historical record. The tradeoff is a conservative portfolio that may underperform in normal regimes but is protected in tail events.

**Drawdown constraints** are another practically important addition. Rather than constraining instantaneous losses (VaR/CVaR), constrain the portfolio's **maximum drawdown over a path**: $\max_{t \leq T} (\max_{s \leq t} V_s - V_t) \leq D_{\max}$. Drawdown constraints are not convex in general, but can be linearized via auxiliary variables for scenario-based paths. Alternatively, **expected maximum drawdown** constraints are tractable. Drawdown constraints directly address the operational reality of portfolio management: a strategy with 40% maximum drawdown will trigger redemptions and risk-limit violations even if its long-run return is excellent.

In practice, institutional portfolio construction combines multiple risk measures: minimize a composite objective (e.g., `lambda * variance + (1-lambda) * CVaR`) subject to return target, factor exposure limits (from Barra), drawdown constraint (from historical simulation), and liquidity constraints (maximum position size as fraction of average daily volume so the position can be unwound within N days). Every constraint represents a real operational concern, not mathematical elegance. The portfolio optimizer's job is to find the point of maximum expected return within this multi-dimensional feasible region.

resources.label: Rockafellar and Uryasev (2000) — Optimization of CVaR (paper)
cvxpy — risk parity and CVaR examples

subtopics.title: VaR, stress testing, and volatility targeting in practice
body: **Value at Risk (VaR)** is the regulatory-mandated risk metric for banks and large funds. Three calculation methods: (1) **Historical simulation**: rank the past $T$ days' P&L, read off the $\alpha$th percentile. Simple, assumption-free, but slow to react to changing volatility. (2) **Variance-covariance (parametric)**: assume normal P&L with daily mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, then $\text{VaR}_{95\%} = -\mu + 1.645 \sigma$. Fast but assumes normality - underestimates tail risk. (3) **Monte Carlo simulation**: simulate 10,000+ P&L paths from a risk model (which can include fat tails, volatility clustering), compute the quantile. Most flexible but computationally expensive.

**Backtesting VaR** (required by Basel III) counts the number of '**exceptions**' (days when actual losses exceeded the VaR estimate) over the past 250 trading days. Expected exceptions at 99% confidence: 2.5 per year. Zones: green (0-4 exceptions), yellow (5-9), red (10+). Too many exceptions means the VaR model is underestimating risk; too few means it is over-conservative. Banks with yellow or red zone violations face regulatory capital surcharges. **Expected Shortfall** (Basel IV) was adopted because VaR's exceptions only count, not measure the magnitude - a model with 5 exceptions each losing 50% is far worse than 5 exceptions each losing 1%.

**Stress testing** complements VaR by asking 'what if an extreme historical scenario happened again?'. Historical stress scenarios: the 2008 credit crisis (S&P -40%, credit spreads +400bps, VIX +50), the 1987 Black Monday (S&P -22% in one day), the 2020 COVID crash (S&P -34% in 23 days). Apply the correlated factor moves from each scenario to your current portfolio and measure the P&L impact. Hypothetical stress scenarios: a geopolitical event causing crude oil +50%, a central bank policy error causing yields +200bps in a week. Reverse stress testing goes the other direction: 'what scenario would cause the portfolio to lose 20%?' and then assess how plausible that scenario is.

**Volatility targeting** is the dynamic risk management technique of adjusting position sizes (leverage) inversely with measured volatility to maintain a constant portfolio risk over time. If your target is 10% annualized volatility and current realized volatility is 20%, cut position sizes in half. If realized volatility is 5%, double positions. The daily volatility estimate is typically a GARCH or EWMA estimate: $\sigma_t^2 = \lambda \sigma_{t-1}^2 + (1-\lambda) r_{t-1}^2$. Volatility targeting improves Sharpe ratio by reducing drawdowns during high-volatility regimes (when mean-reversion is unreliable) while allowing higher leverage in low-volatility regimes.

A complete **risk management system** integrates all of these: pre-trade risk checks (would this order breach VaR or position limits?), real-time position monitoring (delta, VaR, factor exposures updated intraday), end-of-day risk reporting (VaR, stress test results, drawdown, attribution), and circuit breakers (automatic stop-loss triggers if portfolio drawdown exceeds threshold). The most dangerous failures in risk management are not incorrect formulas but **model risk** (using a VaR model that misses key tail dependencies) and **operational risk** (a trader overriding the risk system because 'the model is wrong' in a high-stress period - which is exactly when discipline matters most).

resources.label: Basel Committee — Minimum capital requirements for market risk (FRTB)
Riskfolio-Lib — portfolio optimization and risk Python library
