topics.title: Alternative data
body: Data types (satellite, credit-card, web-scraped, shipping AIS, earnings-call NLP), acquisition (Scrapy/Playwright, vendor negotiation), point-in-time construction (avoiding survivorship/look-ahead), **NLP for finance** (FinBERT/Sec-BERT, sentiment from 10-Ks, event studies), and signal decay.
Edge increasingly comes from unique data plus the engineering to clean it without leakage.

subtopics.title: Types of alternative data: satellite, credit-card, shipping, and web
body: **Alternative data** refers to any non-traditional data set that can generate trading signals, as opposed to traditional sources (OHLCV prices, financial statements, analyst forecasts). The alt data market has grown dramatically - hundreds of data vendors now sell satellite imagery, transaction data, web-scraped content, and more. The competitive edge in alt data comes from: (1) being first to acquire a unique data set, (2) being better at extracting signal from a widely available data set, or (3) combining multiple partial signals into a composite that none alone provides.

**Satellite imagery** is used in several ways. Counting cars in retailer parking lots (Orbital Insight, RS Metrics) estimates foot traffic before quarterly earnings. Measuring oil storage tank shadow heights (tank roofs float on the liquid level) estimates petroleum inventory before EIA reports. Tracking crop field greenness (NDVI from Landsat/Sentinel satellites) forecasts agricultural yields before USDA reports. Measuring light emission from industrial facilities at night estimates factory utilization. The alpha from satellite data arises from acting before the scheduled public data release - you know Walmart Q4 traffic was up before earnings are reported. Signal decay is event-driven: alpha concentrates around the announcement date.

**Credit card and transaction data** from payment processors (Yodlee, Second Measure, Bloomberg SecondMeasure) aggregates consumer spending by merchant. A 3% increase in Amazon Prime credit card spending in the 6 weeks before an earnings release is a forward-looking revenue signal. The challenges: (1) **panel coverage** - the data covers only cardholders who consent to data sharing, introducing selection bias, (2) **point-in-time delivery** - the data must be received with enough lead time to act before earnings, and (3) **signal saturation** - as more quant funds buy the same data, the alpha compresses. The earliest adopters of a data set earn the most; late adopters pay high vendor fees for diminishing alpha.

**Shipping AIS (Automatic Identification System) data**: ships broadcast their position, speed, and cargo via AIS transponders (mandatory for vessels over 300 gross tons under IMO rules). Aggregating AIS signals from commercial data providers (MarineTraffic, Spire) allows tracking of commodity flows: crude oil tankers heading to Cushing forecast WTI storage changes; LNG carriers departing Qatar forecast European gas supply; bulk carriers loaded at Dalian heading to India forecast iron ore demand. Shipping data has a 1-4 week lead time on official inventory or trade statistics, making it a valuable forward-looking signal for commodity and EM equity strategies.

**Web-scraped and sentiment data** is the most accessible alt data type. Job postings data (LinkedIn, Indeed via scraping) indicates company hiring momentum - a rapid increase in engineering job postings at a semiconductor firm may indicate a new product cycle before it appears in guidance. Web traffic data (SimilarWeb, Comscore) estimates consumer app engagement. Product review counts and ratings (Amazon, Google Play) provide real-time product performance signals. Social media sentiment (Twitter/X posts mentioning $TSLA) has mixed evidence of predictive power; meme stock events (GameStop, 2021) showed that retail social media can move prices but is difficult to trade profitably against coordinated retail flows.

resources.label: Quandl / Nasdaq Data Link — alternative data marketplace
Kolanovic, Krishnamachari — Big Data and AI Strategies (JPM, 2017)

subtopics.title: Data acquisition: scraping, APIs, and vendor negotiation
body: **Web scraping** is the most accessible alt data acquisition method. The standard Python stack: **Requests + BeautifulSoup** for static HTML pages; **Playwright** or **Selenium** for JavaScript-rendered pages (dynamically loaded content); **Scrapy** for large-scale crawls across many pages. Key considerations: (1) **robots.txt and terms of service** - many sites prohibit scraping; understand the legal risk before building a commercial data pipeline. (2) **Rate limiting** - throttle requests to avoid triggering anti-scraping defenses; use exponential backoff on errors. (3) **Rotating proxies** - residential or datacenter proxy pools for at-scale scraping without IP bans. (4) **Captcha handling** - captcha-solving services (2Captcha) for sites that require human verification.

**Exchange and financial data APIs**: NSE India provides official market data via the NSE API (with authentication); BSE provides Bhavcopy (daily OHLCV CSV files) for free download. For historical tick data, vendors include **True Data** (NSE tick data, Indian markets), **Bloomberg API** (institutional, expensive), **Refinitiv (LSEG) Eikon** (global), and **Zerodha Historical Data API** (daily OHLCV free tier). Cryptocurrency data is more accessible: Binance, OKX, and Bybit provide free historical REST APIs for 1-minute OHLCV and trade tick data; for L2 order book snapshots, websocket feeds are real-time only (historical LOB data requires vendors like **Tardis Machine**).

**SEC EDGAR** is the primary source of US company filings. The EDGAR full-text search API (`efts.sec.gov`) and bulk data downloads provide 10-K annual reports, 10-Q quarterly reports, 8-K material event disclosures, and institutional investor 13-F holdings (quarterly). For NLP applications, 10-K filings contain MD&A (Management Discussion & Analysis), risk factor disclosures, and financial statements in structured XBRL. **For India**: RBI provides monetary policy and financial stability reports; SEBI provides corporate filings; company investor relations pages provide quarterly results, often as PDF or XBRL. Parsing XBRL financial statements (via `arelle` library in Python) extracts structured financial data without manual text extraction.

**Vendor negotiation for alt data**: alt data vendors charge annual license fees ranging from $10,000 for smaller datasets to $1M+ for comprehensive satellite or credit card feeds. The evaluation process: (1) **Free trial** - most vendors offer 3-6 month evaluation licenses; extract a backtest Sharpe during this period. (2) **Signal evaluation metrics** - compute IC, IC decay, and out-of-sample Sharpe before and after the vendor's claimed signal period. (3) **Panel size and coverage** - for credit card data, what fraction of total consumer spending does the panel represent? Is it representative of the investable universe? (4) **Point-in-time delivery** - when exactly does the data become available, and what is the delivery SLA? (5) **Exclusivity** - some vendors sell data exclusively to one firm; most sell to many, compressing the alpha as the signal becomes crowded.

**Data engineering for alt data pipelines**: alt data is messy. A satellite imagery pipeline: download raw GeoTIFF images → cloud mask (remove cloud coverage artifacts) → co-register images (align timestamps and projection) → extract features (car count via YOLO object detection, NDVI calculation) → merge with company universe (link retail chain GPS coordinates to ticker) → compute weekly signals → join to backtesting framework. Each step requires validation: are cloud masks correct? Are image geolocation errors causing misclassification? Is the ticker linkage accurate? A single bug in the pipeline can introduce systematic look-ahead bias (e.g., using a corrected/revised image from after the earnings date). Testing each pipeline stage with hold-out data and visual inspection is essential before trusting any backtest result.

resources.label: SEC EDGAR — full-text search and bulk data

subtopics.title: Point-in-time construction and avoiding look-ahead leakage
body: **Point-in-time (PIT) data** means knowing, for any historical date $t$, exactly what data was available to a real-world investor on date $t$ - not what we know now. This is the single most important data engineering principle in quantitative finance, and violating it destroys the integrity of every backtest. The core problem: financial data is frequently revised, restated, and updated after the fact. If you use the current value of a variable to backtest a strategy that would have used the original reported value, you have introduced look-ahead bias.

**Earnings restatements** are the canonical example. A company reports Q3 EPS of 2.50 on November 5, 2022. On March 15, 2023, it restates Q3 EPS to 1.80 (due to an accounting error). A non-PIT backtest using data downloaded today will see 1.80 for Q3 2022 - a number not available until March 2023. An earnings-based strategy that 'buys when Q3 EPS beats estimate' would have seen 2.50 on November 5 (the reported number, which beats estimates) but a non-PIT database shows 1.80 (which misses estimates). The strategy's buy signal is wrong. This error systematically biases fundamental factor backtests.

**Index composition** is another major source of PIT error. The S&P 500 adds and removes companies over time. A backtest using today's S&P 500 members for a 20-year backtest will include only companies that survived to today (survivorship bias) and exclude companies that were in the index historically but were later removed (often for poor performance or bankruptcy). To avoid this, you need the point-in-time index membership for each date - which companies were in the S&P 500 on January 1, 2005, not which are in it today. This requires maintaining a historical index membership database (available from Compustat, FactSet, Bloomberg).

**Point-in-time construction in practice**: for fundamental factors (P/E, P/B, revenue), use the **as-reported** financials (from EDGAR XBRL with filing dates) rather than adjusted/revised values. The filing date (not the period end date) determines when the data became public. A Q3 report filed on November 5 cannot be used in your model before November 5. Build a **vintage database**: store each version of each data point with the date it was first available. When backtesting: for each simulation date $t$, query only vintages with availability date $\leq t$. Commercial data providers (Bloomberg, FactSet, Compustat) handle PIT construction; self-built pipelines must implement it explicitly.

**Drift and structural breaks in alt data**: even with correct PIT construction, the relationship between your alt data signal and stock returns may change over time. A signal that worked from 2015-2020 may fail from 2021 onward because: (1) the data became widely available (crowding), (2) a structural change in the underlying behavior (COVID changed consumer spending patterns permanently), or (3) the target companies changed (the signal linked to Company A which was acquired). **Rolling IC analysis** (compute IC month-by-month and plot over time) visualizes whether the signal's predictive power is stable or decaying. A signal with stable IC over 5+ years is more likely to be a durable economic relationship than a lucky in-sample pattern.

resources.label: Advances in Financial ML — López de Prado (ch 2, data leakage)

subtopics.title: NLP for finance: FinBERT, 10-K analysis, and event studies
body: **FinBERT** (Araci, 2019) is a BERT model fine-tuned on financial text (financial news, analyst reports, earnings call transcripts) to classify sentiment as positive, negative, or neutral. It outperforms generic BERT on financial texts because financial language is highly domain-specific: 'significant impairment charge' is unambiguously negative even though 'significant' sounds positive. FinBERT and its successors (**SecBERT**, **FinancialBERT**, **BloombergGPT**) are the standard tools for sentiment extraction from unstructured financial text.

**10-K analysis pipeline**: download 10-K filings from SEC EDGAR → extract the MD&A section (Item 7) → clean text (remove HTML, normalize whitespace) → pass through FinBERT to score sentiment → compute the year-over-year change in sentiment score → use as a factor. The economic intuition: management voluntarily chooses language in the MD&A; increasingly negative language predicts future underperformance (Loughran & McDonald, 2011). The Loughran-McDonald financial sentiment wordlist is the traditional method; FinBERT improves on it by capturing context (negation, conditionals). An important subtlety: the *change* in language is more informative than its absolute level (managers have idiosyncratic writing styles).

**Earnings call transcript NLP**: earnings calls are quarterly conference calls where management discusses results and answers analyst questions. The Q&A section (analysts questioning management) contains more negative sentiment than the prepared remarks (management-controlled, spin-positive). Extracting sentiment from Q&A vs prepared sections separately, and focusing on management's hedging language ('challenging environment', 'uncertain demand outlook'), predicts post-announcement returns over days to weeks. Models using both transcript text and audio features (voice stress, speaking pace - extractable via Whisper ASI + prosodic feature extraction) outperform text-only models on stock return prediction.

**Event studies** measure the abnormal return around a specific event type. Standard methodology (Fama, Fisher, Jensen, Roll, 1969): (1) define the event (earnings announcement date, 8-K filing, index addition/removal), (2) define the estimation window (e.g., days -250 to -10 relative to event) and event window (e.g., days -1 to +1), (3) estimate the expected return model (CAPM or market model) on the estimation window, (4) compute abnormal return (AR) = actual return - expected return on event days, (5) compute cumulative abnormal return (CAR) over the event window, (6) test statistical significance of average CAR across all events. For an NLP signal study: run an event study where events are defined by extreme sentiment shifts in 10-K filings; the CAR around the filing date quantifies how much of the sentiment signal is already priced vs delayed.

**Post-earnings announcement drift (PEAD)** is the most replicated anomaly in earnings-related NLP research: stock prices continue to drift in the direction of earnings surprises for 60+ days after the announcement. An NLP-enhanced PEAD model: (1) compute standardized unexpected earnings (SUE = (EPS actual - EPS estimate) / std(EPS residuals)), (2) enrich with tone of earnings call transcript (positive tone reinforces positive surprise, negative tone hedges the beat), (3) sort stocks on composite SUE+tone score and trade the long-short spread with 60-day holding period. The incremental alpha from adding NLP sentiment to raw SUE has been 0.5-1.5% annually in academic studies, though signal saturation is likely as NLP factor adoption grows.

resources.label: Loughran and McDonald (2011) — When is a liability not a liability? (financial wordlist paper)

subtopics.title: Signal construction and decay measurement
body: A raw alt data series (parking lot car counts, NLP sentiment score, credit card spend index) is not a trading signal. A signal requires: (1) **universe definition** (which stocks does this data cover and with what completeness?), (2) **cross-sectional normalization** (compute z-scores within the investable universe to make signals comparable across companies), (3) **time-series stationarity** (transform the raw level into growth rates or deviations from rolling mean to remove trend), (4) **winsorization** (cap extreme values at $\pm 3\sigma$ to prevent outliers from dominating portfolio weights), and (5) **sector/market-cap neutralization** (demean within each GICS sector and size bucket to prevent systematic tilts).

**Signal decay measurement**: compute IC between the signal and future returns at horizons $h = 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 60$ trading days. Plot the IC as a function of horizon (the IC decay curve). A signal with IC still significant at 60 days is fundamentally different from one that decays to zero by day 5 - both in optimal holding period and in sensitivity to transaction costs. Alternative measurement: for a portfolio constructed from the signal today, what is the portfolio's alpha at each future date? The **alpha decay curve** is similar to the IC decay curve but accounts for portfolio construction (weighting, constraints).

**Signal combination**: combining multiple signals from different data sources improves robustness if they are not perfectly correlated. A composite signal: $z_{\text{composite}} = \sum_k w_k z_k$ where $z_k$ are individual signal z-scores and $w_k$ are combination weights. Weights can be IC-optimal (maximize expected portfolio IC given signal correlation matrix), equal-weighted (simplest, often surprisingly effective), or estimated by regularized regression (ridge/lasso on rolling out-of-sample data). A satellite + NLP + credit card composite signal for US retailers will have lower variance and more stable IC than any single component, because each signal contains partially independent information about true retail performance.

**Signal crowding** is the risk that a signal's edge disappears because many quant funds are simultaneously trading on it. Crowding has two signatures: (1) the signal's IC in the current period is significantly below its historical mean, especially during risk-off events (when crowded trades all unwind simultaneously, the signal predicts in the wrong direction temporarily), and (2) the autocorrelation of returns of the signal's long and short portfolios is negative at short horizons (reversal after crowded positioning). Monitoring crowding requires estimating the ownership overlap: if the top 50 holders of your long portfolio have large position overlap with known quant funds (Acadian, AQR, Two Sigma - available from 13-F filings), your signal may be crowded.

**Alpha half-life and rebalancing frequency optimization**: given an IC decay curve, the optimal rebalancing frequency balances the cost of stale signals (lower alpha from infrequent rebalancing) against the cost of turnover (higher transaction costs from frequent rebalancing). The approximate optimal rebalancing frequency is the horizon at which IC has decayed to $IC_0 / e \approx 37\%$ of its initial value - the 'information half-life'. For a signal with half-life of 10 days, rebalancing every 5 days captures most of the signal while keeping turnover manageable. Signals with short half-lives (< 5 days) require very low transaction costs to be profitable; signals with long half-lives (30+ days) are viable for funds with high AUM and limited capacity for high-turnover strategies.

resources.label: Cahan et al. — Alternative data in investment management (CFA Institute)

connections.note: Earnings-call / filing NLP uses transformer language models
Scraping pipelines (Playwright/Scrapy) are web-engineering

title: Web3 & Blockchain
tagline: From blockchain fundamentals to smart-contract security, DeFi internals, zkEVMs, and MEV.
overview: A blockchain is a beautiful composition of things you already know: cryptographic hashes + Merkle trees (crypto/DSA), a P2P gossip network (low-level/networking), a deterministic bytecode VM (compilers), and an adversarial economic game on top. Web3 is where cryptography becomes money, which makes its security stakes uniquely brutal - bugs are instantly, irreversibly exploited for millions.

The arc: fundamentals + the EVM → Solidity + DeFi primitives → smart-contract security, MEV, and Layer 2 / zero-knowledge. Treat security as the through-line: learn the vuln classes, exploit them in Damn Vulnerable DeFi, and read every post-mortem on Rekt.

Each topic keeps its short description, then a **Deep dive** section of expandable, textbook-length subtopics that teach the idea in full - paired with exact deep-links to the canonical reference for each. This domain is built for genuine mastery, not orientation.

topics.title: Blockchain fundamentals
body: A blockchain is not one invention - it is a **composition** of four things you already understand: cryptographic hashes and Merkle trees (from cryptography and DSA), a peer-to-peer gossip network (from distributed systems), an append-only log made tamper-evident by hash-linking, and an economic consensus game that decides who may append next. Strip away the hype and every 'blockchain magic' claim reduces to one of these four primitives doing its job.

This topic builds that foundation from the ground up: how hashing creates immutability, how Merkle trees make verification cheap, how Proof of Work and Proof of Stake answer 'who writes the next block', how finality differs from confirmation, the two competing ways to model ownership (UTXO vs accounts), and how data actually moves across a serverless network. Master these and the EVM, DeFi, and L2 topics become straightforward applications rather than new mysteries.

subtopics.title: Cryptographic hashing & content addressing
body: A cryptographic hash function maps arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length digest (32 bytes for the ones blockchains use). Bitcoin uses **SHA-256**; Ethereum uses **Keccak-256** (the original Keccak submission, which differs from the finalized NIST SHA-3 only in a padding constant - a detail that trips up people who reach for a SHA-3 library and get different hashes). Three properties make these functions the load-bearing primitive of the entire field.

**Determinism**: the same input always yields the same digest, so any node anywhere can independently recompute and agree. **Pre-image resistance**: given a digest `h`, it is computationally infeasible to find any input `x` with `hash(x) = h` - the function is one-way. **Collision resistance**: it is infeasible to find two distinct inputs that hash to the same digest. Together with the **avalanche effect** (flipping a single input bit changes roughly half the output bits, with no correlation to the change), these mean a digest behaves like a unique, unforgeable fingerprint of its input.

Now apply this to a ledger. Each block contains, in its header, the hash of the previous block. So block N's hash is a function of block N's contents, which include block N-1's hash, which is a function of block N-1's contents including block N-2's hash, and so on back to the **genesis block**. The blocks form a hash-linked chain - hence 'blockchain'.

The consequence is **tamper-evidence**, and it is worth being precise about what it does and does not give you. It does *not* make data physically unchangeable - anyone can edit their local copy of a block. What it gives you is *cheap detection*: if you change one byte in a historical block, that block's hash changes, which no longer matches the `prevHash` stored in the next block, which breaks that block's hash, cascading forward through every subsequent block. To make the tampered chain internally consistent again you would have to recompute every block after the change - and in Proof of Work, redo all their mining. Verification, meanwhile, is just recomputing a chain of hashes: trivial. This asymmetry - expensive to forge, cheap to verify - is the recurring shape of everything in crypto.

**Content addressing** generalizes the idea: instead of naming data by *where* it lives (a URL, a disk offset), you name it by *what it is* (its hash). Git does this (a commit's id is the hash of its contents), IPFS does this, and Ethereum's state does this throughout. The profound property is that the identifier *is* the integrity check - if someone hands you data and claims it is `0xabc...`, you hash it yourself and either it matches or it does not; there is no separate trust step. Internalize this and you will recognize it in Merkle proofs, in CREATE2 addresses, in commitment schemes, and in ZK public inputs.

A practical exercise that makes this concrete: take any string, hash it with Keccak-256 (e.g. in a Foundry console or at an online tool), then change one character and hash again - watch half the digest change. Then hand-build three 'blocks' as JSON objects where each stores the hash of the previous, and convince yourself that editing block 1 forces you to recompute blocks 2 and 3.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — intro to Ethereum (hashing in the protocol)
But how does Bitcoin actually work? — 3Blue1Brown

subtopics.title: Merkle trees & inclusion proofs
body: Hash-linking secures the *order* of blocks, but inside a block there may be thousands of transactions, and we need a way to (a) commit to all of them with a single hash and (b) prove that one specific transaction is among them *without* shipping all the others. The **Merkle tree** solves both at once and is one of the most reused structures in all of computing.

Construction: place your N items as leaves, hash each leaf, then repeatedly hash adjacent pairs - `parent = hash(left ‖ right)` - level by level until a single hash remains, the **Merkle root**. (If a level has an odd count, the last node is duplicated or carried, depending on the scheme.) The root is a 32-byte commitment to the *entire ordered set*: change, add, remove, or reorder any leaf and the root changes. Storing just the root in the block header therefore pins down every transaction in the block.

The payoff is the **Merkle (inclusion) proof**. To prove leaf X is in a tree of N leaves, you do not reveal all N leaves. You reveal X and the ~log₂(N) **sibling hashes** along the path from X up to the root. The verifier hashes X with its sibling, hashes that result with the next sibling, and so on up; if the final value equals the known root, X is proven to be in the set. For N = 1,000,000 that is about 20 hashes instead of a million pieces of data - exponential savings. This is exactly the kind of 'cheap to verify' asymmetry hashing gave us, now applied to set membership.

This is the basis of **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification)** and light clients. A light client downloads only block *headers* (each ~tens of bytes, containing the transaction Merkle root) rather than full blocks. When it wants to confirm 'my transaction is in block 800,000', it asks a full node for a Merkle proof and verifies it against the header's root - trusting math, not the node. Mobile wallets and bridges rely on exactly this.

Ethereum needs more than 'is X in an ordered list' - it needs to commit to a *mutable key→value map* (accounts by address, storage slots by key) and prove statements like 'account A has balance B'. For that it uses a **Merkle-Patricia Trie (MPT)**: a radix trie whose nodes are referenced by hash, combining the prefix-sharing of a trie with the hash-commitment of a Merkle tree. The block header holds a **state root** (commitment to all accounts) and each contract has a **storageRoot**; `eth_getProof` returns Merkle proofs against them. This is precisely what lets rollups post a single state root to L1 and later prove L2 state with a compact proof, and what stateless-client research (Verkle trees) aims to shrink further.

The mental model to carry forward: a Merkle root is a *succinct commitment to a large dataset*, and a Merkle proof is a *logarithmic-size receipt of membership*. You will meet this same shape in blob commitments (EIP-4844), in airdrop allowlists, in ZK Merkle-inclusion circuits, and in every L2's bridge. It is, at bottom, the hash-augmented tree from your DSA course.

subtopics.title: Consensus I — Proof of Work (Nakamoto consensus)
body: Hashing and Merkle trees secure history *given* an agreed sequence of blocks. But who gets to append the next block, and how does a decentralized network with no leader agree on one chain when anyone can propose? This is the **consensus** problem, and Bitcoin's answer - **Proof of Work** - was the breakthrough that made permissionless blockchains possible.

PoW turns block production into a computational lottery. To propose a block, a **miner** assembles candidate transactions, builds the header, and repeatedly varies a **nonce** field, hashing the header each time, searching for a digest numerically *below* a network **target** (equivalently, a hash with at least D leading zero bits). Because the hash is unpredictable, the only strategy is brute-force trial - on average billions of billions of attempts. Finding a valid nonce is therefore *expensive* (proof that you did the work), but *checking* it is a single hash. Asymmetry again.

The **difficulty** (the target) auto-adjusts on a schedule so that, regardless of how much total mining power (**hashrate**) joins or leaves, the average time between blocks stays roughly constant (~10 minutes for Bitcoin). More miners → blocks found faster → difficulty rises to compensate. This self-regulation keeps issuance and timing predictable.

Forks are inevitable because two miners can find valid blocks nearly simultaneously and propagation is not instant. Nakamoto consensus resolves them with the **longest (technically heaviest-work) chain rule**: honest miners always extend the chain with the most cumulative work. Competing tips are temporary; whichever gets the next block first wins, and the orphaned block's transactions return to the mempool. This yields **probabilistic finality** - the deeper a transaction is buried (the more *confirmations* on top), the more work an attacker would need to rewrite it, so confidence rises with depth (the customary '6 confirmations' heuristic).

The attack to understand is the **51% attack**: an entity controlling a majority of hashrate can consistently out-mine the rest, building a private chain longer than the public one and then releasing it to *reorg* history - typically to **double-spend** (spend coins, then erase that spend). It cannot steal arbitrary coins (signatures still gate spending) but it can undo recent transactions. Its defense is economic: acquiring majority hashrate costs enormous capital and energy, and succeeding would devalue the very asset the attacker is mining.

PoW's strengths are battle-tested simplicity and objective, leaderless security. Its weaknesses are the *ongoing energy expenditure* (security equals continuous electricity burn) and a tendency toward mining centralization (cheap-power regions, specialized ASICs). These costs are precisely what motivated Ethereum's move to Proof of Stake.

subtopics.title: Consensus II — Proof of Stake & Ethereum's Gasper
body: **Proof of Stake** replaces 'security from burned electricity' with 'security from bonded capital at risk'. Instead of miners racing on hashpower, **validators** lock up a stake (32 ETH on Ethereum) and are pseudo-randomly chosen to propose and vote on blocks. Misbehavior is punished by **slashing** - destroying part of the stake. The deterrent flips from 'attacking costs you electricity you already spent' to 'attacking costs you your deposit, immediately'.

Ethereum's design (called **Gasper**, after Casper FFG + LMD-GHOST) divides time into 12-second **slots** and 32-slot **epochs** (~6.4 minutes). Each slot has one randomly selected **proposer** who builds the block, and a large **committee** of validators who **attest** (cryptographically vote) for what they see as the head of the chain. With hundreds of thousands of validators, attestations are aggregated; honest participation is rewarded with small issuance, and being offline incurs small penalties.

Two mechanisms run together. **LMD-GHOST** is the fork-choice rule: when there are competing branches, follow the subtree carrying the greatest weight of *Latest Message Driven* attestations - i.e. the branch most validators' most recent votes support. This is a weighted graph traversal over the block tree (the DSA connection is literal). It decides the *head* the chain is currently building on.

**Casper FFG** layers **finality** on top via checkpoint voting at epoch boundaries. When validators representing **two-thirds of total stake** vote to *justify* and then *finalize* a checkpoint, that checkpoint becomes economically irreversible: reverting it would require at least one-third of all staked ETH to have signed conflicting messages, which the protocol can detect and slash. This is **economic finality** - not 'probably safe after 6 blocks' but 'reverting this provably costs the attacker billions in burned stake'. Hence Ethereum's clean notions of 'head', 'justified', and 'finalized' (typically finalized after ~2 epochs, ~13 minutes).

PoS's advantages: ~99.9% less energy, the ability to *penalize* attackers (PoW can only out-compete them, not punish them), and faster, sharper finality. Its debated tradeoffs: greater protocol complexity, the 'nothing-at-stake' and long-range-attack problems (addressed by slashing and weak subjectivity checkpoints), and concerns about stake centralization among large staking pools and liquid-staking providers. Everything in the MEV and Layer-2 topics builds directly on this proposer/attester/committee machinery, so it is worth getting the vocabulary - slot, epoch, attestation, slashing, finality - solid here.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — Proof of Stake (exact consensus page)

subtopics.title: Finality, BFT & the CAP-style tradeoff
body: 'Finality' is the question of *when is a transaction truly irreversible*, and different consensus families answer it differently - a distinction that matters enormously when you design anything that acts on confirmation (exchanges, bridges, payments).

**Probabilistic finality** (Nakamoto PoW, and the fork-choice layer of PoS) never gives a hard guarantee; it gives ever-increasing confidence with depth. A 1-block-deep transaction could still be reorged; a 100-block-deep one is astronomically unlikely to be. You choose a confirmation count based on the value at risk.

**Absolute/economic finality** (Casper FFG, and classical BFT chains) gives a definite point after which reversal is either impossible or provably ruinous. Once Ethereum *finalizes* a checkpoint, clients treat it as permanent. Classical **Byzantine Fault Tolerant** protocols - PBFT and its descendants like **Tendermint** (Cosmos) - finalize *every* block immediately through explicit voting rounds: validators pre-vote and pre-commit, and a block is final once two-thirds commit. The cost is that they require a known validator set and halt (favor consistency over availability) if more than one-third are offline or Byzantine.

This surfaces a fundamental tradeoff reminiscent of CAP. **Availability-favoring** designs (PoW, LMD-GHOST head selection) keep producing blocks even under network partition, accepting temporary forks that resolve later. **Consistency-favoring** designs (Tendermint BFT) refuse to finalize during a partition rather than risk two conflicting finalized histories - they stop rather than fork. Ethereum deliberately blends both: it stays live and produces blocks (availability) while finalizing checkpoints when two-thirds participate (consistency), and will stop *finalizing* (but keep producing) if participation drops.

The 'two-thirds' threshold is not arbitrary: with up to $f$ Byzantine actors you need $3f+1$ total participants to guarantee that any two quorums of $2f+1$ overlap in at least one honest actor, preventing two contradictory decisions. This $n ge 3f+1$ BFT bound is the same result from distributed-systems theory, and it is why slashing-backed PoS targets a two-thirds honest-stake assumption. Practically: when integrating a chain, always ask 'what is this chain's finality model, and how long until I should treat a transaction as irreversible?' - the answer differs by orders of magnitude across PoW, Ethereum PoS, and BFT chains.

subtopics.title: UTXO vs the account model
body: There are two fundamentally different ways to represent 'who owns what' on a ledger, and the choice shapes everything from privacy to smart-contract design. Understanding both - and why each chain picked one - is core literacy.

**UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output)**, used by Bitcoin: there is no stored 'balance' anywhere. The ledger is a set of *unspent outputs*, each a chunk of coin locked to a spending condition (typically 'whoever can sign for this public key'). Your wallet's balance is simply the sum of UTXOs you can unlock - like a pocket full of discrete bills and coins. A transaction *consumes* whole UTXOs as inputs and *creates* new UTXOs as outputs; if you pay 0.7 from a 1.0 UTXO, you create a 0.7 output to the recipient and a 0.3 'change' output back to yourself. Inputs must exactly cover outputs plus fee.

UTXO's properties follow from this: it is naturally **parallelizable** (independent UTXOs can be validated concurrently - no shared mutable balance), it is **stateless** in the sense that validating a transaction only requires checking that its inputs exist and are unspent, and it offers better **privacy hygiene** (new addresses per output, no persistent account identity). The downsides: it is awkward for *stateful* logic (there is no natural 'contract with persistent storage'), and reasoning about complex multi-step applications is hard because state is scattered across outputs.

**Account model**, used by Ethereum: global state is an explicit mapping from address → account record `{nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash}`. A transfer is a simple mutation: decrement the sender's balance, increment the recipient's. This is intuitive (a balance is just a number) and, crucially, ideal for **smart contracts** - a contract *is* an account with code and persistent storage, so stateful applications (a token's balances, an AMM's reserves) map naturally onto persistent account state.

The account model's costs mirror UTXO's benefits inverted: state is *global and mutable*, so execution is essentially **sequential** (two transactions touching the same account cannot be trivially parallelized), and you need a **nonce** per account - a counter incremented each transaction - to prevent replay and to give a deterministic ordering (a transaction with nonce 5 cannot execute before the account's nonce-4 transaction). Nonce management is a real operational concern: a stuck low-nonce transaction blocks all higher-nonce ones from that account.

Neither is 'better' - they are different points on a tradeoff surface. UTXO chains buy parallelism and privacy at the cost of programmability; account chains buy rich, composable smart contracts at the cost of sequential global state. Some systems blend them (Cardano's eUTXO adds programmability to UTXO; Solana adds parallel execution to an account model via explicit account-access declarations). Ethereum's rigid one-key-EOA account model is later softened by **account abstraction** (covered in the ZK topic), which lets accounts define custom validation logic.

resources.label: Bitcoin developer guide — transactions & the UTXO set

subtopics.title: P2P networking, the mempool & gossip
body: A blockchain has no central server - it is a **peer-to-peer** network of nodes, each holding (some of) the chain and relaying data to others. How nodes find each other and how transactions and blocks propagate is not incidental plumbing; it directly determines fork rates, MEV, and decentralization, so it is worth understanding concretely.

**Peer discovery** uses a structured overlay. Ethereum's `discv5` is a **Kademlia-style DHT**: each node has an ID, 'distance' between IDs is defined by XOR, and nodes maintain routing tables of peers at exponentially spaced distances, enabling $O(log N)$ lookups to find peers responsible for any target. This is the same DHT design behind BitTorrent and other distributed systems - reused, not reinvented. A new node bootstraps from a few hardcoded nodes, then fills its peer table via the DHT.

**Propagation** works by **gossip** (epidemic broadcast): when a node hears a new transaction or block, it forwards it to a subset of its peers, who forward to theirs, and within a few hops the message floods the entire network. Gossip is robust (no single point of failure, tolerates churn) but *not instantaneous* - there is a propagation delay of hundreds of milliseconds to seconds across the global network, and this delay is exactly why two proposers can briefly build competing blocks before the fork-choice rule resolves the fork.

Between 'broadcast' and 'included in a block', a pending transaction sits in each node's **mempool** (memory pool) - the set of valid, unconfirmed transactions waiting to be picked up by a proposer. This is a deceptively important object. It is **public**: anyone running a node can watch transactions *before* they are mined. That visibility is the entire foundation of **MEV** (a later topic) - searchers scan the mempool for profitable opportunities (a large swap about to move a price, a loan about to become liquidatable) and craft transactions to exploit the predictable consequences. It is also why privacy-preserving submission (private relays like Flashbots Protect) exists: to keep a transaction *out* of the public mempool so it cannot be front-run.

Latency has economic weight. Because propagation is finite, validators and searchers who are better-connected (colocated, peered with builders, running optimized networking) gain an edge - the same low-latency arms race as HFT, transplanted on-chain. This is the literal connection to the low-level networking domain: kernel-bypass, optimized gossip, and geographic placement matter once money depends on milliseconds.

A concrete way to make all of this real: run a node (or use a public node's mempool stream), watch pending transactions arrive in real time, and trace one transaction from 'seen in mempool' to 'included in block N' to 'finalized'. Seeing the lifecycle live collapses a lot of abstract description into intuition - and sets up the EVM topic, which picks up exactly where 'included in a block' leaves off.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — how Ethereum works (dev docs intro)
But how does Bitcoin actually work? — 3Blue1Brown

connections.note: Merkle trees are hash-augmented binary trees with logarithmic inclusion proofs
Keccak-256 is the hashing primitive behind content addressing and Merkle roots
Fork-choice (LMD-GHOST) and gossip/DHT routing are graph algorithms

topics.title: Ethereum & the EVM
body: The previous topic ended at 'a transaction is included in a block'. This one picks up there and goes inside the machine. Ethereum is best understood as a **deterministic world computer**: a single global state, advanced by transactions, where the rules of advancement are executed by the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**. Every node runs the same EVM on the same transactions and must arrive at the same new state - that determinism is what makes consensus over computation possible.

We will build the full picture: the two account types, how a transaction is signed and travels through its lifecycle, how **gas** prices computation and how EIP-1559 sets fees, the EVM's three-region execution architecture (stack/memory/storage), how raw **opcodes** and bytecode actually run, and how the **Merkle-Patricia Trie** commits the whole world state to a single root. By the end you should be able to read a contract's bytecode on evm.codes and explain what every gas charge is for.

subtopics.title: Accounts: EOA vs contract
body: Ethereum's state is one mapping from 20-byte address → account, and there are exactly two kinds of account, distinguished only by whether they have code. An **Externally Owned Account (EOA)** is controlled by a private key: it has a balance and a nonce but no code and no storage. A **contract account** has code (`codeHash`) and persistent storage (`storageRoot`) but no private key - it cannot initiate anything on its own; it only runs when something calls it.

Every account, of either type, stores exactly four fields. The **nonce** is a counter: for an EOA it is the number of transactions it has sent (incremented each time), and for a contract it is the number of contracts it has created. The **balance** is the account's ether holdings, denominated in **wei** (1 ETH = 10¹⁸ wei - always reason in wei to avoid rounding bugs). The **storageRoot** is the Merkle-Patricia root of the contract's storage trie (empty for an EOA). The **codeHash** is the hash of the account's EVM bytecode (the hash of empty code marks an EOA; this is how the protocol tells the two apart).

The nonce does two jobs worth dwelling on. First, **replay protection**: because each transaction must carry the sender's current nonce and the protocol rejects a nonce it has already seen, you cannot rebroadcast someone's old signed transaction to repeat it. Second, **strict ordering**: transactions from one account execute in nonce order, so a transaction with nonce 5 will not be included until the nonce-4 transaction has been. This is why a single stuck (underpriced) transaction can block every later transaction from the same account - a common operational gotcha - and why deterministic contract addresses for plain `CREATE` are derived from `(deployer, nonce)`.

The single most important structural fact: **contracts cannot act autonomously**. There is no cron, no background thread, no self-firing code on Ethereum. *Nothing happens without an EOA-signed transaction at the root of the call tree.* An EOA's transaction can call a contract, which can call another contract, which can call ten more - an arbitrarily deep call tree - but the spark that ignites any execution is always an externally signed transaction. This is why protocols that need 'do X when condition Y holds' (liquidations, limit orders, scheduled rebases) depend on **keeper bots** - off-chain agents that watch for the condition and send the triggering transaction (often incentivized by a reward). It is also part of the motivation for account abstraction, which lets the *account* carry richer logic even though the trigger is still external.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — accounts (EOA vs contract, the four fields)

subtopics.title: The transaction: structure, signing, lifecycle
body: A transaction is a single signed message that is the *only* way to change Ethereum's state. Its core fields are: `nonce` (sender's ordering counter), `to` (recipient address, or empty for contract creation), `value` (wei to transfer), `data` (arbitrary calldata - the payload), `gasLimit` (max gas you authorize), and the fee fields (`maxFeePerGas`, `maxPriorityFeePerGas` post-EIP-1559). There is no explicit 'from' field - and that absence is the whole point of the next paragraph.

Transactions are signed with **ECDSA over the secp256k1 curve** - the same curve and signature scheme Bitcoin uses. You hash the transaction's fields, sign that hash with your private key, and attach the signature as `(v, r, s)`. The sender address is then *recovered* from the signature via `ecrecover` rather than asserted: given the message hash and `(v, r, s)`, the curve math reproduces the signer's public key, from which the address is derived. The implication is strong - you cannot forge a 'from' without the private key, and the protocol does not need to store who signed; it derives it. (The `v` value also encodes the chain id since EIP-155, which prevents replaying a mainnet transaction on another chain.)

The **lifecycle**, step by step: (1) you construct and sign the transaction locally (in a wallet); (2) you broadcast it to a node, which validates it - signature recovers to an account with sufficient balance to cover `value + gasLimit × maxFeePerGas`, and the nonce is the account's next expected nonce; (3) the node adds it to its **mempool** and gossips it to peers; (4) the current slot's **proposer** (or, in practice, a block **builder** - see MEV) selects transactions, almost always prioritizing higher fees, and chooses their *order*; (5) the EVM executes them sequentially against the current state, producing a new state root and a **receipt** per transaction (status, gas used, logs); (6) the block is proposed, attested, and eventually **finalized**, at which point the state change is irreversible.

The `data` field is where the richness lives, and learning to read it is a genuine skill. For a plain ETH transfer between EOAs, `data` is empty. For a contract call, `data` is the ABI encoding: the first 4 bytes are the **function selector** (the first 4 bytes of `keccak256("transfer(address,uint256)")`), followed by the 32-byte-padded arguments. For a contract *creation*, `to` is empty and `data` is the contract's init bytecode. On a block explorer, decoding the input data of any transaction shows you precisely which function was called with which arguments - the fastest way to understand what a transaction *did* rather than what its sender claims.

One more subtlety: a transaction either fully succeeds or fully **reverts** (all-or-nothing within the EVM), but a *reverted* transaction is still included in a block and still costs gas for the work performed before the revert. 'Included' is not 'succeeded' - always check the receipt's status. This atomicity is also what makes flash loans and complex multi-step DeFi safe to attempt: if any step fails, the whole thing unwinds.

subtopics.title: Gas: metering computation
body: Because the EVM is Turing-complete and every node must execute every transaction, an unmetered loop could halt the entire network (the halting problem made economic). **Gas** is the solution: every operation costs a fixed amount of gas reflecting its computational, bandwidth, and (especially) state-storage burden, and you must pay for what you use. Gas decouples the *amount* of work (measured in gas units, a protocol constant per opcode) from the *price* of work (gas price in ETH, set by the market).

A rough cost ladder builds intuition: an arithmetic op like `ADD` costs 3 gas; a memory expansion is cheap but grows quadratically; reading storage (`SLOAD`) costs ~2,100 gas cold / 100 warm; *writing* a storage slot from zero to nonzero (`SSTORE`) costs 20,000 gas; deploying contract code costs 200 gas per byte; the base cost of any transaction is 21,000 gas. The dominance of storage costs - storage is 3-4 orders of magnitude more expensive than arithmetic - is the single most important fact for gas optimization, and it is *because* storage is permanent global state that every node must keep forever.

You set a **gasLimit** (the maximum gas you authorize) and the EVM meters execution against it. If execution finishes under the limit, you pay only for what was used and the rest is not charged. If execution *hits* the limit mid-way, the EVM throws **out-of-gas**, reverts all state changes - but you still pay for all the gas consumed up to that point. This 'revert but still pay' rule is the anti-DoS mechanism: an attacker cannot make nodes do unbounded free work, because work always costs gas whether or not it ultimately succeeds.

Estimating gasLimit matters in practice. Wallets simulate the transaction to estimate gas, but state can change between estimation and execution (e.g. a branch that writes a fresh storage slot now versus an already-set one), so a too-tight limit causes out-of-gas reverts and a too-loose one risks more cost only if the work actually runs. Understanding that gas equals 'units of metered work' (and that storage writes dominate) is what lets you reason about why one transaction costs $2 and another costs $200.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — gas and fees (units, limits, out-of-gas)

subtopics.title: EIP-1559: the fee market
body: Before EIP-1559, Ethereum used a first-price auction: you guessed a gas price, and proposers took the highest bids. It was inefficient (chronic overpaying and failed underbids) and volatile. **EIP-1559** (live since the London upgrade, August 2021) restructured the fee per gas into two components with very different destinations and dynamics.

The **base fee** is set *by the protocol*, not by users, and is **burned** (permanently removed from supply). It adjusts every block by a deterministic rule: each block has a target size (15M gas) and a max (30M); if the previous block was above target, the base fee rises by up to 12.5%, and if below, it falls by up to 12.5%. This makes the base fee an automatic **congestion controller** that converges toward a price where blocks sit ~50% full, so users can predict the next block's base fee precisely instead of guessing. Burning it also means heavy network usage is *deflationary* for ETH (the 'ultrasound money' narrative).

The **priority fee** (the 'tip') goes to the block proposer and is how you *bid for faster inclusion* when blocks are contended. You also set a `maxFeePerGas` - the absolute ceiling you will pay per gas - and the protocol charges `baseFee + min(priorityFee, maxFeePerGas - baseFee)`, refunding any difference. So your total cost ≈ `gasUsed × (baseFee + priorityFee)`, capped by `gasUsed × maxFeePerGas`.

The practical consequences are everywhere. During demand spikes - a hyped NFT mint, a market crash triggering mass liquidations - block space is scarce, the base fee ratchets up rapidly, and priority-fee bidding wars ensue; this is exactly the contention that MEV searchers compete in (they bid large priority fees, or pay builders directly, to win ordering). And it sharpens the incentive for **gas optimization** in Solidity: every storage write you eliminate, every variable you pack into a shared slot, directly reduces `gasUsed` and thus user cost, which matters most precisely when fees are high.

resources.label: EIP-1559 — fee market change (the exact specification)

subtopics.title: EVM architecture: stack, memory, storage
body: The EVM is a **deterministic, quasi-Turing-complete, 256-bit, big-endian stack machine**. Unpack that: *deterministic* (same input + state → same output on every node), *quasi-Turing-complete* (it can compute anything, but gas bounds execution so it always halts), *256-bit* (its native word is 32 bytes, chosen to match Keccak-256 output and elliptic-curve field sizes - which is why `uint256` is the cheapest, most natural integer type), and a *stack machine* (no registers; operands are pushed and popped on a stack).

There are three data regions, and the entire art of EVM programming and gas optimization is understanding their cost and lifetime. The **stack** holds up to **1024** 256-bit words and is where computation happens: opcodes pop their inputs off the top and push results. It is essentially free but tiny - exceed 1024 depth (e.g. via deep recursion or too many local variables) and you hit a stack-too-deep error.

**Memory** is a linear, byte-addressable scratchpad, zero-initialized and *wiped at the end of each call*. It is cheap to use but volatile - it exists only for the duration of the current execution frame. Its cost grows **quadratically** as you expand it (to disincentivize using memory as cheap storage), so reading/writing a few KB is fine but ballooning memory is expensive. Memory is where you build dynamic arrays, ABI-encode call data, and stage return values.

**Storage** is the persistent `uint256 → uint256` key-value map that *is* the contract's part of the world state (committed in its `storageRoot`). It survives across transactions forever, and it is therefore *extremely* expensive: `SSTORE` and `SLOAD` cost orders of magnitude more than stack/memory ops, because every full node on Earth must store and maintain that data indefinitely. This single asymmetry drives almost all of Solidity gas optimization: keep working data on the stack and in memory, touch storage as rarely as possible, batch writes, and pack multiple small variables into one 32-byte slot (covered in the Solidity topic).

The clean analogy to systems programming: stack ≈ CPU registers/stack (fast, tiny, ephemeral), memory ≈ process heap (cheap, larger, freed on return), storage ≈ disk/persistent DB (slow, expensive, permanent, globally replicated). If you have written low-level code, this maps almost one-to-one - which is why systems programmers pick up the EVM quickly. There are also **transient storage** (EIP-1153, `TSTORE`/`TLOAD`) for cheap within-transaction persistence, and **calldata** (read-only transaction input), which we treat in the Solidity data-locations subtopic.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — the EVM (architecture, the exact internals page)

subtopics.title: Opcodes, bytecode & reading the machine
body: Compiled Solidity becomes **EVM bytecode**: a flat byte sequence of ~140 distinct **opcodes**, each one byte, interleaved with the immediate data of `PUSH` instructions. There are no functions, variables, types, or scopes at this level - only a stack, three memory regions, and jumps. Learning to read bytecode is the same cognitive skill as reading assembly, and it demystifies contracts completely.

The opcode families: **arithmetic/logic** (`ADD`, `MUL`, `SUB`, `DIV`, `MOD`, `EXP`, `LT`, `GT`, `EQ`, `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`); **environment/context** (`CALLER` = msg.sender, `CALLVALUE` = msg.value, `CALLDATALOAD`, `ADDRESS`, `TIMESTAMP`, `NUMBER`); **memory/storage** (`MLOAD`, `MSTORE`, `SLOAD`, `SSTORE`); **control flow** (`JUMP`, `JUMPI` for conditional jump, and `JUMPDEST` which marks legal jump targets - you can only jump to a `JUMPDEST`); **logging** (`LOG0`-`LOG4` for events); and **call/create** (`CALL`, `STATICCALL`, `DELEGATECALL`, `CREATE`, `CREATE2`).

**Function dispatch** is worth seeing explicitly. A contract has no built-in notion of methods; instead, compiled contracts begin with a **dispatcher**: they load the first 4 bytes of calldata (the function selector), then compare it against each function's selector (`keccak256` of the signature, first 4 bytes) and `JUMPI` to the matching code block, reverting if none match. So calling `transfer(to, amount)` really means: send calldata whose first 4 bytes are `0xa9059cbb` followed by the encoded arguments, and the dispatcher routes to the transfer logic. Selector collisions (two functions with the same first-4-bytes hash) are a real, occasionally exploited edge case.

The most consequential opcode to understand deeply is **`DELEGATECALL`**. A normal `CALL` to contract B runs B's code in B's context (B's storage, `msg.sender` = the caller). `DELEGATECALL` runs B's code in *the caller's* context - the caller's storage, the original `msg.sender` and `msg.value` preserved. This is what makes **proxy/upgradeable** contracts possible (the proxy holds state and delegatecalls into a swappable logic contract) and is simultaneously the source of the nastiest bugs (storage collisions, the Parity self-destruct freeze) - all detailed in the Solidity and Security topics. `STATICCALL` is `CALL` that forbids state changes (used for `view` calls), and `CREATE2` deploys to a deterministic, pre-computable address (used by factories and account abstraction).

The fastest way to truly internalize all of this: open **evm.codes**, paste a small contract's bytecode (or write a few opcodes in its playground), and *single-step* through execution, watching the stack, memory, and storage mutate with each instruction and seeing the gas meter tick. An hour of stepping teaches more than any amount of reading - and it builds exactly the reverse-engineering intuition you will use when auditing or analyzing unverified contracts.

resources.label: evm.codes — interactive opcode reference & step-through playground
Ethereum.org — opcodes reference (gas + semantics per opcode)

subtopics.title: World state & the Merkle-Patricia Trie
body: Everything above operates on Ethereum's **world state**: a single mapping from every 20-byte address to its account record, where each contract's storage is itself a mapping from 256-bit key to 256-bit value. The protocol must commit this entire, *mutable* dataset to one 32-byte root per block - so that block headers are tiny, light clients can verify facts, and rollups can post and prove state. The structure that achieves this is the **Merkle-Patricia Trie (MPT)**.

An MPT combines two ideas. A **Patricia (radix) trie** stores key→value pairs by walking the key's nibbles down a tree, sharing common prefixes (efficient for the sparse, hex-keyed address space). The **Merkle** part means each node is identified and linked by the *hash* of its contents, exactly like a Merkle tree - so the root hash commits to the whole structure, and any change to any value propagates up to change the root. Ethereum's MPT has three node types (branch, extension, leaf) plus an RLP encoding for serialization; the details matter when you implement one, but the conceptual takeaway is 'a hash-committed, prefix-sharing key-value map'.

Two roots are central. The **state root** in each block header commits to *all accounts* (address → account record). Each contract account's **storageRoot** commits to *that contract's storage slots* (slot key → value). Because both are Merkle structures, you can produce a compact **proof** that 'account A has balance B and nonce N' or 'slot S of contract C equals V' that verifies against just the root - which is exactly what the `eth_getProof` RPC returns. This is the machinery behind trustless light clients (verify state from headers + proofs, no full node) and behind both optimistic and ZK rollups (which commit L2 state as a root on L1 and later prove things about it).

The MPT has a known weakness: its proofs are relatively large (a path of branch nodes, each up to 16 children), which is a barrier to **stateless clients** (clients that hold no state and verify everything via proofs). The planned upgrade is **Verkle trees**, which replace the hash-based Merkle commitments with **polynomial/vector commitments (KZG-style)** to produce dramatically smaller proofs - a direct example of advanced cryptography reshaping a core data structure to unlock a scaling property. Whether MPT or Verkle, the role is identical: turn a huge mutable state into one verifiable root, so the rest of the system can reason about state succinctly. That capability is the quiet foundation under light clients, bridges, and the entire L2 ecosystem in later topics.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — Merkle-Patricia Trie (state encoding, exact page)
Ethereum Yellow Paper — formal state & EVM specification
