The EVM is exactly the stack-based bytecode VM studied in compilers
Reading EVM opcodes is the same skill as reading x86/ARM assembly

topics.title: Solidity
body: Solidity is the dominant smart-contract language, and the gap between 'can write Solidity' and 'understands Solidity' is exactly the gap between writing contracts that compile and writing contracts that are safe and cheap. The difference lives at the boundary where the language meets the EVM: how variables map to 32-byte storage slots, what `memory` vs `storage` vs `calldata` really mean, how visibility and mutability are enforced, how events leave the contract, how reverts unwind state, and how multiple inheritance resolves.

This topic treats Solidity as 'a typed surface over the EVM you learned in the last topic'. Every subtopic ties a language feature back to its EVM mechanics, because that is where the gas wins and the bugs both live. The end goal is to read any contract and see not just the logic but the storage layout, the call semantics, and the attack surface underneath it.

subtopics.title: Storage layout & variable packing
body: A contract's state variables are laid out in **32-byte storage slots**, assigned **sequentially in declaration order** starting at slot 0. This is not an implementation detail you can ignore - it is directly observable (anyone can read your contract's slots with `eth_getStorageAt`), it dominates gas cost, and it is the root of a whole class of upgrade bugs. Understanding it is the difference between a contract that costs 80k gas and one that costs 200k for the same logic.

**Packing**: the compiler will fit multiple consecutive variables into a single slot if they collectively fit in 32 bytes. Declaring `uint128 a; uint64 b; uint64 c;` places all three in slot 0 (16 + 8 + 8 = 32 bytes), so initializing them touches one slot, not three. But declaring `uint128 a; uint256 big; uint64 b;` cannot pack `a` and `b` together because `big` (a full slot) sits between them - so reordering to `uint256 big; uint128 a; uint64 b;` packs `a` and `b` into one slot and saves a `SSTORE`. Because a cold `SSTORE` from zero is ~20,000 gas, careful ordering is one of the highest-leverage optimizations available, and the first thing a reviewer checks.

**Dynamic types do not fit inline**, so they use a hashing scheme you must know to read storage. For a dynamic **array** declared at slot `p`: the array's *length* is stored at slot `p`, and its *elements* live consecutively starting at `keccak256(p)`. For a **mapping** declared at slot `p`: there is nothing stored at `p` itself; the value for key `k` is stored at `keccak256(h(k) ‖ p)` (key padded/encoded, concatenated with the slot, hashed). This is *why mappings cannot be iterated* - there is no stored list of keys, only hash-derived locations, so you cannot enumerate them without separately tracking keys. Nested mappings hash recursively. Knowing these formulas lets you read any contract's private state directly from the chain - 'private' in Solidity restricts *calling*, never *reading*.

**Constants and immutables** are special: `constant` values are inlined into the bytecode at compile time (no storage at all), and `immutable` values are set once in the constructor and baked into the deployed code (read like constants, no `SLOAD`) - both are far cheaper than regular storage variables and should be used whenever a value never changes after deployment.

Finally, storage layout is a **security surface**, which is why it recurs in the Security topic. Upgradeable **proxies** use `DELEGATECALL`, so the *implementation's* code writes into the *proxy's* slots by slot number. If the proxy and the implementation - or two versions of the implementation - disagree about which variable occupies which slot, a write meant for `owner` can land on `paused` or `totalSupply`, silently corrupting state. This is a **storage collision**, and the standard defenses (EIP-1967's fixed pseudo-random slots for proxy metadata, storage gaps, and the Diamond pattern's namespaced storage) only make sense once you understand the sequential-slot layout above.

resources.label: Solidity docs — layout of state variables in storage (exact page)

subtopics.title: Data locations: storage, memory, calldata
body: Every reference type - arrays, structs, `string`, `bytes`, mappings - lives in one of three **data locations**, and choosing the wrong one is either a gas waste or a correctness bug. Value types (`uint`, `bool`, `address`, fixed `bytesN`) are copied by value and do not need a location; reference types do. The three locations map directly onto the EVM regions from the previous topic.

**storage** is the persistent on-chain state. Critically, a `storage` reference is a **pointer into state**, not a copy - so writing through it mutates the contract permanently. **memory** is a temporary, mutable, in-call scratch copy that is discarded when the function returns. **calldata** is the **read-only, immutable** region holding the original transaction input - it cannot be modified, and because it is the raw input there is no copy cost to read from it.

The rules that follow are practical and worth memorizing. Parameters of `external` functions should be `calldata` whenever you do not need to mutate them - it is the cheapest option because it avoids copying input into memory. Use `memory` when you need a mutable working copy of data (e.g. building an array to return). Take a `storage` reference *only* when you intend to read-modify-write state, and do it deliberately.

The classic, costly bug is **aliasing vs copying** with structs and arrays. Consider a `Position[] positions` in storage. Writing `Position storage p = positions[i]; p.size += 1;` takes a *pointer* to the stored struct and the increment persists on-chain - correct if that is what you want. But writing `Position memory p = positions[i]; p.size += 1;` makes a *memory copy*, increments the copy, and the copy vanishes at function end - the on-chain value is unchanged, and the bug is silent (no error, just lost updates). Reviewers specifically scan for `memory` where `storage` was intended and vice versa.

This is exactly the stack/heap-copy versus pointer distinction from systems programming, transplanted on-chain with gas attached - which is why C and Rust programmers internalize Solidity's memory model fast. The mental check before every reference-type declaration: *do I need to persist a change (storage), work on a throwaway copy (memory), or just read the input cheaply (calldata)?*

resources.label: Solidity docs — data location & assignment semantics

subtopics.title: Visibility, state mutability & modifiers
body: **Visibility** controls who may call a function or read a variable, and there are four levels. `external` functions are callable only from outside the contract (their arguments live in calldata - cheapest for large inputs). `public` functions are callable both externally and internally, and for public *state variables* the compiler auto-generates a getter. `internal` is callable only from this contract and contracts that inherit it. `private` is callable only from the exact contract that declares it (not even derived contracts).

The non-obvious and safety-critical point: visibility restricts **calling**, never **data confidentiality**. All contract storage is publicly readable on-chain regardless of `private` - so a `private` password, seed, or 'commit' value is fully exposed to anyone who reads the slot. 'Private' is an access-control keyword, not an encryption one; treating it as secrecy has caused real losses. If you need confidentiality you need commit-reveal schemes or off-chain cryptography (and ultimately the ZK/FHE primitives in the later topic).

**State mutability** annotations let callers and the EVM reason about side effects. A `view` function promises not to modify state (it may read); it can be called for free off-chain via `STATICCALL` since it changes nothing. A `pure` function promises not even to read state - it depends only on its inputs. **`payable`** is required for a function to accept ether: a non-`payable` function reverts if any `msg.value` is sent to it. Forgetting `payable` on a function meant to receive funds, or marking something `payable` that should not be, are common mistakes; relatedly, the special `receive()` and `fallback()` functions govern what happens when a contract is sent plain ETH or an unknown selector.

The authentication footgun lives here: use **`msg.sender`**, never **`tx.origin`**, for access control. `msg.sender` is the immediate caller; `tx.origin` is the original EOA at the root of the whole call chain. `require(tx.origin == owner)` is exploitable - if the owner is lured into calling a malicious contract, that contract calls your function while `tx.origin` is still the owner, so the check passes for the attacker. This recurs as a named vulnerability in the Security topic.

**Modifiers** are reusable wrappers that run guard logic around a function body, with `_;` marking where the body executes. They are the idiom for centralizing checks - `onlyOwner` (access control), `nonReentrant` (a lock flag flipped during the call), `whenNotPaused` (circuit breaker). They make intent legible and reduce copy-paste, but they have their own hazards: a modifier that places `_;` *before* its check, or omits `_;` entirely, silently disables the function or its guard. In production you typically inherit audited modifiers from OpenZeppelin (`Ownable`, `ReentrancyGuard`, `Pausable`, `AccessControl`) rather than hand-rolling them.

resources.label: Solidity docs — visibility, getters & state mutability

subtopics.title: Events, logs & the ABI
body: Contracts communicate with the outside world through **events**, which the EVM records as **logs** in each transaction's receipt (via the `LOG0`-`LOG4` opcodes). Logs are how frontends update, how indexers like The Graph build queryable databases, and how anyone reconstructs 'what happened' - a transfer, a swap, an ownership change. They are dramatically cheaper than storage (logs are not part of the state that nodes must keep forever; they live in receipts), which is why emitting an event is the right way to expose information that on-chain logic does not itself need.

An event can mark up to **three parameters as `indexed`**, which become **topics** - separately stored, efficiently filterable fields. The remaining (non-indexed) parameters are ABI-encoded into the log's `data` blob. Indexing the fields you will filter on (e.g. `indexed address from`, `indexed address to` in a `Transfer`) lets off-chain consumers query 'all transfers to address X' efficiently; over-indexing wastes a topic slot, under-indexing forces consumers to scan and decode everything.

The asymmetry to burn in: **a contract can emit logs but cannot read them.** Logs exist for external observers, not for on-chain logic - there is no opcode to read past logs from within a contract. So you must never design a contract whose behavior depends on reading its own (or another contract's) emitted events. Anything a contract needs to act upon must live in **storage**, even though storage is far more expensive. This is a frequent source of confusion for newcomers who try to 'remember' something cheaply via an event.

The **ABI (Application Binary Interface)** is the JSON schema that defines how to encode calls to a contract and decode its returns and events. It specifies each function's **selector** (the first 4 bytes of `keccak256` of the canonical signature, e.g. `transfer(address,uint256)`), the encoding rules for arguments (static types packed into 32-byte words; dynamic types - arrays, bytes, strings - encoded as an offset pointer plus a length-prefixed tail), and the event topic/data layout. The ABI is the bridge between human-readable Solidity and the raw `data` field the EVM consumes.

Because the ABI fully describes the interface, *any* tool with a contract's ABI and address can interact with it - `cast`, ethers.js/viem, a block explorer's 'Read/Write Contract' tab - without the source. Conversely, decoding an unknown transaction's calldata against a candidate ABI (or via a 4-byte selector database) is how you reverse-engineer what an unverified contract call did. Understanding ABI encoding is also necessary for low-level `abi.encode`/`abi.encodePacked` (note: `encodePacked` can produce hash collisions across different argument arrangements - a real signature-bug source) and for constructing the calldata of `call`/`delegatecall` by hand.

resources.label: Solidity docs — contract ABI specification (encoding rules, selectors)

subtopics.title: Errors, reverts & the execution model
body: Solidity's error handling is built on the EVM's **revert** semantics, and understanding it is what makes 'check a condition, otherwise undo everything' a safe, atomic operation. When a call frame reverts - via `require`, `revert`, a failed `assert`, an out-of-gas, or a low-level failure - **all state changes made in that frame are rolled back atomically**, as if the call never executed, and control returns to the caller with a failure signal. Remaining gas is returned, but gas already consumed is not refunded.

The three tools and when to use them: **`require(condition, ...)`** validates inputs and preconditions (caller has enough balance, deadline not passed, address not zero) and reverts if false - this is your everyday guard. **`revert CustomError(args)`** triggers a revert with structured data; **custom errors** (declared as `error InsufficientBalance(uint256 available, uint256 required);`) are strongly preferred over `require` with a string message because they are far cheaper (a 4-byte selector plus ABI-encoded args versus storing and returning a whole string) and they carry machine-readable data for callers and tests. **`assert(condition)`** is reserved for invariants that should be *impossible* to violate - it signals a bug in your own logic, not bad user input, and you should never expect it to fire in correct code.

Reverts **propagate up the call stack** by default - an inner revert bubbles out and reverts the whole transaction - which is the desired behavior for atomic operations (the entire flash-loan or multi-step trade unwinds if any step fails). But sometimes you call an *untrusted* contract and do not want its failure to take you down. For that, Solidity has **`try/catch`** around external calls and contract creations: you can catch a revert (with or without its reason/custom error), and handle it gracefully (skip a failing recipient, log and continue). This is essential when iterating over user-supplied addresses or integrating third-party protocols.

Low-level calls are a distinct and dangerous case. `address.call(...)`, `delegatecall`, and `staticcall` return a `(bool success, bytes data)` tuple and **do not revert automatically on failure** - they just return `success == false`. Ignoring that boolean is a classic bug: the call silently fails, your contract proceeds as if it succeeded, and funds or state diverge. Always check the returned `success` (and decode/handle `data`), or use a checked wrapper. The same applies to ERC-20 tokens that return `false` instead of reverting on failed transfers - hence the ubiquity of `SafeERC20`.

Tying it together: the revert model is *why* the EVM's all-or-nothing transaction atomicity (from the previous topic) is so powerful. You can attempt a complex sequence, assert your invariants along the way, and trust that any violation cleanly erases the entire attempt. Designing contracts around 'validate first (checks), then update all state (effects), then make external calls (interactions)' - the CEI pattern - leverages exactly this, and is the foundation of reentrancy safety in the Security topic.

resources.label: Solidity docs — error handling: require, revert, custom errors, try/catch

subtopics.title: Inheritance, libraries & C3 linearization
body: Solidity supports **multiple inheritance**, which raises the classic diamond-ambiguity problem: if two base contracts both define `foo()`, which runs? Solidity resolves it with **C3 linearization** - the very same algorithm Python uses for its Method Resolution Order. You list base contracts most-base-first, the compiler computes a single linear ordering consistent with all the inheritance constraints, and `super.foo()` calls the *next* implementation in that linearized chain rather than a single fixed parent.

This is exactly what makes composable **mixins** work. OpenZeppelin's design - stacking `Context`, `Ownable`, `Pausable`, `ReentrancyGuard`, `ERC20` - relies on linearization so that overridden functions chain correctly via `super`. The practical rules: declare bases in a valid order (a contract that overrides another must be listed *after* it, i.e. more-derived = later), mark base functions `virtual` to allow overriding and overriding functions `override` (listing all overridden bases when there are several), and be deliberate about how constructors run along the chain (base constructors execute in linearized order). Getting the order or the `virtual/override` wiring wrong does not usually error - it silently changes *which* implementation actually executes, which can be a serious bug.

**Libraries** are a related reuse mechanism with different mechanics. A `library` is stateless code; its `internal` functions are inlined into the calling contract (cheap, no external call), while `external`/`public` library functions are deployed once and invoked via `DELEGATECALL` (running in the caller's context - so a shared library can operate on the caller's storage). The `using LibName for Type;` syntax attaches library functions to a type as if they were methods (`array.sum()`), which is how `SafeERC20`, `EnumerableSet`, and math libraries read so cleanly. Because public library calls use delegatecall, they carry the same context caveats discussed for proxies.

Abstract contracts and **interfaces** round out the toolkit: an `interface` declares function signatures with no implementation (the standard way to type an external contract you call, e.g. `IERC20`), and `abstract` contracts provide partial implementations meant to be completed by children. Interfaces are how you interact with arbitrary deployed contracts in a type-safe way and how standards (ERC-20, ERC-721) are expressed.

In real-world development you will almost never write base contracts from scratch - you will inherit audited, battle-tested ones (OpenZeppelin, Solmate/Solady for gas-optimized variants) and compose them. The actual skill, then, is *composing correctly*: understanding linearization well enough to know which `super` call runs when, ordering your bases right, wiring `virtual`/`override`, and initializing along the chain (especially under the upgradeable/initializer pattern, where constructors are replaced by `initialize` functions and getting the init order wrong leaves a contract exploitably uninitialized - again, the Security topic).

resources.label: OpenZeppelin Contracts — audited, composable base contracts

topics.title: DeFi primitives
body: DeFi (decentralized finance) is financial engineering rebuilt as open, composable, public smart contracts - and that openness is its defining feature. Every reserve, every interest-rate curve, every liquidation parameter is on-chain and readable, protocols snap together like Lego (an AMM feeds a lending market feeds a yield aggregator), and the same transparency that enables composability also means every bug is a public, instantly-exploitable bounty. To work in DeFi you need the *math* of each primitive (so you can reason about value flows and risk) and the *mechanism* (so you can see where it breaks).

This topic walks the core primitives in dependency order: AMMs and the constant-product curve, the impermanent-loss math that governs LP profitability, Uniswap v3's concentrated liquidity, lending markets and liquidations, the three stablecoin designs, oracles (the lynchpin of solvency), bridges (the most-hacked component), and the token standards plus proxy patterns that everything is built from. The AMM math connects directly to market microstructure, and the oracle/bridge sections connect directly to the Security and MEV topics.

subtopics.title: AMMs & the constant-product formula (Uniswap v2)
body: Traditional markets match buyers and sellers via an **order book**. An **Automated Market Maker** replaces that with a **liquidity pool** and a deterministic pricing **curve**, so anyone can trade against the pool at any time with no counterparty needed - the pool *is* the counterparty, and its math sets the price. This is the foundational DeFi primitive, and Uniswap v2's design is the one to understand completely first.

Uniswap v2 uses the **constant-product** formula $x cdot y = k$, where `x` and `y` are the pool's reserves of token A and token B, and `k` must stay constant across any swap (ignoring fees). The **spot price** of A in terms of B is simply the reserve ratio $y / x$. So price is fully determined by the *composition* of the pool - no oracle, no order book, just the ratio of what is in it. Liquidity providers (LPs) deposit both tokens in proportion and receive **LP tokens** representing their share; they earn the trading fees.

Derive the swap output yourself once and it sticks. To swap $Delta x$ of token A in for token B out, the product must be preserved: $(x + Delta x)(y - Delta y) = x cdot y = k$. Solving, $Delta y = y - k/(x + Delta x) = (y cdot Delta x)/(x + Delta x)$. Add the **0.3% fee** by using $Delta x_{	ext{eff}} = 0.997 cdot Delta x$ in that formula (the fee stays in the pool, accruing to LPs). Notice the structure: the larger your trade relative to the reserves, the worse your effective price - this is **price impact / slippage**, and it shrinks as pool depth grows. Deep liquidity = small slippage; thin pools = brutal slippage. This is why 'total value locked' and pool depth matter so much.

A crucial property falls out: any trade *moves the price along the curve*, so the moment the pool's price diverges from the broader market price, there is a risk-free **arbitrage** - buy the underpriced side from the pool, sell elsewhere, until the pool price re-converges. This arbitrage is what keeps AMM prices honest and tethered to the real market, and it is exactly the activity MEV searchers race to capture (the MEV topic). The same mechanism is why a large swap is sandwich-able: it predictably moves price, and bots can trade just before and after it.

The constant-product curve is one of a family. **Curve's StableSwap** uses a flattened hybrid curve optimized for assets that should trade near 1:1 (stablecoin↔stablecoin), giving far lower slippage in that region. **Balancer** generalizes to weighted pools of more than two assets (e.g. 80/20), turning the pool into a self-rebalancing index. They are all 'a reserve-based curve that prices trades' - master $x cdot y = k$ and the others are variations on the theme. The deep connection to finance: an AMM LP is effectively running a passive, rules-based market-making strategy, which is why impermanent loss (next) is best understood in microstructure terms.

resources.label: Uniswap docs — how Uniswap v2 works (protocol overview)

subtopics.title: Impermanent loss (and LVR)
body: **Impermanent loss (IL)** is the central, often-misunderstood risk of providing liquidity: when the price ratio of the two pooled assets changes, the value of your LP position ends up *lower* than if you had simply held the two tokens in your wallet. It is called 'impermanent' because the gap closes if the price returns to where you entered - but if the price has permanently moved when you withdraw, the loss is realized.

The mechanism is the flip side of the arbitrage that keeps AMM prices correct. Suppose ETH rises on the broader market. Arbitrageurs will buy ETH from your pool (because it is now cheap there) until the pool's price catches up - meaning *they systematically take the appreciating asset out of your pool and leave you the depreciating one*. As an LP you are, by construction, on the losing side of every price move: the pool sells winners and buys losers automatically. You are compensated only by the trading **fees** you collect; an LP position is profitable precisely when accumulated fees exceed the impermanent loss.

The quantity is exact for a constant-product pool. If the price ratio changes by a factor `p` (e.g. ETH doubles, so $p = 2$), the value of the LP position relative to just holding is $(2 cdot sqrt{p})/(1 + p)$, so the **divergence loss** is $(2 cdot sqrt{p})/(1+p) - 1$. Plug in: a 2x move ($p=2$) gives ≈ -5.7%; a 4x move gives ≈ -20%; a 1.25x move gives only ≈ -0.6%. Small moves cost almost nothing; large divergences are severe. This convex, always-negative-from-price-moves payoff means an LP is structurally **short volatility / short gamma** - a profound and direct bridge to options pricing in the quant domain.

The modern, sharper framing is **Loss-Versus-Rebalancing (LVR, 'lever')**. It reframes IL not as 'vs holding' but as the loss an LP suffers to arbitrageurs relative to a hypothetical strategy that rebalances at the true market price with zero latency. In this lens, the LP's loss is exactly the **adverse selection** cost of always quoting stale prices to better-informed arbitrageurs - which is the *same* adverse-selection concept from market microstructure (the LP is an uninformed market maker being picked off by informed flow). LVR cleanly separates 'loss to arbitrage' from 'fee income', making it the right tool to evaluate whether an AMM's fee tier actually compensates LPs.

Practical takeaways: IL is worst for *volatile, uncorrelated* pairs and minimal for *tightly correlated* ones (which is why stablecoin and liquid-staking-derivative pools are popular - low IL). Higher fee tiers and higher volume offset IL. And in Uniswap v3 (next), concentrating liquidity *amplifies* IL because your capital is more exposed within its range - turning passive LPing into active risk management.

resources.label: Automated Market Making and Loss-Versus-Rebalancing (LVR paper)

subtopics.title: Uniswap v3: concentrated liquidity & ticks
body: Uniswap v2 has a hidden inefficiency: liquidity is spread uniformly across the *entire* price range from $0$ to $infty$, but trades only ever happen near the current price, so the vast majority of an LP's capital sits at prices that may never occur and earns nothing. **Uniswap v3's** breakthrough is **concentrated liquidity**: LPs choose a finite price range $[p_a, p_b]$ to provide liquidity within, concentrating their capital where trading actually happens. Within a tight range, a given amount of capital provides far deeper liquidity (less slippage, more fees) - capital efficiency can improve by 100x-1000x versus v2 for stable pairs.

The mechanism is built on **ticks**. The continuous price axis is discretized into ticks spaced by a constant factor: the price at tick `i` is $1.0001^i$, so each tick is a 0.01% (one basis point) price step. An LP's position is defined by a lower and upper tick, and the protocol tracks the total liquidity active at each tick. As the market price moves and **crosses a tick**, positions whose range it enters become active and those it leaves become inactive. For numerical precision the contracts track $sqrt{	ext{price}}$ (in Q64.96 fixed point) rather than price directly, because the liquidity math is cleaner in square-root space.

The behavior of a position depends on where price is relative to its range. While the current price is *inside* `[p_a, p_b]`, the position is active, holds a mix of both tokens, and earns fees. When price moves *above* the range, the position converts entirely to the cheaper token and stops earning; *below* the range, entirely to the other token and stops earning. So a concentrated LP must either pick a wide range (more v2-like, lower capital efficiency, but stays active) or a tight range (high efficiency and fees while in-range, but goes idle and is fully converted once price exits - requiring active **rebalancing**).

Concentration is a double-edged sword on impermanent loss. Because your capital is densely deployed near the current price, price moves within your range inflict **amplified IL** compared to the same capital spread thin in v2 - you feel the divergence more sharply. v3 LPing is therefore much closer to **active market making**: you are continuously choosing ranges, monitoring price, harvesting fees, and rebalancing, with a real risk/reward tradeoff between fee income and amplified IL/LVR. This is why v3 LP strategies are a quant discipline (and why automated 'liquidity manager' protocols exist).

v3 also unlocked **fee tiers** (e.g. 0.05% / 0.30% / 1.00%) so pools can match fee to expected volatility, and it enabled using the time-weighted price of a v3 pool as a more robust oracle (the cumulative-tick accumulator) - relevant to the oracle subtopic. The throughline from v2: same constant-product intuition, but with liquidity placed surgically, which is both its power and its complexity.

subtopics.title: Lending: Compound, Aave, liquidations & flash loans
body: On-chain lending markets let suppliers earn yield and borrowers access liquidity, all **overcollateralized** - because there is no identity or credit enforcement, you must always lock collateral worth *more* than you borrow. Suppliers deposit assets into a shared pool; borrowers post collateral and draw a smaller amount against it; the spread between borrow and supply rates (minus reserves) is the protocol's revenue. This is the second great DeFi primitive after AMMs, and it composes with them constantly.

**Compound** popularized the **cToken** model: when you supply DAI you receive cDAI, an interest-bearing receipt whose *exchange rate* against DAI grows every block as borrowers pay interest - so you redeem more DAI later than you deposited, with interest accruing automatically into the token's value (no separate claim step). **Interest rates are algorithmic**, set by a **utilization curve**: as the fraction of the pool that is borrowed (utilization) rises, both borrow and supply rates rise - steeply past an optimal 'kink' - to attract suppliers and discourage borrowing, keeping the pool from being fully drained.

**Aave** formalizes risk with the **health factor** = (Σ collateral × each asset's liquidation threshold) / (Σ borrowed value). While health factor > 1 the position is safe; the moment a price move pushes it below 1, the position becomes **liquidatable**: any third party (a liquidator bot) may repay part of the borrower's debt and, in return, seize an equivalent amount of the borrower's collateral *plus a bonus* (the liquidation incentive, e.g. 5-10%). Liquidations are the mechanism that keeps the protocol solvent - bad positions are closed before they go underwater - and they are a major, fiercely competitive **MEV** opportunity (bots race to be the liquidator that captures the bonus).

Aave also pioneered the **flash loan**: borrow *any amount* of a pooled asset with *zero collateral*, on the sole condition that you repay it (plus a small fee) **within the same transaction**. The EVM's atomicity enforces it - if your transaction ends without repaying, the entire transaction (loan included) reverts, so the protocol can never lose. Flash loans are a legitimate power tool (collateral swaps, refinancing, self-liquidation, capital-efficient arbitrage), but they are also the favorite weapon of attackers because they erase the capital barrier to attacks: 'you would need $50M to manipulate this' is no longer a defense when anyone can borrow $50M for one transaction. Almost every large oracle-manipulation exploit (Security topic) is flash-loan-funded.

Putting it together, a lending market is a tight loop of: utilization → rates → borrowing demand, plus prices → health factors → liquidations. Its solvency rests entirely on (a) correct collateral parameters and (b) a *manipulation-resistant price oracle* - which is exactly why oracles are the next subtopic and the single most security-critical dependency in DeFi.

resources.label: Aave v3 — technical paper (health factor, liquidations, eMode)

subtopics.title: Stablecoins: custodial, crypto-backed, algorithmic
body: A **stablecoin** aims to hold a stable value (almost always a $1 peg) on a volatile chain - the indispensable unit of account for trading, lending, and payments. There are three fundamentally different ways to hold a peg, with very different trust and risk profiles, and the differences are the whole lesson.

**Custodial / fiat-backed** (USDC, USDT): a centralized issuer holds reserves (dollars, Treasury bills) off-chain and mints exactly one token per dollar held, redeemable 1:1. The peg is maintained by trust in the reserves plus arbitrage (if the token trades below $1, redeem it for $1; above, mint new ones). This is the simplest and most robust *at the peg*, but it reintroduces **centralization and counterparty risk**: the issuer can freeze or blacklist addresses (USDC has, by court order), reserves can be questioned, and the token is only as good as the issuer's solvency and banking (USDC briefly depegged to ~$0.88 during the March 2023 SVB scare because some reserves were stuck in a failing bank).

**Crypto-backed / overcollateralized** (DAI, via MakerDAO): users lock volatile crypto collateral worth *more* than the DAI they mint - e.g. deposit $150 of ETH to mint $100 of DAI - in a vault. If the collateral's value falls and the collateralization ratio drops too low, the vault is **liquidated** (collateral auctioned to cover the debt), just like a lending position. The peg holds via overcollateralization, a stability fee (interest), and the Peg Stability Module. It is **decentralized and censorship-resistant** but **capital-inefficient** (you lock more than you mint) and exposed to sharp collateral crashes (and, increasingly, to the centralized assets like USDC it holds in reserve - a composability risk).

**Algorithmic** (the cautionary tale, Terra's UST): attempted to hold the peg with *no hard collateral*, purely by a mint/burn arbitrage against a sister token (LUNA). UST could always be redeemed for $1 of newly-minted LUNA and vice versa, which works while confidence holds. But the design is **reflexive**: when UST slipped below peg and people redeemed en masse, the protocol minted ever more LUNA, collapsing LUNA's price, which destroyed the very backstop meant to defend the peg - a **death spiral** that erased ~$40B in days in May 2022. The enduring lesson, now canon: a peg backed only by the reflexive market value of an associated token, with no exogenous collateral, is structurally fragile under a loss of confidence. 'Fractional-algorithmic' and overcollateralized-with-buffer designs exist, but pure algorithmic stablecoins are widely regarded as unsound.

The framework to carry: every stablecoin trades off **decentralization, capital efficiency, and peg robustness** - you can optimize two at the expense of the third. Custodial is robust + efficient but centralized; crypto-backed is robust + decentralized but inefficient; algorithmic chased efficient + decentralized and paid with fragility. When evaluating any stablecoin, ask exactly *what backs it and what happens to that backing under stress*.

resources.label: MakerDAO docs — DAI, vaults & the collateral system

subtopics.title: Oracles & price feeds
body: Smart contracts are **deterministic and sandboxed**: every node must reproduce the same result, so a contract cannot reach out to fetch external data (a price, a temperature, a sports score) - if it could, different nodes might get different answers and consensus would break. **Oracles** are the infrastructure that brings off-chain data on-chain in a consensus-safe way, and because a lending protocol's solvency, a derivative's settlement, and a liquidation's trigger all depend on *the price the contract believes*, the oracle is the single most security-critical dependency in DeFi.

**Chainlink** is the dominant **decentralized oracle network**. A set of independent, staked node operators each fetch a price from many sources (exchanges, aggregators), and the values are aggregated on-chain (e.g. a median) into a price feed that updates when the price deviates past a threshold or a heartbeat interval elapses. Decentralization across many independent operators and many data sources is what makes the feed hard to corrupt - no single exchange or node can move it. Using a robust feed like this is the safe default for any protocol that needs an external price.

The cardinal sin - responsible for a large fraction of all DeFi hacks - is using a **single AMM pool's spot price** as an oracle. Recall the AMM section: spot price is just the instantaneous reserve ratio `y/x`, and it can be moved *arbitrarily within a single transaction* by a large swap. Combine that with **flash loans** (borrow unlimited capital for one transaction) and an attacker can: flash-borrow a fortune, swap to skew the target pool's spot price massively, exploit a victim protocol that reads that spot price (e.g. borrow far more than their real collateral warrants, or mint mispriced shares), then reverse the swap and repay the loan - all atomically and risk-free. The bug is not the flash loan; it is trusting a manipulable instantaneous price.

The defenses are all about making price **expensive to manipulate over the window that matters**. Use a decentralized feed (Chainlink). Or use a **TWAP** - a time-weighted average price - so that moving the reported price requires holding the manipulation across *many blocks*, which is prohibitively costly and exposes the attacker to arbitrage the whole time (Uniswap v2/v3 expose cumulative-price accumulators precisely so contracts can compute TWAPs). Or cross-check multiple independent sources and reject outliers. The reasoning pattern - 'what is the cheapest way for an adversary to make this contract believe a false price, and does my oracle make that prohibitively expensive?' - is the core security question for any price-dependent protocol.

Oracles also extend beyond prices: **proof-of-reserve** feeds, randomness (Chainlink VRF - verifiable on-chain randomness, since `block` values are weakly manipulable by proposers), and cross-chain messaging (CCIP). But price feeds are where the money and the risk concentrate, and 'never trust a manipulable spot price' is the rule that prevents the most exploits.

resources.label: Chainlink — data feeds documentation (the exact feeds page)

subtopics.title: Bridges & cross-chain messaging
body: A **bridge** moves assets or data between two chains that cannot natively talk to each other. The dominant asset-bridge pattern is **lock-and-mint**: lock token X in a contract on chain A, and mint a wrapped representation (wX) on chain B; to go back, burn wX on B to unlock X on A. More general **message-passing** bridges relay arbitrary function calls across chains. In every design, the crux is the same hard problem: how does chain B become *convinced* that something really happened on chain A, given that B cannot read A's state directly? *How a bridge answers that question is its entire security model.*

Bridges are the **single most exploited component in all of crypto**, by value lost. Ronin (~$625M), Wormhole (~$325M), BNB Bridge (~$570M), Nomad (~$190M), Harmony (~$100M) - the largest hacks in the space are overwhelmingly bridge hacks. The reason is structural: a bridge concentrates *enormous locked value* behind a *single trust assumption* (a multisig, a validator set, a verification function), so one compromise - a leaked set of signing keys, an over-trusted validator quorum, a bug in proof verification - drains everything at once. Ronin fell because 5 of 9 multisig keys were compromised; Wormhole because of a signature-verification bug; Nomad because a flawed initialization made *any* message appear valid.

The security models form a spectrum. At the **trusted** end: an external **multisig** or a federated **validator set** attests that the source event happened, and the destination believes them - this is fast, cheap, and chain-agnostic, but you are trusting that the signers are honest and their keys are safe (the source of most hacks). In the middle: **optimistic** bridges (relay a claim, allow a challenge window for fraud proofs, like Nomad's intended design) and **light-client / 'native' verification** (the destination chain runs a light client of the source chain and verifies block headers + Merkle proofs - far more trust-minimized, but expensive and complex to build for every chain pair). At the **trust-minimized** end: **ZK bridges** that prove the source chain's consensus and state transition with a succinct validity proof the destination verifies cryptographically (no trusted signers at all) - the direction the ecosystem is steadily moving (zkBridge in the ZK topic).

Because the destination must trust *something*, the analyst's first question for any bridge is always concrete: **'what exactly am I trusting, and what is the blast radius if it is compromised?'** A multisig - whose keys, how many, held by whom? A validator set - who, how many, slashed if dishonest? A proof system - audited, and does it actually verify what it claims? The answer ranges from 'a 5-of-9 multisig run by one company' (high risk) to 'a cryptographic proof of Ethereum consensus' (minimal trust), and that answer should govern how much value you are willing to route through it.

Bridges also matter because they make the multi-chain / L2 world usable - the entire rollup ecosystem (next topics) depends on **canonical bridges** between L1 and L2, which use the rollup's own proof system (fraud proofs or validity proofs) and are therefore far more trust-minimized than generic third-party bridges. Understanding the trust spectrum here is what lets you tell a safe canonical bridge from a risky 'fast' one.

resources.label: Rekt.news — leaderboard of the largest hacks (mostly bridges)

subtopics.title: Token standards & proxy/upgrade patterns
body: Standards are the **interfaces** that make DeFi composable - because every token implements the same functions, any protocol can integrate any token without bespoke code. **ERC-20** is the fungible-token interface: `totalSupply`, `balanceOf`, `transfer`, plus the **allowance** pattern `approve`/`transferFrom` that lets a third party (a DEX, a lending pool) move your tokens on your behalf up to a limit. That allowance pattern is essential to DeFi *and* a major attack surface - users routinely grant *unlimited* approvals to contracts, so a malicious or exploited contract with your approval can drain the approved token; 'revoke unused approvals' is basic hygiene, and `permit` (ERC-2612, gasless signed approvals) is the modern alternative.

**ERC-721** is the non-fungible standard: each token has a unique `tokenId` with individual ownership and transfer - the basis of NFTs (art, identity, positions; a Uniswap v3 LP position is itself an ERC-721). **ERC-1155** is a multi-token standard that manages many token types - fungible and non-fungible - in one contract with batch operations, far more efficient for games and marketplaces that mint thousands of item types. Knowing which standard fits which use case (and that a v3 LP is an NFT, a vault share is an ERC-20) is everyday fluency.

**ERC-4626** standardizes **tokenized vaults**: a yield-bearing wrapper around an underlying ERC-20 with a uniform interface - `deposit`/`mint`/`withdraw`/`redeem` and `convertToShares`/`convertToAssets`. It exists because before it, every yield protocol reinvented share-accounting slightly differently and several got it subtly wrong; 4626 makes vaults composable and auditable. It is also a great security case study: the **first-depositor / inflation ('donation') attack**, where an attacker deposits 1 wei, then directly transfers a large amount of the underlying to the vault to inflate the share price so the next depositor's shares round down to zero (and they lose funds). Modern 4626 implementations defend with virtual shares/decimals offsets - a concrete example of how integer rounding becomes a vulnerability (Security topic).

Because deployed contracts are **immutable**, upgrading them requires **proxy patterns**. A **proxy** contract holds the state and, on every call, `DELEGATECALL`s into a separate **implementation (logic)** contract - so logic runs against the proxy's storage. To upgrade, you point the proxy at a new implementation; the state stays put. **EIP-1967** standardizes *where* the implementation (and admin) addresses are stored - at fixed pseudo-random slots like `keccak256('eip1967.proxy.implementation') - 1` - specifically so they sit far away from normal variable slots and cannot be accidentally overwritten. The **Transparent** and **UUPS** proxy variants differ in where the upgrade logic lives (in the proxy admin vs in the implementation); the **Diamond standard (EIP-2535)** splits logic across many 'facet' contracts behind one proxy with namespaced storage, for very large systems.

Proxies are powerful but introduce their own bug classes that the Security topic covers in depth: **storage collisions** (proxy and implementation disagreeing on slot layout, corrupting state), **uninitialized implementations** (a logic contract whose `initialize` was never locked can be hijacked and `selfdestruct`-ed - the second Parity freeze), and **function selector clashes** in diamonds. The rule of thumb: upgradeability is a feature with real attack surface, so it demands strict storage-layout discipline (append-only variables, storage gaps), locked initializers, and ideally a timelock + multisig on the upgrade key so users can exit before a malicious upgrade takes effect.

resources.label: EIP-20 — fungible token standard (the exact EIP)
EIP-4626 — tokenized vault standard (the exact EIP)

connections.note: AMMs replace the order book with a bonding curve - the same pricing questions
Impermanent loss / LVR is adverse selection in microstructure terms
AMM and liquidation mechanics are exactly what MEV bots arbitrage and sandwich

topics.title: Development tooling (Foundry)
body: Tooling is not a side concern in smart-contract development - because contracts are immutable and adversarial, your test suite *is* your safety net, and the quality of your tooling largely determines whether bugs ship. **Foundry** has become the default professional toolkit precisely because it lets you write tests *in Solidity* against a real EVM, fuzz aggressively, fork mainnet, and script deployments - all extremely fast.

This topic covers the four pillars - Forge (testing), cheatcodes (controlling the test EVM), fuzzing/invariant testing (tests as proofs), and Cast/Anvil plus deterministic deploys - and then static analysis with Slither. The throughline is a layered assurance strategy: cheap automated checks catch the known patterns, fuzzing catches the accounting bugs, and the deep logic flaws are left to manual audit and formal methods (Security topic). A contract that has only example-based unit tests is under-tested by modern standards.

subtopics.title: Forge: Solidity-native testing
body: **Forge** is Foundry's testing engine, and its defining choice is that you write tests *in Solidity*, not JavaScript. The advantage is more than convenience: tests run directly in a native EVM implementation in-process, so there is no ABI marshalling between a JS runtime and the chain, no async ceremony, and execution is extremely fast (an entire suite can run in seconds). You stay in one language and one mental model, which matters when you are reasoning about subtle storage and call semantics.

The structure mirrors standard test frameworks. A test contract inherits Foundry's `Test` base, defines a `setUp()` function that runs fresh before *each* test (deploying contracts, seeding balances), and any function named `test...` is a test case. You assert with `assertEq`, `assertTrue`, `assertGt`, `assertApproxEqAbs`, etc. Functions named `testFail...` are expected to revert (though the explicit `vm.expectRevert` is preferred for precision). Running `forge test` executes everything; `-vvvv` prints the full call trace with per-call gas, which is invaluable for understanding *why* a test failed deep in a call tree.

Two features elevate Forge beyond a unit-test runner. **Mainnet forking** (`forge test --fork-url $RPC_URL`, or `vm.createSelectFork`) spins up a local EVM seeded with *real mainnet state*, so you can test your contract against the actual deployed Uniswap, Aave, or any live protocol - and, critically, **reproduce historical hacks** by forking to the block just before an exploit and replaying it. This is the single best way to learn DeFi security. The other is built-in **gas profiling**: `forge snapshot` records gas per test and diffs it across changes, so you can prove an optimization actually saved gas and catch regressions.

Coverage (`forge coverage`), gas reports, and deployment scripting (`forge script`, next subtopic) round it out. The recommended way to actually learn: pick a small token or vault, write its tests in Forge while reading the Foundry Book, and use `-vvvv` traces to *watch* state, reverts, and gas change. You will absorb more EVM and Solidity from debugging a failing Forge test than from any tutorial, because the feedback is immediate and concrete.

resources.label: Foundry Book — Forge testing guide (writing & running tests)

subtopics.title: Cheatcodes: controlling the test EVM
body: **Cheatcodes** are special operations exposed through Foundry's `vm` interface that manipulate the test EVM in ways impossible on a real chain - and that is exactly what makes them invaluable: they let you construct *precisely* the conditions you need to test, including adversarial ones. They are the difference between 'I hope this branch is covered' and 'I have placed the contract in exactly this state and asserted the outcome'.

The essential identity and balance cheatcodes: **`vm.prank(addr)`** makes the *next* call appear to come from `addr` (and `vm.startPrank`/`stopPrank` for a range), which is how you test access control - call a privileged function as a non-owner and assert it reverts, then as the owner and assert it succeeds. **`vm.deal(addr, amount)`** sets an account's ETH balance; **`deal(token, addr, amount)`** (from the std library) sets an ERC-20 balance by writing the storage slot directly - so you can fund test actors without elaborate setup.

The time and control cheatcodes let you test temporal logic deterministically: **`vm.warp(timestamp)`** sets `block.timestamp` and **`vm.roll(blockNumber)`** sets `block.number`, so you can fast-forward past a timelock, a vesting cliff, or a TWAP window and assert behavior on both sides of the boundary. **`vm.expectRevert(selector)`** asserts the next call reverts *with a specific error* (not just any revert - precision matters so you do not mask a different bug). **`vm.expectEmit(...)`** asserts that a specific event with specific args is emitted. **`vm.mockCall(...)`** forces an external call to return a value you choose, so you can simulate an oracle returning a manipulated price, or a dependency failing.

The deepest ones reach into storage directly: **`vm.store(addr, slot, value)`** and **`vm.load(addr, slot)`** read and write arbitrary storage slots. This both lets you set up otherwise-unreachable adversarial states and forces you to use the **storage-layout** knowledge from the Solidity topic (you must compute which slot a variable or mapping entry lives in). Using `vm.store` to corrupt a proxy's implementation slot, for instance, lets you test your collision defenses.

Together, cheatcodes turn testing from 'observe what happens' into 'engineer the exact scenario and assert the exact result' - including the malicious scenarios an attacker would create. The discipline of writing, for every privileged function and every state-machine transition, a test that uses `vm.prank`/`vm.warp`/`vm.expectRevert` to pin down who-can-do-what-when is what gives a codebase real confidence.

resources.label: Foundry Book — cheatcodes reference (the exact catalog)

subtopics.title: Fuzzing & invariant (stateful) testing
body: Example-based tests only check the cases you thought of - and the bugs that drain protocols are precisely the ones nobody thought of. **Property-based fuzzing** flips this: instead of hardcoding inputs, you declare a test with *parameters* (`function testDeposit(uint256 amount) public`), and Forge throws hundreds or thousands of pseudo-random inputs at it, automatically probing boundary values (0, 1, `type(uint256).max`, values near your require-thresholds). When it finds an input that breaks an assertion, it **shrinks** the failure to a minimal reproducing counterexample, handing you the smallest input that triggers the bug. You can constrain the input domain with `vm.assume(...)` or `bound(...)` to keep fuzzing in the meaningful range.

**Invariant testing** is the more powerful, and more conceptually important, mode. Rather than fuzzing a single function call, Forge runs *random sequences of calls* across your contracts - this is **stateful fuzzing** - and after every step it checks your declared **invariants**: properties that must hold *no matter what sequence of operations* occurs. You define `invariant_...()` functions and configure which contracts/functions the fuzzer may call (the 'handlers' and 'targets'). The fuzzer becomes an adversary trying to drive your system into a state where an invariant breaks.

The canonical examples make the idea click. For an **AMM**, the invariant is that the constant-product `k = reserveX · reserveY` must never *decrease* due to a bug (it may only grow from fees) - if any random sequence of swaps/mints/burns makes it drop, you have found an accounting flaw. For a **lending market**, total debt must always be backed (`Σ collateral ≥ Σ borrows` adjusted for thresholds), or the sum of all users' balances must always equal `totalSupply`. For a **vault**, total assets must always equal the sum of what is redeemable. You *specify what must always be true* and let the machine try to falsify it - this is genuinely 'tests as proofs' (lightweight, probabilistic, but enormously effective).

This matters because invariant fuzzing catches exactly the bug classes that unit tests miss: subtle accounting drift, rounding that accumulates, reentrancy that corrupts state across calls, and edge-case sequences (deposit-then-donate inflation attacks, liquidation-during-update races). It is the smart-contract analogue of coverage-guided fuzzers like AFL/libFuzzer in security research - the same philosophy of 'generate adversarial inputs to break a stated property', applied to stateful on-chain systems. Echidna (Security topic) is a complementary, more configurable fuzzer in the same family.

Practical advice: write invariants *first*, as you would write a spec, then let fuzzing pressure-test them; invest in good handlers (so the fuzzer explores realistic action sequences); and treat any invariant failure as a high-priority finding. A protocol with a strong invariant suite is dramatically harder to break than one with only happy-path unit tests.

resources.label: Foundry Book — invariant testing (stateful fuzzing, handlers)

subtopics.title: Cast, Anvil & deterministic (CREATE2) deploys
body: **Cast** is Foundry's command-line Swiss army knife for interacting with chains and decoding data - the tool you live in for inspection, scripting, and forensics. `cast call` reads from a contract (a `view` function) without sending a transaction; `cast send` sends a state-changing transaction; `cast 4byte` and `cast 4byte-decode` resolve a function selector to its signature and decode calldata; `cast storage` reads a contract's storage slot (exercising your storage-layout knowledge); `cast run` replays and traces a historical transaction locally; and there are unit converters (`cast --to-wei`), ABI encoders/decoders, and block/tx inspectors. Turning 'what did this transaction actually do' into a one-line command is most of the value.

**Anvil** is Foundry's local development node: a fast, EVM-accurate chain you run on your machine for end-to-end testing before touching a public testnet. It supports instant or interval mining, forking mainnet state, and **account impersonation** (`anvil_impersonateAccount`) so you can send transactions *as* any address (e.g. a whale or a protocol admin) to set up scenarios. Combined with `forge script`, Anvil gives you a reproducible local pipeline: deploy, seed, interact, and assert, all locally, before spending real gas.

**`forge script`** is the deployment-and-automation mechanism: you write deployment logic in Solidity (a script contract with a `run()` function), simulate it locally, then broadcast it to a network with `--broadcast`, getting deterministic, version-controlled deployments instead of fragile ad-hoc commands. Scripts can read environment variables for keys/addresses and verify contracts on explorers in the same flow.

**CREATE2** deserves its own attention because it underpins a lot of modern architecture. Normal `CREATE` derives a new contract's address from `(deployer, nonce)` - so it depends on deployment order and is not known in advance. **CREATE2** derives the address deterministically from `keccak256(0xff ‖ deployerAddress ‖ salt ‖ keccak256(initCode))[12:]` - independent of nonce, fully computable *before* deployment. This enables three important patterns: **counterfactual instantiation** (you can compute a contract's address and even let users interact with / send funds to it before it is deployed, deploying only when needed - core to some wallet and account-abstraction designs), **consistent cross-chain addresses** (deploy the same contract to the same address on many chains by using the same salt and init code), and **deterministic factories** (a factory that mints predictable child-contract addresses). Understanding the address-derivation formula is what lets you reason about - and audit - all three.

Together, Cast (inspect/script), Anvil (local node), forge script (deploy), and CREATE2 (deterministic addresses) form the operational layer around the testing core. Mastery here means you can go from 'an idea' to 'tested, deployed, and inspectable on-chain' without leaving the toolkit, and you can dissect anyone else's deployed contract with Cast when something goes wrong.

resources.label: Foundry Book — Cast reference (chain interaction & decoding)
Foundry Book — Anvil (local node, forking, impersonation)

subtopics.title: Static analysis with Slither
body: **Slither** (by Trail of Bits) is a static-analysis framework that parses Solidity into an intermediate representation (**SlithIR**) and runs roughly 90 built-in **detectors** for known bug patterns - all without executing the code, in seconds. It is the cheapest, fastest first line of defense, and there is no excuse for a serious contract not to pass it. Running `slither .` in a project surfaces issues ranked by confidence and severity.

Its detectors cover the recurring vulnerability classes you will meet in the Security topic: reentrancy (it distinguishes the dangerous from the benign forms), uninitialized storage variables and uninitialized proxy state, dangerous `delegatecall` and arbitrary-`call` destinations, incorrect or non-standard ERC implementations, `tx.origin` authentication, shadowed state variables, unused return values from low-level calls, and many more. Because it reasons over data and control flow, it catches things a regex linter never could - e.g. that a state write happens *after* an external call (a CEI-pattern violation enabling reentrancy).

Slither is more than a checker. Its **printers** generate useful artifacts: inheritance graphs (to verify your C3 linearization is what you intend), function-level call graphs, data-dependency and variable-write summaries, and human-readable contract summaries - all of which speed up understanding an unfamiliar codebase during review. And it is **extensible**: you can write custom detectors in Python to encode project-specific invariants ('this privileged function must always be behind `onlyOwner`', 'this value must never be set without emitting an event'), turning institutional knowledge into automated checks.

The right way to use it is in **CI**: run Slither (and `forge test`/coverage) on every push, fail the build on high-severity findings, and triage the rest. This catches whole categories of mistakes before a human ever reviews the code, freeing reviewers to focus on the deep logic. The related `crytic`/Trail-of-Bits ecosystem also gives you **Echidna** (property fuzzer) and other tools that compose with Slither.

The mental model to leave with - the **assurance ladder**, cheapest first: **Slither** (static analysis) finds the *known patterns* in seconds; **Forge invariant fuzzing** and **Echidna** find the *accounting and sequence* bugs by trying to break your stated properties; **Certora / formal verification** (Security topic) *proves* specific properties hold for all inputs; and a careful **human audit** finds the deep, design-level logic flaws none of the tools can express. Each rung catches what the cheaper one misses, and none replaces the others - real protocols climb the whole ladder.

resources.label: Slither — static analyzer (repo, detector list, printers)

connections.note: Invariant/property fuzzing here is the smart-contract cousin of AFL/libFuzzer
Foundry itself is written in Rust, as is much of the ZK/L2 tooling stack
This tooling is the practical front line of the security assurance ladder

topics.title: Smart-contract security
body: Smart-contract security is its own discipline because the stakes and constraints are uniquely brutal: code is immutable once deployed, it holds money directly, it runs in a fully adversarial environment where every line is public, and a single bug is exploited atomically for the entire contract's balance within minutes of discovery. There is no 'patch it tomorrow'. This is why mastery means thinking like an attacker - enumerating how each line could be abused - and why the security mindset is the through-line of the whole domain.

This topic is a structured catalog of the vulnerability classes that have actually drained funds - reentrancy, access control, oracle/price manipulation, arithmetic, proxy storage collisions, signature bugs - each explained with mechanism, real incident, and mitigation, followed by the audit methodology and tooling that find them. The fastest way to convert this reading into real intuition is to *exploit* the bugs yourself in Damn Vulnerable DeFi and to reproduce real hacks on a Foundry mainnet fork.

subtopics.title: Reentrancy (including read-only)
body: **Reentrancy** is the archetypal smart-contract bug - it caused **The DAO** hack in 2016 (~$60M, which led to the Ethereum/Ethereum Classic hard fork) and still recurs constantly. The mechanism: when contract A makes an external call to contract B, control passes to B *before A has finished executing*. If A sent funds or made the call *before* updating its own state, B's code (specifically its `receive`/`fallback` for an ETH transfer, or any function it controls) can call *back into* A while A is still in its inconsistent intermediate state - and exploit it.

The classic example is a vulnerable `withdraw()`: it (1) checks your recorded balance, (2) sends you ETH via `msg.sender.call{value: bal}("")`, then (3) sets your balance to 0. Because the external send in step 2 hands control to the attacker's fallback *before* step 3 zeroes the balance, the fallback re-calls `withdraw()`, which still sees the old nonzero balance, and sends again - looping until the contract is drained. The bug is the *ordering*: state was mutated after the external interaction.

The defenses, used together: the **Checks-Effects-Interactions (CEI)** pattern - first do all **checks** (validate inputs/conditions), then apply all **effects** (update every piece of state, e.g. zero the balance), and only *then* perform **interactions** (external calls). With state already finalized before the call, a re-entrant call finds nothing left to exploit. CEI is the real fix. As a defense-in-depth backstop, add a **reentrancy guard** - a `nonReentrant` modifier (e.g. OpenZeppelin's) that sets a lock flag on entry and reverts any re-entrant call. Note the guard is a safety net; correct ordering is the actual cure, and a guard alone does not protect against the next variant.

**Read-only reentrancy** is the subtle modern form that bypasses naive guards and has caused multi-million-dollar losses. Here the attacker re-enters during a callback and calls a *`view`* function of a victim contract that returns a *stale or inconsistent* value mid-transaction - for example, a pool's `getPrice()` reading reserves that are temporarily skewed because the transaction is half-complete. A *third* protocol that trusts that view (e.g. uses the pool as an oracle) is fed the bad value and exploited - even though no state was written reentrantly and the pool's own `nonReentrant` guard (which only protects state-changing functions) never tripped. The lesson: you must reason about **cross-contract state consistency during external calls**, not just whether *your own* state writes are reentrancy-safe.

Cross-function and cross-contract reentrancy generalize the idea: the re-entrant call need not target the *same* function, only any function that shares state with the inconsistent one. The robust posture is to apply CEI everywhere, guard all externally-reachable state-changing functions, be deeply suspicious of any external call (especially ETH transfers and ERC-777/callback tokens), and treat every `view` consumed by other protocols as needing to be consistent at all times. Exploiting the reentrancy challenges in Damn Vulnerable DeFi makes all of this reflexive.

resources.label: Damn Vulnerable DeFi — reentrancy & callback challenges

subtopics.title: Access control & tx.origin
body: A boringly large fraction of the total value lost in DeFi comes not from clever exploits but from **missing or incorrect access control** - a privileged function left unprotected, an initializer anyone can call, an upgrade path with no guard. These are unglamorous and devastating, and the defense is disciplined enumeration: list *every* state-changing or value-moving function and answer, for each, 'who is authorized to call this, and is that authorization actually enforced on-chain?'

Concrete historical disasters illustrate the category. The **Parity multisig freeze** (2017, ~$280M frozen forever) happened because a library contract's `initWallet`/kill function was left unprotected and uninitialized: someone called it, became owner, and invoked `selfdestruct`, bricking every wallet that delegatecalled into the library. Countless rug-style and exploit losses trace to an `onlyOwner` that was forgotten on a `mint`, a `setOracle`, or a `withdraw`. An unprotected `selfdestruct`, an open `initialize()`, or a public upgrade function are each a total-loss waiting to happen.

The specific Solidity footgun is **`tx.origin` vs `msg.sender`** (introduced in the Solidity topic, named as a vulnerability here). `msg.sender` is the immediate caller; `tx.origin` is the original EOA at the root of the entire call chain. Authenticating with `require(tx.origin == owner)` is exploitable via a **phishing** flow: if the owner can be tricked into calling an attacker's contract, that contract calls your function, and `tx.origin` is *still the owner*, so the check passes - the attacker acts with the owner's authority. **Always authenticate with `msg.sender`** (and use `tx.origin` only, if ever, for the narrow purpose of detecting whether the caller is a contract, itself now discouraged).

Beyond the binary 'is it guarded', good security treats **privilege design** as a finding in itself. A single EOA owner is a single point of failure (key theft = total loss), so production systems use a **multisig** (e.g. Safe, requiring m-of-n signers) or a DAO. Powerful actions (upgrades, parameter changes, treasury moves) should sit behind a **timelock**, so users can observe a pending change and exit before it executes - this is what makes a malicious or compromised upgrade survivable. Use **role-based access** (OpenZeppelin `AccessControl`) to separate duties (a `PAUSER_ROLE` distinct from an `ADMIN_ROLE`), prefer **two-step ownership transfer** (so you cannot accidentally hand ownership to a wrong/dead address), and minimize the number and power of privileged functions. **Centralization risk is a security severity**, not a governance footnote: a contract one key-compromise away from total loss is insecure even if every function is technically guarded.

resources.label: SWC Registry — tx.origin authentication & access-control weaknesses

subtopics.title: Oracle & price manipulation via flash loans
body: This is the **dominant DeFi exploit class** by frequency and cumulative loss, and it ties together the AMM, lending, oracle, and flash-loan concepts from the DeFi topic into one attack. The pattern: a victim protocol reads a price from a *manipulable* source - most commonly the **spot price of a single AMM pool** (the instantaneous reserve ratio) - and the attacker temporarily distorts that source to make the protocol act on a false price.

The full exploit, step by step and atomic: (1) the attacker takes a **flash loan** of enormous size (no collateral needed, repay within the transaction); (2) they **swap** that capital into the target AMM pool, massively skewing its reserves and thus its spot price; (3) they interact with the **victim protocol** that trusts that spot price - for example, a lending market now thinks the attacker's collateral is worth far more than it is, so it lets them borrow far more than they should, or a vault mints/prices shares incorrectly; (4) they **reverse** the swap to restore the pool (recovering most of the capital) and **repay** the flash loan. The net result is extracted value, the whole thing is **risk-free** (any failure reverts the entire transaction), and it requires no upfront capital.

The crucial reframing: **flash loans did not create this bug - they removed the capital barrier to it.** Before flash loans, 'an attacker would need $50M to move that pool' was an implicit defense; now anyone can summon $50M for one transaction, so that defense is gone. The vulnerability is, and always was, **trusting an instantaneous, cheaply-manipulable price**. The fix lives entirely on the *oracle* side, not by trying to block flash loans (you cannot).

The mitigations, from the oracle subtopic, made concrete: use a **decentralized oracle** (Chainlink) whose price aggregates many independent sources and cannot be moved by skewing one pool; or use a **TWAP** (time-weighted average price) so that moving the reported price requires sustaining the manipulation across *many blocks* - prohibitively expensive and exposed to arbitrage the entire time; or **cross-check multiple independent sources** and reject outliers. The governing question for any price-dependent contract is: *'what is the cheapest way for an adversary to make this contract believe a false price, and does my oracle make that cost prohibitive?'*

The great flash-loan/oracle hacks - **bZx, Harvest Finance, Cheese Bank, Cream, Mango Markets** - are the canonical case studies. Reproducing one on a **Foundry mainnet fork** (fork to the block before the hack, replay the exploit transaction-by-transaction, watch the price move and the funds drain) is, bar none, the most instructive security exercise in the field, because it forces you to integrate AMMs, lending, oracles, flash loans, and atomicity into a single working mental model of an attack.

resources.label: Damn Vulnerable DeFi — oracle manipulation & flash-loan challenges

subtopics.title: Arithmetic: overflow, unchecked & precision
body: Integer arithmetic is a recurring source of exploits, though the nature of the risk has shifted. Before **Solidity 0.8**, arithmetic **overflowed/underflowed silently** - a `uint256` at 0 minus 1 wrapped to the maximum value (2²⁵⁶-1), and a balance could be inflated to astronomical numbers. This caused many early hacks (the 'batchOverflow' ERC-20 bug minted enormous balances). The community patched it with the **SafeMath** library (checked arithmetic that reverts on over/underflow), which became ubiquitous.

Since Solidity 0.8, the **compiler inserts overflow/underflow checks by default** - arithmetic reverts on wrap, so the raw overflow bug is largely gone *unless* you explicitly opt out with an **`unchecked { }`** block. `unchecked` is used legitimately for gas savings where overflow is provably impossible (e.g. a loop counter bounded by an array length), but it reintroduces the old danger the moment that 'provably impossible' assumption is wrong. So overflow today is mostly an `unchecked`-misuse and pre-0.8-legacy concern - review every `unchecked` block for whether its no-overflow assumption truly holds.

The arithmetic risk that is very much *live* in modern code is **precision and rounding**. Solidity has **no floating point** - all division is integer division, which **truncates toward zero**. This produces several real bug families. **Multiply before dividing**: `(a * b) / c` preserves precision while `(a / c) * b` can truncate `a/c` to zero and lose everything - operation order matters. **Decimal mismatches**: tokens have different `decimals` (USDC and USDT use 6, most ERC-20s use 18, WBTC uses 8); mixing them without normalizing causes off-by-10¹² errors that misprice everything - always scale to a common precision. **Rounding direction**: always round in the *protocol's* favor (round deposits/shares down, debts up) so a user cannot extract value through repeated favorable rounding (a 'rounding dust' drain).

The **first-depositor / share-inflation ('donation') attack** on ERC-4626 vaults (introduced in the DeFi topic) is fundamentally a rounding bug and the canonical modern example. The attacker deposits 1 wei to mint 1 share, then *directly transfers* (donates) a large amount of the underlying asset to the vault, inflating the assets-per-share ratio so extremely that the next honest depositor's share calculation **rounds down to zero shares** - they deposit real money and receive nothing, which the attacker can then withdraw. Modern vaults defend with **virtual shares/assets** (a fixed offset that makes the ratio un-inflatable) and minimum-deposit requirements. It is the cleanest illustration of how integer truncation, attacker-controlled state, and share accounting combine into a real loss - and why arithmetic deserves the same scrutiny as reentrancy.

subtopics.title: Proxy storage collisions & delegatecall
body: Upgradeable contracts are powerful but carry a distinctive, high-severity set of bugs rooted in how `DELEGATECALL` interacts with storage (the mechanics are in the Solidity and DeFi topics; here they are vulnerabilities). Recall: a proxy holds the state and `delegatecall`s into an implementation, so the implementation's code executes against the **proxy's storage**, addressed by **slot number** - not by variable name. The danger follows directly: if the proxy and implementation, or two versions of an implementation, **disagree about which variable lives in which slot**, a write intended for one variable lands on another.

A **storage collision** is exactly this silent corruption. Suppose v1 of an implementation has `address owner` at slot 0, and an upgrade to v2 inadvertently inserts a new variable at slot 0, shifting `owner` to slot 1. After the upgrade, code that writes `owner` writes slot 1, while the proxy still has the *old* `owner` value at slot 0 - or worse, an unrelated value written to slot 0 is now interpreted as `owner`, letting an attacker who can influence that variable seize ownership. No error is thrown; the contract just behaves wrongly. The defenses are strict **storage-layout discipline**: only ever *append* new variables in upgrades (never insert or reorder), and reserve space with **storage gaps** (`uint256[50] __gap;`) so future variables have somewhere to go without shifting existing ones.

**EIP-1967** prevents a specific, important collision: where to store the proxy's *own* metadata (the implementation address, the admin) without clobbering the implementation's variables. It standardizes fixed, pseudo-random slots - e.g. the implementation address lives at `keccak256('eip1967.proxy.implementation') - 1` - chosen to be astronomically far from the sequential slots (0, 1, 2, ...) that normal variables occupy, so they cannot collide. The **Diamond standard (EIP-2535)** goes further for large systems, giving each 'facet' a **namespaced storage struct** at its own hashed base slot, isolating facets from one another.

Two more delegatecall-specific traps complete the picture. An **uninitialized implementation contract** is dangerous because the *logic* contract itself can be called directly (not just through the proxy); if its `initialize()` was never locked, an attacker can call it, become its owner, and - if the logic contains a `delegatecall` or `selfdestruct` - destroy the implementation, bricking every proxy pointing at it (the **second Parity freeze**, ~$280M, was exactly this). The fix is to **lock initializers on the implementation** (call `_disableInitializers()` in its constructor) and use the initializer pattern correctly. Separately, **any `delegatecall` to an attacker-controllable address is total compromise**, because it runs arbitrary code in *your* storage context - never delegatecall to an untrusted target.

The synthesis: upgradeability trades immutability's safety for flexibility, and that trade comes with the storage-collision and initialization attack surface. Mitigate it with append-only layouts and gaps, EIP-1967/UUPS standard slots, locked initializers, audited proxy bases (OpenZeppelin's upgrades plugin even validates layout compatibility across versions), and - to protect users from the upgrade key itself - a **timelock plus multisig** on upgrades so a malicious or compromised upgrade can be seen and escaped before it lands.

resources.label: EIP-1967 — standard proxy storage slots (the exact EIP)
OpenZeppelin — writing upgradeable contracts (storage rules & gaps)

subtopics.title: Signatures: replay, malleability, EIP-712
body: Many protocols accept **off-chain signatures** to enable gasless approvals (ERC-2612 `permit`), meta-transactions, and signed orders (order-book DEXs like 0x and Seaport, where makers sign orders that takers fill on-chain). Anywhere a signature authorizes an action, the signing and verification logic is a rich and frequently-botched attack surface, with three main bug families.

**Replay** is the first: a signature, once revealed on-chain, is public, so if the signed message lacks the right binding it can be re-submitted to repeat the action or replayed in another context. Defenses are mandatory bindings: include a **nonce** (and consume it on use) so a signature works exactly once; include the **chain id** so a signature valid on mainnet cannot be replayed on a testnet or a fork; and include the **verifying contract's address** so a signature for contract A cannot be used against contract B. A signed approval with no nonce, or no chain/contract binding, is a replay waiting to happen (several airdrops and bridges have been drained this way).
