**EIP-712** is the standard that gets this right and is what you should use for any structured-data signing. It defines a canonical way to hash typed structured data together with a **domain separator** - a struct committing to the contract's name, version, **chain id**, and **address** - so the resulting hash is bound to one specific contract on one specific chain, killing cross-context replay by construction. EIP-712 also makes wallets display **human-readable** fields (you see 'approve 100 USDC to Uniswap', not an opaque hash), which is itself a security property (it reduces blind-signing phishing). Getting the domain separator or the type hashes wrong - a mismatched type string, a forgotten field - is a genuine vulnerability that can make signatures forgeable or cross-usable.

**ECDSA malleability** is the subtle third family. For any valid signature `(r, s)`, the value `(r, -s mod n)` (the 'high-s' counterpart) is *also* a valid signature for the same message - so **a signature is not a unique identifier of an authorization**. If your contract uses the signature (or its hash) as a uniqueness key, a deduplication marker, or an idempotency token, an attacker can produce a *second* valid signature for the *same* message and bypass your 'already used' check. The defense is to use a vetted library - **OpenZeppelin's `ECDSA`** - which rejects the malleable high-`s` form and reverts on the malformed inputs that make raw `ecrecover` return the zero address (another classic bug: `ecrecover` returns `address(0)` on bad input, so `require(signer == ecrecover(...))` passes if you ever compare against an uninitialized/zero `signer`). Never hand-roll `ecrecover` verification.

The unifying discipline: treat a signature as authorizing *one specific action, in one specific context, exactly once*. Bind it with EIP-712 (domain separator), consume a nonce, verify with a malleability-safe library, and never assume signature uniqueness. These bugs sit at the seam between cryptography and contract logic - which is why they connect directly to the ECDSA/EIP-712 material in the cryptography domain.

resources.label: EIP-712 — typed structured data hashing & signing (the exact EIP)

subtopics.title: Audit methodology, fuzzing & formal verification
body: A real audit is **systematic**, not a vibes-based read-through. The workflow: first, understand the system's **invariants** - the properties that must *always* hold (the protocol is solvent: assets ≥ liabilities; shares always redeem for their fair value; only authorized roles can perform privileged actions). Second, map the **trust and threat model** - who are the actors, what can each do, which inputs and external contracts are untrusted, where does value flow. Third, go **function by function** asking the standard battery of questions (reentrancy? access control? arithmetic/rounding? oracle trust? external-call return-value handling? upgrade/storage safety? signature handling?). Finally, rate each finding by **severity = impact × likelihood**, and write it up with a concrete exploit scenario and a fix.

The **assurance ladder** (from the Tooling topic) supplies the automated muscle, cheapest first. **Slither** (static analysis) catches the known patterns in seconds and belongs in CI. **Fuzzing** comes next: **Foundry invariant tests** and **Echidna** (a powerful property-based fuzzer in the Trail-of-Bits ecosystem) run random *sequences* of calls trying to violate your stated invariants - this is where accounting drift, rounding exploits, and sequence-dependent reentrancy surface. Then **formal verification** - **Certora Prover** uses a specification language (CVL) to *mathematically prove* a property holds for *all* possible inputs and states (e.g. 'totalSupply always equals the sum of balances'), not just the cases a fuzzer happened to try; the SMT-based proof either verifies or returns a concrete counterexample.

Each rung catches what the cheaper ones miss, and crucially **none replaces a careful human**: tools find pattern-matchable and property-violating bugs, but the deepest exploits are *design-level logic flaws* - a mechanism that is individually-correct-but-collectively-exploitable, an economic incentive that can be gamed, a composition of two safe protocols that is unsafe together. Those require an experienced auditor reasoning about the system as a whole. The strongest assurance comes from the *combination*: CI static analysis + a strong invariant/fuzz suite + formal proofs of the critical properties + multiple independent human audits + a public **bug bounty** (e.g. Immunefi) + a **timelock/multisig** so issues can be responded to.

To build the attacker's intuition that powers all of this, there is a clear practical curriculum, all hands-on: complete every challenge in **Damn Vulnerable DeFi** (you *write the exploit*, which teaches the bug far better than reading about it); read every relevant post-mortem on **Rekt.news** and reproduce a few on a **Foundry mainnet fork**; work through **Secureum**'s materials and quizzes; and compete in live audit contests on **Code4rena** and **Sherlock**, where you review real, unfamiliar code under time pressure against a paid pool of other auditors. Finding (or failing to find) real bugs against a scoreboard is how the patterns become reflexive.

The mindset to internalize, and the reason security is the domain's through-line: assume your code *will* be attacked by motivated, well-funded adversaries with unlimited flash-loan capital, infinite patience, and full visibility into your source and state. Every external call is hostile until proven otherwise; every price is manipulable until your oracle proves it is not; every privileged function is a target; every line of arithmetic can truncate. Designing under that assumption - minimal trust, explicit invariants, defense in depth, and an exit hatch for users - is what separates contracts that survive from contracts that become the next Rekt headline.

resources.label: Secureum — security pitfalls & best practices (the curriculum)

connections.note: Auditing reuses the OWASP-style 'enumerate the vuln classes' methodology
Replay/malleability bugs come from misusing ECDSA & EIP-712
Most bugs originate in Solidity storage/visibility/call semantics

topics.title: MEV (Maximal Extractable Value)
body: MEV is the hidden financial layer of blockchains - the profit that can be extracted by controlling *which* transactions are included and *in what order*. It exists because two facts from the fundamentals topic combine explosively: pending transactions sit in a *public* mempool for anyone to see, and whoever builds a block has total freedom over its transaction ordering. Order is money, the mempool is a transparent queue, and a whole industry of searchers, builders, and relays has grown up to capture and reshape this value.

This topic explains what MEV is and why it is inevitable, the taxonomy of extraction (arbitrage, sandwiches, liquidations, JIT), the architecture built to manage it (proposer-builder separation, MEV-Boost, the searcher→builder→relay→proposer pipeline), and how it is actually executed (Flashbots bundles, simulation, private orderflow) plus where it is heading (MEV-Share, SUAVE). MEV is the crypto incarnation of high-frequency trading - the same latency, simulation, and adversarial-ordering game - which is why it connects straight to the quant/HFT and low-level networking domains.

subtopics.title: What MEV is & why it is inevitable
body: **Maximal Extractable Value** (originally 'Miner Extractable Value', renamed after the Merge since proposers, not miners, now order blocks) is the maximum value that can be captured by *including, excluding, and reordering* transactions within a block, *beyond* the standard block reward and priority fees. It is the economic surplus latent in the power to sequence transactions.

It is inevitable given two structural properties. First, the **mempool is public**: anyone running a node can observe pending, unconfirmed transactions before they are mined - so a profitable opportunity created by someone's pending transaction (a large swap about to move a price, a loan that just became liquidatable, a mispriced NFT listing) is visible to everyone simultaneously. Second, the **block builder has ordering freedom**: nothing forces transactions to be ordered by arrival time; the builder can place its own or others' transactions anywhere, including immediately before or after a victim's. Put together, the mempool is a transparent order queue and the builder is an auctioneer who can rearrange it at will.

The mental model that makes everything else fall into place: think of the mempool as a fully transparent order book where every pending order is visible *before* it executes, and the block proposer/builder gets to decide the final matching order. Any transaction whose effect on state is *predictable* - and most DeFi transactions are - is an opportunity for someone to position around it. This is precisely the information and ordering advantage that high-frequency traders fight for on traditional exchanges, except here the 'order book' is public to all and the 'matching engine' is auctioned every block.

MEV's scale is enormous - cumulative extraction is measured in the billions of dollars - and it cuts both ways: some MEV is *beneficial* (arbitrage that keeps prices accurate, liquidations that keep lending solvent), and some is *predatory* (sandwich attacks that tax ordinary users). Understanding MEV is therefore essential whether your goal is to *capture* it (as a searcher), to *defend users* from it (as a protocol or wallet designer), or simply to understand why your swap sometimes executes at a worse price than you expected.

subtopics.title: Arbitrage, sandwiches, liquidations & JIT
body: **Arbitrage** is the benign core of MEV and the 'good' kind that keeps DeFi honest. When the same asset is priced differently across venues - two AMMs, or an AMM versus a centralized exchange (CEX-DEX arbitrage) - a searcher atomically buys where it is cheap and sells where it is expensive, pocketing the spread and, in doing so, pushing the prices back into alignment. This is exactly the arbitrage mechanism from the AMM subtopic; it is what tethers AMM prices to the real market, and it is socially useful (it improves price accuracy for everyone). DEX-DEX arbitrage within a single transaction is risk-free given atomicity.

**Sandwich attacks** are the predatory kind that taxes ordinary traders. Seeing a victim's large swap pending in the mempool, the attacker **front-runs** it with their own buy of the same asset (pushing the price up along the AMM curve), lets the victim's swap execute at the now-worse price (the victim suffers extra slippage), then **back-runs** with a sell, capturing the price difference the victim's own trade created. The victim's slippage tolerance is literally the attacker's profit margin. This is why setting a *tight slippage limit* and using private transaction submission are the key user defenses (covered next).

**Liquidations** are competitive but socially necessary MEV. When a lending position's health factor drops below 1 (DeFi topic), it becomes liquidatable, and searchers race to be the one who repays the bad debt and claims the collateral *plus the liquidation bonus*. The competition is intense (often won by the highest priority-fee bid or the fastest bundle), and while it is a transfer from the liquidated borrower, it performs the essential function of keeping the protocol solvent before positions go underwater. **JIT (just-in-time) liquidity** is a v3-specific play: a searcher adds a large, tightly-concentrated liquidity position in the *same block* as a big incoming swap to capture most of that swap's fees, then removes the liquidity immediately after - earning fees with near-zero duration risk, at the expense of passive LPs.

Other forms exist - long-tail arbitrage, NFT-mint sniping, oracle-update back-running, and 'generalized front-running' where bots blindly copy any profitable-looking pending transaction. But arbitrage, sandwiches, and liquidations are the bulk of the value and the ones to understand deeply.

The **user defenses** follow directly from the mechanics. Set a **tight slippage tolerance** so a sandwich cannot move the price far enough to profit (too tight and your trade may revert in volatile conditions - a tradeoff). Submit through a **private relay** (e.g. Flashbots Protect / MEV-blocker RPCs) so your transaction never enters the public mempool and thus cannot be seen and sandwiched. Use **MEV-aware routers/aggregators** that split orders and route to minimize impact. And understand that on an AMM, a large market order in a thin pool is *inherently* exposed - splitting it or using a different venue may be the right call. For a protocol designer, the lesson is to avoid mechanisms that create easy, predictable MEV against your users.

subtopics.title: Proposer-Builder Separation (PBS) & MEV-Boost
body: Left unmanaged, MEV is a **centralizing force**. Extracting MEV well requires sophisticated, capital-intensive infrastructure (mempool monitoring, simulation, optimization), and whoever extracts the most can afford to bid the most for block space and earn the most - so over time block production would concentrate among a few hyper-optimized players, undermining Ethereum's decentralization. **Proposer-Builder Separation (PBS)** is the architectural response: split the *building* of a block (a specialized, competitive, MEV-intensive task) from the *proposing* of it (a simple task any validator can do).

Under PBS, **builders** compete to assemble the most valuable possible block - they ingest the public mempool and private orderflow (searcher bundles), solve the optimization of which transactions in which order maximize total value, and produce a complete block with a bid (the value they will pay the proposer). **Proposers** (ordinary validators) do *not* need any MEV machinery; they simply receive bids from many builders and select the highest-paying block header to propose. This lets a solo validator with 32 ETH earn near the same MEV-adjusted reward as a sophisticated operation, preserving decentralization at the validator layer while concentrating the specialized work in a competitive builder market.

Today this is implemented **off-protocol** via **MEV-Boost**, software the large majority of Ethereum validators run. The missing trust piece is the **relay**: a builder will not reveal its full block (with all the valuable MEV) to a proposer who could simply steal the transactions and propose them itself, and a proposer will not commit to a block it cannot see. The relay sits between them as a trusted intermediary - it receives full blocks from builders, validates them, shows only the *header* and bid to the proposer, and releases the full block body *only after* the proposer has cryptographically committed (signed) to that header. This prevents both parties from cheating.

So the **actor pipeline** to memorize is: **searchers** find MEV opportunities and submit bundles → **builders** assemble complete, ordered blocks from the mempool plus bundles, competing on total value → **relays** broker trust and prevent header/body theft → **proposers** (validators) pick the highest-paying header and propose it. Each arrow is effectively an auction. The roadmap aims to move PBS *into* the protocol itself (**enshrined PBS / ePBS**) so the trusted relay is no longer needed, and related research (inclusion lists, encrypted mempools) aims to curb censorship and predatory MEV.

subtopics.title: Flashbots bundles, simulation & the frontier
body: A searcher does **not** broadcast their MEV transactions to the public mempool - doing so would reveal the strategy and invite other bots to copy or counter-front-run it, and would expose the searcher to failed-transaction gas costs. Instead, searchers submit **bundles** directly to builders. A **bundle** is an *ordered list of transactions that must execute together, atomically, in exactly the specified order, or not at all* - submitted via `eth_sendBundle`. Critically, a bundle can include *someone else's* transaction pulled from the public mempool (e.g. the victim's swap), which is how a sandwich is constructed: `[attacker buy, victim swap, attacker sell]` as one atomic, ordered unit.

Before bidding, searchers **simulate** their bundles against current state with `eth_callBundle` to verify profitability and correctness - if the bundle would not profit (because state changed, or a competitor got there first), they do not submit it, so they never waste gas on a failing attempt. Searchers pay builders for inclusion either through a high **priority fee** or via a direct **`coinbase` transfer** (paying the block proposer) *conditioned on the bundle succeeding* - so the searcher only pays when the bundle actually lands and profits. This private, atomic, simulated, pay-on-success workflow is what makes professional MEV a precise engineering discipline rather than a gamble, and it is the literal analogue of an HFT firm's co-located, simulated, latency-optimized execution stack.

The frontier is about **redistributing** MEV and **decentralizing** its infrastructure, because the status quo has two problems: predatory MEV taxes users, and the builder/relay layer is worryingly centralized. **MEV-Share** lets users *selectively* reveal parts of their transaction to searchers (rather than the all-or-nothing of the public mempool) and receive a **rebate** of the MEV their transaction creates - so value flows *back* to the user who generated it, instead of being fully captured by searchers. **MEV-blocker / protect RPCs** give ordinary users private submission plus rebates by default. And **SUAVE** (Single Unifying Auction for Value Expression) is Flashbots' proposed decentralized, chain-agnostic network that unbundles the mempool, block-building, and orderflow auction into a shared public good - aiming to break the centralization of today's builders and relays.

Two deeper threads to be aware of. **Encrypted mempools / threshold encryption** would hide transaction contents until they are ordered, eliminating front-running at the source (you cannot sandwich what you cannot read) - a cryptographic rather than economic fix. And **cross-domain MEV** (across L2s, across chains, and CEX-DEX) is the next frontier as the ecosystem fragments, raising the same latency and trust questions one level up.

The synthesis to carry away: MEV is the inevitable economic consequence of public mempools plus ordering freedom; it is simultaneously a useful price-correction/solvency mechanism and a user-harming tax; an entire auction-based infrastructure (PBS, MEV-Boost, searchers/builders/relays, bundles) has formed to capture and manage it; and the active research direction is to *redistribute* it to users (MEV-Share), *decentralize* its plumbing (SUAVE, ePBS), and *suppress* its predatory forms (encrypted mempools). It is, end to end, high-frequency trading reimagined for a transparent, programmable, adversarial public ledger - which is exactly why the quant/HFT and low-level networking skill sets transfer directly.

connections.note: MEV searching is HFT-style latency/arbitrage, on-chain
MEV extracts value from AMM, lending and liquidation mechanics
Competitive searching/building pushes toward low-latency networking

topics.title: Layer 2 internals
body: Layer 2 is Ethereum's answer to the scaling problem, and it is the present and future of the ecosystem - the large majority of Ethereum activity now happens on L2s. The core idea is elegant: execute transactions *off* L1 (cheap and fast) while *settling* to L1 (inheriting its security). Everything that distinguishes one L2 from another reduces to one question - *how does L1 become convinced the off-chain execution was correct?* - which splits the world into optimistic rollups (assume correct, allow challenges) and ZK rollups (prove correct cryptographically).

This topic covers why L2 is necessary (the scaling trilemma), the two proof models in depth (optimistic + fraud proofs; ZK + validity proofs), the zkEVM type spectrum and the STARK-vs-SNARK tradeoff, and the data-availability layer (EIP-4844 blobs and full danksharding) that makes rollups cheap. L2 is where scaling, cryptography (ZK), and economics converge most tightly, so it leans on the Merkle-state-root machinery from the EVM topic and the ZK proof systems from cryptography.

subtopics.title: Why L2: the scaling trilemma
body: Ethereum L1 processes only ~15-30 transactions per second, and the reason is fundamental, not a tuning oversight: **every full node must independently re-execute every transaction and store all resulting state**. That redundancy is exactly what gives Ethereum its security and decentralization - anyone can verify the whole chain on consumer hardware - but it hard-caps throughput, because the network can only go as fast as a modestly-powered node can keep up.

The **scaling trilemma** (Vitalik's framing) states that a blockchain wants three properties - **scalability** (high throughput), **security** (costly to attack), and **decentralization** (verifiable by many on cheap hardware) - but naive designs can only strongly satisfy two. The obvious throughput fix, *bigger blocks*, buys scalability by raising node requirements, which prices out home validators and sacrifices decentralization (the road to a few datacenter nodes). So Ethereum deliberately refused to scale L1 execution by enlarging blocks, and instead bet on a different architecture.

**Rollups** resolve the trilemma by separating *execution* from *consensus/data*. They execute transactions off-chain (where one specialized operator can be fast and cheap, no global redundancy needed), but they post the transaction *data* and a *commitment* to the resulting state back to L1, and they make L1 able to *verify* (or adjudicate) the correctness of that execution. Thousands of L2 transactions are batched, compressed, and settled as essentially one L1 'transaction'. Security is inherited from L1 (the data is there, and correctness is enforceable on L1), while throughput multiplies because L1 only has to *verify* a batch, not *re-execute* every transaction in it. This 'execute off-chain, verify on-chain' shift is the whole game.

The two rollup families differ *only* in the verification mechanism: **optimistic** rollups assume execution was honest and let anyone challenge it after the fact with a fraud proof; **ZK (validity)** rollups attach a cryptographic proof that execution was correct, verified immediately on L1. Sequencers (who order L2 transactions), bridges (the canonical L1↔L2 contracts), and data posting are essentially shared between the two. So if you understand the two proof models plus the data-availability layer, you understand L2 - everything else is engineering detail.

resources.label: Vitalik — An Incomplete Guide to Rollups

subtopics.title: Optimistic rollups & fraud proofs
body: An **optimistic rollup** (Arbitrum, Optimism, Base) takes the optimistic stance: it executes a batch of L2 transactions off-chain, posts the batch data plus the resulting **state root** to L1, and simply *asserts* the new state is correct **without proving it**. A **sequencer** orders and executes L2 transactions, giving users fast 'soft' confirmations within a couple of seconds. The security does not come from a proof at posting time - it comes from the *threat* of a fraud proof during a challenge window.

The **challenge (dispute) window** is the heart of the model: for a period of ~7 days after a state root is posted, *anyone* watching the chain can submit a **fraud proof** demonstrating that a posted state transition was invalid. If a fraud proof succeeds, the bad state root is reverted and the dishonest proposer is slashed (loses their bond). The security assumption is therefore '*at least one honest party is watching and will challenge fraud*' (the 1-of-N honest-verifier assumption) - which is weaker than a cryptographic guarantee but, with sufficiently many watchers and economic incentives, robust in practice.

Fraud proofs come in two architectures. **Interactive** fraud proofs (Arbitrum's, now the **BOLD** protocol) use a **bisection game**: the challenger and the asserter disagree about the result of a multi-step computation, so they recursively bisect the disputed execution - 'do you agree with the state at the midpoint?' - narrowing the dispute down to a *single* instruction. L1 then executes just that one instruction to determine who is right. This is brilliant because L1 only ever runs a single step, no matter how large the disputed computation, keeping on-chain cost tiny. **Non-interactive / single-round** fraud proofs (Optimism's **Cannon**) instead re-execute the entire disputed step directly inside an on-chain MIPS/EVM emulator in one shot - simpler, at the cost of more L1 work for the disputed step.

The price of optimism is the **7-day withdrawal delay**. To move funds from L2 back to L1 *trustlessly*, you must wait out the full challenge window - because if withdrawals settled instantly, a fraudulent withdrawal could not be contested. (Third-party 'fast bridges' will front you the funds immediately for a fee, taking on the delay risk themselves, but that is a separate, trusted service - the canonical bridge is the 7-day one.) This latency-to-finality is the central UX tradeoff of optimistic rollups.

The net assessment: optimistic rollups are **simpler to build** and have achieved full **EVM-equivalence** (existing Solidity contracts and tooling work unchanged), which is why they captured the market first. Their tradeoffs are the long withdrawal delay and the 1-of-N honest-watcher trust assumption. Their throughput advantage comes from L1 only needing to *store the data and adjudicate disputes*, not re-execute - exactly the trilemma resolution above.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — optimistic rollups (sequencer, fraud proofs, exact page)

subtopics.title: ZK rollups & validity proofs
body: A **ZK (validity) rollup** (zkSync Era, Starknet, Scroll, Polygon zkEVM, Linea) takes the opposite stance: rather than assuming correctness and allowing challenges, it *proves* correctness upfront. For each batch, it generates a **validity proof** - a zkSNARK or zkSTARK - that cryptographically attests 'this batch of transactions was executed correctly, producing this new state root from the old one'. The proof and the new state root are posted to L1, where a verifier contract checks the proof; if it passes, the new state is accepted as correct.

The defining advantage is **instant, cryptographic finality**. Because L1 *verifies a proof* rather than waiting to see if anyone challenges, an accepted batch is final the moment its proof is verified - there is **no 7-day challenge window** and **no fraud-proof trust assumption**. Withdrawals can be fast (as soon as the batch including them is proven), and the security is mathematical: forging an accepted-but-invalid state would require breaking the underlying cryptography, not merely outlasting honest watchers. This is the strongest security model available for an L2.

The price is **computational**: generating these proofs (the 'proving' step) is expensive and the engineering is extraordinarily hard - especially building a prover that can handle *arbitrary EVM execution* (a **zkEVM**), since you must express every EVM opcode, precompile, and quirk as a system of polynomial constraints the proof system can prove over. This is one of the hardest engineering problems in the entire space, which is why ZK rollups took longer to reach EVM compatibility than optimistic ones did. Proving is also where most of an L2's cost and latency now sit, driving heavy investment in hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA/ASIC provers - a direct link to the GPU-systems domain).

Beyond instant finality, validity proofs unlock capabilities optimistic rollups cannot match: **succinct verification** (a tiny proof can attest to an enormous computation, so even an L1 light client could in principle verify an entire L2's state transition), and a natural path to **privacy** (the same machinery that proves correctness can prove statements without revealing inputs). The validity proofs here are *literally* the zkSNARK/STARK constructions from the cryptography domain, deployed at production scale - which is why this topic is the deepest crypto↔web3 fusion and flows directly into the next, application-focused ZK topic.

resources.label: Ethereum.org — ZK rollups (validity proofs, exact page)

subtopics.title: zkEVM types & STARK vs SNARK
body: Building a ZK rollup that runs Ethereum contracts forces a tradeoff between *how faithfully you reproduce the EVM* and *how fast you can prove it*, and Vitalik's **zkEVM type spectrum** is the standard map of that tradeoff. **Type 1** is fully **Ethereum-equivalent**: it proves Ethereum L1 blocks exactly as they are, with no changes to the EVM, hashing, or state structure - maximally compatible but the *hardest and slowest to prove* (this is the target of the Ethereum-proving effort and projects like Taiko). **Type 2** is **EVM-equivalent**: identical from a contract's and developer's point of view, with only minor internal changes (e.g. to state storage) for proving efficiency. **Type 2.5 / Type 3** sacrifice a bit of equivalence - different gas costs for some operations, a few unsupported precompiles - in exchange for *much faster proving* (where several systems launched, then migrated upward). **Type 4** compiles high-level Solidity/Vyper down to a *custom, ZK-friendly VM* rather than EVM bytecode (historically Starknet's Cairo VM and early zkSync): the *fastest to prove* but *not bytecode-compatible* (existing bytecode and some low-level tooling do not just work). The industry trend is steadily *up* the spectrum toward Type 1 as proving technology improves.

The proof *systems* underneath have their own consequential tradeoff, along three axes: proof size / verification cost, trusted-setup requirement, and quantum resistance. **SNARKs** (e.g. **Groth16**, **PLONK**) produce *tiny* proofs with *cheap* on-chain verification, but the classic constructions require a **trusted setup** - a one-time ceremony that generates secret randomness ('toxic waste') which *must* be destroyed, because anyone retaining it could forge proofs - and they rest on *elliptic-curve pairing* assumptions that are **not quantum-resistant**. **STARKs** (Starknet) require **no trusted setup** at all (they are 'transparent', relying only on hash functions), are **post-quantum secure** (hash-based, not curve-based), and scale better for very large computations - but they produce *larger* proofs, meaning higher on-chain verification cost.

So the one-line summary to remember: **SNARKs = small and cheap, but trusted-setup and quantum-fragile; STARKs = transparent and quantum-safe, but bigger and costlier on-chain.** The choice depends on priorities - minimal L1 cost and proof size push toward SNARKs; eliminating the trusted-setup trust assumption and future-proofing against quantum computers push toward STARKs.

These lines are actively blurring, which is why you should anchor on the *axes* rather than today's specific scheme. **PLONK with KZG commitments** gives a *universal* (reusable across circuits) trusted setup instead of a per-circuit one; **Halo2** achieves no trusted setup with a different commitment scheme; **FRI-based** systems and recursive/folding constructions (Nova, etc.) trade these properties in new ways, and 'STARK-proving wrapped in a SNARK for cheap verification' is a common production pattern. The field moves fast; the durable knowledge is *trusted-setup vs transparent, curve-based vs hash-based (quantum), proof size vs verification cost* - and these are exactly the cryptography-domain concepts, now load-bearing for scaling.

resources.label: Vitalik — the different types of zkEVMs (Type 1-4)

subtopics.title: EIP-4844 blobs (proto-danksharding)
body: Rollups inherit L1 security by posting their data to L1, and for a long time that data went in as ordinary transaction **calldata** - which is permanently stored by every node forever and therefore *expensive*. Data posting was the dominant cost in a rollup's economics, and thus in L2 user fees. **EIP-4844 ('proto-danksharding')**, live since the Dencun upgrade (March 2024), introduced a purpose-built, far cheaper data channel: **blobs**.

A **blob** is a large (~128 KB) packet of data attached to a new transaction type, with two crucial differences from calldata. First, blobs are committed to on-chain via **KZG polynomial commitments** (not stored as raw EVM-accessible bytes) - the chain holds a small commitment that lets anyone verify blob contents, while the EVM cannot directly read the blob data (it can only access the commitment). Second, and decisively, **blobs are not stored permanently**: nodes keep them for only ~18 days, which is long enough for anyone to download, verify, and (for optimistic rollups) challenge, or (for ZK rollups) prove - after which the data can be pruned because its job is done. Rollups only need data to be *available long enough to be checked*, not stored eternally, so permanent storage was pure waste.

Because blob space is a **separate fee market** (its own EIP-1559-style base fee that adjusts to blob demand) and blobs avoid permanent storage, rollup data costs **dropped by roughly an order of magnitude** essentially overnight when 4844 launched - the single largest fee reduction L2 users have experienced. This is what made everyday L2 transactions cost cents rather than dollars.

The **KZG commitment** is worth appreciating: it is a *polynomial commitment* that lets the chain (and provers, and data-availability samplers) reason about and verify pieces of the blob *without holding the whole thing* - the same polynomial-commitment cryptography that underlies many SNARKs and that Verkle trees will use for L1 state. It also required its own one-time trusted-setup ceremony (the **KZG Ceremony / 'Summoning'**, one of the largest ever, with tens of thousands of participants so that only one honest contributor is needed for security). EIP-4844 is explicitly a *stepping stone* (hence 'proto'-danksharding) toward full danksharding, next.

resources.label: EIP-4844 — shard blob transactions (the exact EIP)

subtopics.title: Full danksharding & data-availability sampling
body: **Full danksharding** is the endgame of Ethereum's rollup-centric roadmap: scale blob capacity *massively* (from the handful of blobs per block under 4844 to many megabytes per block) so that dozens of rollups can all post abundant, cheap data. But scaling data raises a deep problem - the **data availability (DA)** problem: how can a node be *sure that all of a block's data was actually published* (so that fraud proofs or validity proofs can be constructed against it) *without itself downloading all of that data*? If a block producer could publish a state root while *withholding* some of the underlying data, no one could prove fraud against the hidden part.

The solution is **Data Availability Sampling (DAS)**, built on **erasure coding**. First, the data is expanded with **Reed-Solomon** redundancy so that any ~50% of the encoded pieces suffice to reconstruct the *entire* original dataset (the same erasure-coding idea used in RAID and CDs). Then each light node **randomly samples** a small handful of pieces and checks that they are available. The probabilistic guarantee is the magic: because reconstruction needs only ~50% of the pieces, an adversary who wants to hide *any* data must withhold *more than 50%* of the pieces - but if more than half are missing, then each random sample has a high chance of hitting a missing piece, so after a node checks just a few random samples and finds them all available, it can conclude *with overwhelming probability that the whole dataset is available*. Many light nodes each sampling a few pieces collectively guarantee availability that none of them individually downloaded.

**KZG commitments** tie the scheme together: each sampled piece comes with a proof that it is a genuine part of the committed blob, so samples cannot be faked or substituted - you are sampling the *real* data, not attacker-supplied decoys. The combination (erasure coding + random sampling + polynomial commitments) lets the network guarantee that gigabytes of data are available while each participant downloads only kilobytes - a profound scaling unlock.

DAS is what makes 'enough cheap data for a world of rollups' actually feasible without sacrificing decentralization (light nodes, not just beefy full nodes, can verify availability). It is also the technical foundation for **modular data-availability layers** - projects like **Celestia** and **EigenDA** specialize *purely* in providing cheap, sampled data availability, letting rollups choose where to post data. The throughline of the whole L2 topic returns here: scaling is achieved by *not* making everyone do everything, and by using cryptography (Merkle/KZG commitments, validity proofs, erasure-coded sampling) to let each participant verify a small slice while trusting math for the rest.

resources.label: Vitalik — An Incomplete Guide to Rollups

connections.note: ZK rollups depend on the validity-proof systems explored next
STARKs/SNARKs are the ZK proof systems from cryptography
State commitments are Merkle/Verkle-tree roots posted to L1

topics.title: Zero-knowledge applications on-chain
body: Zero-knowledge proofs are the most powerful primitive in modern web3 - they let a contract trust *computation it did not perform* and *facts it cannot see*, which quietly removes the EVM's two biggest constraints: cost (everything must be re-executed by every node) and transparency (everything is public). The previous topic used ZK for scaling (validity rollups); this one is about ZK as an *application* primitive: writing circuits, verifying proofs on-chain, and the applications - private voting, identity, coprocessors, account abstraction - it unlocks.

We build from intuition (what a ZK proof guarantees and why it matters on-chain), through the practical toolchain (Circom, Halo2, Noir), the on-chain verification mechanics and proof-system tradeoffs, the application landscape, and finally the adjacent frontier - account abstraction with intents, and confidential computation via FHE. This is the deepest fusion of the cryptography and web3 domains; the concrete milestone that makes it all real is writing a circom circuit that proves Merkle inclusion and verifying its proof in a Solidity contract.

subtopics.title: ZK intuition: proving without revealing
body: A **zero-knowledge proof** lets a **prover** convince a **verifier** that a statement is true while revealing *nothing beyond the truth of the statement itself*. Formally it has three properties: **completeness** (if the statement is true and both parties are honest, the verifier is convinced), **soundness** (if the statement is false, no cheating prover can convince the verifier, except with negligible probability), and **zero-knowledge** (the proof leaks no information about the prover's secret 'witness' beyond the statement's validity). The classic intuition pump is the 'Ali Baba cave': proving you know the secret word to open a door connecting two tunnels, without ever saying the word.

On-chain, two of these properties do most of the work, and it is worth separating them. **Succinctness** - the proof is *tiny and fast to verify* even when the underlying computation is enormous - is what powers scaling and coprocessors: a contract can verify 'this huge program ran correctly' in milliseconds and constant gas. **Zero-knowledge / privacy** - the proof reveals nothing about secret inputs - is what powers private applications: prove you satisfy a condition without exposing *how*. Many systems are technically 'zk-SNARKs/STARKs' (succinct arguments) used purely for succinctness, with privacy a separate optional benefit; being precise about which property you are using clarifies a lot of confusion.

The canonical concrete statements make it tangible. *Preimage knowledge*: 'I know `x` such that `hash(x) = h`' - prove it without revealing `x` (the basis of password-less auth and commit schemes). *Merkle membership*: 'my leaf is in the tree with root `r`' - prove it without revealing *which* leaf, the foundation of private allowlists, anonymous airdrops, mixers, and private voting. *Range/predicate*: 'I am over 18' or 'my balance exceeds 1 ETH' - prove the predicate without revealing the underlying value. *Execution*: 'I correctly ran this program on these inputs' - the validity-rollup use from the L2 topic.

Mechanically, you turn your statement into an **arithmetic circuit** - a system of polynomial constraints over a finite field (the two common arithmetizations are **R1CS**, rank-1 constraint systems, and **PLONKish** custom-gate systems). Proving 'the statement holds' becomes 'I have an assignment of values (the witness) that satisfies all these constraints', and the proof system compiles that into a short proof. The central practical skill is **circuit design**: expressing your logic as efficient constraints, because *proving cost scales with the number of constraints* - so a naive circuit can be orders of magnitude slower to prove than a clever one. This is a genuinely different programming model (everything is constraints over a field; there are no native branches or variable-length loops), which is the main learning curve.

subtopics.title: Writing circuits: Circom, Halo2, Noir
body: **Circom** is the most popular on-ramp and the recommended first tool. It is a domain-specific language where you declare **signals** (the variables of your circuit) and **constraints** relating them - the core constraint operator `<==` both assigns and constrains, and `===` adds a pure constraint (e.g. `a * b === c`). The Circom compiler emits an **R1CS** constraint system plus witness-generation code (in C/WASM). You then pair it with **snarkjs** to run a trusted setup, generate proofs, and export a Solidity verifier. Writing a **Merkle-inclusion** circuit in Circom (hash a leaf with its siblings up to a public root) is the standard first project precisely because it forces you to think in constraints - you implement the hash function *as constraints*, which is illuminating.

**Halo2** (Rust, from the Zcash and Privacy & Scaling Explorations ecosystems) is the next step up in power and complexity. It uses a **PLONKish** arithmetization with *custom gates* (you can define rich, reusable gate types) and *lookup arguments* (efficiently prove a value is in a precomputed table - great for things like range checks and bitwise ops that are expensive as raw constraints). Crucially, Halo2 needs **no per-circuit trusted setup** (it uses a universal/transparent commitment scheme depending on configuration). It is the tool of choice for serious, audited, performance-critical circuits and for building zkEVMs, at the cost of a steeper learning curve and more boilerplate.

**Noir** (by Aztec) is the most developer-friendly option: a high-level, Rust-like language that *abstracts the constraint system away entirely*, so you write what reads like ordinary code (functions, conditionals, structs) and the compiler handles the translation to constraints. It compiles to a **backend-agnostic intermediate representation (ACIR)**, so the same Noir program can target different proving backends (e.g. Barretenberg) without rewriting. Noir's goal is to let normal engineers write ZK applications without circuit-level expertise - the 'Solidity of ZK' aspiration.

Across all three the unifying mental model is that **you are authoring a compiler back-end target**: high-level intent → a constraint system → a proof, exactly analogous to how a compiler lowers source code to an IR and then to machine code (the compilers-domain connection is literal - Circom/Noir *are* compilers to constraint systems). The tradeoff axis is **ergonomics vs control/maturity**: Noir is easiest but younger and more abstracted; Circom is a good middle ground with a huge ecosystem; Halo2 gives the most control and performance for the most effort. The recommended path is to start in **Circom** to *feel* constraints (and trusted setups) concretely, then graduate to **Noir** for application development or **Halo2** for heavy-duty circuits.

resources.label: snarkjs — proving toolkit (setup → prove → Solidity verifier)

subtopics.title: On-chain verification & proof-system tradeoffs
body: The on-chain half of any ZK application is a **verifier contract**. With Groth16, snarkjs *generates a Solidity `Verifier` for you*, whose `verifyProof(a, b, c, publicSignals)` function performs an **elliptic-curve pairing check** using Ethereum's **bn254 (alt-bn128) precompiles** (`ecAdd`, `ecMul`, `ecPairing` at addresses 0x06-0x08). The remarkable property is that this verification costs on the order of **~250k gas regardless of how large the proven computation was** - whether the proof attests to one hash or to a million-step program, on-chain verification is roughly constant. That constant cost is the entire economic point: move arbitrarily heavy computation off-chain, pay a fixed, modest fee to verify it on-chain.

The structure of a real ZK dapp follows from this: design the circuit (with **public inputs** the verifier will check against - e.g. the Merkle root, a nullifier, the claimed output - and **private inputs** the prover keeps secret), generate proofs off-chain (in the browser or a server), and submit `(proof, publicInputs)` to the verifier contract, which gates some on-chain action (release funds, record a vote, mint a credential) on the proof verifying. Getting the public/private split right is a design *and* security decision: anything that must be enforced or is already public goes in public inputs; secrets go in private inputs; and **nullifiers** (a public, deterministic value derived from a secret) are the standard trick to prevent double-use - e.g. double-voting or double-spending in a mixer - without revealing identity.

The **proof-system tradeoffs**, as you meet them at the verification layer: **Groth16** gives the *smallest proof and cheapest verification*, but requires a **per-circuit trusted setup** - change the circuit and you must redo the setup ceremony, which is operationally painful. **PLONK** uses a *single universal trusted setup* reusable across *any* circuit (up to a size bound), trading slightly larger proofs for vastly better developer ergonomics (no re-ceremony per circuit). **Halo2 / FRI-based / STARK** systems require **no trusted setup at all** (transparent), at the cost of larger proofs and higher verification gas. You choose based on how often your circuit changes and how much you want to avoid trusting a setup.

The **trusted setup** deserves a precise mental model because it is the main security caveat of the cheapest systems. The setup produces public proving/verifying keys *and* secret randomness ('**toxic waste**'); if *anyone* retains the toxic waste, they can forge proofs of false statements. The defense is a **multi-party ceremony**: many independent participants each contribute randomness sequentially, and the setup is secure as long as *at least one* participant honestly destroyed their contribution (the '1-of-N honest' assumption). Famous ceremonies include Zcash's original 'powers of tau' and the **Perpetual Powers of Tau** (an ongoing, universal PLONK/KZG setup anyone can contribute to) and Ethereum's KZG Ceremony for EIP-4844. Understanding *why* transparent (no-setup) systems are prized - they eliminate this trust assumption and the quantum-fragility of pairing-based curves - ties directly back to the STARK-vs-SNARK tradeoff from the L2 topic.

resources.label: snarkjs — full Groth16/PLONK workflow incl. Solidity verifier

subtopics.title: Applications: privacy, identity, coprocessors
body: ZK unlocks a class of applications that are simply *impossible* on a transparent, re-execute-everything chain. **Private voting** - e.g. **MACI** (Minimal Anti-Collusion Infrastructure) - lets people vote such that votes are tallied correctly (provable) but individual votes are hidden *and* a voter cannot even *prove to a briber* how they voted, which defeats vote-buying and coercion (the anti-collusion property is the subtle, important part). **Private identity / proof-of-personhood**: prove 'I am over 18', 'I am a unique human', 'I am not on a sanctions list', or 'I hold a valid credential' without revealing your documents or identity - selective disclosure backed by a proof. **zkEmail** proves facts about a real, DKIM-signed email on-chain (e.g. 'this email from this domain confirmed X') without revealing the email's contents - turning Web2 signatures into on-chain-verifiable claims.

**zkBridges** apply the validity-proof idea to cross-chain messaging (recall the bridge trust spectrum from the DeFi topic): instead of trusting a multisig or validator set to attest that something happened on the source chain, a zkBridge generates a *succinct proof of the source chain's consensus and state*, which the destination chain verifies cryptographically - moving bridges toward the trust-minimized end and attacking the single most-exploited component in crypto at its root. **ZK coprocessors** (e.g. **Axiom**) are a powerful newer pattern: they let a contract trustlessly *query and compute over Ethereum's historical state* via a proof - so a contract can use data it could never afford to access directly on-chain (a user's average balance over the last year, a TWAP computed over thousands of historical blocks, aggregate protocol statistics). The coprocessor does the heavy computation off-chain and returns a result plus a proof that it was computed correctly from real on-chain history.

The pattern unifying all of these is worth stating explicitly because it reframes what smart contracts can do: **ZK lets a contract trust computation and data it neither performed nor stored, as long as that computation/data comes with a verifiable proof.** A contract no longer has to choose between 'compute it on-chain at ruinous cost and full transparency' and 'trust an external party' - it can instead *verify a proof*. That single capability lifts the two defining limits of the EVM (cost and transparency), which is why this is the most active research frontier in the entire space, and why so much of the ecosystem's long-term roadmap routes through ZK.

For building intuition, the progression is: implement a Merkle-inclusion proof end-to-end (circuit → proof → Solidity verifier), then a simple nullifier-based private action (a minimal mixer or anonymous allowlist), then study a real system (MACI, Tornado-style designs, or a coprocessor's architecture). Each step makes the public/private-input design and the nullifier pattern concrete.

resources.label: RareSkills — ZK applications, circuits & worked examples

subtopics.title: Account abstraction (ERC-4337) & intents
body: Recall from the fundamentals/EVM topics how *rigid* an EOA is: it is controlled by exactly one secp256k1 keypair, can only authenticate with ECDSA, must itself hold ETH to pay gas, cannot batch operations, and has no recovery if the key is lost. **Account abstraction (AA)** removes this rigidity by making *the account itself a smart contract* that defines its own validation logic. A smart account can authenticate with *arbitrary* schemes (passkeys/WebAuthn so you sign with FaceID, multisig, **session keys** that grant limited temporary permissions to a game or app), support **social recovery** (guardians can rotate your key if you lose it), enforce **spending limits** and allowlists, **batch** many actions into one atomic operation, and have its gas **sponsored** by someone else or paid in any ERC-20 token.

**ERC-4337** delivers all of this *without any protocol change* (no hard fork) by building a parallel transaction system on top of the existing chain. Instead of regular transactions, users sign **UserOperations** (intent-like objects describing what they want their account to do) and send them to a separate **UserOperation mempool**. Specialized actors called **bundlers** collect UserOperations and package them into a regular transaction that calls a single, audited, canonical **EntryPoint** contract. The EntryPoint, for each UserOperation, calls the user's smart account: first `validateUserOp` (the account checks the signature/authorization however it likes - this is where the custom validation logic lives) and then the execution. **Paymasters** are optional contracts that plug into this flow to *sponsor* gas (so a dapp can pay for its users) or accept gas payment in tokens. (**EIP-7702**, a newer complement shipped with the Pectra upgrade, lets an *existing EOA* temporarily delegate to contract code, giving EOAs smart-account superpowers without migrating - bridging the two worlds.)

**Intents** are the user-experience layer that sits on top and represents where the ecosystem is heading. Instead of a user specifying the exact transactions (*how* to achieve something - which router, which path, which gas settings), they sign a declarative statement of *what* they want: 'swap X for at least Y of Z, by the best route available', or 'get me the cheapest bridge to chain B'. A competitive network of **solvers** then races to find and execute the optimal way to fulfill the intent, and they compete on price (the best solver wins the user's business). Solvers often capture or *return* MEV in the process (connecting directly to the MEV topic - intent-based systems like CoW Swap explicitly use batch auctions to give users MEV protection and price improvement).

The synthesis: AA + intents converge wallet UX, MEV, and (increasingly) ZK into the next-generation user experience. Smart accounts make wallets *programmable and recoverable*; intents make interaction *declarative and competitively-solved*; ZK enters as solvers and accounts adopt private or succinct validation (e.g. proving authorization without revealing the key scheme, or private intent matching). Where the early topics described how the rigid base layer works, this is how that base layer is being abstracted away into something usable by ordinary people - the practical endpoint of the whole domain.

resources.label: EIP-4337 — account abstraction via EntryPoint (the exact EIP)

subtopics.title: Frontier: FHE & confidential on-chain compute
body: ZK proves a computation was performed correctly, but note a limitation: *the prover still sees the plaintext data* - ZK gives privacy *from the verifier/public*, not from whoever runs the computation. **Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** closes that gap: it allows computation *directly on encrypted data*, producing an encrypted result, so that the party performing the computation *never sees the plaintext at all*. You can add, multiply, and compose arbitrary functions over ciphertexts, and only a holder of the decryption key can read the output.

Brought on-chain - **Zama's fhEVM** is the leading effort - FHE enables genuinely **confidential smart contracts**: encrypted token balances and transfers (no one can see who holds or moves what), **sealed-bid auctions** (bids stay encrypted until the auction resolves), private on-chain games, and confidential DeFi (e.g. dark-pool-style trading without exposing orders). The contract operates on ciphertexts; decryption is gated to authorized parties via threshold schemes. The cost is *enormous computational overhead* (FHE operations are orders of magnitude slower than plaintext), so it is early, frontier technology - but it represents the missing 'confidential execution' piece that ZK alone does not provide.

These privacy primitives increasingly **compose**, and seeing how is the high-level payoff. **ZK** gives *succinct verifiable computation* with privacy from the public. **FHE** gives *confidential computation* (privacy from the executor). **MPC (multi-party computation)** and **threshold cryptography** distribute trust so that *no single party* holds a secret or a decryption key (used, for instance, to decrypt FHE results or sign without any one node knowing the key). Combined, they point toward a chain that can run *private, verifiable, trust-distributed* applications - the long-term vision in which 'public blockchain' no longer forces 'everything is exposed', and confidentiality becomes a first-class on-chain property rather than something bolted on off-chain.

For you, the practical calibration is this: **ZK is production-ready and where to invest first** - it already powers rollups, bridges, identity, and coprocessors, and the tooling (Circom/Noir/Halo2) is mature enough to build real applications today. **FHE and MPC are research-stage** - worth tracking and prototyping, especially as hardware and schemes improve, but not yet practical for high-throughput use. All three, importantly, are *extensions of the same cryptographic primitives* from the cryptography domain (lattices, pairings, secret sharing) into a privacy-preserving execution layer for web3 - which is exactly why this domain and cryptography are each other's deepest connection, and why a strong cryptography foundation is the highest-leverage investment for the frontier of this field.

resources.label: Zama — fhEVM documentation (confidential smart contracts via FHE)

connections.note: These are the zkSNARK/STARK constructions from cryptography, deployed
On-chain FHE (fhEVM) and threshold schemes extend the privacy primitives
Circom/Noir are compilers that lower circuits to constraint systems

title: Low-Level AI / GPU Systems
tagline: The layer beneath PyTorch: CUDA, kernels, memory, and the systems that make models run fast.
overview: This is the new domain — the bridge between AI/ML (what to compute) and Low-Level Systems (how the hardware computes it). Every model you train or serve ultimately becomes GPU kernels moving tensors through a memory hierarchy. Understanding this layer is what separates someone who *calls* `model.forward()` from someone who can make it 10× faster or fit on half the hardware.

The mental shift: modern deep learning is **memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound** — the game is keeping the tensor cores fed by minimizing data movement (fusion, tiling, quantization). The arc: GPU execution model → CUDA → memory hierarchy → GEMM + tensor cores → Triton/kernel DSLs → fusion (FlashAttention) → inference + distributed systems → ML compilers. It pulls directly on cache/SIMD intuition from low-level systems and the transformer/attention internals from AI.

topics.title: The GPU execution model
body: The hardware reality: a GPU is thousands of simple cores grouped into **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**, executing in **SIMT** lockstep over **warps** of 32 threads. Master the hierarchy — grid → blocks → warps → threads — **occupancy** (how many warps an SM keeps resident to hide latency), warp divergence (the cost of branching), and why GPUs win on throughput, not latency.
This is the GPU analogue of CPU microarchitecture: instead of one fast out-of-order core, you have massive parallelism that only pays off if you feed it coalesced, branch-uniform work.

resources.label: CUDA C++ Programming Guide — programming model
Programming Massively Parallel Processors (PMPP) — Hwu, Kirk, Hajj

connections.note: SIMT/occupancy is the GPU counterpart of CPU pipelines and ILP
Shaders run on this exact SIMT model — graphics and compute share the silicon
Every forward/backward pass is dispatched as kernels onto this model

topics.title: CUDA programming
body: The CUDA C++ model: writing `__global__` kernels, launch configuration (`<<<grid, block>>>`), thread indexing, the host/device memory split, streams and asynchronous execution, and synchronization (`__syncthreads`, events). Start with element-wise kernels, then a naive matmul, then a reduction — the three patterns underneath everything.
The GPU MODE (formerly CUDA MODE) lectures and srush's GPU Puzzles are the best hands-on on-ramps; PMPP is the textbook.

resources.label: GPU Puzzles — srush (learn CUDA by solving)

connections.note: CUDA is C++ with explicit host/device memory — the same ownership discipline
Kernel performance is dominated by how you use the memory hierarchy
Triton is the Pythonic alternative that hides much of this boilerplate

topics.title: Memory hierarchy & coalescing
body: Where performance is actually decided: global memory (high latency, must be **coalesced** — consecutive threads touching consecutive addresses), **shared memory** (fast on-chip scratchpad, beware **bank conflicts**), registers, and L2. The core technique is **tiling**: stage data into shared memory once and reuse it, turning a bandwidth-bound kernel into a compute-bound one.
This is the same locality principle as CPU caches (SoA layout, line utilization) — just explicit and programmer-managed.

resources.label: CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide — memory optimizations

connections.note: Coalescing and tiling are the GPU form of cache-line locality and blocking
Tiling into shared memory is the heart of fast GEMM
Minimizing global-memory round-trips is exactly why we fuse kernels

topics.title: GEMM optimization & tensor cores
body: Matrix multiply is *the* operation of deep learning, so optimizing it teaches everything: hierarchical **tiling** (block → warp → thread), register blocking, double-buffering, and finally **tensor cores** (WMMA / `mma` instructions, mixed-precision FP16/BF16/FP8 accumulation) — the dedicated matmul units that deliver most of a GPU's FLOPs. NVIDIA's **CUTLASS** is the production template library.
Simon Boehm's step-by-step CUDA matmul (naive → near-cuBLAS) is the single best artifact for internalizing this.

resources.label: Simon Boehm — How to Optimize a CUDA Matmul Kernel
NVIDIA — Tensor Core / WMMA programming guide

connections.note: Tensor-core mma instructions are SIMD intrinsics for matrices
GEMM is the bulk of every linear layer and attention projection
Fast GEMM is a shared-memory tiling problem first, math second

topics.title: Triton & kernel DSLs
body: **Triton** lets you write fused GPU kernels in Python at the *block* level — you reason about tiles and `tl.load`/`tl.store`/`tl.dot`, and the compiler handles coalescing, shared-memory staging, and scheduling. It's the pragmatic middle ground: most of CUDA's performance with a fraction of the effort, which is why PyTorch's `torch.compile` generates Triton.
Work the official tutorials in order (vector-add → fused softmax → matmul → fused attention); they teach the whole performance story by construction.

connections.note: Triton lowers through an MLIR/LLVM pipeline to PTX — it's a compiler
Triton's reason to exist is writing fused kernels like FlashAttention
torch.compile's Inductor backend emits Triton automatically

topics.title: Kernel fusion & FlashAttention
body: The central optimization of modern DL: **fuse** sequences of ops into one kernel so intermediates never hit slow global memory. **FlashAttention** is the landmark example — it computes attention *without materializing* the N×N score matrix by tiling and an online-softmax recurrence, turning a memory-bound op into a compute-bound one (and saving O(N²) memory).
Horace He's 'Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr from First Principles' is the essential framing: classify every op as compute-, memory-, or overhead-bound, then fuse the memory-bound ones.

resources.label: Horace He — Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr From First Principles
resources.note: the mental model for GPU perf

connections.note: FlashAttention is the fast implementation of transformer self-attention
Fusion + recomputation are the levers that make large-model training fit
Fusion's whole point is eliminating global-memory round-trips

topics.title: LLM inference systems
body: Serving is its own discipline: the **KV cache** dominates memory, so **PagedAttention** (vLLM) manages it like OS virtual memory (paging, no fragmentation) to maximize batch size and throughput. Add **continuous batching**, **speculative decoding** (draft + verify), and aggressive **quantization** (INT8/INT4, GPTQ/AWQ, FP8) to cut memory and latency.
This is where GPU systems, OS ideas (paging!), and LLMs converge into the engines (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp) that actually run models in production.

connections.note: KV cache, quantization and speculative decoding are LLM-internals made fast
PagedAttention literally borrows OS virtual-memory paging for the KV cache
These engines are the serving layer of the MLOps stack

topics.title: Multi-GPU & distributed systems
body: When a model or batch won't fit on one GPU: **collective communications** (all-reduce, all-gather, reduce-scatter) via **NCCL**, the interconnects that make them fast (NVLink, NVSwitch, InfiniBand/RDMA), and the parallelism strategies — **data**, **tensor**, **pipeline**, and **sequence/expert** parallelism — plus **ZeRO/FSDP** sharding of optimizer state, gradients, and parameters.
The bottleneck is communication, so the art is overlapping compute with comms and choosing the parallelism that minimizes cross-device traffic.

connections.note: This is the systems realization of tensor/pipeline parallelism and ZeRO
RDMA/InfiniBand bypass the kernel — the same tech as HFT/low-level networking
Multi-node training is a distributed-systems orchestration problem

topics.title: ML compilers & profiling
body: How frameworks turn graphs into fast kernels: **torch.compile** (TorchDynamo capture → Inductor → Triton), **XLA** (the compiler behind JAX/TF, operator fusion), **TVM**, and the **MLIR**-based stacks underneath. Closing the loop is measurement: **roofline analysis** (are you compute- or memory-bound?) and profiling with **Nsight Compute/Systems**.
You can't optimize what you can't measure — roofline + Nsight tell you which of the previous topics to reach for.

connections.note: XLA/Inductor/TVM are MLIR/LLVM-family compilers for tensor programs
torch.compile is often the cheapest single training/inference speedup
Roofline analysis is the GPU analogue of CPU top-down methodology

subtopics.title: SIMT execution and the warp: the hardware contract
body: A GPU does not execute individual threads independently - it executes **warps** of 32 threads in **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads)** lockstep. All 32 threads in a warp execute the same instruction at the same time, each on a different data element. This is the fundamental hardware contract: one instruction fetch, one decode, but 32 ALU operations happen simultaneously. The performance model falls out of this single fact. If your algorithm maps cleanly onto this - 32 independent data-parallel operations per step - you get maximal throughput. If it doesn't, you pay.

The SIMT model is a superset of the older SIMD model. In CPU SIMD (AVX-512 etc.), a single thread explicitly operates on a vector register. In SIMT, the 'vector' is implicit - the hardware virtualises 32 scalar threads into a single hardware vector lane, each with its own program counter state and register file. From the programmer's perspective, threads are independent; from the hardware perspective, they are locked to the same instruction stream. This virtualisation is what makes GPU programming feel easier than SIMD intrinsics while delivering comparable throughput.

**Register file size** is what makes the warp model work. A modern SM (e.g., A100) has a 256KB register file. With 32 threads per warp and 64 warp slots per SM, an SM can hold up to 2048 thread contexts simultaneously. Context switching between warps is a zero-cost hardware operation - the scheduler simply selects a ready warp and issues its next instruction. There is no state save/restore overhead (unlike OS thread context switches). This is why GPUs tolerate high memory latency: instead of stalling, the scheduler switches to another warp whose memory request returned.

**Warp execution state**: at any given cycle, each warp is in one of several states - (1) **eligible**: all 32 threads have their operands ready, the warp can issue; (2) **stalled on memory**: outstanding load/store waiting; (3) **stalled on compute dependency**: waiting for a prior instruction's result (e.g., FP multiply has a 4-cycle latency); (4) **stalled on synchronisation**: waiting for a `__syncthreads()` barrier. The warp scheduler selects from eligible warps each cycle. An SM with many concurrently resident warps has more eligible candidates to choose from, hiding the latency of the stalled ones. **Occupancy** - the ratio of active warps to the SM's maximum - is the quantitative measure of this.

A critical asymmetry: GPU warp scheduling is **in-order** within a warp. There is no out-of-order execution within a single warp - instructions issue in program order. However, the SM can **dual-issue** (on Ampere/Hopper) - dispatch instructions to two different execution units (e.g., FP32 unit and load/store unit) in the same cycle from the same warp, if there is no data dependency. This dual-issue is what lets memory-bound kernels overlap compute and memory operations in practice - a load followed by independent arithmetic can execute simultaneously.

The practical implication for kernel design: write code where the 32 threads in a warp do the same thing (no divergence), access memory addresses that form a single contiguous 128-byte aligned block (coalescing), and where the working set per thread is small enough that enough warps can be resident to hide latency. Every GPU optimisation technique is ultimately about satisfying one or more of these three requirements. Deviating from any one of them is the source of measurable performance loss.

resources.label: Modal GPU Glossary - warps and SIMT
CUDA C++ Programming Guide - SIMT architecture

subtopics.title: The SM hierarchy: grids, blocks, and occupancy arithmetic
body: A CUDA kernel launch creates a **grid** of **thread blocks** (or cooperative groups). The grid is a logical decomposition of the problem; the hardware mapping is: the driver distributes blocks to available SMs. An SM executes one or more blocks simultaneously (up to its resource limits), and each block contains 32-1024 threads. Threads within a block can communicate via **shared memory** and synchronise with `__syncthreads()`. Threads in different blocks cannot synchronise within a kernel - this is not a software choice but a hardware invariant that enables the scheduler to distribute blocks freely without deadlock.

**The A100 numbers** (commit these to memory): 108 SMs; each SM has 64 FP32 cores, 4 Tensor Core units (each handling 16x16x16 FP16 matmul), 192KB L1/shared memory, 256KB register file, 2048 max threads resident, 32 max resident blocks, 64 max resident warps. Total: 108 × 2048 = 221,184 threads resident simultaneously. Total FP32 throughput: 108 × 64 × 2 (dual-issue on Ampere, theoretical) × clock = ~19.5 TFLOP/s. Total memory bandwidth: 2 TB/s HBM2e. The ratio TFLOP/s per TB/s = ~9.75 FLOP per byte, which is the **arithmetic intensity** at which your kernel transitions from compute-bound to memory-bound (the ridge point of the roofline).

**Occupancy** is the fraction of the SM's maximum warp capacity that is actually used: $\text{occupancy} = \text{active warps} / \text{max warps (64 on A100)}$. Three resources limit active warps: (1) **registers**: if a kernel uses 64 registers/thread, then 64 × 32 = 2048 registers/warp, and the register file (256KB = 65536 registers) supports at most 32 warps - 50% occupancy; (2) **shared memory**: if a block uses 48KB shared memory and the SM has 96KB, at most 2 blocks fit - if each block has 128 threads (4 warps), only 8 warps are active - 12.5% occupancy; (3) **block count**: at most 32 blocks/SM regardless of resources. Use `cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor()` or Nsight to find the actual limiting factor.

**Does higher occupancy always help?** No - this is a common misconception. Occupancy is a proxy for latency hiding, not a goal itself. If your kernel is compute-bound (arithmetic intensity > ridge point) and has enough warps to keep the tensor cores busy, adding more warps via reduced register use does not improve throughput. The correct mental model: you need *enough* warps to hide the memory latency of the working set. Empirically, 25-50% occupancy is often sufficient, and kernel tuning that reduces register usage to chase 100% occupancy can actually hurt by reducing the working set each thread can keep in registers (increasing cache pressure).

**Block size tuning**: the block size is the key tuning knob. Rules of thumb: (a) always use a multiple of 32 (to ensure full warps - a 33-thread block wastes one warp slot with 31 idle threads); (b) prefer 128, 256, or 512 threads/block for most kernels; (c) for shared-memory-heavy kernels, smaller blocks may allow more blocks/SM; (d) for compute-heavy kernels with large register usage, larger blocks reduce per-SM overhead. Profile with NVIDIA's occupancy calculator or Nsight's 'Launch Statistics' section - it will show you the binding resource and the achieved vs theoretical occupancy.

**Cooperative Groups** (CUDA 9+) generalize the synchronization model. Instead of only synchronizing at the full-block granularity with `__syncthreads()`, you can create groups at warp granularity (`coalesced_group`) or sub-warp granularity (`thread_block_tile<N>`). **Warp-level intrinsics** (`__shfl_sync`, `__ballot_sync`, `__reduce_sync`) operate without shared memory at the register level - a warp shuffle exchanges registers between threads in a warp in a single instruction. A warp reduction (sum 32 values) can be done in 5 shuffles (log2(32) = 5), each costing 1 instruction with 4-cycle latency, for ~20 cycles total vs 32 sequential additions.

resources.label: CUDA C++ Programming Guide - cooperative groups

subtopics.title: Warp divergence: branching costs and predication
body: When threads in a warp take different branches of an `if` statement, the warp **serializes**: it executes the if-branch with threads that took it active (others masked off), then the else-branch with the other set active. The total execution time is the sum of both branch lengths, not the max. If the if-branch takes 10 cycles and the else-branch takes 5 cycles, a diverged warp takes 15 cycles while a converged warp would take max(10, 5) = 10. At worst (50/50 split, both branches equal length), you lose 50% of throughput on that warp. This is called **warp divergence** and it is one of the most common GPU performance bugs.

**When does divergence happen?** Any condition that varies between threads within a warp. Common causes: (1) **boundary conditions** - e.g., `if (idx < N)` where N is not a multiple of 32; the last warp has some out-of-bounds threads. This is unavoidable but benign (single divergence at the end). (2) **Data-dependent branching** - `if (data[idx] > threshold)` where the threshold splits the data arbitrarily within a warp. (3) **Tree traversal** - BVH, kd-tree, hash table probing - different threads take different paths through a data structure. (4) **While loops with per-thread exit conditions** - `while (x[i] != 0)` - threads exit at different iterations.

**Reconvergence**: modern GPUs use **IPDOM (Immediate Post-Dominator)** reconvergence. The hardware identifies the first point where all branches merge (the immediate post-dominator in the control flow graph) and reconverges the warp there. Nested branches create a divergence stack: each divergence pushes a reconvergence point, and the warp serializes each sub-branch. Volta introduced **Independent Thread Scheduling (ITS)**: each thread has its own program counter and call stack. This allows threads to reconverge at finer granularity and makes warp-synchronous programming (relying on implicit warp synchrony) unsafe - you must use explicit sync barriers.

**Predication**: the compiler converts short if-else blocks into predicated instructions. Instead of branching, both branches are compiled into a sequence of conditionally executed instructions: `SETP.GT.F32 p, x, threshold; @p MOV y, a; @!p MOV y, b`. Both instructions execute every cycle, but the write is suppressed for inactive threads. This eliminates divergence at the cost of executing more instructions. The compiler applies predication when branches are short (typically < 4 instructions) - for longer branches, the overhead of executing both paths exceeds the cost of divergence.

**Practical mitigation patterns**: (1) **Warp-uniform branching**: structure data so all threads in a warp take the same branch. For a sparse computation, sort elements by type before dispatching to a GPU kernel. (2) **Thread coarsening for boundary conditions**: process multiple elements per thread so boundary conditions only affect the last few threads in the last block. (3) **Avoid data-dependent while loops**: prefer for loops with a fixed iteration count, use early-exit with a `__ballot_sync` vote to check if all threads want to exit. (4) **Sort by work type**: if you have a particle simulation where some particles are 'alive' and others 'dead', compact the alive particles into a contiguous array before GPU dispatch - every warp is then uniform.

**The 'warp divergence is everything' fallacy**: many GPU beginners over-weight divergence. The L1 cache miss latency (200-400 cycles on A100) and memory bandwidth are almost always the dominant bottleneck for non-trivial kernels. Divergence of 1-2 branches per warp costs 10-20 cycles. Profiling with Nsight will tell you the truth: look at 'Warp State Statistics' (divergence) vs 'Memory Workload Analysis' (bandwidth). Fix the bigger number first.

resources.label: CUDA C++ Programming Guide - control flow

subtopics.title: Latency hiding and the throughput vs latency duality
body: A GPU's L2 cache is ~240 cycles away; DRAM (HBM) is ~500 cycles away (on A100). A single-threaded CPU would stall for these latencies. A GPU hides them by switching warps: while warp A waits for a memory response, warp B (and C, D...) executes. The number of warps needed to fully hide memory latency is: $\text{warps needed} = \text{latency (cycles)} / \text{throughput (cycles per instruction issue)}$. With 4-cycle issue throughput and 500-cycle L2 miss, you need 500/4 = 125 independent operations in flight, which requires roughly 4 warps (4 × 32 = 128 threads). If you have 64 warps resident (max occupancy on A100), you can hide even DRAM latency.

**Little's Law formulation**: throughput = concurrency / latency. For a memory system with 2TB/s bandwidth and 500ns latency: concurrency = 2TB/s × 500ns = 1MB of in-flight data. With 128-byte cache lines, you need 1MB/128 = 8192 outstanding cache-line requests. Each SM issues ~4 requests concurrently per warp, with 64 warps = 256 concurrent requests, and 108 SMs × 256 = 27,648 total - comfortably exceeding the 8192 needed. This is why high-occupancy kernels can saturate memory bandwidth even with high per-request latency.

**The throughput vs latency tradeoff**: a GPU achieves high throughput by sacrificing per-request latency. A GPU's arithmetic instruction throughput is 19.5 TFLOP/s; but the latency of a single FP32 multiply is 4 cycles. On a 1.4 GHz clock, 4 cycles = 2.8 ns. A CPU achieves ~2 ns latency for the same operation. The GPU wins on throughput because 64 × 108 = 6912 multiply units execute simultaneously; the CPU's 32-wide AVX-512 SIMD unit has only 1 execution unit per core.
