For latency-sensitive tasks (one result needed immediately), the CPU wins. For throughput tasks (billions of independent operations), the GPU wins by orders of magnitude.

**Compute-bound vs memory-bound**: the **roofline model** formalises this. For a computation with arithmetic intensity $I = F/B$ (FLOP per byte of memory traffic), you are (1) **compute-bound** if $I > I^* = P_{peak}/BW_{peak}$ (the ridge point); throughput is $P_{peak}$; (2) **memory-bound** if $I < I^*$; throughput is $I \times BW_{peak}$. For A100: $I^* = 19.5 \text{ TFLOP/s} / 2 \text{ TB/s} = 9.75 \text{ FLOP/byte}$. A vector addition (2 reads + 1 write, 1 FLOP per element): $I = 1/12 \approx 0.08$ FLOP/byte - heavily memory-bound; achieves $\sim 0.08 \times 2 = 160$ GFLOP/s despite 19.5 TFLOP/s peak. A matmul on 4096×4096 FP16: $I = 2N^3 / (2 \cdot N^2 \cdot 2B) \approx N/4 = 1024$ FLOP/byte - compute-bound; achieves close to 312 TFLOP/s (tensor core peak).

**Theoretical vs achieved peak**: cuBLAS achieves ~85-90% of theoretical tensor core FLOPs on large square matrices. The gap comes from: (1) epilogue overhead (writing results back to global memory); (2) pipeline fill/drain at block boundaries; (3) memory access for A and B matrices eating some cycles. For memory-bound operations, you can often achieve 70-90% of HBM bandwidth with well-optimised kernels. The gap from 100% is mostly pointer arithmetic, predication, and L1 hit overhead.

resources.label: Horace He - Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr (roofline section)
Williams et al. - Roofline model (original paper)

subtopics.title: Kernel launch model: __global__, grids, and thread indexing
body: A CUDA kernel is a C++ function decorated with `__global__` that runs on the GPU. Calling `myKernel<<<gridDim, blockDim, sharedMem, stream>>>(args)` is an **asynchronous dispatch**: it returns immediately on the host, queuing the kernel in the specified stream. The grid and block dimensions can be 1D, 2D, or 3D (`dim3` struct). The canonical 1D launch: `myKernel<<<(N+255)/256, 256>>>()` creates ceil(N/256) blocks of 256 threads each. Inside the kernel, `threadIdx.x` (0-255), `blockIdx.x`, and `blockDim.x` (256) let each thread compute its global index: `int idx = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x`.

**Thread indexing patterns**: for a 2D matrix (rows × cols), a 2D launch is natural: `dim3 block(32, 32)` (1024 threads = 32 warps); `dim3 grid(ceil(cols/32), ceil(rows/32))`. Inside: `int col = blockIdx.x * 32 + threadIdx.x; int row = blockIdx.y * 32 + threadIdx.y`. The 2D structure maps to the data layout without per-element arithmetic. For 3D tensors (batch × H × W), a 3D grid. For very large N (> 2^31 - 1 elements, the max gridDim), use 64-bit index arithmetic: `long long idx = (long long)blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x`.

**The `__device__` and `__host__` qualifiers**: `__global__` functions are called from host, run on device. `__device__` functions are called from device, run on device - these are your GPU helper functions (e.g., a PRNG, a specialised math op). `__host__` is the default for CPU functions. You can declare `__host__ __device__` for functions that need to compile for both (common in templated math libraries like Thrust or Eigen). Note: `__global__` functions cannot return a value (must be `void`) and cannot be called recursively (except in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism).

**Grid-stride loops**: the canonical pattern for processing N elements with fewer threads. Instead of `idx < N ? process(idx) : skip`, each thread processes multiple elements: `for (int i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x; i < N; i += gridDim.x * blockDim.x)`. This (a) handles arbitrary N without edge cases; (b) reuses thread blocks (reducing launch overhead for large N); (c) allows tuning the grid size independently of N. A grid of 1024 blocks × 256 threads = 262,144 threads processing N = 10^8 elements will each iterate ~381 times. This pattern is used in almost every production-grade elementwise kernel.

**Kernel launch overhead**: launching a kernel has ~5-10 µs overhead on modern systems (includes command submission, HW scheduling, SM activation). For tiny kernels (1 element), this overhead dominates. Strategies: (1) **fuse kernels** - one kernel that does multiple operations; (2) **CUDA Graphs** - record a sequence of kernel launches and memory copies into a graph object, then replay the entire graph with a single `cudaGraphLaunch()` call, amortising scheduling overhead over all nodes; (3) **persistent kernels** - a kernel that loops internally and pulls work from a queue, never returning to the host. CUDA Graphs are the modern standard and can reduce overhead by 10-50x for pipelines with many short kernels.

resources.label: CUDA C++ Programming Guide - programming model
CUDA C++ Programming Guide - CUDA graphs

subtopics.title: Host/device memory model and efficient transfers
body: CUDA has two separate memory spaces: **host** (CPU RAM, accessed via regular pointers) and **device** (GPU HBM/VRAM, accessed via device pointers). `cudaMalloc(&d_ptr, size)` allocates device memory; `cudaFree(d_ptr)` releases it. `cudaMemcpy(dst, src, size, direction)` transfers data synchronously (blocking the CPU until complete); `cudaMemcpyAsync` is non-blocking and takes a stream argument. The direction enum: `cudaMemcpyHostToDevice`, `cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost`, `cudaMemcpyDeviceToDevice` (GPU-to-GPU, uses NVLink if available and faster than going through host).

**PCIe bandwidth limits**: the PCIe 4.0 x16 link that connects CPU and GPU provides 32 GB/s bidirectional, but the effective usable bandwidth is 25-28 GB/s after protocol overhead. PCIe 5.0 doubles this to ~56 GB/s. Compare to HBM2e bandwidth of 2 TB/s - PCIe is 80x slower. The implication: **host-to-device transfers are extremely expensive relative to on-device computation**. A 1GB tensor transfer takes ~40ms over PCIe but <1ms to process with a simple kernel. Minimize PCIe traffic: preload data, keep tensors resident on GPU across kernels, use in-place operations.

**Pinned (page-locked) memory**: normal `malloc`/`new` host allocations are **pageable** - the OS can swap them out. `cudaMallocHost(&h_ptr, size)` allocates **pinned** (page-locked) memory that cannot be swapped. `cudaMemcpyAsync` requires pinned memory to be truly asynchronous - if you pass a pageable pointer, it silently falls back to synchronous transfer. Pinned memory enables **DMA transfers** that bypass the CPU, achieving full PCIe bandwidth. Downside: pinning locks physical pages, reducing available physical memory and potentially affecting overall system memory management. Allocate the minimum needed.

**Unified Memory (UM)**: `cudaMallocManaged(&ptr, size)` allocates a shared address space accessible from both CPU and GPU. On access fault from either side, the CUDA runtime migrates the page to the accessing processor. This simplifies code dramatically - just one pointer, no explicit copies. Performance caveats: (a) first access after migration incurs a page fault (takes ~10 µs per page = 4KB); (b) concurrent CPU/GPU access triggers expensive coherence traffic; (c) no HBM prefetch means memory-bound kernels that access UM can be significantly slower than explicit transfers. UM is good for prototyping and for large datasets that don't fit in device memory (demand paging); production code should use explicit transfers with prefetch hints (`cudaMemPrefetchAsync`).

**CUDA streams and the async transfer pattern**: a stream is a queue of GPU operations (kernels + copies) that execute in order. Operations in different streams can overlap. The canonical pattern for hiding PCIe transfer latency: divide data into N chunks; for each chunk, `cudaMemcpyAsync(H2D)`, then `kernel<<<..., stream>>>()`, then `cudaMemcpyAsync(D2H)`, all in the same per-chunk stream. With 3 streams and 3 chunks, chunk 1's kernel executes while chunk 2 is being transferred H2D and chunk 0's result is being transferred D2H - all three PCIe lanes (H2D, D2H, compute) overlap. Modern hardware (A100+) has separate copy engines for H2D and D2H, allowing truly simultaneous bidirectional transfer.

resources.label: CUDA C++ Programming Guide - memory management
NVIDIA blog - how to overlap data transfers
CUDA Best Practices Guide - data transfer

subtopics.title: The parallel reduction pattern: from O(N) serial to O(log N) parallel
body: A **reduction** takes an array of N elements and combines them with an associative operator (sum, max, product) to produce a single scalar. Serially: O(N) time. On a GPU: O(log N) steps, where each step halves the active elements. The textbook parallel reduction: (1) load N elements into shared memory; (2) in each step, half the threads add pairs: `if (threadIdx.x < stride) shmem[threadIdx.x] += shmem[threadIdx.x + stride]`; (3) halve stride; repeat; (4) thread 0 holds the block result; (5) atomic-add block results into global output. This is the foundational pattern for loss computation, normalisation statistics, and dot products.

**Mark Harris's 7-step optimisation series** (NVIDIA, 2007 - still essential) shows how a naive reduction goes from 0.5% to 95%+ of memory bandwidth: Step 1: sequential addressing (avoids divergence); Step 2: sequential thread loading (coalescing); Step 3: last-warp unrolling (avoid `__syncthreads` overhead for the last 32 threads); Step 4: complete unrolling with templates; Step 5: first add during load; Step 6-7: multiple elements per thread. Each step has a measured speedup; the series teaches CUDA optimisation thinking better than any abstract tutorial.

**Warp-level reductions** using `__shfl_down_sync`: instead of shared memory, reduce within a warp using register shuffles. `__shfl_down_sync(mask, val, offset)` returns the value from the thread `threadIdx.x + offset` in the same warp. A complete warp sum in 5 instructions: `val += __shfl_down_sync(0xffffffff, val, 16); val += __shfl_down_sync(0xffffffff, val, 8); ...` down to offset 1. No shared memory needed, no `__syncthreads`. Then one thread from each warp writes to shared memory for the cross-warp reduction. This is the pattern used in cuDNN, PyTorch's reduction kernels, and Apex's fused layer norm.

**Atomic operations**: `atomicAdd(&global[0], partial_sum)` is the simplest way to combine per-block partial results. On pre-Volta hardware, global atomic adds were slow (serialised through L2). On Ampere+, native FP32 atomic adds are hardware-accelerated and can achieve 2-4x the bandwidth of shared-memory-based approaches for sparse reductions. `atomicMax`, `atomicCAS` (compare-and-swap), `atomicExch` are the other key primitives. **Lock-free data structures** on GPUs (queues, hash tables) are built from `atomicCAS`. The challenge: atomic operations serialize when multiple threads hit the same address - locality matters. Histogram computation with 256 bins and 10^8 elements needs either local histograms in shared memory (then reduce) or accept atomic serialization.

**The scan (prefix sum) pattern**: scan takes an array and returns the cumulative sum: `out[i] = sum(in[0..i])`. Parallel scan is O(N log N) with the naive algorithm or O(N) with the Blelloch (work-efficient) algorithm. Blelloch scan: up-sweep phase (binary tree reduction), then down-sweep phase (propagate partial results). Thrust's `inclusive_scan` and CUB's `DeviceScan` implement this efficiently. Scan underlies stream compaction (filter an array), sparse-to-dense conversion, and RLE encoding. **Stream compaction** - removing elements that fail a predicate - is: (1) compute a per-element flag (0/1 array); (2) exclusive scan the flags to get output indices; (3) scatter elements to their output positions. This is how sparse attention, variable-length batching, and active particle filtering are implemented on GPUs.

resources.label: Mark Harris - Optimizing Parallel Reduction in CUDA (PDF)
CUDA C++ Programming Guide - atomic functions

subtopics.title: Streams, events, and asynchronous programming
body: A CUDA **stream** is an ordered sequence of GPU operations that execute in FIFO order. The default stream (stream 0 on pre-CUDA 7 semantics, or the per-thread default stream with `--default-stream per-thread`) serialises all operations across all streams when used - avoid it in performance-critical code. `cudaStreamCreate(&stream)` creates a new non-blocking stream. `cudaStreamSynchronize(stream)` blocks the CPU until all operations in the stream complete. `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` blocks until ALL streams complete.

**Event-based timing**: `cudaEvent_t start, stop; cudaEventCreate(&start); cudaEventCreate(&stop); cudaEventRecord(start, stream); kernel<<<...>>>(); cudaEventRecord(stop, stream); cudaEventSynchronize(stop); float ms; cudaEventElapsedTime(&ms, start, stop)`. This is the only correct way to time GPU kernels - `std::chrono` on the host measures launch overhead, not execution time. Always use events for benchmarking. The resolution of CUDA events is ~0.5 µs.

**Stream priorities**: `cudaStreamCreateWithPriority(&stream, flags, priority)` with priority 0 (low) or -1 (high). Higher-priority streams preempt lower-priority ones at warp-granularity. Use case: a real-time inference server where user-facing requests (high priority) must preempt background retraining jobs (low priority). Preemption is not instantaneous - it waits for the current warp batch to finish (~100 µs max). For hard real-time (<1 ms), use separate GPU contexts or MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) partitioning.

**CUDA Graphs**: the Graph API records a sequence of operations (kernels, memcpys, events, child graphs) once and replays them repeatedly with near-zero CPU overhead. Use case: ML inference pipelines where the same operations run on each request. Recording: `cudaStreamBeginCapture(stream)` → operations → `cudaStreamEndCapture(stream, &graph)` → `cudaGraphInstantiate(&graphExec, graph)`. Replaying: `cudaGraphLaunch(graphExec, stream)` - this single call submits the entire graph to the GPU. For pipelines with 10-100 short kernels, CUDA Graphs reduce total CPU-side scheduling overhead from ~50-100 µs to ~5-10 µs. PyTorch uses CUDA Graphs internally for `torch.compile` and `torch.cuda.graph()`.

**Multi-stream overlap on Ampere**: A100 has one compute engine and 3 copy engines (H2D, D2H, P2P). This means one kernel + one H2D transfer + one D2H transfer can all run concurrently in different streams. The practical limit: the GPU has a finite number of SMs. If one kernel launches 108 blocks (one per SM) and another launches in a second stream, the second waits for SM availability. True overlap only happens when kernels underutilize the GPU (e.g., a small kernel on 20 SMs while the other 88 SMs are idle). Use `cudaStreamWaitEvent` to create inter-stream dependencies: stream B should not start kernel 2 until stream A's kernel 1 is done, but both run concurrently otherwise.

subtopics.title: The GPU memory hierarchy: registers, shared, L1/L2, and HBM
body: The GPU memory hierarchy is the primary determinant of kernel performance. From fastest to slowest: **registers** (~1 cycle, per-thread, 256KB/SM = 65,536 32-bit registers on A100); **L1 cache / shared memory** (same physical SRAM, configurable split, ~23 cycles for L1 hit, ~192KB/SM on A100); **L2 cache** (~200 cycles, 40MB on A100, shared across all SMs); **HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)** (~500 cycles, 80GB, 2 TB/s bandwidth on A100). Getting data to registers from HBM and back is the bottleneck for almost all non-trivial kernels.

**Registers**: each SM has a fixed-size register file (256KB on A100). Registers are the fastest storage but do not persist between kernel launches. The compiler allocates variables to registers automatically; `__launch_bounds__` and `nvcc` flags control register usage. **Register spilling**: when a kernel uses more registers than available (e.g., a complex kernel needs 128 registers/thread but the SM has only 65536/(32 threads × max_warps) available), the compiler spills excess registers to L1/L2 or DRAM as **local memory** (a per-thread private region). Local memory accesses look like global memory accesses with regular addressing - they incur full DRAM latency if they miss L1. Spilling kills performance. Check via `nvcc --ptxas-info` or Nsight 'Registers Per Thread' metric.

**Shared memory**: a software-managed on-chip scratchpad. `__shared__ float tile[32][32]` declares 32×32 × 4 bytes = 4KB shared memory per block. All threads in a block see the same shared memory; it persists for the lifetime of the block. This is the GPU's analog of the CPU's L1 cache, but explicitly controlled. The programmer decides what to load, when, and how to reuse it. The bandwidth is ~19 TB/s (shared memory bandwidth on A100) - comparable to the register file bandwidth and 10x faster than L2. The pattern: load from global → compute from shared → write to global. The 'load' amortises global memory traffic over many reuses.

**L2 cache**: 40MB on A100, shared across all 108 SMs. Capacity per SM: 40MB/108 ≈ 370KB - slightly larger than shared memory. The L2 is most useful for data with **temporal locality** that doesn't fit in shared memory: bias vectors in linear layers (reused for every batch element), small weight matrices, BN statistics. For large GEMM on A100 (512×512 tiles), the L2 can hold the B matrix tile across all blocks, significantly reducing HBM traffic. L2 **cache residency hints** (`cudaAccessPropertyPersisting` on Ampere) can pin specific arrays in L2, preventing eviction by other data. Use for frequently-reused small tensors (embeddings, bias).

**HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)**: stacked DRAM dies connected via a silicon interposer directly to the GPU die, achieving 2 TB/s bandwidth (A100 SXM) vs 50-100 GB/s for DDR5 on a CPU. HBM is large (40-80 GB on A100/H100) but high latency (~500 cycles = ~350 ns). The reason GPUs use HBM instead of GDDR6 (which is cheaper): HBM has ~8× the bandwidth for the same power, crucial for memory-bound workloads. **The arithmetic intensity crossover**: for a kernel with $I$ FLOP/byte, if $I < 9.75$ (A100 ridge point), you are limited by HBM bandwidth. Nearly all elementwise operations (LayerNorm, GeLU, dropout, elementwise multiply) fall well below this - they are memory-bound and benefit from kernel fusion to reduce HBM round-trips.

resources.label: PMPP Ch 5 - memory architecture and data locality

subtopics.title: Memory coalescing: the access pattern law
body: **Memory coalescing** is the single most important memory access optimisation. When a warp of 32 threads issues load/store instructions, the GPU memory controller attempts to combine them into a small number of memory transactions. **Coalesced**: 32 threads access 32 consecutive 4-byte words starting at a 128-byte aligned address → 1 memory transaction (128 bytes = 1 cache line). **Uncoalesced**: 32 threads access 32 arbitrary addresses → up to 32 separate transactions. The performance ratio is 32x: uncoalesced access wastes 31/32 of the memory bandwidth.

**The SoA vs AoS layout**: the most common coalescing mistake is using **Array of Structures (AoS)** where **Structure of Arrays (SoA)** is needed. AoS: `struct Particle { float x, y, z, w; }; Particle particles[N]` - thread $i$ accesses `particles[i].x`, which is at byte offset `i * 16`. Threads 0-31 access addresses 0, 16, 32, ... 496 - these are strided accesses, 4 cache lines for 32 floats instead of 1. SoA: `float x[N], y[N], z[N], w[N]` - thread $i$ accesses `x[i]`, at byte offset `i * 4`. Threads 0-31 access bytes 0-127 - perfectly coalesced. For workloads processing particles, vertices, or any per-element struct, always prefer SoA. The penalty for AoS is ~4x bandwidth reduction for 4-float structs.

**2D array coalescing and row-major layout**: a 2D matrix stored in row-major order (`M[row][col]`) in C coalesces perfectly when threads process **columns** together: `M[row][threadIdx.x]` for consecutive threadIdx.x values accesses consecutive columns = consecutive memory addresses. Accessing **rows** together: `M[threadIdx.x][col]` accesses addresses separated by `cols * sizeof(float)` bytes = strided. For matrix transpose, this creates a fundamental conflict: reading input coalesced means writing output uncoalesced. The solution is **shared memory tiling**: read a 32×32 tile coalesced into shared memory, **transpose in shared memory**, then write coalesced. This is the canonical shared-memory-as-scratchpad pattern.

**Stride and the myth of 'close enough'**: any stride that is not 1 (in units of element size) causes partial cache-line waste. Stride-2 access uses 50% of each cache line; stride-32 uses 3% (4 bytes from a 128-byte line). The GPU hardware coalescing logic handles consecutive accesses, accesses within a 128-byte segment (partial coalescing), and some strided patterns by merging transactions for elements in the same cache line. But fundamentally: if your warp's 32 thread addresses span more than 4 cache lines, you are paying more than necessary. Always check access patterns with Nsight 'Memory Workload Analysis - L2 Hit Rate' and 'Global Memory Access Pattern'.

**Irregular access patterns**: scatter (write to arbitrary addresses) and gather (read from arbitrary addresses) are the GPU's nemesis. Sparse matrix operations, graph neural networks (neighbourhood aggregation), and hash-based structures involve irregular access by nature. The L2 cache (40MB on A100) can mitigate repeated irregular reads but not writes. Strategies: (1) **sorting** - sort work items by memory access address to increase locality; (2) **remapping** - remap sparse data to a dense format (CSR → blocked CSR for SpMM); (3) **coarser aggregation** - aggregate multiple irregular accesses per thread to amortise L2 miss cost; (4) **texture memory** - the GPU texture cache is optimised for 2D spatial locality (sparse 2D accesses); use `cudaTextureObject_t` for 2D irregular reads.

resources.label: NVIDIA blog - how to access global memory efficiently
CUDA Best Practices Guide - memory coalescing

subtopics.title: Shared memory: bank conflicts and the on-chip scratchpad
body: Shared memory on NVIDIA GPUs is divided into **32 banks** (for 32-bit access). Consecutive 4-byte words are assigned to consecutive banks: word at address $a$ → bank $a \mod 32$. When threads in a warp access shared memory, the hardware can service all 32 accesses in one cycle **if and only if** each bank is accessed by at most one thread (or all threads access the same address in the same bank - a **broadcast**). If multiple threads access different addresses in the same bank, a **bank conflict** occurs: the accesses are serialised. An N-way conflict costs N cycles instead of 1.

**The canonical 32-way bank conflict**: `float smem[32][32]; smem[threadIdx.x][0]` - all 32 threads access column 0, addresses 0, 128, 256, ..., 3968. These are all in bank 0 (0 mod 32 = 0, 128/4 mod 32 = 32 mod 32 = 0, ...). 32-way conflict. **The fix**: pad the array by 1 element per row: `float smem[32][33]`. Now `smem[threadIdx.x][0]` addresses are 0, 132, 264, ..., 4092. Banks: 0, 33%32=1, 264/4%32=66%32=2, ..., 31. All different banks - conflict-free. The padding wastes 32 × 4 = 128 bytes of shared memory but eliminates the 32x serialisation. This 'pad by 1' trick is used in virtually every production tiled GEMM kernel.

**64-bit and 128-bit access**: with 64-bit access (`double`, `int2`), the bank width doubles to 8 bytes; bank conflicts occur when threads access the same 8-byte bank. With 128-bit access (`float4`, `int4`), bank width is 16 bytes. Using `float4` loads (128-bit) halves the number of shared-memory access instructions - one `float4` load moves 16 bytes in one instruction vs four separate `float` loads. For tiled GEMM, loading in `float4` units reduces instruction count by 4× and can improve throughput.

**Using shared memory for inter-thread communication**: beyond tiling, shared memory is the mechanism for any intra-block communication that requires actual data sharing (not just synchronisation). Examples: (1) **stencil computations** - each thread reads a point, neighbours store to shared, then each thread reads from shared to avoid redundant global reads; (2) **histogram** - threads atomically update a shared histogram, then one thread writes to global; (3) **scan** (prefix sum) within a block - Blelloch scan in shared; (4) **sort** within a block - bitonic sort or radix sort in shared. The `__syncthreads()` barrier is mandatory between shared-memory writes and subsequent reads by different threads.

**Shared memory vs L1 configurability**: on Ampere, the 192KB on-chip SRAM per SM is divided between shared memory and L1 cache. The split is configurable per kernel via `cudaFuncSetAttribute(kernel, cudaFuncAttributePreferredSharedMemoryCarveout, N)` where N is the percentage of SRAM to give to shared memory. Maximum shared memory per block: 164KB on A100 (with `__maxnreg__` and carveout directives). For small kernels with large working sets in shared memory (e.g., a block-level FFT on 2048-point sequences), maximising the shared memory carveout reduces L1 capacity but that's acceptable if L1 is not the bottleneck.

resources.label: CUDA Best Practices Guide - shared memory bank conflicts
NVIDIA blog - using shared memory in CUDA C/C++

subtopics.title: Tiling: the algorithmic transformation that makes everything faster
body: **Tiling** (also called **blocking**) is the central optimisation technique for memory-bound GPU kernels. The idea: instead of reading each element from global memory once per use, stage a **tile** (a block of data) into shared memory, use it for multiple operations, then move to the next tile. The global memory traffic is reduced by a factor of the tile's reuse count. For a matmul computing `C[i][j] = sum_k A[i][k] * B[k][j]`, naive computation reads A[i][k] once for each j (N times total) and B[k][j] once for each i (N times total), for N³ global reads total. With BK×BN tiling, each tile of A is reused BN times and each tile of B is reused BM times, reducing global reads by a factor of BN and BM respectively.

**Tiling derivation for matmul**: tile size BM×BK for A, BK×BN for B. Load tile to shared: BM×BK reads for A + BK×BN reads for B = BK(BM+BN) reads. Compute from shared: BM×BN×2BK FLOPs (BK iterations × 2 per multiply-add). Repeat for K/BK tiles. Total global reads: (K/BK) × BK(BM+BN) = K(BM+BN) per output tile of BM×BN. Per output element: K(BM+BN)/(BM×BN) = K/BN + K/BM bytes. With BM=BN=64 and K=4096: (4096/64 + 4096/64) × 4 bytes = 512 bytes per output element vs K×8 = 32,768 bytes without tiling. 64× reduction in global memory traffic.

**Double buffering**: the warp stalls during the shared memory load (`__syncthreads` + global load latency ~200 cycles). **Double buffering** overlaps the global memory load of the next tile with the computation on the current tile. Allocate 2× the shared memory: while computing on tile $k$ in buffer A, asynchronously load tile $k+1$ into buffer B (using `cp.async` on Ampere, which issues a non-blocking DMA directly from global to shared memory without going through registers). After computation completes, swap buffer pointers. On Ampere with `cp.async`, this can hide nearly all global-to-shared latency, achieving close to peak arithmetic throughput.

**Register tiling (thread-level tiling)**: after tiling at the block level (shared memory), tile again at the **thread level** using registers. Each thread computes a TM×TN submatrix of the output rather than a single element. A thread holds TM elements from A and TN elements from B in registers across the inner loop, issuing TM×TN FMAs per inner loop iteration. This dramatically increases register-level reuse. For GEMM: with TM=TN=8, each thread computes 64 outputs, performing 64 FMAs per loop instead of 1 - the FP32 pipeline is nearly fully pipelined. The roofline shifts: more FLOPs per shared-memory access → higher achieved arithmetic intensity → compute-bound rather than memory-bound.

**When not to tile**: tiling is only beneficial when the tile fits in shared memory and will be reused. Elementwise kernels (ReLU, dropout, bias-add) have zero data reuse - tiling provides no benefit and only adds complexity. Pointwise operations should be **fused** instead: a single kernel reads each element once, applies all elementwise operations (LayerNorm + GeLU + dropout), and writes once. This is what PyTorch's inductor and operator fusion accomplish. The decision: use tiling when a data element is used for multiple output computations (matmul, convolution, attention); use fusion when each element is used once but multiple operations are applied.

resources.label: Simon Boehm - CUDA matmul step-by-step (tiling walkthrough)
PMPP Ch 5-6 - tiling and performance analysis
Lei Mao - CUDA matrix multiplication optimization

subtopics.title: Why GEMM is the heart of deep learning and its memory bottleneck
body: **GEMM (General Matrix-Matrix Multiplication)** `C = αAB + βC` is the dominant operation in all of deep learning. Linear layers: `Y = XW^T` where X is (batch × seqlen × d_model) and W is (d_model × d_out) - this is a batched GEMM. Attention projections: Q, K, V projections are GEMMs. The attention score computation `QK^T` is a batched GEMM. Feed-forward layers: two GEMMs. Convolution is implemented as GEMM via the **im2col transform** (reshape the input into a Toeplitz matrix, then GEMM). For a standard transformer with d=4096 and 96 heads, over 80% of FLOP/s are in GEMM operations. This is why cuBLAS and Tensor Cores are the performance-critical path.

**The naive matmul is memory-bandwidth-bound**: a naive GPU matmul kernel with one thread per output element: `C[row][col] = sum_k A[row][k] * B[k][col]`. For an N×N matmul with N=4096: FLOPs = 2N³ = 137 billion; memory reads = N² (A) + N² (B) = 2N² = 33.5 million floats = 134 MB; arithmetic intensity = 2N³ / (2N² × 4 bytes) = N/4 = 1024 FLOP/byte. So GEMM is actually compute-bound on A100 (I* ≈ 9.75 FLOP/byte) - but only for large N. For small N (batch=1, N=512): intensity = 128 FLOP/byte - still compute-bound but using only 12% of tensor core FLOPs due to under-utilization. For N=64: intensity = 16 FLOP/byte, barely compute-bound. For inference with batch=1, you are almost always memory-bound.

**The naive kernel's actual failure**: the naive kernel `C[row][col] += A[row][k] * B[k][col]` issues K global memory reads of A[row][k] (one per output element column in the same row block) and K global memory reads of B[k][col] (one per output element row). Without caching, this is 2KN² global reads for the full N×N output = 2KN² × 4 bytes = 8N³ bytes of traffic (for K=N, square matmul). Compare to the optimal: 2N² × 4 bytes = 8N² bytes (just reading A and B once). The ratio is N - for N=4096, the naive kernel reads A and B 4096 times more than necessary. This is why tiling is not optional.

**cuBLAS performance**: NVIDIA's cuBLAS achieves 85-92% of theoretical tensor core peak for large square matrices (N ≥ 4096, batch ≥ 4). For smaller dimensions, efficiency drops: for N=512, batch=1, cuBLAS achieves ~30-40% of peak. This is why batching is important: inference with batch=32 is often 10x more efficient (in throughput) than 32 sequential batch=1 inferences, even though the FLOPs are identical. `cublasGemmEx` is the key API: it accepts mixed-precision (input FP16/BF16, accumulate FP32, output FP16) and selects the optimal algorithm from NVIDIA's internal heuristics.

**cuBLAS vs custom kernels**: writing a custom GEMM kernel that beats cuBLAS for standard shapes is very hard and rarely worth it. cuBLAS includes thousands of hand-optimised kernels for specific dimensions, and auto-selects via benchmarking at init time with `cublasSetMathMode(CUBLAS_TENSOR_OP_MATH)`. Custom GEMM kernels are justified when: (1) **non-standard accumulation** - e.g., int8 output; (2) **fused epilogue** - multiply C by a scale factor and ReLU in the same pass (CUTLASS epilogue fusions); (3) **sparse structure** - block sparsity that cuBLAS doesn't handle; (4) **exotic data types** - FP8, int4. CUTLASS is the right abstraction for all of these.

resources.label: Simon Boehm - How to Optimize a CUDA Matmul Kernel

subtopics.title: Hierarchical tiling: block, warp, and thread-level GEMM
body: Production GEMM kernels use **three levels of tiling**: (1) **thread block tile** (data staged into shared memory); (2) **warp tile** (each warp computes a sub-tile of the block's output using warp-level matrix operations); (3) **thread tile** (each thread accumulates a register-level micro-tile). The canonical A100 parameters: block tile BM×BN×BK = 128×128×32; warp tile WM×WN×WK = 64×64×32; thread tile TM×TN = 8×8. With 256 threads/block and 8 warps/block, each warp handles a 64×64 sub-tile; each thread handles 8×8 = 64 output elements. Total block output: 128×128 = 16,384 elements computed per block per BK-step.

**The GEMM pipeline for one block**: (1) Advance the K loop by BK; (2) load BM×BK tile of A and BK×BN tile of B from global → shared memory (double buffered, using cp.async on Ampere); (3) for each warp, load WM×WK subtile of A and WK×WN subtile of B from shared → registers; (4) compute WM×WN outer product: `for k in range(WK): C[i][j] += A_reg[i][k] * B_reg[k][j]` using FFMA (fused multiply-add) instructions; (5) repeat; (6) write C tile to global. The register file usage: TM×TN = 64 output accumulators + TM A elements + TN B elements = 64 + 8 + 8 = 80 FP32 registers per thread. Total per block (256 threads): 20,480 registers - 31% of the 65,536-register file. Occupancy: 3 blocks/SM = 6144 threads, 96% of the 2048-thread max warp capacity.

**Vectorized loads**: instead of loading one float at a time from shared memory (`float a = smem[row][k]`), load 4 floats at once (`float4 a = *(float4*)&smem[row][k]`). This uses a 128-bit load instruction (LDS.128 in PTX) which moves 4 floats in one instruction. For the inner register loop (WM×WN outer product), each thread loads 8-element vectors of A and B using `float4` loads, then executes 64 FFMA instructions. The FFMA-to-LDS ratio becomes 64:4 = 16:1, well above the ~8:1 needed to keep the FP32 units saturated. Vectorised loads also reduce instruction count, lowering instruction issue overhead.

**Prefetching and pipeline latency hiding**: the L1 load latency for shared memory is ~23 cycles; the global load latency is ~200 cycles. Without prefetching, the inner K loop stalls for shared memory loads. The solution on Ampere: use **`cp.async`** to issue asynchronous global→shared copies that don't stall the warp. `cp.async.cg.global.L2::128B.f32` copies directly from L2/HBM to shared memory via the copy engine, while the warp continues executing. `cp.async.commit_group()` + `cp.async.wait_group(N)` controls the number of outstanding async copies (double buffer: wait for group N-1 before issuing group N+1). This overlaps computation on tile K with the load of tile K+1, hiding the ~200-cycle HBM latency.

**Register blocking and outer products**: the key insight for thread-level tiling is that the outer product `A_col × B_row^T` (dimension TM×1 × 1×TN = TM×TN) can be computed with TM×TN independent FFMA instructions, all with no data dependencies. This is the most instruction-level-parallelism (ILP) that can be extracted. On an SM with 4 FP32 FFMA execution units per warp (Ampere), and TM×TN = 64 FMAs, each loading step of 2 vectors (A: 8 floats, B: 8 floats) immediately feeds 64 FMAs without stalls. The L1→Register latency of 4-6 cycles is hidden by the ILP of the 64 FMAs. This is why the thread tile must be large enough to provide ILP that matches the load latency.

resources.label: NVIDIA CUTLASS - source code and documentation

subtopics.title: Tensor Cores: WMMA, MMA, and mixed-precision accumulation
body: **Tensor Cores** are dedicated hardware units on each SM that perform matrix multiply-accumulate at the warp level. A single Tensor Core instruction operates on a 16×16×16 matrix fragment: `D = A × B + C` where A is 16×16 FP16, B is 16×16 FP16, C and D are 16×16 FP32. This executes in 1 warp clock cycle (on Volta) or less on Ampere. The throughput: 64 × (16×16×16 × 2) = 64 × 8192 = 524,288 FP16 FMAs per SM per cycle × 1.41 GHz × 108 SMs ≈ 312 TFLOP/s on A100 - vs 19.5 TFLOP/s for regular FP32 cores. Tensor cores deliver 16× more raw throughput for matrix operations.

**WMMA API**: `nvcuda::wmma` is the PTX-level API. `wmma::fragment<matrix_a, 16, 16, 16, half, wmma::row_major> a_frag` declares a 16×16 matrix fragment. `wmma::load_matrix_sync(a_frag, A_ptr, ldA)` loads from global/shared memory into the fragment. `wmma::mma_sync(c_frag, a_frag, b_frag, c_frag)` performs D=A×B+C. `wmma::store_matrix_sync(C_ptr, c_frag, ldC, wmma::mem_row_major)` writes back. Key constraint: each thread in the warp holds a piece of each fragment - the layout is opaque and hardware-defined. You cannot access individual fragment elements by thread index. For correctness, all 32 threads in the warp must call `mma_sync` with the same fragment objects.

**PTX MMA instructions**: below WMMA, PTX exposes `mma.sync.aligned.m16n8k16.row.col.f32.f16.f16.f32` which operates on a 16×8×16 matrix fragment. This is the level at which CUTLASS operates. The fragment layout for a 16×8×8 accumulator in registers: each of the 32 threads holds 4 accumulator registers (`c0, c1, c2, c3`). The mapping of (row, col) to thread + register index is specified in the PTX manual. Writing kernels at the MMA level gives maximum control over instruction scheduling and register usage but requires understanding the exact fragment layout.

**BF16, INT8, FP8 Tensor Core modes**: A100 adds **BF16** (Brain Float, 8-bit exponent, 7-bit mantissa vs FP16's 5-bit exponent 10-bit mantissa): better numerical range than FP16, same 16-bit storage, same Tensor Core throughput. BF16 is the default training precision for large models (GPT-3 was trained in FP16; LLaMA and later in BF16 to avoid loss spikes from FP16 overflow). **INT8** Tensor Cores (introduced Turing, enhanced A100): 4× the throughput of FP16 (624 TOPS on A100), with FP32 accumulation. Used for quantised inference. **FP8** (Hopper H100): 2 types - E4M3 (4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa) for activations; E5M2 (5 exponent bits, 2 mantissa) for gradients. H100 achieves 3.9 PFLOP/s FP8 sparse. FP8 training requires **loss scaling** (same as FP16) plus a **microscaling** per-tensor scale factor.

**Mixed-precision training gotchas**: storing weights in FP16/BF16 and accumulating in FP32 ('master weights') is the standard. PyTorch's `torch.cuda.amp.autocast()` automatically casts supported ops to FP16/BF16. The common failure mode: **FP16 underflow** - small gradient magnitudes (< 6×10^-5) become 0 in FP16. **Loss scaling** (multiply loss by scale factor S before backprop, divide gradients by S after) shifts the gradient magnitude into FP16 range. `GradScaler` handles this automatically: starts with S=65536, halves when NaN gradients detected, doubles when no NaN for 2000 steps. BF16 has larger exponent range so doesn't need loss scaling - a practical reason large models prefer BF16.

subtopics.title: CUTLASS: the production GEMM abstraction
body: **CUTLASS (CUDA Templates for Linear Algebra Subroutines and Solvers)** is NVIDIA's open-source C++ template library for high-performance GEMM. Where cuBLAS is a closed-source black box, CUTLASS exposes the entire hierarchical tiling strategy via C++ templates: `cutlass::gemm::device::Gemm<ElementA, LayoutA, ElementB, LayoutB, ElementC, LayoutC, ElementAccumulator, OperatorClass, ArchTag, ShapeMMA, ShapeWarp, ShapeInstruction, EpilogueOp, ThreadblockSwizzle, Stages>` instantiates a GEMM kernel tuned for the exact precision, layout, tile sizes, and hardware target. This is the library used internally by NVIDIA for cuDNN convolutions and cuBLAS on Ampere/Hopper.

**CUTLASS 3.x and CuTe**: CUTLASS 3.0 introduced **CuTe** (CUDA Tensor), a new abstraction for layouts and tensor operations. A CuTe Layout is a compile-time description of how a logical multidimensional tensor maps to physical memory: `Layout<Shape<_2, _3>, Stride<_3, _1>>` is a 2×3 row-major matrix (strides: row stride=3, col stride=1). CuTe tiles can be composed, transposed, and partitioned with zero runtime overhead. This makes it possible to express complex hierarchical tiling schemes (global → shared → register) as composable layout transformations, and have the compiler generate optimal code automatically. CUTLASS 3.x kernels on H100 match or exceed vendor-optimised cuBLAS.

**Epilogue fusions in CUTLASS**: the **epilogue** is the post-matmul computation applied to the output tile before writing to global memory. CUTLASS's epilogue abstraction allows arbitrary element-wise operations fused into the GEMM: `LinearCombinationRelu`, `LinearCombinationBias`, `LinearCombinationClamp`. A fused GEMM+bias+ReLU reads A, B, bias once and writes C once; a separate kernel GEMM followed by a bias+ReLU kernel reads C twice (once to add bias, once to write ReLU output) - 2× the memory traffic. CUTLASS epilogues enable **free fusion** of anything that's computed element-wise on the output. PyTorch's `_C._nn.linear` uses fused GEMM+bias+optional-activation via CUTLASS.

**Profiling GEMM performance**: the correct benchmarking approach for GEMM: (1) warm up with 10 runs (auto-tuning happens in cuBLAS on first call); (2) synchronize; (3) time 100 runs with CUDA events; (4) compute TFLOP/s as `2 * M * N * K / avg_time_seconds`. Report effective bandwidth separately: `(M*K + K*N + M*N) * sizeof(dtype) / time`. Compare achieved TFLOP/s to theoretical peak. The gap is dominated by epilogue overhead, pipeline drain, and memory system inefficiency. For square FP16 N=4096 matmul, expect 80-90% of tensor core peak with cuBLAS on A100.

subtopics.title: The Triton abstraction: block-level programming model
body: **Triton** is a Python-embedded DSL for writing GPU kernels at the **block** (tile) level rather than the thread level. The fundamental difference from CUDA: in CUDA, you write code for a single thread and use `threadIdx` to coordinate with other threads. In Triton, you write code for a **program instance** (analogous to a thread block) operating on a **block** of data, and the block operations are vectorised automatically. `tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)` generates a vector of indices `[0, 1, ..., BLOCK_SIZE-1]` that the program instance operates on as a unit. All `tl.load`, `tl.store`, and arithmetic operations are implicitly vectorised over this block.

**The Triton programming model**: a Triton kernel is decorated with `@triton.jit` and receives **pointer arguments** (to tensors) plus **meta-parameters** (compile-time constants like BLOCK_SIZE). Each kernel call corresponds to one 'program instance', launched with a **grid** specified by `triton.cdiv(N, BLOCK_SIZE)` (number of blocks). Inside the kernel, `pid = tl.program_id(axis=0)` gives the block index. Memory accesses: `offsets = pid * BLOCK_SIZE + tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE); mask = offsets < N; x = tl.load(ptr + offsets, mask=mask)`. The `mask` handles boundary conditions without divergence - masked-off elements are ignored. This is the core pattern for all Triton kernels.

**Block-level operations**: Triton provides high-level block operations that compile to optimal CUDA: `tl.dot(a, b)` (block matmul, lowers to Tensor Core `mma` instructions), `tl.sum(x, axis=0)` (warp/block reduction), `tl.max(x, axis=0)`, `tl.exp(x)` (vectorized elementwise), `tl.load`/`tl.store` with mask. The key insight: `tl.dot(a, b)` where a is `[BLOCK_M, BLOCK_K]` and b is `[BLOCK_K, BLOCK_N]` automatically uses the best available hardware (Tensor Cores on Ampere/Hopper), stages data into shared memory, applies double buffering, and handles all tile edge cases. The programmer never explicitly manages shared memory or thread indices.

**Meta-parameters and autotuning**: `triton.Config({'BLOCK_SIZE': 1024, 'num_warps': 4, 'num_stages': 2})` specifies a kernel configuration. `@triton.autotune(configs=[...], key=['N'])` wraps a kernel and benchmarks all configs for each unique `(N,)` key, caching the best result. This replaces CUDA's manual tuning of block sizes and shared memory allocations. Triton's autotuner calls each config 100 times, warms up, and reports the fastest. For production use, pre-cache the autotuner results (save to file) to avoid runtime benchmarking overhead on first call.

**Shared memory and register management**: Triton hides explicit shared memory management. When Triton compiles `tl.load(ptr + offsets)` inside a loop, it determines automatically whether to stage data in shared memory and how to apply double buffering. The `num_stages` meta-parameter controls the pipeline depth (1 = no pipelining, 2 = double buffer, 3+ = deeper pipeline). Setting `num_stages=4` on Hopper with TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator) enables hardware-driven async data movement with 4-stage prefetch. The programmer expresses the algorithm; Triton maps it to the optimal hardware access pattern.

resources.label: GPU MODE - Triton lectures and examples

subtopics.title: Triton tutorials: vector add, fused softmax, and matmul
body: **Vector addition kernel** is the 'hello world'. `@triton.jit def add_kernel(x_ptr, y_ptr, out_ptr, n, BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr): pid = tl.program_id(0); offs = pid * BLOCK_SIZE + tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE); mask = offs < n; x = tl.load(x_ptr + offs, mask=mask); y = tl.load(y_ptr + offs, mask=mask); tl.store(out_ptr + offs, x + y, mask=mask)`. Launch: `add_kernel[(triton.cdiv(n, 1024),)](x_ptr, y_ptr, out, n, BLOCK_SIZE=1024)`. This already matches PyTorch's vectorAdd speed because Triton emits coalesced 128-byte loads and the kernel is bandwidth-bound (no shared memory needed for elementwise ops).

**Fused softmax** (Tutorial 2) is the key example of why fusion matters. A naive 3-kernel softmax: (1) find row max; (2) subtract max and exponentiate; (3) divide by row sum. Each kernel reads+writes the full matrix - 3 global memory round-trips. Fused Triton softmax: one program per row; loads the row in blocks; computes running max (online softmax), running sum, and final normalisation in a single pass. The intermediate max and sum values stay in registers - no HBM traffic. For a (32768, 1024) matrix, the fused kernel is ~3× faster than 3 separate PyTorch ops. The technique generalises to any sequence of row-wise reductions: LayerNorm, Softmax, any normalisation.

**Triton matmul** (Tutorial 3) achieves near-cuBLAS performance for large matrices. The kernel: outer loop over K tiles; inner operations: `tl.load` A-tile and B-tile; `tl.dot(a, b, accumulate=acc)` to accumulate into a register accumulator; after K loop, `tl.store` the output tile with optional epilogue (`acc = tl.sqrt(acc)`, etc.). The `tl.dot` call automatically selects the optimal Tensor Core instruction based on the block shape and data type. With `BLOCK_M=128, BLOCK_N=128, BLOCK_K=32, num_stages=3`, this achieves 85-90% of cuBLAS on A100 for N=4096 in ~70 lines of Python, vs CUTLASS's ~1000+ lines of C++.

**Fused attention** (Tutorial 6 - FlashAttention) is the showcase. The naive attention `softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d)) V` requires materialising the N×N score matrix (O(N²) memory for sequence length N). The Triton fused attention: iterates over K-blocks of K and V; for each block, computes the Q×K^T sub-matrix, applies online softmax to update running max and sum, accumulates into the output. The entire attention computation happens in registers and shared memory - the N×N matrix never materialises in HBM. The Triton implementation (available at `triton-lang.org/tutorials/06-fused-attention.html`) achieves ~90% of the hand-tuned FlashAttention-2 CUDA kernel in ~150 lines of Python.

**Common pitfalls**: (1) `BLOCK_SIZE` must be a **power of 2** and known at compile time (`tl.constexpr`) - Triton cannot vectorise over dynamic block sizes; (2) `tl.dot` requires block sizes that are multiples of 16 (Tensor Core constraint); (3) masking: `tl.load(ptr + offs, mask=mask, other=0.0)` - the `other` value fills masked positions, affecting reductions; use `-inf` for max-reductions (softmax), `0.0` for sum-reductions; (4) atomic operations: `tl.atomic_add(ptr + offset, val)` for scatter updates - slower than shared memory reductions for high-contention cases; (5) L2 cache hints: `tl.load(ptr, cache_modifier='.ca')` for streaming data (don't pollute L2), `.cg` for re-used data (set up for L2 residence).

subtopics.title: The Triton compiler pipeline: Python to PTX
body: Triton's compilation pipeline: Python `@triton.jit` → **Triton IR** (an MLIR dialect) → **TritonGPU IR** (GPU-specific dialect) → **LLVM IR** → **PTX** (NVIDIA's parallel thread execution ISA) → **CUBIN** (GPU binary). The Python source is parsed by Triton's JIT compiler on the first call; subsequent calls with the same argument types and meta-parameter values reuse the cached binary. The entire pipeline takes ~100-500 ms on first call (JIT warmup) but near-zero on subsequent calls. Pre-compilation with `triton.compile()` can move this cost to deployment time.

**Triton IR**: the first intermediate representation captures the block-level operations without hardware specifics. `tt.load`, `tt.store`, `tt.dot`, `tt.reduce` are the core operations. The Triton frontend converts Python AST nodes to these operations, handling Python control flow (loops, conditionals) by unrolling or converting to predicated operations. `tl.for_range` loops that iterate a compile-time-constant number of times are unrolled; dynamic-count loops become actual IR loops.

**TritonGPU IR lowering**: the critical compilation stage. The TritonGPU pass converts abstract block operations to hardware-specific layouts: `tt.dot` → `triton_gpu.dot_op` with a specific layout encoding (the WMMA or MMA fragment layout); `tt.load` → `triton_gpu.async_copy` with staging in `triton_gpu.local_alloc` (shared memory allocations); reductions → warp-shuffle sequences. The **shared memory allocation** pass determines which block tensors need staging in shared memory vs can stay in registers, and computes the layout (including bank-conflict-free padding). The **pipeline** pass inserts async copy instructions and commit/wait groups for double/multi-stage buffering.

**LLVM and PTX**: TritonGPU IR lowers to **LLVM IR** targeting the nvptx64 backend. LLVM handles instruction selection (Triton IR operations → PTX instructions), register allocation, and instruction scheduling. The PTX output contains `cp.async`, `mma.sync`, `ld.shared`, `st.shared`, `shfl.sync` instructions. NVIDIA's `ptxas` assembler then converts PTX to a CUBIN binary targeting the specific SM version (sm_80 for A100, sm_90 for H100). `ptxas` performs a final round of instruction scheduling and register allocation tuning for the specific hardware.

**Debugging and inspection**: `print(triton.compile(kernel, ...).asm['ttir'])` shows Triton IR; `...asm['ttgpuir']` shows TritonGPU IR; `...asm['llir']` shows LLVM IR; `...asm['ptx']` shows PTX. Reading the PTX output is the definitive way to verify that (a) `cp.async` instructions are being generated (double buffering active); (b) `mma.sync` instructions are present (Tensor Cores used); (c) shared memory size is as expected; (d) register count per thread (controls occupancy). `cuobjdump --dump-ptx mykernel.cubin` works on compiled binaries. The PTX instruction count and memory access pattern directly predict kernel performance.

subtopics.title: When to use Triton vs CUDA vs torch.compile
body: **The tool selection decision tree**: (1) If the operation can be expressed as an existing PyTorch op or composition: use PyTorch. `torch.compile` with `mode='max-autotune'` will fuse and optimise the graph. (2) If the fusion or algorithm cannot be expressed in existing PyTorch ops (e.g., a novel attention variant, a custom normalisation, a specialised sparse op): use **Triton** for medium-complexity kernels. (3) If you need maximum performance beyond what Triton achieves, or need features Triton can't express (fine-grained warp sync, specific instruction scheduling, custom PTX): use **CUDA**. (4) If targeting production inference on NVIDIA hardware with standard ops: use **TensorRT** or **cuBLAS**.

**Triton's performance ceiling vs CUDA**: for GEMM, Triton achieves 85-90% of cuBLAS for large shapes. For fused attention, Triton-based FlashAttention-2 achieves ~90% of the hand-written CUDA kernel (the 10% gap is from CUTLASS-level instruction scheduling and pipelining tricks). For elementwise-dominated kernels (softmax, LayerNorm), Triton matches or exceeds naive CUDA because the kernel is bandwidth-bound and Triton's coalesced loads are as good as anything achievable in CUDA. For kernels that require warp-level intrinsics (`__shfl_sync`, warp voting), `cp.async` with fine-grained control, or non-standard memory ordering: CUDA is necessary.

**torch.compile integration**: PyTorch's `torch.compile(model, mode='max-autotune')` uses **TorchInductor** as its backend, which generates Triton code for GPU ops. For a transformer layer, inductor will: fuse the LayerNorm + attention projection GEMM + residual add into one Triton kernel; use Tensor Core GEMM for the attention projection; use online-softmax fusion for attention; fuse the FFN GEMM + GeLU + dropout. The result: ~2-3× faster than eager mode for typical transformers. Writing custom Triton ops and registering them with `torch.library.custom_op` makes them first-class citizens that `torch.compile` can inline and fuse with surrounding ops.

**Custom Triton ops in PyTorch**: to integrate a Triton kernel into a PyTorch model: (1) write the kernel; (2) wrap in a `torch.autograd.Function` with `forward` calling the Triton kernel and `backward` calling the gradient Triton kernel; (3) expose as a Python function. For Flash Attention: the Triton fwd kernel runs attention; the bwd kernel implements the recompute-based backward (recompute attention scores from saved Q, K, V rather than storing the N×N matrix). The `@torch.library.custom_op` decorator makes the op properly traceable and compilable by `torch.compile`. See `xformers` for production-grade examples of this pattern.

subtopics.title: The roofline model and classifying every op
body: **The roofline model** (Williams et al., 2009) gives a simple bound on achievable performance. For a kernel with arithmetic intensity $I = F/B$ (FLOP per byte of memory traffic): $\text{performance} \leq \min(P_{\text{peak}}, I \times BW_{\text{peak}})$. The curve forms a 'roofline': a sloped left portion (memory-bandwidth-bound) and a flat right portion (compute-bound). The transition point (the 'ridge point') is $I^* = P_{\text{peak}} / BW_{\text{peak}}$. For A100 SXM4: $I^* = 312 \text{ TFLOP/s (FP16 TC)} / 2 \text{ TB/s} = 156 \text{ FLOP/byte}$ (tensor core ridge); for FP32 scalar ops: $I^* = 19.5 / 2 = 9.75 \text{ FLOP/byte}$. Most operations in a transformer are below even the FP32 ridge point.

**Classifying transformer operations**: LayerNorm: 5 FLOPs/element × d_model reads + d_model writes = $5 / (2 \times 4) = 0.625$ FLOP/byte - deeply memory-bound. GeLU: ~10 FLOPs/element per read+write: ~1.25 FLOP/byte - memory-bound. Attention softmax: ~4 FLOPs/element: ~0.5 FLOP/byte. Residual add: 1 FLOP/element, 3 memory ops = 0.083 FLOP/byte. Linear layer (d=4096, N=4096): $2N^3 / (2N^2 \times 4B) = N/4 = 1024$ FLOP/byte - compute-bound for large N. The split is clear: GEMMs are compute-bound; everything else is memory-bound. The implication: every non-GEMM operation is a candidate for fusion.

**Horace He's framework**: the paper 'Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr From First Principles' categorizes operations into three types: (1) **compute-bound** (GEMM, convolution) - can't go faster without more FLOPs; fusion doesn't help; (2) **memory-bandwidth-bound** (elementwise, normalization, activation functions) - bottleneck is memory reads/writes; fusion eliminates intermediate reads/writes; (3) **overhead-bound** (very small ops, Python dispatch, CUDA kernel launch overhead) - dominated by fixed costs unrelated to data size. The key insight: for memory-bound ops, the latency is proportional to the number of memory round-trips, not the number of FLOPs. Fusing 10 memory-bound ops into 1 reduces memory traffic by 10× even though FLOPs are unchanged.

**Operator fusion mechanics**: two ops can be fused if: (1) the output of op1 is the input of op2; (2) op2 starts immediately after op1 with no consumer of op1's output other than op2 (otherwise the intermediate must be materialised). For elementwise ops, fusion is trivial: `y = relu(layernorm(x + bias))` computes all three operations per element before writing to HBM. For reductions followed by elementwise ops (LayerNorm: compute mean+variance, then normalise), a two-pass fused kernel: pass 1 computes stats (warp reduce); pass 2 applies normalisation. Online algorithms (online softmax, Welford's online algorithm for running mean/variance) allow single-pass fusion where possible.

**Inplace operations and memory pressure**: fused kernels ideally read each tensor once and write each output tensor once. But gradient computation requires saving intermediate activations for the backward pass. The standard trade-off: **activation checkpointing** (recompute activations instead of storing) vs **activation storage** (store all intermediates, higher memory). FlashAttention uses recomputation in the backward: instead of storing the N×N attention matrix, it recomputes it from the saved Q, K, V. This trades FLOPs (a 2× FLOP overhead for the backward) for memory bandwidth (O(N²) memory eliminated). For memory-bound backpasses, this trade is almost always beneficial.

resources.label: Horace He - Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr From First Principles
Williams et al. - Roofline: An Insightful Visual Performance Model (2009)

subtopics.title: FlashAttention: algorithm, online softmax, and tiling derivation
body: **Standard attention memory complexity**: softmax attention `Attn = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d)) V` for sequence length N requires materialising the N×N score matrix S = QK^T. For N=8192 and batch=1, head=1: S is 8192×8192 × 4 bytes = 256MB per head. With 96 heads and batch=32, total score matrix storage: 768GB. Clearly infeasible. Even for N=2048, the N×N matrix is 16MB per head - significant. The score matrix must be read for the softmax (read 1), read again for the V multiplication (read 2), and its gradient must be stored for backward (read/write 3). This is 3 expensive HBM operations on a matrix that grows quadratically in N.

**Online softmax** (Milakov & Gimelshein, 2018) is the algorithmic breakthrough. Standard softmax: $\text{softmax}(x)_i = e^{x_i} / \sum_j e^{x_j}$. Requires 2 passes: pass 1 finds the max (for numerical stability); pass 2 computes exponentials and sum. Online softmax maintains a running max $m$ and running sum $\ell$ that can be updated incrementally: for a new block of scores $x_{new}$, $m_{new} = \max(m_{old}, \max(x_{new}))$; $\ell_{new} = \ell_{old} \cdot e^{m_{old} - m_{new}} + \sum e^{x_{new} - m_{new}}$. The accumulated output is corrected by the same exponential factor. This enables a **single-pass algorithm** over the N elements - you never need to store all N scores simultaneously.

**FlashAttention tile algorithm** (Dao et al., 2022): process K and V in blocks of size $B_c$ (column block); process Q in blocks of size $B_r$ (row block). For each Q block: for each K/V block: (1) load Q tile from HBM → SRAM; (2) load K tile from HBM → SRAM; (3) compute score tile $S_{ij} = Q_i K_j^T \in \mathbb{R}^{B_r \times B_c}$; (4) update online softmax running max $m$ and normalizer $\ell$ using the online algorithm; (5) compute rescaled V contribution: load V tile; $O_i \mathrel{+}= \text{diag}(e^{m_{old}-m_{new}}) O_i + e^{S_{ij} - m_{new}} V_j$; (6) after all K/V blocks, normalise: $O_i = O_i / \ell$. HBM accesses: $O(N d / B_r)$ reads for Q; $O(N d / B_c)$ reads for K/V; $O(Nd)$ writes for O. Total: $O(N^2 d / B_c)$ for full N - but the key is the N×N matrix never materialises: SRAM holds only $B_r \times B_c$ scores at a time.

**SRAM constraints and tile sizing**: the tile must fit in SRAM. An A100 has 192KB L1/shared per SM. Q tile: $B_r \times d$; K tile: $B_c \times d$; V tile: $B_c \times d$; O tile: $B_r \times d$. Total: $(B_r + B_c)(d + d) \times 2B \leq 192KB$. For d=64 (standard head dim), BF16: $(B_r + B_c) \times 256B \leq 192KB$, so $B_r + B_c \leq 768$. With $B_r = B_c = 64$: $(64 + 64) \times 256 = 32768B = 32KB$ - fits easily. For d=128: $(B_r + B_c) \times 512B \leq 192KB$, so $B_r + B_c \leq 384$. With $B_r = B_c = 128$: $128 \times 1024B = 131KB$ - tight but feasible. The constraint determines the effective tile sizes for different models.

**FlashAttention-2 improvements** (Dao, 2023): (1) **Reduced non-matmul FLOPs**: FA1 has 6 FLOPs per element of the score matrix (exp + max + sum + scale + normalise + correction); FA2 reduces to 4 by reordering operations. For Tensor Core utilisation, non-matmul FLOPs are a bottleneck (tensor core throughput >> CUDA core throughput): FA2 improves Tensor Core utilisation from 35% to 73% on A100. (2) **Parallelism over Q sequence length**: FA1 parallelises over batch + heads; FA2 also parallelises over the Q sequence dimension, enabling more SM utilisation for large N and small batch. (3) **Work partitioning**: better split of work between warps within a thread block. FA2 achieves ~2× the throughput of FA1 on A100, reaching 73% of the theoretical FLO/byte bound.

resources.label: FlashAttention official repo and CUDA/Triton implementations

subtopics.title: Practical fusion patterns and torch.compile fusion
body: **The LayerNorm + linear layer fusion** is the most common production fusion. Naive: LayerNorm writes a normalised tensor to HBM; linear kernel reads it. Fused: LayerNorm stats (mean, variance) are computed in registers, normalisation applied, and the result directly fed into the GEMM epilogue. PyTorch's `apex.normalization.FusedRMSNorm` and `flash_attn.ops.rms_norm` implement this. The GEMM can even fuse the LayerNorm into its prologue: compute LayerNorm on the A matrix tiles as they're loaded into shared memory, saving a full HBM read of the normalised A matrix. Apex's `fused_layer_norm_cuda` achieves 3-5× speedup over PyTorch's unfused implementation.

**Residual stream fusions**: `out = x + dropout(linear(act_fn(linear(x))))` - the feed-forward block. Fused: one kernel reads x once, applies LayerNorm (using Welford online algorithm for mean/variance), writes to shared memory, then the GEMM reads from shared memory (skipping HBM write of the normalised x), applies GeLU activation (in CUTLASS epilogue), writes to HBM; second kernel reads, computes second GEMM, applies dropout, adds residual, writes to HBM. Two kernel calls vs six. Each eliminated kernel saves: one HBM read + one HBM write = 2 × (tensor_size_bytes) / 2TB/s latency.

**torch.compile fusion analysis**: `torch.compile(model, mode='reduce-overhead')` applies basic fusion (mostly overhead reduction via graph capture). `mode='max-autotune'` enables Inductor's full fusion passes: (1) **Pointwise fusion**: adjacent elementwise ops are fused into a single Triton kernel automatically; (2) **Reduction fusion**: a reduction (sum/max) followed by elementwise ops fuses into one kernel; (3) **Horizontal fusion**: two independent ops with the same input can be fused into one pass (e.g., two linear layers sharing input); (4) **Template matmul**: GEMM epilogue fuses with the following elementwise ops. To inspect what inductor actually generates: `torch._inductor.config.trace.enabled = True; torch.compile(f)(x)` writes Triton kernels to a trace directory.

**Detecting and fixing fusion failures**: `torch._dynamo.explain(model)(x)` shows the computation graph and graph breaks (points where eager fallback occurs). Common graph breaks: Python `if` on tensor values (`if x.sum() > 0`), in-place ops on a tensor aliased elsewhere, custom C++ extensions not registered with `torch.library`, `.numpy()` calls, `print(tensor)`. Each graph break creates a new Triton kernel and re-enters Python, adding overhead. Fix by: (a) avoid Python control flow that depends on tensor values; (b) use `torch.compiler.disable()` to explicitly opt out specific functions; (c) rewrite custom ops using `torch.library.custom_op`. Profile total kernel time with `torch.profiler.profile(activities=[ProfilerActivity.CUDA])` to find the dominant kernels.

resources.label: PyTorch Inductor - backend overview and fusion docs
Apex - NVIDIA fused ops (LayerNorm, Adam, etc.)

subtopics.title: The KV cache: memory arithmetic for autoregressive generation
body: Autoregressive LLM generation is fundamentally different from training. At each step, the model generates one new token by computing attention between the new token's Q and all previous tokens' K and V. Naively, this requires recomputing K and V for all previous tokens at every step - O(N²) work for a sequence of N tokens. The **KV cache** stores the K and V projections from all previous tokens in GPU HBM. At step $t$, only the new token is processed (one row of Q, K, V), and the cached K, V are concatenated. Total work per step: O(N) for the attention (one Q vector vs N cached K/V pairs) + O(1) GEMM for the new token's projections.

**KV cache memory arithmetic**: for a model with $n_L$ layers, $n_H$ heads, head dimension $d_h$, and maximum sequence length $N_{max}$: KV cache size = $2 \times n_L \times n_H \times N_{max} \times d_h \times \text{sizeof(dtype)}$. For LLaMA-3-70B (80 layers, 64 KV heads in GQA, $d_h = 128$, FP16): $2 \times 80 \times 8 \times 4096 \times 128 \times 2B = 2 \times 80 \times 8 \times 4096 \times 256B \approx 10.7 \text{ GB per sequence}$. With 80GB total VRAM and 35GB for weights: only ~4 sequences can be processed simultaneously. For GPT-4 class models, KV cache is the primary GPU memory bottleneck, not the model weights.

**Grouped Query Attention (GQA) and Multi-Query Attention (MQA)**: standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA) has $n_H$ K/V heads per layer, giving a KV cache of size $O(n_L \ n_H \ N \ d_h)$. MQA (Shazeer, 2019) uses a single K/V head shared across all $n_H$ Q heads - KV cache is $O(n_L \ N \ d_h)$, an $n_H$-fold reduction. GQA (Ainslie et al., 2023) is a middle ground: $n_{KV}$ K/V heads ($n_{KV} < n_H$), each shared by $n_H/n_{KV}$ Q heads. LLaMA-3 70B uses GQA with $n_{KV} = 8$, $n_H = 64$: 8× KV cache reduction vs MHA. The attention computation: for each group, broadcast the K/V across the $n_H/n_{KV}$ Q heads - no correctness change, significant memory savings.

**Prefill vs decode phases**: LLM generation has two stages. **Prefill** (prompt processing): process the entire input prompt in one forward pass. The input is a batch of length $N_{	ext{prompt}}$ tokens; all tokens can be processed in parallel (like training). The attention is full causal attention computed with FlashAttention. This is compute-bound (GEMM-dominated). **Decode** (generation): at each step, process a single new token per sequence. The attention is a single Q vector vs the full cached KV sequence - a matrix-vector product (GEMV), not a matrix-matrix product (GEMM). Batch decode: with B sequences, the 'matrix' is B×1 Q vectors vs N K/V vectors - still a skinny GEMM, but increasingly well-utilized as batch size grows. The decode phase is memory-bandwidth-bound: the KV cache must be read from HBM on every step.

**KV cache quantisation**: FP16 is the default KV cache dtype. **INT8 KV cache** halves memory: for LLaMA-3-70B, reduces from 10.7GB to 5.4GB per sequence. The challenge: K and V activations have outliers (high-magnitude values that don't quantize well). Per-channel or per-token quantisation schemes (scale each channel/token by its max value before quantising) handle this. **INT4 KV cache** (FP4, Q4KM in GGUF format) achieves 4× compression but requires careful calibration. vLLM supports FP8 and INT8 KV cache with minimal accuracy loss on standard benchmarks. The accuracy impact of KV cache quantisation is generally smaller than weight quantisation because the KV cache is regenerated from inputs (no error accumulation across layers for K/V, only within the attention of that layer).

resources.label: Kwon et al. - Efficient Memory Management for LLM Serving (vLLM paper)
vLLM - documentation and KV cache management
Ainslie et al. - GQA: Training Generalised Multi-Query Transformer Models

subtopics.title: PagedAttention and vLLM's block manager
body: **The KV cache fragmentation problem**: without PagedAttention, serving systems pre-allocate a contiguous chunk of GPU memory per request sized for the **maximum possible** sequence length. A request with max_seq_len=2048 tokens allocates 2048 × (KV_per_token bytes) regardless of the actual output length. Since most outputs are much shorter than the maximum, 70-90% of reserved memory is wasted (**internal fragmentation**). With 40 concurrent requests on an 80GB A100 and 35GB for model weights: 45GB / 40 = 1.125GB per request for KV cache = max sequence length of ~2000 tokens for LLaMA-7B. But if most outputs are 50-100 tokens long, 95% of that 1.125GB is wasted.

**PagedAttention's solution** (Kwon et al., 2023): manage KV cache like an OS page table. Physical KV cache is divided into fixed-size **blocks** (each block = $B$ tokens × KV dimensions, typically $B = 16$). Each request has a **block table** mapping logical block indices (0, 1, 2, ...) to physical block IDs. When a request generates a new token, the block manager allocates a new physical block only if the current block is full. When a request finishes, its physical blocks are immediately returned to the free pool. This eliminates internal fragmentation: a request that generates 47 tokens uses exactly 3 blocks (ceil(47/16) = 3), not a 2048-token allocation.

**The block attention kernel**: standard attention assumes contiguous KV cache. PagedAttention modifies the attention kernel to indirect memory accesses through the block table: `K[block_table[block_idx]][token_within_block]` instead of `K[token_idx]`. The kernel iterates over logical blocks, looks up physical block addresses, and loads K/V accordingly. This adds one level of indirection per block access but only ~3% overhead on A100 for typical block sizes (B=16). vLLM implements a custom CUDA kernel for paged attention (`csrc/attention/`) that handles the block table lookups efficiently.

**Sequence sharing and prefix caching**: multiple requests with the same system prompt can **share** KV blocks for the prompt tokens. The block manager uses **copy-on-write**: shared blocks are read-only; when a request generates new tokens diverging from the shared prefix, a new block is allocated and the shared block is left intact. This is used for: system prompt caching (all requests in a session share the system prompt KV cache); beam search (multiple beams share the prompt and split only at divergence points); speculative decoding (draft and target share prompt). vLLM's **prefix caching** feature stores recently-processed prompt blocks in an LRU cache and reuses them for subsequent requests with matching prefixes.

**Scheduling and preemption**: the block manager enables preemption. If a high-priority request arrives and there is no free physical block, the block manager can **swap** the KV cache of a low-priority request from GPU HBM to CPU RAM (via PCIe), freeing GPU blocks. When the swapped request resumes, its blocks are copied back. This is the GPU analog of OS memory swapping. vLLM's scheduler handles priority queues, preemption, and reordering to maximise GPU utilisation. The **continuous batching** scheduler (Agrawal et al., 2023) adds and removes sequences from the running batch at **iteration boundaries** (after each generated token), rather than waiting for entire requests to finish - critical for latency under load.

resources.label: Orca - continuous batching paper (Agrawal et al.)

subtopics.title: Speculative decoding: draft models and parallel verification
body: **The decode bottleneck**: each decode step processes one token per request. On a single A100 with LLaMA-3-70B, one decode step takes ~20ms (reading 140GB of weights + KV cache). Throughput is limited by memory bandwidth, not compute. An A100 at 2TB/s can read 140GB in 70ms - at batch=1, each token takes 70ms (well below compute saturation). The insight: **the GPU is doing nothing useful for most of the decode step** - compute units are idle while waiting for memory reads. Speculative decoding exploits this idle compute.

**Speculative decoding algorithm** (Leviathan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023): use a small 'draft' model (e.g., LLaMA-3-8B) to generate $k$ candidate tokens quickly, then verify all $k$ tokens with the large 'target' model in a single forward pass. The draft model's $k$ tokens are verified by the target in one batched inference step (vs $k$ sequential target model calls). **Acceptance criterion**: if the target's distribution $P$ and draft's distribution $q$ for token $i$ satisfy $q_i \leq P_i$, accept token $i$ unconditionally. If $q_i > P_i$, accept with probability $P_i / q_i$ (rejection sampling). This guarantees the output distribution is exactly identical to the target model alone - no quality degradation.

**Speedup analysis**: let $\alpha$ = acceptance rate (probability a draft token is accepted). Expected accepted tokens per target model call: $\frac{1 - \alpha^k}{1 - \alpha}$. At $\alpha = 0.8$ and $k = 5$: expected tokens = $(1 - 0.8^5) / (1 - 0.8) = (1 - 0.328) / 0.2 = 3.36$ tokens per step. Total latency per target call: draft model time (fast, e.g., 3ms × 5 = 15ms) + target model time (1 step = 70ms at batch=1). Effective throughput: 3.36 tokens / 85ms = 39.5 tokens/sec vs 14.3 tokens/sec for standard decoding. ~2.8× speedup. The gain depends heavily on $\alpha$ (model similarity) and $k$ (speculation length).

**Self-speculative decoding and Medusa**: a refinement that avoids a separate draft model. **Medusa** (Cai et al., 2024) adds $k$ additional decoding heads to the target model, each predicting the token $i$ steps ahead. The extra heads add ~15% parameter overhead but run in the same forward pass. The acceptance check uses tree attention: generate a 'token tree' from all head predictions, run target attention on the full tree (batched), accept the longest consistent prefix. Speedup: 2-3× on typical text generation tasks. **Self-drafting** (Hooper et al., 2024): use early exits from the target model's layers as the draft, then verify with the full model. Zero draft model overhead, ~1.5-2× speedup.

**Production deployment**: vLLM supports speculative decoding natively via `--speculative-model` flag. Key production considerations: (1) **batch interaction**: at batch=32, the target model is nearly saturated; spec decoding's 'free compute' advantage disappears, and the overhead of running the draft model reduces throughput. Speculative decoding provides the most benefit at batch=1-4. (2) **prompt vs completion**: acceptance rates are higher for predictable code completions and lower for creative text - configure $k$ per task type. (3) **draft model selection**: the draft must use the same tokenizer and similar vocabulary distribution. Smaller models of the same family (LLaMA-3-8B for LLaMA-3-70B) work best.

resources.label: Leviathan et al. - Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding (2023)

subtopics.title: Quantisation for inference: GPTQ, AWQ, INT8, and FP8
body: **Why quantise**: a LLaMA-3-70B in FP16 is 140GB - requires 2 × A100-80GB for inference. In INT4 (4 bits per weight), it is 35GB - fits on 1 × A100-80GB or even a 40GB GPU. Quantisation maps FP16 weights $w$ to $n$-bit integers: $w_q = \text{round}(w / s) + z$ where $s$ (scale) and $z$ (zero point) are FP16 values. The quantised weight occupies $n$ bits; during inference, it is dequantised to FP16 on-the-fly before the GEMM. For memory-bound decode (batch=1), INT4 quantisation halves the effective weight bandwidth and directly doubles token generation speed.

**GPTQ** (Frantar et al., 2022): post-training quantisation using a 2nd-order method. For each layer's weight matrix W, find the quantised matrix $\hat{W}$ minimising $||WX - \hat{W}X||_F^2$ over a calibration dataset X. The key algorithm: quantise one column at a time; after quantising column $i$, update all remaining columns to compensate (using the Hessian $H = 2 X X^T$). This is Optimal Brain Quantisation (OBQ) applied layer-wise. GPTQ enables 4-bit quantisation with typically <1% accuracy loss on standard benchmarks. `auto-gptq` and `bitsandbytes` (NVIDIA) are the standard implementations. GPTQ at 4 bits achieves ~2× memory reduction and ~1.5-1.8× throughput improvement for decode.

**AWQ** (Lin et al., 2023): Activation-Aware Weight Quantization. Key insight: not all weight channels are equally important - channels that interact with large activations (measured on calibration data) must be kept at high precision. AWQ finds a per-channel scale $s_i$ such that scaled weights $w_i / s_i$ quantise well, while the output $w_i \ x_i = (w_i / s_i) \ (s_i \ x_i)$ compensates by scaling activations. This avoids the quantisation error on high-activation channels without storing them in FP16. AWQ at INT4 typically matches FP16 perplexity within 0.5-1 units, slightly better than GPTQ. `llm-awq` and vLLM's built-in AWQ support are the main tools.

**INT8 quantisation for activations (SmoothQuant)**: weight-only quantisation (GPTQ/AWQ) quantises only weights; activations remain in FP16. For compute-bound decode (large batch), quantising activations to INT8 enables INT8 GEMM (Tensor Core INT8 is 2× FP16 throughput on A100). The challenge: activation tensors have outlier values (Dettmers et al., 2022 showed that ~0.1% of activation values are 100× larger than the median). Quantising these naively to INT8 causes large errors. **SmoothQuant** (Xiao et al., 2022): mathematically equivalent reformulation - divide activation outlier channels by a smooth factor $s$, multiply weight channels by $s$. This 'smooths' activations at the cost of slightly increasing weight magnitudes. Both are then INT8-quantisable. INT8 with SmoothQuant achieves near-FP16 accuracy with 2× throughput for large-batch inference.

**FP8 on H100 and Hopper architecture**: H100 introduces native FP8 (E4M3 and E5M2) Tensor Cores with 3.9 PFLOP/s peak (vs 2 PFLOP/s FP16). FP8 training requires: (1) per-tensor scaling (scale weights, activations, and gradients by per-tensor max value before quantisation); (2) delayed scaling (compute scales from the previous batch's statistics to avoid a synchronisation barrier); (3) gradient quantisation using E5M2 (more exponent bits for the wider gradient range). The Transformer Engine library (NVIDIA) manages FP8 scaling automatically in PyTorch. For inference-only FP8: quantise weights to E4M3, compute in FP8, accumulate in FP32. This gives ~2× the throughput of FP16 with minimal accuracy loss for well-calibrated models.

subtopics.title: NCCL collectives: all-reduce, all-gather, and reduce-scatter
body: **NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library)** implements the collective operations needed for distributed DL training. A **collective** is a communication pattern involving all processes (GPUs) in a group simultaneously. The key collectives: **all-reduce** - each GPU contributes a tensor; all GPUs receive the element-wise sum (or max, min, product). Used to synchronise gradients across data-parallel workers. **all-gather** - each GPU contributes a shard; all GPUs receive the full concatenated tensor.
